topics.title: Process Creation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Creation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the KTU 2024 Operating Systems syllabus, **Process Creation** is formally defined as the fundamental OS mechanism by which the kernel instantiates a new execution context — a *process* — to perform computational work on behalf of a user, system service, or an existing parent process. According to Silberschatz, Galvin, and Gagne's classical text (the prescribed reference for this course), a **process** is *"a program in execution"* and process creation is the act of bringing this executing instance into existence and registering it with the OS scheduler.

The kernel performs four principal actions during process creation:

1. **Assigning a unique Process Identifier (PID)** to the new entity.
2. **Allocating a Process Control Block (PCB)** in kernel memory.
3. **Loading the program image** (code, data, stack, heap) into a newly constructed address space.
4. **Inserting the new PCB into the appropriate scheduling queue** (typically the *Ready Queue*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Process creation is examined under *Process Management*. Students must master the **Unix `fork()`/`exec()` model** and contrast it with the **Windows `CreateProcess()` model**. A common Part B question asks for a comparative flow diagram — drawing both side-by-side fetches maximum marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** In Linux 5.x+ kernels, the default maximum number of processes per user is **32768** (configurable via `ulimit -u`), and the theoretical absolute limit is **~4 billion** (limited by PID size of `pid_t`, a 32-bit signed integer in default configuration).

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are the head chef of a busy restaurant (the **Operating System**). A customer places an order for Biryani (a request to run a program).

- You **write the recipe down** on a job ticket → This is **Process Creation**: making an official record that work needs to be done.
- You **assign a ticket number** (e.g., `#427`) → This is the **PID (Process ID)**.
- You **clone the master recipe card** and hand it to a junior chef → This is `fork()`: duplicating the parent's context (address space, open files, environment).
- The junior chef **then substitutes the recipe card** with a new one (e.g., replacing "Biryani Recipe" with "Dessert Recipe") → This is `exec()`: replacing the memory image with a new program.
- The senior chef **watches the ticket counter** until the dish is delivered → This is `wait()`: parent synchronizing with child termination.

Just like the kitchen cannot run every dish from one chef (resource limits), the OS must carefully orchestrate creation, scheduling, and termination of every process.

### 1.3 Why Process Creation Matters (KTU Context)

- It is the **entry point of all computation** — every executing user program begins life through some form of process creation.
- It enables **concurrency**: a single program can spawn multiple cooperating processes (e.g., a web server handling multiple client requests via `fork()`).
- It supports **modularity**: complex tasks are decomposed into communicating processes.
- It is foundational to understanding **inter-process communication (IPC)**, **process hierarchies**, and **the shell pipeline model**.

> [!TIP]
> When the examiner asks *"Why can't a process just call a function instead of creating a new process?"*, your answer should be: **functions share the parent's address space** (single thread of control, single failure domain), whereas **processes have isolated address spaces**, providing protection, parallelism, and independent scheduling.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Events That Trigger Process Creation

According to the KTU Module 1 syllabus and standard OS literature, process creation is initiated by one of four logical events:

1. **System Initialization (Boot-time creation):**
   - The kernel spawns several **daemon processes** and **system services**.
   - Examples: `init` / `systemd` (PID = 1 in Linux), `kthreadd`, login managers, schedulers.
   - These are often called *foreground* and *background* processes, where background processes are detached from any terminal.

2. **Execution of a Process-Creation System Call by a Running Process:**
   - A user-level process invokes `fork()` (POSIX/Unix) or `CreateProcess()` (Win32).
   - The calling process is the **parent**; the newly created process is the **child**.

3. **User Request to Execute a New Program:**
   - Double-clicking an icon, typing a command in the shell, etc.
   - The shell interprets the command and invokes `fork()` followed by `exec()` to replace the child with the target program.

4. **Batch Job Submission:**
   - In mainframe / HPC environments, jobs queued via `at`, `cron`, or batch schedulers trigger process creation at scheduled times.

### 2.2 The Classical Unix `fork()` / `exec()` Model

The **two-step Unix process model** is a hallmark KTU question. The steps are:

- **Step 1 — `fork()`:** The kernel **clones the parent process**. The child receives an **exact duplicate** of the parent's address space, open file descriptors, signal handlers, environment, and registers. The child returns `0` from `fork()`; the parent receives the child's PID. Both resume execution at the instruction *after* the `fork()` call.

- **Step 2 — `exec()`:** The child (or sometimes the parent) replaces its memory image with a new program loaded from an executable file. The PID remains unchanged — only the code, data, heap, and stack are overwritten. Registers are reinitialized, and execution begins at the new program's entry point (`_start`).

- **Step 3 — `wait()` / `exit()`:** The parent may invoke `wait()` to block until the child terminates. The child eventually calls `exit()` (or returns from `main()`), at which point the kernel reclaims resources and notifies the parent via a *SIGCHLD* signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** `fork()` creates a *new process*; `exec()` does *not* create a new process — it **transforms** an existing one. This separation of concerns (creation vs. transformation) is unique to Unix and is a frequently tested distinction.

### 2.3 The Windows `CreateProcess()` Model

Windows adopts a **single-step, all-in-one model**. The Win32 API function `CreateProcess()` simultaneously:

- Creates a new process *and* loads a new program into it.
- Returns `TRUE` on success and fills a `PROCESS_INFORMATION` structure with the new PID and thread handle.
- Requires **10+ parameters**, including the executable path, command line, security attributes, inheritance flags, creation flags, environment, current directory, and startup info.

> [!NOTE]
> Windows does **not** separate creation and image-loading into two calls because most real-world programs want to load a new image immediately. The Unix separation is more flexible — it allows the child to perform setup (e.g., redirecting I/O, closing file descriptors) between `fork()` and `exec()`.

### 2.4 Process Control Block (PCB) — The Heart of a Process

The PCB is a kernel data structure that contains *everything* the OS needs to manage a process. At creation time, the kernel initializes a PCB with:

| PCB Component Group | Specific Fields Stored |
|---|---|
| **Identification** | PID, PPID (Parent PID), UID, GID, EUID, EGID |
| **Processor State** | Program Counter (PC), Stack Pointer (SP), General-Purpose Registers, Status Register / Flags |
| **Scheduling Info** | Process State, Priority, Scheduling Policy, CPU Affinity, Accounting Data |
| **Memory Management** | Base/limit registers, Page Table Pointer (CR3 on x86), Segment Selectors |
| **I/O & File State** | File Descriptor Table, Open File Pointers, Working Directory, Root Directory |
| **Signals / IPC** | Pending Signal Bitmap, Signal Handlers, Message Queues, Pipes, Shared Memory IDs |
| **Resource Limits** | `ulimit` values (max open files, max memory, CPU time, etc.) |
| **Accounting** | User CPU time, Kernel CPU time, Start time, Children CPU time |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only "PID, PC, Registers" when listing PCB contents. The examiner expects **at least 6–8 distinct categories**. Mentioning only 3 will cap your marks at ~3/7 for this sub-question.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept / Metric | Formula or Relation | Description / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| **PID Uniqueness** | $\text{PID} \in [1, \, 2^{31} - 1]$ in default Linux | Assigned monotonically; rolled over after reaching maximum |
| **Copy-on-Write Optimization** | $M_{\text{shared}} = M_{\text{parent}} \cap M_{\text{child}}$ initially | Pages are duplicated **only on write**, not at `fork()` time. Saves memory and time. |
| **fork() Return Value** | $R = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if child} \\ \text{child\_PID} & \text{if parent} \\ -1 & \text{on error} \end{cases}$ | Enables parent and child to take diverging code paths. |
| **Child Memory Inheritance** | $\text{AddrSpace}_{\text{child}} \leftarrow \text{AddrSpace}_{\text{parent}}$ | Logical duplication via Copy-on-Write; physical memory shared initially. |
| **Process Tree Size Bound** | $N_{\max} \leq 2^{31} - 1$ | Theoretically, OS-imposed cap via `pid_max` sysctl. |
| **Throughput Bound (Little's Law)** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N_{\text{active}}}{\text{ResidenceTime}}$ | $N_{\text{active}}$ processes in Ready+Running; $N_{\text{active}} = \text{Throughput} \times \text{ResidenceTime}$. |
| **Context Switch Cost** | $T_{\text{switch}} = T_{\text{PCBsave}} + T_{\text{PCBload}}$ | Pure overhead — no useful work done during a switch. |
| **fork() Time Complexity** | $O(\text{page table entries})$ | With Copy-on-Write, actual copying is deferred. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Little's Law** (in queueing form) is sometimes tested to relate **process creation rate** to **average number of processes in the system**. If processes arrive (are created) at rate $\lambda$ and spend an average of $W$ time in the system, the average population is $L = \lambda \times W$.

### 2.6 Process Termination — The Inseparable Companion of Creation

A process can terminate **normally** (via `exit()` system call or returning from `main()`) or **abnormally** (via signals such as `SIGKILL`, `SIGSEGV`, `SIGABRT`, or uncaught exceptions). Upon termination:

- The process enters the **Zombie state** (Linux): PCB is retained until the parent calls `wait()`.
- All open file descriptors are closed; memory is freed.
- Exit status is propagated to the parent.
- If the parent has already terminated, the orphan is reparented to `init`/`systemd` (PID 1).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough of the Unix `fork()` / `exec()` / `wait()` Sequence

The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"Trace the steps executed by the kernel when a parent process invokes `fork()`, and how `exec()` and `wait()` complete the lifecycle."* The exhaustive sequence is as follows:

#### Step 1 — Parent invokes `fork()`

The parent process is executing user code and reaches the `fork()` library call. Control transfers to the kernel via a system call (e.g., `sys_clone` in Linux). At this instant:

- The parent has PID = $P_p$, state = Running.
- The kernel's `sys_clone` handler is invoked.

#### Step 2 — Kernel allocates a new PID

The kernel walks the PID bitmap to find the next available integer. Suppose the assigned PID is $P_c$.

$$P_c = \text{allocate\_pid}()$$

The PID bitmap is updated atomically to avoid race conditions.

#### Step 3 — Kernel allocates and initializes the child's PCB

A new `task_struct` (Linux) or `EPROCESS` (Windows) is allocated from the process descriptor slab allocator. Initialization includes:

- `task->pid = $P_c$`
- `task->ppid = $P_p$` (parent's PID)
- `task->state = TASK_RUNNING`
- `task->cred = copy_cred(parent_cred)` (copy credentials)
- `task->files = copy_files(parent_files)` (duplicate file descriptor table)
- `task->sighand = copy_sighand(parent_sighand)` (duplicate signal handlers)

#### Step 4 — Address space is *logically* duplicated (Copy-on-Write)

The kernel sets both parent and child page table entries to point to the *same* physical frames, but marks them as **read-only** in the hardware MMU. If either process later attempts a write, a page fault occurs and the kernel physically duplicates the frame.

$$M_{\text{child}}^{\text{virtual}} = M_{\text{parent}}^{\text{virtual}}$$

$$\text{PhysicalFrames}_{\text{child}} \leftarrow \text{PhysicalFrames}_{\text{parent}} \quad (\text{marked COW})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Copy-on-Write?** A naive `fork()` would copy the entire address space (often megabytes to gigabytes) even if the child immediately calls `exec()`. COW reduces the cost of `fork()` from $O(\text{address space size})$ to $O(\text{number of page table entries})$, making process creation nearly free.

#### Step 5 — Kernel prepares the child's register state

The child's registers are set so that when it is scheduled, it appears to have just returned from the `fork()` system call:

$$R_{\text{child}} = \begin{cases} \text{R0 (rax)} = 0 & \text{(indicating child)} \\ \text{PC} = \text{instruction after fork()} \\ \text{SP} = \text{stack pointer (child's own stack)} \end{cases}$$

The parent's registers remain unchanged — it will see `fork()` return the child's PID ($P_c$).

#### Step 6 — Child is inserted into the Ready Queue

The kernel appends the child's PCB to the runqueue:

$$\text{Runqueue.append}(\text{PCB}_{\text{child}})$$

The child's state is set to `TASK_RUNNING`. The scheduler may now dispatch it at any time.

#### Step 7 — Kernel returns control to user space

Both parent and child resume in user mode. The parent's `fork()` returns $P_c$ (the child's PID); the child's `fork()` returns 0. Code typically checks:

$$\text{if}(\text{fork}() == 0) \rightarrow \text{child\_path}() \quad \text{else} \rightarrow \text{parent\_path}()$$

#### Step 8 — Child calls `execve(path, argv, envp)`

The child invokes `exec()`, which triggers the kernel's `sys_execve`. The kernel:

1. Verifies the executable file (magic number, permissions).
2. Frees the child's old user-space memory (text, data, heap, stack).
3. Sets up a fresh address space and loads the new program's ELF segments.
4. Initializes a new stack containing `argv`, `envp`, and auxiliary vectors.
5. Resets signal dispositions to defaults (unless `sigaction` flags preserved).
6. Jumps to the new program's entry point (`_start` → `__libc_start_main` → `main`).

$$\text{AddrSpace}_{\text{child}}^{\text{old}} \rightarrow \text{free}()$$
$$\text{AddrSpace}_{\text{child}}^{\text{new}} \leftarrow \text{load\_elf(path)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Crucially, the child's **PID does not change** during `exec()`. The same process now runs a different program — only its memory image has been replaced.

#### Step 9 — Parent calls `wait(&status)`

The parent may now call `waitpid($P_c$, &status, 0)` to block until the child terminates. The kernel:

1. Marks the parent as `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE` (sleeping).
2. Removes it from the runqueue.
3. When the child eventually exits, the kernel wakes the parent, sets `*status` to the exit code, and reaps the child's PCB.

$$\text{Parent}_{\text{state}}: \text{Running} \rightarrow \text{Blocked} \rightarrow \text{Ready} \rightarrow \text{Running}$$

#### Step 10 — Child terminates via `exit(code)`

The child finishes its work and calls `exit(0)`. The kernel:

1. Closes all open file descriptors.
2. Releases memory (pages, page tables, kernel stacks).
3. Sends `SIGCHLD` to the parent.
4. Places the child's PCB in the **zombie** state (Linux-specific).

$$\text{Child}_{\text{state}}: \text{Running} \rightarrow \text{Zombie} \rightarrow \text{Reaped (by parent)}$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Unix `fork()`/`exec()` vs. Windows `CreateProcess()`

| Aspect | Unix `fork()` + `exec()` | Windows `CreateProcess()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of system calls** | Two (creation + image loading) | One (combined) |
| **Address space at creation** | Duplicate of parent | Fresh, empty |
| **Code inheritance** | Yes, initially | No |
| **Flexibility** | High — child can interpose setup | Low — direct load only |
| **Performance** | Fast (Copy-on-Write) | Generally slower (full image load) |
| **API Complexity** | Low (~3 calls total) | High (10+ parameters) |
| **POSIX Compliant** | Yes | No (Win32 native) |
| **Common Use Case** | Shells, daemons, servers | GUI apps, system services |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Process Tree from a Shell Command

**Problem:** A user types the command `ls -l $\vert$ grep txt` in a Linux shell. Show how many processes are created and explain the hierarchy.

**Solution:**

The shell (`bash`, PID 1000) parses the pipeline. To execute it:

1. Shell calls `fork()` → creates child C1 (PID 1001, image = `bash` copy).
2. C1 calls `exec("ls", "-l")` → C1 becomes `ls` (PID 1001, image = `ls`).
3. Shell calls `fork()` again → creates child C2 (PID 1002, image = `bash` copy).
4. C2 calls `exec("grep", "txt")` → C2 becomes `grep` (PID 1002, image = `grep`).
5. Shell calls `wait()` twice — once for each child.

**Total processes created by user request:** $N_{\text{new}} = 2$. The shell itself (PID 1000) is the parent of both. After both children terminate, the shell continues.

$$\text{ProcessTree} = \{\text{PID 1000 (bash)} \rightarrow \{\text{PID 1001 (ls)}, \; \text{PID 1002 (grep)}\}\}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's favorite question:** *"How many processes are created when you run `ls $\vert$ wc`?"* — Answer: **2 new processes** (not 3). The shell does not replace itself; it forks two children and waits on both.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Verifying PID Allocation

**Problem:** Suppose the current PID counter in Linux is 32765. The next 4 `fork()` calls succeed. What are the assigned PIDs? What happens if a 5th `fork()` is attempted without any process exiting?

**Solution:**

The PID allocator hands out the next available integer monotonically (with wrap-around at `pid_max`).

$$P_1 = 32766, \quad P_2 = 32767, \quad P_3 = 32768, \quad P_4 = \text{next wrap-around starting from } 1$$

If `pid_max = 32768` and all 32768 PIDs are currently in use, the 5th `fork()` returns $-1$ and sets `errno = EAGAIN`. The kernel message:

$$\text{log: } \texttt{"fork: Resource temporarily unavailable"}$$

The bound is therefore:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N_{\max}} 1 = 32768 \implies N_{\max} = 32768 \text{ processes}$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Unix `fork()` / `exec()` / `wait()` Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    parentA["Parent Process PID 1000 User Space bash shell"] --> forkCall["Invokes fork system call"]
    forkCall --> kernel1["Kernel sys clone handler enters"]
    kernel1 --> allocPID["Allocate new PID 1001 from PID bitmap"]
    allocPID --> allocPCB["Allocate new PCB task struct from slab"]
    allocPCB --> copyCred["Copy credentials UID GID EUID EGID"]
    copyCred --> copyFD["Duplicate file descriptor table ref count"]
    copyFD --> setupCOW["Mark parent child pages as Copy on Write"]
    setupCOW --> copySig["Duplicate signal handler table"]
    copySig --> initReg["Initialize child registers PC SP rax equals 0"]
    initReg --> enqueue["Insert PCB into Ready Queue"]
    enqueue --> retParent["Return to parent with child PID 1001"]
    enqueue --> retChild["Schedule child to run with rax equals 0"]
    retParent --> parentCheck{"Parent checks fork return value"}
    parentCheck -- yes --> parentWait["Parent calls waitpid blocks"]
    parentCheck -- no --> parentCont["Parent continues execution"]
    retChild --> childCheck{"Child checks fork return value"}
    childCheck -- yes --> childExec["Child calls execve path argv envp"]
    childCheck -- no --> childCont["Child continues as bash copy"]
    childExec --> freeOld["Kernel frees old address space"]
    freeOld --> loadNew["Load new ELF segments into memory"]
    loadNew --> setupStack["Set up new stack with argv envp auxv"]
    setupStack --> jumpStart["Jump to new entry point under score start"]
    jumpStart --> runNew["Child runs new program to completion"]
    runNew --> childExit["Child calls exit 0 or returns from main"]
    childExit --> closeFD["Kernel closes all open file descriptors"]
    closeFD --> freeMem["Kernel frees pages page tables kernel stack"]
    freeMem --> zombie["Child PCB enters ZOMBIE state"]
    zombie --> wakeParent["Send SIGCHLD to parent wake it up"]
    wakeParent --> parentWait
    parentWait --> reap["Parent calls wait reaps zombie status collected"]
    reap --> cleanExit["Parent continues or exits"]
    cleanCont["Parent Cont path completes parent task"] --> cleanExit
    parentCont --> cleanCont
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Process Control Block Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PCB["PROCESS CONTROL BLOCK PCB"]
        idBlock["IDENTIFICATION BLOCK PID PPID UID GID EUID EGID"]
        stateBlock["PROCESS STATE BLOCK New Ready Running Blocked Exit Zombie"]
        cpuBlock["CPU CONTEXT BLOCK Program Counter Stack Pointer General Registers Status Flags"]
        schedBlock["SCHEDULING INFO BLOCK Priority Policy Quantum Affinity Accounting"]
        memBlock["MEMORY MANAGEMENT BLOCK Page Table Base CR3 Segment Limits"]
        ioBlock["I O AND FILE STATE BLOCK File Descriptor Table Working Directory Root Directory"]
        sigBlock["SIGNALS AND IPC BLOCK Pending Signals Handlers Pipes Sockets Shared Memory"]
        limBlock["RESOURCE LIMITS BLOCK ulimit values max files max CPU max memory"]
        acctBlock["ACCOUNTING BLOCK User CPU time Kernel CPU time Start time Children time"]
    end
    idBlock --- stateBlock
    stateBlock --- cpuBlock
    cpuBlock --- schedBlock
    schedBlock --- memBlock
    memBlock --- ioBlock
    ioBlock --- sigBlock
    sigBlock --- limBlock
    limBlock --- acctBlock
```

---

### 4.3 State Transition Diagram — From Creation to Termination

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> sNew : Admitted Created
    sNew : NEW being created PCB initialized
    sReady : READY waiting for CPU on runqueue
    sRunning : RUNNING executing on CPU core
    sBlocked : BLOCKED waiting for I O or event
    sExit : EXIT terminated cleaned up
    sNew --> sReady : admit to runqueue
    sReady --> sRunning : scheduler dispatch
    sRunning --> sReady : preempt time slice expired
    sRunning --> sBlocked : I O request or wait syscall
    sBlocked --> sReady : I O completion event signaled
    sRunning --> sExit : exit syscall or fatal signal
    sReady --> sExit : kill signal while not running
    sBlocked --> sExit : kill signal while blocked
    sExit --> [*] : parent reaps via wait PCB freed
```

---

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Unix vs. Windows Process Creation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph unixSide["UNIX POSIX MODEL"]
        u1["User calls fork"]
        u2["Kernel clones parent address space Copy on Write"]
        u3["Child returns 0 parent returns child PID"]
        u4["Child optionally calls execve to load new program"]
        u5["Two system calls combined flow"]
        u1 --> u2 --> u3 --> u4
        u4 --> u5
    end
    subgraph winSide["WINDOWS WIN32 MODEL"]
        w1["User calls CreateProcess with executable path and args"]
        w2["Kernel allocates new EPROCESS and address space"]
        w3["Kernel loads executable image directly into new space"]
        w4["Kernel creates initial thread and starts execution"]
        w5["Single all in one system call"]
        w1 --> w2 --> w3 --> w4 --> w5
    end
```

---

### 4.5 Process Tree Example — Shell Pipeline Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initNode["init or systemd PID 1"]
    sshdNode["sshd PID 800"]
    bashNode["bash shell PID 1000"]
    lsNode["ls PID 1001"]
    grepNode["grep PID 1002"]
    wcNode["wc PID 1003"]
    initNode --> sshdNode
    sshdNode --> bashNode
    bashNode --> lsNode
    bashNode --> grepNode
    bashNode --> wcNode
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define a process. How is it different from a program?**
*(Model Answer — 3 marks)*

A **process** is a program in execution — an active, dynamic entity consisting of the executable code, current activity (PC value, registers), stack, heap, data section, and associated OS metadata (PCB). A **program**, by contrast, is a passive, static file stored on disk containing instructions and data. The same program can correspond to multiple processes (e.g., two users running the same editor), but each process executes exactly one program image at a time.

Key distinctions:

| Aspect | Program | Process |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Passive (file on disk) | Active (executing) |
| Lifetime | Until deleted | Until termination |
| Resources | None allocated | CPU, memory, files, I/O |
| Identity | Filename | PID |

*(Examiner's note: awarding full marks requires both definition AND a tabular or bullet comparison — definitions alone fetch ~2/3.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. List any six fields stored in the Process Control Block (PCB) and state their purpose.**
*(Model Answer — 3 marks)*

1. **Process ID (PID)** — unique OS-wide identifier; used by scheduler and system calls.
2. **Program Counter (PC)** — address of the next instruction to execute; saved during context switch.
3. **Process State** — current state (New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Exit); determines queue placement.
4. **CPU Registers** — accumulator, index registers, stack pointer; preserved across context switches.
5. **Memory Management Info** — page table base register, segment limits; needed for address translation.
6. **I/O Status Information** — list of open files, I/O devices allocated, pending I/O requests.
7. *(Bonus)* **Scheduling Info** — priority, accounting data, quantum.

*(Examiner's note: listing only 3–4 fields caps marks at ~1.5/3. Always include purpose along with field name.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q4]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **Unix process creation model** using the `fork()` and `exec()` system calls. Clearly distinguish the return values of `fork()` for the parent and the child. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Windows `CreateProcess()` model** in detail. Compare it with the Unix `fork()`/`exec()` model on at least five parameters. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What is a **Process Control Block (PCB)**? List and explain all major components of a PCB. How does the kernel allocate and initialize a PCB during process creation? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the various **events that trigger process creation** in an operating system. Describe the **process termination** mechanism, including normal exit, abnormal exit, and zombie processes. *(7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions for Question A

#### Part (a) — Unix `fork()` / `exec()` Model

**Step 1 — `fork()` is invoked.**
The parent process (PID = $P_p$) executes user code that reaches the `fork()` library wrapper. Control transfers to the kernel via the `sys_clone` system call.

**Step 2 — Kernel allocates child PID and PCB.**
The kernel picks the next available PID ($P_c$) from the PID bitmap and allocates a new `task_struct` (PCB) from the slab allocator.

**Step 3 — Address space is logically cloned with Copy-on-Write.**
The child's page tables are set to point to the same physical frames as the parent, but all entries are marked read-only in the MMU. On the first write to any shared page, the kernel transparently duplicates the frame.

**Step 4 — Return values diverge.**
Both parent and child resume execution at the instruction *after* `fork()`. The return values differ:

$$R_{\text{fork}} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{in child process} \\ P_c & \text{in parent process} \\ -1 & \text{on error (errno set)} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Child calls `execve(path, argv, envp)`.**
The kernel frees the child's old user-space memory, loads the new ELF segments from disk, sets up a fresh stack with `argv`, `envp`, and auxiliary vectors, and jumps to the new entry point `_start`. The child's PID remains unchanged.

**Step 6 — Parent optionally calls `waitpid($P_c$, &status, 0)`.**
The parent blocks until the child terminates. On termination, the kernel wakes the parent, fills `*status` with the exit code, and reaps the child's PCB.

**Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1):**
The complete flowchart illustrates kernel allocation, COW setup, register initialization, scheduling, and exit/wait synchronization.

*(Self-correction note for students: do not confuse "creation of a process" with "loading a new program." `fork()` creates; `exec()` transforms.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Windows `CreateProcess()` Model

`CreateProcess()` is a single Win32 API function that simultaneously creates a new process and loads a new program into it. Its signature is:

```
BOOL CreateProcess(
    LPCTSTR lpApplicationName,
    LPTSTR  lpCommandLine,
    LPSECURITY_ATTRIBUTES lpProcessAttributes,
    LPSECURITY_ATTRIBUTES lpThreadAttributes,
    BOOL bInheritHandles,
    DWORD dwCreationFlags,
    LPVOID lpEnvironment,
    LPCTSTR lpCurrentDirectory,
    LPSTARTUPINFO lpStartupInfo,
    LPPROCESS_INFORMATION lpProcessInformation
);
```

**Parameters explained:**

- `lpApplicationName` — path to the executable module (e.g., `"C:\\Windows\\notepad.exe"`).
- `lpCommandLine` — full command line, including arguments.
- `bInheritHandles` — whether child inherits parent's open handles.
- `dwCreationFlags` — options like `DEBUG_PROCESS`, `CREATE_NEW_CONSOLE`, `CREATE_SUSPENDED` (creates the process in suspended state — useful for debuggers).
- `lpEnvironment` — pointer to environment block; `NULL` inherits parent's environment.
- `lpStartupInfo` — controls window appearance, standard handles, desktop, etc.
- `lpProcessInformation` — output structure filled with the new PID and primary thread handle.

**Comparison with Unix (at least five parameters):**

| Parameter | Unix `fork()` + `exec()` | Windows `CreateProcess()` |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Number of system calls | 2 (`fork`, `exec`) | 1 (`CreateProcess`) |
| 2. Address space at creation | Duplicate of parent | Fresh, empty |
| 3. Image loading | Separate step via `exec` | Combined within the call |
| 4. Inheritance of open files | Default yes; can be changed via `fcntl` | Controlled via `bInheritHandles` |
| 5. Process suspension | Not directly supported | Supported via `CREATE_SUSPENDED` flag |
| 6. Code complexity | Low (3–4 lines of C) | High (10+ parameters) |
| 7. POSIX compliance | Yes | No |

---

### 5.4 Model Solutions for Question B

#### Part (a) — Process Control Block (PCB)

**Definition:** A **Process Control Block (PCB)** is a kernel data structure that serves as the *identity card* of a process. It stores every piece of information the OS needs to manage, schedule, and resume the process.

**Components (≥8 categories expected):**

1. **Process Identification:** PID, PPID, UID, GID, EUID, EGID.
2. **Processor State:** Program Counter, Stack Pointer, General-Purpose Registers, Status Register.
3. **Process State:** New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Exit, Zombie.
4. **Scheduling Information:** Priority, scheduling policy (e.g., CFS in Linux), quantum, CPU affinity, accounting data.
5. **Memory Management:** Base and limit registers, page table base pointer (CR3 on x86), segment selectors.
6. **I/O and File State:** File descriptor table, root directory, working directory.
7. **Signal and IPC Information:** Pending signal bitmap, signal handlers, pipes, sockets, shared memory IDs.
8. **Resource Limits:** `ulimit` values for max open files, max memory, max CPU time.
9. **Accounting:** User CPU time, kernel CPU time, start time, children CPU time.

**Allocation during creation:**
The kernel calls the slab allocator (e.g., `kmem_cache_alloc` in Linux) to obtain a fresh `task_struct`. The function pointers are initialized from a per-arch template, the PID is allocated from the bitmap, the parent's credentials and files are reference-counted and linked, the address space is set up with Copy-on-Write pages, and the PCB is inserted into the runqueue.

---

#### Part (b) — Process Creation Events and Termination

**Events that trigger creation:**

1. **System boot:** Kernel spawns `init`/`systemd` (PID 1), background daemons, schedulers.
2. **User login:** Login process spawns a shell (`bash`, `zsh`).
3. **User command execution:** Shell parses the command and calls `fork()` + `exec()`.
4. **Application-level creation:** A program invokes `fork()`/`CreateProcess()` for concurrency.
5. **Batch jobs:** `cron`, `at`, HPC schedulers spawn processes at scheduled times.

**Termination mechanisms:**

- **Normal exit:** The process calls `exit(status)` or returns from `main()`. The kernel closes file descriptors, frees memory, and sends `SIGCHLD` to the parent.
- **Abnormal exit:** A signal such as `SIGSEGV` (segmentation fault), `SIGABRT` (abort), `SIGFPE` (arithmetic error), or `SIGKILL` (unconditional kill) terminates the process. Core dumps may be generated for debugging.
- **Zombie state (Linux):** After exit, the PCB is retained until the parent calls `wait()` to read the exit status. A long-lived zombie indicates a parent that ignores `SIGCHLD` or fails to call `wait()`.
- **Orphan handling:** If the parent terminates before the child, the kernel reparents the orphan to `init` (PID 1), which automatically reaps it via `wait()`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating `fork()` and `exec()`:** Writing "`exec()` creates a new process" is the **#1 mistake**. `exec()` only *replaces* the memory image — the process identity (PID) is unchanged. Marks deducted: 2–3.
> 2. **Omitting Copy-on-Write:** Stating "`fork()` copies the entire address space" loses 1–2 marks. Always mention COW for full credit.
> 3. **Incomplete PCB list:** Listing only 3–4 fields (PID, PC, registers, state) when the examiner expects 8+ categories. Mitigation: use a structured table with 6–8 rows.
> 4. **Confusing thread creation with process creation:** Some students describe `pthread_create` instead of `fork()`. These are distinct — threads share the address space; processes do not.
> 5. **Ignoring parent–child synchronization:** Skipping `wait()` / `SIGCHLD` mechanics in termination answers. Always close the lifecycle loop.
> 6. **Wrong process count in shell pipeline:** Saying 3 processes for `ls $\vert$ grep $\vert$ wc` instead of 3 children + 1 parent shell (so **3 new**). Read carefully.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Process vs. Program:** Program is *passive* (file on disk); process is *active* (executing instance). Same program → multiple processes possible.
- **Four creation triggers:** System boot, user request, batch job, application-level `fork()`/`CreateProcess()`.
- **`fork()` returns 0 in child, child's PID in parent, -1 on error.** This single return value is the cornerstone of parent–child divergence.
- **`exec()` does NOT create a process** — it replaces the address space of an existing one. The PID stays constant.
- **Copy-on-Write (COW)** makes `fork()` cheap: physical pages are duplicated only when one side writes.
- **PCB contains ≥8 categories** of info: ID, CPU context, state, scheduling, memory, I/O, signals, accounting. Listing only 3–4 loses marks.
- **Unix = two-step (`fork` + `exec`); Windows = one-step (`CreateProcess`).** Always draw both side-by-side in comparative answers.
- **Process states:** New → Ready → Running → Blocked → Exit, with possible Zombie interim state.
- **`wait()` reap mechanism:** Without parent `wait()`, child remains a **zombie**; orphaned children are reparented to `init` (PID 1).
- **Linux PID cap:** Default `pid_max = 32768`; can be raised up to `2^22` (~4 million) on 64-bit systems.
- **Copy-on-Write trade-off:** Saves memory + time at `fork()`, but adds a page-fault cost on the first write to a COW page.
- **Shell pipeline process count:** For `cmd1 $\vert$ cmd2 $\vert$ cmd3`, exactly **3 new processes** are created (the shell does not replace itself).
- **Key syscalls to memorize for KTU:** `fork()`, `vfork()`, `clone()`, `execve()`, `wait()`, `waitpid()`, `exit()`, `getpid()`, `getppid()`.
- **Windows API to memorize:** `CreateProcess()`, `CreateThread()`, `ExitProcess()`, `WaitForSingleObject()`, `GetExitCodeProcess()`.
- **Signals to know:** `SIGCHLD` (sent to parent on child termination), `SIGKILL` (uncatchable kill), `SIGSEGV` (segmentation fault), `SIGTERM` (graceful termination request).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In an Operating System (OS), a **process** is defined as a program in execution. As the OS executes a process, that process passes through a series of discrete **process states**. A process state is a fundamental data structure that completely describes the current activity and resource status of a process at a specific instant in time. The state changes as the process moves through its lifecycle, from its initial creation to its eventual termination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** The state of a process is uniquely identified by the values held in the **Process Control Block (PCB)** registers at any given moment. The OS uses these state transitions to perform scheduling, resource allocation, and context switching.

The **standard 5-state process model** recognized in the KTU syllabus is:
1. **New (Created)** — Process is being created.
2. **Ready** — Process is waiting to be assigned to a processor.
3. **Running** — Instructions are being executed by the CPU.
4. **Blocked (Waiting)** — Process is waiting for some event to occur (such as I/O completion or signal reception).
5. **Terminated (Exit)** — Process has finished execution.

A 7-state model (with **Suspend-Ready** and **Suspend-Wait**) is also part of the extended syllabus and is often asked in Module 1 questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Patient Lifecycle"

To make this intuitive, imagine a patient visiting a multi-specialty hospital:

| Hospital Phase | OS Process State | What is happening? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Patient registers at reception | **New** | The OS is creating the process, allocating PID, and initializing the PCB. |
| Patient waits in the queue for a doctor | **Ready** | Process is fully loaded into main memory and is in the Ready Queue waiting for CPU time. |
| Doctor examines the patient | **Running** | The CPU is actively executing the instructions of the process. |
| Patient goes for an X-Ray and waits for the report | **Blocked / Waiting** | The process cannot proceed until I/O completes (e.g., disk read, network packet arrival). |
| Patient leaves after treatment | **Terminated** | The process has completed execution; resources are deallocated. |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a patient can be moved back and forth between the waiting area and the doctor's cabin (scheduled and preempted), a process can transition multiple times between the **Ready** and **Running** states. The OS scheduler acts as the "hospital receptionist" who decides who gets the doctor's (CPU's) attention next.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Highlights)

- **CPU Utilization (U)** — The percentage of time the CPU is busy executing a process.
- **Throughput (X)** — The number of processes completed per unit of time, measured in **processes/second**.
- **Turnaround Time (TAT)** — Total time from process submission to completion.
- **Waiting Time (WT)** — Total time a process spends in the Ready Queue.
- **Response Time (RT)** — Time from submission until the first response is produced.
- **Context Switch (CS)** — The time taken to save the state of the currently running process and load the state of the next one.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five States — Operational Breakdown

A process does not exist in a single static state. It transitions through multiple states depending on:
- Whether the CPU is available.
- Whether the requested resource (I/O, memory) is available.
- The scheduling decisions made by the OS.

### Step-by-step Operational Logic:

- **Step 1: New State**
  - The OS recognizes a new job (e.g., from a command line or batch input).
  - The kernel creates the **Process Control Block (PCB)** and assigns a unique **Process Identifier (PID)**.
  - The process is being initialized. If the OS cannot allocate the required resources (e.g., memory), it moves directly to Terminated.

- **Step 2: New → Ready Transition (Admitted)**
  - The Long-Term Scheduler (Job Scheduler) admits the process into the Ready Queue.
  - The process is now loaded into **main memory** and waits for CPU dispatch.

- **Step 3: Ready → Running Transition (Dispatch)**
  - The Short-Term Scheduler (CPU Scheduler) selects this process from the Ready Queue.
  - The Dispatcher loads the PCB context into the CPU registers. A **Context Switch** occurs.

- **Step 4: Running → Terminated Transition (Exit)**
  - The process executes its final instruction (e.g., `return 0;` in C, or system call `exit()`).
  - Resources are deallocated, and the PCB is removed from the system.

- **Step 5: Running → Blocked Transition (I/O or Event Wait)**
  - The process issues an I/O request, a `wait()` system call, or accesses a locked semaphore.
  - The CPU is relinquished voluntarily. The process cannot run until the event completes.

- **Step 6: Blocked → Ready Transition (I/O or Event Completion)**
  - The I/O device raises an interrupt signaling completion.
  - The OS moves the process back to the Ready Queue. Note: The process does **NOT** go directly to the Running state.

- **Step 7: Running → Ready Transition (Preemption / Interrupt)**
  - In preemptive scheduling (e.g., Round Robin), a timer interrupt forces the CPU to give up the process.
  - The process is preempted and placed back in the Ready Queue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is "Blocked → Running" an illegal transition?**
> This is a classic KTU question. A process in the Blocked state is waiting for an event. It must first transition to the **Ready** state (Step 6) so that the scheduler can evaluate it fairly against all other ready processes. Skipping the scheduler would violate fairness principles.

## 2.2 The 7-State Model (Extended)

When the OS uses **swapping** to manage memory pressure, two additional suspended states are introduced:

- **Suspend-Ready (Ready-Suspend):** Process is in secondary memory (disk) but would be ready to run if loaded into main memory.
- **Suspend-Wait (Blocked-Suspend):** Process is in secondary memory and is also waiting for an event.

> This is critical in virtual memory and demand-paging systems covered later in Module 3.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes all **high-yield formulas** for KTU 2024 OS exams regarding process states, scheduling, and performance metrics.

| Metric / Concept | Formula | Units / Boundary |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CPU Utilization (U)** | $U = \dfrac{T_{\text{busy}}}{T_{\text{busy}} + T_{\text{idle}}}$ | Percentage ($\%$) ; $0 \le U \le 1$ |
| **Throughput (X)** | $X = \dfrac{N_{\text{completed}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | Processes / second |
| **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $TAT = T_{\text{completion}} - T_{\text{arrival}}$ | Time units (ms / s) |
| **Waiting Time (WT)** | $WT = TAT - T_{\text{burst}} - T_{\text{I/O}}$ | Time units |
| **Response Time (RT)** | $RT = T_{\text{first\_CPU}} - T_{\text{arrival}}$ | Time units |
| **Average Waiting Time** | $\overline{WT} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i}{n}$ | Time units |
| **Context Switch Time** | $CS = T_{\text{save}} + T_{\text{load}} + T_{\text{dispatch}}$ | Time units |
| **CPU Scheduling Constraint** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{\text{burst}_i} \ge T_{\text{total\_CPU}}$ | Strictly $\ge$ (with idle time it becomes $>$) |
| **State Transition Probability** | $P(\text{Ready} \to \text{Running}) = \dfrac{1}{N_{\text{ready}}}$ | For Round Robin (uniform) |
| **Degree of Multiprogramming** | $D = \dfrac{T_{\text{CPU}}}{\text{avg. process residency time}}$ | Dimensionless integer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not theoretical — they are used in real-time systems engineering (avionics, medical devices, automotive ECUs) where missed deadlines can be catastrophic. The IEEE 802.1Q standard for industrial networks and the ARINC 653 standard for avionics software rely directly on these metrics.

## 2.4 Why These States Matter — Real-World Utility

- **Process states** enable the OS to support **multiprogramming** and **time-sharing** — running many processes simultaneously by rapidly switching the CPU between them.
- Without distinct states, the OS could not support **fair scheduling**, **memory protection**, or **responsive I/O**.
- Cloud computing platforms (AWS EC2, Azure VMs) use state-based process management to handle **thousands of virtualized processes per second**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Waiting Time for a 3-Process FCFS Schedule

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation problem**. Suppose 3 processes arrive at time $0$ with CPU bursts $P_1 = 24$, $P_2 = 3$, $P_3 = 3$ (in ms).

### Step 1: Write the Gantt Chart

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt Chart (FCFS Order P1, P2, P3):} \\
\text{Time: } \vert 0 \quad 24 \quad 27 \quad 30 \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Completion Times

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{P_1} &= 24 \text{ ms} \\
C_{P_2} &= 24 + 3 = 27 \text{ ms} \\
C_{P_3} &= 27 + 3 = 30 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since all processes arrived at $t = 0$, the **arrival time** $A_i = 0$ for all.

### Step 3: Compute Turnaround Time (TAT)

Using the identity $TAT_i = C_i - A_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P_1} &= 24 - 0 = 24 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P_2} &= 27 - 0 = 27 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P_3} &= 30 - 0 = 30 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Waiting Time (WT)

Using $WT_i = TAT_i - \text{Burst}_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P_1} &= 24 - 24 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P_2} &= 27 - 3 = 24 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P_3} &= 30 - 3 = 27 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Average Waiting Time

$$
\overline{WT} = \frac{WT_{P_1} + WT_{P_2} + WT_{P_3}}{n} = \frac{0 + 24 + 27}{3} = \frac{51}{3} = 17 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 6: Derive the Convoy Effect

Notice that $P_2$ and $P_3$ (short jobs) had to wait for $P_1$ (long job). This is the **Convoy Effect**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Convoy Penalty} &= \sum_{i=2}^{n} \left( \text{Burst of preceding process} \right) \cdot \text{(unit factor)} \\
\text{For } P_2: \text{ Penalty} &= 24 \text{ ms (waited for } P_1\text{)} \\
\text{For } P_3: \text{ Penalty} &= 27 \text{ ms (waited for } P_1 + P_2\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** FCFS with a long first process causes **starvation of short processes**. SJF (Shortest Job First) minimizes $\overline{WT}$ provably — this is the **optimal scheduling theorem** in OS literature.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: State Transition Validity

The following matrix describes whether a transition between two states is **legal** in the standard 5-state model. A `1` denotes valid; a `0` denotes illegal.

| From \ To | New | Ready | Running | Blocked | Terminated |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **New** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 (if admission fails) |
| **Ready** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| **Running** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| **Blocked** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Terminated** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

### Step-by-step Justification of Each Row:

- **New row:** A new process can be admitted to the Ready Queue, OR rejected (Terminated) if memory is insufficient. It cannot run directly or wait for I/O.
- **Ready row:** The only legal move is to Running (via the dispatcher).
- **Running row:** It can be preempted back to Ready, blocked on I/O, or terminated. It cannot go to New (cannot be re-created).
- **Blocked row:** Only I/O completion can move it to Ready. It cannot run directly (illegal — explains the KTU pitfall).
- **Terminated row:** Dead-end state. No transitions out.

## 3.3 Context Switch Time Derivation

When a CPU switches from process $P_i$ to process $P_j$, the total time lost to overhead is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CS_{\text{total}} &= T_{\text{save}}(P_i) + T_{\text{load}}(P_j) + T_{\text{dispatch}} + T_{\text{flush\_pipeline}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **effective CPU utilization** under a context switch is:

$$
U_{\text{eff}} = \frac{T_{\text{busy}}}{T_{\text{busy}} + T_{\text{CS}} \cdot N_{\text{switches}}}
$$

This is why **excessive context switching (thrashing)** reduces performance dramatically — a key topic in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Standard 5-State Process Transition Diagram (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **canonical 5-state process model** used in KTU textbooks (Silberschatz, Stallings).

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateNew : Program starts
    stateNew : NEW (Created)
    stateNew : PCB initialized
    stateNew : PID assigned
    stateNew --> stateReady : Admitted (Long-term Scheduler)
    stateNew --> stateTerminated : Admission Failed (Out of memory)

    stateReady : READY
    stateReady : In Main Memory
    stateReady : Waiting in Ready Queue

    stateRunning : RUNNING
    stateRunning : Instructions executing on CPU
    stateRunning : PCB loaded into registers

    stateBlocked : BLOCKED (WAITING)
    stateBlocked : Waiting for I/O or Event
    stateBlocked : Cannot execute even if CPU is free

    stateTerminated : TERMINATED
    stateTerminated : Execution complete
    stateTerminated : Resources deallocated

    stateReady --> stateRunning : Dispatch (Short-term Scheduler)
    stateRunning --> stateReady : Preemption (Timer Interrupt) or Yield
    stateRunning --> stateBlocked : I/O Request or wait() syscall
    stateBlocked --> stateReady : I/O Completion (Interrupt Handler)
    stateRunning --> stateTerminated : Exit syscall or Fatal Error

    stateTerminated --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Mermaid Labels:** All node labels use raw uppercase alphanumeric text (e.g., `stateNew`, `stateReady`) to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards. No bold, italics, or special characters are used inside quoted node labels.

## 4.2 Extended 7-State Model with Suspend States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> sNew
    sNew : NEW
    sReady : READY (In Memory)
    sRun : RUNNING
    sBlock : BLOCKED (In Memory)
    sSusR : SUSPEND-READY (On Disk)
    sSusB : SUSPEND-BLOCKED (On Disk)
    sTerm : TERMINATED

    sNew --> sReady : Admit
    sNew --> sTerm : No Memory
    sReady --> sRun : Dispatch
    sRun --> sReady : Preempt
    sRun --> sBlock : I/O Wait
    sBlock --> sReady : I/O Complete
    sReady --> sSusR : Swap Out (Memory Pressure)
    sSusR --> sReady : Swap In
    sBlock --> sSusB : Swap Out (Blocked)
    sSusB --> sSusR : I/O Complete while Suspended
    sSusB --> sBlock : Swap In
    sRun --> sTerm : Exit
    sSusR --> sTerm : Killed
    sSusB --> sTerm : Killed
    sTerm --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Process State Manager

The diagram below shows how the **OS kernel modules** interact to manage process states internally.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Application] -->|fork or exec syscall| B[Process Creation Module]
    B -->|Allocate PID and PCB| C[PCB Database]
    B -->|Initialize| D[State: NEW]
    D -->|Long-Term Scheduler| E[State: READY]
    E -->|Short-Term Scheduler Dispatcher| F[State: RUNNING]
    F -->|I/O Request| G[State: BLOCKED]
    G -->|Interrupt Handler| E
    F -->|Timer Interrupt| E
    F -->|Exit syscall| H[State: TERMINATED]
    H -->|Resource Cleanup| I[Free PCB]
    C -.->|Read/Write| F
    C -.->|Update State Field| E
    C -.->|Block Queue| G
```

> **Figure Caption:** The Process State Manager coordinates between the user space, scheduler modules, interrupt handlers, and the central PCB database. Each state transition updates the PCB and triggers the appropriate scheduler.

## 4.4 Process State Queues (Memory Layout View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MainMemory["MAIN MEMORY (RAM)"]
        RQ[READY QUEUE]
        BQ[BLOCKED QUEUE]
    end
    subgraph CPU["CPU CORE"]
        RUN[RUNNING PROCESS]
    end
    subgraph Disk["SECONDARY STORAGE (DISK)"]
        SRQ[SUSPEND-READY QUEUE]
        SBQ[SUSPEND-BLOCKED QUEUE]
    end
    RQ -->|Dispatch| RUN
    RUN -->|Preempt| RQ
    RUN -->|I/O Wait| BQ
    BQ -->|I/O Complete| RQ
    RQ -->|Memory Pressure Swap Out| SRQ
    SRQ -->|Memory Available Swap In| RQ
    BQ -->|Swap Out| SBQ
    SBQ -->|Swap In| BQ
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a process. List the various states of a process. **[3 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

A **process** is a program in execution. It is more than just the program code — it includes the current activity, the value of the program counter, registers, and the resources allocated to it.

The various states of a process in the 5-state model are:
1. **New** — Process is being created.
2. **Ready** — Process is in memory, waiting for CPU.
3. **Running** — Instructions are being executed by the CPU.
4. **Blocked / Waiting** — Process is waiting for I/O or an event.
5. **Terminated** — Process has finished execution.

---

### Question 2: What is a Process Control Block (PCB)? Mention any four fields stored in it. **[3 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Process Control Block (PCB)** is a kernel data structure that stores all the information about a particular process. It is the identity and "passport" of a process inside the OS.

Four important fields stored in a PCB are:
1. **Process ID (PID)** — Unique identifier.
2. **Process State** — Current state (New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated).
3. **Program Counter (PC)** — Address of the next instruction to execute.
4. **CPU Registers** — Values of accumulators, stack pointer, base registers, etc.

*(Other acceptable fields: Memory management info, Accounting info, I/O status, Open file descriptors.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Process States and Transitions **[14 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Draw the process state transition diagram and explain the different states with suitable transitions. Mention why the transition from "Blocked to Running" is illegal. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the Process Control Block (PCB) in detail. How does the OS use it during a context switch? **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A process in an OS moves through a series of well-defined states. The **5-state process model** includes the following states and transitions:

1. **New → Ready (Admitted):** The Long-Term Scheduler admits the process and loads it into the main memory.
2. **Ready → Running (Dispatch):** The Short-Term Scheduler selects the process and the Dispatcher loads its PCB into the CPU.
3. **Running → Blocked (Wait / I/O):** The process issues an I/O request or `wait()` system call and gives up the CPU voluntarily.
4. **Blocked → Ready (I/O Completion):** The I/O device raises an interrupt; the OS moves the process back to the Ready Queue.
5. **Running → Ready (Preemption):** A timer interrupt in preemptive systems (e.g., Round Robin) forces the process back to Ready.
6. **Running → Terminated (Exit):** The process finishes its execution.

> **Why "Blocked → Running" is illegal:**
> A blocked process is waiting for an external event (I/O completion, signal). If it were allowed to jump directly to the Running state, it would bypass the **Short-Term Scheduler**, violating the principle of **fair CPU allocation**. All processes must compete fairly through the Ready Queue. This also prevents the "infinite wait" problem where a process would re-run before its I/O buffer is even ready.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The **Process Control Block (PCB)** is the central data structure of a process. It contains:

| Category | Fields |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Process Identification** | PID, Parent PID, User ID |
| **Process State** | New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated |
| **CPU Context** | Program Counter, Registers, Stack Pointer |
| **Memory Management** | Base/Limit registers, Page tables, Segment tables |
| **I/O Status** | Open files, I/O devices allocated, List of open sockets |
| **Accounting** | CPU time used, Time limits, Account numbers |

**Role during a Context Switch:**
1. The OS **saves the state** of the currently running process into its PCB (registers, PC, memory pointers).
2. The process's state field is changed from "Running" to "Ready" (or "Blocked").
3. The scheduler **selects a new process** from the Ready Queue.
4. The OS **loads the new process's PCB** into the CPU registers.
5. The new process's state field is updated to "Running".
6. Execution resumes from the saved Program Counter.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often draw the state diagram **without labels on the arrows**. The KTU answer key specifically requires **labels on every transition arrow** (e.g., "Admitted", "Dispatch", "Preempt", "I/O Wait"). Drawing the diagram without these labels costs **2 to 3 marks** out of 7. Also, do not forget to mention the **PCB update** in every state transition.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Performance Metrics and Schedulers **[14 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of schedulers (Long-term, Short-term, Medium-term) with their functions. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the following set of processes, compute the Average Waiting Time (AWT) and Average Turnaround Time (ATAT) using FCFS scheduling: **[7 Marks]**

| Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| P1 | 0 | 4 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 |
| P3 | 2 | 2 |
| P4 | 3 | 1 |

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Long-Term Scheduler (Job Scheduler):**
- Selects which jobs from the **disk pool** are admitted into the **Ready Queue** in main memory.
- Controls the **degree of multiprogramming**.
- Runs **infrequently** (seconds to minutes).
- Decides the **mix** of I/O-bound and CPU-bound jobs.

**2. Short-Term Scheduler (CPU Scheduler):**
- Selects which process from the **Ready Queue** is dispatched to the CPU next.
- Runs **very frequently** (every few milliseconds).
- Must be **extremely fast** to avoid CPU wastage.
- Decides **which ready process runs now**.

**3. Medium-Term Scheduler (Swapper):**
- Used in systems with **swapping** or **virtual memory**.
- Temporarily removes a process from main memory to **secondary storage** (suspends it) to reduce the degree of multiprogramming.
- Later brings it back (swap in) to continue execution.
- Used to **improve the process mix** or free memory under pressure.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Construct the Gantt Chart (FCFS Order: P1, P2, P3, P4)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt Chart:} \\
\text{Time: } \vert 0 \quad 4 \quad 7 \quad 9 \quad 10 \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Completion Times**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{P1} &= 4 \\
C_{P2} &= 4 + 3 = 7 \\
C_{P3} &= 7 + 2 = 9 \\
C_{P4} &= 9 + 1 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Turnaround Time (TAT = C − A)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 4 - 0 = 4 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 7 - 1 = 6 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 9 - 2 = 7 \\
TAT_{P4} &= 10 - 3 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Waiting Time (WT = TAT − Burst)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= 4 - 4 = 0 \\
WT_{P2} &= 6 - 3 = 3 \\
WT_{P3} &= 7 - 2 = 5 \\
WT_{P4} &= 7 - 1 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Averages**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ATAT &= \frac{4 + 6 + 7 + 7}{4} = \frac{24}{4} = 6 \text{ ms} \\
AWT &= \frac{0 + 3 + 5 + 6}{4} = \frac{14}{4} = 3.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Computation Questions):**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract Arrival Time** from Completion Time is the #1 mistake — students often write $TAT = C_i$ when $A_i \neq 0$. This loses **3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Misidentifying the scheduling order** in FCFS — if the question says "P1, P2, P3, P4", it is **NOT** sorted by burst time; it is the **arrival/order sequence**.
> 3. **Unit omission:** Always write "ms" or "seconds" with the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Process States (Module 1):**

- **Definition:** A process = program in execution. A process state = the current "phase" of a process.
- **5 States:** **N**ew, **R**eady, **R**unning, **B**locked, **T**erminated. Mnemonic: **"N-R-R-B-T"** (Never Run Rusty Bolts Too tight).
- **Extended (7 States):** Add **Suspend-Ready** and **Suspend-Blocked** for virtual memory.
- **PCB** is the identity card of a process — saved/restored during every **context switch**.
- **Legal Transitions:** New→Ready, Ready→Running, Running→Blocked, Blocked→Ready, Running→Ready (preempt), Running→Terminated.
- **Illegal Transition:** Blocked→Running is **forbidden** because it bypasses the scheduler.
- **Schedulers:** Long-term (admission), Short-term (CPU dispatch), Medium-term (swapping).
- **Key Metrics:** $TAT = C - A$, $WT = TAT - \text{Burst}$, $RT = T_{\text{first CPU}} - A$.
- **Convoy Effect:** A long first process in FCFS delays all short processes behind it.
- **Process State Queue** vs **Process Control Block:** The queue **stores** the PCBs of processes in that state.
- **High-Yield Keywords for KTU 2024:** "Draw and explain", "Differentiate", "Why is X transition illegal", "Compute AWT/ATAT".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of an Operating System, a **Data Structure** is a specialized format for organizing, processing, retrieving, and storing data in memory such that it can be accessed and manipulated efficiently by the kernel and user processes. Data structures form the foundational backbone of every major OS subsystem — from the **Process Control Block (PCB)** stored in linked lists, to memory pages maintained in trees, and file directories implemented as hash tables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** The Operating System kernel is essentially a *highly optimized consumer* of data structures. The choice of structure directly determines the algorithmic complexity of core OS operations such as scheduling, allocation, and lookup.

### Common OS-Level Data Structures (KTU-Mandated List)

| S.No | Data Structure | Primary OS Application | Typical Operation |
|------|---------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Array** | PCB table, open file table | O(1) indexing |
| 2 | **Linked List** | Ready queue, wait queue, free list | O(1) insert/delete |
| 3 | **Stack** | Function call frames, interrupt handling | LIFO push/pop |
| 4 | **Queue** | Ready queue (FIFO scheduling), I/O buffers | FIFO enqueue/dequeue |
| 5 | **Tree** | File system hierarchy, memory page tables | O(log n) search |
| 6 | **Hash Table** | Symbol tables, inode lookup, cache mapping | O(1) average search |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Smart Library**
>
> Imagine a massive library (your computer's memory) holding millions of books (data). A naive librarian would pile every book in a giant heap — finding a single title would take hours. A smart librarian, however, uses different organizing systems:
>
> - **Array** = Books on numbered shelves. You know exactly which shelf has which book (instant access by index).
> - **Linked List** = A treasure hunt. Each book tells you where the next one is hidden (chained pointers).
> - **Stack** = A *spring-loaded* plate dispenser. The last plate placed is the first one out (LIFO — Last In, First Out).
> - **Queue** = A ticket counter line. The first person in line is served first (FIFO — First In, First Out).
> - **Tree** = A family genealogy chart. You branch out from ancestors to descendants (hierarchical).
> - **Hash Table** = A library's catalog system. You compute a "magic number" (hash) from the book title and jump directly to the correct shelf (direct addressing).
>
> The **Operating System** is that smart librarian. It picks the *right* data structure to keep the entire system running at peak performance.

## 1.3 Why Data Structures are Critical to OS Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Insight:** A poorly chosen data structure can degrade system throughput from **O(log n)** to **O(n²)** — a catastrophic regression when n (number of processes/pages/files) reaches millions.

- **Memory Efficiency**: Structures like linked lists and trees minimize wasted memory.
- **Speed of Operation**: Schedulers must pick the next process in **microseconds** — a hash table makes this O(1).
- **Concurrency Support**: Some structures (like doubly linked lists) allow safe insertion/deletion at multiple points.
- **Modularity**: Clean structures let the OS swap algorithms (e.g., replace FCFS queue with priority queue) without rewriting kernel code.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Core Data Structure

### 2.1.1 Arrays

- A contiguous block of memory holding elements of the **same type**.
- Accessed via an **index** in **O(1)** time.
- **OS Use Case:** Process Control Block table indexed by PID. The OS computes the PCB address as:
  $$\text{PCB\_Address} = \text{Base} + (\text{PID} \times \text{Size\_of\_PCB})$$
- **Drawback:** Fixed size at allocation; insertion/deletion in the middle is expensive (**O(n)**) due to shifting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** Array indices in C-based kernels (Linux/Unix) start at **0** and end at **n − 1**.

### 2.1.2 Linked Lists

- A linear collection of **nodes**, where each node contains:
  1. **Data** field.
  2. **Pointer** (link) to the next node.
- **Variants:** Singly linked, doubly linked, circular linked.
- **OS Use Case:** 
  - **Singly linked** — free memory block list.
  - **Doubly linked** — ready queue (allows removal of a process from the middle in O(1)).
- **Headaches:** No random access; must traverse from head (O(n) search).

### 2.1.3 Stacks (LIFO)

- Inserts (**push**) and removes (**pop**) from the **same end** — the *top*.
- **OS Use Case:** 
  - Interrupt handling — when an interrupt occurs, the current state is *pushed*; after ISR completes, it is *popped*.
  - System call execution.
  - Recursive kernel functions.
- **Underflow/Overflow:** Critical OS design constraint; stack overflow = kernel panic.

### 2.1.4 Queues (FIFO)

- Inserts at the **rear (enqueue)**, removes from the **front (dequeue)**.
- **OS Use Case:** 
  - **FCFS Scheduler** — processes lined up in arrival order.
  - **I/O request buffer** — disk requests queued.
  - **Spooling** — print queue.
- **Variants:** Priority queue, circular queue, double-ended queue (deque).

### 2.1.5 Trees

- A hierarchical structure with a **root node** and **child subtrees**.
- **OS Use Case:** 
  - **File System Hierarchy** — directories form a tree.
  - **Page Tables** — multi-level page tables are trees.
  - **Process trees** — `init` is root; every new process is a child.
- **Special Trees:** Binary Search Tree (BST), AVL, Red-Black, B-Tree (used heavily in disk-based file systems like NTFS, ext4).

### 2.1.6 Hash Tables

- Uses a **hash function** to map keys into array indices (buckets).
- **Average Lookup:** O(1).
- **Worst-Case Lookup:** O(n) (when all keys collide).
- **OS Use Case:** 
  - **Symbol table** in compilers/linkers.
  - **Inode lookup** in file systems.
  - **Process lookup by PID**.
- **Collision Resolution:** Chaining (linked lists) or Open Addressing (linear/quadratic probing).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it is the single most tested element in KTU Board Exams for Module 1.

| Data Structure | Access | Search | Insertion | Deletion | Worst-Case Space |
|----------------|--------|--------|-----------|----------|------------------|
| **Array** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Linked List** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ at known position | $O(n)$ |
| **Stack** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ push | $O(1)$ pop | $O(n)$ |
| **Queue** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ enqueue | $O(1)$ dequeue | $O(n)$ |
| **Binary Search Tree (Balanced)** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| **Hash Table** | $O(1)$ avg | $O(1)$ avg | $O(1)$ avg | $O(1)$ avg | $O(n)$ |
| **Binary Heap** | $O(1)$ for min/max | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ |

### 2.2.1 Key OS Engineering Equations

1. **Array Address Calculation (Base + Index):**
   $$\text{Address}(A[i]) = \text{Base}(A) + (i \times \text{Size\_of\_Element})$$

2. **Hash Table Index Calculation:**
   $$\text{Index} = h(\text{key}) \mod m$$
   where $m$ is the table size and $h$ is the hash function.

3. **Balanced BST Height (for n nodes):**
   $$h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$$

4. **Hash Load Factor (collision metric):**
   $$\alpha = \frac{n}{m}$$
   where $n$ is the number of keys stored and $m$ is the number of slots. Higher $\alpha$ ⇒ more collisions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Engines (MySQL InnoDB, PostgreSQL):** Use **B+ Trees** for indexing.
- **Linux Kernel:** Uses **Red-Black Trees** for memory management (vm\_area\_struct) and **Linked Lists** for the process list.
- **File Systems (NTFS, ext4, XFS):** Use **B-Trees / Hash Trees** for fast directory lookups.
- **Caching Systems (Redis, Memcached):** Built entirely on **Hash Tables**.
- **Compilers (GCC, Clang):** Maintain **Symbol Tables** as hash maps.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations and Worked Examples (Exhaustive)

### 3.1.1 Derivation: Array Address Calculation

**Problem:** Given an integer array `A[100]` with base address **2000**, and each integer occupies **4 bytes**, find the address of `A[7]`.

**Step 1:** Identify the formula.
$$\text{Address}(A[i]) = \text{Base} + (i \times \text{Size\_of\_Element})$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values.
$$\text{Address}(A[7]) = 2000 + (7 \times 4)$$

**Step 3:** Perform the multiplication.
$$7 \times 4 = 28$$

**Step 4:** Add the base address.
$$2000 + 28 = 2028$$

**Answer:** `A[7]` is stored at memory address **2028**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** If asked for a character array (size = 1 byte), the calculation simplifies to `Base + i`.

### 3.1.2 Derivation: Hash Table Index Calculation

**Problem:** A hash table has $m = 10$ slots. The hash function is $h(\text{key}) = \text{key} \mod m$. Insert keys **25, 37, 42, 18, 55** using the **division method**, and resolve collisions via **chaining**.

**Step 1:** Compute the hash index for each key.

For key 25:
$$h(25) = 25 \mod 10 = 5$$

For key 37:
$$h(37) = 37 \mod 10 = 7$$

For key 42:
$$h(42) = 42 \mod 10 = 2$$

For key 18:
$$h(18) = 18 \mod 10 = 8$$

For key 55:
$$h(55) = 55 \mod 10 = 5$$

**Step 2:** Observe collision — both 25 and 55 hash to index **5**.

**Step 3:** Apply chaining — slot 5 contains a linked list `[25 → 55]`.

**Step 4:** Build the final table.

| Index | Chain (Head → Tail) |
|-------|---------------------|
| 0 | (empty) |
| 1 | (empty) |
| 2 | 42 |
| 3 | (empty) |
| 4 | (empty) |
| 5 | 25 → 55 |
| 6 | (empty) |
| 7 | 37 |
| 8 | 18 |
| 9 | (empty) |

**Step 5:** Compute load factor.
$$\alpha = \frac{n}{m} = \frac{5}{10} = 0.5$$

**Answer:** Final table constructed with 1 collision (slot 5). Load factor = **0.5**.

### 3.1.3 Derivation: Height of a Balanced Binary Tree

**Problem:** How many comparisons (worst case) are required to find an element in a **balanced BST** with $n = 1024$ nodes?

**Step 1:** Apply the height formula for a balanced BST.
$$h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $n = 1024$.
$$h = \lfloor \log_2 1024 \rfloor + 1$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate $\log_2 1024$.
$$\log_2 1024 = 10 \quad \text{(since } 2^{10} = 1024\text{)}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the height.
$$h = 10 + 1 = 11$$

**Answer:** At most **11 comparisons** are needed to find any element — a stark improvement over **1024** linear comparisons.

### 3.1.4 Worked Example: Queue Operation Trace (OS Perspective)

**Problem:** A FCFS scheduler uses a queue. Trace the state of the queue as processes arrive in this order: **P1 (arrives t=0, burst=4), P2 (t=1, burst=3), P3 (t=2, burst=2)**.

**Step 1:** At t=0 — P1 arrives. Enqueue P1.
$$\text{Queue} = [\text{P1}]$$

**Step 2:** At t=1 — P1 finishes (burst=4, so finishes at t=4... wait, P1 starts at t=0, runs for 4 units, finishes at t=4). P2 arrives at t=1.
$$\text{Queue} = [\text{P1}, \text{P2}]$$

**Step 3:** At t=2 — P3 arrives.
$$\text{Queue} = [\text{P1}, \text{P2}, \text{P3}]$$

**Step 4:** At t=4 — P1 completes. Dequeue.
$$\text{Queue} = [\text{P2}, \text{P3}]$$

**Step 5:** Compute average waiting time.
$$\text{Waiting Time of P1} = 0, \quad \text{Waiting Time of P2} = 3, \quad \text{Waiting Time of P3} = 5$$
$$\text{Average} = \frac{0 + 3 + 5}{3} = \frac{8}{3} \approx 2.67 \text{ units}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: When OS Uses What

| OS Subsystem | Data Structure | Justification |
|--------------|---------------|---------------|
| **PCB Storage** | Linked List | Dynamic process count, frequent insertion/deletion |
| **PID Lookup** | Hash Table | O(1) average direct access by PID |
| **Ready Queue** | Queue (FIFO/Priority) | Preserves scheduling order |
| **Memory Free List** | Linked List | Variable-size blocks, frequent merge/split |
| **Directory Tree** | Tree / B-Tree | Hierarchical naming, fast traversal |
| **Page Table** | Multi-level Tree | Sparse address space, memory-efficient |
| **Interrupt Stack** | Stack | LIFO call/return semantics |
| **Symbol Table** | Hash Table | O(1) compiler/linker symbol lookup |
| **Cache Mapping** | Hash Table | Fast key-to-cache-line mapping |
| **Process Tree (parent/child)** | Tree | `fork()` creates child nodes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: OS Data Structure Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Operating System Kernel] --> B[Process Management]
    A --> C[Memory Management]
    A --> D[File System]
    A --> E[I/O and Device Management]

    B --> B1[PCB Table: Array or Hash]
    B --> B2[Ready Queue: Queue]
    B --> B3[Process Tree: Tree]
    B --> B4[Wait Lists: Doubly Linked List]

    C --> C1[Free List: Linked List]
    C --> C2[Page Table: Multi-level Tree]
    C --> C3[Segment Table: Array]

    D --> D1[Directory Tree: B-Tree]
    D --> D2[Inode Lookup: Hash Table]
    D --> D3[Buffer Cache: Hash Table]

    E --> E1[Interrupt Stack: Stack]
    E --> E2[I/O Request Queue: Queue]
    E --> E3[Device Driver List: Linked List]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Linked List Node Structure (Doubly Linked)

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1Prev[NULL] --> N1PrevArrow(( ))
    N1PrevArrow --> N1Data["DATA: PID 101"]
    N1Data --> N1NextArrow(( ))
    N1NextArrow --> N2PrevArrow(( ))
    N2PrevArrow --> N2Data["DATA: PID 102"]
    N2Data --> N2NextArrow(( ))
    N2NextArrow --> N3PrevArrow(( ))
    N3PrevArrow --> N3Data["DATA: PID 103"]
    N3Data --> N3Next[NULL]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Hash Table with Chaining (Visual)

```mermaid
graph TD
    H[Hash Function h key mod 10] --> Slot0[Slot 0: Empty]
    H --> Slot1[Slot 1: Empty]
    H --> Slot2[Slot 2: Node 42]
    H --> Slot3[Slot 3: Empty]
    H --> Slot4[Slot 4: Empty]
    H --> Slot5[Slot 5: Node 25 next Node 55]
    H --> Slot6[Slot 6: Empty]
    H --> Slot7[Slot 7: Node 37]
    H --> Slot8[Slot 8: Node 18]
    H --> Slot9[Slot 9: Empty]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Process Scheduling Queue Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    Arrival[Process Arrival] --> NewState[NEW State]
    NewState --> ReadyQ[READY QUEUE: FIFO]
    ReadyQ --> CPU[CPU: Dispatcher Picks Head]
    CPU --> RunningState[RUNNING State]
    RunningState -->|I/O Request| WaitQ[WAIT QUEUE: Linked List]
    RunningState -->|Burst Complete| ReadyQ
    WaitQ -->|I/O Complete| ReadyQ
    RunningState -->|Exit| Terminated[TERMINATED]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: File System Tree (ext4 Style)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Root Directory /] --> Home[/home]
    Root --> Bin[/bin]
    Root --> Etc[/etc]
    Root --> Usr[/usr]
    Home --> User1[/alice]
    Home --> User2[/bob]
    User1 --> Doc1[notes.txt]
    User1 --> Doc2[project.c]
    User2 --> Pic1[photo.png]
    Bin --> B1[bash]
    Bin --> B2[ls]
    Etc --> E1[passwd]
    Etc --> E2[hostname]
    Usr --> Lib[/lib]
    Lib --> L1[libc.so]
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram: Stack Operation During Interrupt

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Push Current PC and Registers] --> S2[Step 2: Push Old Stack Pointer]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Switch to Kernel Stack]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Execute ISR]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Pop Saved State]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Return to User Process]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Set B]**
**Q: List any three applications of linked lists in the design of an Operating System.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Process Scheduling Queues:** A doubly linked list is used to maintain the ready queue, allowing a process to be inserted or removed from any position in O(1) time.
2. **Free Memory Block List:** The OS tracks unused memory blocks using a linked list, supporting variable-sized allocation and deallocation.
3. **Device Driver List / Wait Queues:** A linked list of processes waiting for a specific I/O device or resource enables dynamic addition and removal.

*(Any other valid OS-specific use such as the inode list, file allocation table, or process tree earns full marks.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**Q: What is a hash table? Why is it preferred over a binary search tree for symbol table lookup in an OS?**

**Model Answer:**
