- **Definition:** A hash table is a data structure that maps keys to values using a **hash function**, providing average-case **O(1)** lookup, insertion, and deletion.
- **Why preferred over BST:**
  1. Hash table offers **O(1) average** access, while BST offers **O(log n)**.
  2. Symbol tables experience **high-frequency lookups** (e.g., linker, dynamic loader). The constant-time advantage compounds significantly.
  3. Symbol table keys are not naturally ordered, so BST's ordering property provides no benefit.
  4. Hash table implementation is **simpler** in terms of memory layout (array of buckets) compared to BST's pointer-heavy structure.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Actual Pattern, Adapted]**

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the role of **queues** in the implementation of process scheduling in an Operating System. Discuss the **FCFS** and **Round Robin** scheduling algorithms and illustrate the operation with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **hash table**? Explain the **division method** of hashing with an example. How are collisions resolved using **chaining**? Demonstrate with a trace. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a): Queues in Process Scheduling

**Step 1 — Introduction:**
A queue is a **FIFO (First In, First Out)** data structure. In an OS, the **ready queue** holds all processes in the *Ready* state, waiting for the CPU. The dispatcher removes the process at the **head** and allocates the CPU.

**Step 2 — FCFS (First Come First Served):**
- Non-preemptive.
- Processes are executed in order of arrival.
- Implemented simply as a FIFO queue.
- **Disadvantage:** *Convoy effect* — short processes wait behind long ones.

**Worked Example for FCFS:**

| Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time |
|---------|--------------|------------|
| P1 | 0 | 4 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 |
| P3 | 2 | 2 |

**Gantt Chart:**
```
| P1 (0-4) | P2 (4-7) | P3 (7-9) |
```

**Waiting Times:**
- P1: 0
- P2: 4 − 1 = 3
- P3: 7 − 2 = 5

**Average Waiting Time:**
$$\text{AWT} = \frac{0 + 3 + 5}{3} = 2.67 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Round Robin (RR):**
- Preemptive variant of FCFS.
- Each process gets a fixed **time quantum** (e.g., 2 ms).
- Implemented as a **circular queue** — the tail connects back to the head.
- After quantum expiry, the process is moved to the **tail** of the queue.

**Step 4 — Advantages of Queue-Based Scheduling:**
1. Simple, fair ordering.
2. Easy to insert new arrivals at the rear.
3. Dispatcher removal is O(1) from the head.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b): Hash Table

**Step 1 — Definition:**
A hash table is a data structure that stores key-value pairs and uses a **hash function** $h(\text{key})$ to compute an index into an array of buckets. Average case lookup is **O(1)**.

**Step 2 — Division Method:**
$$h(\text{key}) = \text{key} \mod m$$
where $m$ is the table size (typically a prime number to reduce clustering).

**Step 3 — Example Trace:**
Let $m = 7$ and keys = {25, 38, 49, 17, 31}.

| Key | $h(\text{key}) = \text{key} \mod 7$ | Index |
|-----|--------------------------------------|-------|
| 25 | $25 \mod 7 = 4$ | 4 |
| 38 | $38 \mod 7 = 3$ | 3 |
| 49 | $49 \mod 7 = 0$ | 0 |
| 17 | $17 \mod 7 = 3$ | 3 (**collision with 38**) |
| 31 | $31 \mod 7 = 3$ | 3 (**collision with 38, 17**) |

**Step 4 — Collision Resolution by Chaining:**
Each bucket holds a **linked list**. Slot 3 becomes: `[38 → 17 → 31]`.

| Index | Chain |
|-------|-------|
| 0 | 49 |
| 1 | (empty) |
| 2 | (empty) |
| 3 | 38 → 17 → 31 |
| 4 | 25 |
| 5 | (empty) |
| 6 | (empty) |

**Step 5 — Load Factor:**
$$\alpha = \frac{n}{m} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.714$$

**Step 6 — OS Relevance:**
Hash tables are used in the kernel for **PID lookup**, **inode mapping**, and **page cache** because of their O(1) access pattern.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Predicted Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Explain **arrays** and **linked lists** as data structures. Compare their merits and demerits. Discuss where each is used in the Operating System. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **tree**? Describe **binary search trees** and **B-trees**. How does the file system use trees to organize directories? Explain with a diagram. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a): Arrays vs. Linked Lists

**Step 1 — Array Definition:**
A contiguous memory block storing elements of identical type, accessed via an index. Time complexity: **O(1)** random access.

**Step 2 — Linked List Definition:**
A linear collection of nodes, each containing data and a pointer to the next (and possibly previous) node. Time complexity: **O(n)** access, **O(1)** insertion/deletion at known position.

**Step 3 — Comparative Table:**

| Property | Array | Linked List |
|----------|-------|-------------|
| Memory | Contiguous, fixed | Scattered, dynamic |
| Access | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Insertion (middle) | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Deletion (middle) | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Memory Overhead | None | Extra pointer per node |
| Cache Friendliness | Excellent | Poor |
| Resize | Costly | Easy |

**Step 4 — OS Use Cases:**
- **Array:** PCB table indexed by PID, segment table, fixed-size device register file.
- **Linked List:** Ready queue, wait queue, free memory list, LRU cache list, device driver list.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
For fixed-size, lookup-intensive tasks, the OS uses arrays. For dynamic, frequently-mutated collections, the OS uses linked lists.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b): Trees in File Systems

**Step 1 — Definition of a Tree:**
A hierarchical, non-linear data structure with a **root node** and zero or more **subtrees**. Each node has one parent (except root) and zero or more children.

**Step 2 — Binary Search Tree (BST):**
- Each node has at most 2 children: **left** and **right**.
- Property: Left subtree keys < node key < right subtree keys.
- Search/Insert/Delete: **O(log n)** in balanced BST.

**Step 3 — B-Tree (Generalization):**
- A self-balancing search tree with nodes holding **multiple keys** and **multiple children**.
- Used heavily in **disk-based file systems** and **databases** because:
  1. Disk access is expensive; one B-Tree node can match many keys at once.
  2. Height remains low even with millions of entries.
- A B-Tree of order $m$ has height:
  $$h \approx \log_m n$$
  making it ideal for tertiary storage.

**Step 4 — File System Tree Structure:**
The OS organizes directories in a tree where:
- **Root** is `/` (Linux/Unix) or `C:\` (Windows).
- **Internal nodes** are directories.
- **Leaves** are files.
- Pathnames encode the tree path (e.g., `/home/alice/notes.txt`).

**Step 5 — Why B-Tree Specifically?**
- In **NTFS** and **ext4**, directories are stored as B-Trees for fast `O(log n)` lookup of filenames.
- For a million files, linear search would require 1,000,000 comparisons; a B-Tree needs only ~20.

**Step 6 — Diagram Reference (Already shown in Section 4.5 above).**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing Stack with Queue in Interrupt Handling:** Students often swap LIFO/FIFO. **Remember:** Interrupts use a **Stack** (last triggered ISR finishes first), but the **I/O request queue** is a **Queue** (FIFO).
> 2. **Hash Function vs. Hash Table:** The hash *function* is just the formula $h(\text{key})$; the hash *table* is the entire data structure including the array and collision resolution.
> 3. **Linked List Traversal vs. Array Indexing:** Don't claim linked lists give O(1) access. That is the exclusive property of arrays.
> 4. **Average vs. Worst Case for Hash Tables:** Always write "**O(1) average**" and "**O(n) worst case**" — not just O(1).
> 5. **BST vs. Balanced BST:** Insertions in an unbalanced BST can degrade to O(n). Always mention "balanced" for O(log n) guarantees.
> 6. **Forgetting to Draw Gantt Charts in Scheduling Questions:** A textual description without a Gantt chart loses 2-3 marks. Always include one.
> 7. **Array Address Formula Mistakes:** Multiplying by the wrong element size is the most common numerical error. Re-verify the size of `int` (4 bytes) vs `char` (1 byte) vs `double` (8 bytes).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam**

- [x] **Array** = contiguous, O(1) access, used for **PCB tables**.
- [x] **Linked List** = dynamic, O(1) insert/delete, used for **ready/wait queues** and **free lists**.
- [x] **Stack** = LIFO, used for **interrupt handling** and **function call frames**.
- [x] **Queue** = FIFO, used for **FCFS scheduling** and **I/O buffers**.
- [x] **Tree** = hierarchical, used for **file systems** and **page tables**.
- [x] **B-Tree** = disk-friendly tree, used in **NTFS, ext4, databases**.
- [x] **Hash Table** = O(1) average lookup, used for **PID lookup, inode tables, symbol tables**.
- [x] **Hash Function (Division Method):** $h(\text{key}) = \text{key} \mod m$.
- [x] **Collision Resolution:** Chaining (linked list per bucket) **or** Open Addressing (probing).
- [x] **Load Factor:** $\alpha = n/m$; higher $\alpha$ = more collisions.
- [x] **Array Address:** $\text{Base} + (i \times \text{Size})$.
- [x] **Balanced BST Height:** $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$.
- [x] **OS Subsystems ↔ Data Structure mapping** must be memorized verbatim.
- [x] **Linux kernel uses Red-Black Trees** for memory regions (vm\_area\_struct).
- [x] **Doubly linked lists** are preferred over singly linked for queues requiring O(1) middle-deletion.

> **End of Module 1 — Data Structures Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process API
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process API

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process API (Application Programming Interface)** refers to the standardized set of **system calls** provided by the operating system kernel that allows user-level programs and applications to create, control, execute, manage, and terminate processes. In the context of the KTU 2024 Operating Systems syllabus (Module 1: Introduction & Processes), the Process API forms the **interface boundary** between user space applications and the kernel's process management subsystem.

The Process API is implemented primarily in the `unistd.h` and `sys/wait.h` header files in POSIX-compliant systems (Linux/Unix). The core functions include:

- **`fork()`** — Creates a new process by duplicating the calling process.
- **`exec()` family (`execl`, `execv`, `execlp`, `execvp`, `execle`, `execve`)** — Replaces the current process image with a new program.
- **`wait()` / `waitpid()`** — Causes the parent process to pause execution until a child state changes (typically termination).
- **`exit()`** — Terminates the calling process and flushes standard I/O buffers.
- **`_exit()`** — Terminates the calling process immediately without buffer flushing (kernel-level termination).
- **`getpid()`** — Returns the Process ID (PID) of the calling process.
- **`getppid()`** — Returns the Parent Process ID (PPID) of the calling process.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Government Office Workflow**
>
> Imagine a **Government Office** where a senior officer (parent process) receives a new file. To handle it efficiently, the officer **clones** the entire desk — same files, same setup, same working knowledge — and gives the clone a junior officer (child process) to work on the task. Both officers initially have identical work but operate independently. The senior officer can either wait for the junior to finish (`wait()`) or continue with other work. If the junior is given a completely new set of instructions (`exec()`), they essentially "become" a different specialist, forgetting the old task and starting fresh.
>
> - **`fork()`** = Duplicating the desk
> - **`exec()`** = Replacing the work material with new instructions
> - **`wait()`** = Supervisor waiting for the report
> - **`exit()`** = Submitting the final report and closing the file

This analogy makes the *Process API* click: it is the **kit of tools** the OS gives programs to spawn, transform, and clean up their work units (processes).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Identifiers

> [!TIP]
> - **PID (Process Identifier):** A unique positive integer (**32-bit on Linux**, range typically **2 to 32768** by default, configurable up to **4,194,304** in `/proc/sys/kernel/pid_max`).
> - **PPID (Parent Process Identifier):** The PID of the creator process.
> - **Init Process (PID 1):** The ancestor of all user processes in Linux; adopts orphaned processes.
> - **TASK_RUNNING, TASK_STOPPED, TASK_TRACED:** Standard Linux process states.
> - **Return value semantics of `fork()`:** **0** in the child, **child's PID (> 0)** in the parent, **-1** on error.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Core Process API Calls

### 2.1.1 `fork()` — Process Creation Primitive

- **Header:** `#include <unistd.h>`
- **Prototype:** `pid_t fork(void);`
- **Return Type:** `pid_t` (signed integer type for PIDs)
- **Call Count Behavior:** Called **once**, returns **twice** (this is the unique property).
- **Memory Behavior:** Uses **Copy-on-Write (CoW)** optimization — physical pages are not duplicated immediately; they are shared until one process attempts to modify them.
- **Why it exists:** Allows concurrent program execution, enables parallelism, and is foundational to shells, daemons, and process hierarchies.

**Return Value Logic:**
- **In Child Process:** `fork()` returns `0`.
- **In Parent Process:** `fork()` returns the **PID of the newly created child** (which is guaranteed to be a positive integer).
- **On Error:** Returns `-1` (e.g., when the system process table is full or the RLIMIT_NPROC resource limit is exceeded).

### 2.1.2 `exec()` Family — Program Image Replacement

- **Headers:** `#include <unistd.h>`
- **Behavior:** Replaces the **entire process image** (code, data, heap, stack) with a new program. The PID remains the same; the process identity is preserved.
- **Variants:**
  - `execl(path, arg0, arg1, ..., NULL)` — list of arguments
  - `execv(path, argv[])` — vector (array) of arguments
  - `execlp(file, arg0, ..., NULL)` — searches PATH
  - `execvp(file, argv[])` — vector + PATH search
  - `execle(path, arg0, ..., NULL, envp[])` — list + custom environment
  - `execve(path, argv[], envp[])` — vector + custom environment (**the actual kernel syscall**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Only `execve()` is a true system call. All other `exec*()` variants are **C library wrappers** that construct argument lists and invoke `execve()`.

### 2.1.3 `wait()` and `waitpid()` — Synchronization Primitives

- **Header:** `#include <sys/wait.h>`
- **Prototype:**
  - `pid_t wait(int *status);`
  - `pid_t waitpid(pid_t pid, int *status, int options);`
- **Behavior:** Suspends the calling (parent) process until one of its children terminates (or stops, if `WUNTRACED` is set).
- **`status` argument:** A pointer to an integer where the kernel stores the **exit status** of the child.
- **Macros to interpret `status`:**
  - `WIFEXITED(status)` — True if child terminated normally via `exit()`.
  - `WEXITSTATUS(status)` — Returns the low-order 8 bits of the exit code.
  - `WIFSIGNALED(status)` — True if child terminated due to an uncaught signal.
  - `WTERMSIG(status)` — Returns the signal number that caused termination.

### 2.1.4 `exit()` vs `_exit()`

| Function | Header | Behavior | Buffer Flushing | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `exit(status)` | `<stdlib.h>` | Library function, calls atexit handlers, flushes stdio buffers, then calls `_exit()` | **Yes** | Normal user program termination |
| `_exit(status)` | `<unistd.h>` | Direct kernel system call termination | **No** | Used after `fork()` in child to prevent buffer duplication |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Using `exit()` after `fork()` in the child can cause **double-flushing** of buffered output (since both parent and child inherit the same buffer). Always use `_exit()` immediately after `fork()` in the child if no further I/O is required.

## 2.2 Process States & Lifecycle

> [!NOTE]
> A process under the Process API moves through a well-defined state machine:

1. **NEW** — Process is being created (after `fork()`).
2. **READY** — Process is loaded in memory, waiting for CPU allocation.
3. **RUNNING** — Process instructions are being executed by the CPU.
4. **BLOCKED / WAITING** — Process is waiting for some event (I/O completion, signal, child termination).
5. **TERMINATED / EXIT** — Process has finished execution (`exit()` or `_exit()` called).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Reference Table for Exam Day**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Boundary / Limit | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| `fork()` return in child | `0` | Always 0 on success | Integer |
| `fork()` return in parent | `child_PID > 0` | ≥ 1 | Integer (PID) |
| `fork()` error | `-1` | `errno` set (`EAGAIN`, `ENOMEM`) | Integer |
| PID range (Linux default) | `2` to `pid_max - 1` | `pid_max = 32768` (default) | Integer |
| Max processes per user | `RLIMIT_NPROC` | Usually `64` to `unlimited` | Integer |
| Exit status encoding | `status = (exit_code << 8) & 0xFF00` | exit code in low 8 bits | Bits |
| Process count after `fork()` | `N → 2N` (exponential if chained) | $2^k$ after $k$ `fork()` calls | Count |
| Zombie's resource footprint | Just a slot in the process table | Exists until parent calls `wait()` | ~1 KB |
| Orphan re-parenting | PPID reassigned to **PID 1** (init/systemd) | Always true on Linux | PID |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Shells (bash, zsh):** Every command you type causes the shell to `fork()` and the child to `exec()` the command, then `wait()` for completion.
- **Web Servers (Apache, Nginx):** `fork()` (or `clone()`) to handle each incoming connection concurrently.
- **Container Runtimes (Docker):** Use `clone()` with namespace flags — an extended form of `fork()`.
- **Process Supervisors (systemd, init):** Continuously call `wait()` to reap zombie processes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Process Count After Chained `fork()` Calls

This is a classic KTU numerical/analytical question pattern. Let us derive the process count.

**Problem Setup:** Consider a program that calls `fork()` exactly $k$ times in sequence (i.e., not inside any conditional branch). How many distinct processes exist at the end?

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $P_i$ denote the number of processes in existence just **after** the $i$-th `fork()` call, and $P_0 = 1$ (the original parent process alone).

**Base Case (i = 0):**
$$P_0 = 1$$

**Inductive Step:** Each existing process, upon reaching the $i$-th `fork()` call, spawns exactly **one new child**. So the number of new processes added equals $P_{i-1}$:
$$P_i = P_{i-1} + P_{i-1} = 2 \cdot P_{i-1}$$

**Solving the Recurrence:**
$$P_1 = 2 \cdot P_0 = 2 \cdot 1 = 2$$
$$P_2 = 2 \cdot P_1 = 2 \cdot 2 = 4$$
$$P_3 = 2 \cdot P_2 = 2 \cdot 4 = 8$$

By induction, the closed-form solution is:
$$P_k = 2^k$$

### 3.1.2 Closed-Form Proof

We can verify by induction. Assume $P_{i-1} = 2^{i-1}$. Then:
$$P_i = 2 \cdot P_{i-1} = 2 \cdot 2^{i-1} = 2^i \quad \blacksquare$$

**Example (KTU-favorite question):** If `fork()` is called 3 times, total processes = $2^3 = 8$ processes. Out of these 8, **1 is the original ancestor**, and **7 are descendants**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Number of `printf` Statements After `fork()`

A frequently asked KTU question: *"How many times will `printf` execute after this fork pattern?"*

**Example Code Pattern:**
```c
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    fork();
    printf("OS Lab\n");
}
```

### 3.2.1 Derivation

After the loop completes, the total number of processes is $2^n$. The `printf` statement is executed:
- **In the original process:** once per iteration = $n$ times
- **In each subsequent process:** each inherits the execution state and continues the loop

By the end of the loop, the **total number of `printf` calls executed across all processes** is:
$$T(n) = n \cdot 2^{n-1}$$

**Reasoning:** In the $i$-th iteration (0-indexed), there are $2^i$ processes existing, each executing `printf` once. So:
$$T = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 2^i = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 2^i = 2^n - 1$$

**For example,** if $n = 3$:
$$T = 2^3 - 1 = 7 \text{ printf calls}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Watch out!** This is a common confusion point. The formula is $2^n - 1$, **not** $n \cdot 2^n$. Each `printf` is shared by all running processes at the moment of execution, leading to geometric growth, not linear-times-exponential.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Parent-Child Differentiation

The Process API allows the same code to behave differently in parent vs. child using the return value of `fork()`. This is the foundation of the **Standard Fork Pattern**:

$$
\text{Action} = \begin{cases} \text{Child code path} & \text{if } \text{fork()} = 0 \\ \text{Parent code path} & \text{if } \text{fork()} > 0 \\ \text{Error handling} & \text{if } \text{fork()} = -1 \end{cases}
$$

### 3.3.1 Worked Example: `fork()` + `exec()` + `wait()` Triad

**Question:** Explain the sequence of system calls when a user types `ls -l` in a terminal.

**Solution Steps:**

1. **Shell parses** the input, splits into tokens: `["ls", "-l"]`.
2. **Shell calls `fork()`**:
   - Child receives `fork() == 0`.
   - Parent receives `fork() == child_PID > 0`.
3. **Child calls `execvp("ls", argv)`**:
   - The child's memory image is replaced with the `ls` program.
   - The PID remains the same.
4. **Parent calls `wait(&status)`**:
   - The parent (shell) blocks until the child (ls) terminates.
5. **Child finishes, calls `exit(0)`**:
   - The kernel frees user-space resources but keeps the process table entry.
6. **`wait()` returns in parent**:
   - Parent regains control, prints the next prompt.

## 3.4 Derivation: Zombie Process Behavior

A **zombie process** is a terminated process whose parent has not yet called `wait()`.

**Resource Footprint Derivation:** The kernel retains only:
- **PID**
- **Termination status**
- **Resource usage information** (for `getrusage()`)

Memory usage: approximately **1 KB** (just the process descriptor entry, `task_struct` in Linux).

**Reaping:** Only `wait()`, `waitpid()`, or termination of the parent (which causes init to inherit and reap) eliminates the zombie.

**Mathematical implication:** If a parent creates $N$ children per second and never calls `wait()`, zombie count grows linearly:
$$Z(t) = N \cdot t$$
where $Z(t)$ is the number of zombies at time $t$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : fork() called
    NEW --> READY : admitted to ready queue
    READY --> RUNNING : scheduler dispatches
    RUNNING --> READY : time slice expires
    RUNNING --> BLOCKED : I/O or wait() syscall
    BLOCKED --> READY : I/O complete or child exits
    RUNNING --> TERMINATED : exit() or _exit()
    TERMINATED --> [*] : parent calls wait() and reaps
```

## 4.2 fork() + exec() + wait() Architectural Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User types command in shell] --> B[Shell process parses input]
    B --> C[Shell calls fork system call]
    C --> D{Fork returns}
    D -->|Return 0 in child| E[Child process path]
    D -->|Return child PID in parent| F[Parent shell process]
    E --> G[Child calls exec to load new program]
    G --> H[New program image loaded into same PID]
    H --> I[Child executes new program]
    I --> J[Program finishes, calls exit]
    J --> K[Kernel marks child as zombie]
    F --> L[Parent calls wait and blocks]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Kernel reaps zombie, wait returns]
    M --> N[Parent shell resumes, prints new prompt]
    N --> A
```

## 4.3 Parent-Child Process Tree After Multiple Forks

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Original Parent PID 100] --> C1[Child PID 101]
    P0 --> C2[Child PID 102]
    P0 --> C3[Child PID 103]
    C1 --> GC1[Grandchild PID 104]
    C1 --> GC2[Grandchild PID 105]
    C2 --> GC3[Grandchild PID 106]
    C3 --> GC4[Grandchild PID 107]
    C3 --> GC5[Grandchild PID 108]
    C3 --> GC6[Grandchild PID 109]
    style P0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style C2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style C3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style GC1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style GC2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style GC3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style GC4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style GC5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
    style GC6 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 Zombie vs. Orphan Process Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Z[ZOMBIE PROCESS]
        Z1[Process exits via exit] --> Z2[Process table entry remains]
        Z2 --> Z3[Parent has NOT called wait]
        Z3 --> Z4[Consumes PID slot, no memory]
    end
    subgraph O[ORPHAN PROCESS]
        O1[Parent process exits first] --> O2[Child still running]
        O2 --> O3[PPID reassigned to PID 1 init]
        O3 --> O4[Init will eventually wait and clean up]
    end
    style Z1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style O1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
```

## 4.5 Process API Function Call Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Process API Layer] --> B[Process Creation]
    A --> C[Process Execution]
    A --> D[Process Synchronization]
    A --> E[Process Termination]
    B --> B1[fork system call]
    B --> B2[clone system call]
    C --> C1[execve kernel syscall]
    C --> C2[exec family wrappers]
    C2 --> C2a[execl]
    C2 --> C2b[execv]
    C2 --> C2c[execlp]
    C2 --> C2d[execvp]
    D --> D1[wait function]
    D --> D2[waitpid function]
    E --> E1[exit library function]
    E --> E2[_exit kernel syscall]
    A --> F[Process Identification]
    F --> F1[getpid]
    F --> F2[getppid]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the `fork()` system call. Explain the return values of `fork()` in both the parent and child processes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `fork()` is a POSIX system call defined in `unistd.h` that creates a new process by duplicating the calling process. (1 mark)
- The new process is called the **child process**, and the original is the **parent process**. (0.5 marks)
- **Return values:** (1.5 marks)
  - In the **child process**: returns `0`.
  - In the **parent process**: returns the **PID** of the newly created child (a positive integer).
  - On **error**: returns `-1` and sets `errno` (e.g., `EAGAIN` if process limit reached).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between `exit()` and `_exit()` system calls.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `exit()` | `_exit()` |
|---|---|---|
| Header file | `<stdlib.h>` | `<unistd.h>` |
| Type | Library function | System call |
| Buffer flushing | Flushes stdio buffers | Does not flush buffers |
| `atexit` handlers | Invokes registered handlers | Skips handlers |
| Use case | Normal program termination | Immediate kernel-level termination, often after `fork()` in child |

`exit()` is essentially a wrapper that performs cleanup (flushes buffers, runs atexit handlers) and then calls `_exit()` to actually terminate the process at the kernel level. **(3 marks for a complete answer with example use case.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the `fork()` system call. Discuss the various states a process undergoes during its lifetime. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of `fork()`:**

When a process invokes `fork()`, the kernel performs the following steps:

1. **Allocates a new process descriptor** (`task_struct` in Linux) in the kernel's process table.
2. **Assigns a unique PID** to the new process.
3. **Duplicates the parent's address space** using **Copy-on-Write (CoW)** — the child initially shares physical pages with the parent; copies are made only when either process modifies a page.
4. **Copies file descriptors, signal handlers, and environment** from parent to child.
5. **Schedules the child** for execution.
6. **Returns twice**: `0` in the child, child's PID in the parent.

**Diagram (text representation):**
```
Parent Process (PID 100)
        |
        | fork()
        v
   +--------+--------+
   |                 |
Parent (100)    Child (101)
Returns 101     Returns 0
```

**Process States:**

A process transitions through the following states during its lifetime:
- **NEW:** Process is being created.
- **READY:** Process is waiting in the ready queue for CPU allocation.
- **RUNNING:** Process is currently executing on the CPU.
- **BLOCKED:** Process is waiting for I/O, signal, or `wait()` completion.
- **TERMINATED:** Process has finished execution.

Transitions occur due to scheduler decisions, I/O requests, and exit calls. **(7 marks complete with state diagram and fork explanation.)**

---

**(b)** What is a zombie process? Explain how it is created and how it can be eliminated. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A **zombie process** (also called a defunct process) is a process that has completed execution (`exit()` has been called) but still has an entry in the process table because its parent has not yet read the exit status via `wait()` or `waitpid()`.

**How it is created (2 marks):**
1. A parent process calls `fork()` to create a child.
2. The child terminates (e.g., by calling `exit(0)`).
3. The kernel keeps the child's process table entry to store the exit status.
4. The parent, however, does not call `wait()` to retrieve this status.
5. The child process now exists as a zombie — it has released all memory and resources, but its PID and exit status remain in the process table.

**How it is eliminated (3 marks):**
1. The parent calls `wait()` or `waitpid()` — the kernel reads the exit status, removes the process table entry, and the zombie is reaped.
2. If the parent itself terminates, the orphaned zombie is adopted by **init (PID 1)**, which periodically calls `wait()` to clean up all zombies.
3. Sending `SIGCHLD` to the parent can also prompt it to reap zombies if the signal handler is set up correctly.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students mistakenly say that a zombie "uses a lot of memory." In reality, a zombie consumes only the **process table entry** (~1 KB), not user memory. Also, remember that `kill -9` **cannot** kill a zombie — it is already dead. The only cure is the parent calling `wait()`.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the `exec()` family of system calls with suitable examples. How does `exec()` differ from `fork()`? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**The `exec()` Family (5 marks):**

The `exec()` family of functions replaces the current process image with a new program. After `exec()`, the process retains its **PID** but executes an entirely different program. The variants are:

| Function | Argument Style | Path/PATH | Environment |
|---|---|---|---|
| `execl` | List (`arg0, arg1, ..., NULL`) | Full path required | Inherited |
| `execv` | Vector (`char *argv[]`) | Full path required | Inherited |
| `execlp` | List | Searches **PATH** | Inherited |
| `execvp` | Vector | Searches **PATH** | Inherited |
| `execle` | List | Full path | Custom envp |
| `execve` | Vector | Full path | Custom envp (**actual syscall**) |

**Example:**
```c
#include <unistd.h>
int main() {
    char *args[] = {"ls", "-l", NULL};
    execvp("ls", args);  // Replaces current process with "ls -l"
    return 0;  // This line never executes if exec succeeds
}
```

**Difference between `exec()` and `fork()` (2 marks):**

| Aspect | `fork()` | `exec()` |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Creates a **new process** | Replaces the **current process image** |
| Process count | Increases by 1 | Stays the same |
| PID | New child gets a new PID | PID is unchanged |
| Memory | Duplicates address space | Replaces address space entirely |
| Return value | Returns twice | Returns only on **error** (-1) |

---

**(b)** Write a C program using the Process API to create a child process that executes the `ls -l` command, while the parent waits for the child to complete. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/wait.h>

int main() {
    pid_t pid;
    int status;

    // Step 1: Create child process
    pid = fork();

    if (pid < 0) {
        // Error in fork
        fprintf(stderr, "Fork Failed");
        return 1;
    }
    else if (pid == 0) {
        // Child process
        printf("Child: I am the child process. My PID = %d\n", getpid());
        printf("Child: Executing 'ls -l'...\n");
        execlp("ls", "ls", "-l", NULL);

        // execlp only returns on error
        fprintf(stderr, "execlp failed\n");
        exit(1);
    }
    else {
        // Parent process
        printf("Parent: I am the parent. My PID = %d, Child PID = %d\n",
               getpid(), pid);
        printf("Parent: Waiting for child to complete...\n");

        // Step 2: Wait for child
        wait(&status);

        if (WIFEXITED(status)) {
            printf("Parent: Child exited with status %d\n",
                   WEXITSTATUS(status));
        }
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of the code (for full marks):**
- `fork()` creates a new process; return value differentiates parent from child. **(2 marks)**
- `execlp()` in the child replaces the child process image with `ls -l`. **(2 marks)**
- `wait(&status)` in the parent blocks until the child terminates. **(2 marks)**
- `WIFEXITED` and `WEXITSTATUS` macros interpret the child's exit status. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Valuation Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Forgetting `#include <sys/wait.h>`** — causes compilation errors.
> 2. **Not checking the return value of `fork()`** — leads to undefined behavior if `fork()` fails.
> 3. **Forgetting `NULL` terminator in `execlp` argument list** — causes the exec call to fail or pass garbage arguments.
> 4. **Using `exit()` instead of `_exit()` after `fork()` in child** — can cause double output flushing.
> 5. **Not using `wait()` in the parent** — leads to zombie process creation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Process API** is the bridge between user programs and the kernel's process management subsystem.
- **`fork()`** creates a new process; called once, returns twice (0 in child, child PID in parent, -1 on error).
- **Memory after `fork()`** uses **Copy-on-Write (CoW)** for efficiency.
- **`exec()` family** replaces the process image but **preserves the PID**; only `execve()` is the true kernel syscall.
- **`wait()` / `waitpid()`** is essential to reap child processes and prevent **zombie accumulation**.
- **`exit()` (stdlib)** vs **`_exit()` (unistd)**: `exit()` flushes buffers and runs atexit handlers; `_exit()` is a direct kernel termination.
- **Zombie process**: terminated but not reaped by parent; only takes ~1 KB (process table entry).
- **Orphan process**: parent terminated before child; adopted by **PID 1 (init/systemd)** on Linux.
- **Process count after `n` sequential `fork()` calls**: $2^n$ processes.
- **`printf` after `n` sequential `fork()` calls in a loop**: $2^n - 1$ total prints (not $n \cdot 2^n$).
- **`getpid()` and `getppid()`** are used to identify the process and its parent.
- **Init process (PID 1)** is the ancestor of all user processes and the ultimate reaper of orphans and zombies.
- **Real-world use**: Shells, web servers, container runtimes (Docker uses `clone()`, an extended `fork`).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sharing Processor Among Processes - User and Kernel Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sharing Processor Among Processes - User and Kernel Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In modern multiprogramming Operating Systems, a single physical **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** must execute multiple concurrent processes. **Sharing the processor among processes** is the mechanism by which the OS multiplexes the CPU's execution time across several ready processes, giving the illusion of simultaneous execution. This is achieved through **CPU scheduling**, **context switching**, and the critical concept of **Dual-Mode Operation** — the hardware-enforced separation between **User Mode** and **Kernel Mode** governed by a special **Mode Bit** in the processor's Program Status Word (PSW) or Control Register.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dual-Mode Operation** is the foundational protection mechanism of any modern OS. The CPU hardware supports at least two distinct execution states: **User Mode (Mode Bit = 1)** where application code runs with restricted privileges, and **Kernel Mode (Mode Bit = 0)** where the OS kernel executes with unrestricted access to all hardware and memory.

### Formal Definitions Aligned with KTU 2024 Syllabus

| Term | KTU 2024 Definition |
|---|---|
| **Processor Sharing** | The technique of multiplexing a single CPU among multiple processes through rapid context switching, often called **time-sharing**. |
| **User Mode** | A restricted execution mode in which user applications execute. Code in user mode cannot directly execute I/O instructions, access kernel memory, or modify control registers. |
| **Kernel Mode** | A privileged execution mode (also called **Supervisor Mode** or **System Mode**) in which the OS kernel executes. It has complete control over all hardware and system resources. |
| **Mode Bit** | A single bit in the processor's status register that indicates the current execution mode. 0 = Kernel Mode, 1 = User Mode (on most architectures). |
| **Privileged Instructions** | A subset of CPU instructions that are executable only in kernel mode (e.g., `I/O instructions`, `STI/CLI`, `HLT`, `LTR`, `MOV to CR0`). |
| **System Call** | The controlled, well-defined interface through which user processes request kernel services, triggering a controlled switch to kernel mode. |
| **Protection Ring** | The hierarchical privilege levels (typically 4 rings, with Ring 0 = kernel and Ring 3 = user on x86) forming concentric circles of decreasing privilege. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Hotel Manager and the Guests**
>
> Imagine a grand hotel (the **Computer System**) with one chef's kitchen (the **CPU**). Hundreds of guests (the **Processes**) are seated in the dining hall, and each guest has ordered a dish. The hotel manager (the **Operating System Kernel**) must ensure every guest eventually gets their meal prepared, even though there's only one kitchen.
>
> - The **Manager** has the master keys, controls the gas stoves, the refrigerator, and the pantry — this is **Kernel Mode (privileged)**.
> - The **Guests** can only place orders through the waiter; they are strictly forbidden from entering the kitchen, touching the gas, or rearranging the menu — this is **User Mode (restricted)**.
> - When a guest says *"I want ice cream"* (a **System Call**), the waiter carries the request to the manager, who then enters the kitchen (switches to kernel mode), retrieves the ice cream, and returns it via the waiter (returns to user mode).
> - The **Mode Bit** is like a sign on the kitchen door: if the sign reads "CLOSED" (User Mode), the gas won't ignite; if it reads "OPEN" (Kernel Mode), the manager has full access.
> - If a guest somehow sneaks into the kitchen and tries to light the stove (**executes a privileged instruction in user mode**), the hotel's fire alarm triggers a **Trap/Exception**, and security (the **OS**) immediately intervenes.

This analogy captures the *essence of why* dual-mode operation exists: **Protection, Isolation, and Controlled Resource Sharing**.

## 1.3 The Core Philosophy: Why Two Modes?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The fundamental reason for dual-mode operation is to ensure the **protection of the operating system itself** from misbehaving or malicious user programs. Without it, any process could:
> 1. Crash the entire system by halting the CPU.
> 2. Read/write sensitive data of other processes.
> 3. Seize control of I/O devices, bypassing the OS.
> 4. Modify the page tables and remap memory of other processes.

The two-mode architecture guarantees that **no user process can directly access hardware** — every hardware interaction must pass through the trusted OS via a **System Call**.

---

## 1.4 Historical Context & Evolution

| Era | Mode Design | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Early Batch Systems (1950s–60s)** | No mode distinction; jobs ran one after another in physical isolation. | IBM 1401, early mainframes. |
| **Multiprogramming Era (1960s–70s)** | Introduction of two modes: Supervisor & User. | IBM OS/360, Multics. |
| **Modern Era (1980s–present)** | Refined dual-mode with **protection rings (Ring 0–3)** and hardware-enforced mode bits. | Intel x86, ARM Cortex-A, RISC-V. |
| **Virtualization Era (2000s–present)** | Extension to **Ring -1 (Hypervisor Mode)** and **Ring -2 (SMM)**. | Intel VT-x, AMD-V, ARM Virtualization Extensions. |

---

## 1.5 Key Hardware Support: The Mode Bit

The CPU contains a special flag — the **Mode Bit** — typically residing in a register called:

- **PSW (Program Status Word)** — on older architectures.
- **CPSR/SPSR** (Current/Saved Program Status Register) — on ARM.
- **RFLAGS / CR0** — on x86 (along with the IOPL and CPL fields).

$$
\text{Mode Bit} = 
\begin{cases}
0 & \rightarrow \text{Kernel Mode (Supervisor / Privileged)} \\
1 & \rightarrow \text{User Mode (Restricted)}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** Examiners often test whether students can list *which* registers/flags control the mode. Remember the **Mode Bit is hardware-enforced** — the OS merely *requests* a mode switch via a `TRAP` or `SYSCALL` instruction, but the *enforcement* is done by the CPU's microcode/control logic.

---

## 1.6 Visual Summary of Modes at a Glance

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              USER MODE (Ring 3)            │
│  • Application code (Word, Chrome, etc.)   │
│  • Restricted Instruction Set              │
│  • No Direct I/O Access                    │
│  • No Access to Kernel Memory              │
└──────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘
                   │  System Call (TRAP / SYSCALL)
                   ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│             KERNEL MODE (Ring 0)           │
│  • OS Kernel & Drivers                     │
│  • Full Hardware Access                    │
│  • Privileged Instructions                  │
│  • Memory Management, I/O, IPC             │
└────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

This visual representation is the foundational mental model that every KTU OS student must internalize before tackling scheduling, synchronization, and memory management modules.

---

## 1.7 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

This sub-topic bridges **Module 1's Process Management** with the rest of the syllabus. It establishes:

1. The **mechanism** of how the OS reclaims control of the CPU (via interrupts, traps, and system calls).
2. The **trust boundary** that separates user code from kernel code — a concept re-used in security, file systems, and device drivers.
3. The **scheduling foundation** — without dual-mode operation, the OS could not safely switch between processes.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked *"How does the OS share the CPU?"*, the complete answer should mention: (1) **Time-sharing** through **Context Switching**, (2) **Scheduler** choosing the next process, (3) **Interrupts/Traps** that force control back to the kernel, and (4) **Dual-Mode Operation** that protects the kernel during execution.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanism of Processor Sharing

To share a single CPU among N processes, the OS performs a continuous cycle of four fundamental operations:

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Process Selection (Scheduling Decision):**
   The OS scheduler — running in kernel mode — selects the next process from the **Ready Queue** using a scheduling algorithm (FCFS, SJF, RR, Priority, MLFQ, etc.).

2. **Context Switch Preparation:**
   The kernel saves the entire execution state of the currently running process into its **Process Control Block (PCB)**, including:
   - Program Counter (PC)
   - General Purpose Registers
   - Stack Pointer (SP)
   - Memory Management Registers (Page Table Base Register)
   - Open File Descriptors
   - Mode Bit (saved implicitly as part of the privilege level)

3. **Mode Transition (Kernel → User):**
   The OS loads the PCB of the newly selected process, restores its registers, and issues a special instruction (e.g., `IRET` on x86, `ERET` on MIPS) that atomically:
   - Sets the **Mode Bit = 1** (User Mode).
   - Jumps to the **Program Counter** of the new process.
   - Hands control of the CPU to the user process.

4. **Execution & Event-Driven Return:**
   The user process executes normally until one of the following events forces a return to kernel mode:
   - **Interrupts** (I/O completion, timer interrupt) → hardware-driven, asynchronous.
   - **Traps/Exceptions** (divide by zero, page fault, illegal instruction) → synchronous, caused by the process.
   - **System Calls** (explicit `read()`, `write()`, `fork()`) → synchronous, voluntary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Check:** A **System Call** is a *synchronous* request from a user process to the kernel. An **Interrupt** is an *asynchronous* event from a hardware device. A **Trap/Exception** is a *synchronous* event caused by the process's own execution (e.g., dividing by zero).

---

## 2.2 Privileged vs. Non-Privileged Instructions

| Aspect | Privileged Instructions | Non-Privileged Instructions |
|---|---|---|
| **Execution Mode** | Kernel Mode only | Both modes |
| **Examples** | `I/O instructions (IN/OUT)`, `HLT`, `STI/CLI`, `LTR`, `LIDT`, `MOV to CR0`, `INVLPG`, `WRMSR` | `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `MOV (between registers)`, `CMP`, `JMP`, `CALL` |
| **If attempted in User Mode** | CPU raises a **General Protection Fault (#GP)** or **Illegal Instruction Exception** | Executes normally |
| **Purpose** | Protect hardware and system integrity | Normal computation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Privileged Instructions Exist:** They allow the OS to maintain *monopoly control* over I/O and critical configuration. Without them, any user program could format the disk, read another process's memory, or lock up the CPU — destroying the system's protection guarantees.

### The Mode Bit's Role in Instruction Decoding

The CPU's instruction decode logic consults the **Mode Bit** *before* executing each instruction. The hardware logic can be conceptualized as:

$$
\text{Instruction Allowed} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{True} & \text{if } (\text{Instruction is non-privileged}) \\
\text{True} & \text{if } (\text{Instruction is privileged}) \land (\text{Mode Bit} = 0) \\
\text{False} & \text{if } (\text{Instruction is privileged}) \land (\text{Mode Bit} = 1)
\end{cases}
$$

If `False`, the CPU generates a **trap** to a pre-defined kernel handler (the *exception handler*).

---

## 2.3 System Calls: The Bridge Between Modes

A **System Call** is the *only* legal mechanism by which a user process can request a kernel service. The flow is:

### Step-by-Step System Call Execution

1. **User Process Issues System Call:**
   The user program calls a library function (e.g., `printf()` in C invokes `write()` → `sys_write`).

2. **Library Wrapper Sets Up Arguments:**
   The C library (glibc on Linux) places the **system call number** (e.g., `1` for `exit`, `4` for `write` on x86-64) and arguments in registers (`rax`, `rdi`, `rsi`, `rdx`).

3. **`SYSCALL` Instruction Executed:**
   The CPU performs an atomic transition:
   - Saves the user's **RIP, RFLAGS, RSP, CS, SS** into the **MSRs** (Model-Specific Registers) or kernel stack.
   - Loads the kernel's **CS, SS, RIP** from the **SYSCALL Target Address MSRs** (e.g., `IA32_LSTAR`).
   - Sets **Mode Bit = 0** (Kernel Mode).

4. **Kernel System Call Handler Runs:**
   The kernel's dispatcher examines `rax` (the call number) and jumps to the appropriate handler.

5. **Service Performed & Return:**
   The kernel executes the requested operation (e.g., writes data to a file), places the return value in `rax`, and executes `SYSRET` (or `IRET`):
   - Restores user registers and flags.
   - Sets **Mode Bit = 1** (User Mode).
   - Resumes user process execution at the instruction after `SYSCALL`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing **System Calls** with **Function Calls**. A function call (e.g., `add(a,b)`) stays entirely within user mode. A system call *crosses the protection boundary* and executes kernel code.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since this is a system-architecture topic, the "formulas" are mostly conceptual relationships, boundary conditions, and architectural parameters.

| # | Concept / Formula | Notation | Purpose / Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mode Bit Value** | $\text{MB} \in \{0, 1\}$ | $0$ = Kernel, $1$ = User |
| 2 | **CPU Utilization** | $U_{CPU} = 1 - p^n$ | Where $p$ = fraction idle per process, $n$ = # processes. Used in scheduling to find minimum processes for full utilization. |
| 3 | **Context Switch Time** | $T_{CS} = T_{save} + T_{load}$ | Time overhead of switching between processes. |
| 4 | **Effective CPU Time** | $T_{eff} = T_{total} - T_{CS} \times N_{switches}$ | CPU time available to processes after context switch overhead. |
| 5 | **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\# \text{ processes completed}}{T_{total}}$ | Processes finished per unit time. |
| 6 | **Turnaround Time** | $T_{TAT} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$ | Total time process spends in system. |
| 7 | **Waiting Time** | $T_{wait} = T_{TAT} - T_{burst}$ | Time spent in ready queue. |
| 8 | **Response Time** | $T_{response} = T_{first\_run} - T_{arrival}$ | Time to first response (interactive systems). |
| 9 | **Mode Switch Overhead** | $T_{mode\_switch} \approx 10 - 100 \text{ ns}$ | Time for a single user→kernel→user transition (architecture-dependent). |
| 10 | **System Call Overhead** | $T_{syscall} = T_{trap} + T_{exec} + T_{return}$ | Total cost of a system call. |
| 11 | **CPU Protection Condition** | $\text{No I/O instruction may execute when } \text{MB} = 1$ | Hardware-enforced invariant. |
| 12 | **Context Switch Rate Limit** | $f_{CS} \leq \frac{1}{T_{CS}}$ | Maximum sustainable switching frequency. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula 2 — CPU Utilization Law:** This is a standard KTU formula. If each of $n$ processes spends a fraction $p$ of its time waiting for I/O, the probability that **all** $n$ processes are idle simultaneously is $p^n$. Hence the CPU is busy for $1 - p^n$ of the time. To achieve $U_{CPU} = 1$, you need $n \to \infty$ for any $p > 0$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Processor Sharing + Dual Mode |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems (RTOS)** | Kernel mode handles hardware interrupts for sensors; user mode runs application logic. Strict separation ensures deterministic response. |
| **Cloud Computing (Hypervisors)** | Extends dual-mode to *Ring -1* (hypervisor mode) to safely run multiple VMs on one physical CPU. |
| **Mobile OS (Android/iOS)** | Sandboxing of apps in user mode prevents one app from accessing another's data — directly leverages dual-mode protection. |
| **Database Systems** | User-mode SQL engine issues system calls for disk I/O; kernel-mode disk drivers perform the actual DMA. |
| **Cybersecurity** | Dual-mode is the bedrock of **rings of protection**. A user-mode malware cannot directly overwrite the kernel — it must exploit a vulnerability to escalate privileges. |
| **High-Performance Computing** | Context switch and mode switch overhead directly affect HPC benchmarks; CPU affinity and core pinning are used to minimize these. |

---

## 2.6 Protection Rings (x86 Architecture)

While KTU 2024 primarily focuses on 2 modes (User and Kernel), the actual x86 architecture defines **4 privilege levels (Rings 0–3)**:

| Ring | Privilege Level | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Ring 0** | Highest (Kernel) | OS Kernel, Device Drivers |
| **Ring 1** | High | Device Drivers (in some OS designs, e.g., older Windows) |
| **Ring 2** | Moderate | System Services (rarely used) |
| **Ring 3** | Lowest (User) | Application Software |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Fact:** Modern OSes like Linux and Windows use only Ring 0 and Ring 3. Rings 1 and 2 exist in hardware but are largely unused. They are, however, used in microkernel designs (e.g., MINIX, QNX) for driver isolation.

### Cross-Reference: Mode Bit in Different Architectures

| Architecture | Mode Bit Location | Modes |
|---|---|---|
| **x86 / x86-64** | `RFLAGS` (lowest 2 bits: IOPL + CPL via CS) | Ring 0–3 |
| **ARM Cortex-A** | `CPSR[4:0]` (Mode field) | USR, FIQ, IRQ, SVC (Supervisor), ABT, UND, SYS |
| **MIPS** | `Status` register (KSU + EXL + ERL bits) | Kernel, Supervisor, User |
| **RISC-V** | `mstatus.MPP` (Previous Privilege Mode) | M-mode, S-mode, U-mode |

This architectural diversity is important for embedded OS students in Kerala's electronics and IoT programs.
---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Number of Processes for Full CPU Utilization

**Problem Statement (KTU Exam Favorite):**
Suppose each of $n$ processes spends a fraction $p$ of its time waiting for I/O to complete. What is the minimum number of processes needed to keep the CPU 100% busy? Derive the formula for CPU utilization.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the variables.

Let:
- $n$ = total number of processes in the ready queue.
- $p$ = probability that any single process is blocked (waiting for I/O).
- $(1 - p)$ = probability that any single process is actively using the CPU.

**Step 2:** Compute the probability that all processes are blocked simultaneously.

The events "process is blocked" are assumed independent. The probability that *all* $n$ processes are blocked at the same instant is:

$$
P(\text{all blocked}) = p \times p \times p \cdots \text{ ($n$ times)} = p^n
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the probability that the CPU is busy.

The CPU is busy whenever *at least one* process is ready to run. By the complement rule:

$$
P(\text{CPU busy}) = 1 - P(\text{all blocked}) = 1 - p^n
$$

**Step 4:** Define CPU utilization.

By definition, CPU utilization equals the fraction of time the CPU is doing useful work:

$$
U_{CPU} = 1 - p^n
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $n$ when $U_{CPU} = 1$ (full utilization).

Setting $U_{CPU} = 1$:

$$
1 = 1 - p^n \implies p^n = 0
$$

Since $0 < p < 1$ in practice, this is achieved only as:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} p^n = 0
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the minimum $n$ to achieve a *desired* utilization $U^*$.

Rearranging the formula:

$$
U^* = 1 - p^n \implies p^n = 1 - U^* \implies n = \frac{\log(1 - U^*)}{\log(p)}
$$

Since $\log(1 - U^*) < 0$ and $\log(p) < 0$ (for $0 < p < 1$), the ratio is **positive** — confirming the result is physically meaningful.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Formula:**
>
> $$\boxed{n_{\min} = \frac{\log(1 - U^*)}{\log(p)}}$$
>
> This gives the *minimum* number of processes required to achieve a target CPU utilization $U^*$, given that each process spends a fraction $p$ of its time idle.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Question:** A system has processes that spend **40%** of their time waiting for I/O. How many processes are needed to achieve **90%** CPU utilization? What if we want 99%?

**Solution:**

**Case 1: $U^* = 0.90$, $p = 0.40$**

$$
n = \frac{\log(1 - 0.90)}{\log(0.40)} = \frac{\log(0.10)}{\log(0.40)}
$$

Evaluating the logarithms (using base 10 for clarity):

$$
\log_{10}(0.10) = -1.000
$$
$$
\log_{10}(0.40) = -0.398
$$

Therefore:

$$
n = \frac{-1.000}{-0.398} = 2.51
$$

Since we cannot have a fractional process, we round **up** to $n = 3$.

**Case 2: $U^* = 0.99$, $p = 0.40$**

$$
n = \frac{\log(0.01)}{\log(0.40)} = \frac{-2.000}{-0.398} = 5.02
$$

Rounding up: $n = 6$ processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** With $n = 3$ and $p = 0.40$:
> $U = 1 - (0.40)^3 = 1 - 0.064 = 0.936$ — which exceeds 0.90 ✓
>
> With $n = 5$ and $p = 0.40$:
> $U = 1 - (0.40)^5 = 1 - 0.01024 = 0.9898$ — which is *less* than 0.99 ✗
>
> So $n = 5$ is insufficient; $n = 6$ is required. This confirms the *ceiling* rounding.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: System Call Cost Analysis

**Problem Statement (Advanced KTU):**
A system call on x86-64 takes 100 ns to execute in kernel mode. The `SYSCALL`/`SYSRET` overhead is 20 ns. The user-kernel transition is 10 ns each way. Calculate the total cost of a system call that does 5 disk reads (each triggering a context switch to a kernel thread).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Decompose the system call into atomic operations.

A single disk read via system call involves:
- `SYSCALL` instruction: 10 ns (user→kernel transition).
- Kernel handler execution: 20 ns.
- Actual I/O completion handler: 100 ns.
- `SYSRET` instruction: 10 ns (kernel→user transition).

**Step 2:** Define the cost of one system call.

$$
T_{syscall} = T_{transition} + T_{handler} + T_{work} + T_{return}
$$

$$
T_{syscall} = 10 + 20 + 100 + 10 = 140 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3:** Define the cost of 5 sequential system calls (no batching).

$$
T_{total} = 5 \times T_{syscall} = 5 \times 140 = 700 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4:** Define the cost of 5 system calls with context switching between processes.

If each disk read blocks the calling process and the scheduler dispatches another process:

$$
T_{total} = 5 \times (T_{syscall} + T_{context\_switch})
$$

Let $T_{context\_switch} = 1 \mu s = 1000$ ns:

$$
T_{total} = 5 \times (140 + 1000) = 5 \times 1140 = 5700 \text{ ns} = 5.7 \mu s
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the overhead percentage.

$$
\text{Overhead \%} = \frac{T_{context\_switch} \times 5}{T_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{5000}{5700} \times 100 \approx 87.72\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** The above calculation shows that context switch overhead can dominate execution time in I/O-bound scenarios. This is why modern kernels use **interrupt coalescing**, **async I/O**, and **zero-copy** techniques to minimize mode transitions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Round Robin Quantum Selection

**Problem Statement (Classic KTU):**
In Round Robin scheduling, choosing the time quantum $q$ involves a trade-off. Show that:
- If $q$ is too **small**, context switch overhead dominates.
- If $q$ is too **large**, the system degenerates to FCFS.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the total time for one process to complete $k$ CPU bursts of length $b$ each.

If the process's quantum expires before each burst ends, it will be preempted $(b/q) - 1$ times within its execution.

Total time including overhead:

$$
T_{RR} = k \cdot b + (k \cdot \lceil b/q \rceil) \cdot T_{CS}
$$

where $T_{CS}$ is the context switch time.

**Step 2:** Take the limit as $q \to \infty$ (quantum too large).

$$
\lim_{q \to \infty} T_{RR} = k \cdot b + k \cdot T_{CS}
$$

The number of context switches approaches $k$ (one per burst), and no preemption occurs within a burst → **behaves like FCFS**.

**Step 3:** Take the limit as $q \to 0$ (quantum too small).

$$
\lim_{q \to 0} T_{RR} \to \infty
$$

Context switches happen so often that **overhead dominates**, and no useful work is done.

**Step 4:** Find the optimal $q$.

A common rule of thumb (used in Linux's CFS) is to make the context switch time a small fraction (e.g., 1%) of the quantum:

$$
T_{CS} \leq 0.01 \cdot q \implies q \geq 100 \cdot T_{CS}
$$

For $T_{CS} = 10 \mu s$:

$$
q \geq 100 \times 10 \mu s = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Formula — Optimal Quantum:**
>
> $$\boxed{q_{opt} \approx \frac{T_{CS}}{0.01} = 100 \cdot T_{CS}}$$
>
> Linux uses $q$ in the range of 1–10 ms; Windows uses ~15–30 ms.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative View of User vs. Kernel Mode

| Attribute | User Mode (Mode Bit = 1) | Kernel Mode (Mode Bit = 0) |
|---|---|---|
| **Privilege Level** | Lowest (Ring 3 on x86) | Highest (Ring 0 on x86) |
| **Memory Access** | Limited to process's own virtual address space | Full physical memory + all process spaces |
| **I/O Instructions** | **FORBIDDEN** — trap on attempt | **ALLOWED** |
| **CPU Control Instructions** | **FORBIDDEN** (HLT, CLI) | **ALLOWED** |
| **Page Table Modification** | **FORBIDDEN** (CR3 write) | **ALLOWED** |
| **Interrupt Handling** | Cannot mask interrupts | Can mask (CLI/STI) |
| **Process State** | User process running | OS kernel, drivers, interrupt handlers |
| **Triggers Mode Switch** | System call, interrupt, exception | `IRET`/`SYSRET` to return |
| **Trust Level** | Untrusted | Fully trusted |
| **Examples of Code** | Web browser, game, text editor | Scheduler, file system, network stack |
| **Failure Impact** | Can only crash its own process (Segfault) | Can crash the entire system (Kernel Panic) |
| **Mode Switch Cost** | ~10–100 ns | ~10–100 ns |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Context Switch Cost in a Real System

**Problem:** A system has 5 processes. Each context switch takes $5 \mu s$. The timer interrupt fires every 10 ms. If 60% of the time is spent in actual CPU work and 40% in idle wait, calculate:
(a) The total time lost to context switches per second.
(b) The effective CPU utilization.

### Solution

**Part (a):**
Number of context switches per second = $\frac{1}{10 \text{ ms}} \times 5 \text{ processes} = \frac{1}{0.01} \times 5 = 500$ context switches per second.

Wait — let me re-read. If the timer fires every 10 ms, that's 100 times per second. Each tick triggers one context switch (to next process). So 100 switches/sec.

$$
N_{switches} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{-3}} = 100 \text{ switches/sec}
$$

$$
T_{lost} = N_{switches} \times T_{CS} = 100 \times 5 \mu s = 500 \mu s = 0.5 \text{ ms/sec}
$$

**Part (b):**

Useful CPU work = $0.60 \times 1 \text{ sec} = 600$ ms.
Idle time = $0.40 \times 1 \text{ sec} = 400$ ms.
Lost to context switches = 0.5 ms.

$$
T_{effective} = 600 - 0.5 = 599.5 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
U_{eff} = \frac{599.5}{1000} \times 100\% = 59.95\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The context switch overhead (0.05%) is negligible here, but in systems with high-frequency interrupts (e.g., 10 kHz networking), overhead can exceed 5%, severely degrading performance.
