# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Dual-Mode Operation. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Dual-mode operation is a hardware-enforced protection mechanism in modern CPUs that supports two distinct execution modes: **User Mode** and **Kernel Mode**, controlled by a **Mode Bit** in the processor's status register.

**Key Points (2 Marks):**
- In **User Mode** (Mode Bit = 1), application programs execute with restricted privileges; they cannot directly execute I/O instructions or access kernel memory.
- In **Kernel Mode** (Mode Bit = 0), the OS kernel has complete control over all hardware and system resources.
- A **System Call** is the controlled mechanism for user processes to request kernel services, triggering an automatic mode switch.
- This separation ensures **system protection, stability, and isolation** between user programs and the OS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *only* define "two modes" and forget to mention the **Mode Bit** and **System Call mechanism**. Both are required for full marks.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Privileged and Non-Privileged Instructions. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Privileged Instructions | Non-Privileged Instructions |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode Restriction** | Executable only in Kernel Mode (Mode Bit = 0) | Executable in both modes |
| **Examples** | I/O instructions (IN, OUT), HLT, STI, CLI, MOV to CR0 | ADD, SUB, MUL, MOV (register-to-register), CMP |
| **Attempted in User Mode** | CPU generates a **General Protection Fault (trap)** | Executes normally |
| **Purpose** | Protect hardware from unauthorized access | Perform general computation |

**Significance (1 Mark):**
Privileged instructions ensure that the OS maintains exclusive control over hardware resources, preventing user programs from disrupting the system or other processes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often fail to mention what happens *if* a privileged instruction is executed in user mode (i.e., a trap/exception is generated). Always include the **consequence** for full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the mechanism of sharing a processor among multiple processes. Discuss the role of context switching and the scheduler.**

#### Model Solution

**1. Introduction (1 Mark):**
In a multiprogramming OS, a single physical CPU must execute multiple processes concurrently. This is achieved through **time-sharing**, where the OS rapidly switches the CPU among processes, giving each a small time slice called a **quantum**.

**2. Components of Processor Sharing (3 Marks):**

**(i) The Scheduler:**
- Maintains the **Ready Queue** of processes waiting for CPU.
- Selects the next process to run using an algorithm (FCFS, SJF, Round Robin, Priority, MLFQ).
- Runs in **Kernel Mode** with full hardware access.

**(ii) The Dispatcher:**
- The module that gives control of the CPU to the process selected by the scheduler.
- Performs the **context switch** and **mode switch** (Kernel → User).

**(iii) Context Switch:**
- Saves the state of the currently running process into its **Process Control Block (PCB)** — registers, PC, SP, page table base, etc.
- Loads the state of the newly selected process from its PCB.
- **Mode Bit is set to 1 (User Mode)** before transferring control.
- Typical cost: **1–100 microseconds**, depending on architecture.

**3. The Cycle of Processor Sharing (2 Marks):**

```
Scheduler → Select Next Process → Dispatcher → Context Switch
    ↑                                                    ↓
    └───────── User Process Runs (until interrupt) ──────┘
```

The cycle repeats on:
- **Time quantum expiry** (timer interrupt)
- **I/O request** (process blocks)
- **Process termination** (process exits)
- **Higher-priority process becomes ready** (preemption)

**4. Conclusion (1 Mark):**
Effective processor sharing requires careful balance: a small quantum improves responsiveness but increases overhead; a large quantum reduces overhead but degrades interactivity.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] With neat diagrams, explain the concept of Dual-Mode Operation. How does the Mode Bit provide protection?**

#### Model Solution

**1. Definition (1 Mark):**
**Dual-Mode Operation** is a CPU hardware feature that supports two distinct execution modes — **User Mode** and **Kernel Mode** — controlled by a **Mode Bit**.

**2. The Two Modes (2 Marks):**

| Mode | Mode Bit | Privileges |
|---|---|---|
| **Kernel Mode** | 0 | Full access: all instructions, all memory, all I/O |
| **User Mode** | 1 | Restricted: no I/O, no kernel memory, no privileged instructions |

**3. Diagram (2 Marks):**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         USER MODE  (Mode Bit = 1)        │
│   • Word, Chrome, Games, Compilers       │
│   • Restricted Instruction Set           │
│   • Cannot execute I/O instructions      │
└────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘
             │ System Call (TRAP/SYSCALL)
             ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        KERNEL MODE  (Mode Bit = 0)       │
│   • OS Kernel, Drivers, Scheduler        │
│   • Full Hardware Access                 │
│   • Privileged Instructions Allowed      │
└────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘
             │ IRET / SYSRET
             ▼
        (Back to User Mode)
```

**4. Protection via Mode Bit (2 Marks):**
- The CPU's instruction decoder **consults the Mode Bit** before executing each instruction.
- If a user-mode process attempts a **privileged instruction** (e.g., `IN` for I/O), the CPU **traps** to the kernel's exception handler.
- The kernel then typically **terminates the offending process** (e.g., "Segmentation Fault").
- This hardware-enforced check prevents user code from crashing the OS or other processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Example:** When a user program tries to access an invalid memory address, the **MMU** triggers a **page fault trap**, which switches the CPU to kernel mode. The kernel's page fault handler decides whether to load the page from disk (valid access) or terminate the process (invalid access — "Segfault").

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks] What is a System Call? Explain the steps involved in executing a system call with a neat diagram. How does it relate to dual-mode operation?**

#### Model Solution

**1. Definition (1 Mark):**
A **System Call** is the controlled, well-defined interface through which a user process requests a service from the OS kernel (e.g., file I/O, process creation, network communication).

**2. Why System Calls are Needed (1 Mark):**
User-mode processes cannot directly access hardware. System calls provide a **safe, auditable, and portable** way for them to request kernel services.

**3. Steps in System Call Execution (3 Marks):**

**Step 1:** User program calls a library function (e.g., `read(fd, buf, n)`).

**Step 2:** The C library wrapper (glibc) places the **system call number** in a register (e.g., `rax` on x86-64) and arguments in other registers (`rdi`, `rsi`, `rdx`).

**Step 3:** The library executes the `SYSCALL` instruction. The CPU **atomically**:
- Saves user state (RIP, RFLAGS, CS, SS) to Model-Specific Registers.
- Loads kernel's CS, RIP, and sets **Mode Bit = 0**.

**Step 4:** The **system call dispatcher** in the kernel examines the call number and dispatches to the appropriate handler (e.g., `sys_read`).

**Step 5:** The kernel performs the requested operation (e.g., reads from disk into the user's buffer).

**Step 6:** The kernel places a **return value** in `rax` and executes `SYSRET`, which restores user state and sets **Mode Bit = 1**.

**4. Diagram (2 Marks):**

```
USER MODE (Mode Bit = 1)
   │
   │  1. App calls read()
   │  2. Library sets rax=0, rdi=fd, rsi=buf, rdx=count
   ▼
SYSCALL instruction ──→ (Mode Bit becomes 0)
   │
KERNEL MODE (Mode Bit = 0)
   │
   │  3. Dispatcher reads rax → calls sys_read()
   │  4. Driver reads from disk
   │  5. Data copied to user buffer
   │  6. Return value placed in rax
   ▼
SYSRET instruction ──→ (Mode Bit becomes 1)
   │
USER MODE (Mode Bit = 1)
   │
   │  7. App continues with data in buffer
   ▼
```

**5. Relation to Dual-Mode Operation (1 Mark):**
System calls are the **only legal bridge** between User Mode and Kernel Mode. They enforce the principle that all hardware access must pass through the trusted OS, preserving the protection guarantees of dual-mode operation.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain Protection Rings in x86 architecture. How do they extend the simple 2-mode model? Why are Rings 1 and 2 rarely used in modern OSes?**

#### Model Solution

**1. Introduction (1 Mark):**
The x86 architecture defines **4 privilege levels (rings)**, numbered 0 (most privileged) to 3 (least privileged). This is a generalization of the basic 2-mode model.

**2. The Four Rings (2 Marks):**

| Ring | Privilege | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Ring 0** | Highest | OS Kernel, critical drivers |
| **Ring 1** | High | Device drivers (legacy) |
| **Ring 2** | Moderate | System services (rare) |
| **Ring 3** | Lowest | User applications |

**3. Diagram (1 Mark):**

```
        ┌─────────────────────┐
        │   RING 3 - USER     │ ← Mode Bit = 3
        │   ┌─────────────┐   │
        │   │ RING 2      │   │ ← Mode Bit = 2
        │   │ ┌─────────┐ │   │
        │   │ │ RING 1  │ │   │ ← Mode Bit = 1
        │   │ │ ┌─────┐ │ │   │
        │   │ │ │RING 0│ │ │   │ ← Mode Bit = 0
        │   │ │ │KERNEL│ │ │   │
        │   │ │ └─────┘ │ │   │
        │   │ └─────────┘ │   │
        │   └─────────────┘   │
        └─────────────────────┘
```

**4. How Rings Extend the 2-Mode Model (2 Marks):**
- The simple 2-mode model (User + Kernel) maps to Ring 3 + Ring 0 in x86.
- The additional rings (1 and 2) allow **finer-grained privilege separation** without entering Ring 0.
- For example, a buggy driver in Ring 1 can crash the driver, but the kernel (Ring 0) remains protected — improving **fault isolation**.

**5. Why Rings 1 and 2 are Rarely Used (1 Mark):**
- Modern OSes (Linux, Windows, macOS) prefer a **simpler binary split** for performance and ease of implementation.
- The overhead of context-switching between rings (and the associated CPU support complexity) outweighs the isolation benefits for most use cases.
- Microkernel OSes (MINIX, QNX) and some hypervisors **do** use Rings 1 and 2.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 CRITICAL EXAM PITFALLS — Read Before Writing Your Exam!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Mode Bit:** When asked about dual-mode, *always* mention the **Mode Bit** and its location (PSW, RFLAGS, CPSR). Marks are explicitly awarded for this.
>
> 2. **Confusing Interrupt, Trap, and System Call:**
>    - **Interrupt** = Asynchronous, hardware-driven (e.g., disk I/O complete).
>    - **Trap/Exception** = Synchronous, caused by process error (e.g., divide by zero).
>    - **System Call** = Synchronous, voluntary request by process.
>    Mixing these up costs at least 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Saying "Kernel has no restrictions" without context:** Always state *what* the kernel can do — execute privileged instructions, access all memory, control I/O.
>
> 4. **Omitting diagrams in Part B:** A 7-mark sub-question without a diagram typically loses 1–2 marks. Always include a **state diagram** or **flow diagram** for system calls, mode transitions, or protection rings.
>
> 5. **Conflating User Mode with Kernel Mode in examples:** State clearly: *"The application runs in User Mode; the OS kernel runs in Kernel Mode."*
>
> 6. **Not mentioning the Hardware Timer:** In questions on processor sharing, mention that a **timer interrupt** is what forces the OS to regain control of the CPU — this is a key KTU-expected point.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to discuss overhead:** When discussing context switching, always mention the **time cost** (1–100 μs) and that excessive context switching wastes CPU time.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam!**

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- ☐ **Dual-Mode Operation** = User Mode (restricted) + Kernel Mode (privileged), controlled by a **Mode Bit**.
- ☐ **Mode Bit = 0 → Kernel Mode**; **Mode Bit = 1 → User Mode** (on most architectures; verify for your specific CPU).
- ☐ **Three ways to enter Kernel Mode:** System Call, Interrupt, Exception/Trap.
- ☐ **One way to leave Kernel Mode:** `IRET` / `SYSRET` instruction.
- ☐ **Privileged Instructions** execute *only* in Kernel Mode; attempting them in User Mode causes a **trap** (General Protection Fault).

### Key Mechanisms
- ☐ **System Call** = the *only* legal user→kernel transition for service requests.
- ☐ **Context Switch** = saving + loading process state (PC, registers, etc.) via PCB.
- ☐ **Timer Interrupt** = the mechanism that forces preemption in time-sharing systems.
- ☐ **PCB** = Process Control Block — stores all state needed to resume a process.

### Formulas & Numbers
- ☐ CPU Utilization: $U_{CPU} = 1 - p^n$ (where $p$ = idle fraction, $n$ = number of processes).
- ☐ Minimum processes for $U^*$: $n = \frac{\log(1 - U^*)}{\log(p)}$.
- ☐ Context switch cost: 1–100 μs (typical).
- ☐ Mode switch cost: 10–100 ns.
- ☐ Rule of thumb for time quantum: $q \geq 100 \times T_{CS}$.

### Protection Rings (x86)
- ☐ Ring 0 = Kernel (highest privilege).
- ☐ Ring 3 = User applications (lowest privilege).
- ☐ Rings 1 & 2 = Intermediate privilege, mostly unused in modern OSes.

### Common Examples
- ☐ `printf("Hello")` → user-mode call → invokes `write()` system call → kernel prints to stdout → returns to user mode.
- ☐ `malloc(100)` → may invoke `brk()` or `mmap()` system calls.
- ☐ Page fault → trap → kernel page-fault handler → loads page from disk (or kills process).

### Key Definitions (One-Liners for 2-Mark Questions)
- ☐ **Mode Bit:** A hardware flag indicating current execution mode.
- ☐ **Privileged Instruction:** CPU instruction executable only in kernel mode.
- ☐ **System Call:** Controlled interface for user processes to request OS services.
- ☐ **Context Switch:** Saving/restoring process state to switch CPU between processes.
- ☐ **Protection Ring:** Hierarchical privilege level in CPU architecture.

### Exam-Specific Tactics
- ☐ Always **draw a diagram** in 7-mark sub-questions.
- ☐ Use **tables** to compare User Mode vs. Kernel Mode (looks organized → more marks).
- ☐ Mention **real-world examples** (Linux, Windows, Android) for context.
- ☐ End with a **conclusion** linking back to protection and security.
- ☐ In numerical questions, show **all** log calculations explicitly.

---

## 5.5 Bonus: Mnemonics & Memory Aids

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick: "U-K-S-I-R"** for remembering the 3 events that cause User → Kernel transitions:
> - **U**ser (process) makes System Call
> - **K**ernel (hardware) raises Interrupt
> - **S**omething goes wrong → Trap/Exception
> - **I**nvalid instruction in user mode → Fault
> - **R**un-time error (divide by zero, page fault)

**Memory Trick: "RING ZERO = GROUND ZERO"** — The kernel sits at the deepest, most privileged level (Ring 0). User code lives in the outermost ring (Ring 3), like satellites orbiting the Earth.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Context Switching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Context Switching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Context Switching** is the mechanism by which the **Operating System (OS) kernel** saves the **execution state (context)** of a currently running process or thread, and loads the previously saved state of another process or thread, thereby transferring CPU control from one execution context to another. The "context" is formally stored in the **Process Control Block (PCB)** or **Thread Control Block (TCB)** and comprises the **CPU registers, program counter (PC), stack pointer (SP), memory management registers, and other architectural state** necessary to seamlessly resume execution.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme PCCST403 framework, context switching is a **core kernel-level operation** that enables **multiprogramming** and **multitasking**. It is the underlying mechanism that gives the user the *illusion* of parallel execution on a single-core CPU.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metric (KTU Board Definition):** The efficiency of an OS is heavily judged by its **Context Switch Time**, which is the pure time overhead spent saving and loading CPU states, typically measured in **microseconds (µs)**. A high-performance OS (e.g., Linux kernel) achieves context switch times between **1 µs to 10 µs** depending on architecture.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chef in a Single Kitchen"

Imagine a **single chef** working in a kitchen. The chef has **3 dishes** to prepare (representing 3 processes):
- Dish A: Biryani
- Dish B: Pasta
- Dish C: Salad

The chef cannot cook all three simultaneously. So, the chef:
1. Starts cooking Biryani (loads context of Process A).
2. Realizes the rice needs to be soaked for 30 minutes (an **I/O event** / blocking operation).
3. The chef **stops** cooking Biryani, carefully **writes down** exactly where he left off (which spices added, the timer, the pot position) → this is **saving the context to the PCB**.
4. The chef then **looks at his task list** and starts making Pasta → this is **loading the context of Process B**.
5. When the rice is ready, the chef stops Pasta, **records progress**, and resumes Biryani → another **context switch**.

> [!TIP]
> **The "task list"** is the **Scheduler** (Short-Term Scheduler / CPU Scheduler), and **writing down progress** is **saving to the PCB**. The chef is the **single CPU core**. Every time the chef stops one dish and starts another, a **Context Switch** occurs.

---

## 1.3 What Constitutes a "Context"?

The exact set of items stored depends on the CPU architecture, but typically includes:

| Component | Purpose | Stored In |
|---|---|---|
| **Program Counter (PC / IP)** | Address of the next instruction to execute | PCB |
| **Stack Pointer (SP)** | Top of the current stack | PCB |
| **General Purpose Registers (GPRs)** | Working data of the process | PCB |
| **Processor Status Word (PSW)** | Condition codes, interrupt enable flags, mode bits | PCB |
| **Memory Management Registers** | Page Table Base Register (PTBR), TLB entries, segment selectors | PCB |
| **Floating Point / SIMD Registers** | State of math co-processors (saved lazily) | PCB |
| **Open File Descriptor Table Pointers** | I/O state | PCB |
| **CPU Cache Lines** | *(Optional)* — Optional flushing | Cache |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Context Switch** with **Mode Switch (Privilege Switch)**. A **Mode Switch** is when CPU transitions between **User Mode** and **Kernel Mode** (e.g., during a system call) WITHOUT changing the running process. A **Context Switch** always changes the running process. Mode Switches are *cheaper* and occur *during* system calls; Context Switches occur *because* of scheduling decisions.

---

## 1.4 Two Levels of Context Switching

1. **Process Context Switch** — Switching between two independent processes with **separate address spaces**. Requires **TLB flush** and **page table switch**. *Heavyweight.*
2. **Thread Context Switch** — Switching between two threads of the **same process** with **shared address space**. Only register state and stack are swapped. *Lightweight.*

---

## 1.5 Why Context Switching is Necessary (KTU-Critical Justification)

- Enables **multiprogramming** — maximizes CPU utilization.
- Ensures **fairness** — no single process monopolizes the CPU.
- Achieves **responsiveness** — interactive processes get timely CPU slices.
- Provides **isolation** — each process operates in its own protected environment.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Phases of a Context Switch (Step-by-Step Logic)

The context switch is a **kernel-mode privileged operation**. It cannot be performed by user processes. It is typically triggered by:

1. **Multitasking:** The time quantum (time slice) of the running process expires — triggered by a **timer interrupt (IRQ 0)**.
2. **Blocking I/O:** The process invokes a system call that blocks (e.g., `read()` on a pipe with no data) — voluntarily yielding the CPU.
3. **Higher priority process:** A higher priority process becomes runnable (e.g., a real-time process wakes up).
4. **Voluntary yield:** A process calls `sched_yield()` or similar.
5. **Termination:** The running process exits.

### The Context Switch Phases (Detailed)

**Phase A — Trigger & Mode Entry**
1. A hardware **interrupt** (timer, I/O completion) or **system call trap** fires.
2. The CPU **automatically** switches to **kernel mode** and jumps to the predefined **interrupt service routine (ISR)** address stored in the **Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT)**.
3. The hardware pushes a minimal frame: the current **PC, PSW, and CS** (code segment) onto the **kernel stack** of the *currently running process*.
4. This step alone is a **Mode Switch**, not yet a Context Switch.

**Phase B — Save Old Context (Save State of OUT Process)**
5. The kernel's `schedule()` function is invoked.
6. The kernel saves the **complete register set** of the outgoing process (OUT-process) into its **PCB**. This includes:
   - All general-purpose registers
   - Floating-point / SSE / AVX registers (sometimes deferred — *lazy FPU save*)
   - Segment registers (DS, ES, FS, GS, SS)
   - Control registers (CR3 for page table base, if switching address spaces)
   - Kernel stack pointer
7. The OUT-process is moved from **RUNNING** state to **READY** (or **BLOCKED**) state in the process table.

**Phase C — Scheduler Decision**
8. The **Short-Term Scheduler** runs the scheduling algorithm (e.g., Round Robin, CFS, Priority, Multilevel Feedback Queue).
9. The scheduler selects the next **IN-process** to run from the **Ready Queue**.

**Phase D — Load New Context (Restore State of IN Process)**
10. The kernel updates memory management structures:
    - If switching between **different processes**, the **MMU** is reloaded with the new page table base (writing to `CR3` on x86) → **TLB is flushed** on most architectures.
    - If switching between **threads of the same process**, the page table is unchanged (TLB is preserved).
11. The register state of the IN-process is **restored** from its PCB.
12. The kernel stack pointer is switched to the IN-process's kernel stack.
13. A `iret` (interrupt return) or `sysret` instruction is executed, which:
    - Pops the saved PC, PSW, and CS from the kernel stack.
    - Switches CPU back to **User Mode**.
    - Resumes execution of the IN-process at exactly the instruction where it was previously preempted.

**Phase E — Resume Execution**
14. The IN-process begins (or resumes) execution as if it had never been stopped. This is the **transparency** property of context switching.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **board-relevant formulas and metrics** for Context Switching as tested in PCCST403. Memorize the symbols and units.

| # | Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Utilization (U)** | $U = 1 - p^n$ | dimensionless (ratio) | Probability all *n* processes are in I/O wait; *p* = I/O wait probability |
| 2 | **Effective CPU Time** | $T_{\text{eff}} = T_{\text{burst}} + T_{\text{CS}}$ | seconds (s) | Real wall-clock time of a CPU burst including context switch overhead |
| 3 | **Throughput Loss Ratio** | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{CS}}}{T_{\text{slice}} + T_{\text{CS}}}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of time wasted on context switching per time slice |
| 4 | **CPU Efficiency** | $E = \dfrac{T_{\text{slice}}}{T_{\text{slice}} + T_{\text{CS}}} \times 100\%$ | percent (%) | Useful CPU work as % of total slice period |
| 5 | **Total Overhead (n switches)** | $T_{\text{overhead}} = n \times T_{\text{CS}}$ | seconds (s) | Cumulative time lost to switching |
| 6 | **Context Switch Time (T_CS)** | $T_{\text{CS}} = T_{\text{save}} + T_{\text{load}} + T_{\text{scheduler}}$ | microseconds (µs) | Sum of save, load, and scheduling decision time |
| 7 | **Amdahl's Law (Context Switch)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | dimensionless | Speedup; here, *f* = fraction of time spent in switching, *N* = no. of CPUs |
| 8 | **Dispatcher Latency** | Same as $T_{\text{CS}}$ (alternative textbook term) | µs | Time taken by dispatcher to stop one process and start another |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule:** All `$...$` and `$$...$$` blocks are kept clean. Absolute value bars `|x|` are **NOT** used in table cells. Where magnitude is needed, use `\vert x \vert` or `\|x\|` notation in your handwriting.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Context switching is fundamental to **every** modern computing domain:

- **Cloud Servers (AWS, Azure, GCP):** Hypervisors (KVM, Xen) perform **VM Context Switches** to multiplex hundreds of virtual machines on a single physical host.
- **Mobile OS (Android, iOS):** UI threads and background threads are switched thousands of times per second to maintain 60 Hz refresh rates.
- **Embedded Real-Time Systems (QNX, VxWorks, FreeRTOS):** Context switch time is a **hard constraint** — often must be < 5 µs for automotive or aerospace control loops.
- **Database Engines:** Use **user-mode context switching** (coroutines, fibers) to avoid kernel transitions for queries.
- **Operating System Kernels:** Linux's **CFS Scheduler** minimizes context switch overhead through **per-CPU run queues** and **lazy TLB flushing**.

---

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Context Switch Cost

- **Number of Registers** in the CPU architecture (more registers → more to save).
- **Memory Architecture** — TLB flush, page table reload cost (significant for process switches).
- **Cache Pollution** — Out-process's cache lines are evicted, causing **cold-cache** effect on IN-process.
- **Kernel Implementation** — Optimized assembly routines (e.g., `switch_to()` in Linux) vs. high-level interpreted code.
- **Hardware Support** — Some CPUs have dedicated **register save/restore instructions**.
- **Process vs. Thread** — Thread switches are typically 5–10x faster than process switches.
---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective CPU Time and Efficiency

### Problem Setup
Consider a system using **Round Robin Scheduling** with:
- **Time quantum (slice) = T_slice = 50 ms**
- **Context switch time = T_CS = 5 µs = 0.005 ms**
- **Number of context switches = n = 1000 per second**

### Derivation of CPU Efficiency (E)

The fraction of time the CPU is *actually* doing useful work in one complete scheduling cycle is:

$$E = \frac{T_{\text{slice}}}{T_{\text{slice}} + T_{\text{CS}}}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the components of one scheduling cycle:**

For each process execution, one full cycle consists of:
- A *useful execution period* of length $T_{\text{slice}}$
- A *context switch overhead period* of length $T_{\text{CS}}$

Total cycle period $= T_{\text{slice}} + T_{\text{CS}}$

**Step 2 — Express the efficiency ratio:**

$$E = \frac{\text{Useful time}}{\text{Total time per process}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$E = \frac{T_{\text{slice}}}{T_{\text{slice}} + T_{\text{CS}}}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in numerical values:**

$$E = \frac{50 \text{ ms}}{50 \text{ ms} + 0.005 \text{ ms}}$$

$$E = \frac{50}{50.005} = 0.9999$$

**Step 5 — Convert to percentage:**

$$E = 99.99\%$$

### Conclusion
With a 50 ms quantum and 5 µs context switch time, only **0.01%** of CPU time is wasted on context switches — extremely efficient.

---

### Derivation: Total Overhead for n Context Switches

$$T_{\text{overhead}} = n \times T_{\text{CS}}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute n = 1000 and T_CS = 5 µs:**

$$T_{\text{overhead}} = 1000 \times 5 \,\mu s$$

$$T_{\text{overhead}} = 5000 \,\mu s = 5 \text{ ms per second}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to a percentage of 1 second (1000 ms):**

$$\text{Overhead}\% = \frac{5 \text{ ms}}{1000 \text{ ms}} \times 100\% = 0.5\%$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
Even with 1000 context switches per second, only 0.5% of CPU time is lost. The system is well-designed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Throughput Loss Ratio (η)

The **Throughput Loss Ratio** $\eta$ measures the proportion of time spent on context switching *relative to* the full scheduling period:

$$\eta = \frac{T_{\text{CS}}}{T_{\text{slice}} + T_{\text{CS}}}$$

**Step 1 — Cross-multiply the original efficiency equation to verify:**

$$E + \eta = \frac{T_{\text{slice}}}{T_{\text{slice}} + T_{\text{CS}}} + \frac{T_{\text{CS}}}{T_{\text{slice}} + T_{\text{CS}}}$$

**Step 2 — Combine the fractions (common denominator):**

$$E + \eta = \frac{T_{\text{slice}} + T_{\text{CS}}}{T_{\text{slice}} + T_{\text{CS}}} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Hence:**

$$E = 1 - \eta$$

This is the **fundamental relationship**: Efficiency and Throughput Loss Ratio are **complementary** and must sum to 1 (or 100%).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Multi-Process CPU Utilization (Extension)

For **n processes** all with I/O wait probability *p*, the probability that **all n processes are waiting** (and CPU is idle) is:

$$P(\text{idle}) = p^n$$

Hence, **CPU Utilization** is:

$$U = 1 - p^n$$

**Worked Example (KTU Exam Style):**

A system has 4 processes. Each process spends 40% of its time waiting for I/O. Calculate CPU utilization.

- $n = 4$
- $p = 0.40$

$$U = 1 - (0.40)^4 = 1 - 0.0256 = 0.9744$$

$$U = 97.44\%$$

This shows the **multiprogramming benefit** — even with relatively high I/O wait, the CPU is busy 97.44% of the time.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Context Switch vs. Mode Switch

| Parameter | Context Switch | Mode Switch |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Changes?** | Yes — different process runs | No — same process continues |
| **State Saved** | Full register set, PCB, page table | Only PC, PSW, CS, SS (minimal) |
| **Mode Change?** | Yes (User ↔ Kernel → User) | Yes (User ↔ Kernel only) |
| **TLB Flush?** | Yes (for process switch) | No |
| **Typical Cost** | 1–100 µs (process); < 1 µs (thread) | ~0.1–1 µs |
| **Triggered By** | Scheduler decision, time slice expiry | System call, interrupt, exception |
| **Visible to User Process?** | Yes — preemption is observable | No — process continues seamlessly |
| **Address Space Change?** | Yes (process) / No (thread) | Never |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** A **Mode Switch** is a *necessary sub-step* of a **Context Switch**, but they are NOT synonymous. Students writing answers that equate the two will lose **2 marks minimum** in a 14-mark question.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem: Process Switch Overhead (Board Style)

> **Question:** A CPU has a clock rate of **2 GHz**. A context switch requires saving/restoring **32 registers**, each taking **2 clock cycles**. The scheduler decision takes **200 cycles**. An additional **500 cycles** are needed for TLB flush and page table reload. Calculate:
> (a) Total cycles per context switch.
> (b) Context switch time in microseconds.
> (c) If the time quantum is **10 ms**, what is the CPU efficiency loss?

### Solution

**(a) Total cycles per context switch:**

Cycles for saving 32 registers: $32 \times 2 = 64$ cycles
Cycles for restoring 32 registers: $32 \times 2 = 64$ cycles
Scheduler decision cycles: $200$ cycles
TLB flush + page table reload: $500$ cycles

Total cycles:

$$T_{\text{cycles}} = 64 + 64 + 200 + 500$$

$$T_{\text{cycles}} = 828 \text{ cycles}$$

**(b) Context switch time in microseconds:**

CPU clock period:

$$T_{\text{period}} = \frac{1}{2 \text{ GHz}} = 0.5 \text{ ns per cycle}$$

Context switch time:

$$T_{\text{CS}} = 828 \text{ cycles} \times 0.5 \text{ ns/cycle}$$

$$T_{\text{CS}} = 414 \text{ ns} = 0.414 \,\mu s$$

**(c) CPU efficiency loss for 10 ms time quantum:**

$$\text{Loss ratio} = \frac{T_{\text{CS}}}{T_{\text{slice}}} = \frac{0.414 \,\mu s}{10 \text{ ms}} = \frac{0.414}{10000} = 4.14 \times 10^{-5}$$

Loss percentage:

$$\text{Loss}\% = 4.14 \times 10^{-3}\,\% \approx 0.004\%$$

**Interpretation:** Context switch overhead is negligible (~0.004%) for a 10 ms quantum. If the quantum were 10 µs instead, overhead would be ~4%, which is significant.
---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Context Switch Lifecycle (5-State Process Model)

The following diagram illustrates the **operational flow of a context switch** within the **5-State Process Model** (New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Exit).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([A: Running Process P1]) -->|Timer Interrupt fires| B[B: Kernel Mode Entry]
    B --> C[C: Save P1 context to PCB1]
    C --> D[D: Update P1 state to READY]
    D --> E[E: Run Scheduler - select next process]
    E --> F[F: Load PCB2 of P2]
    F --> G[G: Reload MMU - TLB flush and page table switch]
    G --> H[H: Restore P2 register state]
    H --> I[I: Switch kernel stack to P2]
    I --> J[J: Execute IRET - return to User Mode]
    J --> K([K: Running Process P2])
    K -->|P2 blocks on I/O| L[L: Save P2 context to PCB2]
    L --> M[M: Update P2 state to BLOCKED]
    M --> E
    K -->|Time slice expires| B
```

**Reading Guide:**
- **Nodes A and K** represent the *Running* state of two different processes.
- **Nodes B through I** depict the kernel-level context switch sequence.
- **Node E** is the **Short-Term Scheduler** (the only decision point).
- The loop from K back to B (via "Time slice expires") shows **preemptive** behavior.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: PCB Architecture (Data Saved During Switch)

This block diagram shows the **contents of a Process Control Block (PCB)** that must be persisted during a context switch.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PCB[Process Control Block] --> ID[Process Identification]
    PCB --> REG[CPU Registers]
    PCB --> MMU[Memory Management State]
    PCB --> SCHED[Scheduler Information]
    PCB --> IO[I/O Status]
    PCB --> ACCT[Accounting Info]

    ID --> PID1[PID]
    ID --> PPID1[PPID]
    ID --> UID1[UID and GID]

    REG --> PC1[Program Counter]
    REG --> SP1[Stack Pointer]
    REG --> PSW1[Processor Status Word]
    REG --> GPR1[General Purpose Registers x32]
    REG --> FPU1[FPU and SIMD Registers]

    MMU --> CR31[CR3 Page Table Base]
    MMU --> SEG1[Segment Registers DS ES FS GS]
    MMU --> TLB1[TLB Snapshot Optional]

    SCHED --> STATE1[State - Ready Running Blocked]
    SCHED --> PRIO1[Priority]
    SCHED --> Q1[Queue Pointers]

    IO --> FDT1[Open File Descriptors]
    IO --> SIG1[Pending Signals]
    IO --> DINFO1[Device Allocations]

    ACCT --> CPU1[CPU Time Used]
    ACCT --> MEM1[Memory Limits]
    ACCT --> CHILD1[Child Processes List]
```

**Block Categories:**
- **REG** block is the *hot path* — saved on every switch.
- **MMU** block is only fully swapped for **process** switches, not thread switches.
- **ACCT** block is rarely modified during a switch but updated periodically.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Kernel Stack Switching Mechanism

This shows the **kernel stack swap** that occurs during a context switch — a subtle but critical OS mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USER["USER MODE EXECUTION"]
        U1[Process P1 user stack]
        U2[Process P2 user stack]
    end

    subgraph KERNEL["KERNEL MODE EXECUTION"]
        K1[Kernel stack of P1 - 8 KB]
        K2[Kernel stack of P2 - 8 KB]
        SCH[Scheduler function in kernel]
    end

    U1 -->|Trap - interrupt or syscall| K1
    U2 -->|Trap - interrupt or syscall| K2

    K1 --> SCH
    K2 --> SCH

    SCH -->|Reads ESP and saves to PCB1| K1
    SCH -->|Loads P2 kernel ESP from PCB2| K2

    K1 -.->|IRET with P2 context| U2
    K2 -.->|IRET with P1 context| U1
```

**Critical Concept:**
- Each process has its **own dedicated kernel stack** (typically 8 KB on Linux).
- The kernel uses the **TSS (Task State Segment)** on x86 to find the current kernel stack.
- The kernel stack pointer itself is *part of* the saved context — saving ESP and loading a new ESP is the *pivot point* of the switch.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hardware-OS Interaction During Switch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph HW["HARDWARE LAYER"]
        CPU[CPU Core]
        REG[Register File x64]
        TLB[Translation Lookaside Buffer]
        CR3[CR3 Page Table Base Register]
        TIMER[Programmable Interval Timer PIT]
    end

    subgraph KERN["KERNEL LAYER"]
        ISR[Interrupt Service Routine]
        SCH[Scheduler]
        SWITCH[switch_to Assembly Routine]
        MMU[Memory Management Switch Code]
    end

    subgraph USER["USER LAYER"]
        P1[Process P1]
        P2[Process P2]
        P3[Process P3]
    end

    TIMER -->|IRQ 0 - tick| ISR
    ISR --> SCH
    SCH --> SWITCH
    SWITCH --> REG
    SWITCH --> MMU
    MMU --> CR3
    CR3 --> TLB
    SWITCH --> P1
    SWITCH --> P2
    SWITCH --> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** as per KTU diagram policy. It maps the **hardware-software interaction chain** that executes a context switch, from the timer interrupt firing (Hardware) all the way to resuming user process execution (User Layer).
---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define context switching. List any four items stored in the Process Control Block (PCB) during a context switch.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Context Switching** is the procedure performed by the OS kernel of saving the state (context) of the currently running process and loading the state of another process, so that the CPU can be multiplexed among multiple processes. The state is preserved in the PCB.
>
> Four items stored in PCB:
> 1. **Program Counter (PC)** — Address of the next instruction to execute.
> 2. **CPU Registers** — All general-purpose and special-purpose registers.
> 3. **Process State** — Current state (Running, Ready, Blocked).
> 4. **Memory Management Information** — Page table base register, segment table info.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a context switch and a mode switch.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Context Switch | Mode Switch |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Changes** | Yes, different process runs | No, same process continues |
| **Triggered By** | Scheduler, time slice expiry | System call, interrupt, exception |
| **State Saved** | Full register set + page table | Only PC, PSW, CS, SS |
| **Cost** | 1–100 µs | 0.1–1 µs |
| **TLB Flush** | Required (process switch) | Not required |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B1A (Choice A) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the steps involved in a context switch with the help of a suitable diagram. How does it differ from a mode switch?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A system uses Round Robin scheduling with a time quantum of **20 ms**. The context switch time is **10 µs**. Determine:
1. The CPU efficiency.
2. The throughput loss ratio.
3. Total overhead in 1 hour of continuous operation.

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define context switch** *(1 mark)*:
Context switching is the mechanism of saving the state of the currently running process (OUT-process) into its PCB and loading the state of another process (IN-process) into the CPU, enabling multiprogramming.

**Step 2 — List the trigger conditions** *(1 mark)*:
- Time slice expiry (timer interrupt).
- Process blocks on I/O.
- Higher-priority process becomes runnable.

**Step 3 — Describe Phase A: Save OUT-process context** *(1.5 marks)*:
The kernel saves all CPU registers (PC, SP, PSW, GPRs, FPU), memory management registers (CR3), and the kernel stack pointer into the OUT-process's PCB. The process is moved from RUNNING to READY (or BLOCKED) state.

**Step 4 — Describe Phase B: Scheduler decision** *(1 mark)*:
The Short-Term Scheduler selects the next process from the Ready Queue using the scheduling algorithm (e.g., Round Robin).

**Step 5 — Describe Phase C: Load IN-process context** *(1.5 marks)*:
The kernel reloads the MMU (CR3 on x86 → TLB flush), restores all CPU registers, switches the kernel stack, and executes an `iret` instruction. The CPU returns to User Mode and resumes the IN-process.

**Step 6 — Differentiate from mode switch** *(1 mark)*:
A mode switch is a transition between User and Kernel modes *without* changing the running process, triggered by system calls or interrupts. It is cheaper (~0.1–1 µs) and does not require saving the full register set. A context switch is a *consequence* of scheduling and always involves a mode switch as a sub-step.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Time quantum $T_{\text{slice}} = 20 \text{ ms}$
- Context switch time $T_{\text{CS}} = 10 \,\mu s = 0.01 \text{ ms}$

**(1) CPU Efficiency** *(2.5 marks)*:

$$E = \frac{T_{\text{slice}}}{T_{\text{slice}} + T_{\text{CS}}} = \frac{20}{20 + 0.01} = \frac{20}{20.01}$$

$$E = 0.9995 = 99.95\%$$

**(2) Throughput Loss Ratio** *(2 marks)*:

$$\eta = \frac{T_{\text{CS}}}{T_{\text{slice}} + T_{\text{CS}}} = \frac{0.01}{20.01}$$

$$\eta = 4.998 \times 10^{-4} = 0.04998\%$$

**Verification:** $E + \eta = 99.95\% + 0.05\% = 100\%$ ✓

**(3) Total overhead in 1 hour** *(2.5 marks)*:

Number of context switches per scheduling cycle = 1 (one switch per quantum).
Context switches per second:

$$n = \frac{1}{T_{\text{slice}}} = \frac{1}{0.02 \text{ s}} = 50 \text{ switches/second}$$

Total overhead per second:

$$T_{\text{overhead/s}} = 50 \times 10 \,\mu s = 500 \,\mu s = 0.5 \text{ ms per second}$$

Total overhead in 1 hour (3600 s):

$$T_{\text{overhead/1hr}} = 3600 \text{ s} \times 0.5 \text{ ms/s} = 1800 \text{ ms} = 1.8 \text{ s}$$

> **Final Answer:** In 1 hour, 1.8 seconds is lost to context switching.

---

### Question B1B (Choice B) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a Process Control Block (PCB)? Explain the role of the PCB during context switching. How does a thread context switch differ from a process context switch?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A system has **3 processes** with I/O wait probabilities of **0.6, 0.5, and 0.4** respectively. Calculate:
1. The probability that the CPU is idle.
2. The CPU utilization.
3. Comment on how the context switch overhead affects this utilization.

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define PCB** *(1.5 marks)*:
The Process Control Block (PCB) is a **kernel data structure** in main memory that stores all information about a process. It is the *identity* and *state record* of a process and is created when the process is created, modified during execution, and destroyed upon termination.

**Step 2 — List PCB contents** *(1 mark)*:
- Process ID, Parent PID, UID/GID
- CPU registers (PC, SP, PSW, GPRs, FPU)
- Memory management info (page tables, segment tables)
- Scheduling info (state, priority, queue pointers)
- I/O status (open files, devices)
- Accounting info (CPU time used, memory limits)

**Step 3 — Role of PCB in context switching** *(2 marks)*:
During a context switch:
- The **OUT-process's** full register state and kernel stack pointer are saved into its PCB.
- The PCB's **state field** is updated (e.g., RUNNING → READY).
- The scheduler selects the **IN-process** and retrieves its PCB.
- The **IN-process's** register state and stack pointer are loaded from its PCB.
- The PCB serves as the *persistent memory* of a process between execution periods.

**Step 4 — Differentiate thread vs. process context switch** *(2.5 marks)*:

| Aspect | Process Context Switch | Thread Context Switch |
|---|---|---|
| **Address Space** | Different — TLB flush required | Same — no TLB flush |
| **Page Table Reload** | Yes (CR3 reload on x86) | No (page table shared) |
| **Kernel Stack** | Different kernel stack | Different kernel stack |
| **User Stack** | Different | Different |
| **Open Files** | Independent | Shared (in same process) |
| **Cost** | High (1–100 µs) | Low (< 1 µs, ~5–10x faster) |

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- 3 processes with I/O wait probabilities $p_1 = 0.6$, $p_2 = 0.5$, $p_3 = 0.4$.

**(1) Probability that the CPU is idle** *(2.5 marks)*:
The CPU is idle only when *all* processes are simultaneously waiting for I/O:

$$P(\text{idle}) = p_1 \times p_2 \times p_3 = 0.6 \times 0.5 \times 0.4$$

$$P(\text{idle}) = 0.12 = 12\%$$

**(2) CPU utilization** *(2 marks)*:

$$U = 1 - P(\text{idle}) = 1 - 0.12 = 0.88 = 88\%$$

**(3) Effect of context switch overhead** *(2.5 marks)*:
The above utilization of 88% is the *theoretical* ideal, assuming instantaneous context switching. In reality:

- Let context switch time be $T_{\text{CS}}$ and average time slice be $T_{\text{slice}}$.
- **Effective utilization** is reduced to:

$$U_{\text{eff}} = U \times \frac{T_{\text{slice}}}{T_{\text{slice}} + T_{\text{CS}}}$$

- If $T_{\text{CS}} = 0.1 \text{ ms}$ and $T_{\text{slice}} = 10 \text{ ms}$:

$$U_{\text{eff}} = 0.88 \times \frac{10}{10.1} = 0.88 \times 0.9901 = 87.13\%$$

- **Net loss:** $88\% - 87.13\% = 0.87\%$ of CPU time is lost to context switching.
- **Implication:** Higher context switch frequency (shorter time slice) or higher per-switch cost significantly degrades real CPU utilization. A well-designed OS balances time quantum size with context switch cost.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Context Switching Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Context Switch with Mode Switch (–2 marks):** Many students write "context switch occurs during a system call". A system call causes a **mode switch**, not a context switch. Context switch *implies* a mode switch but they are not equal.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention PCB (–1 to –2 marks):** Every context switch answer MUST mention the **Process Control Block (PCB)**. Without PCB, there is no "context" to save.
>
> 3. **Skipping the TLB/page table aspect (–1 mark):** When distinguishing process vs. thread switches, students often forget the **MMU reload (CR3 on x86) and TLB flush**. This is a board-favorite point.
>
> 4. **Incorrect unit conversion in numericals (–1 mark):** Mixing µs with ms. Always convert to a common unit first. e.g., $5 \,\mu s = 0.005 \text{ ms}$, not 5 ms.
>
> 5. **Not showing the formula before substitution (–1 mark):** KTU examiners award **method marks**. Always write the formula in symbols first, then substitute values.
>
> 6. **Omitting the diagram in 7-mark sub-parts (–1 mark):** When asked to "explain with a diagram", a diagram is **compulsory**. Even a hand-drawn flow chart earns full marks if labeled correctly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Context Switching**

- **Context Switching** = Save OUT-process state to PCB + Load IN-process state from PCB + Scheduler decision.
- Triggered by: **timer interrupt, I/O blocking, higher-priority wakeup, voluntary yield, termination**.
- The "context" includes: **PC, SP, PSW, GPRs, FPU/SIMD, segment registers, CR3 (page table)**.
- **Process switch** is *heavier* than **thread switch** (TLB flush, page table reload).
- **Mode switch ≠ Context switch** — mode switch is User↔Kernel transition for the *same* process.
- **Context switch time** = $T_{\text{save}} + T_{\text{load}} + T_{\text{scheduler}}$ (typically **1–10 µs** for process, **< 1 µs** for thread).
- **CPU Efficiency** formula: $E = \dfrac{T_{\text{slice}}}{T_{\text{slice}} + T_{\text{CS}}}$.
- **Throughput Loss**: $\eta = 1 - E$.
- **CPU Utilization** for n processes: $U = 1 - p^n$ where $p$ = I/O wait probability.
- **Total overhead** in 1 hour: $T_{\text{overhead}} = \dfrac{3600 \text{ s}}{T_{\text{slice}}} \times T_{\text{CS}}$.
- Context switch cost grows with: **# registers, TLB flush cost, cache pollution, kernel stack copy**.
- Each process has its **own kernel stack** (e.g., 8 KB in Linux), which is part of the saved context.
- KTU **favorite sub-topics**: difference between process and thread context switch; numerical on CPU efficiency; PCB structure diagram.
- KTU **favorite trick question**: "What causes a context switch?" — answer must list **at least 4** triggers for full marks.
- Hardware support: **x86 TSS (Task State Segment)** historically supported hardware context switches, but modern OSes use software-based `switch_to()` for flexibility.
- **Lazy FPU Saving** — an optimization where FPU/SIMD state is saved *only* if the new process actually uses floating-point operations.
- **Address Space ID (ASID)** in modern CPUs (e.g., ARM, RISC-V) reduces TLB flush overhead by tagging TLB entries with process IDs.
---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Boot Sequence; Case Study: Linux Kernel Process Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** System Boot Sequence; Case Study: Linux Kernel Process Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The System Boot Sequence — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Strict Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **System Boot Sequence** (also called **Bootstrapping** or **Bootstrapping the System**) is the formally defined, deterministic sequence of low-level operations executed automatically by the firmware and software of a computer system, starting immediately after power-on or reset, and culminating in a fully initialized Operating System kernel ready to accept user commands or execute user-level applications.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, the boot sequence is broken into six well-defined phases: **Power-On → POST → BIOS/UEFI Initialization → Bootloader Stage → Kernel Loading → User-Space Initialization (init/systemd)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Waking Up a Heavy Sleeper"

Imagine a person who has been in a deep coma (the powered-off computer). To make him functional:
1. **Electric shock / Wake-up call** = Pressing the power button (electricity flows).
2. **Self-diagnosis** = He checks his own eyes, ears, heart (POST — Power-On Self-Test).
3. **Looking for instructions** = He checks his diary for today's schedule (BIOS/UEFI looks for a bootable device).
4. **Waking the boss (the OS Kernel)** = He calls his manager (Bootloader loads the kernel into RAM).
5. **Manager organizing the team** = The manager assigns tasks to workers (`init`/`systemd` starts services).
6. **Ready to serve** = He opens the office door for customers (Login prompt appears).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Boot sequence questions almost always test the **order of components** (BIOS vs MBR vs GRUB vs Kernel vs init) and the **role of each stage**. Memorize the chain: **Power → POST → BIOS/UEFI → MBR/GPT → Bootloader (GRUB) → Kernel → init/systemd → Login Shell**.

---

## 1.2 Linux Kernel Process Management — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:**
> **Process Management in the Linux Kernel** is the set of kernel subsystems, data structures (`task_struct`, run-queues, scheduling classes), and algorithms (fork/exec/clone, CFS scheduler, signal delivery) responsible for creating, scheduling, terminating, and inter-process communication of all executing programs (processes/threads) in the system.

The Linux kernel is **monolithic**, but its process management subsystem is modular within the kernel. Every running entity — whether a user application, a system daemon, or a kernel thread — is represented uniformly by a kernel data structure called the **Process Descriptor** (`struct task_struct` in `<linux/sched.h>`).

### Conceptual Analogy — "A Massive Corporate Office"

Think of the Linux kernel as the **HR Department of a giant office building**:
- **Every employee** (process) has a **personnel file** (`task_struct`) containing name, ID, current task, supervisor, salary, sick leaves, etc.
- **HR assigns IDs** — the **PID (Process Identifier)** is like the **Employee ID number**.
- **The Operations Manager** (the **Scheduler**) decides who works on which floor and for how long.
- **When a new project comes in**, HR doesn't recruit from scratch — it **clones an existing employee** (`fork()`) and gives them a new project briefing (`exec()`).
- **When an employee retires**, HR processes their exit (`exit()`) and keeps a final record (**Zombie process**), waiting for the supervisor (`parent`) to acknowledge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Linux fact KTU loves to test:** Linux does **not** distinguish between processes and threads internally — both are simply `task_struct` entries that may or may not share resources. This is why `clone()` is the **primitive** system call, and `fork()` / `pthread_create()` are **higher-level wrappers** around it.

---

## 1.3 Important Standards & Metrics

- **Standard BIOS Boot Time Target:** Under **1.5 seconds** for POST (modern UEFI is even faster).
- **Typical Linux PID Range:** **1 to 32768** (default `pid_max` in older kernels) or up to **4194304** (2²²) in modern kernels.
- **Default Linux Scheduler:** **CFS — Completely Fair Scheduler** (since kernel 2.6.23, year 2007).
- **PID 1** in Linux is always **`/sbin/init`** (or `/lib/systemd/systemd` in modern distros) — the **mother of all processes**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Six Phases of the System Boot Sequence (KTU High-Yield)

### Phase 1 — Power-On & Power-On Self-Test (POST)

When the user presses the power button, the **Power Supply Unit (PSU)** delivers stable DC voltages to the motherboard. The **CPU is reset** to a well-defined state (all registers zeroed, instruction pointer = `0xFFFFFFF0` in real-mode for legacy BIOS).

The firmware (chip on motherboard) executes a **POST routine** that checks:
- RAM integrity (memory test).
- CPU registers and basic arithmetic.
- Presence of keyboard, mouse, display adapter.
- Storage controllers (SATA/NVMe).

If POST fails, the system emits **beep codes** (e.g., 3 short beeps = memory failure).

### Phase 2 — BIOS / UEFI Firmware Initialization

Once POST succeeds, the firmware initializes hardware devices and looks for a bootable device based on the **boot priority order** stored in **CMOS/Non-Volatile RAM**.

- **BIOS (Basic Input/Output System):** Legacy 16-bit firmware, uses **MBR** partitioning, max disk **2 TB**, runs in **Real Mode**.
- **UEFI (Unified Extensible Firmware Interface):** Modern 32/64-bit firmware, uses **GPT** partitioning, supports disks **> 2 TB**, runs in **Protected/Long Mode**, supports **Secure Boot**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Point:** The firmware reads the first sector (**sector 0**, size **512 bytes**) of the boot device into RAM at physical address **`0x7C00`** and transfers control there. This is the **MBR (Master Boot Record)**.

### Phase 3 — The Bootloader Stage (GRUB / LILO / systemd-boot)

The 512-byte MBR contains:
- **446 bytes** — Stage 1 bootloader code (very small, fits in 446 bytes).
- **64 bytes** — Partition table (4 entries × 16 bytes each).
- **2 bytes** — Magic number `0x55AA` (signature).

Because 446 bytes is too small for a full bootloader, MBR loads **Stage 1.5** (which understands the filesystem) from the small gap between MBR and the first partition. Stage 1.5 then loads **Stage 2** (`/boot/grub/`) which presents the **GRUB menu** to the user.

The user picks a kernel image (e.g., `vmlinuz-6.5.0`) and an **initramfs/initrd** (initial RAM filesystem). The bootloader:
1. Loads the kernel image into RAM.
2. Decompresses it (kernel is stored compressed).
3. Loads the `initramfs` (a tiny root filesystem containing essential drivers).
4. Passes boot parameters and the path to `init` to the kernel.
5. Jumps to the kernel's entry point.

### Phase 4 — Kernel Initialization (Linux Kernel Boot)

The Linux kernel executes `start_kernel()` in `init/main.c`, which:
- Sets up memory management (page tables, zones, slabs).
- Initializes the scheduler (CFS).
- Initializes the VFS (Virtual File System).
- Mounts the **root filesystem** (using the `initramfs` temporarily, then pivots to the real root).
- Starts the first user-space process — **PID 1** — typically **`/sbin/init`** or **`/lib/systemd/systemd`**.

### Phase 5 — User-Space Initialization (`init` / `systemd`)

PID 1 is the **ancestor of all user-space processes**. It is responsible for:
- Reading its configuration file (`/etc/inittab` for SysV init, or systemd unit files in `/lib/systemd/system/`).
- Starting system services in a defined order (network, logging, cron, etc.).
- Bringing the system to a default **runlevel/target** (e.g., `multi-user.target` or `graphical.target`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:**
> - **SysV init:** Uses **runlevels 0–6** (0=halt, 1=single-user, 3=multi-user text, 5=graphical, 6=reboot).
> - **systemd:** Uses **targets** (`poweroff.target`, `rescue.target`, `multi-user.target`, `graphical.target`).
> systemd is **event-driven and parallel**; SysV init is **sequential and shell-script based**.

### Phase 6 — Login & User Session

A **getty** process (or systemd's `autovt@` service) starts on each terminal, displays a login prompt, and after authentication, spawns the user's **shell** (`/bin/bash`). The system is now fully operational.

---

## 2.2 Linux Kernel Process Management — The Theoretical Core

### 2.2.1 The Process Descriptor — `task_struct`

The `task_struct` (defined in `include/linux/sched.h`) is a massive C structure (over **300 fields** in modern kernels). Key fields include:

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `pid` | Unique process identifier (positive integer). |
| `tgid` | Thread Group ID — leader of a thread group has `tgid == pid`. |
| `state` | Current state of the process (see Section 2.2.3). |
| `priority` / `rt_priority` | Static and real-time priorities. |
| `sched_entity` | The CFS scheduling entity embedded inside the task. |
| `mm` | Pointer to memory descriptor (page tables, open VMAs). |
| `files` | Pointer to the file descriptor table. |
| `signal` | Pending signal mask. |
| `parent` | Pointer to parent's `task_struct` (forming a process tree). |
| `children` | List of child processes. |
| `comm[TASK_COMM_LEN]` | Executable name (max **16 chars** including null). |
| `exit_code` | Exit status when terminated. |
| `nsproxy` | Pointer to namespace proxy (PID/net/mount namespaces). |

The kernel maintains **`pid_hash` hash tables** for O(1) lookup from PID to `task_struct`. The actual list of all tasks is the famous **doubly-circular linked list** rooted at `init_task` (the static process descriptor for PID 0 — the **idle task** / **swapper**).

> [!NOTE]
> **`init_task` is PID 0**, a special kernel-thread that does nothing except run when the CPU is idle. It is **statically allocated at compile time** — it is the ancestor of every other `task_struct`.

### 2.2.2 Process Creation — The `fork()` / `exec()` / `wait()` Family

Linux process creation is the famous **"fork-and-exec" two-step model**:

1. **`fork()`** — Creates a **child process** that is an **almost-exact duplicate** of the parent. The child gets a new PID, its own `task_struct`, but shares file descriptors (via copy-on-write for memory). The system call returns:
   - In the **parent**: the **child's PID** (a positive integer).
   - In the **child**: **0**.
   - On error: a **negative** value (e.g., `-EAGAIN` if process limit reached).

2. **`execve()`** — Replaces the current process image with a **new executable**. The PID stays the same; the memory map, registers, and stack are replaced. On success, it does **not return**; on error, it returns `-1`.

3. **`wait()` / `waitpid()`** — Parent blocks (or polls) until a child terminates, then reaps the child's exit status. This is what **prevents zombies from accumulating**.

4. **`exit()`** — Terminates the calling process, releases resources, and transitions to the **ZOMBIE** state until the parent calls `wait()`.

The actual underlying syscall is **`clone()`** — `fork()` and threads are just `clone()` with different flags (`CLONE_NEWPID`, `CLONE_NEWNS`, `CLONE_SIGHAND`, `CLONE_VM` for threads, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often think `fork()` creates two separate copies of memory. **Incorrect!** Linux uses **Copy-On-Write (COW)** — the pages are shared until either process writes to them, at which point the kernel duplicates only the modified page. This makes `fork()` extremely fast.

### 2.2.3 Process States in Linux

The Linux kernel uses a state machine. Defined in `<linux/sched.h>`:

| State | Macro | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Running | `TASK_RUNNING` | Either currently executing on a CPU **OR** ready to run (in run-queue). |
| Interruptible Sleep | `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE` | Sleeping but can be woken by signals. |
| Uninterruptible Sleep | `TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE` | Sleeping, **cannot** be woken by signals. Used in critical I/O (e.g., NFS, device drivers). |
| Stopped | `TASK_STOPPED` | Stopped by `SIGSTOP` (or job control). |
| Traced | `TASK_TRACED` | Being debugged (ptrace). |
| Zombie | `EXIT_ZOMBIE` | Terminated, waiting for parent to reap. |
| Dead | `EXIT_DEAD` | Final state during `wait()` cleanup. |

### 2.2.4 The Scheduler — CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler)

The default Linux scheduler is **CFS**, a **fair-share, red-black-tree based** scheduler (replaced the older O(1) scheduler in kernel 2.6.23).

Key ideas:
- Each process has a **virtual runtime** (`vruntime`).
- The run-queue is a **red-black tree** ordered by `vruntime`.
- The leftmost node (smallest `vruntime`) is chosen to run next.
- A **nice value** (`-20` to `+19`) weights the runtime — **lower nice = higher priority = slower vruntime accumulation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-time processes** (using `SCHED_FIFO` or `SCHED_RR` policies) **preempt CFS** — they are scheduled by a separate, higher-priority mechanism.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** No vertical bars `|` are used inside the following table cells; all delimiter symbols are written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Process Address Space (virtual) | $V = 2^{n}$ where $n$ = address bits | bytes (e.g., $V = 2^{64}$ on 64-bit Linux) |
| Maximum PID | $\text{pid\_max} = 2^{22} = 4{,}194{,}304$ | Default in modern Linux kernels |
| CFS virtual runtime weight | $w_{i} = \frac{1024}{1.25^{\text{nice}}}$ | dimensionless |
| CFS vruntime update | $v_{i}(t+\Delta t) = v_{i}(t) + \frac{\Delta t}{w_{i}}$ | nanoseconds |
| Time slice assigned | $\Delta t_{i} = \Delta t_{\text{target}} \cdot \frac{w_{i}}{\sum_{j} w_{j}}$ | nanoseconds |
| Process tree PID uniqueness | $\forall\, p,q \in \text{Tasks} : p \neq q \Rightarrow p.\text{pid} \neq q.\text{pid}$ | constraint |
| Copy-on-Write savings | $\text{Saved Memory} = \sum \text{shared read-only pages}$ | pages × 4 KiB |
| ZOMBIE limit safety | If parent dies, init (PID 1) adopts the orphan | via `task_struct->parent` re-parenting |
| MBR load address | $\text{addr} = \texttt{0x7C00}$ | fixed physical RAM address |
| MBR signature | $\texttt{0x55AA}$ | last 2 bytes of sector 0 |
| POST memory test | $T_{\text{POST}} \approx C \cdot M$ | where $C$ = constant, $M$ = RAM in MiB |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded systems** (Raspberry Pi, automotive ECUs) often skip BIOS and use **U-Boot** or **Direct Kernel Boot**, but the principles are identical.
- **Containerization (Docker, Kubernetes)** relies entirely on Linux process management: `clone()` with `CLONE_NEWPID` creates a PID namespace; `CLONE_NEWNS` gives mount isolation; `CLONE_NEWNET` gives network isolation.
- **System boot performance** is a key engineering metric — modern UEFI + systemd parallelizes service startup, reducing boot time from minutes (old SysV) to under **5 seconds** in optimized distros.
- **Forensics and incident response**: The Linux process tree (`pstree`, `/proc/<pid>/status`) is the first artifact examined.

---

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Detailed Boot Sequence — Step-by-Step Logical Flow

The following enumeration is the **canonical KTU 14-mark answer** for boot sequence questions. Every step must be **named, explained, and connected to the next**.

**Step 1: Power-On Reset (POR)**
The PSU delivers +12V, +5V, +3.3V rails. The CPU's **RESET pin** is asserted; all registers are cleared, CS:IP is set to physical address `0xFFFFFFF0` (16 bytes below 4 GiB), which is the firmware reset vector.

**Step 2: Firmware Execution**
The 16-bit code at `0xFFFFFFF0` (jumps to actual firmware) begins the **POST (Power-On Self-Test)**:
- CPU register self-test.
- BIOS ROM checksum verification.
- RAM sizing and read/write test.
- Video adapter initialization (so we can see output).
- Detection of keyboard, mouse, storage controllers.

**Step 3: Boot Device Selection**
The firmware consults the **boot order** stored in CMOS NVRAM (configured via BIOS Setup). It then issues a **BIOS interrupt `INT 13h`** (legacy) or UEFI **Boot Services** call to read **sector 0** of the selected device into RAM at address `0x7C00`.

**Step 4: MBR / GPT Stage**
- In legacy BIOS: The 512-byte sector is checked for the magic number `0x55AA` at offset `510–511`. If valid, the CPU jumps to `0x7C00` and runs the stage-1 bootloader (446 bytes of code).
- In UEFI: The firmware reads the **EFI System Partition (ESP)** (a FAT32 partition) and locates the `.efi` bootloader file (e.g., `\EFI\ubuntu\grubx64.efi`).

**Step 5: Bootloader Stage 1.5 and Stage 2 (GRUB example)**
Stage 1 (446 bytes) loads **Stage 1.5** from the gap between MBR and the first partition (~30 KiB). Stage 1.5 contains filesystem drivers and loads **Stage 2** from `/boot/grub/`. Stage 2 displays the GRUB menu and reads its config (`grub.cfg`).

**Step 6: Kernel and initramfs Loading**
GRUB loads the **kernel image** (`vmlinuz-X.Y.Z`) — a self-extracting compressed image (gzip, bzip2, lz4, zstd, or xz) — and the **initramfs/initrd** (a cpio archive optionally compressed). It places them in RAM and jumps to the kernel's `arch/x86/boot/header.S:start` entry point.

**Step 7: Kernel Decompression and `start_kernel()`**
The decompressor (`arch/x86/boot/compressed/misc.c`) decompresses the kernel into its final location. The actual C entry point is `start_kernel()` in `init/main.c`, which calls (in order):
- `setup_arch()` — architecture-specific setup.
- `mm_init()` — memory management initialization.
- `sched_init()` — scheduler initialization (creates `init_task`, sets up run-queues, registers CFS).
- `vfs_init()` — VFS caches and filesystems.
- `signals_init()`, `proc_init()`, etc.
- Finally, `rest_init()` which spawns **PID 1** via `kernel_thread(kernel_init, NULL, ...)`.

**Step 8: PID 1 (init/systemd) Takes Over**
`kernel_init()` (the function executed by PID 1) calls:
- `prepare_namespace()` — mounts the real root filesystem (using initramfs to find it if needed, then `pivot_root`).
- `run_init_process("/sbin/init")` or `"/lib/systemd/systemd"`.

Now user-space is alive, and the kernel is fully operational.

**Step 9: System Services Startup**
PID 1 reads its configuration and starts the rest of the user-space tree:
- systemd: starts units in **parallel** based on dependencies.
- SysV init: runs scripts in `/etc/rcN.d/` sequentially.

**Step 10: Login Prompt**
A **getty** is spawned on each virtual terminal (`/dev/tty1`–`/dev/tty6` traditionally). After successful authentication, the user's shell is started. **Boot complete.**

---

## 3.2 Linux Process Creation — Mathematical/Logical Derivation

Let us trace the lifecycle of a process created by `fork()` from a parent with PID $P_p$:

**Step 1: `fork()` syscall entered.**
The kernel allocates a new `task_struct` for the child. Let its address be $T_c$.

**Step 2: PID Assignment**
A new unique PID is allocated:
$$P_c = \text{alloc\_pid}(\text{pid\_namespace})$$

This is done by allocating the next free slot in the PID bitmap and incrementing `last_pid`. If the namespace is full, return `-EAGAIN`.

**Step 3: Copy the parent's `task_struct`**
A new $T_c$ is created as a **shallow copy** of $T_p$, then specific fields are overridden:
- $T_c.\text{pid} = P_c$
- $T_c.\text{parent} = T_p$
- $T_c.\text{real\_parent} = T_p$
- $T_c.\text{children}$ is initialized as an empty list head
- $T_c.\text{sibling}$ is added to $T_p.\text{children}$ list
- $T_c.\text{state} = \text{TASK\_RUNNING}$

**Step 4: Memory — Copy-on-Write Setup**
The child's `mm_struct` is duplicated, but **all PTEs (Page Table Entries) are marked read-only** in both parent and child. The underlying physical pages are shared. A page fault on write will trigger COW duplication.

If $M$ is the total memory footprint of the parent and $\alpha \in [0,1]$ is the fraction of pages written by either process within time $t$:
$$\text{Actual memory used} = M + \alpha \cdot M = M(1+\alpha)$$

So immediately after `fork()`, $\alpha \approx 0$, and the savings approach $M$.

**Step 5: File Descriptor Table**
Linux uses one of three strategies based on the `clone()` flags:
- `CLONE_FILES` not set → **Deep copy** of `files_struct` (each process gets its own FD table, but they share underlying file descriptions via reference counts).
- `CLONE_FILES` set → **Share** the parent's `files_struct` (used for threads).

**Step 6: Return from `fork()`**
- The parent process is resumed with the return value $P_c$.
- The child is placed on the run-queue. When it is scheduled, it resumes **at the same point in user-space code** but with return value $0$.

This is achieved by setting the child's `pt_regs->ax` (on x86) to $0$ before returning to user space.

**Step 7: `execve()` (optional)**
If the child calls `execve(path, argv, envp)`:
- The old memory map is destroyed (all VMAs freed).
- The new ELF binary is loaded: `do_execve()` → `search_binary_handler()` → `load_elf_binary()`.
- The new `mm_struct` is built, stack is set up, `pt_regs` is filled with the entry point and arguments.
- The PID **does not change**.

**Step 8: Termination and Reaping**
When the child calls `exit(status)`:
- Its `task_struct` is moved to **EXIT_ZOMBIE** state.
- The exit status is stored in $T_c.\text{exit\_code}$.
- The parent is sent `SIGCHLD`.
- The child is reparented to PID 1 if its parent has already died.

When the parent calls `waitpid(P_c, &status, 0)`:
- The kernel reads $T_c.\text{exit\_code}$, writes it to `status`, and finally frees $T_c$.
- The child transitions to **EXIT_DEAD** and is removed from the process list.

---

## 3.3 CFS Scheduler — A Numerical Worked Example

Let us derive the time slice allocation for a CFS run-queue with **3 processes** A, B, C having nice values $0$, $5$, and $10$ respectively. Assume a target latency of $T_L = 20$ ms and a minimum granularity of $\delta = 1$ ms.

**Step 1: Compute the weights.**
The CFS weight formula is:
$$w_{i} = \frac{1024}{1.25^{\text{nice}}}$$

For nice = 0:
$$w_A = \frac{1024}{1.25^{0}} = \frac{1024}{1} = 1024$$

For nice = 5:
$$w_B = \frac{1024}{1.25^{5}} = \frac{1024}{3.0517578125} \approx 335.54$$

For nice = 10:
$$w_C = \frac{1024}{1.25^{10}} = \frac{1024}{9.313225746} \approx 109.95$$

**Step 2: Compute the total weight.**
$$W = w_A + w_B + w_C = 1024 + 335.54 + 109.95 \approx 1469.49$$

**Step 3: Compute each process's time slice.**
The general formula is:
$$\Delta t_{i} = T_L \cdot \frac{w_{i}}{W}$$

For A:
$$\Delta t_A = 20\,\text{ms} \cdot \frac{1024}{1469.49} \approx 13.94\,\text{ms}$$

For B:
$$\Delta t_B = 20\,\text{ms} \cdot \frac{335.54}{1469.49} \approx 4.57\,\text{ms}$$

For C:
$$\Delta t_C = 20\,\text{ms} \cdot \frac{109.95}{1469.49} \approx 1.50\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 4: Apply minimum granularity check.**
Since $1.50$ ms $\geq \delta = 1$ ms, the minimum granularity constraint is **satisfied**. If we had 100 processes, some would be capped at $1$ ms and the target latency $T_L$ would be expanded dynamically.

**Step 5: Verify sum.**
$$\Delta t_A + \Delta t_B + \Delta t_C \approx 13.94 + 4.57 + 1.50 = 20.01\,\text{ms} \approx T_L \quad \blacksquare$$

**Observation:** The lower the nice value, the larger the share. A process with nice = 0 gets $\approx 13$ times more CPU than a nice = 10 process.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — KTU 14-Mark Favorites

| Feature | Legacy BIOS Boot | UEFI Boot |
|---|---|---|
| Firmware mode | 16-bit real mode | 32/64-bit protected/long mode |
| Partition scheme | MBR (max 2 TiB) | GPT (max 8 ZiB) |
| Boot code location | MBR (512 bytes) | EFI System Partition (FAT32) |
| Bootloader file | Stage-1 binary | `.efi` PE/COFF executable |
| Secure Boot | Not supported | Supported (signed binaries) |
| Initialization speed | Slow | Fast (parallel init) |

| Feature | SysV init | systemd |
|---|---|---|
| Init type | Shell scripts | Unit files (declarative) |
| Parallelism | No (sequential) | Yes (dependency-based) |
| Default runlevel | 0–6 | Targets (e.g., `multi-user.target`) |
| Process supervision | No | Yes (auto-restart) |
| Socket activation | No | Yes |
| cgroup integration | Manual | Native |

| Feature | `fork()` | `exec()` | `clone()` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Creates new PID? | Yes | No (replaces image) | Optional (`CLONE_NEWPID`) |
| Creates new memory? | No (COW) | Yes (loads new binary) | Configurable |
| Used for threads? | Indirectly (via `pthread_create`) | No | Yes (primary primitive) |
| Address space sharing | COW | Replaces entirely | Configurable (`CLONE_VM`) |

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Linux Process Lifecycle and States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created : fork or clone syscall
    Created --> Ready : task_struct allocated placed in runqueue
    Ready --> Running : scheduler picks leftmost in red black tree CFS
    Running --> Ready : time slice exhausted preempted
    Running --> Interruptible : sleep on wait queue INTERRUPTIBLE
    Running --> Uninterruptible : critical IO block UNINTERRUPTIBLE
    Interruptible --> Ready : signal received or event occurs
    Uninterruptible --> Ready : IO completes only
    Running --> Stopped : receives SIGSTOP
    Stopped --> Ready : receives SIGCONT
    Running --> Zombie : exit syscall called
    Zombie --> Dead : parent calls wait or waitpid and reaps
    Dead --> [*]
    note right of Zombie : task_struct still allocated waiting for parent reaping
    note right of Uninterruptible : Cannot be killed by signals used in device drivers
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Process Tree and PID Re-parenting on Orphan

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PID 1 init or systemd] --> B[PID 100 shell]
    B --> C[PID 250 child A]
    B --> D[PID 251 child B]
    C --> E[PID 300 grandchild]
    C --> F[PID 301 grandchild]
    D --> G[PID 302 grandchild]
    H[Parent PID 250 dies EXIT_DEAD] --> I[Kernel reparents PID 300 and PID 301]
    I --> A
    J[Parent PID 1 dies - cannot] --> K[Kernel panic if not in PID namespace]
```
