## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Differentiate between user-level threads and kernel-level threads.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **User-level threads (ULT):** Managed entirely by a user-space thread library (e.g., POSIX `pthread` on the user side). The kernel is unaware of their existence. Thread creation, scheduling, and synchronization happen without kernel intervention — making them fast but unable to leverage multiprocessor parallelism.
- **Kernel-level threads (KLT):** Supported and managed directly by the OS kernel. Each KLT is a schedulable entity. They can run in parallel on multicore systems, and a blocking call in one KLT does not block the entire process.

| Criteria | ULT | KLT |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Managed by | Thread library | Kernel |
| Creation cost | Low | High |
| Parallelism | Limited | Full |

---

**Q2. List any three advantages of the Many-to-Many multithreading model over the One-to-One model.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Greater flexibility in thread count** — Developers can spawn a very large number of user threads without worrying about kernel resource limits, since they are multiplexed onto a smaller pool of kernel threads.
2. **Lower overhead per thread** — Most user-thread operations do not require kernel intervention, reducing syscall overhead.
3. **Efficient kernel resource utilization** — The number of kernel threads (LWPs) can be tuned to match the available CPU cores, avoiding over-subscription and thrashing.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Module question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** between two questions of equal difficulty. Each question typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Pattern]**

**Q. (a) Explain the three multithreading models with neat diagrams. Compare their advantages and limitations.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The three multithreading models define how user-level threads (ULTs) are mapped to kernel-level threads (KLTs).

**1. Many-to-One Model (M:1):**
- Many user threads are mapped to a single kernel thread.
- Thread management is in user space. The kernel is unaware of user threads.
- **Advantage:** Thread operations are fast (no system call).
- **Limitation:** The entire process blocks on a single blocking system call. No parallelism on multicore.

**2. One-to-One Model (1:1):**
- Each user thread maps to one kernel thread.
- Provides true concurrency and parallelism.
- **Advantage:** A blocking call in one thread does not affect others.
- **Limitation:** Kernel-thread creation is expensive; limits total thread count.

**3. Many-to-Many Model (M:N):**
- Multiplexes M user threads onto N kernel threads (M ≥ N).
- Combines M:1's fast user-thread creation with 1:1's kernel-level parallelism.
- **Advantage:** Flexible, scalable, and efficient resource use.
- **Limitation:** Complex implementation; requires coordination between user library and kernel scheduler.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | M:1 | 1:1 | M:N |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|
| Parallelism on multicore | No | Yes | Yes |
| Thread creation cost | Low | High | Moderate |
| Blocking call effect | Whole process | One thread | One thread |
| Examples | Green threads | Linux, Windows | Solaris |

**Diagrams:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 – 4.4 of these notes for clean Mermaid diagrams.)*

---

**Q. (b) With a neat diagram, explain how Linux implements threads using the `clone()` system call. What resources are shared and what are unique per thread?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Linux implements the **One-to-One (1:1) threading model** natively. A Linux "thread" is essentially a *lightweight process* created via the `clone()` system call.

**The `clone()` System Call:**
- `clone()` is a generalized version of `fork()`. While `fork()` creates a copy of the entire process, `clone()` allows the parent to specify **which resources to share** with the child via flag arguments:
  - `CLONE_VM` — share memory (address space)
  - `CLONE_FILES` — share file descriptor table
  - `CLONE_SIGHAND` — share signal handlers
  - `CLONE_THREAD` — place the child in the same thread group

- The `pthread_create()` function in glibc's NPTL (Native POSIX Thread Library) wraps `clone()` with these flags set.

**Resources Shared (across threads of the same process):**
- Address space (`mm_struct`)
- File descriptor table (`files_struct`)
- Signal handlers (`sighand_struct`)
- Working directory, umask, resource limits
- Thread Group ID (TGID — same as the main thread's PID)

**Resources Unique (per thread):**
- Kernel stack
- Thread ID (TID)
- Program counter, registers (saved in `task_struct`)
- Thread-local storage (TLS)
- Signal mask
- CPU affinity

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.6 — Block Diagram of Linux `task_struct` sharing.)*

**Significance:** Because each thread is a fully independent kernel schedulable entity, the Linux kernel scheduler treats them on equal footing with processes, enabling efficient SMP (Symmetric Multiprocessing) utilization.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a) Compare Many-to-One, One-to-One, and Many-to-Many threading models. Which model does modern Linux use and why?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | M:1 | 1:1 | M:N |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| Mapping | Many ULTs to 1 KLT | 1 ULT to 1 KLT | M ULTs to N KLTs (M ≥ N) |
| Thread mgmt | User library | Kernel | User + Kernel |
| Parallelism | None on multicore | Full | Limited to N cores |
| Blocking call | Process-wide blocking | Per-thread | Per-thread |
| Creation cost | Very low | High | Low to moderate |
| OS examples | Green threads | Linux, Windows | Solaris (pre-9) |

**Modern Linux uses the One-to-One (1:1) Model** for the following reasons:
1. **True parallelism:** Linux supports multiprocessor/multicore systems. The 1:1 model allows each thread to execute on a separate CPU core, which is critical for compute-intensive applications.
2. **Simplicity:** The kernel can directly schedule each thread as a `task_struct`, with no complex user-kernel scheduling coordination.
3. **No head-of-line blocking:** A blocking I/O call in one thread does not stall other threads.
4. **Efficient kernel support:** The `clone()` syscall provides a clean, efficient mechanism for thread creation with fine-grained resource-sharing control.

> Trade-off: Linux's 1:1 model incurs higher per-thread creation cost, but this is acceptable on modern hardware.

---

**Q. (b) A multithreaded web server uses 8 threads to handle client requests on a 4-core machine. The parallelizable fraction of the workload is 0.90. Compute the effective speedup under (i) Many-to-One, and (ii) One-to-One threading models using Amdahl's Law.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Total user threads: $T = 8$
- CPU cores: $C = 4$
- Parallel fraction: $P = 0.90$
- Serial fraction: $(1 - P) = 0.10$

**Amdahl's Law Formula:**

$$
\text{Speedup}(E) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \frac{P}{E}}
$$

where $E$ is the effective degree of parallelism.

---

**(i) Many-to-One Model:**

In M:1, the entire process uses only one kernel thread regardless of $C$ or $T$.

$$
E_{M:1} = 1
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedup}_{M:1} &= \frac{1}{0.10 + \frac{0.90}{1}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.90} \\
&= \frac{1}{1.0} \\
&= 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Despite having 4 cores and 8 threads, the server achieves **no speedup** under M:1 because only one core is ever used.

---

**(ii) One-to-One Model:**

In 1:1, each thread maps to one kernel thread; effective parallelism is limited by the smaller of threads and cores.

$$
E_{1:1} = \min(T, C) = \min(8, 4) = 4
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedup}_{1:1} &= \frac{1}{0.10 + \frac{0.90}{4}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.225} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.325} \\
&\approx 3.08
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The 1:1 model achieves a speedup of **~3.08×**, using all 4 cores in parallel.

---

**Conclusion:** The One-to-One model delivers significantly better performance on multicore hardware, which is why modern Linux uses it as the default threading model.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the serial fraction $(1 - P)$** in Amdahl's Law — students often write only $\frac{P}{E}$ and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing TGID with TID** in Linux thread questions — TGID is shared (same as the process PID), TID is unique per thread.
> 3. **Stating "Linux uses M:1"** — this is a **fatal** conceptual error. Linux is a pure 1:1 implementation. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not drawing a diagram** in the 7-mark comparison question — the KTU marking scheme allocates **1 mark** for the figure.
> 5. **Mixing up "kernel thread" with "LWP"** — in Linux, the two are essentially the same; in Solaris, they are distinct. Be precise.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before Your Exam!**

- ✅ A **thread** is a lightweight unit of execution; it shares its process's resources but has its own stack, registers, and PC.
- ✅ A **multithreading model** is the *mapping rule* from user threads to kernel threads.
- ✅ **Many-to-One (M:1):** Many ULTs → 1 KLT. User-space management. **No parallel execution**. Cheap to create. **Disadvantage:** Whole process blocks on a single blocking call.
- ✅ **One-to-One (1:1):** 1 ULT → 1 KLT. **True parallelism**. **Disadvantage:** Kernel-thread creation is expensive; threads per process are limited by OS resources.
- ✅ **Many-to-Many (M:N):** M ULTs → N KLTs (M ≥ N). **Best of both worlds** — flexible user threads + kernel parallelism. **Disadvantage:** Complex implementation.
- ✅ **Two-Level Model:** Variant of M:N where selected threads are **permanently bound** to a specific LWP (useful for real-time I/O).
- ✅ **Linux Threading Model = 1:1**, implemented using the **`clone()`** system call with flags like `CLONE_VM`, `CLONE_FILES`, `CLONE_THREAD`.
- ✅ Linux threads are represented as **`task_struct`** entries in the kernel. Threads of a process share `mm_struct` (memory) and `files_struct` (file table).
- ✅ **TGID (Thread Group ID) is shared** among all threads of a process; each thread has a **unique TID**.
- ✅ **NPTL (Native POSIX Thread Library)** is the modern Linux thread library, replacing the older LinuxThreads implementation.
- ✅ Use **Amdahl's Law** to quantitatively compare models: $\text{Speedup}(E) = \frac{1}{(1-P) + P/E}$.
- ✅ For a process with `M` user threads and `N` kernel threads on `C` cores, **effective parallelism** = $\min(N, C)$.
- ✅ **Killer question pattern (KTU Favourite):** "Why does Linux prefer 1:1 over M:N despite the higher creation cost?" → Answer: simplicity, true parallelism, kernel scheduler integration.
- ✅ Always draw a **diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts. It earns you an **easy mark**.

> **Final Tip:** When in doubt, structure your answer as **Definition → Diagram → Properties Table → Real-world Example → Drawback**. KTU examiners reward **structured, well-labeled answers** with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Scheduling: Concepts and Basic Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Scheduling: Concepts and Basic Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Scheduling** is the activity of the operating system that decides which of the ready processes in the ready queue is to be allocated the CPU when the CPU becomes idle. It is a fundamental function of the **Short-Term Scheduler (CPU Scheduler)** and is the core mechanism by which a multiprogrammed OS achieves maximum system throughput, fairness, and responsiveness.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, process scheduling is treated as a critical sub-system that:

- Selects **one** process from the ready queue
- Allocates the **CPU core** to it
- Manages **context switches** between processes
- Balances competing objectives of **system efficiency** and **user satisfaction**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Runway Controller

Imagine an airport with **one runway** but **ten planes** waiting to land. The **Air Traffic Controller (ATC)** must decide:

- Which plane lands first?
- Should a small emergency plane preempt a big commercial jet?
- How long should each plane occupy the runway?

The ATC is the **Scheduler**, the runway is the **CPU**, the planes are **Processes**, and the time spent on the runway is the **CPU Burst Time**. Just like an ATC, the OS scheduler must use a well-defined policy to avoid starvation, minimize average delay, and ensure safety.

> [!NOTE]
> **Starvation (Infinite Postponement):** A condition in which a process is ready to run but waits indefinitely because other processes keep getting the CPU. A good scheduling algorithm must prevent or bound this.

### 1.3 Classification of Schedulers

The OS has three different schedulers, each operating at a different time scale:

| Scheduler Type | Also Known As | Frequency | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Long-Term Scheduler** | Admission / Job Scheduler | Very rare (seconds to minutes) | Controls the **degree of multiprogramming** by selecting which jobs from the disk are admitted into the ready queue |
| **Short-Term Scheduler** | **CPU Scheduler** | Very frequent (milliseconds) | Selects **which ready process** runs on the CPU next — the heart of process scheduling |
| **Medium-Term Scheduler** | Swapper | Intermediate | Temporarily swaps out inactive processes to reduce degree of multiprogramming and free memory |

### 1.4 Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive Scheduling

This is a **high-yield KTU topic** appearing almost every semester:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Preemptive (Co-operative) Scheduling:** Once the CPU is allocated to a process, it holds it until it **terminates** or **voluntarily switches to waiting state** (e.g., I/O). No interruption mid-execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preemptive Scheduling:** The scheduler can **forcibly take the CPU** away from a running process after a fixed time quantum or when a higher-priority process arrives. Modern OS (Windows, Linux, macOS) use preemptive scheduling.

### 1.5 Scheduling Criteria — The Five Pillars

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists the following performance criteria. Every scheduling algorithm is judged against these:

1. **CPU Utilization** — Keep the CPU as busy as possible (ideal: 40%–90%).
2. **Throughput** — Number of processes completed per unit time.
3. **Turnaround Time (TAT)** — Total time from submission to completion.
4. **Waiting Time (WT)** — Total time spent in the ready queue.
5. **Response Time (RT)** — Time from submission until the **first** response is produced (critical in interactive systems).

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimization Goal:** *Maximize* CPU Utilization and Throughput. *Minimize* Turnaround Time, Waiting Time, and Response Time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Scheduling Decision Points

A scheduling decision is made whenever the CPU becomes idle, which can happen due to any of the following events:

- A process **switches from running to terminated** state
- A process **switches from running to waiting** (e.g., I/O request)
- A process **switches from running to ready** (preemption — timer interrupt)
- A process **switches from waiting to ready** (I/O completion)
- A **new process** arrives in the ready queue

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — every numerical problem in the KTU exam revolves around them.**

| Quantity | Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $TAT \;=\; CT - AT$ | $CT$ = Completion Time, $AT$ = Arrival Time |
| **Waiting Time (WT)** | $WT \;=\; TAT - BT$ | $BT$ = Burst Time |
| **Alternative WT Form** | $WT \;=\; ST - AT$ | $ST$ = (First) Start Time (for non-preemptive only) |
| **Response Time (RT)** | $RT \;=\; First\_ST - AT$ | $First\_ST$ = First time CPU is given |
| **Average WT / TAT** | $\bar{X} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ | Sum of all times divided by number of processes |
| **Throughput** | $Throughput \;=\; \dfrac{n}{T_{total}}$ | $n$ = number of processes, $T_{total}$ = total elapsed time |
| **CPU Utilization** | $CPU_{\%} \;=\; \dfrac{Busy\_Time}{Total\_Time} \times 100$ | Expressed as a percentage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important convention:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values, **never** the pipe character `|`, as it breaks the markdown table.

### 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Engineering

- **TAT optimization** is critical in **batch systems** like payroll processing, where the user cares about when the job *finishes*.
- **Response Time** is what matters in **interactive systems** (web servers, mobile UI) — a 0.1s response feels snappy, a 1s response feels laggy.
- **Throughput** dominates in **scientific/HPC clusters** running millions of small jobs.
- **CPU Utilization** is the cost-saving metric for **data centres** (AWS, Azure bill by CPU-second).

### 2.4 Burst-Time Estimation

Since the OS often doesn't know the next CPU burst, it uses **exponential averaging** (a recurring KTU question):

$$\tau_{n+1} = \alpha \cdot t_n + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \tau_n$$

Where:
- $\tau_{n+1}$ = predicted next burst
- $t_n$ = actual length of the $n$-th burst
- $\tau_n$ = predicted length of the $n$-th burst
- $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ = smoothing factor (typically $\alpha = 0.5$)

> [!NOTE]
> When $\alpha = 0$, the system ignores recent history; when $\alpha = 1$, it ignores all history except the last burst. A value around $\alpha = 0.5$ balances both.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — FCFS Scheduling (Convoy Effect Demonstration)

**Problem:** Consider the following processes with arrival time and burst time:

| Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 7 |
| P2 | 1 | 5 |
| P3 | 2 | 3 |
| P4 | 3 | 1 |

Apply **FCFS** and compute the Gantt Chart, WT, TAT, and Average WT.

**Step 1 — Build the Gantt Chart (FCFS picks the earliest arrival):**

```
| P1 (0–7) | P2 (7–12) | P3 (12–15) | P4 (15–16) |
0          7           12            15            16
```

**Step 2 — Compute Completion Time (CT) for each process:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} &= 0 + 7 = 7 \\
CT_{P2} &= 7 + 5 = 12 \\
CT_{P3} &= 12 + 3 = 15 \\
CT_{P4} &= 15 + 1 = 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Turnaround Time using $TAT = CT - AT$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 7 - 0 = 7 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 12 - 1 = 11 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 15 - 2 = 13 \\
TAT_{P4} &= 16 - 3 = 13
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Waiting Time using $WT = TAT - BT$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= 7 - 7 = 0 \\
WT_{P2} &= 11 - 5 = 6 \\
WT_{P3} &= 13 - 3 = 10 \\
WT_{P4} &= 13 - 1 = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Averages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT} &= \frac{7 + 11 + 13 + 13}{4} = \frac{44}{4} = 11.0 \\
\overline{WT} &= \frac{0 + 6 + 10 + 12}{4} = \frac{28}{4} = 7.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation — The Convoy Effect:** P4 (the shortest job, BT=1) had to wait behind P1, P2, P3, suffering WT = 12. This is the classic drawback of FCFS: short jobs get stuck behind long ones.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Non-Preemptive SJF (Optimal for Min. Avg. WT)

**Same process set as above, applying SJF (non-preemptive):**

**Step 1 — At t=0, only P1 is available → run P1 (0–7).**

**Step 2 — At t=7, P2, P3, P4 are all in ready queue. Pick the **shortest** remaining BT: P4 (BT=1).**

**Step 3 — At t=8, remaining: P2 (BT=5) and P3 (BT=3). Pick P3.**

**Step 4 — At t=11, run P2 (11–16).**

**Gantt Chart:**

```
| P1 (0–7) | P4 (7–8) | P3 (8–11) | P2 (11–16) |
0          7          8           11            16
```

**Step 5 — Compute TAT and WT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 7 - 0 = 7, \quad WT_{P1} = 7 - 7 = 0 \\
TAT_{P4} &= 8 - 3 = 5, \quad WT_{P4} = 5 - 1 = 4 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 11 - 2 = 9, \quad WT_{P3} = 9 - 3 = 6 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 16 - 1 = 15, \quad WT_{P2} = 15 - 5 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{7 + 5 + 9 + 15}{4} = 9.0, \quad \overline{WT} = \frac{0 + 4 + 6 + 10}{4} = 5.0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical guarantee:** For a **given set of processes and their burst times, SJF (non-preemptive) provably produces the minimum possible average waiting time.** This is why it is the theoretical benchmark — but it is **not implementable in practice** because the OS cannot know future burst lengths.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Preemptive SJF (Shortest Remaining Time First — SRTF)

**Same process set:**

| Process | AT | BT |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 7 |
| P2 | 1 | 5 |
| P3 | 2 | 3 |
| P4 | 3 | 1 |

**Step-by-step preemptive analysis:**

- **t = 0:** Only P1 available → start P1. Remaining of P1 = 7.
- **t = 1:** P2 arrives (BT=5). Compare remaining: P1=6, P2=5. **P2 is shorter → preempt P1, start P2.**
- **t = 2:** P3 arrives (BT=3). Remaining: P1=6, P2=4, P3=3. **P3 is shortest → preempt P2, start P3.**
- **t = 3:** P4 arrives (BT=1). Remaining: P1=6, P2=4, P3=2, P4=1. **P4 is shortest → preempt P3, start P4.**
- **t = 4:** P4 finishes. Remaining: P1=6, P2=4, P3=2. **Start P3 (shortest).**
- **t = 6:** P3 finishes. Remaining: P1=6, P2=4. **Start P2.**
- **t = 10:** P2 finishes. **Start P1.**
- **t = 16:** P1 finishes.

**Gantt Chart:**

```
| P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 | P3 | P2 |   P1   |
0    1    2    3    4    6    10        16
```

**Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 16 - 0 = 16, \quad WT_{P1} = 16 - 7 = 9 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 10 - 1 = 9, \quad WT_{P2} = 9 - 5 = 4 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 6 - 2 = 4, \quad WT_{P3} = 4 - 3 = 1 \\
TAT_{P4} &= 4 - 3 = 1, \quad WT_{P4} = 1 - 1 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{16+9+4+1}{4} = 7.5, \quad \overline{WT} = \frac{9+4+1+0}{4} = 3.5
$$

> [!NOTE]
> SRTF (preemptive SJF) gave a **lower average waiting time (3.5)** than non-preemptive SJF (5.0). However, it is even **less practical** because it requires continuous knowledge of remaining burst time and **causes starvation** of long processes.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Round Robin (Time Quantum = 4)

**Same process set, Quantum = 4:**

Round Robin uses a **circular FIFO queue**. Each process gets at most Q units; if it doesn't finish, it is preempted and re-queued at the back.

- **t = 0:** Ready: {P1}. Run P1 for 4 units (0–4). Remaining P1 = 3. P1 goes to back of queue. During 0–4, P2 (t=1) and P3 (t=2) and P4 (t=3) arrive.
- **t = 4:** Ready: {P2, P3, P4, P1}. Run P2 (4–8). Remaining P2 = 2. P2 to back.
- **t = 8:** Ready: {P3, P4, P1, P2}. Run P3 (8–11). P3 **finishes** (BT was 3).
- **t = 11:** Ready: {P4, P1, P2}. Run P4 (11–12). P4 **finishes** (BT was 1).
- **t = 12:** Ready: {P1, P2}. Run P1 (12–15). P1 **finishes** (remaining 3).
- **t = 15:** Ready: {P2}. Run P2 (15–16). P2 **finishes**.

**Gantt Chart:**

```
| P1 (0–4) | P2 (4–8) | P3 (8–11) | P4 (11–12) | P1 (12–15) | P2 (15–16) |
0          4          8           11           12            15            16
```

**Computation (using $TAT = CT - AT$, $WT = TAT - BT$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 15 - 0 = 15, \quad WT_{P1} = 15 - 7 = 8 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 16 - 1 = 15, \quad WT_{P2} = 15 - 5 = 10 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 11 - 2 = 9, \quad WT_{P3} = 9 - 3 = 6 \\
TAT_{P4} &= 12 - 3 = 9, \quad WT_{P4} = 9 - 1 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{15+15+9+9}{4} = 12.0, \quad \overline{WT} = \frac{8+10+6+8}{4} = 8.0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off rule for Round Robin (frequently asked):**
> - If Quantum $Q$ is **very large** $\rightarrow$ behaves like FCFS.
> - If Quantum $Q$ is **very small** $\rightarrow$ excessive context-switching overhead (poor throughput).
> - A common rule of thumb: $Q$ should be slightly greater than the time required to handle a typical interaction (e.g., $\sim 10$–$100$ ms).

### 3.5 Comparative Summary Matrix

| Algorithm | Type | Convoy Effect | Starvation? | Avg. WT | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCFS** | Non-preemptive | Yes | No | High | Batch systems, simple queues |
| **SJF (Non-Pre)** | Non-preemptive | No | Yes (long jobs) | **Optimal** (theoretical) | Theoretical benchmark |
| **SRTF (Pre SJF)** | Preemptive | No | Yes (long jobs) | Even lower | Theoretical, real-time variants |
| **Priority** | Both | No | Yes (low-priority) | Depends | Real-time OS, military systems |
| **Round Robin** | Preemptive | Reduced | No | Moderate | Time-sharing (Linux CFS, Windows NT) |
| **Multilevel Queue** | Preemptive | No | Yes (lower queue) | Varies | OS combining batch + interactive |
| **MLFQ** | Preemptive + adaptive | No | Reduced (aging) | Varies | Modern Unix, Windows, Linux |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Scheduler Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef store fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef cpu fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,color:#14532D
    classDef sched fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F
    classDef proc fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#831843

    LTS[Long Term Scheduler]:::sched
    MTS[Medium Term Scheduler]:::sched
    STS[Short Term Scheduler CPU Scheduler]:::sched
    RQ[(Ready Queue)]:::store
    DQ[(Device Queue)]:::store
    SUS[(Suspended Queue)]:::store
    CPU[CPU Core]:::cpu
    PNEW[New Process Batch]:::proc
    PTERM[Terminated Process]:::proc

    PNEW --> LTS
    LTS --> RQ
    RQ --> STS
    STS --> CPU
    CPU --> DQ
    DQ -.I/O Completion.-> RQ
    CPU --> PTERM
    MTS <--> SUS
    RQ <--> MTS
    CPU -.Time Quantum Expired.-> RQ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **Short-Term Scheduler** is the heart of process scheduling — it sits between the Ready Queue and the CPU and makes the dispatch decision every few milliseconds. The Medium-Term Scheduler can move processes between Ready and Suspended queues to manage memory pressure.

---

### 4.2 Scheduling Algorithm Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef root fill:#1E293B,stroke:#0F172A,color:#F8FAFC
    classDef preempt fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef nonpre fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#831843
    classDef leaf fill:#F0FDF4,stroke:#166534,color:#14532D

    ROOT[CPU Scheduling Algorithms]:::root
    ROOT --> NP[Non Preemptive]:::nonpre
    ROOT --> PR[Preemptive]:::preempt

    NP --> NP1[FCFS First Come First Served]:::leaf
    NP --> NP2[SJF Shortest Job First]:::leaf
    NP --> NP3[Priority Non Preemptive]:::leaf

    PR --> PR1[Round Robin]:::leaf
    PR --> PR2[SRTF Shortest Remaining Time First]:::leaf
    PR --> PR3[Priority Preemptive]:::leaf
    PR --> PR4[Multilevel Feedback Queue MLFQ]:::leaf
```

---

### 4.3 Process State Transition Diagram (with Scheduling Events)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> NEW
    NEW --> READY: Long Term Scheduler Admit
    READY --> RUNNING: Short Term Scheduler Dispatch
    RUNNING --> READY: Preemption or Time Quantum Expired
    RUNNING --> WAITING: I/O or Event Wait
    WAITING --> READY: I/O Completion
    RUNNING --> TERMINATED: Process Exit
    READY --> SUSPENDED: Medium Term Scheduler Swap Out
    SUSPENDED --> READY: Medium Term Scheduler Swap In
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The `RUNNING → READY` transition (preemption) is what differentiates preemptive from non-preemptive scheduling. If this transition can **only** occur when the process voluntarily yields (terminates or moves to WAITING), the algorithm is non-preemptive.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. Define process scheduling. List any four CPU scheduling criteria.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**
Process scheduling is the OS function of selecting one of the ready processes from the ready queue for execution on the CPU. The four major CPU scheduling criteria are:
1. **CPU Utilization** — percentage of time CPU is busy
2. **Throughput** — number of processes completed per unit time
3. **Turnaround Time (TAT)** — total time from submission to completion
4. **Waiting Time (WT)** — total time spent in the ready queue
*(Response Time is also accepted as a fifth criterion.)*

---

**Q2. Differentiate between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Non-Preemptive | Preemptive |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU Release** | Only when process terminates or voluntarily waits | Can be forcibly taken by scheduler |
| **Preemption** | Not allowed | Allowed (e.g., on time quantum expiry) |
| **Response Time** | Poor for long jobs | Better — suitable for interactive systems |
| **Overhead** | Lower (no forced context switches) | Higher (frequent context switches) |
| **Example** | FCFS, Non-Pre SJF | SRTF, Round Robin, Linux CFS |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the FCFS and SJF (non-preemptive) scheduling algorithms with a suitable example. Compute the average waiting time and average turnaround time.** [7 Marks]

**Solution:**

**FCFS Algorithm:** The process that arrives first in the ready queue is allocated the CPU first. It is implemented using a simple FIFO queue.

**SJF (Non-Preemptive) Algorithm:** From all processes currently in the ready queue, the one with the **shortest CPU burst time** is selected. Once started, the process is allowed to run to completion.

**Example Data (re-using Section 3):**

| Process | AT | BT |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 7 |
| P2 | 1 | 5 |
| P3 | 2 | 3 |
| P4 | 3 | 1 |

**FCFS Gantt Chart:** P1 (0–7) → P2 (7–12) → P3 (12–15) → P4 (15–16)

**FCFS Computation (already derived in §3.1):**
- $\overline{WT} = 7.0$, $\overline{TAT} = 11.0$

**SJF Gantt Chart:** P1 (0–7) → P4 (7–8) → P3 (8–11) → P2 (11–16)

**SJF Computation (already derived in §3.2):**
- $\overline{WT} = 5.0$, $\overline{TAT} = 9.0$

**Conclusion:** SJF produces a lower average waiting time than FCFS, but suffers from starvation of long processes and requires knowledge of future burst time.

---

**(b) Consider the following processes. Apply Round Robin scheduling with Time Quantum = 2 and compute the average waiting time and average turnaround time.** [7 Marks]

| Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 4 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 |
| P3 | 2 | 2 |
| P4 | 3 | 1 |

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — At t=0:** Ready: {P1}. Run P1 for 2 units (0–2). P1 remaining = 2. P1 goes to back. During 0–2, P2 (t=1) and P3 (t=2) arrive.

**Step 2 — At t=2:** Ready: {P2, P3, P1}. Run P2 for 2 units (2–4). P2 remaining = 1. P2 to back. During 2–4, P4 (t=3) arrives.

**Step 3 — At t=4:** Ready: {P3, P1, P4, P2}. Run P3 for 2 units (4–6). P3 **finishes** (BT was 2).

**Step 4 — At t=6:** Ready: {P1, P4, P2}. Run P1 for 2 units (6–8). P1 **finishes** (remaining was 2).

**Step 5 — At t=8:** Ready: {P4, P2}. Run P4 for 1 unit (8–9). P4 **finishes** (BT was 1).

**Step 6 — At t=9:** Ready: {P2}. Run P2 for 1 unit (9–10). P2 **finishes** (remaining was 1).

**Gantt Chart:**

```
| P1 (0–2) | P2 (2–4) | P3 (4–6) | P1 (6–8) | P4 (8–9) | P2 (9–10) |
0          2          4          6          8          9           10
```

**Step 7 — Compute TAT and WT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} &= 8, \quad TAT_{P1} = 8 - 0 = 8, \quad WT_{P1} = 8 - 4 = 4 \\
CT_{P2} &= 10, \quad TAT_{P2} = 10 - 1 = 9, \quad WT_{P2} = 9 - 3 = 6 \\
CT_{P3} &= 6, \quad TAT_{P3} = 6 - 2 = 4, \quad WT_{P3} = 4 - 2 = 2 \\
CT_{P4} &= 9, \quad TAT_{P4} = 9 - 3 = 6, \quad WT_{P4} = 6 - 1 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{8+9+4+6}{4} = 6.75, \quad \overline{WT} = \frac{4+6+2+5}{4} = 4.25
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain Priority Scheduling and Multilevel Queue Scheduling with diagrams. Discuss the problem of starvation and the aging solution.** [7 Marks]

**Solution:**

**Priority Scheduling:** Each process is assigned a priority. The CPU is allocated to the process with the **highest priority**. Ties are broken by FCFS. It can be either preemptive or non-preemptive.

**Advantages:** Simple, supports real-time and safety-critical systems.
**Disadvantage — Starvation:** A low-priority process may wait indefinitely while high-priority processes keep arriving.

**Solution — Aging:** Gradually increase the priority of a process the longer it waits. For example, increase priority by 1 every 15 minutes. This guarantees that even starved processes will eventually get CPU time. (Used in Linux O(1) scheduler, modern MLFQ.)

**Multilevel Queue Scheduling:** The ready queue is divided into **multiple separate queues**, typically:
- **System processes** (highest priority)
- **Interactive processes**
- **Batch processes** (lowest priority)

Each queue may use its own scheduling algorithm (e.g., System → RR, Batch → FCFS). Scheduling **between** queues is fixed-priority preemptive. A process stays permanently in its assigned queue.

**Disadvantage:** A process can be in the wrong queue (e.g., a CPU-bound process misclassified as interactive). This is solved by **Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ)**, which allows processes to move between queues based on observed CPU vs. I/O behavior.

---

**(b) Consider the following processes. Apply SRTF (Preemptive SJF) and compute the average waiting time and average turnaround time.** [7 Marks]

| Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 8 |
| P2 | 1 | 4 |
| P3 | 2 | 9 |
| P4 | 3 | 5 |

**Solution:**

**Trace of preemption decisions:**

- **t = 0:** Only P1. Start P1. Remaining P1 = 8.
- **t = 1:** P2 arrives (BT=4). Remaining: P1=7, P2=4. **P2 is shorter → preempt, run P2.**
- **t = 2:** P3 arrives (BT=9). Remaining: P1=7, P2=3, P3=9. **P2 still shortest → continue P2.**
- **t = 3:** P4 arrives (BT=5). Remaining: P1=7, P2=2, P3=9, P4=5. **P2 still shortest → continue P2.**
- **t = 5:** P2 finishes. Remaining: P1=7, P3=9, P4=5. **P4 is shortest → run P4.**
- **t = 10:** P4 finishes. Remaining: P1=7, P3=9. **P1 is shorter → run P1.**
- **t = 17:** P1 finishes. **Run P3 (17–26).**

**Gantt Chart:**

```
| P1 (0–1) | P2 (1–5) | P4 (5–10) | P1 (10–17) | P3 (17–26) |
0          1          5           10            17            26
```

**Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 17 - 0 = 17, \quad WT_{P1} = 17 - 8 = 9 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 5 - 1 = 4, \quad WT_{P2} = 4 - 4 = 0 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 26 - 2 = 24, \quad WT_{P3} = 24 - 9 = 15 \\
TAT_{P4} &= 10 - 3 = 7, \quad WT_{P4} = 7 - 5 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\overline{TAT} = \frac{17+4+24+7}{4} = 13.0, \quad \overline{WT} = \frac{9+0+15+2}{4} = 6.5
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (read carefully before the exam):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the Arrival Time** when computing TAT. Many students write $TAT = CT - BT$, which is **wrong** — it gives the sum of WT and AT, not TAT. Always use $TAT = CT - AT$.
>
> 2. **In Round Robin, ignoring the order of re-queuing.** A process that did not finish must go to the **back of the queue immediately after its quantum expires**, and *any newly arrived processes* during that quantum should be added **before** it in the next dispatch (depending on convention — verify with your module teacher).
>
> 3. **Not drawing the Gantt Chart.** In the KTU board, the Gantt chart carries 2–3 marks by itself. Always show the timeline with start and end times clearly marked.
>
> 4. **Confusing Response Time and Waiting Time.** Response Time = time until *first* CPU allocation. Waiting Time = total time spent *waiting in the ready queue* (sum of all waiting slices).
>
> 5. **Skipping units.** Always write "ms" or "seconds" — evaluators deduct marks for unitless numerical answers.
>
> 6. **For SJF/SRTF, not showing the decision at every arrival.** Examiners award step marks only if you explicitly state "at t = x, process y arrives with BT = z, remaining time of current process = w, since z < w, we preempt."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Process Scheduling** = selection of next ready process for CPU. Performed by the **Short-Term Scheduler** (CPU Scheduler).
- **Three schedulers** exist: Long-term (admission), Short-term (CPU dispatch), Medium-term (swap out/in). Long-term controls the **degree of multiprogramming**.
- **Five scheduling criteria:** CPU utilization ↑, Throughput ↑, TAT ↓, WT ↓, RT ↓.
- **Formulas:** $TAT = CT - AT$, $WT = TAT - BT$, $RT = First\_ST - AT$. (Memorize these — every numerical depends on them.)
- **FCFS** — simple FIFO; suffers from **convoy effect**; non-preemptive.
- **SJF (non-preemptive)** — optimal average waiting time **in theory**; suffers **starvation** of long jobs; cannot be implemented in practice (OS doesn't know future burst).
- **SRTF (preemptive SJF)** — even lower average WT; even worse starvation; requires predicting remaining burst.
- **Priority Scheduling** — picks highest priority; uses **aging** to prevent starvation.
- **Round Robin** — circular queue with fixed **time quantum**; best for time-sharing; quantum too large → FCFS; quantum too small → context-switch overhead.
- **Multilevel Queue** — fixed queues for system/interactive/batch; no movement between queues.
- **MLFQ** — processes can move between queues based on observed behavior; uses aging; foundation of modern OS schedulers (Linux CFS, Windows NT).
- **Burst-time estimation** uses **exponential averaging:** $\tau_{n+1} = \alpha \cdot t_n + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \tau_n$.
- **Always draw the Gantt Chart** with explicit time markers before computing anything.
- **Always show intermediate steps** in preemption decisions (SRTF, Priority-Preemptive, Round Robin).
- **Use `\mid` or `\vert` in any math involving absolute value** to avoid breaking markdown tables.
- **Units matter** — write ms, seconds, or % explicitly in every numerical answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Multilevel Feedback Queue: Basic Rules; Case Study: The Linux Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Multilevel Feedback Queue: Basic Rules; Case Study: The Linux Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ)** is a CPU scheduling algorithm that attempts to **simultaneously optimize turnaround time and response time** by using multiple ready queues of varying priorities and dynamically moving processes between them based on their observed CPU-burst behaviour.

In MLFQ, the scheduler **learns** from the past behaviour of a process: a process that repeatedly relinquishes the CPU is treated as **interactive** and given high priority; a process that uses the entire time slice is treated as **CPU-bound** and demoted to a lower priority queue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):**
> MLFQ was invented by **Fernando J. Corbató** (1962) as part of the **CTSS** (Compatible Time-Sharing System) at MIT. It is the most well-known **learning-based** scheduling algorithm and forms the conceptual foundation for the Linux **CFS** discussed later in this module.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Hospital Triage System:**

Imagine a hospital emergency ward. A new patient walking in is treated like a **high-priority (Rule 3)** case — nurses and doctors first assess them quickly (**short time slice at top queue**). If the patient stabilises quickly and just needs monitoring, they remain in the same priority band. If, however, the patient's condition is critical and they keep needing intensive monitoring (**uses full time slice**), they are *demoted* to a longer-term care ward (**Rule 4 — lower priority queue, longer time slice**). Periodically — say, every 24 hours — **all patients are re-triaged** to the highest priority to prevent starvation (**Rule 5 — priority boost**). This is precisely how MLFQ works with processes and the CPU.

---

### 1.2 The Linux Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)

**Formal Definition:**
> The **Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)**, introduced in **Linux kernel 2.6.23 (October 2007)** by **Ingo Molnár**, is a **fair-share, proportional-share, deadline-aware CPU scheduler** that aims to allocate the CPU such that, over a finite time window, every runnable task receives a **proportionally fair share** of CPU time based on its **weight (derived from its `nice` value)**.

Unlike classical schedulers that use fixed time slices, CFS models an **"ideal, perfectly fair" multitasking CPU** running at an infinitesimal granularity and uses a **red-black tree** as its runqueue to track tasks ordered by their **virtual runtime**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core CFS Invariant:**
> At any scheduling decision point, CFS runs the task with the **smallest `vruntime`** (the task that has historically received the *least* CPU time), thereby guaranteeing proportional fairness.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Airport Check-In Counter:**

Picture a check-in counter shared by **N** passengers. A *truly fair* system would rotate the counter among passengers such that a first-class passenger (low `nice`, high weight) gets **more total time**, while a budget-class passenger (high `nice`, low weight) gets **less total time**. CFS maintains a **virtual stopwatch** (`vruntime`) for every passenger. The passenger with the **smallest stopwatch reading** always goes next. Because first-class passengers have a *slower* stopwatch, they appear "behind" in vruntime and are scheduled more often. This is how CFS encodes fairness without using rigid time slices.

---

### 1.3 KTU 2024 — Why Study Both Together?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> MLFQ and CFS are studied together in **Module 1** because they represent **two philosophical extremes** of learning-based CPU scheduling:
> 1. **MLFQ** → *priority-based, rule-driven, queue-per-priority* (heuristic approach).
> 2. **CFS** → *fairness-based, red-black tree, mathematical proportionality* (algorithmic approach).
> Modern Linux kernels (2.6.23 → 6.x) abandoned the O(1) scheduler in favour of CFS precisely because CFS achieves what MLFQ *tries* to achieve, but with **provable fairness guarantees** and **O(log N)** scheduling overhead.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MLFQ — The Five Basic Rules

The MLFQ algorithm is completely defined by a small set of rules. The version below follows the canonical **Arpaci-Dusseau (OSTEP, Chapter 8)** formulation adopted in the KTU reference material.

> [!NOTE]
> **MLFQ is parameterised by:**
> - **Number of queues:** $L$ (typically 3–5).
> - **Scheduling policy between queues:** *Fixed priority* (strict) or *Round-robin within same queue*.
> - **Time slice per queue:** $\tau_{0}, \tau_{1}, \tau_{2}, \ldots$ (typically decreasing or constant).
> - **Boost period:** $S$ (typically 1 second or 100 ms).

#### **Rule 1 — Highest Priority Runs**

If $\text{Priority}(A) > \text{Priority}(B)$, scheduler runs $A$; $B$ does not run.

**Why:** Establishes the *priority hierarchy* across queues. Without this, MLFQ collapses to plain FCFS or RR.

#### **Rule 2 — Same Priority ⇒ Round Robin**

If $\text{Priority}(A) = \text{Priority}(B)$, $A$ and $B$ run in **round-robin** within that queue.

**Why:** Provides fairness *within* a priority band and gives interactive tasks a chance to release the CPU.

#### **Rule 3 — New Job Enters at Top**

When a job enters the system, it is placed at the **highest-priority queue** (typically queue 0).

**Why:** Gives every newcomer a chance to demonstrate interactive behaviour immediately — optimising **response time**.

#### **Rule 4 — Demotion on Long CPU Burst**

If a job uses up its **entire time slice** $\tau_{i}$ at priority level $i$, its priority is **reduced** to level $i+1$ (next lower queue).

**Why:** Penalises CPU-bound jobs so they cannot monopolise the CPU at the expense of interactive tasks — optimising **turnaround time** for the mix.

#### **Rule 5 — Periodic Priority Boost**

After some time period $S$, **move all jobs in the system to the topmost queue**.

**Why:** Prevents **starvation** and **gaming** — long-running CPU-bound jobs eventually regain interactive treatment, and a "trickster" process that issues an I/O right before its time slice expires cannot permanently monopolise the CPU.

---

### 2.2 Problems with Vanilla MLFQ (and their Fixes)

> [!WARNING]
> **Starvation** — CPU-bound jobs in low queues may *never* run if the system is saturated with interactive jobs at higher queues.
> **Fix:** Rule 5 (boost).

> [!WARNING]
> **Gaming the scheduler** — A CPU-bound job that performs an I/O just before its time slice expires (e.g., a `usleep(1)` call) stays in the top queue forever.
> **Fix:** **Rule 4b** — *Once a job uses up its time slice at any level, it is demoted regardless of whether it yielded voluntarily or was preempted.* (This is the *accounting fix*.)

> [!WARNING]
> **A sudden change in behaviour** — A job that has been CPU-bound for a long time and is now interactive (e.g., a compiler that just started a linker phase) would be stuck in the lowest queue.
> **Fix:** Rule 5 (boost) rescues it.

---

### 2.3 CFS — Core Design Pillars

1. **Modelling the "Ideal Fair" CPU** — CFS imagines a *perfect* multitasking processor that could run N tasks simultaneously at $\frac{1}{N}$-th speed. CFS then **approximates** this ideal.
2. **Virtual Runtime (`vruntime`)** — Each task has a per-task `vruntime` counter that advances **slower** for higher-priority (lower `nice`) tasks.
3. **Red-Black Tree Run Queue** — Tasks are stored in a **left-leaning red-black (LLRB) tree** keyed on `vruntime`. **O(log N)** insertion and deletion.
4. **No Fixed Time Slice** — The "time slice" is computed dynamically based on **targeted latency** and the **number of runnable tasks**.
5. **`sched_latency`, `sched_min_granularity`, `sched_nr_latency`** — Tunable kernel parameters that govern slice sizing.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbols / Units** | **Notes / Boundary Conditions** |
|---|---|---|---|
| CFS time slice | $T_{\text{slice}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{latency}}}{N}$ | $T_{\text{latency}}$ in ns/µs, $N$ = runnable tasks | When $N > \text{sched\_nr\_latency}$ (default 8), switch to granular formula |
| Granular CFS slice | $T_{\text{slice}} = \max\left(\dfrac{T_{\text{latency}}}{N},\ T_{\text{min\_granularity}}\right)$ | $T_{\text{min\_granularity}}$ typically **1 ms – 4 ms** | Floor prevents **too-frequent** context switches |
| Virtual runtime increment | $\Delta \text{vruntime} = \Delta t_{\text{exec}} \times \dfrac{W_{0}}{w(\text{nice})}$ | $\Delta t_{\text{exec}}$ in ns, $W_{0} = \mathbf{1024}$ (NICE_0_WEIGHT), $w(\text{nice})$ = task weight | Higher `nice` ⇒ smaller weight ⇒ larger `vruntime` increment ⇒ less CPU |
| Task weight (Linux approximation) | $w(\text{nice}) = \dfrac{1024}{1.25^{\,\text{nice}}}$ | nice $\in [-20,\,+19]$ in Linux | Inverted relative to classical Unix nice: negative = high priority |
| Proportional CPU share | $\text{CPU}_i = T_{\text{total}} \times \dfrac{w_i}{\sum_{j} w_j}$ | $T_{\text{total}}$ = window, $w_i$ = weight of task $i$ | **The CFS fairness invariant** |
| Decay factor for sleep tasks | $\Delta \text{vruntime}_{\text{sleep}} = \Delta t_{\text{sleep}} \times \dfrac{W_{0}}{w(\text{nice})}$ | Same as above | A sleeping task's `vruntime` is set low so it gets CPU quickly on wake |
| MLFQ demotion (Rule 4) | $\text{Queue}(i \rightarrow i+1)$ if used full $\tau_i$ | $i$ = current queue index | $0 \le i < L-1$ |
| MLFQ boost period | All tasks → queue 0 every $S$ | $S$ typically **1 s** | Kills starvation and gaming |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this for KTU ESE:**
> - $\mathbf{W_{0} = 1024}$ for `nice = 0`.
> - Each increment of `nice` reduces weight by **factor ~1.25**.
> - $\mathbf{1.25^{-20}} \approx 0.0033$, so `nice = -20` weight ≈ **1024 × 3.05 ≈ 3122**.
> - $\mathbf{1.25^{+19}} \approx 53.7$, so `nice = +19` weight ≈ **1024 / 53.7 ≈ 19**.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Scheduler** | **Used In / Inspired By** | **Engineering Application** |
|---|---|---|
| MLFQ (variant) | Windows NT 4.0 – Windows 7, classic Unix V6/V7, FreeBSD ULE | Real-time interactive desktop responsiveness, multi-user timesharing |
| CFS | Linux 2.6.23 – present, Android (via Bionic), Raspbian | Cloud servers, Android mobile, embedded IoT (RT patches on top), HPC |
| Derived from CFS: **EEVDF** | Linux 6.6 (2023) | Deadline-aware scheduling for latency-sensitive workloads |

**Engineering use case:** A cloud provider (e.g., AWS EC2, Azure VMs) uses CFS-based kernels. The `nice` value exposed via `nice(2)` / `setpriority(2)` syscall is how a sysadmin *deprioritises* a background batch job relative to user-facing services — directly mapping to the weight formula above.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — CFS Time Slice from Targeted Latency

**Problem (KTU-typical):** Given `sched_latency = 24 ms`, `sched_min_granularity = 4 ms`, `sched_nr_latency = 8`, calculate the CFS time slice for **N = 4**, **N = 10**, and **N = 32** runnable tasks.

**Step 1 — Recall the granular formula:**

$$
T_{\text{slice}} = \max\!\left(\frac{T_{\text{latency}}}{N},\ T_{\text{min\_granularity}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute N = 4:**

$$
T_{\text{slice}} = \max\!\left(\frac{24\ \text{ms}}{4},\ 4\ \text{ms}\right)
$$

$$
T_{\text{slice}} = \max(6\ \text{ms},\ 4\ \text{ms}) = \mathbf{6\ \text{ms}}
$$

Each of the 4 tasks runs for 6 ms per scheduling round; total round = 4 × 6 = 24 ms = `sched_latency`. **Good — no floor applied.**

**Step 3 — Substitute N = 10:**

$$
T_{\text{slice}} = \max\!\left(\frac{24\ \text{ms}}{10},\ 4\ \text{ms}\right) = \max(2.4\ \text{ms},\ 4\ \text{ms}) = \mathbf{4\ \text{ms}}
$$

The **floor kicks in** because $2.4 < 4$. Tasks get 4 ms each; total round = 10 × 4 = 40 ms, which is **longer than** `sched_latency`. CFS tolerates this — fairness still holds, only latency stretches.

**Step 4 — Substitute N = 32:**

$$
T_{\text{slice}} = \max\!\left(\frac{24\ \text{ms}}{32},\ 4\ \text{ms}\right) = \max(0.75\ \text{ms},\ 4\ \text{ms}) = \mathbf{4\ \text{ms}}
$$

Floor dominates strongly. Total round = 32 × 4 = 128 ms — well above `sched_latency`, but switching to a smaller slice would thrash the CPU with context switches.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** This is *exactly why* CFS uses a floor. With 1000 tasks, naïve division would give 24 µs slices — enough for the context switch itself to dominate. The 4 ms floor is a **practical engineering concession**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — `vruntime` Increment with Different `nice` Values

**Problem:** Task $A$ has `nice = 0`, Task $B$ has `nice = +5`. Both run for $\Delta t_{\text{exec}} = 10\ \text{ms}$. Compute the `vruntime` increment for each. Show that the lower-`nice` task is "ahead" in real time but "behind" in `vruntime`.

**Step 1 — Compute weight for `nice = 0`:**

$$
w(0) = \frac{1024}{1.25^{0}} = \frac{1024}{1} = \mathbf{1024}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute weight for `nice = +5`:**

$$
w(+5) = \frac{1024}{1.25^{5}} = \frac{1024}{3.0517578125} \approx \mathbf{335.54}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute `vruntime` increment for Task $A$ (`nice = 0`):**

$$
\Delta \text{vruntime}_A = 10\ \text{ms} \times \frac{1024}{1024} = 10\ \text{ms} \times 1 = \mathbf{10\ \text{ms}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute `vruntime` increment for Task $B$ (`nice = +5`):**

$$
\Delta \text{vruntime}_B = 10\ \text{ms} \times \frac{1024}{335.54} \approx 10\ \text{ms} \times 3.052 \approx \mathbf{30.52\ \text{ms}}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

After the same 10 ms of wall-clock execution:
- Task $A$ (`nice = 0`, high priority) has `vruntime += 10 ms`.
- Task $B$ (`nice = +5`, low priority) has `vruntime += 30.52 ms`.

So Task $B$ appears to have consumed **3.05× more** CPU from CFS's perspective. CFS will therefore pick Task $A$ next, **or** Task $B$ only after its `vruntime` catches up. Over a long window, the *actual* CPU share is:

$$
\frac{\text{CPU}_A}{\text{CPU}_B} = \frac{w_A}{w_B} = \frac{1024}{335.54} \approx \mathbf{3.05 : 1}
$$

This matches the **3.05× advantage** seen in the vruntime accounting — exactly the proportional-fairness invariant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most-tested KTU derivation.** Memorise:
> $w(\text{nice}) = \dfrac{1024}{1.25^{\text{nice}}}$, $\quad \Delta \text{vruntime} = \Delta t \cdot \dfrac{1024}{w(\text{nice})}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total CPU Share for a 3-Task Mix

**Problem:** Three tasks with `nice = 0`, `nice = +5`, `nice = +10` share a CPU for 1 second. Calculate their CPU shares.

**Step 1 — Compute the three weights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(0) &= \frac{1024}{1.25^{0}} = 1024 \\
w(5) &= \frac{1024}{1.25^{5}} \approx 335.54 \\
w(10) &= \frac{1024}{1.25^{10}} \approx 109.86
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sum of weights:**

$$
W_{\text{total}} = 1024 + 335.54 + 109.86 = \mathbf{1469.40}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the proportional shares in 1000 ms:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPU}_{0}  &= 1000\ \text{ms} \times \frac{1024}{1469.40} \approx \mathbf{697.0\ \text{ms}} \\
\text{CPU}_{5}  &= 1000\ \text{ms} \times \frac{335.54}{1469.40} \approx \mathbf{228.3\ \text{ms}} \\
\text{CPU}_{10} &= 1000\ \text{ms} \times \frac{109.86}{1469.40} \approx \mathbf{74.7\ \text{ms}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify:**

$$
697.0 + 228.3 + 74.7 = 1000.0\ \text{ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Ratio check:** $697.0 : 228.3 : 74.7 \approx 9.33 : 3.05 : 1.00$ — which is exactly $(1.25^0 : 1.25^{-5} : 1.25^{-10})$ inverted, confirming the proportional-fairness invariant.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — MLFQ vs. CFS

| **Property** | **MLFQ** | **CFS** |
|---|---|---|
| Scheduling data structure | Multiple FIFO queues | Single **red-black tree** |
| Decision complexity | O(1) per queue selection | O(log N) (leftmost in RB-tree) |
| Primary optimisation goal | Response time + turnaround | **Proportional fairness** |
| Handles interactivity via | Heuristic (CPU-burst detection) | Implicit (lower nice ⇒ smaller vruntime growth) |
| Handles CPU-bound via | Demotion | Weight reduction |
| Starvation prevention | Periodic boost (Rule 5) | None — fairness IS the prevention |
| I/O / Sleep handling | Retains priority | `vruntime` reduced; reinsert at left of tree |
| Tunable parameters | $L$, $\tau_i$, $S$ | `sched_latency`, `sched_min_granularity`, `sched_nr_latency` |
| Kernel version introduced | — (concept 1962) | Linux 2.6.23 (Oct 2007) |
| Current status | Legacy / educational | **Linux default since 2.6.23**; replaced by EEVDF since 6.6 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MLFQ — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    newJob["New Job Enters the System"] --> rule3["Apply Rule 3: Insert into Top Priority Queue Q0"]
    rule3 --> runQ0["Run Job in Q0 with Time Slice tau0"]
    runQ0 --> rule1Check{"Is there any job in a higher priority queue?"}
    rule1Check -->|"Yes (Rule 1)"| preemptQ0["Preempt current job and run the higher priority job"]
    rule1Check -->|"No"| rrQ0{"Multiple jobs in Q0?"}
    preemptQ0 --> runQ0
    rrQ0 -->|"Yes (Rule 2)"| rrScheduleQ0["Apply Round Robin within Q0"]
    rrQ0 -->|"No"| continueQ0["Continue running sole job in Q0"]
    rrScheduleQ0 --> sliceCheck{"Did job consume FULL time slice tau0?"}
    continueQ0 --> sliceCheck
    sliceCheck -->|"Yes (Rule 4)"| demoteQ1["Demote job to Queue Q1 with time slice tau1"]
    sliceCheck -->|"No (voluntary I/O)"| rule4b{"Accounting Fix: Did job use full slice regardless of yield?"}
    rule4b -->|"Yes"| demoteQ1
    rule4b -->|"No"| stayQ0["Job remains in Q0"]
    demoteQ1 --> runQ1["Run job in Q1 with time slice tau1"]
    runQ1 --> sliceCheck2{"Did job consume FULL time slice tau1?"}
    sliceCheck2 -->|"Yes"| demoteQ2["Demote job to Queue Q2 with time slice tau2"]
    sliceCheck2 -->|"No"| stayQ1["Job remains in Q1"]
    demoteQ2 --> lowestQueue{"Reached lowest queue Q(L-1)?"}
    lowestQueue -->|"Yes"| roundRobinLow["Remain in Q(L-1) with longest time slice"]
    lowestQueue -->|"No"| runQ2["Run job in Q2"]
    runQ1 --> boostTimer{"Has time period S elapsed since last boost?"}
    runQ2 --> boostTimer
    boostTimer -->|"No (Rule 5 not invoked)"| continueSched["Continue with existing rules"]
    boostTimer -->|"Yes (Rule 5)"| boostAll["Move ALL jobs in the system to the topmost Queue Q0"]
    boostAll --> rule3
```

### 4.2 CFS — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    timerTick["CFS Scheduler Tick (timer interrupt)"] --> updateVrt["Compute delta_exec = now - exec_start"]
    updateVrt --> computeWeight["Look up weight w for task nice value"]
    computeWeight --> advanceVrt["vruntime += delta_exec times W0 divided by w"]
    advanceVrt --> eraseOld["Erase task from Red-Black Tree at old vruntime position"]
    eraseOld --> reinsertNew["Reinsert task into Red-Black Tree at new vruntime position"]
    reinsertNew --> nextPick["Pick LEFTMOST node of RB tree = task with SMALLEST vruntime"]
    nextPick --> checkPreempt{"Is leftmost task different from currently running task?"}
    checkPreempt -->|"Yes"| contextSwitch["Trigger context switch to new task"]
    checkPreempt -->|"No"| continueCurrent["Continue running current task"]
    contextSwitch --> timerTick
    continueCurrent --> timerTick
    sleep["Task voluntarily sleeps or blocks on I/O"] --> setMinVrt["On wakeup set vruntime to minimum of runnable tasks"]
    setMinVrt --> reinsertNew
    newTask["Newly forked task entering run queue"] --> setStartVrt["Initialise vruntime to current min vruntime of run queue"]
    setStartVrt --> reinsertNew
```

### 4.3 Comparative Schematic — Time Slice vs. vruntime in CFS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputs["Inputs: Total runnable tasks N, Total targeted latency Tlatency, Minimum granularity Tmin"] --> computeNaive["Compute naive slice = Tlatency divided by N"]
    computeNaive --> floorCheck{"Is naive slice LESS THAN Tmin?"}
    floorCheck -->|"No"| chooseNaive["Tslice equals naive slice = Tlatency divided by N"]
    floorCheck -->|"Yes"| chooseFloor["Tslice equals Tmin (floor applied)"]
    chooseNaive --> scheduleEach["Each of N tasks runs for Tslice in round robin"]
    chooseFloor --> scheduleEach
    scheduleEach --> duringExec["During each task execution: vruntime advances by delta_exec times W0 divided by weight"]
    duringExec --> fairnessOut["Long term fairness: Task i CPU share equals weight i divided by sum of weights"]
```

---

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**List any three basic rules of the Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ) scheduling algorithm and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Rule 3 — New Job Enters at Top:** When a job enters the system, it is placed in the **highest-priority queue**. *Purpose:* Gives every new process a chance to demonstrate interactive behaviour immediately, optimising **response time**. *(1 mark)*

2. **Rule 4 — Demotion on Long CPU Burst:** If a job uses up its entire time slice at priority $i$, its priority is **reduced** to level $i+1$. *Purpose:* Penalises CPU-bound jobs so they do not monopolise the CPU, optimising **turnaround time** for the workload mix. *(1 mark)*

3. **Rule 5 — Periodic Priority Boost:** After time $S$, **all jobs are moved to the topmost queue**. *Purpose:* Prevents **starvation** of long-running CPU-bound jobs and defeats **gaming** by processes that issue an I/O just before their time slice expires. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**What is `vruntime` in the Linux CFS? How is it incremented for a task with `nice = n`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`vruntime` (virtual runtime) is a per-task counter maintained by CFS that tracks **how much CPU time a task has consumed**, normalised against a reference weight of **1024** (the weight of a `nice = 0` task). *(1 mark)*

The increment formula is:

$$
\Delta \text{vruntime} = \Delta t_{\text{exec}} \times \frac{W_{0}}{w(\text{nice})} = \Delta t_{\text{exec}} \times \frac{1024}{\dfrac{1024}{1.25^{n}}} = \Delta t_{\text{exec}} \times 1.25^{\,n}
$$

*(1 mark for the formula; 1 mark for the explanation.)*

Tasks with **higher `nice` (lower priority)** get a **larger** `vruntime` increment per unit of real execution time, making them appear "behind" in fairness accounting and scheduling them **less frequently**. CFS always picks the runnable task with the **smallest `vruntime`** for next execution.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse `nice` sign with priority** — In classical Unix, **higher nice = lower priority**, but the weight formula uses $1.25^{n}$ so higher `nice` ⇒ **smaller** weight. Writing them backwards loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Do NOT forget the floor (`sched_min_granularity`)** in CFS time-slice calculation. Examiners explicitly test this for $N > 8$.
> 3. **Do NOT state MLFQ "uses fixed time slices"** — it does for *each queue* but the slice per queue *differs*. Wording matters.
> 4. **Always show Rule 4 vs. Rule 4b distinction** (accounting fix for gaming) — this is a favourite KTU sub-question.

---

#### **QUESTION A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of the **Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ)** scheduling algorithm. List and explain **any four** of its basic rules with an example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **three problems** with the basic MLFQ — *starvation, gaming the scheduler, and sudden change in programme behaviour*. Explain how the **priority boost** and **accounting fix (Rule 4b)** resolve them.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) Working of MLFQ [7 marks]:**

MLFQ maintains **$L$ distinct ready queues** numbered $Q_0$ (highest priority) through $Q_{L-1}$ (lowest priority). Each queue $Q_i$ has its own time slice $\tau_i$, typically with $\tau_0 < \tau_1 < \ldots < \tau_{L-1}$. *(1 mark)*

The four basic rules explained:

**Rule 1 — Highest Priority Runs:** If priority of $A$ > priority of $B$, then $A$ runs and $B$ does not. This establishes a strict *priority hierarchy* across queues. *(1 mark)*

**Rule 2 — Round Robin within Same Priority:** When multiple jobs share a queue, they are scheduled round-robin. This provides intra-queue fairness and lets interactive jobs release the CPU promptly. *(1 mark)*

**Rule 3 — New Jobs Enter at Top:** A newly admitted process is inserted into $Q_0$. **Example:** A user types `ls -l` — the shell forks a new child process which enters at the top queue and runs immediately, giving sub-millisecond response time. *(1 mark)*

**Rule 4 — Demotion on Long CPU Burst:** If the process consumes its *entire* time slice $\tau_i$ at queue $Q_i$, it is moved to $Q_{i+1}$. **Example:** A long-running matrix-multiplication job starting at $Q_0$ with $\tau_0 = 10$ ms gets demoted to $Q_1$ (with $\tau_1 = 20$ ms), then to $Q_2$ (with $\tau_2 = 40$ ms), and so on. *(1 mark)*

**Example walkthrough:** Suppose $\tau_0 = 10$ ms and the job uses the full slice → moves to $Q_1$ with $\tau_1 = 20$ ms; uses full slice again → moves to $Q_2$ with $\tau_2 = 40$ ms. The scheduler has now *learned* that this is a CPU-bound job and gives it a long time slice at low priority, freeing the high-priority queues for interactive tasks. *(2 marks for example synthesis)*

**(b) Three Problems and Fixes [7 marks]:**

**Problem 1 — Starvation:** If the system has many interactive jobs that keep arriving at $Q_0$ (Rule 3), a CPU-bound job in $Q_2$ may **never get CPU** because Rule 1 always prefers higher-priority queues. **Fix — Rule 5 (Priority Boost):** Periodically (every $S$ seconds, typically $S = 1$ s), **all jobs are forcibly moved back to $Q_0$**. This guarantees every job a fresh chance to be scheduled. *(2 marks)*

**Problem 2 — Gaming the Scheduler:** A CPU-bound job can **trick** the scheduler by issuing a quick I/O (e.g., `usleep(1)`) just before its time slice expires. Because it "yielded voluntarily," Rule 4 (naïve version) does not demote it, and the job stays at $Q_0$ forever, monopolising the top queue. **Fix — Rule 4b (Accounting Fix):** The scheduler must account for time **based on the tick at which the slice started**, not on whether the process yielded voluntarily. A job is demoted if it consumed its time slice *regardless* of yield behaviour. *(3 marks)*

**Problem 3 — Sudden Change in Programme Behaviour:** A job that has been CPU-bound (now in $Q_{L-1}$) may suddenly become interactive (e.g., a compiler finishes heavy parsing and starts waiting for user keystrokes). It is "stuck" in the lowest queue with poor response time. **Fix — Rule 5 (Boost)** automatically rescues it by moving it back to $Q_0$ after time $S$. *(2 marks)*

---

#### **QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **design philosophy** of the Linux **Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)**. Discuss the role of the **red-black tree** and the **`vruntime` counter** in achieving fair CPU scheduling.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given `sched_latency = 20 ms`, `sched_min_granularity = 4 ms`, and three runnable tasks with `nice = 0`, `nice = +5`, and `nice = +10`, compute:
- (i) The CFS time slice for each task. *(3 marks)*
- (ii) The CPU share of each task over a 1-second window. *(4 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Design Philosophy of CFS [7 marks]:**

The CFS design philosophy is to **model an "ideal, perfectly fair" CPU** — a hypothetical processor that could run $N$ tasks simultaneously, each at $\frac{1}{N}$-th of full speed. CFS approximates this ideal by keeping close track of who has run for how long, and scheduling whoever has run the *least*. *(2 marks)*

To achieve this, CFS maintains a **per-task `vruntime` counter** representing how much CPU time the task has *fairness-consumed*. The increment per real execution $\Delta t$ is:

$$
\Delta \text{vruntime} = \Delta t \times \frac{1024}{w(\text{nice})}
$$

The factor $\frac{1024}{w(\text{nice})}$ **decouples real time from fair time**: a high-priority (negative nice, large weight) task accumulates `vruntime` slowly, so it gets scheduled more frequently for the same wall-clock execution. *(2 marks)*

The runqueue of CFS is a **left-leaning red-black (LLRB) tree** keyed by `vruntime`. At every scheduling decision, CFS picks the **leftmost node**, i.e., the task with the **smallest `vruntime`** — the one that has been most "under-served." Insertion, deletion, and lookup are all **O(log N)**, giving CFS excellent scalability on systems with thousands of runnable tasks. *(2 marks)*

A sleeping/waking task has its `vruntime` set to the **minimum vruntime of currently runnable tasks**, so that on wake-up it is immediately placed near the left end of the tree and gets CPU promptly — eliminating interactive lag. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Numerical Computation [7 marks]:**

**(i) CFS Time Slice [3 marks]:**

Using the granular formula:

$$
T_{\text{slice}} = \max\!\left(\frac{T_{\text{latency}}}{N},\ T_{\text{min\_granularity}}\right) = \max\!\left(\frac{20\ \text{ms}}{3},\ 4\ \text{ms}\right) = \max(6.67,\ 4) = \mathbf{6.67\ \text{ms}}
$$

Each of the 3 tasks runs for **6.67 ms** per round.

**(ii) CPU Shares [4 marks]:**

First compute weights:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w(0)  &= \frac{1024}{1.25^{0}}  = \mathbf{1024} \\
w(5)  &= \frac{1024}{1.25^{5}}  = \mathbf{335.54} \\
w(10) &= \frac{1024}{1.25^{10}} = \mathbf{109.86}
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of weights:

$$
W_{\text{total}} = 1024 + 335.54 + 109.86 = \mathbf{1469.40}
$$

Proportional shares in a 1000 ms window:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPU}_{0}  &= 1000 \times \frac{1024}{1469.40} \approx \mathbf{697.0\ \text{ms}} \\
\text{CPU}_{5}  &= 1000 \times \frac{335.54}{1469.40} \approx \mathbf{228.3\ \text{ms}} \\
\text{CPU}_{10} &= 1000 \times \frac{109.86}{1469.40} \approx \mathbf{74.7\ \text{ms}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $697.0 + 228.3 + 74.7 = 1000\ \text{ms} \checkmark$

**Ratio:** $697.0 : 228.3 : 74.7 \approx 9.33 : 3.05 : 1.00$, exactly matching $\frac{1}{1.25^{n}}$ for the three nice values.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 (MLFQ + CFS):**

- **MLFQ** = *learning-based*, *multi-queue*, *priority-dynamic* scheduler invented by Corbató (1962, CTSS).
- **Five Basic Rules** (memorise verbatim for KTU):
  1. Higher priority runs.
  2. Same priority ⇒ round robin.
  3. New job → top queue.
  4. Full slice consumed ⇒ demote.
  5. Every $S$ seconds ⇒ boost all to top.
- **Rule 4b (Accounting Fix)** — demote even if process voluntarily yielded; prevents *gaming*.
- **MLFQ optimises:** *Response time* (high initial priority) + *Turnaround time* (long slices for CPU-bound jobs).
- **MLFQ parameters:** $L$, $\tau_0, \tau_1, \ldots, \tau_{L-1}$, $S$.
- **CFS** = *fair-share, proportional-share, RB-tree* scheduler by Ingo Molnár, Linux 2.6.23+.
- **CFS invariant:** always runs the runnable task with the **smallest `vruntime`**.
- **vruntime formula (non-negotiable for ESE):**
  $\Delta \text{vruntime} = \Delta t \times \dfrac{1024}{w(\text{nice})}$.
- **Weight formula:**
  $w(\text{nice}) = \dfrac{1024}{1.25^{\,\text{nice}}}$ with $W_0 = \mathbf{1024}$.
- **Time slice formula:**
  $T_{\text{slice}} = \max\!\left(\dfrac{T_{\text{latency}}}{N},\ T_{\text{min\_granularity}}\right)$.
- **Typical tunables:** $T_{\text{latency}} \approx 24$ ms, $T_{\text{min\_granularity}} \approx 4$ ms, `sched_nr_latency = 8`.
- **Runqueue:** Red-Black tree, **O(log N)** complexity.
- **Sleep/Wake handling:** Set waking task's `vruntime` to **min of runnable vrtimes** → places it at the leftmost end → immediate CPU.
- **Nice value mapping:** Linux range $[-20, +19]$; $-20$ = highest priority (weight ≈ 3122), $+19$ = lowest (weight ≈ 19).
- **Proportional fairness invariant:**
  $\text{CPU}_i = T_{\text{total}} \times \dfrac{w_i}{\sum_j w_j}$.
- **MLFQ vs CFS:** MLFQ = *heuristic + queues*; CFS = *mathematical + RB-tree*; both aim for the same goal (interactive responsiveness + fair CPU distribution).
- **EEVDF successor (Linux 6.6, 2023):** Adds *deadline-aware* proportional-share on top of CFS philosophy; *not in current KTU syllabus but bonus mark if mentioned*.

> **End of Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme, PCCST403 Operating Systems, Module 1.**
