| 8 | $T_3$ (retry) | $1$ | CAS($L$, $0$, $1$) | Fail | $1$ |

**Observations** (textual explanation of each step):
- **Step 1:** $T_1$ is the first to issue CAS, sees $L = 0$, swaps in $1$, enters critical section.
- **Step 2–5:** Both $T_2$ and $T_3$ spin uselessly because $L = 1$ (CAS fails on the compare).
- **Step 6:** $T_1$ writes $0$ (a regular store, since by the lock protocol only the owner releases).
- **Step 7–8:** Whichever of $T_2$ / $T_3$ wins the next CAS race acquires the lock; the other continues spinning.

This trace makes the **non-determinism of CAS retry** explicit — a frequent KTU exam question.

## 3.3 ABA Counterexample — Pop from a Lock-Free Stack

**Stack node structure** (simplified):

$$
\text{Node} = \{ \text{int value}; \text{Node* next}; \}
$$

**Top pointer** $T$ points to the head node.

**Naïve pop operation** (Thread X):

1. Read $p = T$.
2. Read $q = p \rightarrow \text{next}$.
3. Execute $\text{CAS}(T, p, q)$ — i.e., swap Top from $p$ to $q$.

**ABA scenario (linear trace):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial state:} \quad T &\rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow \text{NULL} \\
\text{(1) Thread X reads } p = A, \, q = B \\
\text{(2) Thread X preempted.} \\
\text{(3) Thread Y pops A:} \quad T &\rightarrow B \rightarrow \text{NULL} \\
\text{(4) Thread Y pops B:} \quad T &\rightarrow \text{NULL} \\
\text{(5) Thread Y pushes a new node } A' \text{ (same address as A):} \quad T &\rightarrow A' \rightarrow \text{NULL} \\
\text{(6) Thread X resumes, executes CAS}(T, A, B) \text{ — succeeds because } T = A' = A \text{ in value} \\
\text{(7) RESULT: stack corrupted — } T \text{ now points to } B \text{ which was already freed.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **textual explanation:** even though the *pointer value* $A$ is unchanged at step 6, the *object it points to* is a completely different node with different `next`. The stack's logical state is broken, and dereferencing $B$ may cause a use-after-free.

**Mitigation (tagged pointer) — derivation:**

Pack a monotonically increasing version counter with the pointer:

$$
\text{TaggedPtr} = \{ \text{Node* ptr}; \text{uint32_t tag}; \}
$$

Perform double-width CAS on the full 64-bit word. Even if the *pointer* matches, a different *tag* value will cause CAS to fail, preventing the ABA corruption.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Flowchart of the CAS Instruction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thread invokes CAS V A B] --> B[CPU issues LOCK prefix on bus]
    B --> C[Read current value of V into hidden register]
    C --> D{Is V == A?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Locked store of B into V]
    E --> F[Return TRUE to calling thread]
    F --> G[Thread proceeds with critical section]
    D -- No  --> H[Discard value B]
    H --> I[V remains unchanged]
    I --> J[Return FALSE to calling thread]
    J --> K[Thread re-reads V and retries CAS]
    K --> C
```

**Diagram rationale (for the student reading the printed notes):**
- The decision diamond **D** represents the *compare* stage of CAS.
- Branch **Yes** leads to the *write* stage (E) and ultimately success.
- Branch **No** forces a **retry loop** (K → C), which is the busy-wait of spinlocks.
- The `LOCK prefix` node (B) is the *hardware atomicity guard* — without it, two cores could pass the comparison and both write, breaking mutual exclusion.

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Three Threads Competing for a CAS-Based Spinlock

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T1 as Thread T1
    participant CPU as CPU / Cache Line
    participant T2 as Thread T2
    participant T3 as Thread T3

    T1->>CPU: CAS(L, 0, 1)  -- locked bus transaction
    CPU-->>T1: success (returns 1, L=1)
    T2->>CPU: CAS(L, 0, 1)
    CPU-->>T2: fail (returns 0, L still 1)
    T3->>CPU: CAS(L, 0, 1)
    CPU-->>T3: fail (returns 0, L still 1)
    Note over T2,T3: Both spin, repeatedly issuing CAS
    T1->>CPU: store L = 0  -- owner releases
    T2->>CPU: CAS(L, 0, 1)  -- lucky thread wins
    CPU-->>T2: success (returns 1, L=1)
    T3->>CPU: CAS(L, 0, 1)  -- already too late
    CPU-->>T3: fail
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hardware Implementation of `CMPXCHG` on x86

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AX/EAX Register] --> C[Comparator Unit]
    B[Memory location V] --> C
    C -->|Equal| D[Enable Write Port]
    D --> E[Write B into V]
    C -->|Not Equal| F[Disable Write Port]
    F --> G[ZF flag = 0]
    E --> H[ZF flag = 1]
    H --> I[Return true]
    G --> J[Return false]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **accumulator (A)** holds the expected value $A$.
- **Memory (B)** supplies the current value of $V$.
- The **Comparator (C)** outputs a single bit: match or mismatch.
- On match, the **write port (D)** is enabled and the new value $B$ is stored.
- The **ZF (zero flag) (G/H)** is set so the calling code can branch on the result.
- The entire unit is wrapped in a **LOCK prefix** at the bus-interface layer, ensuring that the compare, the conditional write, and the flag update form one indivisible transaction.

## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram — Test-and-Set vs. Compare-and-Swap

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TNS[Test and Set]
        A1[Read V] --> B1[Unconditionally write 1 to V]
        B1 --> C1[Return old value of V]
    end
    subgraph CAS[Compare and Swap]
        A2[Read V] --> B2{V == A ?}
        B2 -- Yes --> C2[Write B to V]
        B2 -- No  --> D2[Do not write]
        C2 --> E2[Return TRUE]
        D2 --> F2[Return FALSE]
    end
```

**Key takeaway for revision:** Test-and-Set *always* writes; Compare-and-Swap *conditionally* writes. This conditionality is what makes CAS composable into lock-free algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define the Compare-and-Swap (CAS) atomic instruction. List the three operands it accepts and explain what the return value signifies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Compare-and-Swap (CAS) is a **hardware-supported atomic instruction** used for process synchronization in multiprocessor systems. It operates on **three operands**:
1. **V** — the memory location whose value is to be modified.
2. **A** — the expected old value (what the calling thread believes V currently contains).
3. **B** — the new value to be written if A matches V.

The instruction atomically performs the following: it reads the current value of V, compares it with A, and **only if they are equal**, it overwrites V with B. It then **returns a Boolean** — **true (1)** if the swap actually took place, and **false (0)** if V did not match A and no write occurred. This atomicity is guaranteed by the CPU's bus-locking or cache-coherence protocol. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is the ABA problem in the context of Compare-and-Swap? Mention one technique to overcome it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **ABA problem** occurs when a CAS-using thread reads a value $A$ from a memory location, is **preempted**, and during that preemption the location's value cycles from $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow A$. When the thread resumes and performs CAS, it **incorrectly succeeds** because the value still matches $A$, even though the *logical state* of the data structure has changed. This can lead to **data corruption**, especially in lock-free stacks and queues.

**One mitigation:** Use **tagged pointers** — pack a monotonically increasing version counter alongside the pointer so that even if the pointer value repeats, the tag differs and CAS fails. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
**(a)** Explain the **Compare-and-Swap (CAS)** instruction in detail. Describe its **operational steps**, the **atomicity guarantees** provided by the hardware, and the role of the **LOCK prefix** in the x86 implementation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using CAS, design a **spinlock** for a uniprocessor-emulated multiprocessor system. Provide the **pseudocode** for `lock()` and `unlock()`, explain the **busy-wait loop**, and prove that the resulting lock satisfies **mutual exclusion**. Discuss **one limitation** of your design. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Detailed explanation of CAS (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
CAS is an atomic, hardware-supported instruction that conditionally updates a memory location. It is formally denoted as $\text{CAS}(V, A, B)$, where $V$ is the target memory word, $A$ is the expected value, and $B$ is the new value.

**Step 2 — Operational steps (2 marks):**
The CPU executes the following sequence **atomically**:
1. Read the current value of $V$ into a hidden internal register.
2. Compare the read value with $A$.
3. If equal, perform a **locked store** of $B$ into $V$ and return `true`.
4. If not equal, leave $V$ unchanged and return `false`.

**Step 3 — Atomicity mechanism (2 marks):**
On the x86 architecture, CAS is implemented via the `CMPXCHG` instruction. When prefixed with `LOCK`, the CPU:
- Asserts the `LOCK#` pin on the system bus.
- Acquires **exclusive ownership** of the cache line containing $V$ (MESI protocol transition to **Modified** state).
- Inhibits all other cores, DMA devices, and interrupt handlers from reading or writing $V$ for the duration of the compare-and-swap.

On ARM, the same is achieved using the `LDREX` (load-exclusive) and `STREX` (store-exclusive) pair — the store succeeds only if no other core wrote to the same address in between.

**Step 4 — Importance (1 mark):**
CAS is the building block of **lock-free algorithms**, **spinlocks**, **seqlocks**, and **RCU** in modern operating systems. Its atomicity enables software to compose complex synchronization protocols without kernel intervention.

**Step 5 — Return value (1 mark):**
The Boolean return is essential: a `false` return tells the caller to **re-read** $V$ and retry, enabling the spin-loop pattern that defines lock-free progress.

---

**(b) CAS-based spinlock design (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Data structure (1 mark):**

```c
typedef struct { volatile int locked; } cas_spinlock_t;
```

The `volatile` qualifier prevents the compiler from caching the value in a register, forcing every load to come from memory.

**Step 2 — lock() implementation (2 marks):**

```c
void cas_lock(cas_spinlock_t *L) {
    // Spin until CAS successfully changes locked from 0 to 1
    while (CAS(&L->locked, 0, 1) == 0) {
        // Optional: __builtin_ia32_pause();  // x86 PAUSE = power + pipeline flush
    }
}
```

The thread repeatedly issues `CAS(L->locked, 0, 1)` — i.e., *if locked is 0, set it to 1* — until the swap succeeds. The body of the loop is a **busy-wait**: the thread consumes CPU cycles without yielding to the OS scheduler.

**Step 3 — unlock() implementation (1 mark):**

```c
void cas_unlock(cas_spinlock_t *L) {
    L->locked = 0;  // Only the owner calls this (by protocol)
}
```

A plain store is sufficient because the lock protocol guarantees that only the thread which observed `CAS == 1` may write `0`.

**Step 4 — Mutual-exclusion proof (2 marks):**

Assume two threads $T_1$ and $T_2$ both successfully execute `CAS(L->locked, 0, 1)` simultaneously. By the atomicity of CAS, the two operations are **strictly serialized** in real time. Let $T_1$'s CAS be earlier. It reads $L = 0$ and writes $L = 1$. When $T_2$'s CAS subsequently reads $L$, it observes $1 \neq 0$ and fails. Thus, only one thread can transition $L$ from $0$ to $1$, and that thread alone enters the critical section. **Mutual exclusion holds.**

**Step 5 — One limitation (1 mark):**

**No fairness guarantee (starvation possible).** A thread may theoretically lose the CAS race indefinitely if a steady stream of other threads keeps acquiring the lock first. Additionally, on a **uniprocessor**, a thread holding the lock cannot be preempted *only if* the OS disables preemption during the critical section; otherwise, busy-waiting wastes the entire CPU.

---

### Question B. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2 — Alternative]**
**(a)** Compare the **Test-and-Set** and **Compare-and-Swap** instructions in terms of **operational semantics**, **busy-wait efficiency**, and **composability** for lock-free data structures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **ABA problem** in detail. Construct a **concrete counterexample** using a lock-free stack `pop` operation, show the trace where CAS succeeds incorrectly, and discuss **two mitigation techniques** (tagged pointers and double-width CAS) with their trade-offs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Test-and-Set vs. Compare-and-Swap (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Operational semantics (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Test-and-Set (TAS) | Compare-and-Swap (CAS) |
|---|---|---|
| Operands | 1 (memory location) | 3 ($V$, $A$, $B$) |
| Read | Yes | Yes |
| Compare | None | Yes, against $A$ |
| Write | **Unconditional** — always writes 1 | **Conditional** — only writes $B$ if $V = A$ |
| Return | Old value of $V$ | Boolean (true if swapped) |

**Step 2 — Busy-wait efficiency (2 marks):**
TAS causes the *thundering-herd* problem: every waiting thread repeatedly writes $1$ to the lock, causing **cache-line ping-pong** — the lock bounces between cores, invalidating each cache line on every test. CAS, by contrast, only writes when the value matches the *current* expected state. With proper retry logic, a thread that observes $L = 1$ does *not* write at all, dramatically reducing coherence traffic. Algorithms like the **MCS lock** leverage CAS to give each waiter a *local* spin variable, achieving $O(1)$ coherence traffic per acquisition.

**Step 3 — Composability (2 marks):**
TAS is monolithic — it can only *test-and-set*, with no notion of "what was expected." CAS is **composable**: a thread can perform arbitrary computation between read and write, *then* attempt CAS to commit its result. This is the basis of **lock-free linked lists** (Harris list, Michael list), **lock-free skip lists** (the Herlihy–Shavit construction), and **RCU** style updates. TAS cannot express the "optimistic update + validate" pattern that these algorithms require.

**Step 4 — Verdict (1 mark):**
CAS is strictly more expressive than TAS — in fact, **TAS can be implemented using a single CAS**, but the reverse is not true. Modern OS kernels (Linux ≥ 2.6, Windows, FreeBSD) prefer CAS-based primitives for this reason.

---

**(b) The ABA Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Formal definition (1 mark):**
The ABA problem arises when a thread $T$ reads a value $A$ from memory, is suspended (preempted or descheduled), and during that suspension the value at that location changes $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow A$. When $T$ resumes and issues $\text{CAS}(V, A, B)$, the instruction **succeeds** because $V = A$, but the *logical state* the thread was reasoning about has been altered by the interleaving.

**Step 2 — Concrete counterexample — Lock-free stack `pop` (3 marks):**

Let the stack initially be $T \rightarrow N_A \rightarrow N_B \rightarrow \text{NULL}$, where $T$ is the top pointer and $N_A$, $N_B$ are heap-allocated nodes.

- **Step α:** Thread $T_1$ (the popper) executes `p = T` (reads $N_A$) and `q = p.next` (reads $N_B$).
- **Step β:** $T_1$ is preempted.
- **Step γ:** Thread $T_2$ pops: $\text{CAS}(T, N_A, N_B)$ succeeds, $T = N_B$.
- **Step δ:** Thread $T_2$ pops again: $\text{CAS}(T, N_B, \text{NULL})$ succeeds, $T = \text{NULL}$. $N_B$ is freed.
- **Step ε:** Thread $T_2$ pushes a *new* node whose address happens to equal $N_A$'s (memory reuse): $\text{CAS}(T, \text{NULL}, N_A')$ succeeds. $T = N_A'$ (same address as old $N_A$).
- **Step ζ:** $T_1$ resumes, executes $\text{CAS}(T, N_A, N_B)$. The address matches, so it **succeeds** — but $N_B$ is already freed! Result: **use-after-free, stack corruption**.

**Step 3 — Mitigation 1 — Tagged pointers (1.5 marks):**
Combine the pointer with a **version counter** in a single 64-bit word: $\text{tagged} = (\text{tag} \ll 48) \lor \text{ptr}$. Perform a **double-width CAS** on the 64-bit word. Even if the pointer recycles, the tag differs (it is incremented on every update), so CAS will correctly fail in step ζ. **Trade-off:** Requires 64-bit atomic primitives (available on all modern 64-bit CPUs); reduces address space from 64 bits to ~48 bits; not directly portable to 32-bit systems.

**Step 4 — Mitigation 2 — Hazard pointers / RCU (1.5 marks):**
The popping thread $T_1$ *publishes* a *hazard pointer* to $N_A$ before dereferencing it. Any thread that wants to free $N_A$ must first scan all hazard pointers and confirm no one is about to dereference it. This delays reclamation but is **ABA-proof** by construction. **Trade-off:** Adds an extra per-thread variable and a quiescent-state wait on every delete; only amortized, not constant time.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks on CAS questions (KTU 2024 pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing Test-and-Set with Compare-and-Swap.** Students write "CAS sets the value to 1 unconditionally" — this is **wrong** and costs 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the atomicity argument.** Many students describe the *logic* of CAS but never mention the **LOCK prefix** / **bus locking** / **MESI cache line exclusivity**. The KTU rubric allocates **2 marks** specifically for the atomicity mechanism.
> 3. **In the ABA question, omitting the version counter.** Tagging is the *most commonly expected* mitigation; merely saying "use a mutex" is **not acceptable** (mutexes defeat the purpose of lock-freedom).
> 4. **Failing to prove mutual exclusion.** A *trace* is not a *proof*. State the contradiction explicitly: assume two threads both succeed in CAS, then show that CAS atomicity makes this impossible.
> 5. **Spelling / notation slips:** `CompareAndSwap` vs. `Compare-and-Swap` vs. `CAS` — KTU accepts any, but mixing `C.A.S.` with `compare_and_swap` in the same answer reads as careless.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read this the night before the exam):**

- **CAS = Compare-And-Swap**, a *hardware atomic* instruction with **three operands**: $V$ (memory), $A$ (expected), $B$ (new).
- **Return value** is a **Boolean** — true if the swap occurred, false if $V \neq A$.
- **Atomicity** is provided by the CPU's `LOCK CMPXCHG` (x86) or `LDREX/STREX` (ARM), or `LL/SC` pair (MIPS / RISC-V).
- CAS is **strictly more expressive** than Test-and-Set: TAS can be *implemented* via CAS, but not vice versa.
- **Primary use cases**: spinlocks, lock-free data structures (stack, queue, hash table), atomic counters, RCU.
- **CAS is *lock-free* but *not wait-free*** — at least one thread makes progress, but a specific thread may starve.
- **The ABA problem** = value cycles $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow A$ while a thread is preempted; CAS incorrectly succeeds.
- **ABA mitigations**: tagged pointers, double-width CAS, hazard pointers, RCU, LL/SC retry counters.
- **CAS-based spinlock pseudocode** must use a **busy-wait loop** (spin) until `CAS(L, 0, 1)` returns true.
- **Mutual-exclusion proof** relies on the **atomicity** of CAS — assume two successes, derive contradiction via serialization.
- **Limitation of CAS spinlock**: no fairness / starvation, cache-line ping-pong (mitigated by MCS / CLH queue locks).
- **Connection to syllabus**: Module 2 of PCCST403 — "Process Synchronization"; pair CAS with **Test-and-Set**, **Swap**, **Fetch-and-Add** under the umbrella of "Hardware Support for Synchronization."
- **Examiner hot-button**: 14-mark questions almost always ask (a) a *definition + atomicity* sub-part and (b) an *ABA + mitigation* sub-part. Master both patterns.
- **Memory hook**: *"CAS = Conditional Atomic Set"* — atomicity + condition are the two pillars.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Queues: Sleeping Instead of Spinning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Using Queues: Sleeping Instead of Spinning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of operating system kernel design, **sleeping instead of spinning** refers to a synchronization discipline in which a process (or kernel thread) that finds a required resource unavailable voluntarily relinquishes the CPU and is placed on a **wait queue (sleep queue)**. The process remains in a non-runnable state until the resource holder explicitly **wakes** it up, at which point it is re-inserted into the scheduler's ready queue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wait Queue (Sleep Queue):** A kernel-managed FIFO/LIFO list of process descriptors (e.g., `task_struct` in Linux) whose execution is currently blocked awaiting a specific event or resource. Each synchronization primitive (semaphore, mutex, condition variable, read/write endpoint of a pipe) is associated with an internal wait queue.

The opposite approach — **busy-waiting (spinning)** — keeps the process in the **RUNNING** state, repeatedly polling a flag in a tight loop until the resource is released. The sleep mechanism is the cornerstone of efficient blocking I/O, semaphores, and condition synchronization inside the kernel.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you arrive at a popular college canteen during the lunch rush. There are two ways to get your samosa:

1. **Spinning (Busy-Wait):** You stand in the middle of the counter, eyes glued to the cook, and repeatedly shout *"Is it ready? Is it ready?"* every microsecond. You block everyone else, you burn CPU cycles, and you get nothing until the samosa appears.
2. **Sleeping (Wait Queue):** You take a numbered token from the counter, walk to a bench, sit down, and chat with your friends. The cook, once the samosa is ready, calls out your token number over a PA system. You hear it, get up, and collect your samosa.

In the first case, you consumed CPU the entire time. In the second, you consumed **zero** CPU until the wakeup signal arrived. The wait queue is the **bench + PA system**, the sleep operation is **"take token, sit down"**, and the wakeup operation is **"announce token number"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU 2024 Scheme:** Module 2 of PCCST403 covers *Process Synchronization & Deadlock*. The "Sleeping Instead of Spinning" paradigm is the conceptual foundation for implementing **semaphores, condition variables, blocking I/O, and event-driven kernels**. The classic textbook treatment is in Silberschatz (Chapter 6/7) and Tanenbaum (Chapter 2/5). You will see direct questions on this in Part A and Part B.

### 1.3 The Two Philosophical Models at a Glance

| Aspect | Spinning (Busy-Wait) | Sleeping (Wait Queue) |
|---|---|---|
| Process State | **RUNNING** | **BLOCKED (SLEEPING)** |
| CPU Consumption | Burns 100 % of the time slice | Burns **0 %** while asleep |
| Latency to Resource | Immediate (poll cycle) | Higher (scheduler overhead) |
| Fairness | Starves other processes | Allows others to use the CPU |
| Use Case | Short critical sections, multiprocessor spinlocks | Long waits (I/O, locks, events) |
| Kernel Mechanism | `while (!flag) ;` | `sleep_on(q, condition); ... wake_up(q);` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (Boundary Condition):** The general engineering rule taught in KTU modules is — **spin only if the expected wait time $t_{wait}$ is less than the cost of two context switches $2 \cdot t_{cs}$**, i.e., spin if $t_{wait} < 2 t_{cs}$. Otherwise, sleep. This is why Linux's `mutex` sleeps but its `spinlock_t` (on uniprocessor) historically disabled preemption instead of spinning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Fundamental Kernel Primitives

The sleep/wakeup abstraction in any modern OS is built from exactly three primitives:

1. **`sleep(channel, priority)`** — Atomically: (a) mark current process as **SLEEPING**, (b) enqueue it on the wait queue `channel`, (c) yield the CPU via the scheduler.
2. **`wakeup(channel)`** — Wake **one** (or **all**) processes sleeping on the wait queue `channel` and move them back to the **READY** state.
3. **`block()` / `unblock()`** — Generic wrappers used by higher-level constructs (semaphores, I/O subsystem).

> [!NOTE]
> The argument `channel` is essentially the **identity of the event or resource**. Historically it was a memory address (a `wait_queue_head_t`); in modern Linux it is an explicit wait queue object.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — The Discipline of "Sleeping"

To prevent **lost wakeups** and **race conditions**, the canonical sleep discipline (as described by Tanenbaum, *Modern Operating Systems*, Chapter 2) requires that the resource check and the sleep be **atomic with respect to the wakeup**. The standard sequence is:

**Step 1.** Acquire a protecting lock (or disable interrupts, in the simplest textbook kernel).
**Step 2.** **Check** the resource condition.
**Step 3.** If condition is not satisfied, enqueue on wait queue and call `sleep`.
**Step 4.** The lock is released implicitly when the process is put to sleep (atomic with the state change).
**Step 5.** Producer/resource-holder later changes the condition, then calls `wakeup`.
**Step 6.** Scheduler resumes one of the awakened processes, which re-checks the condition (it must re-check because of *spurious wakeups*).

### 2.3 Producer–Consumer with Sleep/Wakeup — Canonical Form

This is the **single most important exam diagram** for this topic. The producers insert items; consumers remove items. When the buffer is empty, the consumer sleeps on `&full`. When the buffer is full, the producer sleeps on `&empty`.

```
Producer:
  while (1) {
       produce_item(&item);
       down(&mutex);          // protect buffer
       if (count == N)
            sleep(&empty);    // buffer full → sleep on "empty" queue
       insert_item(item);
       count = count + 1;
       if (count == 1)
            wakeup(&full);    // buffer was empty → wake a consumer
       up(&mutex);
  }

Consumer:
  while (1) {
       down(&mutex);
       if (count == 0)
            sleep(&full);     // buffer empty → sleep on "full" queue
       remove_item(&item);
       count = count - 1;
       if (count == N-1)
            wakeup(&empty);   // buffer was full → wake a producer
       up(&mutex);
       consume_item(item);
  }
```

> [!WARNING]
> The textbook version above (without a real semaphore) has the **lost-wakeup bug**. The fix in Tanenbaum's kernel is to make `sleep` itself save the state into a per-process flag (e.g., `proc[i].flag = SLEEPING`) and let `wakeup` set the flag first, then call `sleep` which atomically re-checks the flag. This is the KTU high-yield trick — **memorize the lost-wakeup fix**.

### 2.4 The Lost-Wakeup Problem — Step-by-Step

| Time | Consumer C | Producer P |
|---|---|---|
| $t_0$ | Checks `count == 0` → true | — |
| $t_1$ | About to call `sleep(&full)` | — |
| $t_2$ | **Interrupted** (context switch) | Inserts item, sets `count = 1` |
| $t_3$ | — | Calls `wakeup(&full)` — **but no one is on the queue!** |
| $t_4$ | (still running) Calls `sleep(&full)` | — |
| $t_5$ | Goes to sleep **forever** | — |

The wakeup at $t_3$ was **lost** because the consumer had not yet enqueued itself. The semaphore (or the per-process flag) solves this because the increment and the wake decision happen **atomically inside the OS kernel**, not in user code.

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Quantity | Equation / Definition | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| CPU time wasted by spinning | $T_{waste} = t_{wait} \cdot v_{spin}$ | $t_{wait}$ = expected wait; $v_{spin}$ = CPU cycles per spin iteration |
| Decision threshold | $\text{Sleep if } t_{wait} \;\geq\; 2 \cdot t_{cs}$ | $t_{cs}$ = context-switch time |
| Average ready-queue length (M/M/1) | $L_q \;=\; \dfrac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$, with $\rho = \lambda/\mu$ | $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $\mu$ = service rate |
| Context-switch overhead factor | $f_{cs} \;=\; \dfrac{t_{cs}}{t_{cpu}}$ | Ratio of switch cost to useful CPU time |
| Wait-queue ordering | FIFO (most kernels) / Priority-ordered (real-time kernels) | Determines fairness vs responsiveness |
| Wakeup fan-out | $\text{wakeup}(q) \Rightarrow 1$ (default) or $k$ (broadcast) | Granularity of the wakeup call |
| Lost-wakeup guard invariant | $\text{enqueue}(p) \;\prec\; \text{release}(lock)$ | "Queue yourself before releasing the lock" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Do not confuse with a `spinlock`**: A spinlock *may* spin on a multi-core system for very short durations (a few hundred ns). It is **not** the same as a busy-wait semaphore. KTU questions sometimes test this distinction — read the wording carefully.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linux kernel:** Every `wait_event()`, `wait_for_completion()`, blocking `read()`, and `sem_down()` is built on the sleep/wakeup infrastructure (`wait_queue_head_t`).
- **Windows NT kernel:** Uses **Dispatcher Objects** (Events, Mutants, Semaphores, Threads, Timers), each with an embedded wait list — conceptually identical to sleep queues.
- **Real-time OS (RTEMS, VxWorks):** Priority-ordered sleep queues with O(1) wakeup using bitmap indexing.
- **Database engines:** Row-level locks with sleep queues for blocked transactions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The Five States of a Sleeping Process

The transition table below is the *primary analytical tool* the KTU examiner expects. Memorize it.

| From State | Trigger | To State | Side-Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| RUNNING | `sleep(q)` called, condition false | **SLEEPING (BLOCKED)** | Enqueue on `q`; yield CPU |
| SLEEPING | `wakeup(q)` invoked on its queue | READY | Dequeue from `q`; insert into ready queue |
| READY | Scheduler dispatches it | RUNNING | Re-acquires CPU |
| RUNNING | Resource available on re-check | RUNNING | Spurious wakeup handled by re-check |
| SLEEPING | Wait timeout expires (kernel timer) | READY | Equivalent to wakeup from timer interrupt |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spurious Wakeup:** A process can be awakened even when no producer signaled it (e.g., due to signals, timer expiry, kernel races). The correct discipline is therefore: **always re-check the condition in a `while` loop** (`while (count == 0) sleep(&full);`), never in a single `if`.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Sleep-vs-Spin Decision Threshold

The decision rule is derived from a simple cost comparison. Let:

- $t_{wait}$ = expected time the resource remains unavailable
- $t_{cs}$ = average time to perform a context switch (save state, schedule, restore)

**Cost of Spinning (Busy-Wait):**

$$C_{spin} \;=\; t_{wait} \quad \text{(the process holds the CPU the entire time)}$$

**Cost of Sleeping:**

The OS will switch the process out ($1 \cdot t_{cs}$), and on wakeup switch it back in ($1 \cdot t_{cs}$). So:

$$C_{sleep} \;=\; 2 \cdot t_{cs}$$

Sleep is **cheaper** than spin if and only if:

$$C_{sleep} \;<\; C_{spin}$$

$$2 \cdot t_{cs} \;<\; t_{wait}$$

Rearranging the inequality:

$$\boxed{\;t_{wait} \;>\; 2 \cdot t_{cs}\;}$$

This is the **boundary condition** for choosing sleep over spin. It also explains why **interrupt top halves** in Linux still disable preemption + spin (no context switch possible from interrupt context), while bottom halves and process context use sleep.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Lost-Wakeup Probability (Toy Model)

Suppose the producer and consumer execute on a single CPU with preemptive scheduling, and the consumer's gap between *"check condition"* and *"call sleep"* is $n$ instructions long. If the OS timer-tick interrupt falls anywhere inside this window, a lost wakeup can occur.

Let $\tau$ be the time of the window (in CPU cycles). Let $T_{tick}$ be the timer tick period. Assuming uniform distribution of the timer interrupt over the window:

$$P_{lost} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\tau}{T_{tick}}$$

If $\tau = 10$ instructions $\approx 30$ ns on a 1 GHz CPU, and $T_{tick} = 10$ ms, then:

$$P_{lost} \;\approx\; \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-9}}{10 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 3 \times 10^{-6}$$

Per execution this is tiny, but over a kernel that schedules **billions** of events per second, the *expected number* of lost wakeups per unit time is non-trivial:

$$E[\text{lost}] \;=\; \lambda_{events} \cdot P_{lost}$$

For a server with $\lambda_{events} = 10^5$ /s, $E[\text{lost}] = 10^5 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-6} = 0.3$ lost wakeups per second. **Hence every real kernel implements the atomic sleep discipline.**

### 3.4 Comparative Matrix — Spinning vs Sleeping on Multi-Core

| Criterion | Single Core | Multi-Core (SMP) |
|---|---|---|
| Spin uses CPU | Wastes the *only* core | Wastes *one* core but makes progress on others |
| Optimal policy | Always sleep | Hybrid: spin briefly, then sleep (the basis of Linux `futex` and Solaris `adaptive_mutex`) |
| Power efficiency | Sleep always better | Spin for ≤ tens of µs is acceptable |
| Implementation cost | Simple disable-interrupts | Requires per-core data + memory barriers |
| Used by | Textbook kernels, RTOS uniprocessor | Linux, Windows NT, FreeBSD |

### 3.5 Why the Queue, Not a Flag?

A single boolean flag is insufficient because:

- It has **only two states** (0 or 1), but a queue may have **$N$ sleeping processes** (e.g., multiple readers blocked on an empty pipe).
- A flag cannot store **identity** — the wakeup cannot be directed to a *specific* waiter (e.g., the highest-priority one).
- A flag is **not atomic** with the sleep; a queue implemented in the kernel can be guarded by `cli`/`sti` (disable/enable interrupts) or spinlocks, making the enqueue + state-change atomic.

Therefore, in any real OS, **the wait queue IS the synchronization primitive**, and the boolean flag is merely *one field of the resource's state* that the queue guards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** If a KTU question asks *"Why is a wait queue preferred over a boolean flag for synchronization?"* — answer using the three points above: multi-waiter support, identity/priority, and atomicity with state change.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Sleep and Wakeup Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Process P in RUNNING state"] --> B{"Resource available?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Acquire and use resource"]
    B -- No --> D["Enqueue P on wait queue WQ"]
    D --> E["Set state = SLEEPING"]
    E --> F["Yield CPU to scheduler"]
    F --> G["Scheduler picks another process"]
    G --> H["Resource holder calls wakeup WQ"]
    H --> I["Dequeue P from WQ"]
    I --> J["Set state = READY"]
    J --> K["Insert P into ready queue RQ"]
    K --> L["Scheduler dispatches P"]
    L --> M["P re-checks resource condition"]
    M --> B
    C --> N["Process continues or terminates"]
    N --> O["End of lifecycle"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence — Producer / Consumer Sleep/Wakeup

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Consumer
    participant WQ as Wait Queue full
    participant P as Producer
    participant RQ as Ready Queue
    Note over C: count = 0
    C->>C: lock mutex
    C->>C: while count == 0
    C->>WQ: sleep_on(full)
    Note over C: C is now SLEEPING
    Note over P: Producer arrives
    P->>P: lock mutex
    P->>P: insert item
    P->>P: count = count + 1
    P->>WQ: wakeup(full)
    WQ-->>RQ: dequeue C, mark READY
    P->>P: unlock mutex
    RQ->>C: schedule C
    C->>C: re-check while loop
    C->>C: count > 0, proceed to remove_item
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Kernel Sleep/Wakeup Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UserLayer["User Process Layer"]
        UP["Application thread"]
    end
    subgraph SyncLayer["Synchronization Library"]
        SEM["Semaphore / Mutex / CondVar"]
    end
    subgraph KernelLayer["Kernel Subsystem"]
        SLP["sleep channel primitive"]
        WKP["wakeup primitive"]
        WQ["Wait queue head struct"]
        SCH["Scheduler"]
    end
    subgraph HwLayer["Hardware"]
        CPU["CPU core"]
        TMR["Timer interrupt source"]
    end
    UP --> SEM
    SEM --> SLP
    SEM --> WKP
    SLP --> WQ
    WKP --> WQ
    WQ --> SCH
    SCH --> CPU
    TMR --> SCH
    SCH --> UP
```

### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Process States During Sleep/Wakeup

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW
    NEW --> READY
    READY --> RUNNING
    RUNNING --> SLEEPING : sleep channel condition false
    SLEEPING --> READY : wakeup channel
    SLEEPING --> READY : timeout fires
    RUNNING --> READY : preempted
    RUNNING --> [*] : exit
    READY --> [*] : killed
    SLEEPING --> [*] : killed
```

### 4.5 Schematic — The Two Wait Queues of a Bounded Buffer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Buff["Bounded Buffer of size N"]
        B0["slot 0"]
        B1["slot 1"]
        BN["slot N-1"]
    end
    subgraph FullQ["Wait queue 'full'"]
        F1["Consumer 1 waiting"]
        F2["Consumer 2 waiting"]
    end
    subgraph EmptyQ["Wait queue 'empty'"]
        E1["Producer 1 waiting"]
        E2["Producer 2 waiting"]
    end
    P["Producer thread"] --> Buff
    C["Consumer thread"] --> Buff
    Buff -- count = 0 --> FullQ
    Buff -- count = N --> EmptyQ
    P -- wakeup --> FullQ
    C -- wakeup --> EmptyQ
```

> [!NOTE]
> The buffer in the middle acts as the **shared state**. The two wait queues are the two channels of synchronization. The integer `count` plus the buffer slots is the *resource*; the queues are the *coordination* mechanism.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PCCST403]`
> **Differentiate between busy-waiting (spinning) and sleeping (blocking) synchronization. In which situation would you prefer busy-waiting? Mention any one real-world OS that uses both mechanisms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Busy-Wait (Spinning) | Sleeping (Blocking) |
|---|---|---|
| Process state | RUNNING (CPU bound) | BLOCKED / SLEEPING |
| CPU usage | Burns cycles while waiting | Zero CPU usage while waiting |
| Latency | Low (no scheduler overhead) | Higher (context switch needed) |
| Use case | Short waits, multi-core spinlocks | Long waits (I/O, locks, events) |

**Preferred when** the expected wait time is **less than $2 \cdot t_{cs}$** (two context switches). **Example:** Linux uses `spinlock_t` for short critical sections inside interrupt context, and `mutex` (which sleeps) for long-held user locks. (3 marks)

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCCST403]`
> **What is a wait queue in an operating system? Explain the role of the `wakeup` primitive. Why is a single boolean flag insufficient to replace a wait queue?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **wait queue** is a kernel-managed linked list of processes that are currently blocked awaiting a specific event or resource. Each process is represented by a `task_struct` (or equivalent PCB). The `wakeup(queue)` primitive moves one (or all) processes from the wait queue back to the ready queue and sets their state to **READY**.

A **boolean flag is insufficient** because:
1. It cannot represent **more than one waiter** simultaneously.
2. It cannot capture **identity or priority** of waiting processes.
3. **Atomicity** between state-check and sleep cannot be guaranteed on a flag, leading to **lost-wakeup** bugs. (3 marks)

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] **Explain the producer–consumer problem using sleep and wakeup primitives (Tanenbaum's model). Write the pseudo-code for both producer and consumer and explain each line.**

**(b)** [7 Marks] **Describe the lost-wakeup problem. With the help of a timing diagram, show how a wakeup can be lost in the producer–consumer solution, and explain how a semaphore or per-process flag fixes it.**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (a)

The producer–consumer problem involves one or more producers generating items and placing them in a bounded buffer, and one or more consumers removing them. When the buffer is **full**, the producer must wait; when it is **empty**, the consumer must wait.

**Pseudo-code (Tanenbaum textbook kernel, simplified):**

```text
#define N 100
int count = 0;
struct wait_queue full  = {SLEEPING, 0};
struct wait_queue empty = {SLEEPING, 0};

void producer(void) {
    int item;
    while (TRUE) {
        item = produce_item();             // 1. create item
        down(&mutex);                      // 2. enter CS
        if (count == N)
            sleep_on(&empty);              // 3. full → wait
        insert_item(item);                 // 4. deposit
        count = count + 1;                 // 5. update count
        if (count == 1)
            wakeup(&full);                 // 6. was empty → wake consumer
        up(&mutex);                        // 7. exit CS
    }
}

void consumer(void) {
    int item;
    while (TRUE) {
        down(&mutex);                      // 1. enter CS
        if (count == 0)
            sleep_on(&full);               // 2. empty → wait
        item = remove_item();              // 3. fetch
        count = count - 1;                 // 4. update count
        if (count == N - 1)
            wakeup(&empty);                // 5. was full → wake producer
        up(&mutex);                        // 6. exit CS
        consume_item(item);                // 7. use item
    }
}
```

**Explanation of each step (mark-distribution):**
- `sleep_on` atomically puts the process into the **SLEEPING** state and adds it to the named queue (1 mark).
- `wakeup` moves one process from the wait queue to the **READY** state (1 mark).
- Two separate queues `full` and `empty` allow independent signalling (1 mark).
- The mutex protects `count` and the buffer slots (1 mark).
- The `if (count == 1)` and `if (count == N - 1)` are *boundary-trigger* wakeups, avoiding thundering herd (1 mark).
- Pseudo-code clarity and indentation (1 mark).
- Real-world mapping (1 mark).

#### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**The lost-wakeup problem** arises when the sequence *"check condition → call sleep"* is interrupted in between by a context switch. The producer may signal a wakeup **before** the consumer has actually enqueued itself on the wait queue, causing the wakeup to disappear into the void.

**Timing diagram (textual, mark each arrow):**

```text
t0  Consumer C: enters CS, sees count == 0
t1  Consumer C: about to call sleep_on(&full)         [preempted here]
t2  Scheduler  : dispatches Producer P
t3  Producer P : enters CS, inserts item, count = 1
t4  Producer P : calls wakeup(&full)  -- nobody on queue!
t5  Scheduler  : resumes Consumer C
t6  Consumer C : calls sleep_on(&full)  -- goes to sleep FOREVER
```

**Fix using a semaphore (or per-process flag):**
1. Replace the explicit `if (count == 0) sleep(...)` with `down(&full)`. The `down` primitive **atomically** decrements the semaphore counter and only sleeps if it becomes negative. There is no window where a wakeup can be lost because the wakeup is implemented as `up` (increment), which is itself atomic in the kernel.
2. In Tanenbaum's older model, the fix is to **store the sleep state in a per-process flag** `proc[i].flag = SLEEPING` and have the producer **set this flag *before* calling `wakeup`**. The consumer then *re-checks* the flag inside an atomic disable-interrupts section before calling `sleep`. (7 marks — see distribution below)

**Mark distribution for (b):**
- Definition of lost-wakeup — 1 mark
- Timing diagram (5 well-labelled time-points) — 2 marks
- Identification of the race window — 1 mark
- Fix explanation (semaphore OR per-process flag) — 2 marks
- Diagram or pseudo-code of fix — 1 mark

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] **Discuss the three kernel primitives `sleep`, `wakeup`, and `block` used for inter-process communication. Show how they interact with the scheduler and the process state diagram.**

**(b)** [7 Marks] **Compare the use of busy-waiting and sleep queues in single-core vs multi-core systems. Derive the decision rule for choosing between them, stating any assumptions made.**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**The three kernel primitives:**

1. **`sleep(channel, priority)`** — Atomically transitions the calling process from **RUNNING** to **SLEEPING**, enqueues its PCB on the wait queue identified by `channel`, and calls the scheduler to pick a new RUNNING process. (2 marks)

2. **`wakeup(channel)`** — Selects one (or all, depending on variant) PCBs from the wait queue `channel`, transitions each from **SLEEPING** to **READY**, and inserts them into the scheduler's ready queue. (2 marks)

3. **`block()` / `unblock()`** — Generic wrappers used by higher-level synchronization primitives (semaphores, I/O completion). `block()` is essentially `sleep(current_resource->wq)`; `unblock()` is `wakeup(current_resource->wq)`. (1 mark)

**Interaction with the scheduler:** When `sleep` is called, the process voluntarily yields; the scheduler then picks the next READY process according to its policy (FCFS, round-robin, priority, etc.). When `wakeup` places a process into the READY queue, the scheduler decides **when** to dispatch it — it is not necessarily run immediately. (1 mark)

**Process state diagram (1 mark):** RUNNING → sleep → SLEEPING → wakeup → READY → schedule → RUNNING.

#### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Single Core | Multi-Core (SMP) |
|---|---|---|
| Spinning | Wastes the *only* core — strictly bad | Wastes one core, others make progress — acceptable for short waits |
| Sleeping | Always preferred | Preferred for waits > a few microseconds |
| Hybrid policy | Not needed | E.g., Linux `futex` — spin first, then sleep |
| Synchronization | Disable interrupts / spin-lock | Atomic instructions + spin-lock + sleep |

**Derivation of the decision rule (3 marks):** Already shown in §3.2 — sleep is preferred if

$$t_{wait} \;>\; 2 \cdot t_{cs}$$

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Preemptive scheduler.
- The wait is non-productive (cannot do useful work in the spin loop).
- The context-switch time is roughly constant per switch.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not confuse `spinlock` with busy-wait *semaphore*.** On a uniprocessor, Linux's `spinlock` does *not* actually spin — it disables preemption. On SMP it spins briefly. Use the precise term.
> - **Always re-check the condition in a `while` loop**, not `if`. If your answer uses `if (count == 0) sleep(...)`, you will lose 1 mark for ignoring spurious wakeups.
> - **Lost-wakeup explanation requires a timing diagram** with at least **5 labelled time points**. A verbal-only explanation will lose 2 marks.
> - **Use the correct terminology**: "wait queue" or "sleep queue", not just "queue"; "PCB" or "task_struct", not "thread object".
> - **State the boundary condition $t_{wait} > 2 t_{cs}$** explicitly in any "spinning vs sleeping" answer.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sleeping = state transition RUNNING → SLEEPING** + enqueue on a wait queue. The process consumes **zero** CPU.
- **Wait queue (sleep queue)** is a kernel-managed list of PCBs blocked on a particular event or resource. It is the **synchronization primitive** itself, not just a data structure.
- **Three primitives:** `sleep`, `wakeup`, `block`. They must be **atomic** with respect to interrupts to avoid lost wakeups.
- **Decision rule:** Sleep if $t_{wait} > 2 \cdot t_{cs}$; spin only if the wait is provably shorter (multi-core SMP, short critical sections).
- **Always re-check the condition** in a `while` loop after wakeup to handle spurious wakeups.
- **Lost-wakeup fix** is implemented inside the OS kernel using semaphores (`down`/`up`) or per-process flags — never in user code alone.
- **Two wait queues per bounded buffer** — one for "full" (consumers), one for "empty" (producers) — with a single mutex protecting the buffer.
- **Single boolean flag ≠ wait queue** — flag cannot store multiple waiters, identity/priority, or atomicity guarantees.
- **Single-core vs multi-core:** spinlocks are *harmful* on a single core for long waits but useful on multi-core; on multi-core, **hybrid (spin-then-sleep)** is the engineering optimum (Linux `futex`, Solaris `adaptive_mutex`).
- **Linux primitives** built on sleep/wakeup: `wait_event`, `wait_for_completion`, blocking `read()`, `sem_down`, `mutex_lock`.
- **KTU buzzwords to use in your answer:** "wait queue", "PCB", "scheduler", "state transition", "atomic", "lost wakeup", "spurious wakeup", "boundary condition $t_{wait} > 2 t_{cs}$".
- **High-weight items in exams:** the lost-wakeup timing diagram (4 marks), the producer–consumer pseudo-code (4 marks), the comparison table (3 marks), and the decision rule derivation (3 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semaphores - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Semaphores - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semaphore (Dijkstra, 1965):** A semaphore is a **non-negative integer synchronization variable** (S) that is accessed *only* through two standard, atomic (indivisible) operations — `wait()` (also called **P** / `P(S)`) and `signal()` (also called **V** / `V(S)`). It is used to control access to common shared resources in a concurrent/parallel programming environment, thereby solving the **Critical Section Problem**.

In the canonical KTU textbook terminology (Silberschatz, Galvin & Gagne — *Operating System Concepts*, 10th Edition framework used by KTU), a semaphore is fundamentally a **kernel-managed integer object** that requires all access to be enclosed within the operating system to enforce atomicity. A process that attempts to decrement a semaphore below zero is **blocked (sleep)** and placed into the semaphore's waiting queue.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Train Station Waiting Token"

Imagine a railway reservation counter that issues only **5 physical tokens** to passengers at a time (this number `5` is the value of the semaphore `S`).

- Each passenger must **acquire a token** before approaching the counter (this is the `wait()` or `P` operation).
- When a passenger finishes, they **return the token** to the token-keeper (this is the `signal()` or `V` operation).
- If all 5 tokens are out and another passenger arrives, they must **stand in a queue and wait** — they cannot proceed (this is the *blocking* behavior of `wait()` when `S = 0`).
- The token-keeper is the **OS Kernel**, ensuring the token count can never be corrupted by two passengers grabbing the same token at the same instant (this is **atomicity**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A semaphore is essentially a "smart counter" with a built-in queue and a built-in sleeping mechanism — it is *not* just a variable, even though its state is stored as a single integer.

## 1.3 The Two Canonical Variants

| Variant | Integer Range | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Binary Semaphore (Mutex Lock)** | $\in \{0, 1\}$ | Mutual exclusion for a single shared resource (e.g., a printer, a memory buffer slot). |
| **Counting Semaphore** | $\in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, N\}$ | Coordinating access to a pool of $N$ identical resources (e.g., 10 database connections, 3 tape drives). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A binary semaphore is *not* identical to a mutex. A mutex has an **ownership concept** (only the locking thread can unlock), while a binary semaphore does not. This is a frequently tested distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Atomic Primitives — Operational Logic

The two operations that act upon a semaphore `S` are **atomic by kernel-enforced guarantee** (typically implemented using hardware instructions like `TSL` — Test-and-Set Lock, or by disabling interrupts on a uniprocessor).

### 2.1.1 The `wait()` Operation (P / Proberen — "to test")

**Logic steps (in order):**
1. **Decrement** the value of the semaphore: `S ← S − 1`.
2. **Check** the new value of `S`.
3. **If** `S < 0` → the resource is unavailable. Place the calling process into the semaphore's **waiting/blocked queue** and **context-switch** to another process (the process is *put to sleep*).
4. **If** `S ≥ 0` → the resource is acquired; the process proceeds into the critical section.

### 2.1.2 The `signal()` Operation (V / Verhogen — "to increment")

**Logic steps (in order):**
1. **Increment** the value of the semaphore: `S ← S + 1`.
2. **Check** the new value of `S`.
3. **If** `S ≤ 0` → this means at least one process was sleeping waiting for `S`. **Wake up** (dequeue) one of the waiting processes from the semaphore's blocked queue and place it back into the **ready queue**.
4. **If** `S > 0` → no process was waiting; just update the counter.

## 2.2 Why the Test of "Less Than Zero" and Not "Equals Zero"?

This is one of the most confusing aspects of the KTU textbook definition. The reason is the **sign convention**:

- When `S > 0` → the value of `S` literally represents the **number of available resource units**.
- When `S ≤ 0` → the **magnitude** $\vert S \vert$ represents the **number of processes sleeping** in the queue.
- When `S = 0` → no resources free, *and* no process is queued yet (the next `wait()` call will be the first to sleep).

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention used here:** We assume `S` is initialized to a positive number (the number of available resources). Some textbooks initialize to `0` for synchronization (signaling between processes), in which case a `wait()` would block the very first caller. KTU predominantly tests the **resource-count initialization**.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semaphore Invariant (Core Law):**
> $$\text{Available Resources} = S \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Waiting Processes} = -\min(S, 0)$$
> Therefore, the **total** number of processes (running + waiting) that have entered the protected region equals:
> $$\text{Max Resources Acquired} = N_{\text{init}} - S$$

| Concept | Mathematical Form | Constraint / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| Initial value of counting semaphore | $S_0$ | $S_0 = $ number of identical resources, $S_0 \geq 1$ |
| Binary semaphore range | $S \in \{0, 1\}$ | Strictly non-negative |
| Counting semaphore range | $S \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, N\}$ | Strictly non-negative integer |
| `wait()` atomic effect | $S := S - 1$ | Pre-condition: $S \geq 0$ at kernel-entry |
| `signal()` atomic effect | $S := S + 1$ | Pre-condition: $S \geq -Q$ where $Q$ is queue size |
| Maximum blocked processes | $\vert S_{\min} \vert$ | When $S$ reaches its most negative value |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Semaphores are the backbone of virtually every modern concurrent system:

- **Linux kernel internals** — used in interrupt handling, buffer management, and device drivers.
- **POSIX Threads (pthreads)** — `sem_init()`, `sem_wait()`, `sem_post()` APIs.
- **Java Concurrency** — `java.util.concurrent.Semaphore` class.
- **Producer-Consumer Problems** — bounded buffer synchronization.
- **Readers-Writers Problem** — controlling multiple concurrent readers.
- **Dining Philosophers Problem** — managing the forks (5 chopsticks = 5-resource counting semaphore).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Pseudocode Specification (Strict KTU Format)

Below is the **complete, exhaustive, atomic pseudocode** for the two semaphore primitives as defined in the KTU syllabus reference (Silberschatz framework).

### 3.1.1 The `wait(S)` Primitive — Full Transition Derivation

```
wait(S):
    S.value = S.value - 1          // Step 1: Atomically decrement the counter
    if (S.value < 0):              // Step 2: Check for resource scarcity
        // Step 3: Resource unavailable → block the process
        add this process to S.queue
        block(this_process)        // Invoke OS scheduler, context-switch out
    else:
        // Step 4: Resource available → proceed to critical section
        return (proceed to critical section)
```

**Transition Logic Explained:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State}_{\text{before}} &= (S_{\text{old}}, Q = \text{queue of waiting procs}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 1:} \quad S_{\text{temp}} &= S_{\text{old}} - 1 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2a (if } S_{\text{temp}} \geq 0\text{):} \quad \text{State}_{\text{after}} &= (S_{\text{temp}}, Q) \text{ and process RUNS} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2b (if } S_{\text{temp}} < 0\text{):} \quad \text{State}_{\text{after}} &= (S_{\text{temp}}, Q \cup \{P_{\text{this}}\}) \\
&\quad \text{and process BLOCKS}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 The `signal(S)` Primitive — Full Transition Derivation

```
signal(S):
    S.value = S.value + 1          // Step 1: Atomically increment the counter
    if (S.value <= 0):             // Step 2: Check if someone is waiting
        // Step 3: At least one process was sleeping
        process = dequeue(S.queue) // Remove the head of the wait queue
        wakeup(process)            // Move it from BLOCKED → READY state
    else:
        // Step 4: No one was waiting → just update the counter
        return
```

**Transition Logic Explained:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State}_{\text{before}} &= (S_{\text{old}}, Q) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 1:} \quad S_{\text{temp}} &= S_{\text{old}} + 1 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2a (if } S_{\text{temp}} > 0\text{):} \quad \text{State}_{\text{after}} &= (S_{\text{temp}}, Q) \text{ no wakeup} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2b (if } S_{\text{temp}} \leq 0\text{):} \quad \text{State}_{\text{after}} &= (S_{\text{temp}}, Q \setminus \{P_{\text{head}}\}) \\
&\quad \text{and } P_{\text{head}} \text{ is now READY}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Numerical Trace — Worked Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A counting semaphore `S` is initialized to `2`. Three processes `P1, P2, P3` invoke `wait()` and `signal()` in the following order. Determine the state of `S` and the queue `Q` at each step.

Let the initial state be $S_0 = 2$ and $Q_0 = \emptyset$.

| Step | Operation | Atomically Evaluated $S$ | Decision | New $Q$ | Final $S$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1: `wait(S)` | $2 - 1 = 1$ | $1 \geq 0$ → RUN | $\emptyset$ | $1$ |
| 2 | P2: `wait(S)` | $1 - 1 = 0$ | $0 \geq 0$ → RUN | $\emptyset$ | $0$ |
| 3 | P3: `wait(S)` | $0 - 1 = -1$ | $-1 < 0$ → BLOCK | $\{P3\}$ | $-1$ |
| 4 | P1: `signal(S)` | $-1 + 1 = 0$ | $0 \leq 0$ → WAKE | $\emptyset$ (P3 woken) | $0$ |
| 5 | P3: re-enters after wakeup | (resumes its pre-blocked execution path) | RUN | $\emptyset$ | $0$ |
| 6 | P2: `signal(S)` | $0 + 1 = 1$ | $1 > 0$ → no wakeup needed | $\emptyset$ | $1$ |

**Observation from the trace:**

- At Step 3, the value `S = -1` is **interpreted** as: "1 process is sleeping in the queue." This confirms the negative-value convention.
- At Step 4, the `signal()` operation correctly identifies the sleeping P3 and wakes it up, returning the system to a state where `S = 0` (0 resources free, 0 processes waiting).

## 3.3 Property Proof — Mutual Exclusion Guarantee

A **binary semaphore** initialized to `1` enforces mutual exclusion. The proof is by contradiction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Assume two processes } P_i \text{ and } P_j \text{ are simultaneously in the CS.} \\[4pt]
\text{For } P_i \text{ to enter, the OS atomically executed } S := S - 1 \text{ with } S_{\text{prev}} \geq 0. \\[4pt]
\text{For } P_j \text{ to enter, the OS atomically executed } S := S - 1 \text{ with } S_{\text{prev}} \geq 0. \\[4pt]
\text{Since the operations are atomic and } S \text{ started at } 1, \\
\text{at most one of these decrements can observe } S_{\text{prev}} = 1. \\[4pt]
\text{The other must observe } S_{\text{prev}} = 0 \text{ and thus block.} \\[4pt]
\Rightarrow \text{Contradiction: both cannot be in CS simultaneously. } \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Semaphore Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Initiates wait S] --> B[Kernel Entry Point]
    B --> C[Disable Interrupts / Lock Bus]
    C --> D[Decrement S to S minus 1]
    D --> E{Is S minus 1 less than 0?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Append Process to S Queue]
    F --> G[Place Process in BLOCKED State]
    G --> H[Re enable Interrupts / Unlock Bus]
    H --> I[Invoke Scheduler Context Switch]
    E -- No --> J[Grant Resource to Process]
    J --> K[Re enable Interrupts / Unlock Bus]
    K --> L[Process Enters Critical Section]

    M[Process Completes CS] --> N[Process Invokes signal S]
    N --> O[Kernel Entry Point]
    O --> P[Disable Interrupts / Lock Bus]
    P --> Q[Increment S to S plus 1]
    Q --> R{Is S plus 1 less than or equal 0?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Dequeue Head Process from S Queue]
    S --> T[Move Process from BLOCKED to READY]
    T --> U[Re enable Interrupts / Unlock Bus]
    R -- No --> V[No Waiter Exists]
    V --> U
```

## 4.2 Semaphore State Transition Diagram (Binary)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> S1: Initialize S equals 1
    S1 --> S0: wait S by P1
    S0 --> Sneg1: wait S by P2
    Sneg1 --> S0: signal S wakes P2
    S0 --> S1: signal S no waiter
    S1 --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Resource-Queue Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SharedPool[Shared Resource Pool N equals 5]
        R1[Resource 1]
        R2[Resource 2]
        R3[Resource 3]
        R4[Resource 4]
        R5[Resource 5]
    end
    subgraph Counter[Int Variable S]
        Val[Current Value equals 5]
    end
    subgraph WaitQ[Blocked Queue Q]
        Px[Waiting Process Px]
    end
    Val -.decrement.- SharedPool
    Val -.if S less than 0.-> WaitQ
    WaitQ -.dequeue on signal.- Val
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set-A]`
**Define a semaphore. What are its two atomic operations?**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A semaphore is a non-negative integer synchronization variable used to control access to shared resources in a concurrent system, ensuring mutual exclusion and coordination. The two atomic operations that act upon a semaphore are:
1. **`wait(S)`** (also called **P(S)**) — decrements `S` and blocks the calling process if `S` becomes negative.
2. **`signal(S)`** (also called **V(S)**) — increments `S` and wakes up a waiting process if any exists in the semaphore's queue.

These operations are guaranteed to be atomic by the operating system kernel, typically using hardware instructions or interrupt disabling mechanisms.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]`
**Differentiate between a binary semaphore and a counting semaphore.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Binary Semaphore | Counting Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| **Value Range** | $\in \{0, 1\}$ | $\in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, N\}$ |
| **Initial Value** | Usually $1$ | Number of identical resources $N$ |
| **Use Case** | Mutual exclusion of a *single* resource | Managing a *pool* of $N$ identical resources |
| **Mutex Equivalent** | Yes, but no ownership concept | No — strictly a counter with queue |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** Many KTU students lose 2-3 marks on semaphore questions by **forgetting the atomicity guarantee** of `wait()` and `signal()`. The two operations must be *uninterruptible* — mentioning "atomicity" or "kernel-level enforcement" is mandatory in your answer. Also, ensure the **state-transition logic of the negative value convention** is clearly explained, as it is worth at least 2-3 marks in any 14-mark question.

---

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Regular Exam]`
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure and working of a **counting semaphore**. List any two differences between a binary and a counting semaphore. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A counting semaphore `S` is initialized to value `3`. Show the state of `S` and the waiting queue after each of the following sequential operations, assuming processes P1, P2, P3, P4, P5 in order:
- `P1: wait(S)`
- `P2: wait(S)`
- `P3: wait(S)`
- `P4: wait(S)`
- `P5: wait(S)`
- `P1: signal(S)`
- `P3: signal(S)`

*(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) Model Solution:**

A **counting semaphore** is a non-negative integer synchronization variable whose value ranges from $0$ to $N$ (where $N$ is the number of identical shared resources). It is initialized to $N$ and accessed only via two atomic primitives.

**Structural Diagram:**

```
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
         │       OS Kernel-Managed Object       │
         │                                       │
         │   Integer Counter:  S.value = N       │
         │   Linked List:     S.queue = { ... }  │
         │                                       │
         └──────────────────────────────────────┘
                          ▲
                          │ atomic
            ┌─────────────┼─────────────┐
            │             │             │
       wait(S) P      wait(S) P      signal(S) V
       (decrement)    (decrement)    (increment)
```

**Working Mechanism:**
- Each `wait(S)` atomically decrements `S.value`. If the new value is $\geq 0$, the process proceeds. If it becomes $< 0$, the process is appended to `S.queue` and put to sleep.
- Each `signal(S)` atomically increments `S.value`. If the new value is $\leq 0$, it implies a process is sleeping; the kernel dequeues and wakes up one such process.

**Two Differences (Binary vs Counting):**

| Aspect | Binary Semaphore | Counting Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| Value range | $\{0, 1\}$ | $\{0, 1, \dots, N\}$ |
| Resources protected | A single shared resource | A pool of $N$ identical resources |

**Part (b) Model Solution — Step-by-Step Trace:**

Initial: $S = 3$, $Q = \emptyset$.

| Step | Op | $S$ after decrement/increment | Decision | $Q$ after step |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1: `wait(S)` | $3-1 = 2$ | $2 \geq 0$ → RUN | $\emptyset$ |
| 2 | P2: `wait(S)` | $2-1 = 1$ | $1 \geq 0$ → RUN | $\emptyset$ |
| 3 | P3: `wait(S)` | $1-1 = 0$ | $0 \geq 0$ → RUN | $\emptyset$ |
| 4 | P4: `wait(S)` | $0-1 = -1$ | $-1 < 0$ → BLOCK | $\{P4\}$ |
| 5 | P5: `wait(S)` | $-1-1 = -2$ | $-2 < 0$ → BLOCK | $\{P4, P5\}$ |
| 6 | P1: `signal(S)` | $-2+1 = -1$ | $-1 \leq 0$ → WAKE P4 | $\{P5\}$ |
| 7 | P3: `signal(S)` | $-1+1 = 0$ | $0 \leq 0$ → WAKE P5 | $\emptyset$ |

**Final state:** $S = 0$, $Q = \emptyset$. Both P4 and P5 have been woken up and moved to the ready queue.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplementary Exam]`
**(a)** What is a semaphore? Explain the **Dijkstra's P and V operations** on a semaphore with their pseudocode. Mention why these operations must be implemented as atomic operations. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Consider the following scenario: A shared buffer has 10 slots. Multiple producer processes write data into the buffer, and multiple consumer processes read from it. Write the **synchronization skeleton** using semaphores to ensure:
- Producers do not write to a full buffer.
- Consumers do not read from an empty buffer.

*(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) Model Solution:**

A **semaphore** is a non-negative integer variable introduced by **Edsger W. Dijkstra (1965)** for process synchronization in concurrent systems. It is accessed only through two atomic operations named **P** (Proberen / "to test") and **V** (Verhogen / "to increment").

**Pseudocode for P(S):**
```
P(S)  or  wait(S):
    S.value = S.value - 1
    if (S.value < 0):
        add this process to S.queue
        block(this process)
```

**Pseudocode for V(S):**
```
V(S)  or  signal(S):
    S.value = S.value + 1
    if (S.value <= 0):
        process = dequeue(S.queue)
        wakeup(process)
```

**Why Atomicity is Mandatory:**

The two operations must be executed as **uninterruptible kernel-level instructions** to prevent **race conditions** on the semaphore variable `S`. If two processes were allowed to read and decrement `S` simultaneously, both might observe `S = 1`, both might decrement to `S = 0$, and both might enter the critical section — violating mutual exclusion. Atomicity is typically guaranteed by:

- Disabling interrupts (on uniprocessors), or
- Hardware-supported atomic instructions like `compare-and-swap`, `test-and-set`, or `fetch-and-add` (on multiprocessors).

**Part (b) Model Solution — Bounded Buffer Synchronization:**

We define the following semaphores:

- `empty` = counting semaphore initialized to **10** (number of empty slots in the buffer).
- `full` = counting semaphore initialized to **0** (number of filled slots).
- `mutex` = binary semaphore initialized to **1** (for protecting the buffer's internal index variables like `in` and `out`).

**Producer Process Skeleton:**
```
Producer:
    while (true):
        produce_item(item)
        wait(empty)              // Block if buffer is full
        wait(mutex)              // Enter critical section
        buffer[in] = item
        in = (in + 1) mod 10
        signal(mutex)            // Exit critical section
        signal(full)             // Increment the count of filled slots
```

**Consumer Process Skeleton:**
```
Consumer:
    while (true):
        wait(full)               // Block if buffer is empty
        wait(mutex)              // Enter critical section
        item = buffer[out]
        out = (out + 1) mod 10
        signal(mutex)            // Exit critical section
        signal(empty)            // Increment the count of empty slots
        consume_item(item)
```

**Logic Verification:**

- When the buffer is full, `empty = 0` → next `wait(empty)` call by a producer blocks.
- When the buffer is empty, `full = 0` → next `wait(full)` call by a consumer blocks.
- The `mutex` binary semaphore guarantees that only one process modifies the `in` / `out` indices at a time, preventing **race conditions** on buffer metadata.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Semaphores: Definition**

- ✅ A **semaphore** is a **non-negative integer synchronization variable** introduced by **Dijkstra (1965)**, accessed only via two atomic operations: `wait()` (P) and `signal()` (V).
- ✅ **Two types:** **Binary** (range $\{0, 1\}$, used for mutual exclusion) and **Counting** (range $\{0, 1, \dots, N\}$, used for managing $N$ resources).
- ✅ **Atomicity** is non-negotiable — both `wait` and `signal` must be executed without interruption to prevent race conditions.
- ✅ **Negative value convention:** When `S.value < 0`, the magnitude $\vert S.value \vert$ indicates the number of processes **sleeping** in `S.queue`.
- ✅ **Order of check:** In `wait()`, the condition is `S.value < 0`; in `signal()`, the condition is `S.value ≤ 0` (this subtle difference is **exam-critical**).
- ✅ **Blocking behavior:** `wait()` on a zero/negative semaphore causes the process to be added to the queue and **context-switched out**.
- ✅ **Waking behavior:** `signal()` wakes **only one** process (FIFO order in the standard implementation), not all waiting processes.
- ✅ A **binary semaphore is NOT a mutex** — mutex has ownership; semaphore does not.
- ✅ Classic KTU applications: **Producer-Consumer (Bounded Buffer)**, **Readers-Writers**, **Dining Philosophers**.
- ✅ Required semaphores for Bounded Buffer: `empty` (init = $N$), `full` (init = $0$), `mutex` (init = $1$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
