## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks each — Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
*State any two differences between a Reader-Writer lock and a binary semaphore.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Reader-Writer Lock | Binary Semaphore |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| Concurrent holders | Multiple readers simultaneously | Exactly one thread |
| Mode distinction | Two access modes (shared/exclusive) | Single access mode (locked/unlocked) |
| Starvation handling | Policies can prevent starvation | Unspecified by default |
| Use case | Read-heavy shared data | Generic mutual exclusion |

*(Any two well-explained points carry 2 marks. Definition-level clarity is expected.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
*Why are spin locks unsuitable for holding across blocking operations? Mention one scenario where a spin lock must be used.*

**Model Answer:**
A spin lock **busy-waits** (consumes CPU cycles) and **disables preemption**. If a thread holding a spin lock attempts to block (e.g., on a `wait_queue`, allocating memory that may sleep, or invoking a mutex), the kernel scheduler cannot preempt it, leading to a **deadlock** — other CPUs waiting for the same lock will spin forever while the holder is descheduled.

**Scenario where spin lock is mandatory:** Protecting shared data accessed inside an **interrupt handler** (interrupt context), because interrupt handlers cannot sleep and must complete in the shortest possible time. (2 marks for correct identification + reasoning.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Full ESE Pattern)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Reader-Preference** and **Writer-Preference** variants of the Reader-Writer lock with suitable pseudo-code. Discuss the starvation problem in each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Describe the Linux kernel **spin lock** implementation. Explain the `test-and-test-and-set` optimization and the role of memory barriers. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Reader-Writer Lock Variants [7 marks]**

**Reader-Preference Pseudo-Code:**

```c
acquire_read_lock(L):
    while (true):
        wait until L.writer_count == 0
        L.reader_count++
        if (L.writer_count == 0):
            break
        else:
            L.reader_count--

release_read_lock(L):
    L.reader_count--

acquire_write_lock(L):
    L.writer_count++
    wait until L.reader_count == 0 AND no other writer

release_write_lock(L):
    L.writer_count--
```

**Behavior:** A new reader is admitted as long as `writer_count == 0`, even if other writers are waiting. **Problem:** Under sustained read traffic, `writer_count > 0` is never reached, and the writer is starved indefinitely.

**Writer-Preference Pseudo-Code:**

```c
acquire_read_lock(L):
    L.read_waiting++
    while (L.writer_count > 0 OR L.write_waiting > 0):
        wait
    L.read_waiting--
    L.reader_count++

acquire_write_lock(L):
    L.write_waiting++
    while (L.reader_count > 0 OR L.writer_count > 0):
        wait
    L.write_waiting--
    L.writer_count = 1
```

**Behavior:** Once a writer announces itself (`write_waiting > 0`), no new readers enter; existing readers finish, then the writer proceeds. **Problem:** If writers keep arriving, readers may starve.

**Fair (FIFO) Solution [extra credit note]:** Use a ticket number `T` and serve requests in arrival order. *(Mention briefly to show breadth.)*

**Common Mark Distribution:**
- 2 marks: Correct pseudo-code for both variants
- 2 marks: Explanation of behavior
- 2 marks: Starvation analysis
- 1 mark: Diagram or summary table

---

**(b) Linux Spin Lock Implementation [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Naive Test-and-Set (1 mark)**

$$L \leftarrow \text{TAS}(L)$$

Problem: Every spin generates a locked bus transaction, causing severe **cache-line bouncing** on SMP systems (the MESI protocol invalidates other CPUs' cached copies constantly).

**Step 2: Test-and-Test-and-Set Optimization (3 marks)**

```c
void spin_lock(spinlock_t *L) {
    preempt_disable();
    while (1) {
        if (atomic_read(&L->locked) == 0) {  // Non-atomic, just cached read
            if (atomic_xchg(&L->locked, 1) == 0)  // Atomic XCHG only once
                break;  // Acquired!
        }
        cpu_relax();  // Hint: PAUSE on x86, reduces power + pipeline flush
    }
}
```

**Effect:** All spinning threads read a *shared cached copy* (`locked == 1`) without triggering bus locks. Only when the lock appears free do they attempt the atomic XCHG. This dramatically reduces cache traffic.

**Step 3: Memory Barriers (2 marks)**

- `smp_mb()` (full memory barrier) is issued *before and after* the atomic operation.
- **Acquire semantics:** After acquiring, all subsequent loads/stores in the critical section see the data the previous owner wrote.
- **Release semantics:** Before releasing, all prior stores in the critical section are visible to the next owner.
- On x86, `XCHG` is *implicitly* a full barrier; on ARM, explicit `DMB` instructions are required.

**Step 4: Preemption Disable (1 mark)**

`preempt_disable()` ensures the holder cannot be context-switched, which would deadlock the system (a preempted holder would sleep, but the lock waits for it on another CPU).

**Mark Distribution Recap:**
- 1 mark: Naive TAS
- 3 marks: Test-and-test-and-set optimization with code
- 2 marks: Memory barrier role
- 1 mark: Preemption disable

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a clear state-transition diagram, explain the **operation of a Reader-Writer lock**. Mention its invariants. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** What are the **limitations of spin locks**? Compare spin locks and mutexes in terms of (i) sleeping behavior, (ii) preemption, (iii) interrupt-safety, and (iv) reentrancy. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Reader-Writer Lock Operation with State Diagram [7 marks]**

*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the state machine.)*

**States and Invariants:**

1. **FREE state:** $R = 0, W = 0$. Lock is unowned. [1 mark]
2. **READ state:** $R \geq 1, W = 0$. Multiple readers may hold the lock concurrently. New readers admitted; writers must wait. [1 mark]
3. **WRITE state:** $R = 0, W = 1$. Single writer holds lock exclusively; all other threads blocked. [1 mark]
4. **WRITE-WAITING state:** A writer is in the queue. Behavior depends on policy (preference). [1 mark]

**Invariants [3 marks]:**
- $R \geq 0$ (always non-negative)
- $W \in \{0, 1\}$ (writer count is binary)
- $R \cdot W = 0$ (cannot have active readers and writer simultaneously)
- $\text{lock\_free} \iff (R = 0 \wedge W = 0)$

**Transitions explained:** State diagram in Section 4.1 should be reproduced. Cover `rdlock`, `wrlock`, `unlock` events and the conditions for each.

---

**(b) Limitations of Spin Locks & Comparison with Mutexes [7 marks]**

**Limitations of Spin Locks [3 marks]:**

1. **CPU Wastage:** Busy-waiting burns CPU cycles — a spinning thread is at 100% CPU utilization.
2. **No Sleeping Allowed:** Holding across blocking calls causes deadlock.
3. **Not Reentrant:** A thread that already holds the lock cannot acquire it again (deadlock).
4. **Priority Inversion:** A low-priority thread holding the lock can block a high-priority spinner indefinitely.
5. **Preemption Disabled:** Side effect that complicates kernel code design.

**Comparison Table [4 marks]:**

| Property | Spin Lock | Mutex |
|----------|-----------|-------|
| (i) Sleeping behavior | **Disallowed** (would deadlock) | **Allowed** (sleeps on wait queue) |
| (ii) Preemption | **Disabled** while held | Not affected (preemptible) |
| (iii) Interrupt safety | **Yes** (use `spin_lock_irqsave` variant) | **No** (cannot use in interrupt context) |
| (iv) Reentrancy | **No** (recursive acquisition deadlocks) | **No** in standard form (Linux `mutex` is non-reentrant; some systems support recursive mutex) |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing rwlock with mutex:** Many students answer "what is a reader-writer lock" by describing a mutex. *Examiner's tip: Always mention "multiple concurrent readers are allowed."*
> 2. **Forgetting to discuss starvation:** When asked about reader-preference, the *starvation of writers* is mandatory. Marks are deducted if omitted.
> 3. **Spin lock + sleeping:** A frequent error is suggesting that a thread can sleep while holding a spin lock. This is a *fatal kernel bug*. Examiners explicitly test this misconception.
> 4. **Skipping memory barriers:** In Linux spin lock questions, the role of `smp_mb()` and preemption disable is often ignored. **3 marks are typically reserved** for this — don't lose them.
> 5. **No pseudo-code in design questions:** A 7-mark sub-question *must* have a diagram or pseudo-code. Pure prose explanations lose structure marks.
> 6. **Mixing up CAS and TAS:** Compare-And-Swap returns success/failure; Test-And-Set always sets and returns the old value. Examiners check this distinction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Reader-Writer Lock:** Multiple readers, single writer. Invariant: $R \cdot W = 0$.
- **Three policies:** Reader-preference (starves writers), Writer-preference (starves readers), Fair/FIFO (no starvation, more overhead).
- **Use rwlock when reads dominate ($\geq 90\%$); use mutex otherwise.**
- **Spin Lock:** Busy-waits; uses atomic TAS or CAS; disables preemption; cannot be held across sleep.
- **Hold time rule:** Spin lock CS must be **< 5 µs** (approx one context switch).
- **Test-and-test-and-set:** Optimization that reads `L` non-atomically first to avoid cache-line bouncing; only attempts atomic XCHG when `L` appears free.
- **Memory barriers:** `smp_mb()` provides acquire/release semantics; without them, the critical section may see stale data on weakly-ordered CPUs (ARM, POWER).
- **Linux variants:**
  - `spin_lock()` — disables preemption
  - `spin_lock_irqsave()` — disables preemption + interrupts (for use with shared interrupt handlers)
  - `spin_lock_bh()` — disables software interrupts (bottom halves)
- **Atomic primitives in Linux:** `atomic_t`, `atomic_read`, `atomic_set`, `atomic_xchg`, `atomic_cmpxchg`.
- **Reentrancy:** Spin locks are NEVER reentrant — recursive acquisition = immediate deadlock on the same CPU and live-lock on SMP.
- **Starvation:** Discussed in both rwlock (R vs W) and spin lock (priority inversion).

> **Key Takeaway in One Line:**
> *"A Reader-Writer lock trades the simplicity of a mutex for parallelism on read-heavy data; a spin lock trades the politeness of a mutex for zero-latency acquisition in non-sleepable contexts."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Semaphores

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semaphore (Dijkstra, 1965):** A semaphore `S` is a non-negative integer synchronization variable that is accessed **only** through two indivisible (atomic) standard operations traditionally called `wait()` (also called `P()` or `down()`) and `signal()` (also called `V()` or `up()`). It is a fundamental tool for solving process/thread synchronization and mutual exclusion problems, proposed by **Edsger W. Dijkstra** in 1965.

Formally, a semaphore is a protected integer variable whose value can only be modified by the atomic `wait` and `signal` operations. The atomicity guarantee is the cornerstone that makes semaphores correct under concurrent execution.

The two canonical atomic operations on a semaphore `S` are:

$$
P(S) \text{ or } wait(S) \text{ or } down(S):
\begin{cases}
\text{while } (S \leq 0) \text{ do no-op}; \\
S \leftarrow S - 1;
\end{cases}
$$

$$
V(S) \text{ or } signal(S) \text{ or } up(S):
\begin{cases}
S \leftarrow S + 1;
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming convention (Dijkstra's Dutch origins):**
> - `P()` comes from the Dutch word *Proberen* (to test).
> - `V()` comes from the Dutch word *Verhogen* (to increment).
> Modern textbooks often prefer the unambiguous names `wait()` and `signal()`.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **library with only 3 identical study rooms** and a single key-rack at the reception desk. The number "3" written on a chalkboard at the rack represents the semaphore value.

- When a student wants a study room, they go to the rack. If the number on the board is greater than 0, they erase it and write (number − 1), then take a room key. This is the **`wait()`** operation — you *decrement* the count, and if it is already 0, you must *wait* (block) on the rack.
- When a student finishes, they return the key. The receptionist *increments* the count on the board. This is the **`signal()`** operation — it *releases* a resource and wakes up one waiting student, if any.

The chalkboard is protected (atomic) because the receptionist handles exactly one student at a time. No student can ever grab a key when the count is already 0, and the count can never go negative. This is exactly how a **counting semaphore** enforces bounded resource access.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a semaphore as a counter + waiting queue + atomic guard.** This mental model is essential before tackling any KTU numerical or trace-based question.

## 1.3 Classification of Semaphores

There are two principal types of semaphores in the KTU syllabus:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Binary Semaphore (Mutex Lock)**
> - Value is restricted to either `0` or `1`.
> - Equivalent to a simple lock used to provide **mutual exclusion** to a critical section.
> - Also called a **mutex semaphore**.
> - Same notation: `wait(mutex)` and `signal(mutex)`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2. Counting Semaphore**
> - Value can range over **non-negative integers**: $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$.
> - Initial value `S` represents the number of identical **shared resources** available.
> - Used to control access to a finite pool of `N` identical resources (e.g., database connection pool of size 10).

## 1.4 Distinction: Semaphore vs. Mutex (Common Confusion)

| Property | Binary Semaphore | Mutex (Lock) |
|---|---|---|
| Owner / Ownership | No ownership — any process may `signal` | Strict ownership — only the locking thread may unlock |
| Priority Inversion Handling | Unprotected | Supports priority inheritance |
| Use case | Signaling between tasks | Mutual exclusion only |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Atomic Operations — Why Atomicity is Non-Negotiable

If `wait()` and `signal()` were not atomic, two concurrent decrements on a shared counter would produce a **race condition** (lost update problem). Suppose `S = 1` and two processes $P_1, P_2$ both call `wait(S)` interleaved:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1: \text{ reads } S = 1 \\
P_2: \text{ reads } S = 1 \quad \text{(race!)} \\
P_1: S \leftarrow 0 \\
P_2: S \leftarrow 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Both processes wrongly think the resource is available. Hence, the test-and-decrement in `wait()` (and the increment in `signal()`) must be executed as a single, **uninterruptible** machine instruction — typically implemented using hardware-supported **compare-and-swap (CAS)** or **test-and-set (TAS)** instructions.

## 2.2 The Blocking Semaphore (Modern Definition)

The classical spin-wait definition above wastes CPU cycles. The **modern, blocking definition** (used in all KTU 2024 Scheme questions) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
wait(S): \quad & S \leftarrow S - 1; \\
& \text{if } S < 0 \text{ then } \text{add this process to } S.\text{queue and block}; \\
\\
signal(S): \quad & \text{if } S.\text{queue is not empty then wake one waiting process}; \\
& S \leftarrow S + 1;
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Invariant:** The value of `S` is the number of *available* permits, while the number of blocked processes in `S.queue` plus the in-use permits is constant and equal to the **initial** value $\vert S_0 \vert$. The semaphore value is therefore always *non-negative* in its abstract logical sense, but in the implementation the integer can briefly go negative to indicate "this many processes are queued".

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Expression / Condition | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Counting semaphore range** | $S \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ | Logical value cannot be negative |
| **Internal counter** | $S_{internal} \in \mathbb{Z}$ | May be negative ($-n$ means $n$ waiting processes) |
| **Invariant** | $\vert \text{in-use} \vert + \vert \text{blocked} \vert = \vert S_0 \vert$ | Conservation of permits |
| **Binary semaphore** | $S \in \lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$ | Strict mutual exclusion |
| **Resource pool size** | $N$ resources $\Rightarrow$ initial $S_0 = N$ | Counting semaphore value |
| **Critical section guard** | `wait(mutex); …; signal(mutex);` | $S_0 = 1$ (binary) |
| **Producer–Consumer buffer** | $S_{empty} = N, \; S_{full} = 0, \; S_{mutex} = 1$ | Standard initial values |
| **Readers–Writers** | $S_{rw\_mutex} = 1, \; S_{mutex} = 1, \; readcount = 0$ | $S$ + integer counter combo |
| **Dining Philosophers** | $S_i = 1 \; \forall i$ | One chopstick semaphore per philosopher |
| **Atomic hardware primitive** | `test_and_set()` / `compare_and_swap()` | Used to implement semaphore |
| **P() / V() operations** | $P(S): \text{decr} \mid V(S): \text{incr}$ | Dijkstra notation |
| **Starvation bound** | $O(n)$ for $n$ processes in worst case | FIFO queue = strict fairness |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *logical* non-negative value of a semaphore with the *internal* machine counter. In the **blocking** implementation, the counter can indeed become negative. Always state the *abstract* invariant: $S \geq 0$ in the logical model.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Semaphores are the bedrock synchronization primitive in:

- **Linux kernel `kernel/sem.c`** — implements System V semaphores for inter-process communication.
- **POSIX named and unnamed semaphores** (`sem_init`, `sem_wait`, `sem_post`) used in multithreaded servers.
- **Producer–Consumer pipelines** in streaming systems (Kafka, FFmpeg decoders).
- **Database connection pools** where the number of connections is bounded.
- **Embedded RTOS** (FreeRTOS binary semaphores for ISR-to-task signaling).

## 2.5 Critical Properties (Why Semaphores Work)

1. **Mutual exclusion** is guaranteed for a binary semaphore with $S_0 = 1$.
2. **Bounded resource usage** is enforced for a counting semaphore initialized to the resource count.
3. **No busy-waiting** in the blocking implementation — the kernel parks the process in a wait queue and re-schedules it.
4. **Atomicity** is delegated to hardware primitives like `test_and_set` (TAS) or `compare_and_swap` (CAS), making the abstraction portable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Traces & Analytical Matrix

> Since semaphores are not a formulaic mathematical topic, the "derivation" here takes the form of **rigorous logical traces** and **invariant proofs** that examiners expect at the 14-mark level.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mutual Exclusion Invariant for a Binary Semaphore

**Claim:** If `mutex` is a binary semaphore initialized to $S_0 = 1$, then at most one process can be inside the critical section at any time.

**Proof by contradiction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Assume at time } t: & \text{ two processes } P_1 \text{ and } P_2 \text{ are both in the critical section.} \\
\text{For } P_1 \text{ to enter:} & \text{ it must have completed } wait(\text{mutex}) \Rightarrow \text{mutex was decremented.} \\
\text{For } P_2 \text{ to enter:} & \text{ it must have completed } wait(\text{mutex}) \Rightarrow \text{mutex was decremented again.} \\
\text{Since } S_0 = 1: & \text{ after first } wait: \text{mutex} = 0. \\
& \text{ second } wait \text{ must block because } \text{mutex} = 0. \\
\text{Contradiction.} & \Rightarrow \text{ Mutex property holds.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Trace — Producer–Consumer Problem (Standard KTU Question)

**Problem Setup:** Bounded buffer of size $N = 5$. One producer, one consumer. Initialize:

$$
S_{empty} = 5, \quad S_{full} = 0, \quad S_{mutex} = 1
$$

**Producer Process:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{while (true)} \lbrace \\
&\qquad \text{produce item } x; \\
&\qquad wait(S_{empty}); \quad \text{/* decrement empty slots */} \\
&\qquad wait(S_{mutex}); \quad \text{/* enter critical section */} \\
&\qquad \text{put } x \text{ into buffer}; \\
&\qquad signal(S_{mutex}); \quad \text{/* leave critical section */} \\
&\qquad signal(S_{full}); \quad \text{/* increment filled slots */} \\
&\rbrace
\end{aligned}
$$

**Consumer Process:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{while (true)} \lbrace \\
&\qquad wait(S_{full}); \quad \text{/* wait for an item */} \\
&\qquad wait(S_{mutex}); \quad \text{/* enter critical section */} \\
&\qquad \text{remove item } y \text{ from buffer}; \\
&\qquad signal(S_{mutex}); \quad \text{/* leave critical section */} \\
&\qquad signal(S_{empty}); \quad \text{/* increment empty slots */} \\
&\qquad \text{consume item } y; \\
&\rbrace
\end{aligned}
$$

### Trace Table (Buffer fills then empties)

| Step | Producer / Consumer Action | $S_{empty}$ | $S_{full}$ | $S_{mutex}$ | Buffer State |
|------|---------------------------|-------------|------------|-------------|--------------|
| 0 (init) | — | 5 | 0 | 1 | Empty |
| 1 | P produces, wait(empty)→4, wait(mutex)→0, put, signal(mutex)→1, signal(full)→1 | 4 | 1 | 1 | [x₁] |
| 2 | P produces, wait(empty)→3, put, signal(full)→2 | 3 | 2 | 1 | [x₁, x₂] |
| 3 | C consumes, wait(full)→1, wait(mutex)→0, get, signal(mutex)→1, signal(empty)→4 | 4 | 1 | 1 | [x₂] |
| 4 | C consumes, wait(full)→0, get, signal(empty)→5 | 5 | 0 | 1 | Empty |
| 5 | P produces, wait(empty)→4, wait(full)→1 | 4 | 1 | 1 | [x₃] |

> [!NOTE]
> **Invariant Maintained:** $\vert S_{empty} \vert + \vert S_{full} \vert = N = 5$ is preserved at every step. This is the **conservation invariant** examiners test for full marks.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Order of `wait` and `signal` Operations

| Synchronization Pattern | Correct Order | Consequence of Wrong Order |
|---|---|---|
| Single mutex (CS) | `wait(mutex); …; signal(mutex);` | Standard — no other way |
| Producer–Consumer | `wait(empty); wait(mutex); put; signal(mutex); signal(full);` | Reversing `empty` and `mutex` ⇒ works but `mutex` held longer; reversing `put` and `signal` ⇒ race |
| Readers–Writers (read-pref) | `wait(mutex); readcount++; if first → wait(rw); signal(mutex); … read … ; wait(mutex); readcount--; if last → signal(rw); signal(mutex);` | Wrong `if last` check ⇒ extra signal ⇒ writer enters with readers present |
| Dining Philosophers | `wait(chopstick[i]); wait(chopstick[(i+1) % 5]); eat; signal(chopstick[i]); signal(chopstick[(i+1) % 5]);` | All grab right first ⇒ circular wait ⇒ deadlock |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Proof: Semaphore Implementation using `test_and_set`

The atomic hardware primitive `test_and_set(target)` atomically reads `target`, sets it to `true`, and returns the old value. It is the standard building block for a spinlock-based semaphore.

**Implementation of binary semaphore `wait(S)`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.\ & \text{key} = \text{true}; \\
2.\ & \text{while}(\text{test\_and\_set}(\& \text{lock})) \; \text{/* spin on local lock */} \\
3.\ & \quad \text{no-op}; \\
4.\ & \text{if } S.\text{value} == 1 \text{ then } S.\text{value} \leftarrow 0; \\
5.\ & \text{else} \lbrace \text{add this process to } S.\text{list; } \text{block}(\text{lock}); \rbrace \\
6.\ & \text{lock} = \text{false};
\end{aligned}
$$

**Implementation of `signal(S)`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.\ & \text{while}(\text{test\_and\_set}(\& \text{lock})) \; \text{no-op}; \\
2.\ & \text{if } S.\text{list} \neq \emptyset \text{ then} \lbrace \text{wakeup}(P); S.\text{value} \leftarrow 0; \rbrace \\
3.\ & \text{else } S.\text{value} \leftarrow 1; \\
4.\ & \text{lock} = \text{false};
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works:** The local `lock` variable ensures only one process can be in the body of `wait`/`signal` at a time. The "value" of `S` is then manipulated without race conditions. A blocked process releases the spinlock *before* sleeping, so the waker can acquire it.

## 3.5 Counting Semaphore from Binary Semaphores (Classic Derivation)

A counting semaphore can be constructed from `N` binary semaphores plus a tracking integer, though a more efficient KTU-standard construction uses a *guard* binary semaphore plus the count:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{data:} \quad \text{integer } value; \quad \text{binary\_semaphore } \text{waitqueue}; \\
\\
& wait(\text{count\_sem } S): \quad & wait(S.\text{waitqueue}); \\
& \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \quad & S.\text{value} \leftarrow S.\text{value} - 1; \\
& \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \quad & \text{if } S.\text{value} < 0 \text{ then } \lbrace \text{block}; signal(S.\text{waitqueue}); \rbrace; \\
& \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \quad & \text{else } signal(S.\text{waitqueue}); \\
\\
& signal(\text{count\_sem } S): \quad & wait(S.\text{waitqueue}); \\
& \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \quad & S.\text{value} \leftarrow S.\text{value} + 1; \\
& \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \quad & \text{if } S.\text{value} \leq 0 \text{ then } \text{wakeup}(P); \\
& \qquad \qquad \qquad \qquad \quad & signal(S.\text{waitqueue});
\end{aligned}
$$

The `waitqueue` binary semaphore serves as a **mutual-exclusion lock** around the integer `value`, ensuring atomicity of test-and-decrement.

## 3.6 Deadlock and Starvation Analysis Matrix

| Problem | Phenomenon | Cause | Classic Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deadlock** | All processes blocked forever | Circular wait on resources | Order resource acquisition; Dining Philosophers with one priority reversal |
| **Starvation** | A process never gets the resource | Unfair queue policy (LIFO) | Use **FIFO** waiting queue |
| **Priority Inversion** | Lower-priority process holds resource needed by high-priority | Scheduling pre-emption | **Priority Inheritance Protocol** (used in Mars Pathfinder fix) |
| **Lost Wakeup** | `signal` occurs before `wait` and the wakeup is lost | Race between check and block | Atomic `wait` (block inside the lock) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Semaphore Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Semaphore Variable S"] --> B["Binary Semaphore"]
    A --> C["Counting Semaphore"]
    B --> B1["Value in 0 or 1"]
    B --> B2["Used for Mutual Exclusion"]
    B --> B3["Equivalent to Mutex Lock"]
    C --> C1["Value in Non Negative Integers"]
    C --> C2["Represents N Resources"]
    C --> C3["Used for Resource Pool Access"]
    A --> D["Atomic Operations"]
    D --> D1["wait or P or down"]
    D --> D2["signal or V or up"]
    D1 --> E1["Decrement S"]
    D1 --> E2["Block if S becomes negative"]
    D2 --> F1["Increment S"]
    D2 --> F2["Wakeup one waiting process"]
```

## 4.2 Process State Transition under `wait()` and `signal()`

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Running
    Running --> WaitingQueue : wait S where S equal 0
    Running --> CriticalSection : wait S where S greater than 0
    CriticalSection --> Running : signal S
    WaitingQueue --> ReadyQueue : signal S wakes process
    ReadyQueue --> Running : Scheduler dispatches
    CriticalSection --> [*] : Process exits
```

## 4.3 Producer–Consumer Synchronization Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ProducerSide["Producer Process"]
        P1["Produce Item"] --> P2["wait empty slots"]
        P2 --> P3["wait mutex"]
        P3 --> P4["Append to Buffer"]
        P4 --> P5["signal mutex"]
        P5 --> P6["signal full slots"]
        P6 --> P1
    end

    subgraph SharedBuffer["Bounded Buffer of size N"]
        Buf["BUF with N slots"]
    end

    subgraph ConsumerSide["Consumer Process"]
        C1["wait full slots"] --> C2["wait mutex"]
        C2 --> C3["Remove from Buffer"]
        C3 --> C4["signal mutex"]
        C4 --> C5["signal empty slots"]
        C5 --> C6["Consume Item"]
        C6 --> C1
    end

    P4 -- writes --> Buf
    C3 -- reads --> Buf
```

## 4.4 Dining Philosophers — Deadlock Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Ph0["Philosopher 0"] -->|waits on| Ch0["Chopstick 0"]
    Ph0 -->|waits on| Ch1["Chopstick 1"]
    Ph1["Philosopher 1"] -->|waits on| Ch1
    Ph1 -->|waits on| Ch2["Chopstick 2"]
    Ph2["Philosopher 2"] -->|waits on| Ch2
    Ph2 -->|waits on| Ch3["Chopstick 3"]
    Ph3["Philosopher 3"] -->|waits on| Ch3
    Ph3 -->|waits on| Ch4["Chopstick 4"]
    Ph4["Philosopher 4"] -->|waits on| Ch4
    Ph4 -->|waits on| Ch0

    Deadlock["Circular Wait equals Deadlock"] -.-> Ph0
    Deadlock -.-> Ph1
    Deadlock -.-> Ph2
    Deadlock -.-> Ph3
    Deadlock -.-> Ph4
```

## 4.5 Semaphore Implementation Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Application Layer - Synchronization Primitives"] --> L2["Semaphore API - wait signal"]
    L2 --> L3["Internal - integer value and waiting queue"]
    L3 --> L4["Spinlock Layer - test_and_set or compare_and_swap"]
    L4 --> L5["Hardware Layer - Atomic CPU Instruction"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a semaphore. Differentiate between binary and counting semaphores.**

**Model Answer:**

A **semaphore** is a non-negative integer synchronization variable introduced by Dijkstra (1965), accessed only through two atomic operations: `wait()` (also called `P()` or `down()`) which decrements the value, and `signal()` (also called `V()` or `up()`) which increments it. If a `wait()` would make the value negative, the calling process is blocked and placed in a FIFO queue.

| Aspect | Binary Semaphore | Counting Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| Value range | $\lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$ | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| Initial value | 1 (typical) | $N$ (number of resources) |
| Purpose | Mutual exclusion | Bounded resource allocation |
| Queue | At most one waiting process | Many waiting processes |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the `wait()` and `signal()` operations. Why must they be executed atomically?**

**Model Answer:**

The `wait(S)` operation tests the value of `S` and, if positive, decrements it; otherwise the process blocks. The `signal(S)` operation increments `S` and wakes one blocked process, if any. Atomicity is essential because without it, two concurrent processes could both read `S = 1`, both decrement it, and both enter the critical section — violating mutual exclusion. Atomicity is usually guaranteed by hardware primitives such as `test_and_set` or `compare_and_swap`, or by disabling interrupts within the kernel.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme rule: Each Part B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Two alternative questions (A and B) are provided as the internal choice.

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the producer–consumer problem using semaphores. State the initial values of the semaphores used.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Trace the producer–consumer problem for buffer size $N = 3$ with 4 items produced and 3 items consumed. Show the state of the semaphores and the buffer at each step.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Producer–Consumer Using Semaphores

**Semaphores Used and Their Initial Values:**

$$
S_{empty} = N \quad (\text{counts empty slots}), \quad S_{full} = 0 \quad (\text{counts filled slots}), \quad S_{mutex} = 1 \quad (\text{binary lock})
$$

**Producer Code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{while (true)} \lbrace \\
&\quad \text{produce an item } x; \\
&\quad wait(S_{empty}); \quad /* \text{wait for an empty slot} */ \\
&\quad wait(S_{mutex}); \quad /* \text{enter critical section} */ \\
&\quad \text{put } x \text{ into buffer}; \\
&\quad signal(S_{mutex}); \quad /* \text{leave critical section} */ \\
&\quad signal(S_{full}); \quad /* \text{increment filled slots} */ \\
&\rbrace
\end{aligned}
$$

**Consumer Code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{while (true)} \lbrace \\
&\quad wait(S_{full}); \quad /* \text{wait for a filled slot} */ \\
&\quad wait(S_{mutex}); \quad /* \text{enter critical section} */ \\
&\quad \text{remove item } y \text{ from buffer}; \\
&\quad signal(S_{mutex}); \quad /* \text{leave critical section} */ \\
&\quad signal(S_{empty}); \quad /* \text{increment empty slots} */ \\
&\quad \text{consume item } y; \\
&\rbrace
\end{aligned}
$$

**Diagram:** See Section 4.3 above for the schematic.

**Key Points for Full Marks (Mention in the Answer):**
1. Three semaphores used — two counting for buffer slot tracking, one binary for mutual exclusion.
2. **Order of `wait` and `signal`** is critical. Producer first waits on `empty` (semantic check) and then on `mutex` (atomicity).
3. **Invariant:** $\vert S_{empty} \vert + \vert S_{full} \vert = N$ at every step.
4. `mutex` ensures only one process manipulates the buffer at a time.

#### Part (b) — Trace Table for $N = 3$, 4 produces and 3 consumes

**Initial values:** $S_{empty} = 3, S_{full} = 0, S_{mutex} = 1$. Buffer = `[ , , ]`.

| Step | Operation | $S_{empty}$ | $S_{full}$ | $S_{mutex}$ | Buffer |
|------|-----------|-------------|------------|-------------|--------|
| 0 | Init | 3 | 0 | 1 | `[_,_,_]` |
| 1 | P puts x₁: wait(emp)→2, wait(mut)→0, put, signal(mut)→1, signal(full)→1 | 2 | 1 | 1 | `[x₁,_,_]` |
| 2 | P puts x₂: wait(emp)→1, put, signal(full)→2 | 1 | 2 | 1 | `[x₁,x₂,_]` |
| 3 | P puts x₃: wait(emp)→0, put, signal(full)→3 | 0 | 3 | 1 | `[x₁,x₂,x₃]` |
| 4 | P puts x₄: wait(emp) blocks (emp = 0) → **P blocks** | 0 | 3 | 1 | `[x₁,x₂,x₃]` |
| 5 | C takes: wait(full)→2, wait(mut)→0, get x₁, signal(mut)→1, signal(emp)→1 | 1 | 2 | 1 | `[_,x₂,x₃]` |
| 6 | C takes: wait(full)→1, get x₂, signal(emp)→2 | 2 | 1 | 1 | `[_,_,x₃]` |
| 7 | C takes: wait(full)→0, get x₃, signal(emp)→3 | 3 | 0 | 1 | `[_,_,_]` |
| 8 | P unblocks (step 4): put x₄, signal(full)→1 | 2 | 1 | 1 | `[x₄,_,_]` |

**Final State:** $S_{empty} = 2, S_{full} = 1, S_{mutex} = 1$. Buffer holds one item `x₄`. Consumer still needs to consume.

**Invariant Check:** $\vert S_{empty} \vert + \vert S_{full} \vert = 2 + 1 = 3 = N$ ✓ (last row).

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** [7 Marks] State and explain the Dining Philosophers Problem. Show how it leads to a deadlock and propose a semaphore-based solution that avoids deadlock.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare semaphores with monitors. Mention at least three advantages of monitors over semaphores.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Dining Philosophers Problem

**Problem Statement:** Five philosophers sit around a circular table. Between each pair of adjacent philosophers lies one chopstick (5 chopsticks total). Each philosopher alternates between *thinking* and *eating*. To eat, a philosopher needs **both** adjacent chopsticks. After eating, the philosopher puts down both chopsticks and thinks again.

**Deadlock Scenario:** If every philosopher simultaneously picks up the chopstick to their *right*, each holds one chopstick and waits forever for the chopstick to the left — a classic **circular wait** deadlock.

**Deadlock Conditions Violated:** None — all four Coffman conditions (mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, circular wait) hold, so deadlock is possible.

**Semaphore-Based Solution (Resource Hierarchy / Asymmetric):**

Assign chopstick labels $0, 1, 2, 3, 4$. Each philosopher $P_i$ picks the *lower-numbered* chopstick first, then the higher-numbered one.

$$
\begin{aligned}
chopstick[i] & = 1, \quad i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 \\
\\
\text{Philosopher } P_i: & \\
\text{while (true)} \lbrace & \\
\quad \text{think}; & \\
\quad \text{if } i \neq 4 \text{ then} \lbrace wait(chopstick[i]); \; wait(chopstick[(i+1) \bmod 5]); \rbrace & \\
\quad \text{else} \lbrace wait(chopstick[0]); \; wait(chopstick[4]); \rbrace & \\
\quad \text{eat}; & \\
\quad \text{signal}(chopstick[i]); \; \text{signal}(chopstick[(i+1) \bmod 5]); & \\
\rbrace &
\end{aligned}
$$

The last philosopher $P_4$ reverses the order, breaking the circular wait and eliminating deadlock.

> [!NOTE]
> **Other Solutions (for bonus marks):** Allow at most 4 philosophers to sit at the table (using a `room` semaphore with initial value 4), or use a state-based solution (THINKING, HUNGRY, EATING) with a `test(i)` function.

#### Part (b) — Semaphores vs. Monitors

| Aspect | Semaphore | Monitor |
|---|---|---|
| Abstraction level | Low-level | High-level language construct |
| Mutual exclusion | Programmer must enforce via `wait`/`signal` | Implicit; only one process inside |
| Condition variables | None | Provided (`wait`, `signal` on conditions) |
| Type-safety | Prone to programming errors | Compiler-enforced |
| Ease of use | Harder, error-prone | Easier, less error-prone |
| Mutual exclusion | Manual | Automatic |

**Three Advantages of Monitors over Semaphores:**
1. **Encapsulation:** All shared data and synchronization are bundled, so the programmer cannot forget to acquire/release a lock.
2. **Compiler-enforced mutual exclusion:** A monitor guarantees only one process is active inside it, eliminating the most common semaphore bugs.
3. **Condition variables** provide a clean signaling mechanism without scattered `wait`/`signal` calls throughout the code, preventing *errant* signals and *lost wakeups*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Order of `wait(empty)` and `wait(mutex)` in Producer–Consumer.** Reversing them causes the producer to hold the mutex while waiting for an empty slot, potentially leading to deadlock. Always wait on the **count semaphore first**.
> 2. **Confusing the internal counter with the logical value.** In the blocking implementation the integer can go negative; the abstract semaphore value is the number of *free permits*, which is always $\geq 0$.
> 3. **Forgetting to call `signal` on all waiting semaphores.** In Readers–Writers, only the *first* reader does `wait(rw_mutex)` and only the *last* reader does `signal(rw_mutex)` — getting this condition wrong is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **In Dining Philosophers, all picking the right chopstick first** is the canonical deadlock; you must describe the resource hierarchy (or another) solution explicitly to get full 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **semaphore** is a non-negative integer with two **atomic** operations: `wait()` (decrement / block) and `signal()` (increment / wakeup).
- ✅ `wait()` is also called **P()** (*Proberen* — to test) and `signal()` is also called **V()** (*Verhogen* — to increment) — **Dijkstra's original notation**.
- ✅ **Binary semaphore** $\Rightarrow$ value in $\lbrace 0, 1 \rbrace$, used for **mutual exclusion**. A mutex is a stricter version with ownership.
- ✅ **Counting semaphore** $\Rightarrow$ value in $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$, used to manage a pool of $N$ identical resources.
- ✅ **Atomicity** is implemented using hardware `test_and_set` or `compare_and_swap` — never bypass this requirement.
- ✅ The **conservation invariant** is $\vert \text{in-use} \vert + \vert \text{blocked} \vert = \vert S_0 \vert$.
- ✅ **Producer–Consumer** uses `empty` and `full` counting semaphores plus a `mutex` binary semaphore.
- ✅ **Readers–Writers** combines semaphores with a shared integer `readcount` guarded by an additional mutex.
- ✅ **Dining Philosophers** deadlock is broken by the **resource hierarchy** solution (last philosopher picks in reverse order) or by limiting philosophers to $N - 1$ via a `room` semaphore.
- ✅ **FIFO queue** in `signal` ensures fairness and prevents starvation; LIFO can cause starvation.
- ✅ **Priority inversion** can occur even with semaphores — fixed by **Priority Inheritance Protocol**.
- ✅ **Monitor vs. Semaphore** — monitors are higher-level, compiler-enforced, with condition variables. Semaphores are lower-level and error-prone.
- ✅ In KTU 14-mark questions, **always** draw a diagram, state initial semaphore values, write both `wait`/`signal` pseudocode blocks in correct order, and verify the **conservation invariant** for full marks.
- ✅ Standard initial values to memorize: Producer–Consumer: $S_{empty} = N, S_{full} = 0, S_{mutex} = 1$. Readers–Writers: $S_{rw\_mutex} = 1, S_{mutex} = 1, readcount = 0$. Dining Philosophers: $S_i = 1 \;\forall i$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mutex (Mutual Exclusion Lock) — Formal Definition

A **Mutex** is a synchronization primitive that provides **mutual exclusion** access to a shared resource. At any given instant, only **one** thread/process is permitted to enter the **critical section** protected by the mutex. The mutex maintains a binary lock state — either **locked (1)** or **unlocked (0)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A mutex is a program object that allows multiple program threads to take turns sharing the same resource, such as access to a file, by enforcing mutual exclusion via two atomic operations: `lock()` (acquire) and `unlock()` (release).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Single-Key Bathroom

Imagine a public washroom with **only one key** hanging on the door:

1. A person arrives → picks the key → enters → **locks** the door from inside.
2. Other people arriving must **wait** outside, regardless of urgency.
3. When finished, the person unlocks the door, **hangs the key back**, and leaves.
4. The next person in line picks up the key and proceeds.

**This is precisely how a Mutex behaves:**
- `lock()` ≡ picking up the key
- `critical section` ≡ the bathroom interior
- `unlock()` ≡ returning the key

> [!NOTE]
> **Atomicity Guarantee:** The `lock()` and `unlock()` operations are guaranteed by the OS/hardware to be **atomic** (indivisible) — no two threads can ever observe an intermediate state. This is typically implemented using hardware instructions like `compare-and-swap` (CAS), `test-and-set`, or OS-supported futexes.

---

## 1.2 Deadlock — Formal Definition

A **deadlock** is a state in which two or more processes are each waiting for the other to release a resource, and as a result, none of them can ever proceed, execute, or complete their execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Canonical Definition (Stallings):** A set of processes is deadlocked if each process in the set is waiting for an event that only another process in the set can cause.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Narrow Tunnel Jam

Picture two cars approaching each other on a **single-lane bridge** from opposite ends:

1. Car A enters from the West.
2. Car B enters from the East.
3. They meet in the middle — neither can move forward; neither can reverse.
4. **No progress is possible without external intervention** (e.g., one car must back out).

**This is a deadlock.** The "resources" are the road segments, and the "events waited for" are the other car vacating the road.

---

## 1.3 Starvation — Formal Definition

**Starvation** (also called **indefinite postponement** or **livelock-related condition**) occurs when a process is perpetually denied access to the resources it needs because other processes of higher or equal priority continually monopolize them.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Unfair Print Queue

At a print server, a low-priority student sends a 10-page document. A high-priority faculty member keeps sending 1-page jobs. The low-priority job **never gets executed** — not because of a circular wait, but because it is **consistently bypassed**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Deadlock vs. Starvation — The Critical Distinction:**
>
> | Aspect | Deadlock | Starvation |
> |---|---|---|
> | **State** | All involved processes are **blocked** | Some processes proceed; others **wait indefinitely** |
> | **Progress** | **No progress at all** by any process in the cycle | **Some progress** is made (by the favored processes) |
> | **Cause** | Circular wait among all participants | Unfair scheduling/resource allocation |
> | **Resolution** | Requires external intervention (abort, preempt) | Requires fair scheduling (e.g., aging) |
> | **System impact** | Halts the entire deadlocked set | Degrades fairness; may persist indefinitely |
> | **System resource utilization** | Dropped to near zero for the cycle | Remains moderate |

---

## 1.4 The Four Necessary Conditions for Deadlock (Coffman Conditions)

For a deadlock to occur, **ALL four** of the following conditions must hold simultaneously. If even one is broken, deadlock is **impossible**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coffman Conditions (1971) — Mandatory Memorization for KTU:**
>
> 1. **Mutual Exclusion** — At least one resource is held in a non-sharable mode.
> 2. **Hold and Wait** — A process holds at least one resource while waiting for another.
> 3. **No Preemption** — Resources cannot be forcibly taken; only the holding process can release them.
> 4. **Circular Wait** — A closed chain of processes exists, each waiting for a resource held by the next.

These will be expanded in SECTION_2 with rigorous analysis.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mutex — Operational Lifecycle

A mutex object typically embeds the following internal state:

1. **State Variable:** `LOCKED` or `UNLOCKED` (boolean flag).
2. **Owner Pointer:** Reference to the thread/process that currently holds the lock.
3. **Wait Queue:** FIFO or priority-based list of threads blocked on `lock()`.

### The Locking Protocol — Step-by-Step

When a thread invokes `mutex_lock(M)`:

1. **Check state atomically:** Is `M` UNLOCKED?
   - **YES** → Set `M` to LOCKED, record this thread as owner → **enter critical section**.
   - **NO** → Add this thread to `M`'s wait queue → **block (suspend)** this thread.
2. The thread becomes **runnable** only when scheduled (after some other thread invokes `mutex_unlock(M)`).
3. On wake-up, thread resumes → re-attempts the atomic check.

### Spinning vs. Blocking Mutexes

| Variant | Behavior | CPU Cost | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spinlock** | Busy-waits (polls) until lock is free | High (wastes CPU cycles) | Short critical sections; multiprocessor; kernel preemption disabled |
| **Blocking Mutex** | Suspends thread, OS schedules another | Low | Long critical sections; general-purpose user code |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A **spinlock** is itself a form of mutex implemented via `test-and-set` atomic instruction. It satisfies mutual exclusion but is vulnerable to **priority inversion** and can lead to deadlock if used naively in single-CPU systems (since the holder can never run to release the lock while the waiter spins).

---

## 2.2 Deadlock Characterization — The Formal Model

Let us define the system formally:

- Let $\mathcal{P} = \{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\}$ be the set of processes.
- Let $\mathcal{R} = \{R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_m\}$ be the set of resource types.
- Each $R_j$ has $W_j$ identical instances (multi-unit resources).
- A process may request, use, and release resources.

### The Four Coffman Conditions — Rigorous Expansion

#### Condition 1: Mutual Exclusion

**Statement:** At least one resource type must be held in a non-sharable mode.

$$ \exists R_j \in \mathcal{R} \mid \text{at least one instance of } R_j \text{ is allocated to exactly one process at a time} $$

- A **non-sharable** resource cannot be simultaneously used by more than one process.
- Examples: Printer, tape drive, mutex-protected memory region.
- Sharable resources (read-only files, reentrant code) **do not contribute** to deadlock.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern systems often break this condition by making resources logically **sharable** (e.g., read-only access, copy-on-write), thereby **preventing** deadlocks at the design level.

#### Condition 2: Hold and Wait

**Statement:** There exists a process $P_i$ that is currently holding at least one resource $R_a$ AND simultaneously waiting to acquire another resource $R_b$ held by some other process $P_k$.

$$ \exists P_i \in \mathcal{P} \mid \text{Allocated}(P_i) \neq \emptyset \;\land\; \text{Requested}(P_i) \neq \emptyset $$

- The process **does not release** its current resources before requesting new ones.
- A protocol that requires processes to **request all resources upfront** breaks this condition (e.g., dining philosophers with simultaneous chopstick pickup restriction).

#### Condition 3: No Preemption

**Statement:** A resource can be released only **voluntarily** by the process holding it, after that process has completed its task.

- The OS **cannot forcibly deallocate** a resource.
- Contrast with CPU preemption (where the scheduler can forcibly take the CPU).
- Breaking this condition: If the OS can preempt resources (e.g., save state of printer job to spool, reclaim), deadlock can be prevented.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** In a database transaction system, the DBMS may **abort** a transaction and roll it back — effectively preempting all its held locks. This is one way to break "no preemption" in software.

#### Condition 4: Circular Wait

**Statement:** A set of processes $\{P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_k\}$ exists such that:

$$ P_0 \text{ waits for } R_{a_0} \text{ held by } P_1, $$
$$ P_1 \text{ waits for } R_{a_1} \text{ held by } P_2, $$
$$ \vdots $$
$$ P_k \text{ waits for } R_{a_k} \text{ held by } P_0 $$

Formally:

$$ \exists \text{ cycle } (P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_k) \mid \forall i, \; P_i \text{ is waiting for a resource held by } P_{(i+1) \mod (k+1)} $$

- This is the **most commonly broken** condition in practice (via **resource ordering** — assign a total order to all resources and require processes to request them in increasing order).

---

## 2.3 Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) — The Diagrammatic Tool

A **Resource Allocation Graph** is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where:

- $V = \mathcal{P} \cup \mathcal{R}$ (process and resource vertices)
- $E = \text{Request edges} \cup \text{Assignment edges}$

**Notation:**

- **Process vertex:** Circle $\bigcirc$
- **Resource vertex:** Rectangle $\square$ (with **dots** inside equal to the number of instances)
- **Request edge** $P_i \rightarrow R_j$: $P_i$ is requesting an instance of $R_j$
- **Assignment edge** $R_j \rightarrow P_i$: An instance of $R_j$ is allocated to $P_i$

### Detection Rules

| Graph Property | Conclusion |
|---|---|
| **No cycles** | **No deadlock** is possible |
| **Cycle present AND all resources are single-instance** | **Deadlock exists** (cycle $\equiv$ deadlock) |
| **Cycle present AND some resource is multi-instance** | **Deadlock MAY exist** — needs further analysis (insufficient condition) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often claim "cycle = deadlock" for *all* cases. This is **only true** when every resource type has exactly one instance. For multi-instance resources, a cycle is **necessary but not sufficient** for deadlock.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Condition** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Mutex atomicity | $M = \{0, 1\}$ | Lock state is binary |
| Hold & Wait | $\text{Allocated}(P_i) \neq \emptyset \wedge \text{Requested}(P_i) \neq \emptyset$ | Process holds some, waits for more |
| Circular Wait | $\exists (P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_k) \mid P_i \to P_{i+1 \mod (k+1)}$ | Closed directed cycle of waiters |
| RAG (no cycle) | $\text{cycle}(G) = \emptyset \Rightarrow \text{NoDeadlock}$ | Necessary absence of cycle |
| RAG (single-instance) | $\text{cycle}(G) \neq \emptyset \Rightarrow \text{Deadlock}$ | Sufficient when $\forall j: W_j = 1$ |
| RAG (multi-instance) | $\text{cycle}(G) \neq \emptyset \Rightarrow \text{Deadlock MAY exist}$ | Insufficient — needs reduction algorithm |
| Deadlock existence | $\bigwedge_{i=1}^{4} C_i = \text{TRUE}$ | All four Coffman conditions must hold |
| Starvation | $\lim_{t \to \infty} \text{wait\_time}(P_i) = \infty$ | Process is denied forever |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Database Management Systems (DBMS):** Lock-based concurrency control (Two-Phase Locking) is the canonical source of deadlocks; DBMSes run deadlock detectors and victim-selection algorithms.
2. **Embedded Systems & RTOS:** Spinlocks are common in ISR-critical sections; careful analysis prevents deadlock in interrupt handlers.
3. **Multithreaded Servers (Java, C++):** `std::mutex`, `ReentrantLock`, `synchronized` blocks — incorrect lock ordering causes production deadlocks.
4. **Distributed Systems:** Resource allocation across nodes leads to **distributed deadlocks** — detected via Chandy-Misra-Haas algorithm.
5. **Operating System Kernels:** Linux kernel uses `mutex_lock()`, `spin_lock()` extensively; the lockdep validator automatically detects potential deadlock paths.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Coffman Conditions to Necessary-and-Sufficient Form

We will formally prove that **all four Coffman conditions together are sufficient** for a deadlock to exist.

### Setup

Let the system state at time $t$ be described by:
- $\text{Alloc}(t)$: a function mapping each $P_i$ to its currently held resources.
- $\text{Req}(t)$: a function mapping each $P_i$ to its currently requested resources.
- A process is **blocked** if $\text{Req}(P_i) \neq \emptyset$ and every resource in $\text{Req}(P_i)$ is currently allocated to some other process.

### Theorem

A deadlock exists at time $t$ if and only if **all four Coffman conditions** ($C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4$) hold simultaneously for some non-empty subset $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{P}$.

### Proof Sketch (Necessity: $C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge C_3 \wedge C_4 \Leftarrow \text{Deadlock}$)

Assume a deadlock exists at time $t$ in a non-empty set $\mathcal{S}$.

**Step 1 — Prove $C_1$ (Mutual Exclusion):**
Since processes in $\mathcal{S}$ are blocked waiting for resources, and resources can only be allocated to one process at a time, there must exist a resource type that is non-sharable. Otherwise, if all resources were sharable, the waiting processes could obtain them and proceed — contradicting the deadlock state.

**Step 2 — Prove $C_2$ (Hold and Wait):**
A process in $\mathcal{S}$ is blocked because its requested resource is held by another. If it held no resources, it would be a fresh process that has not yet allocated anything; in that case the OS could grant its first request. Since the process is **already** blocked, it must have at least one allocated resource. Therefore $\text{Alloc}(P_i) \neq \emptyset$ and $\text{Req}(P_i) \neq \emptyset$.

**Step 3 — Prove $C_3$ (No Preemption):**
If preemption were allowed, the OS could take a resource from a process in $\mathcal{S}$ and grant it to the blocked waiter. This would unblock at least one process, contradicting the deadlock. Therefore preemption must not be possible.

**Step 4 — Prove $C_4$ (Circular Wait):**
Construct a wait-for graph $W$ where an edge $P_i \to P_j$ exists if $P_i$ is waiting for a resource held by $P_j$. Since $\mathcal{S}$ is finite and no process can be removed (by $C_3$), the only way to sustain an infinite wait is for the wait-for relation to contain a cycle. By König's lemma on finite graphs, a set of vertices with infinite mutual waiting must contain a cycle.

Therefore all four conditions are **necessary** for a deadlock.

### Proof Sketch (Sufficiency: $C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge C_3 \wedge C_4 \Rightarrow \text{Deadlock}$)

Assume all four conditions hold. We will show no process in $\mathcal{S}$ can ever make progress.

**Step 1 — Take any process $P_0 \in \mathcal{S}$.** By $C_2$, $P_0$ holds some resource $R_{a_0}$ and waits for $R_{b_0}$. By $C_1$, $R_{b_0}$ is held by exactly one process; call it $P_1$.

**Step 2 — $P_1 \in \mathcal{S}$ must also be in a deadlock state** (we are examining the entire set). Apply the same reasoning: $P_1$ waits for $R_{b_1}$ held by $P_2$, $P_2$ waits for $R_{b_2}$ held by $P_3$, and so on.

**Step 3 — Since $\mathcal{S}$ is finite**, this chain of "waiting for the next holder" must eventually revisit a process. We obtain a closed cycle:

$$ P_0 \to P_1 \to P_2 \to \cdots \to P_k \to P_0 $$

**Step 4 — In this cycle**, every process is waiting for the next one. By $C_3$, no preemption can break the cycle. By $C_2$, no process will voluntarily release (each holds another resource). Therefore the cycle is **permanent** and the system is **deadlocked**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: RAG Cycle Analysis (Multi-Instance Resources)

Consider the following system state:

- **Resources:** $R_1$ (2 instances), $R_2$ (2 instances), $R_3$ (1 instance)
- **Allocations:**
  - $P_1$ holds 1 instance of $R_1$, requests 1 instance of $R_2$
  - $P_2$ holds 1 instance of $R_2$, requests 1 instance of $R_3$
  - $P_3$ holds 1 instance of $R_2$ and 1 instance of $R_3$, requests 1 instance of $R_1$

### Step-by-Step Resolution

**Step 1:** Draw the RAG.

- $P_1 \to R_2$ (request)
- $R_1 \to P_1$ (allocation)
- $P_2 \to R_3$ (request)
- $R_2 \to P_2$ (allocation)
- $P_3 \to R_1$ (request)
- $R_2 \to P_3$ (allocation)
- $R_3 \to P_3$ (allocation)

**Step 2:** Identify cycles. The wait-for chain is:

$$ P_1 \to P_2 \to P_3 \to P_1 \quad (\text{via } R_2 \to R_3 \to R_1) $$

A cycle exists.

**Step 3:** Apply the reduction algorithm (Silberschatz).

- **Can $P_1$'s request be granted?** $P_1$ requests $R_2$. Available instances of $R_2$: total 2, allocated to $P_2$ and $P_3$ → 0 available. **Cannot grant** unless $P_2$ or $P_3$ finishes.
- **Can $P_2$'s request be granted?** $P_2$ requests $R_3$. Allocated: $P_3$ holds 1, total 1 → 0 available. **Cannot grant.**
- **Can $P_3$'s request be granted?** $P_3$ requests $R_1$. Allocated: $P_1$ holds 1, total 2 → 1 available. **Can grant!**

**Step 4:** Grant $P_3$'s request → $P_3$ finishes → releases $R_1$ (extra instance), $R_2$ (1 instance), $R_3$ (1 instance).

**Step 5:** Now $P_2$'s request for $R_3$ can be granted → $P_2$ finishes → releases $R_2$.

**Step 6:** Now $P_1$'s request for $R_2$ can be granted → $P_1$ finishes.

**Conclusion:** Despite a cycle, the system is **NOT deadlocked** because the cycle is broken by the **available instance of $R_1$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **This worked example demonstrates the multi-instance nuance:** A cycle in the RAG is **not sufficient** to conclude deadlock when resources have multiple instances.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Methods to Handle Deadlocks

| **Method** | **Condition Broken** | **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mutual Exclusion Denial** | $C_1$ | Easy if resources are shareable | Not always feasible (e.g., printer) |
| **Hold and Wait Denial** | $C_2$ | Simple request-all-at-once protocol | Low resource utilization; starvation possible |
| **Preemption Allow** | $C_3$ | Frees resources quickly | Costly state save/restore; not always possible |
| **Circular Wait Prevention (Ordering)** | $C_4$ | Practical; widely used | Requires global resource ordering discipline |
| **Deadlock Detection + Recovery** | None — detects & recovers | Allows maximum concurrency | Detection overhead; recovery may lose work |
| **Ostrich Algorithm (Ignore)** | None | Trivial to implement | Catastrophic if deadlock occurs |

---

## 3.4 Comparison: Mutex vs. Semaphore

| **Aspect** | **Mutex** | **Counting Semaphore** |
|---|---|---|
| **State** | Binary (locked/unlocked) | Integer counter $S \geq 0$ |
| **Ownership** | Strictly enforced (only owner can unlock) | No ownership — any process can `V()` |
| **Use case** | Mutual exclusion of a single resource | Resource pool with $N$ identical units |
| **Priority inversion handling** | Supports priority inheritance | Generally does not |
| **Reentrancy** | Non-reentrant by default; reentrant variants exist | Not applicable |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mutex State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unlocked
    Unlocked --> Locked : lock() called by Thread A
    Locked --> Locked : lock() by Thread B (B blocks, joins wait queue)
    Locked --> Unlocked : unlock() by Thread A
    Unlocked --> Unlocked : unlock() (error / no-op)
    note right of Locked : Owner: Thread A\nWait queue: [Thread B, Thread C, ...]
    Unlocked --> Locked : lock() by Thread B (dequeued)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** When `lock()` is invoked on an already-LOCKED mutex, the calling thread is **suspended** and added to the wait queue. When `unlock()` is called, the OS wakes **one** waiter (typically FIFO), which then atomically transitions the mutex back to LOCKED under its ownership.

---

## 4.2 Resource Allocation Graph — Deadlock Case (Single-Instance Resources)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1((P1)) -- Request R1 --> R1A[R1]
    R1A -- Allocated to P2 --> P2((P2))
    P2((P2)) -- Request R2 --> R2A[R2]
    R2A -- Allocated to P3 --> P3((P3))
    P3((P3)) -- Request R3 --> R3A[R3]
    R3A -- Allocated to P1 --> P1
    classDef proc fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef res fill:#add8e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class P1,P2,P3 proc
    class R1A,R2A,R3A res
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A clear cycle $P_1 \to R_1 \to P_2 \to R_2 \to P_3 \to R_3 \to P_1$ exists. Since all three resources are **single-instance**, the cycle is **sufficient** to declare a deadlock. No process can proceed.

---

## 4.3 Resource Allocation Graph — Cycle WITHOUT Deadlock (Multi-Instance)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1((P1)) -- Request R2 --> R2M[R2]
    R1M[R1] -- 1 instance allocated --> P1
    P2((P2)) -- Request R3 --> R3S[R3]
    R2M -- 1 instance allocated --> P2
    R2M -- 1 instance allocated --> P3
    P3((P3)) -- Request R1 --> R1M
    R3S -- 1 instance allocated --> P3
    R1M -. 1 free instance .-> P3
    classDef proc fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef res fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class P1,P2,P3 proc
    class R1M,R2M,R3S res
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A cycle $P_1 \to R_2 \to P_3 \to R_1 \to P_1$ exists **in the graph structure**, but $R_1$ has a free instance that can satisfy $P_3$'s request. Therefore the system is **not deadlocked**. This is the classic KTU counterexample.

---

## 4.4 Deadlock vs. Starvation — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Pi requests resource Rj] --> B{Is Rj available?}
    B -- YES --> C[Pi acquires Rj, proceeds]
    B -- NO --> D{Is Pi part of a circular wait?}
    D -- YES --> E[DEADLOCK\nAll processes in cycle blocked forever\nRequires external intervention]
    D -- NO --> F{Are higher-priority processes\ncontinually favored?}
    F -- YES --> G[STARVATION\nPi waits indefinitely\nResolve via fair scheduling / aging]
    F -- NO --> H[Pi waits in queue, will eventually get Rj]
    H --> C
    classDef deadlock fill:#ff6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef starvation fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef safe fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class E deadlock
    class G starvation
    class C,H safe
```

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Architecture: How Mutex Protects a Shared Resource

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Threads
        T1[Thread 1]
        T2[Thread 2]
        T3[Thread 3]
    end
    T1 -- lock(M) --> MX{Mutex M}
    T2 -- lock(M) --> MX
    T3 -- lock(M) --> MX
    MX -- Acquired by one thread --> CS[Critical Section\nShared Resource Access]
    CS -- unlock(M) --> MX
    MX -. Blocked: Wait Queue .-> T2
    MX -. Blocked: Wait Queue .-> T3
    classDef mutex fill:#ff69b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef cs fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class MX mutex
    class CS cs
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Mapping:** This is the architecture used in `pthread_mutex_t` (POSIX), `std::mutex` (C++), `java.util.concurrent.locks.ReentrantLock` (Java), and OS-level kernel mutexes. The wait queue is managed by the OS scheduler.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]

**Define deadlock. List the four necessary conditions for its occurrence.**

#### Model Answer:

**Definition:** A set of processes is in a deadlock state if every process in the set is waiting for an event that can only be caused by another process in the set.

**The Four Necessary Conditions (Coffman Conditions):**

1. **Mutual Exclusion:** At least one resource is held in a non-sharable mode; only one process can use it at a time.
2. **Hold and Wait:** A process holding at least one resource is waiting to acquire additional resources held by other processes.
3. **No Preemption:** Resources cannot be preempted; they can only be released voluntarily by the holding process.
4. **Circular Wait:** There exists a circular chain of processes $\{P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_n\}$ such that $P_0$ waits for a resource held by $P_1$, $P_1$ waits for one held by $P_2$, ..., $P_n$ waits for one held by $P_0$.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for listing and brief explanation of conditions)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]

**Distinguish between deadlock and starvation with suitable examples.**

#### Model Answer:

| **Parameter** | **Deadlock** | **Starvation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | All processes in a set are blocked forever, waiting on each other | A process waits indefinitely because it is repeatedly bypassed |
| **Progress** | No process in the cycle makes progress | Other processes do make progress |
| **Cause** | Circular wait among a set of processes | Unfair resource allocation or scheduling policy |
| **Example** | Two cars in a narrow single-lane bridge blocking each other | A low-priority process in a priority scheduler that never gets CPU |
| **Resolution** | Abort a process or preempt a resource | Use fair scheduling algorithms, e.g., aging |
| **System state** | Permanently stuck | Continues, but one process suffers |

*(3 marks — emphasis on distinction and at least one example each)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]

**(a)** Explain the concept of a mutex. How does it differ from a semaphore? Describe the lock() and unlock() operations with their atomicity guarantees. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable Resource Allocation Graph (RAG), explain how a deadlock is detected for **single-instance resource systems**. Justify why a cycle is sufficient for deadlock detection in this case. Provide a counterexample showing that the same is not true for multi-instance resources. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Mutex Definition:**
A mutex (mutual exclusion lock) is a synchronization object that ensures only one thread can execute a critical section at any given time. It maintains a binary state — LOCKED (1) or UNLOCKED (0) — and enforces ownership semantics.

**Operations:**

1. **`lock()`** — Atomically checks and sets the lock.
   - If the mutex is UNLOCKED, it becomes LOCKED, and the calling thread is set as the owner.
   - If LOCKED, the calling thread is suspended and added to the mutex's wait queue.
2. **`unlock()`** — Atomically releases the lock.
   - Only the **owner** thread may invoke this.
   - The mutex becomes UNLOCKED, and one waiter (typically FIFO) is woken up.

**Atomicity Guarantee:**
The operations are implemented using **hardware-supported atomic instructions** such as:
- `test-and-set` — reads and writes a memory location in one indivisible step.
- `compare-and-swap (CAS)` — atomically compares a value and swaps it if it matches an expected value.

These are guaranteed by the CPU's bus-locking or cache-coherence protocols, ensuring no two threads can observe an intermediate state.

**Mutex vs. Semaphore:**

| Feature | Mutex | Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| Value | Binary (0 or 1) | Integer counter $S \geq 0$ |
| Ownership | Yes — only owner unlocks | No — any thread can signal |
| Use | Mutual exclusion | Resource counting |
| Priority inheritance | Often supported | Not generally |

*(7 marks — 2 for definition, 2 for operations and atomicity, 3 for comparison with semaphore)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**RAG for Single-Instance Resources:**

A Resource Allocation Graph is a directed bipartite graph with two types of vertices — processes (circles) and resources (rectangles, with dots = instance count). Edges are either **request edges** ($P_i \to R_j$) or **assignment edges** ($R_j \to P_i$).

**Example of Single-Instance Deadlock:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1((P1)) -- Request R1 --> R1B[R1]
    R1B -- Held by P2 --> P2((P2))
    P2 -- Request R2 --> R2B[R2]
    R2B -- Held by P1 --> P1
    classDef proc fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef res fill:#ff6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class P1,P2 proc
    class R1B,R2B res
```

A cycle $P_1 \to R_1 \to P_2 \to R_2 \to P_1$ exists. Since every resource is **single-instance**, the cycle is **sufficient evidence of deadlock**.

**Justification:**
With single-instance resources, "all of $R_j$ is allocated to at most one process" implies that following the request edge from $P_i$ leads to a unique holder. Thus the wait-for graph is isomorphic to the RAG cycle structure, and a cycle directly indicates that every process in the cycle is waiting on the next, with no escape possible.

**Counterexample for Multi-Instance:**

Consider: $R_1$ has 2 instances; $R_2$ has 2 instances.

- $P_1$ holds 1 instance of $R_1$, requests $R_2$.
- $P_2$ holds 1 instance of $R_2$, requests $R_1$.
- $P_3$ holds 1 instance of $R_2$, requests $R_1$.

A cycle $P_1 \to R_2 \to P_3 \to R_1 \to P_1$ exists **in the graph**, but $R_1$ has a free instance that can be given to $P_3$. After $P_3$ finishes, it releases $R_2$, which unblocks $P_1$, which releases $R_1$, unblocking $P_2$. **No deadlock.**

**Conclusion:** For multi-instance resources, a cycle is **necessary but not sufficient** for deadlock detection; the **graph reduction algorithm** (Silberschatz) must be applied.

*(7 marks — 2 for RAG definition and example, 2 for justification, 3 for counterexample and reduction algorithm mention)*

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]

**(a)** Explain the Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) in detail. Construct a sample RAG involving three processes and three resources, and demonstrate how you would identify whether the system is deadlocked. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the four Coffman conditions for deadlock. For each condition, suggest one practical method to break it, indicating which deadlock-handling strategy it falls under (prevention, avoidance, or detection-recovery). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) — Detailed Explanation:**

The RAG is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ that captures the state of resource allocation in a system.

- **Vertices ($V$):** $V = \mathcal{P} \cup \mathcal{R}$
  - Process vertices $\mathcal{P}$: drawn as circles.
  - Resource vertices $\mathcal{R}$: drawn as rectangles. Each rectangle contains **dots**, where the number of dots equals the number of identical instances of that resource type.
- **Edges ($E$):**
  - **Request edge:** $P_i \to R_j$ — Process $P_i$ has requested an instance of resource $R_j$ and is currently waiting.
  - **Assignment edge:** $R_j \to P_i$ — An instance of $R_j$ has been allocated to process $P_i$.

**Construction — Sample RAG:**

Let us consider:
- **Resources:** $R_1$ (2 instances), $R_2$ (1 instance), $R_3$ (1 instance)
- **State:**
  - $P_1$ holds 1 instance of $R_1$; requests $R_2$
  - $P_2$ holds 1 instance of $R_2$; requests $R_3$
  - $P_3$ holds 1 instance of $R_3$ and 1 instance of $R_1$; requests $R_2$

**RAG Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1((P1)) -- Request R2 --> R2N[R2]
    R1N[R1] -- 1 instance held --> P1
    P2((P2)) -- Request R3 --> R3N[R3]
    R2N -- 1 instance held --> P2
    R3N -- 1 instance held --> P3
    R1N -- 1 instance held --> P3
    P3((P3)) -- Request R2 --> R2N
    classDef proc fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef res fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class P1,P2,P3 proc
    class R1N,R2N,R3N res
```

**Identification of Deadlock:**

**Step 1 — Check for cycles:**
A cycle exists: $P_1 \to R_2 \to P_2 \to R_3 \to P_3 \to R_1 \to P_1$.

**Step 2 — Check resource instance counts:**
- $R_1$: total 2, allocated 2 → 0 free
- $R_2$: total 1, allocated 1 → 0 free
- $R_3$: total 1, allocated 1 → 0 free

**Step 3 — Apply reduction:**
- $P_1$ requests $R_2$ (0 free) → cannot proceed.
- $P_2$ requests $R_3$ (0 free) → cannot proceed.
- $P_3$ requests $R_2$ (0 free) → cannot proceed.

**No process can be reduced. Deadlock confirmed.**

**Step 4 — Identify victims:**
$P_1$, $P_2$, $P_3$ are all deadlocked. To recover, abort one (e.g., $P_3$), reclaim its resources, and break the cycle.

*(7 marks — 2 for RAG explanation, 2 for construction, 3 for deadlock identification via cycle + reduction)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**The Four Coffman Conditions — Methods to Break Each:**

| **Condition** | **Statement** | **Method to Break** | **Strategy** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$C_1$: Mutual Exclusion** | A resource is non-sharable | Make resources logically shareable (e.g., read-only access, spooling) | Prevention |
| **$C_2$: Hold and Wait** | A process holds resources while requesting new ones | Require processes to request **all** resources upfront (or release all before requesting new) | Prevention |
| **$C_3$: No Preemption** | Resources cannot be forcibly taken | Allow the OS to **preempt** a resource by saving its state and rolling back the holding process (used in DBMS transactions) | Prevention |
| **$C_4$: Circular Wait** | A closed chain of waiting processes exists | Impose a **total ordering** on all resources; require processes to request resources in increasing order only | Prevention |

**Justification of Strategy Classification:**

- **Prevention** ensures that at least one of the four conditions is **structurally impossible** by design. The methods listed above are all prevention techniques because they break a condition at the **design/protocol level** rather than dynamically checking state.
- **Avoidance** (e.g., Banker's Algorithm) is **not** listed above because it does not break a Coffman condition; instead, it carefully avoids unsafe states that could lead to deadlock.
- **Detection-Recovery** does not break any condition; it allows all four to hold but periodically runs a detection algorithm and aborts victims upon detection.

**Example of Resource Ordering (Breaking $C_4$):**

If resources are ordered $R_1 < R_2 < R_3 < \ldots$, then a process may only request $R_j$ after acquiring all $R_i$ with $i < j$. This eliminates the possibility of a cycle.

**Example of Preemption (Breaking $C_3$):**

In a database transaction:
1. Transaction $T_1$ holds lock on row $A$, requests lock on row $B$.
2. Transaction $T_2$ holds lock on row $B$, requests lock on row $A$.
3. The DBMS aborts $T_1$, rolls back its changes, and releases lock on $A$.
4. $T_2$ acquires lock on $A$, completes, commits.
5. $T_1$ is restarted.

This forcibly preempts $T_1$'s resource, breaking $C_3$.

*(7 marks — 4 marks for stating conditions with breaking methods, 3 marks for classification into prevention/avoidance/detection and providing examples)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Writing only "cycle = deadlock" without distinguishing single-instance vs multi-instance.** Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for this oversimplification.
> 2. **Confusing Starvation with Deadlock.** They are distinct concepts. Starvation ≠ Deadlock; starvation may occur without a cycle.
> 3. **Listing the four Coffman conditions but forgetting the word "necessary"** (i.e., saying "these conditions cause deadlock" instead of "these conditions are necessary for deadlock"). The word "necessary" is the precise KTU-expected phrasing.
> 4. **In RAG questions, failing to draw dots inside resource rectangles** to indicate instance count. KTU examiners explicitly check this.
> 5. **For Part-B questions, neglecting to provide a counterexample** for the multi-instance RAG case. The counterexample is worth 2–3 marks on its own.
> 6. **Not using a FIFO wait queue explanation** for mutex — examiners often look for "wait queue" terminology.
> 7. **Mixing up Banker's Algorithm (avoidance) with prevention techniques.** Banker's is **avoidance**, not prevention.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
>
> - ✅ **Mutex:** Synchronization primitive enforcing mutual exclusion via atomic `lock()` / `unlock()`. Binary state, owner-enforced.
> - ✅ **Atomic operations:** `test-and-set`, `compare-and-swap (CAS)`, `fetch-and-add` — implemented in hardware.
> - ✅ **Spinlock:** A mutex that busy-waits; suitable for short critical sections on multiprocessors.
> - ✅ **Deadlock definition:** Set of processes where each is waiting for an event only another can cause.
> - ✅ **Starvation definition:** Process denied resources indefinitely due to unfair scheduling.
> - ✅ **Coffman Conditions (all 4 needed):** Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait.
> - ✅ **RAG (Resource Allocation Graph):** Two vertex types (processes, resources); two edge types (request, assignment).
> - ✅ **Single-instance RAG:** Cycle ⇒ Deadlock (necessary AND sufficient).
> - ✅ **Multi-instance RAG:** Cycle ⇒ Deadlock MAY exist (necessary but NOT sufficient). Apply reduction algorithm.
> - ✅ **Breaking conditions = Prevention.** Avoiding unsafe states = Avoidance. Detecting cycles + abort = Detection-Recovery.
> - ✅ **Circular wait is most commonly broken** in practice via total resource ordering.
> - ✅ **Deadlock involves no progress; starvation involves unfair progress** for some processes.
> - ✅ **Examples to memorize:** Narrow bridge (deadlock), print queue with priorities (starvation), dining philosophers (classic deadlock illustration).
> - ✅ **Formula:** Deadlock $\iff \bigwedge_{i=1}^{4} C_i$ where $C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4$ are the four Coffman conditions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deadlock Prevention and Avoidance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Deadlock Prevention and Avoidance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Deadlock Prevention** is a set of static, design-time techniques that guarantee that **at least one of the four necessary Coffman conditions** (mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no-preemption, circular-wait) can *never* hold, thereby structurally ruling out deadlocks before the system even begins execution.
