**Model Answer:**

**Reentrant code** (also called **pure code**) is code that **does not modify itself** during execution and does not contain any per-process data. It uses only the arguments passed to it and operates on the caller's stack, so multiple processes can invoke the same code simultaneously without interfering with each other.

**Importance for sharing:**
- The OS can map a single physical copy of reentrant code (e.g., the `printf` routine in `libc.so`) into the virtual address spaces of **all processes** that need it.
- The shared pages are marked **read-only** in the page table to enforce that the code is not modified.
- This drastically **reduces physical memory usage** — for $N$ processes, the saving is $(N-1) \times S_{\text{code}}$ bytes.
- It also **speeds up process creation** because the loader simply maps the already-resident code pages.

**Example:** The text segment of compilers, editors, browsers, and standard C library functions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse reentrant code with *recursion*. Recursion is a programming technique; reentrancy is a property of the code (no self-modification, no static writable state). Also, do not say "thread-safe code" alone — that is a related but broader concept.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question typically has two sub-parts: (a) worth 7 marks and (b) worth 7 marks. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full 14 marks).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the basic concept of paging with a neat diagram. Show how a logical address is translated into a physical address using a page table.

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** Consider a system with a page size of **4 KB**. A process generates the logical address **0x3A4F**. Compute the page number and offset. If the corresponding frame number in the page table is **5**, what is the physical address?

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Paging Concept:**

Paging is a non-contiguous memory allocation technique. The OS divides:
- The **logical (virtual) address space** of each process into equal-sized **pages**.
- The **physical memory (RAM)** into equal-sized **frames** (same size as a page).

The address translation hardware (MMU) consults a **Page Table** (stored in main memory) that contains the mapping from virtual page number to physical frame number.

**Address Translation Steps:**
1. CPU generates a logical address `LA`.
2. The upper bits of `LA` form the **Page Number (p)**.
3. The lower bits of `LA` form the **Page Offset (d)**.
4. The MMU indexes the page table with `p` to get the **Frame Number (f)**.
5. The MMU concatenates `f` (upper) with `d` (lower) to form the **Physical Address (PA)**.

**Diagram:**

```
Logical Address:  |--- Page Number (p) ---|--- Offset (d) ---|
                                     |
                                     v
                              +-------------+
                              |  Page Table |
                              +-------------+
                                     |
                                     v  Frame Number (f)
Physical Address: |--- Frame Number (f) ---|--- Offset (d) ---|
```

**Advantages (brief mention):** No external fragmentation; simple memory allocation and deallocation; easy to swap; supports protection and sharing.

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Given:**
- Page Size = $4 \text{ KB} = 2^{12}$ bytes.
- Logical Address `LA` = `0x3A4F` (hexadecimal).
- Frame Number `f` = 5.

**Step 1 — Convert LA to Binary:**

`0x3A4F` in hex → binary:

$$
0x3A4F = 0011\ 1010\ 0100\ 1111
$$

This is a **16-bit** address (4 hex digits × 4 bits each = 16 bits).

**Step 2 — Identify Offset and Page Number:**

Since page size is $2^{12}$ bytes, the lower **12 bits** are the offset.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset } d &= \text{lower 12 bits of LA} \\
&= 1010\ 0100\ 1111_2 \\
&= 0xA4F \\
&= 2639_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Page Number } p &= \text{upper bits of LA} \\
&= 0011_2 \\
&= 0x3 \\
&= 3_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify Decomposition:**

$$
\text{Logical Address} = p \times \text{Page Size} + d = 3 \times 4096 + 2639 = 12288 + 2639 = 14927
$$

Cross-check: $0x3A4F = 3 \times 4096 + 0xA4F = 12288 + 2639 = 14927$. ✓

**Step 4 — Form Physical Address:**

Frame Number $f = 5$, Offset $d = 0xA4F$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Address} &= f \times \text{Page Size} + d \\
&= 5 \times 4096 + 2639 \\
&= 20480 + 2639 \\
&= 23119_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**In hexadecimal:**

$$
5 \times 0x1000 + 0xA4F = 0x5000 + 0xA4F = 0x5A4F
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Page Number} = 3,\ \text{Offset} = 0xA4F,\ \text{Physical Address} = 0x5A4F}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Address Calculation):**
> - Do not forget to **convert hex to binary** before extracting offset bits. Using decimal will lead to wrong bit boundaries.
> - A common mistake is to take the offset as $0x4F$ (8 bits) instead of $0xA4F$ (12 bits). Always cross-check: offset must be in range $[0, \text{Page Size} - 1] = [0, 4095]$. Here $0xA4F = 2639 \in [0, 4095]$. ✓
> - State the final answer in **both hex and decimal** for clarity; you will get 1 extra mark for unit consistency.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **support for sharing** in virtual memory systems. Discuss how reentrant code and shared memory are implemented with the help of page tables.

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** In a paging system, the TLB has an access time of **20 ns**, the main memory access time is **100 ns**, and the TLB hit ratio is **80%**. Calculate the **Effective Access Time (EAT)**. What happens to EAT if the hit ratio drops to 60%?

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**Concept of Support for Sharing:**

In a virtual memory system, several processes may need access to the **same code or data**. Rather than loading multiple physical copies, the OS provides mechanisms to map a single physical region into the virtual address spaces of multiple processes. This is called **support for sharing**.

**Benefits of sharing:**
1. Reduces physical memory consumption.
2. Speeds up process creation (shared libraries/pages already in memory).
3. Provides a fast IPC mechanism (shared memory).

**Implementation with Page Tables:**

Consider three processes $P_1, P_2, P_3$ all using the same C library `libc.so`.

- The OS loads the library's text pages into physical frames **F10 and F11** in RAM.
- Each process's page table contains entries mapping the library's virtual page range to the **same** frame numbers F10 and F11.
- These PTEs are marked **Read-Only (R-X)**, so no process can modify the shared code.
- Each process gets **its own private frames** (e.g., F20, F21 for $P_1$'s heap) for its unique data.

**Page Table Entries (illustrative):**

| Process | Virtual Page | Frame Number | Protection |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 0 (libc start) | 10 | R-X |
| $P_1$ | 1 (libc cont.) | 11 | R-X |
| $P_2$ | 0 (libc start) | 10 | R-X |
| $P_2$ | 1 (libc cont.) | 11 | R-X |
| $P_1$ | 50 (heap) | 20 | R/W |
| $P_2$ | 50 (heap) | 30 | R/W |

**Reentrant Code:**

Reentrant code is pure, read-only code that can be safely executed by multiple processes simultaneously. Examples include:
- Standard library functions (`strlen`, `memcpy`).
- Editor / compiler text segments.
- OS kernel routines (in some architectures).

**Shared Memory IPC:**

Processes can request the OS to map a specific region of physical memory into their address spaces (e.g., `shmget` / `mmap` in UNIX). Both processes can read/write the same frames, enabling high-speed IPC. Synchronization (semaphores) is required to avoid race conditions.

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Given:**
- TLB access time $t = 20$ ns
- Memory access time $m = 100$ ns
- TLB hit ratio $p$ (initially 80%, then 60%)

**Formula (derived in Section 3):**

$$
\text{EAT} = t + (2 - p) \cdot m
$$

**Case 1: $p = 0.80$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT}_{80} &= 20 + (2 - 0.80) \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 1.20 \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 120 \\
&= 140 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 2: $p = 0.60$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT}_{60} &= 20 + (2 - 0.60) \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 1.40 \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 140 \\
&= 160 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**

When the TLB hit ratio drops from 80% to 60%, the **EAT increases from 140 ns to 160 ns**, an increase of **20 ns (~14.3%)** per memory access. The slowdown is significant for memory-intensive programs.

**Comparison Table:**

| Hit Ratio $p$ | EAT (ns) | Slowdown vs. $m=100$ ns |
|---|---|---|
| 1.00 (perfect) | 120 | 20% |
| 0.90 | 130 | 30% |
| 0.80 | **140** | 40% |
| 0.70 | 150 | 50% |
| 0.60 | **160** | 60% |
| 0.00 (no TLB) | 220 | 120% |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (EAT Problems):**
> - A common error is to compute EAT as $p \cdot m + (1-p) \cdot 2m$ — this **forgets the TLB lookup time $t$**. Always add $t$ at the start: EAT $= t + p \cdot m + (1-p) \cdot 2m$.
> - Another mistake: using $p = 80$ instead of $p = 0.80$. Hit ratio is a **probability**, so it must be in $[0, 1]$.
> - **Sanity check:** The EAT must always be **between** the best case ($t + m$ when $p = 1$) and the worst case ($t + 2m$ when $p = 0$). For our problem: best = 120 ns, worst = 220 ns. Both 140 ns and 160 ns lie in this range. ✓

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Paging divides** virtual memory into equal **pages** and physical memory into equal **frames**. Page size is typically **4 KB** ($2^{12}$ bytes).
- [x] A **logical address** is split into **Page Number (p)** (upper bits) and **Offset (d)** (lower bits). Number of offset bits = $\log_2(\text{Page Size})$.
- [x] The **Page Table** maps page numbers to frame numbers. It is stored in main memory and indexed by the MMU on every memory access.
- [x] **Paging eliminates external fragmentation** but introduces a small **internal fragmentation** (at most one partial page per process).
- [x] **Support for Sharing** allows multiple processes to map the **same physical frame** into their own virtual address spaces.
- [x] **Reentrant (pure) code** is read-only, self-contained code that is safe to share. The OS marks shared pages as **R-X (read-execute)** to prevent corruption.
- [x] **Copy-on-Write (COW)** is an optimization: shared pages remain shared until a process writes, triggering a private copy.
- [x] **Shared Memory IPC** is the fastest form of inter-process communication; it requires **synchronization** (semaphores) to avoid race conditions.
- [x] **EAT without TLB** = $2m$ (one access to PT, one to data). **EAT with TLB** = $t + (2 - p) \cdot m$.
- [x] A **page fault** occurs when the valid bit in a PTE is 0. The OS services the fault by loading the page from disk, updating the PTE, and restarting the instruction.
- [x] For shared library code of size $S$ and $N$ processes, the **memory saving** is $(N-1) \cdot S$ bytes.
- [x] TLB hit ratio $p$ must be a **fraction** (between 0 and 1), not a percentage. Always verify EAT lies in $[t+m,\ t+2m]$.
- [x] **Protection bits** (R/W/X) in each PTE enforce that shared code is read-only and per-process data is read/write.
- [x] KTU exam questions often combine paging basics with EAT calculations; practice deriving EAT for **single-level, two-level, and TLB-based** paging systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Page Tables and Hardware Support
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Page Tables and Hardware Support

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Page Table?

A **Page Table** is the fundamental kernel data structure used by the Memory Management Unit (MMU) of a computer system to map a process's **virtual (logical) page numbers** to their corresponding **physical frame numbers** in main memory (RAM). It is the cornerstone of the **Paging** memory-management scheme and is maintained by the operating system on a per-process basis.

Every process has its own page table, because every process has its own independent virtual address space. The page table itself is stored in main memory, and a special register — the **Page Table Base Register (PTBR)** — holds the starting physical address of the currently executing process's page table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A page table is *not* a hardware component. It is a *software/OS-managed* data structure residing in main memory. The *hardware* that helps speed up lookups is the **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** — covered in Section 1.3.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hotel Key-Card System

Imagine a large **1000-room hotel** (this is your *physical memory*). Each guest is given a key card that has a simple number printed on it: room **0, 1, 2, ... 999**. From the guest's perspective, however, the hotel only has **4 suites** — they only ever use a small handful of rooms during their stay, and the key card acts as a small *map* that says:

> "When I dial 1, give me the actual room 47. When I dial 2, give me actual room 102."

In this analogy:
- **The guest's view (4 suites)** = the process's **virtual address space** (logical pages).
- **The hotel's reality (1000 rooms)** = **physical memory** (frames).
- **The key card's tiny printed map** = the **page table**.
- **The front-desk computer that instantly translates "Suite 2" into "Room 102"** = the **MMU + TLB**.

The page table does *exactly* this translation — it converts a virtual page number into a physical frame number.

---

### 1.3 Hardware Support — The Role of the MMU and TLB

Pure page table lookups in main memory are **slow** because every memory access by the CPU requires *two* physical memory fetches (one for the page table entry, one for the actual data). To fix this, modern CPUs include a special, very fast, fully-associative hardware cache called the **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (TLB):** A *Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)* is a small, high-speed **associative memory** (hardware cache) built into the MMU that stores recently used virtual-to-physical page-translations. A TLB hit allows address translation in a *single* CPU cycle, avoiding the second memory access.

Other forms of hardware support that KTU 2024 expects you to know:

- **Single TLB entry (associative lookup):** All entries are searched in parallel.
- **Caches (L1/L2):** Standard CPU caches help amortize the cost of repeated data fetches.
- **Hardware page-table walkers:** Some modern CPUs can walk multi-level page tables on a TLB miss without OS intervention (e.g., x86-64 CR3 walks).
- **Page Table Base Register (PTBR)** and **Page Table Length Register (PTLR):** Hold the base and size of the page table; on context switch, only the PTBR needs to be reloaded.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Tag:** This combined understanding of *page tables (software)* + *TLB (hardware)* is exactly what KTU Module-3 calls **"Page Tables and Hardware Support"** — it is one of the most frequently asked 14-mark topics.

---

### 1.4 Key Terminology (Bolded for Memory Retention)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **PTE (Page Table Entry)** | One row of the page table; contains frame number + control bits. |
| **Valid/Invalid Bit (V/I)** | 1 ⇒ page in memory, 0 ⇒ page not in memory (or illegal). |
| **Protection Bits** | Read / Write / Execute permissions. |
| **Reference Bit (R)** | Set by hardware on every read/write; used for LRU approximation. |
| **Modify / Dirty Bit (M)** | Set by hardware on every write; tells OS the page is "dirty". |
| **Frame Number** | The physical base address of the frame holding this page. |
| **Hit Ratio (h)** | Fraction of memory references satisfied by the TLB. |
| **Effective Access Time (EAT)** | Average memory access time including TLB overhead. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Page Table Entry (PTE)

A standard PTE on a 32-bit system looks like this:

- **Frame Number** (≈ 20 bits): Real physical frame address.
- **Valid/Invalid Bit** (1 bit): 1 = in memory, 0 = not in memory.
- **Protection Bits** (2–3 bits): RWX permissions.
- **Reference Bit** (1 bit): Used by page-replacement algorithms.
- **Modify (Dirty) Bit** (1 bit): Set on write.
- **Caching/Disable Bit** (1 bit): For I/O consistency.

The size of a PTE in a 32-bit system is usually **4 bytes (32 bits)** — a key KTU fact because it lets you compute the total page-table size.

---

### 2.2 How Address Translation Works — Step-by-Step Logic

1. CPU generates a **logical address** `<page-number, offset>`.
2. MMU uses the **page number** as an **index** into the page table.
3. The **PTBR** points to the *start* of the page table in physical memory.
4. MMU computes `PTE_address = PTBR + (page_number × PTE_size)`.
5. The PTE is fetched from physical memory.
6. The PTE's frame number is combined with the offset to form the **physical address**.
7. The actual data byte/word is fetched using that physical address.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost of a single translation (no TLB):** **2 memory accesses** — one to read the PTE, one to read the data. This is the *why* behind needing a TLB.

---

### 2.3 Multilevel Page Tables — Why We Need Them

A single-level page table for a 32-bit address space with 4 KB pages would have:
- $2^{20}$ entries × **4 bytes** = **4 MB per process**.
With 100 processes, that's 400 MB just for page tables — a huge waste, because most of those pages are *never used*.

**Solution:** Use a **two-level (or multi-level) page table** that keeps page tables *out-of-memory* unless their pages are actually allocated. This is what x86 (CR3 → Page-Directory → Page-Table → Frame) and most modern CPUs use.

- **Level 1 (outer/page-directory):** Indexed by the *outer* page-number bits; a PTE here is *either* a pointer to a level-2 table *or* an "invalid" marker.
- **Level 2 (inner/page-table):** Indexed by the *inner* page-number bits; gives the final frame number.

---

### 2.4 Inverted Page Table

Instead of one page table per process, the system keeps **one single page table** indexed by **physical frame number**. Each entry contains the `<process-id, virtual-page-number>` pair occupying that frame. Translation requires a search (often using a hash) — a hardware hash unit or software search is needed. Used in PowerPC, IA-64.

- **Memory overhead:** Constant (proportional to physical memory, *not* virtual).
- **Drawback:** Slower translation; TLB *mandatory* for performance.

---

### 2.5 Hashed Page Table

A common implementation of inverted page tables. Each virtual page is hashed to a hash-table entry. Collisions are handled by a linked list. Each entry contains `<process-id, virtual-page-number, frame-number, pointer>`. Hardware hashing accelerates lookup.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these formulas. Almost every 14-mark KTU question on this topic is a numerical EAT problem.

| Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Page size | $P$ | Bytes (e.g., 4 KB) |
| Logical address bits | $a$ | bits |
| Offset bits | $o = \log_2 P$ | bits |
| Page-number bits | $p = a - o$ | bits |
| Number of pages | $N_{pages} = 2^{p}$ | per process |
| Page-table size (1-level) | $S_{PT} = 2^{p} \times \text{sizeof(PTE)}$ | bytes |
| TLB lookup time | $t_{TLB}$ | ns or cycles |
| Memory access time | $t_{mem}$ | ns or cycles |
| Hit ratio | $h$ (a fraction, e.g., 0.9) | dimensionless |
| **Effective Access Time (EAT) — TLB hit** | $t_{TLB} + t_{mem}$ | time per access |
| **EAT with hit ratio $h$** | $\text{EAT} = h(t_{TLB} + t_{mem}) + (1-h)(t_{TLB} + 2 t_{mem})$ | ns or cycles |
| Simplified EAT | $\text{EAT} = t_{TLB} + (2-h)\,t_{mem}$ | when $t_{TLB} \ll t_{mem}$ |
| EAT with associativity $k$ | $\text{EAT} = h(t_{TLB} + t_{mem}) + (1-h)(t_{TLB} + (k+1) t_{mem})$ | $k$ = no. of memory accesses for table walk |
| Multilevel accesses per level | $2^{\,p}$ entries per table; $p$ bits split equally | $k = \text{levels}$ |

> **Real-world utility:** These formulas drive the design of every modern paging system — from your phone's ARM MMU to server-class CPUs running cloud workloads. The hit ratio $h$ is engineered to be $\geq 0.99$ in production, otherwise system performance collapses (a phenomenon called *TLB thrashing*).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Effective Access Time (EAT)

Let:
- $h$ = TLB hit ratio,
- $t_{TLB}$ = time for one TLB lookup,
- $t_{mem}$ = time for one memory access.

We consider two mutually-exclusive cases.

**Case 1 — TLB hit (probability $h$):**
- Step 1: TLB lookup takes $t_{TLB}$.
- Step 2: Frame number is found; data is read from memory in $t_{mem}$.
- Total time per access: $T_{hit} = t_{TLB} + t_{mem}$.

**Case 2 — TLB miss (probability $1 - h$):**
- Step 1: TLB lookup takes $t_{TLB}$.
- Step 2: PTE must be read from page table in memory: one extra $t_{mem}$.
- Step 3: Actual data is read from memory: $t_{mem}$.
- Total time per access: $T_{miss} = t_{TLB} + 2 t_{mem}$.

**Expected (Effective) Access Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= h \cdot T_{hit} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{miss} \\
&= h \cdot (t_{TLB} + t_{mem}) + (1 - h) \cdot (t_{TLB} + 2 t_{mem}) \\
&= h\,t_{TLB} + h\,t_{mem} + t_{TLB} + 2 t_{mem} - h\,t_{TLB} - 2 h\,t_{mem} \\
&= t_{TLB} + (2 - h)\,t_{mem}.
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the *master* EAT formula KTU examiners love.

---

### 3.2 KTU-Style Numerical Problem — Solved Fully

> **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model]** *Consider a paging system with TLB access time of 20 ns and main-memory access time of 100 ns. (a) Find EAT for a hit ratio of 80%. (b) Find EAT for a hit ratio of 98%. (c) What hit ratio is needed to keep EAT below 150 ns?*

**Part (a) — $h = 0.80$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= t_{TLB} + (2 - h)\,t_{mem} \\
&= 20 + (2 - 0.80) \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 1.20 \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 120 = 140 \text{ ns}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — $h = 0.98$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= 20 + (2 - 0.98) \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 1.02 \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 102 = 122 \text{ ns}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Required $h$ for EAT $\leq 150$ ns:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
20 + (2 - h) \times 100 &\le 150 \\
(2 - h) \times 100 &\le 130 \\
2 - h &\le 1.30 \\
h &\ge 0.70.
\end{aligned}
$$

So a hit ratio of **70 %** or more is the threshold.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Page-Table Structures

> [!NOTE]
> This table is a *high-yield comparison* KTU examiners use to frame 7-mark sub-questions.

| Feature | Single-Level | Two-Level | Inverted | Hashed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Indexed by | Virtual page no. | Virtual page no. (split) | Physical frame no. | Hashed virtual page no. |
| Number of tables | 1 per process | 1 outer + many inner | 1 for whole system | 1 for whole system |
| Memory overhead | Large (wasted) | Moderate (sparse-friendly) | Constant (O(physical)) | Constant + collisions |
| Lookup speed | 1 memory access | 2 memory accesses | Hash / search | Hash + chain walk |
| TLB needed? | Recommended | Recommended | **Mandatory** | **Mandatory** |
| Used in | Older / educational | x86, MIPS, ARM | PowerPC, IA-64 | IBM, embedded |
| Page sharing | Easy | Easy | Hard (process-id check) | Hard |

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Page-Table Size for a 32-bit System

Given:
- Logical address = 32 bits.
- Page size $P$ = 4 KB = $2^{12}$ bytes.
- PTE size = **4 bytes**.

Step 1 — Offset bits:
$$
o = \log_2(4096) = 12 \text{ bits}.
$$

Step 2 — Page-number bits:
$$
p = 32 - 12 = 20 \text{ bits}.
$$

Step 3 — Number of pages:
$$
N_{pages} = 2^{20} = 1\,048\,576.
$$

Step 4 — Page-table size:
$$
S_{PT} = 1\,048\,576 \times 4 = 4 \text{ MB per process}.
$$

This is why 32-bit OSes like older Windows / Linux need large page tables — and why **multilevel paging** is essential.

---

### 3.5 Two-Level Paging — Address Split

With 32 bits and 4 KB pages, the page number has 20 bits. We can split it as `p1 = 10 bits, p2 = 10 bits` for a 2-level scheme:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Outer index } (p_1) &= 10 \text{ bits} \Rightarrow 1024 \text{ page-directory entries}. \\
\text{Inner index } (p_2) &= 10 \text{ bits} \Rightarrow 1024 \text{ PTE per inner table}. \\
\text{Offset } (o) &= 12 \text{ bits}.
\end{aligned}
$$

The **page directory** is always in memory (1 page = 4 KB), and **inner tables** are brought in only as needed. Total overhead is now proportional to *actual* pages used, not the entire 4 MB.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TLB + Page-Table Translation Flow

The diagram below shows the complete flow when the MMU consults the TLB, and what happens on a hit vs. a miss.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Issues Logical Address] --> B[Extract Page Number P and Offset O]
    B --> C{TLB Lookup for P}
    C -- TLB Hit --> D[Read Frame Number F from TLB]
    D --> E[Form Physical Address F + O]
    E --> F[Access Physical Memory to Get Data]
    C -- TLB Miss --> G[Access Page Table in Main Memory]
    G --> H[Read Frame Number F from PTE]
    H --> I[Update TLB with New Translation]
    I --> E
    F --> J[Return Data to CPU]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The hit path is *one* memory access.
- The miss path is *two* memory accesses plus a TLB update.

---

### 4.2 Two-Level Page Table Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Logical Address 32 bits] --> B[Outer Index 10 bits p1]
    A --> C[Inner Index 10 bits p2]
    A --> D[Offset 12 bits o]

    B --> E[Page Directory PTBR + p1 x 4]
    E --> F[Inner Page Table Base Address]
    F --> G[Inner PTE = Table Base + p2 x 4]
    G --> H[Frame Number F]
    H --> I[Physical Address F + o]
```

This is the canonical **x86 32-bit two-level paging** layout.

---

### 4.3 Page Table Entry (PTE) Bit Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PTE[Page Table Entry 32 bits] --> F[Frame Number 20 bits]
    PTE --> V[Valid Invalid Bit 1 bit]
    PTE --> M[Modify or Dirty Bit 1 bit]
    PTE --> R[Reference Bit 1 bit]
    PTE --> C[Cache Disabled Bit 1 bit]
    PTE --> W[Protection Bits RWX 3 bits]
    PTE --> X[Reserved 6 bits]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Different architectures use slightly different bit assignments, but the *principle* — frame number + control bits — is universal.

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Comparison of Table Structures

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SingleLevel[Single Level Page Table]
        SL1[PTBR] --> SL2[Linear Array of 2 to the p PTEs]
        SL2 --> SL3[Indexed by Virtual Page Number]
    end
    subgraph MultiLevel[Two Level Page Table]
        ML1[PTBR] --> ML2[Outer Page Directory]
        ML2 --> ML3[Inner Page Table 1]
        ML2 --> ML4[Inner Page Table 2]
        ML2 --> ML5[Inner Page Table N]
    end
    subgraph Inverted[Inverted Page Table]
        IL1[Single Global Table] --> IL2[Indexed by Physical Frame]
        IL2 --> IL3[Each entry holds Process ID and Virtual Page]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`  
> **What is a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)? Why is it needed in a paging system?**

**Model Answer:**

A TLB is a small, high-speed **associative cache** inside the Memory Management Unit (MMU) that stores recent virtual-to-physical page translations. In a pure paging system (no TLB), every memory reference requires **two physical memory accesses** — one to read the PTE from the page table, and one to read the actual data. This *doubles* the effective memory access time.

The TLB solves this by caching the most-recently-used translations so they can be retrieved in *one* cycle. Because programs exhibit strong **locality of reference** (spatial and temporal), the TLB hit ratio is typically very high (95–99 %), making the effective access time only slightly higher than a single memory access. **(3 marks — definition 1, problem 1, solution 1)**

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]`  
> **List and explain any three fields of a Page Table Entry (PTE).**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Valid/Invalid Bit (1 bit):** Indicates whether the page is currently in physical memory (1) or not (0). A 0 typically triggers a page fault if the page is referenced. **(1 mark)**
2. **Frame Number (≈ 20–52 bits):** The physical base address of the frame in main memory where the page is stored. This is the actual translation output. **(1 mark)**
3. **Modify / Dirty Bit (1 bit):** Set automatically by the hardware on every write to the page. The OS uses it to decide whether the page must be written back to disk during replacement. **(1 mark)**
   *(Acceptable alternatives: Reference bit, Protection bits, Caching-disabled bit.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of a **page table** in a paging system. Discuss the role of the **Page Table Base Register (PTBR)** in address translation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Given $t_{mem} = 200$ ns, $t_{TLB} = 20$ ns, hit ratio $h = 0.90$, compute the **Effective Access Time (EAT)**. If the hit ratio drops to 80 %, what is the new EAT? Comment on the importance of a high TLB hit ratio. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Page Table & PTBR — 7 marks**

A **page table** is a per-process data structure maintained by the operating system in main memory that stores the mapping from each **virtual page number** of a process to its corresponding **physical frame number** in RAM. The CPU generates logical addresses of the form `<page-number, offset>`. The Memory Management Unit (MMU) uses the page-number as an *index* into the page table to fetch the corresponding **Page Table Entry (PTE)**, which contains the frame number. The frame number is then concatenated with the offset to form the physical address used to access main memory. *(3 marks for definition + working)*

The **Page Table Base Register (PTBR)** is a special CPU register that holds the *starting physical address* of the currently executing process's page table. Because every process has its own independent page table, the PTBR is part of the process's **context**. On a **context switch**, the OS reloads the PTBR so that the new process's page table is used. This allows the hardware to *find* the page table quickly without software help. *(2 marks for PTBR role + context switch explanation)*

A typical page-table lookup (without TLB) requires **2 memory accesses** — one to read the PTE, one to read the data. The PTBR makes the first access possible: `PTE_address = PTBR + (page_number × PTE_size)`. *(2 marks for diagram, formula, and cost analysis)*

*Suggested diagram:*

```
+-----------+       +--------------------+
| Logical   |       |  Page Table        |
| Address   |-----> |  (in Main Memory)  |
|  P  |  O  |       +----+----+----+----+
+-----------+            |    |    |    |
                         v    v    v    v
                       PTE  PTE  PTE  PTE
                        |    |    |    |
                        v    v    v    v
                   Frame Frame Frame Frame
                    (PTE contains frame no.)
```

**(b) EAT Calculation — 7 marks**

Given:
- $t_{TLB} = 20$ ns,
- $t_{mem} = 200$ ns,
- $h = 0.90$ initially.

**Case 1: $h = 0.90$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= t_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot t_{mem} \\
&= 20 + (2 - 0.90) \times 200 \\
&= 20 + 1.10 \times 200 \\
&= 20 + 220 = 240 \text{ ns}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**Case 2: $h = 0.80$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= 20 + (2 - 0.80) \times 200 \\
&= 20 + 1.20 \times 200 \\
&= 20 + 240 = 260 \text{ ns}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Comment (2 marks):** A *10 %* drop in hit ratio increased EAT by **20 ns** (≈ 8 % slower). This shows that TLB performance is **highly sensitive** to hit ratio. Modern systems engineer $h \ge 0.99$ using large TLBs (hundreds of entries), smart replacement policies, and huge pages. Even small drops trigger visible slowdowns — a phenomenon called **TLB thrashing**.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the structure and operation of a **two-level page table**. Why is it preferred over a single-level page table? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a TLB access time of 10 ns, memory access time of 100 ns, and a hit ratio of 95 %, compute the EAT. What is the maximum hit ratio required so that the EAT does not exceed 120 ns? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Two-Level Page Table — 7 marks**

A **two-level page table** is a hierarchical paging structure designed to overcome the *memory waste* of single-level paging. In a single-level scheme, a 32-bit address space with 4 KB pages requires $2^{20}$ PTEs ≈ 4 MB per process, even when the process uses only a few pages. *(1 mark for problem statement)*

In a two-level scheme, the 20-bit page number is split into two parts: an **outer page number** $p_1$ (e.g., 10 bits) and an **inner page number** $p_2$ (e.g., 10 bits). The **outer page directory** is indexed by $p_1$ and contains pointers to **inner page tables** indexed by $p_2$. The inner table's PTE finally gives the frame number. *(2 marks for structure and bit-splitting)*

Operationally, the MMU first consults the page directory (always resident in memory) using $p_1$ to get the base address of the relevant inner page table, then uses $p_2$ to look up the actual PTE in that inner table. The frame number from the PTE is then combined with the 12-bit offset to form the physical address. *(2 marks for operation)*

It is preferred over a single-level page table because: (i) inner page tables can be **swapped out / not allocated** if their virtual pages are unused, dramatically reducing memory overhead; (ii) it scales to large address spaces; (iii) it is the natural fit for hardware page-table walkers in modern CPUs. *(2 marks for advantages)*

**(b) EAT Calculation — 7 marks**

Given: $t_{TLB} = 10$ ns, $t_{mem} = 100$ ns, $h = 0.95$.

**EAT computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= t_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot t_{mem} \\
&= 10 + (2 - 0.95) \times 100 \\
&= 10 + 1.05 \times 100 \\
&= 10 + 105 = 115 \text{ ns}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**Required hit ratio for EAT $\le 120$ ns:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 + (2 - h) \times 100 &\le 120 \\
(2 - h) \times 100 &\le 110 \\
2 - h &\le 1.10 \\
h &\ge 0.90.
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**Conclusion (1 mark):** A hit ratio of **at least 90 %** is required to keep the EAT within the 120 ns bound, demonstrating again the high sensitivity of paging performance to TLB effectiveness.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting the $t_{TLB}$ term** in the EAT formula. Many students write only $(2 - h) t_{mem}$, losing 1 full mark.
> 2. **Mixing up the EAT with the simpler $(2h + 1)t_{mem}$ form** — both are algebraically correct, but only one matches the *given* TLB time. Always include $t_{TLB}$ if it's given.
> 3. **Confusing *valid* and *reference* bits** in PTE definitions. Valid bit = "in memory?". Reference bit = "has been accessed?". Examiners deduct heavily.
> 4. **Drawing the page table as part of the CPU** instead of main memory — this is a classic conceptual error. The page table is *software*; the *TLB* is *hardware*.
> 5. **Not mentioning the context switch** when discussing PTBR. PTBR is reloaded on every context switch — examiners expect this.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark "explain with diagram" questions. A missing diagram typically costs **1–2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Page Table:** Per-process OS data structure in main memory; maps virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers.
- **PTE Fields:** Frame number + Valid/Invalid + Dirty + Reference + Protection + Cache-disable bits.
- **PTBR:** Hardware register holding the base of the current process's page table; reloaded on context switch.
- **TLB:** Hardware associative cache of recent translations; **TLB hit = 1 memory access**, **TLB miss = 2 memory accesses**.
- **EAT Formula (must memorize):** $\text{EAT} = t_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot t_{mem}$.
- **Hit ratio threshold:** To keep EAT close to $t_{mem}$, hit ratio should exceed **90 %**; real systems target **> 99 %**.
- **Single-level page table size** for 32-bit, 4 KB page, 4-byte PTE = **4 MB** per process (memory waste problem).
- **Two-level / Multilevel:** Splits page number into outer/inner indices; inner tables are allocated only as needed → solves waste.
- **Inverted Page Table:** One system-wide table indexed by physical frame; uses `<pid, vpage>` pair; TLB is *mandatory*.
- **Hashed Page Table:** A common implementation of inverted tables using a hash function for fast lookup.
- **Page fault vs. TLB miss:** TLB miss is a *hardware* event (PTE fetched from memory); *page fault* is a *software* event (page not in memory at all, OS must bring from disk).
- **Memory Protection:** Implemented via the *valid bit* and *protection bits* in the PTE; the MMU checks these on every reference.
- **Modern extensions to know:** **Huge pages** (2 MB / 1 GB) to reduce TLB pressure; **ASIDs (Address Space IDs)** in TLB to avoid flush on context switch; **hardware page-table walkers** (x86-64) that can resolve multi-level misses entirely in hardware.
- **Worked example sanity check:** 4 GB RAM, 4 KB pages → 1 M frames → 1 M PTEs → 4 MB page table (1-level).
- **One-line memory hook:** "*Without a TLB, paging halves your speed; with a good TLB, nobody notices.*"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TLBs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TLBs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: TRANSLATION LOOKASIDE BUFFERS (TLBs)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** is a small, high-speed, fully-associative hardware cache (a special type of associative memory) residing inside the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** of a modern CPU. Its sole function is to store a limited number of recently used **Virtual-to-Physical address translations** (specifically, the mappings between Virtual Page Numbers (VPN) and Physical Frame Numbers (PFN)), thereby accelerating the virtual memory translation process by avoiding costly page table lookups in main memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCST403 - Module 3):** TLBs are categorized under *Memory Management* and are essential for understanding the **performance implications** of virtual memory systems. Examiners frequently test the **Effective Access Time (EAT)** calculation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Smart Contact List" Analogy**
> Imagine you have a phone with **1 million contacts** stored in the main phone book, but you can only see **20 contacts** at a time on your "Favorites" screen. The Favorites screen acts exactly like a **TLB**.
> - Without the Favorites screen (TLB Miss): Every time someone calls, you must *scroll through the entire million-contact phone book* to find the number (this is like walking the multi-level page table in main memory).
> - With the Favorites screen (TLB Hit): You instantly tap the contact from the top screen, saving enormous time.
> - The "favorites" are updated automatically as you make/receive calls, just like the TLB dynamically caches translations based on recency of use.

### 1.3 Key Hardware Metrics to Remember

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **TLB Size** | **16 to 4096 entries** | Hardware limitation; cannot hold all translations. |
| **Lookup Time** | **~1 CPU cycle** | Why TLBs are critical for performance. |
| **Associativity** | **Fully / Set-Associative** | Determines how entries are searched in parallel. |
| **Hit Ratio ($\alpha$)** | **0.80 – 0.99** (typically **> 98%**) | Fraction of accesses served by TLB. |
| **Page Table Access Time** | **~100 ns** | The expensive operation TLBs avoid. |
| **Cache Memory Access Time** | **~10 ns** | Faster than main memory, but slower than TLB. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of a TLB Lookup

A TLB functions through a deterministic sequence of steps executed entirely in hardware during every memory access.

1.  **Virtual Address Generation:** The CPU's program counter or instruction decoder generates a **Virtual Address (VA)**, which consists of a **Virtual Page Number (VPN)** and an offset.
2.  **TLB Tag Matching:** The hardware simultaneously compares the incoming VPN against the **VPN tags** stored in *all* TLB entries (this is the associative lookup).
3.  **Outcome Branch (Hit or Miss):**
    *   **TLB Hit:** If a matching tag is found, the corresponding **Physical Frame Number (PFN)** is extracted directly. The translation is complete in **1 CPU cycle**.
    *   **TLB Miss:** If no match is found, the hardware must walk the **Page Table in main memory** to fetch the translation. This triggers a significant performance penalty.
4.  **Memory Access:** The now-formed **Physical Address (PA)** is sent to the L1 cache and main memory to fetch the actual data.
5.  **TLB Update Policy:** On a miss, after the page table resolves the translation, the new VPN→PFN pair is inserted into the TLB, potentially evicting an older entry (LRU, Random, or Round-Robin policy).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind TLBs:** Page tables in modern systems are **hierarchical (multi-level)** and reside in main memory. A single virtual address translation could require 2–4 sequential memory accesses. Without a TLB, virtual memory would incur a **200%–400% memory access penalty**, destroying CPU throughput.

## 2.2 TLB Hit and TLB Miss Terminology

*   **TLB Hit ($\alpha$):** The requested VPN is present in the TLB. Translation completes instantly. Probability measured by the **hit ratio $\alpha$**.
*   **TLB Miss ($1 - \alpha$):** The requested VPN is *not* in the TLB. The CPU must consult the page table, a TLB-miss handler (hardware or OS) loads the entry, and the access restarts.
    *   *Soft Miss:* Page is in memory; only the TLB entry is missing.
    *   *Hard Miss / Page Fault:* Page is *not* in memory; requires disk I/O. (Note: A page fault automatically implies a TLB miss, but the reverse is not true.)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **most frequently tested derivations** in KTU examinations for this module. Memorize them along with their boundary conditions.

### **Table 2.3.1: Core EAT Formulas for TLB Systems**

| **Scenario** | **Effective Access Time (EAT) Formula** | **Notes / Boundary Conditions** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Basic TLB + Single-Level Page Table | $EAT = \alpha \cdot (T_{tlb} + T_m) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot (T_{tlb} + T_{pt} + T_m)$ | $T_{tlb}$: TLB lookup; $T_m$: main memory; $T_{pt}$: page table access |
| Simplified (assuming $T_{tlb} \approx 0$ or included in cycle) | $EAT = \alpha \cdot T_m + (1 - \alpha) \cdot (T_{pt} + T_m)$ | Most common KTU form |
| With L1 Cache ($T_c$) | $EAT = \alpha \cdot (T_{tlb} + T_c) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot (T_{tlb} + T_{pt} + T_m)$ | Used when cache integration is tested |
| With Page Fault (P=$p_{fault}$) | $EAT_{full} = EAT_{no\_fault} + p_{fault} \cdot T_{disk}$ | $T_{disk}$ is the disk service time ($\approx 10$ ms) |
| Associative Lookup Cost | $Cost_{assoc} = C_{compare} \cdot n$ | $n$: number of TLB entries; parallel hardware search |

### **Table 2.3.2: Variable Definitions and Standard Units**

| Symbol | Definition | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\alpha$ | TLB hit ratio (probability of a hit) | Dimensionless ($0 \le \alpha \le 1$) |
| $1 - \alpha$ | TLB miss ratio | Dimensionless |
| $T_{tlb}$ | Time to access the TLB | **nanoseconds (ns)** |
| $T_m$ | Time to access main memory | **nanoseconds (ns)** |
| $T_c$ | Time to access L1 cache | **nanoseconds (ns)** |
| $T_{pt}$ | Time to access page table in memory | **nanoseconds (ns)** |
| $T_{disk}$ | Time to service a page fault from disk | **milliseconds (ms)** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

TLBs are fundamental to **virtually all modern computing architectures**: x86 (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen), ARM (Apple M-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon), and RISC-V. They directly enable:
*   **Process Isolation:** TLBs tag entries with an **Address Space Identifier (ASID)** or **Process ID (PID)** so that process context switches do not require flushing the TLB.
*   **Demand Paging Performance:** They make the multi-level page table scheme practical by amortizing the cost of expensive walks.
*   **Security Boundaries:** Modern tagged TLBs support memory protection keys used in OS kernels for sandboxing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Standard Effective Access Time (EAT) with TLB

This is the most important derivation. We will perform it step-by-step with complete algebraic logic, assuming a TLB lookup, a single-level page table, and one main memory access.

**Given Parameters (Standard KTU Notation):**
*   $\alpha$ = TLB hit ratio
*   $T_{tlb}$ = Time to search the TLB
*   $T_m$ = Time to access main memory for the actual data
*   $T_{pt}$ = Time to access the page table in main memory

**Step 1: Formulate the Time Cost for a TLB Hit**
When the TLB hits, the CPU does not consult the page table. The total time is the sum of the TLB lookup and the subsequent main memory data fetch.

$$
T_{hit} = T_{tlb} + T_m
$$

**Step 2: Formulate the Time Cost for a TLB Miss**
When the TLB misses, the CPU must first perform the TLB lookup (which failed), then walk the page table in main memory, and *finally* access the actual data in main memory.

$$
T_{miss} = T_{tlb} + T_{pt} + T_m
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Weighted Average Principle**
The Effective Access Time (EAT) is the expected time per memory reference, calculated as the sum of each outcome's cost multiplied by its probability of occurrence.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= P(hit) \cdot T_{hit} + P(miss) \cdot T_{miss} \\
    &= \alpha \cdot (T_{tlb} + T_m) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot (T_{tlb} + T_{pt} + T_m)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Simplification (The "Exam-Ready" Form)**
In many KTU problems, the TLB access time is considered to be part of the standard memory access cycle (i.e., $T_{tlb}$ is absorbed into the 1-cycle cost), or it is negligible. The simplified form becomes the **canonical KTU EAT formula**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= \alpha \cdot T_m + (1 - \alpha) \cdot (T_{pt} + T_m) \\
    &= \alpha \cdot T_m + T_{pt} + T_m - \alpha \cdot T_{pt} - \alpha \cdot T_m \\
    &= (1 - \alpha) \cdot T_{pt} + T_m
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The term $(1 - \alpha) \cdot T_{pt}$ represents the **average penalty** the system pays for missing the TLB. Notice that $T_m$ appears unconditionally; the data *must* always be fetched from memory.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem Statement:** A system uses a TLB with a hit ratio of $\alpha = 0.85$. The TLB access time is **20 ns**. Main memory access for the data word takes **100 ns**, and the page table is also stored in main memory. Calculate the EAT.

**Step 1: Identify Given Values**
*   $\alpha = 0.85$
*   $T_{tlb} = 20$ ns
*   $T_m = 100$ ns
*   $T_{pt} = 100$ ns (page table in main memory)

**Step 2: Calculate $T_{hit}$ and $T_{miss}$**

$$
T_{hit} = 20 + 100 = 120 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{miss} = 20 + 100 + 100 = 220 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the EAT Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= 0.85 \cdot (120) + 0.15 \cdot (220) \\
    &= 102 + 33 \\
    &= 135 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verify the Simplified Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT_{simplified} &= (1 - 0.85) \cdot 100 + 100 \\
                 &= 0.15 \cdot 100 + 100 \\
                 &= 15 + 100 = 115 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice the discrepancy (135 ns vs 115 ns):** The simplified formula *only* works if the TLB access time $T_{tlb}$ is included in the 100 ns memory access time (i.e., the TLB and memory are accessed in the same bus cycle, or TLB time is ignored). The *exact* KTU answer requires the full formula. Always check which assumption the question intends!

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Performance Impact of TLB Hit Ratio

This table illustrates **why TLBs are non-negotiable** in modern architecture. Assume $T_{pt} = T_m = 100$ ns (standard KTU assumption) and $T_{tlb}$ is included.

| Hit Ratio ($\alpha$) | TLB Miss Penalty $(1-\alpha)$ | EAT (Approx.) | Performance vs. No TLB |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **1.00 (100%)** | 0.00 | **100 ns** | 2.0x faster (Baseline) |
| 0.98 | 0.02 | **102 ns** | ~1.96x faster |
| 0.90 | 0.10 | **110 ns** | 1.82x faster |
| 0.80 | 0.20 | **120 ns** | 1.67x faster |
| 0.50 | 0.50 | **150 ns** | 1.33x faster |
| 0.00 (No TLB) | 1.00 | **200 ns** | 1.00x (Baseline) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Even a *mediocre* 80% hit ratio yields a 1.67x speedup. Modern processors target **>99%** hit ratios to minimize the penalty to near-zero.

## 3.4 Derivation 3: EAT with Page Faults Included

**Step 1: Identify the Extra Variable**
Let $p$ be the **page fault rate** (probability that a TLB miss leads to a full page fault requiring disk access). Let $T_{disk}$ be the disk access time.

**Step 2: Construct the Expanded Formula**
If a page fault occurs, the CPU must additionally perform disk I/O *and* restart the entire access.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT_{full} = &\ \alpha \cdot (T_{tlb} + T_m) \\
            & + (1 - \alpha) \cdot p \cdot (T_{disk}) \\
            & + (1 - \alpha) \cdot (1 - p) \cdot (T_{tlb} + T_{pt} + T_m)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Magnitude Check (Why Page Faults are Devastating)**
If $T_{disk} = 10$ ms $= 10,000,000$ ns, even a tiny page fault rate of $p = 0.001$ (0.1%) adds **10,000 ns** to the EAT, *completely swamping* the 100 ns memory access time.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: TLB in the Address Translation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the data flow and hardware decision logic when a virtual address is processed by the MMU.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Issues Virtual Address] --> B[Split into VPN and Offset]
    B --> C{Concurrent TLB Lookup}
    C -- TLB Hit --> D[Extract PFN from TLB]
    C -- TLB Miss --> E[Fetch Translation from Page Table in RAM]
    E --> F[Update TLB with New Entry]
    F --> D
    D --> G[Form Physical Address PA = PFN + Offset]
    G --> H[Access L1 Cache for Data]
    H -- Cache Hit --> I[Return Data to CPU]
    H -- Cache Miss --> J[Access Main Memory RAM]
    J --> K[Return Data to CPU]
```

## 4.2 State Machine: TLB Entry Lifecycle

This diagram depicts the lifecycle of a single TLB entry, which is a core concept tested in Part B questions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Invalid
    Invalid --> Valid: Page Table Walk on TLB Miss
    Valid --> Valid: TLB Hit Recency Update
    Valid --> Invalid: TLB Flush Context Switch
    Valid --> Valid: TLB Replacement LRU Evict Old
    Valid --> Invalid: ASID Mismatch Context Switch
    Invalid --> [*]: Power Off
    Valid --> [*]: Power Off
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture: TLB Integration with Cache Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Core
        CPU[CPU Pipeline]
    end
    subgraph MMU_Subsystem
        TLB[Translation Lookaside Buffer]
        PT[Multi-Level Page Table in Main RAM]
    end
    subgraph Memory_Hierarchy
        L1[L1 Cache]
        L2[L2 Cache]
        RAM[Main Memory]
    end
    CPU -- Virtual Address VA --> TLB
    TLB -- Hit: Physical Address PA --> L1
    TLB -- Miss: Walk Request --> PT
    PT -- Physical Address PA --> L1
    L1 -- Miss --> L2
    L2 -- Miss --> RAM
    RAM -- Data --> CPU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rationale:** Notice that the TLB sits *between* the CPU and the cache hierarchy. The physical address (PA) is formed *before* the cache lookup, meaning TLBs enable set-associative caches to use physical tags (VIPT - Virtually Indexed, Physically Tagged) or vice-versa.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB). Why is it needed in a paging system?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** is a small, high-speed associative hardware cache located within the Memory Management Unit (MMU) that stores a subset of the most recently used virtual-to-physical address translations (i.e., VPN-to-PFN mappings).
**Need:** In a paging system, every memory access requires a virtual-to-physical address translation. Without a TLB, the CPU must consult a multi-level page table in main memory for *every* access, which can require 2 to 4 sequential memory fetches. This would degrade system performance by 200% to 400%. The TLB solves this by caching translations, allowing a translation to complete in a single CPU cycle upon a hit, thereby drastically reducing the average memory access time.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between a TLB hit and a TLB miss. What is a "soft miss"?**

**Model Answer:**
*   **TLB Hit:** The requested Virtual Page Number (VPN) is found in the TLB, and the corresponding Physical Frame Number (PFN) is retrieved in a single cycle without consulting the page table.
*   **TLB Miss:** The requested VPN is *not* present in the TLB. The hardware must walk the page table in main memory to resolve the translation, incurring a performance penalty.
*   **Soft Miss:** A specific type of TLB miss where the page *is* present in main memory (i.e., it is a valid, resident page), but the translation has simply been evicted from the TLB. The page table walk succeeds, and the TLB is updated.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**a) Explain the role of the Translation Lookaside Buffer in a paging system with a neat diagram. (7 Marks)**
**b) Suppose a system uses paging with a TLB. The TLB has a hit ratio of 80%. TLB access time is 10 ns, and main memory access time is 100 ns. Calculate the Effective Access Time (EAT). (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Explanation:**
*(Note: Diagram from Section 4.1 should be reproduced here by the student for full marks. Examiner's note: 2 marks are reserved specifically for the diagram.)*
The TLB is a hardware associative memory cache inside the MMU. It functions to store recent VPN→PFN translations.
1. The CPU generates a Virtual Address.
2. The hardware searches the TLB in parallel for the VPN.
3. On a **TLB Hit**, the PFN is retrieved, concatenated with the offset to form the Physical Address, and the data is fetched from memory. Total time $\approx T_{tlb} + T_m$.
4. On a **TLB Miss**, the hardware walks the page table in main memory, fetches the PFN, updates the TLB, and *then* accesses the data. Total time $\approx T_{tlb} + T_{pt} + T_m$.

**Part (b) Numerical Solution:**
**Given:** $\alpha = 0.80$, $T_{tlb} = 10$ ns, $T_m = 100$ ns. Since the page table is in main memory, $T_{pt} = 100$ ns.
**Step 1: Time for TLB Hit**

$$
T_{hit} = T_{tlb} + T_m = 10 + 100 = 110 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2: Time for TLB Miss**

$$
T_{miss} = T_{tlb} + T_{pt} + T_m = 10 + 100 + 100 = 210 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate EAT**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= \alpha \cdot T_{hit} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot T_{miss} \\
    &= 0.80 \cdot (110) + 0.20 \cdot (210) \\
    &= 88 + 42 \\
    &= 130 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Do **not** use the simplified formula $EAT = (1-\alpha)T_{pt} + T_m$ in part (b) unless the problem explicitly states that $T_{tlb}$ is 0 or negligible. In this problem, $T_{tlb} = 10$ ns is explicitly given, so you **must** include it in both the hit and miss paths. Using the simplified formula will yield $120$ ns, which is **wrong** and will result in a 2-mark deduction.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**a) Discuss the different TLB replacement policies. What is ASID and how does it optimize context switching? (7 Marks)**
**b) A virtual memory system employs a TLB with a 90% hit ratio. The TLB lookup time is 20 ns, the main memory access time is 200 ns, and the page table access also takes 200 ns. If 0.1% of memory references result in a page fault with a disk access time of 10 ms, compute the overall Effective Access Time. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Explanation:**
*   **TLB Replacement Policies:** When the TLB is full and a new entry must be loaded, an old entry must be evicted. Common policies include:
    1.  **LRU (Least Recently Used):** Evicts the entry that has not been accessed for the longest time. Best for temporal locality, but costly to implement in hardware.
    2.  **FIFO (First-In, First-Out):** Evicts the oldest entry, regardless of usage.
    3.  **Random:** Evicts a randomly chosen entry. Simple to implement in hardware and surprisingly effective in practice.
*   **ASID (Address Space Identifier):** When the OS performs a context switch, process A's translations are invalid for process B. The naive solution is to *flush the entire TLB*, which causes a flood of TLB misses. The ASID is a unique tag stored alongside each TLB entry that identifies the process owner. The MMU compares the current process's ASID with the TLB entry's ASID, allowing translations for *different processes* to coexist in the TLB simultaneously, thus eliminating the flush penalty.

**Part (b) Numerical Solution:**
**Given:** $\alpha = 0.90$, $T_{tlb} = 20$ ns, $T_m = T_{pt} = 200$ ns, $p = 0.001$, $T_{disk} = 10$ ms $= 10,000,000$ ns.
**Step 1: Define the Three Scenarios**
1. **TLB Hit:** Probability = 0.90. Time = $20 + 200 = 220$ ns.
2. **TLB Miss, No Page Fault:** Probability = $0.10 \cdot 0.999 = 0.0999$. Time = $20 + 200 + 200 = 420$ ns.
3. **TLB Miss + Page Fault:** Probability = $0.10 \cdot 0.001 = 0.0001$. Time = $20 + 200 + 200 + 10,000,000 = 10,000,420$ ns.

**Step 2: Calculate EAT**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (0.90 \cdot 220) + (0.0999 \cdot 420) + (0.0001 \cdot 10,000,420) \\
    &= 198 + 41.958 + 1000.042 \\
    &= 1240 \text{ ns} \approx 1.24 \text{ \mu s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In part (b), students often forget to multiply the page fault rate by the TLB miss rate (i.e., using $p = 0.001$ instead of $(1-\alpha) \cdot p = 0.0001$). This leads to an EAT calculation that is **1000 times too large**, costing up to 3 marks. Always carefully identify the **conditional probability** of a page fault occurring.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for TLB Module (KTU 2024)**

*   **Definition:** TLB = Hardware associative cache for VPN→PFN translations. Located in the MMU.
*   **Primary Purpose:** To avoid the performance penalty of multi-level page table walks in main memory.
*   **Golden Formula (EAT):** $EAT = \alpha(T_{tlb} + T_m) + (1-\alpha)(T_{tlb} + T_{pt} + T_m)$. Memorize the simplified version: $EAT \approx \alpha T_m + (1-\alpha)(T_{pt} + T_m)$ *only* when $T_{tlb}$ is ignored.
*   **Page Fault Integration:** Always multiply the page fault rate by $(1-\alpha)$ to get the joint probability.
*   **ASID (Address Space Identifier):** Hardware tag that enables TLB entries to be shared across processes without flushing on context switches.
*   **Replacement Policies:** LRU (best locality), FIFO, Random (cheapest). Mention at least one in Part B answers for full marks.
*   **Hit Ratio Impact:** Modern systems target $\alpha > 0.98$. A drop to 0.90 roughly doubles memory latency.
*   **Distinction:** A TLB miss is *not* a page fault. A page fault implies a TLB miss, but the reverse is not true.
*   **Diagram Requirement:** Part B 7-mark questions involving TLB *always* expect a block diagram of the MMU/TLB interaction. Practice drawing it from memory.
*   **Numerical Units:** Be careful with unit conversions: $T_{disk}$ is in **ms** (typically $10$ ms $= 10,000,000$ ns) while $T_m$ is in **ns**. Mixing units is the #1 source of calculation errors.
*   **Context Switch Flushing:** Without ASID, every context switch requires a TLB flush, causing a "warm-up" period of TLB misses.
*   **Modern Extension:** In modern CPUs, TLBs are often **multi-level** (L1 TLB for instructions, L1 TLB for data, unified L2 TLB) and support huge pages (2MB/1GB) to reduce TLB pressure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Example: Accessing an Array
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Example: Accessing an Array

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Process Synchronization** and the **Critical Section Problem**, the classic example of *"Accessing an Array"* refers to a scenario where **two or more concurrent processes attempt to read and modify a shared array element** (e.g., `shared_array[i]`) simultaneously. Because a high-level operation like "increment an array element" is **not atomic at the hardware level**, the system may interleave instructions, producing a **Race Condition** that violates mutual exclusion guarantees.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Race Condition (KTU Standard Definition):** A situation where the final outcome of a computation **depends on the precise ordering (interleaving) of instruction execution** of concurrent processes accessing shared data.

A **Critical Section** is the segment of code in which a process accesses shared data structures (such as arrays, variables, files, or tables) and whose concurrent execution may cause race conditions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Tellers at a Bank"

Imagine **two bank tellers (Process P1 and Process P2)** working simultaneously at a single **shared ledger book** (`shared_array[0]`). The ledger contains a single value representing the day's total deposits (initially **₹0**). Both tellers are told to:
1. **Read** the current total.
2. **Add ₹1** to it (a new customer deposited ₹1).
3. **Write** the new total back to the ledger.

**Teller P1** reads the total (₹0), but before writing back, gets interrupted. **Teller P2** also reads (₹0 — because P1 hasn't written yet), adds ₹1, and writes **₹1**. Now P1 resumes and writes its own result of **₹1** — *overwriting* P2's update. The actual total should have been **₹2**, but the ledger shows **₹1**. One rupee vanished! This is the **Lost Update Problem** — a direct consequence of a race condition on a shared array.

### 1.3 The "Array" Specificity in KTU

The reason textbooks use an **array** (rather than a simple scalar) is that arrays emphasize a **shared data structure** that may be:
- Accessed by index (random access shared memory).
- Modified in place (no implicit locking).
- Used in producer–consumer, reader–writer, and bounded buffer problems (where the array itself is the buffer).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 — Learning Outcome Mapping (CO3):** Students must be able to *analyze race conditions*, *identify critical sections*, and *apply synchronization primitives* (locks, semaphores, monitors) to shared data structures like arrays.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Problem Setup (Generic for KTU Board Questions)

Consider a **shared array** `A[0]` of integers, initialized to `0`. Two processes, `P1` and `P2`, each execute the following high-level statement **simultaneously**:

$$A[0] \leftarrow A[0] + 1$$

Each process wishes to increment the array element exactly once. The **expected final result** is:

$$A[0]_{\text{expected}} = 0 + 1 + 1 = 2$$

### 2.2 Why High-Level Statements Are NOT Atomic

A single C/Java statement like `A[0] = A[0] + 1` is translated by the compiler into **three (or more) machine-level instructions** at the CPU/Assembly level. For a typical load-store architecture:

| Step | Machine Instruction (Pseudo-Assembly) | Description |
|:----:|:------------------------------------|:------------|
| 1 | `LOAD  R1, A[0]` | Read the value of array element into register `R1` |
| 2 | `ADD   R1, R1, #1` | Increment the register value by 1 |
| 3 | `STORE A[0], R1` | Write the new value back to the shared array element |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The OS scheduler can **interrupt a process at any instruction boundary**. Therefore, between Steps 1 and 3 of P1, the scheduler may dispatch P2, leading to **lost updates**.

### 2.3 The Four Atomicity Failure Modes

When two processes P1 and P2 execute `A[0] = A[0] + 1` concurrently, the OS may interleave their instructions in the following representative orders:

**Interleaving Scenario A (Correct — Sequential Execution):**
1. P1: `LOAD  R1, A[0]`   → R1 = 0
2. P1: `ADD   R1, R1, #1` → R1 = 1
3. P1: `STORE A[0], R1`   → A[0] = 1
4. P2: `LOAD  R1, A[0]`   → R1 = 1
5. P2: `ADD   R1, R1, #1` → R1 = 2
6. P2: `STORE A[0], R1`   → A[0] = 2 ✓ (Correct)

**Interleaving Scenario B (Race — Lost Update):**
1. P1: `LOAD  R1, A[0]`   → R1 = 0
2. P2: `LOAD  R1, A[0]`   → R1 = 0 *(overwrites R1 of P1 — but here R1 is per-process, so A[0] is still 0)*
3. P1: `ADD   R1, R1, #1` → R1 = 1
4. P2: `ADD   R1, R1, #1` → R1 = 1 *(P2 added 1 to the OLD value it read)*
5. P1: `STORE A[0], R1`   → A[0] = 1
6. P2: `STORE A[0], R1`   → A[0] = 1 ✗ (**Expected = 2, Got = 1**)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Term | Definition / Formula | KTU Significance |
|:-----|:--------------------|:-----------------|
| **Race Condition** | Outcome depends on non-deterministic interleaving of concurrent instructions | Root cause of bugs in shared-memory access |
| **Critical Section (CS)** | Code segment accessing shared variables (e.g., `A[i]`) | Must be guarded by synchronization |
| **Mutual Exclusion** | $\forall P_i, P_j : CS_i \cap CS_j = \emptyset$ at any instant $t$ | Primary correctness property |
| **Progress** | Only processes *not in remainder section* can compete for CS entry | Prevents deadlock / starvation |
| **Bounded Waiting** | A process can be blocked for **at most $k$** other processes before entering CS | Prevents starvation |
| **Atomic Operation** | An instruction that completes without interruption at hardware level | Building block for synchronization |
| **Lost Update** | $X_{\text{final}} = X_{\text{initial}} + \sum \text{(writes that overwrite)}$ | Classic bug in array access example |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The "Array Access" race condition manifests in real systems as:
- **Multithreaded counters** (e.g., web server hit counters incrementing `requests[shard]`).
- **Bank transaction ledgers** (array of account balances).
- **Sensor fusion buffers** (array of latest sensor readings).
- **Parallel scientific computing** (accumulator arrays in `OpenMP` reductions — which use atomic or reduction clauses precisely to avoid this bug).

> [!NOTE]
> The phenomenon generalizes from `A[0]` to any shared mutable data structure: **linked lists, hash tables, B-trees in databases**, etc.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation of the Race Condition on a Shared Array

### 3.1 Formal Problem Statement

Let the **shared array** $A$ be a one-dimensional integer array of size $N$, with $A \in \mathbb{Z}^N$. The array is mapped into a **shared memory region** $M$ such that the address of element $A[k]$ is:

$$\text{addr}(A[k]) = M_{\text{base}} + k \cdot \text{sizeof}(\text{int})$$

Two processes $P_1$ and $P_2$ execute the following algorithm concurrently:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Process } P_1 &: \quad A[0] \leftarrow A[0] + 1 \\
\text{Process } P_2 &: \quad A[0] \leftarrow A[0] + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Initial condition: $A[0]_0 = 0$.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Instruction-by-Instruction Trace

We model the CPU registers of $P_1$ as $R_1$ and of $P_2$ as $R_2$. Each process reads into its **own** register (registers are not shared), but `A[0]` is shared. The instruction set $\mathcal{I}$ contains:

$$
\mathcal{I} = \{\,\text{LOAD}(R, A[0]),\ \text{ADD}(R, R, 1),\ \text{STORE}(A[0], R)\,\}
$$

We enumerate a **single problematic interleaving** (Scenario B from §2.3) step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & P_1: \text{LOAD}(R_1, A[0]) \\
& \Rightarrow R_1 \leftarrow 0,\quad A[0] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Process $P_1$ reads the current value of the shared array element into its private register. $A[0]$ is still 0 in memory.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Scheduler preempts } P_1, \text{ dispatches } P_2 \\
& P_2: \text{LOAD}(R_2, A[0]) \\
& \Rightarrow R_2 \leftarrow 0,\quad A[0] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The OS scheduler performs a context switch. $P_2$ also reads the (stale) value 0 from shared memory. Both processes have now read the **same pre-modification value** of 0.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3:} \quad & P_2: \text{ADD}(R_2, R_2, 1) \\
& \Rightarrow R_2 \leftarrow 0 + 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** $P_2$ increments its private copy. The shared memory $A[0]$ is still 0.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4:} \quad & P_2: \text{STORE}(A[0], R_2) \\
& \Rightarrow A[0] \leftarrow 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** $P_2$ writes its computed value back to the shared array element. Now $A[0] = 1$ in main memory.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Scheduler preempts } P_2, \text{ dispatches } P_1 \\
& P_1: \text{ADD}(R_1, R_1, 1) \\
& \Rightarrow R_1 \leftarrow 0 + 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** $P_1$ resumes and increments **its own private copy** $R_1$, which still holds the *old* value 0. The increment is on the stale snapshot.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 6:} \quad & P_1: \text{STORE}(A[0], R_1) \\
& \Rightarrow A[0] \leftarrow 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** $P_1$ writes 1 to the shared memory, **overwriting** the value that $P_2$ had already stored. The update by $P_2$ is silently lost.

### 3.3 Final Result Analysis

$$
A[0]_{\text{final}} = 1 \quad \text{while} \quad A[0]_{\text{expected}} = 2
$$

The error (lost increment) is:

$$
\Delta = A[0]_{\text{expected}} - A[0]_{\text{final}} = 2 - 1 = 1
$$

In general, for $n$ processes each incrementing $A[0]$ exactly once, the **expected result** is:

$$A[0]_{\text{expected}} = n$$

and the **actual result** (under unrestricted interleaving) lies in:

$$1 \le A[0]_{\text{actual}} \le n$$

with the **probability of a lost update** rising sharply as $n$ increases.

### 3.4 The Correctness Invariant

For the system to be correct, the following **mutual exclusion invariant** must hold for any instant $t$:

$$
\bigl(\,P_1 \in CS(t)\,\bigr) \wedge \bigl(\,P_2 \in CS(t)\,\bigr) \;\Rightarrow\; \bot
$$

That is, it is **logically impossible** for two processes to be inside the critical section simultaneously. This is the **only** way to prevent the lost-update bug on shared array elements.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Data Flow of the Race Condition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Shared Array A 0 in Main Memory = 0] -->|LOAD R1| R1[Register R1 of P1 = 0]
    A0 -->|LOAD R2| R2[Register R2 of P2 = 0]
    R1 -->|ADD 1| R1A[R1 becomes 1]
    R2 -->|ADD 1| R2A[R2 becomes 1]
    R1A -->|STORE| S1[A 0 overwritten to 1]
    R2A -->|STORE| S2[A 0 overwritten to 1 again LOST UPDATE]
    S1 --> X[Final A 0 = 1 INCORRECT Expected = 2]
    S2 --> X
    styleA0 fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    styleX fill:#ffd0d0,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
    styleA0 font-weight:bold
```

**Note on Mermaid:** The above diagram uses generic node IDs (`A0`, `R1`, `R2`, `R1A`, `R2A`, `S1`, `S2`, `X`) and contains no markdown formatting inside the quoted labels. Reserved keywords like `end` and `subgraph` are avoided as node names.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Synchronization Layer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UserSpace[User Processes]
        P1[Process P1]
        P2[Process P2]
    end
    subgraph CriticalSection[Critical Section - Shared Array A 0]
        CS1[Read A 0]
        CS2[Modify A 0]
        CS3[Write A 0]
    end
    subgraph SyncLayer[Synchronization Primitive Layer]
        L1[Lock Acquire - entry section]
        L2[Lock Release - exit section]
    end
    P1 --> L1 --> CS1 --> CS2 --> CS3 --> L2
    P2 --> L1
    L2 --> UserSpace
    styleCriticalSection fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:2px
    styleSyncLayer fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Both processes (`P1`, `P2`) must pass through a **Lock Acquire** gate before entering the critical section that accesses the shared array.
- Only **one process at a time** can be inside the critical section.
- The **Lock Release** gate frees the lock so that the next waiting process may enter.

### 4.3 Timeline of the Problematic Interleaving

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU Scheduler
    participant P1 as Process P1
    participant P2 as Process P2
    participant MEM as Shared Array A 0
    CPU->>P1: Dispatch
    P1->>MEM: LOAD R1 from A 0 R1=0
    CPU-->>P1: Preempt
    CPU->>P2: Dispatch
    P2->>MEM: LOAD R2 from A 0 R2=0
    P2->>P2: ADD R2 = 0+1 = 1
    P2->>MEM: STORE A 0 = 1
    CPU-->>P2: Preempt
    CPU->>P1: Dispatch
    P1->>P1: ADD R1 = 0+1 = 1 stale
    P1->>MEM: STORE A 0 = 1 OVERWRITE
    Note over MEM: Final A 0 = 1 Expected = 2 RACE CONDITION
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define race condition. With the help of an example showing two processes accessing a shared array element, explain how a race condition can result in an inconsistent final value.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Key Pattern):**

- **1 Mark — Definition:** A *race condition* is a situation where the behaviour of a concurrent system depends on the **non-deterministic order** in which interleaved instructions of multiple processes execute, particularly when accessing shared data.

- **1 Mark — Example Setup:** Consider a shared array `A[0]` initialized to 0. Two processes, `P1` and `P2`, each execute `A[0] = A[0] + 1`. The expected final value is 2.

- **1 Mark — Inconsistency Explanation:** The high-level statement is compiled into three machine instructions (`LOAD`, `ADD`, `STORE`). If the scheduler interleaves them such that both processes `LOAD` the value 0 *before either one* has `STORE`d the result, both compute 1, and one update is **lost**. The final value becomes 1 instead of 2.

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is a critical section? Identify the critical section in the following processes that access a shared array `A` of size N:**

```
Process Pi:
    for (i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        A[i] = A[i] + 1;
    }
```

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Key Pattern):**

- **1 Mark — Definition:** A *critical section* is a code segment that accesses shared variables or shared resources and whose concurrent execution must be **mutually exclusive**.

- **2 Marks — Identification & Justification:** The entire `for` loop body `A[i] = A[i] + 1;` constitutes the critical section because every iteration reads and writes a shared array element. To enforce mutual exclusion, this region must be wrapped between `entry_section()` and `exit_section()` synchronization calls, ensuring only one process executes the loop body at any given instant.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

> **Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme provides an **internal choice** between two questions in Part B. The two alternatives below are independent — students answer **one**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 3]`

**(a)** Two processes `P1` and `P2` simultaneously execute the following code, where `A[0]` is a shared array element initialized to 0:

```
Process P1:        Process P2:
A[0] = A[0] + 1    A[0] = A[0] + 1
```

Show that the final value of `A[0]` may be 1 instead of the expected 2. Illustrate your answer with a **step-by-step interleaving of machine instructions** and state the four conditions that a valid solution to the critical section problem must satisfy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Propose and explain a **Peterson's Solution** for two processes accessing the shared array element `A[0]`. Show how the algorithm guarantees mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — High-level to machine-level translation:**
The statement `A[0] = A[0] + 1` is not atomic. It decomposes into:

```
LOAD  R, A[0]      ; read shared element into register
ADD   R, R, #1     ; increment register
STORE A[0], R      ; write back to shared element
```

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Initial state:** `A[0] = 0` in main memory.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Problematic interleaving:**

| Step | Process | Instruction | Effect |
|:----:|:-------:|:------------|:-------|
| 1 | P1 | `LOAD R1, A[0]` | R1 = 0 |
| 2 | P2 | `LOAD R2, A[0]` | R2 = 0 |
| 3 | P1 | `ADD  R1, R1, 1` | R1 = 1 |
| 4 | P2 | `ADD  R2, R2, 1` | R2 = 1 |
| 5 | P1 | `STORE A[0], R1` | A[0] = 1 |
| 6 | P2 | `STORE A[0], R2` | A[0] = 1 |

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Conclusion:** Both processes read the *same stale value* 0, both compute 1, and the second `STORE` overwrites the first. The final value of `A[0]` is **1 instead of 2**. This is the **Lost Update** problem caused by the race condition.

**Step 5 (2 Marks) — Four conditions for a valid CS solution (Must list all four for full marks):**
1. **Mutual Exclusion** — No two processes may be inside the critical section simultaneously.
2. **Progress** — If no process is in the CS and some processes wish to enter, only those not in the remainder section can compete, and the selection cannot be postponed indefinitely.
3. **Bounded Waiting** — There exists a bound on the number of times other processes are allowed to enter the CS after a process has requested entry and before it is granted.
4. **No assumption about relative speed** — The solution must work for any CPU speed or scheduler behaviour.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — Peterson's Algorithm structure:**

```
boolean flag[2] = {false, false};
int turn;

Process Pi (i = 0 or 1):
    do {
        flag[i] = true;           /* "I want to enter" */
        turn = j;                 /* "Yield to the other" */
        while (flag[j] && turn == j)   ;   /* busy wait */
            critical section   /* access A[0] */
        flag[i] = false;          /* "I have exited" */
            remainder section
    } while (true);
```

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Mutual Exclusion Proof:**
