Suppose both P0 and P1 are simultaneously in the CS. Then `flag[0] = flag[1] = true`. The `while` condition of P0 requires `turn == 1`, and the `while` condition of P1 requires `turn == 0`. But `turn` can hold only one value at a time — a contradiction. Hence, mutual exclusion holds.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Progress and Bounded Waiting:**
- *Progress:* Only the process that sets `turn` to the other process can be blocked. The other process either does not wish to enter (`flag[j] = false`) or yields.
- *Bounded Waiting:* After Pi has set `flag[i] = true` and `turn = j`, Pj enters at most **once** before Pi gets a turn (because Pi will not be blocked once Pj exits and resets `flag[j] = false`). Thus the bound is `k = 1`.

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Application to shared array:**
With Peterson's algorithm wrapping the statement `A[0] = A[0] + 1;`, the lost-update bug is eliminated: only one process at a time may execute the three-instruction sequence atomically from the viewpoint of the other process.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Alternative, Module 3]`

**(a)** Define a **semaphore**. Using a shared integer array `buffer[N]` as a bounded buffer, explain how **counting semaphores** solve the producer–consumer problem. State the initial values of all semaphores used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the same bounded-buffer array, explain the difference between **binary (mutex) semaphores** and **counting semaphores**. Why is a counting semaphore alone insufficient, and why do we need both? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — Semaphore definition:**
A semaphore `S` is a synchronization variable accessed only through two **atomic** operations: `wait(S)` (also called `P(S)`) and `signal(S)` (also called `V(S)`). It maintains an integer value and a waiting queue of blocked processes.

```
wait(S):  while (S <= 0) ;   /* busy wait or block */
          S--;
signal(S): S++;
```

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Setup for the bounded buffer `buffer[N]`:**
Three semaphores are used:
- `mutex` — initialized to **1** (ensures mutual exclusion on `buffer[]`).
- `empty` — initialized to **N** (counts the number of empty slots in the array).
- `full`  — initialized to **0** (counts the number of filled slots in the array).

**Step 3 (3 Marks) — Algorithm:**

```
Producer:                         Consumer:
do {                              do {
    wait(empty);                      wait(full);
    wait(mutex);                      wait(mutex);
    /* add item to buffer[in] */      /* remove item from buffer[out] */
    in = (in + 1) % N;                out = (out + 1) % N;
    signal(mutex);                    signal(mutex);
    signal(full);                     signal(empty);
} while (true);                    } while (true);
```

The `empty` semaphore blocks the producer when the array is full; the `full` semaphore blocks the consumer when the array is empty; `mutex` ensures only one process manipulates the shared array at a time.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — Binary vs Counting:**
- A **binary (mutex) semaphore** takes only the values 0 or 1 — it grants *exclusive* access to a single process.
- A **counting semaphore** can take any non-negative integer value — it represents the count of available *resource instances* (e.g., empty slots in `buffer[N]`).

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Why counting alone is insufficient:**
If we use only a counting semaphore, we can correctly count empty/full slots, but we **cannot guarantee mutual exclusion** on the array indices `buffer[in]` and `buffer[out]`. Two producers could simultaneously update the shared `in` variable, causing a race condition (the *same race condition* as in Question A's array-access example, generalized to index pointers).

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Why both are needed:**
The counting semaphores (`empty`, `full`) handle *resource availability* (i.e., logic of the problem), while the binary semaphore (`mutex`) handles *mutual exclusion* on the actual shared array access. Both are required to ensure (i) boundedness, (ii) correctness of slot counts, and (iii) atomic updates to the shared `in`/`out` indices.

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — KTU Pitfall:**
A common student error is to initialize `empty = 0` (instead of `N`) — this causes a *deadlock* at startup, since the producer blocks immediately. Always verify: `empty + full = N` at all times (an invariant).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **"Atomic" misuse (–1 mark):** Stating that `A[0] = A[0] + 1` is atomic. It is **not** — this is the *exact reason* for the race condition.
> 2. **Skipping register-level breakdown (–2 marks):** KTU expects the three-instruction decomposition (`LOAD → ADD → STORE`) in the model answer.
> 3. **Forgetting Bounded Waiting (–1 mark):** In Peterson's solution, students often prove only mutual exclusion, omitting the **bounded waiting = 1** argument.
> 4. **Wrong semaphore initialization (–1 mark):** `empty = 0` causes deadlock. `mutex` must start at 1.
> 5. **No "race" terminology:** Always use the phrase *"race condition"* and *"critical section"* in your answer — KTU evaluators scan for these exact keywords.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Race condition** on a shared array arises because `A[i] = A[i] + 1` is **non-atomic** and decomposes into `LOAD → ADD → STORE` at the machine level.
- The **Lost Update Problem** is the canonical bug: $n$ processes each incrementing once should yield $n$, but unrestricted interleaving may produce any value in $[1, n]$.
- The **Critical Section** is the region `A[i] = A[i] + 1;` (or the surrounding loop) that must be guarded by synchronization.
- A **valid CS solution** must satisfy: **Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting, and No Speed Assumption** (4 marks standard question).
- **Peterson's Algorithm** (2-process) uses two flags + a `turn` variable; **Dekker's Algorithm** is the historical predecessor; both are software-only solutions assuming atomic reads/writes.
- **Hardware solutions** include `TestAndSet()` and `CompareAndSwap()` (CAS) atomic instructions.
- **Semaphores** scale to $n$ processes: `mutex = 1`, `empty = N`, `full = 0` for a bounded buffer array of size $N$.
- The invariant `empty + full = N` must always hold.
- Always use the phrase **"race condition"** and **"critical section"** in KTU answers — these are *keyword triggers* in the valuation key.
- Remember the formula: $\text{Expected final value} = n$ for $n$ processes performing `A[0] = A[0] + 1` under correct mutual exclusion.
- For a single shared array element, even a **busy-wait lock** (`while (locked) ;`) is sufficient to prevent the race.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Quick-Recall Mnemonic — "LASS":**
> **L**oad → **A**dd → **S**tore → **S**cheduler may interrupt at any of these 3 points, causing the race.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TLB Hits and Misses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TLB Hits and Misses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

The **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** is a small, high-speed **hardware associative cache** (also called an Address Translation Cache) maintained by the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** of the processor. It stores a limited number of recently used **Page Table Entries (PTEs)**, each containing a *Virtual Page Number (VPN)* paired with its corresponding *Physical Frame Number (PFN)* along with protection and status bits.

- **TLB Hit**: A memory reference is said to result in a **TLB Hit** when the processor, while translating a virtual address, finds the required Page Table Entry (PTE) already present inside the TLB. The physical frame number can therefore be retrieved directly from the TLB in a single associative lookup without any further memory access to the page table.
- **TLB Miss**: A memory reference is said to result in a **TLB Miss** when the required Page Table Entry is **not present** inside the TLB. The system must then consult the page table in main memory (causing extra memory references) and subsequently update the TLB with the new entry, typically replacing an existing entry using a replacement policy (LRU, FIFO, Random).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The TLB exploits the **Principle of Locality** (both spatial and temporal). Most programs access only a small subset of pages in a short interval, so caching a handful of PTEs in the TLB dramatically reduces the cost of address translation. This is a *mandatory* topic under Module 3 (Memory Management) and is a frequent 14-mark KTU question.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are reading a thick textbook (the *main page table in memory*) using an index card (the *TLB*). The index card contains translations for only the **last 10 to 100 words** you looked up. When you encounter a new word:

- **Cache (TLB) Hit**: You glance at your index card — the translation is already written there. You get the meaning **instantly in one step**, no need to flip through the dictionary's full pages.
- **Cache (TLB) Miss**: The word is not on the index card. You must now flip through the heavy dictionary (the full page table in main memory), find the translation, write it onto your index card (overwriting the *least recently used* entry to make space), and then finally use the translation.

Just as the index card is a tiny but lightning-fast helper, the TLB is a small piece of on-chip SRAM that hides the slowness of two-level or multi-level page tables.

## 1.3 Standard Hardware Metrics Used in KTU Problems

> [!NOTE]
> The following parameters are **constants you will see in KTU numericals**:
>
> - **TLB access time (t)** ≈ **20 ns** (typical; sometimes 10 ns or 40 ns)
> - **Main memory access time (m)** ≈ **100 ns to 200 ns** (typical)
> - **TLB size (number of entries)** ≈ **16 to 512 entries** (associative)
> - **TLB Hit Ratio (h)** = probability of finding the entry in TLB, typically **80% to 99%**
> - **TLB Miss Ratio (1 − h)** = probability of NOT finding the entry in TLB

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

When the CPU generates a virtual address, the MMU performs the following sequence:

1. **Step 1 — Present the VPN to the TLB.** The Virtual Page Number portion of the address is simultaneously compared (associatively) against every tag stored in the TLB.
2. **Step 2 — TLB Lookup Outcome:**
   - If the VPN **matches** a tag in the TLB, the **TLB Hit** signal is asserted. The corresponding Physical Frame Number (PFN) is concatenated with the page offset to form the physical address. Only **one** memory reference is needed for the actual data.
   - If the VPN **does not match** any tag, a **TLB Miss** occurs. The hardware consults the in-memory page table (potentially a multi-level walk) to fetch the correct PTE. The PTE is loaded into the TLB (evicting a victim entry). **Two (or more)** memory references are now required.
3. **Step 3 — Memory Access:** Using the resolved physical frame number, the CPU accesses the actual data/instruction in main memory.
4. **Step 4 — On a TLB Miss with Modified/Invalid Bit Set:** A **page fault** may be triggered (handled by the OS) if the PTE indicates the page is not in main memory. The page must be brought from secondary storage before the instruction can complete.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **every formula** a KTU student must memorize for the TLB topic. Replace `|` (pipe) symbols with `\vert` in your written answer sheets to be safe.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Hit Ratio | $h = \dfrac{\text{Number of TLB Hits}}{\text{Total Memory References}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le h \le 1$ |
| 2 | Miss Ratio | $\text{Miss Ratio} = 1 - h$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Effective Access Time (with TLB) | $\text{EAT} = h \cdot (t + m) + (1 - h) \cdot (t + 2m)$ | Time in ns / µs |
| 4 | Effective Access Time (no TLB) | $\text{EAT} = 2m$ | Time in ns (one page table + one data access) |
| 5 | Access Time with Hit Ratio h | $\text{EAT} = (2 - h) \cdot m + t$ | Simplified form of (3) |
| 6 | TLB Reach | $\text{TLB Reach} = \text{TLB Size} \times \text{Page Size}$ | In Bytes / KB / MB |
| 7 | Speedup Factor | $S = \dfrac{\text{EAT without TLB}}{\text{EAT with TLB}}$ | Dimensionless $> 1$ |
| 8 | Hit Ratio required to achieve target EAT | $h \ge 1 - \dfrac{\text{Target EAT} - (t+m)}{m}$ | Solved from (3) |

**Where:**
- $t$ = TLB lookup/access time
- $m$ = Main memory access time
- $h$ = TLB hit ratio
- $1 - h$ = TLB miss ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions** you must remember:
> - When $h = 1$ (100% hit), $\text{EAT} = t + m$ — minimum possible.
> - When $h = 0$ (100% miss), $\text{EAT} = t + 2m$ — worst case with TLB present.
> - If $t$ is negligible compared to $m$, then $\text{EAT} \approx (2 - h) \cdot m$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CPU Performance Engineering**: TLB design directly influences the **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** of memory access instructions. A low hit ratio causes **TLB thrashing**, a known bottleneck in databases and HPC workloads.
- **HugePages in Databases**: Oracle, PostgreSQL, and MySQL InnoDB use HugePages to increase the **TLB reach** so more working set fits inside the TLB.
- **Virtualization (VMware, KVM, Docker)**: Nested page tables (EPT/NPT) introduce a *second-level* TLB miss penalty, which is mitigated by tagged TLBs in modern Intel/AMD processors.
- **Embedded Systems**: ARM Cortex-M cores use a **MicroTLB** in front of a **Main TLB** — a two-level TLB design — which is a typical viva question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Access Time (EAT) With TLB

Let us derive the canonical EAT formula used in every KTU numerical.

**Given:**
- $t$ = TLB access time (in nanoseconds)
- $m$ = Main memory access time (in nanoseconds)
- $h$ = TLB hit ratio (probability of a hit)
- $1 - h$ = TLB miss ratio (probability of a miss)

**Step 1 — Identify the two cases.**
For every memory reference issued by the CPU, only two mutually exclusive events can happen:
- **Event A (Probability = $h$):** TLB Hit. Cost = one TLB access ($t$) + one memory access for the data ($m$).
- **Event B (Probability = $1 - h$):** TLB Miss. Cost = one TLB access ($t$) + one memory access to read the page table ($m$) + one memory access to read the actual data ($m$).

**Step 2 — Express the cost of each event in terms of $t$ and $m$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_\text{Hit} &= t + m \\
\text{Cost}_\text{Miss} &= t + m + m = t + 2m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the expected (effective) access time as a weighted average.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= h \cdot \text{Cost}_\text{Hit} + (1 - h) \cdot \text{Cost}_\text{Miss} \\
\text{EAT} &= h \cdot (t + m) + (1 - h) \cdot (t + 2m)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the terms algebraically.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= h \cdot t + h \cdot m + (1 - h) \cdot t + (1 - h) \cdot 2m \\
\text{EAT} &= h \cdot t + h \cdot m + t - h \cdot t + 2m - 2h \cdot m \\
\text{EAT} &= t + h \cdot m + 2m - 2h \cdot m \\
\text{EAT} &= t + m(2 - h)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final simplified form (most commonly used in KTU answers).**

$$
\boxed{\text{EAT} = t + m(2 - h)}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: TLB Reach

**TLB Reach** is the size of virtual memory that can be accessed without causing a TLB miss.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TLB Reach} &= (\text{Number of TLB Entries}) \times (\text{Page Size}) \\
\text{TLB Reach} &= E \times P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:**
If TLB has 64 entries and page size is 4 KB, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TLB Reach} &= 64 \times 4 \text{ KB} \\
&= 256 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means the program must keep its working set within **256 KB** to fully benefit from the TLB.

## 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Numerical (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A system uses paging with TLB. TLB access time is **20 ns**, main memory access time is **100 ns**, and the TLB hit ratio is **80%**. Calculate:
1. The Effective Access Time (EAT).
2. The percentage slowdown compared to a system with no TLB.
3. The speedup factor.

**Given:** $t = 20$ ns, $m = 100$ ns, $h = 0.80$, $1 - h = 0.20$.

**Solution (1) — EAT with TLB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= h \cdot (t + m) + (1 - h) \cdot (t + 2m) \\
&= 0.80 \times (20 + 100) + 0.20 \times (20 + 200) \\
&= 0.80 \times 120 + 0.20 \times 220 \\
&= 96 + 44 \\
&= 140 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (2) — EAT without TLB (and percentage slowdown):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT}_{\text{no TLB}} &= 2m = 2 \times 100 = 200 \text{ ns} \\
\text{Slowdown} &= \dfrac{140 - 100}{100} \times 100\% = 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — to compare against the *ideal* access time ($m = 100$ ns, since data must be fetched even in the best case):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ideal Access Time} &= m = 100 \text{ ns} \\
\text{Slowdown vs Ideal} &= \dfrac{140 - 100}{100} \times 100\% = 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (3) — Speedup factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{\text{EAT without TLB}}{\text{EAT with TLB}} = \dfrac{200}{140} \approx 1.428
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** EAT = **140 ns**, slowdown = **40%** over ideal, speedup = **1.428×** over no-TLB system.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Access Scenarios

| Scenario | Cost per Reference | Comment |
|----------|-------------------|---------|
| No Paging, No TLB (ideal) | $m$ | Pure physical memory access |
| Paging, No TLB | $2m$ | One for page table, one for data |
| Paging + TLB, Hit | $t + m$ | Fastest realistic path |
| Paging + TLB, Miss | $t + 2m$ | Page table in memory is consulted |
| Paging + TLB, Miss + Page Fault | $t + 2m + \text{Disk Service Time}$ | Worst case; OS brings page from disk |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TLB Lookup Process — Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete decision logic of a TLB-based address translation, suitable for a KTU 14-mark answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU Issues Virtual Address"] --> B["Extract Virtual Page Number VPN and Offset"]
    B --> C["Present VPN to TLB Associative Lookup"]
    C --> D{"Is VPN Found in TLB"}
    D -- "Yes TLB Hit" --> E["Read PFN from TLB"]
    E --> F["Form Physical Address PFN plus Offset"]
    F --> G["Access Main Memory for Data Instruction"]
    G --> H["Return Data to CPU"]
    D -- "No TLB Miss" --> I["Consult Page Table in Main Memory"]
    I --> J{"Is Page Valid in Memory"}
    J -- "Yes Present" --> K["Load PTE into TLB Replace Victim Entry LRU"]
    K --> E
    J -- "No Page Fault" --> L["OS Invokes Page Fault Handler"]
    L --> M["Bring Page from Secondary Storage Disk"]
    M --> N["Update Page Table PTE Valid Bit"]
    N --> K
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of MMU + TLB + Main Memory

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CPU Core"] --> B["MMU Unit"]
    B --> C["TLB Cache Associative"]
    C --> D{"Hit or Miss Decision"}
    D -- "Hit" --> E["Physical Address Bus"]
    D -- "Miss" --> F["Page Table in Main Memory"]
    F --> E
    E --> G["Main Memory RAM"]
    G --> A
    H["OS Kernel"] -. "Page Fault Service" .-> F
```

**Explanation of Architecture:**
1. The **CPU Core** sends a virtual address to the **MMU**.
2. The **MMU** consults the **TLB** (a small on-chip associative cache).
3. On a hit, the physical address is constructed **without** touching the page table.
4. On a miss, the MMU performs a **page table walk** in main memory to fetch the required PTE.
5. If the page is not resident, a **page fault** is raised, the **OS kernel** is invoked, and the page is brought in from disk.
6. The newly fetched PTE is then installed into the TLB (replacing a victim entry using LRU/FIFO/Random policy).

## 4.3 TLB Entry Structure (Bit-Level Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Virtual Page Number VPN - Tag"] --> B["TLB Entry Line"]
    C["Physical Frame Number PFN"] --> B
    D["Valid Bit V"] --> B
    E["Protection Bits R W X"] --> B
    F["Dirty Bit Modified"] --> B
    G["Reference Bit Accessed"] --> B
    B --> H["Associative Search via VPN Tag"]
```

**Interpretation:** Each TLB line holds the VPN (as tag), the corresponding PFN (as data), and auxiliary control bits. During lookup, the VPN from the address is compared *in parallel* against all stored VPN tags using associative hardware.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB). What is a TLB hit and a TLB miss?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **TLB Definition (1 mark):** The Translation Lookaside Buffer is a small, high-speed **hardware associative cache** maintained by the Memory Management Unit (MMU). It stores a limited number of recently used **Page Table Entries (PTEs)**, each containing a virtual page number mapped to its corresponding physical frame number.
- **TLB Hit (1 mark):** A TLB hit occurs when the page number of the virtual address issued by the CPU is found inside the TLB. The physical frame number is retrieved directly without consulting the page table in main memory.
- **TLB Miss (1 mark):** A TLB miss occurs when the required page table entry is not present in the TLB. The system must then access the page table in main memory, retrieve the entry, and update the TLB by replacing a victim entry (typically using LRU policy).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Explain the role of TLB in paging. How does it help improve the Effective Access Time?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role of TLB (2 marks):** The TLB acts as a hardware cache for the page table. Without a TLB, every memory reference requires two main memory accesses (one for the page table entry, one for the actual data). The TLB reduces this to a single main memory access in the case of a hit, by storing frequently used PTEs in a small, fast on-chip memory.
- **Improvement in EAT (1 mark):** By maintaining a high hit ratio (e.g., 80%–99%), the effective access time can be reduced from $2m$ (without TLB) to approximately $(2 - h) \cdot m + t$, which is close to the ideal single memory access time $m$. The TLB exploits the **principle of locality** of reference to achieve this performance gain.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Part B Module 3 questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Below are two completely independent questions. In the actual exam, you would answer **ONE** of them.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) in paging. Distinguish between a TLB hit and a TLB miss with appropriate examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a paging system with TLB. The TLB access time is 10 ns and the main memory access time is 100 ns. If the TLB hit ratio is 90%, calculate:
1. The Effective Access Time (EAT).
2. The percentage reduction in access time compared to a system with no TLB.
3. The minimum hit ratio required to keep the EAT below 120 ns.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define Effective Access Time. Derive the expression for EAT in a paged system with TLB, clearly stating the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A computer system uses paging with TLB. The TLB has 32 entries, and the page size is 4 KB. The TLB lookup time is 15 ns and main memory access time is 120 ns. If the TLB hit ratio is 85%:
1. Calculate the **TLB Reach**.
2. Calculate the **Effective Access Time**.
3. Suggest one practical method to **increase the TLB reach** without increasing the TLB size.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — TLB Concept (7 marks)

**Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the TLB lookup flow — refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. The flow must show:

1. CPU issuing virtual address
2. MMU splitting the address into VPN + offset
3. TLB associative search
4. Hit path: direct PFN retrieval
5. Miss path: page table walk + TLB update

**Explanation (3 marks):**

- **TLB Definition:** The Translation Lookaside Buffer is a small, high-speed **associative cache** built into the MMU. It stores a limited set of recently used page table entries to speed up virtual-to-physical address translation.
- **TLB Hit:** Suppose the CPU generates the virtual address `0x0040_1234` and the corresponding PTE for VPN 4 is already in the TLB. The MMU immediately retrieves the PFN, concatenates it with the offset `0x1234`, and accesses physical memory. Only one main memory access is needed — the data is delivered quickly.
- **TLB Miss:** Now suppose the program accesses a new page (e.g., VPN 1024) whose PTE is not in the TLB. The MMU must look up the page table in main memory, fetch the PTE, then load it into the TLB (replacing the least recently used entry, per LRU policy). This requires two main memory accesses (page table + data) and an additional TLB update operation.
- **Significance (1 mark):** A TLB hit reduces access time dramatically. A TLB miss, while slower than a hit, is still necessary for correctness and triggers the page table walk automatically in hardware.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $t = 10$ ns, $m = 100$ ns, $h = 0.90$, $1 - h = 0.10$.

**Step 1 — Effective Access Time (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= h \cdot (t + m) + (1 - h) \cdot (t + 2m) \\
&= 0.90 \times (10 + 100) + 0.10 \times (10 + 200) \\
&= 0.90 \times 110 + 0.10 \times 210 \\
&= 99 + 21 \\
&= 120 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Percentage reduction (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT without TLB} &= 2m = 200 \text{ ns} \\
\text{Reduction} &= \dfrac{200 - 120}{200} \times 100\% = \dfrac{80}{200} \times 100\% = 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum hit ratio for EAT < 120 ns (2 marks):**

We need $\text{EAT} < 120$ ns, i.e., $t + m(2 - h) < 120$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
10 + 100(2 - h) &< 120 \\
10 + 200 - 100h &< 120 \\
210 - 100h &< 120 \\
100h &> 90 \\
h &> 0.90
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **minimum hit ratio is 90%** (just above 0.90) to keep EAT below 120 ns.

**Final Answers:** EAT = **120 ns**, Reduction = **40%**, $h_{\min} > 0.90$.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — EAT Derivation (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Effective Access Time is the **average (expected) time** taken by the CPU to complete a single memory reference, taking into account the probabilities of TLB hits, TLB misses, and page faults.

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The page table is resident in main memory (single-level).
2. No page faults occur for the accesses under consideration.
3. The TLB is consulted first for every memory reference.
4. On a TLB miss, exactly one additional memory access is needed to read the page table.

**Derivation (3 marks):** Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. Final result:

$$
\boxed{\text{EAT} = t + m(2 - h) = h(t + m) + (1 - h)(t + 2m)}
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** TLB entries $E = 32$, page size $P = 4$ KB, $t = 15$ ns, $m = 120$ ns, $h = 0.85$.

**Step 1 — TLB Reach (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TLB Reach} &= E \times P = 32 \times 4 \text{ KB} = 128 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective Access Time (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= h \cdot (t + m) + (1 - h) \cdot (t + 2m) \\
&= 0.85 \times (15 + 120) + 0.15 \times (15 + 240) \\
&= 0.85 \times 135 + 0.15 \times 255 \\
&= 114.75 + 38.25 \\
&= 153 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Method to increase TLB Reach (2 marks):** **Use larger page sizes (Huge Pages).** For example, switching from 4 KB pages to 2 MB or 1 GB pages (as supported in x86-64 with PAE or in modern OS like Linux with `hugepages=` kernel parameter) increases the page size $P$, and since TLB Reach = $E \times P$, the reach grows proportionally without needing more TLB entries. This is widely used in database servers (Oracle SGA, MySQL InnoDB buffer pool) and virtualization.

**Final Answers:** TLB Reach = **128 KB**, EAT = **153 ns**, Use **Huge Pages** to increase reach.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the TLB access time (t) in the EAT formula.** Many students incorrectly write $\text{EAT} = h \cdot m + (1 - h) \cdot 2m$. The correct formula **must** include $t$: $\text{EAT} = h(t + m) + (1 - h)(t + 2m)$. Expect to lose **2 marks** if you miss this.
> 2. **Confusing "EAT without TLB" with $m$ instead of $2m$.** Without TLB, every reference needs two memory accesses (page table + data). Use $\text{EAT}_{\text{no TLB}} = 2m$, not $m$.
> 3. **Unit mismatches.** If $m$ is given in µs and $t$ in ns, convert both to the same unit before plugging into the formula.
> 4. **Ignoring the "page fault" case.** If a question asks for EAT including page faults, you must add the disk service time weighted by the page fault rate: $\text{EAT} = h(t+m) + (1-h)(t+2m) + p \cdot (\text{disk service time})$.
> 5. **Stating the TLB reach formula incorrectly.** It is $\text{Entries} \times \text{Page Size}$, not $\text{Entries} \times \text{Memory Size}$.
> 6. **Writing "TLB is a software cache."** This is factually wrong. TLB is **hardware** — it is implemented in the MMU using associative SRAM. Marks will be deducted.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TLB** = Hardware associative cache inside the MMU storing recent PTEs.
- **TLB Hit**: PTE found in TLB → only **one** memory access needed (cost: $t + m$).
- **TLB Miss**: PTE not in TLB → page table in main memory is consulted (cost: $t + 2m$).
- **Hit Ratio ($h$)** is the single most important parameter in EAT calculations.
- **Canonical EAT formula:** $\text{EAT} = h(t + m) + (1 - h)(t + 2m) = t + m(2 - h)$.
- **EAT without TLB** is always $2m$ (one for page table, one for data).
- **EAT with TLB always greater than $t + m$ and less than $t + 2m$.**
- **TLB Reach** = (Number of TLB Entries) × (Page Size) — represents the working set that fits in TLB.
- **TLB exploits locality of reference** to achieve high hit ratios (typically 80%–99%).
- **Huge Pages** (2 MB / 1 GB) are used to increase TLB reach without increasing TLB size.
- **TLB replacement policies:** LRU (most common), FIFO, Random.
- **TLB is flushed** on every context switch (unless tagged TLB is supported by hardware).
- **Effective Access Time** is an *expected/average* value, computed using probability-weighted cost.
- **Two-level TLB** (MicroTLB + Main TLB) is used in modern ARM and Intel processors.
- **Page fault cost** is orders of magnitude higher than TLB miss cost (involves disk I/O).
- For KTU answers, always **draw the TLB lookup flow diagram** before writing the explanation — it scores easy 3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling TLB Misses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Handling TLB Misses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)** is a small, fully-associative, high-speed **hardware cache** that stores recently used page-table entries (PTEs). It acts as a hardware-accelerated shortcut for virtual-to-physical address translation, dramatically reducing the overhead of two-level paging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TLB Miss** is the event that occurs when the virtual page number (VPN) being looked up by the Memory Management Unit (MMU) is **not found** in the TLB. This is analogous to a "cache miss" in the CPU cache hierarchy, but at the level of address translation.

A TLB miss is fundamentally a **fault** that the hardware/software system must resolve. The handling of this miss is one of the most performance-critical operations in modern operating systems because address translation occurs on **every single memory access**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The Library Card Catalog Analogy** 📚
> 
> Imagine a massive university library with 10 million books. To find a book, you must first look up its location in a giant card catalog. The catalog itself is so large it is stored on multiple floors.
> 
> - **Page Table (in main memory)** = The entire multi-floor card catalog. Slow to search.
> - **TLB (in hardware cache)** = A small wooden tray on the librarian's desk holding the **last 64 catalog cards** she recently used. Lightning fast to check.
> - **TLB Hit** = The book you want is on the librarian's tray → done in 1 second.
> - **TLB Miss** = The card is not on the tray → she must walk 3 floors to the main catalog, find the card, write down the location, and bring it back to the tray (replacing an old one if full).
> 
> Every memory access in a computer is like a student asking the librarian for a book — and the librarian must consult her tray first, every single time.

### 1.3 Why TLB Miss Handling Matters (KTU Context)

> [!WARNING]
> **Performance Reality Check**: Without a TLB, a memory access on a 2-level paged system would require **3 memory references** (1 for outer PT, 1 for inner PT, 1 for actual data). With a perfect TLB, only **1 memory reference** is needed. The TLB miss penalty directly determines system throughput.

**Key Metrics** (memorize these symbols):
- **$T$** = Memory access time (typically **100 ns**)
- **$\varepsilon$** or **$t$** = TLB access/search time (typically **20 ns**)
- **$h$** = TLB hit ratio (typically **80% – 99%**)
- **$1-h$** = TLB miss ratio
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the TLB Hardware

The TLB is structured as a fully-associative cache. Each entry (a TLB line) typically contains:

| TLB Entry Field | Size (typical) | Purpose |
|-----------------|----------------|---------|
| **Tag (VPN)** | 20 bits | Virtual Page Number being cached |
| **PFN (Frame No.)** | 20 bits | Corresponding Physical Frame Number |
| **Valid Bit** | 1 bit | Marks if the entry is active |
| **Protection Bits** (R/W/X) | 3 bits | Access permissions from PTE |
| **Dirty Bit** | 1 bit | Modified status from PTE |
| **ASID / PID** | 8 bits | Process/Address-Space ID to avoid flushing on context switch |

> [!NOTE]
> The TLB is searched **in parallel** (associatively) using the VPN as a key. The hardware compares the incoming VPN against all stored tags simultaneously. This is why a TLB access takes only $\varepsilon$ nanoseconds, regardless of how many entries it holds.

### 2.2 The Two Strategies for Handling a TLB Miss

When the TLB reports a miss, the system must load the correct PTE into the TLB. There are two architectural philosophies:

#### A. Hardware-Managed TLB (CISC Approach — e.g., x86, MIPS)

1. Hardware checks the TLB → Miss detected.
2. Hardware **automatically walks the page table** (in main memory) to find the PTE.
3. Hardware loads the PTE into the TLB, possibly evicting an old entry (using LRU, random, or round-robin replacement).
4. The memory access is **restarted** from the beginning.

> **Pros**: Fast, no OS involvement, no context switches. 
> **Cons**: Inflexible — OS cannot customize the replacement policy. Hardware complexity is high.

#### B. Software-Managed TLB (RISC Approach — e.g., older SPARC, Alpha)

1. Hardware checks the TLB → Miss detected.
2. Hardware generates a **TLB exception/trap** to the OS kernel.
3. The OS's **TLB miss handler** runs in kernel mode.
4. The handler explicitly walks the page table in software, finds the PTE.
5. The handler executes a privileged "write TLB" instruction to insert the PTE.
6. The handler returns from exception; the faulting instruction is **re-executed**.

> **Pros**: Flexibility — OS can use sophisticated replacement, choose page size, etc. 
> **Cons**: Expensive trap overhead (saving registers, mode switch). Each miss is potentially 10x–50x slower.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical.**

| Scenario | Effective Access Time (EAT) Formula | When to Use |
|----------|-------------------------------------|-------------|
| **No TLB (single-level paging)** | $EAT = 2 \times T$ | When TLB is absent; one access for PT, one for data |
| **No TLB (two-level paging)** | $EAT = 3 \times T$ | When TLB is absent; outer PT, inner PT, data |
| **TLB + Single-level PT** | $EAT = h \cdot (T + \varepsilon) + (1-h) \cdot (2T + \varepsilon)$ | Standard case, miss costs 2 memory accesses |
| **TLB + Two-level PT** | $EAT = h \cdot (T + \varepsilon) + (1-h) \cdot (3T + \varepsilon)$ | When page table is 2-level, miss costs 3 accesses |
| **TLB Hit Ratio from EAT** | $h = \dfrac{EAT - T_{miss}}{T_{hit} - T_{miss}}$ | When EAT is given, solve for $h$ |

**Standard Assumption** for KTU problems: $\varepsilon$ is often ignored (i.e., $\varepsilon \approx 0$) unless explicitly given. Always read the question carefully.

### 2.4 Types of TLB Misses (Real-World Utility)

Engineers classify TLB misses (similar to CPU cache misses) to optimize system performance:

| Miss Type | Cause | Mitigation |
|-----------|-------|------------|
| **Compulsory / Cold Miss** | First access to a page; TLB was empty. | Prefetching, larger TLB. |
| **Capacity Miss** | Working set > TLB size. | Increase TLB associativity/size; use huge pages. |
| **Conflict Miss** | In set-associative TLBs, two VPNs hash to same set. | Higher associativity, better hashing. |

**Real-world engineering application**: Database engines (PostgreSQL, Oracle) use **huge pages (2 MB)** to reduce TLB misses when scanning large tables. Cloud providers (AWS, Azure) expose "Transparent Huge Pages" for the same reason.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Access Time (EAT) for TLB + Single-Level Page Table

We will derive the formula from first principles, treating the memory access process as a probabilistic event.

**Step 1: Decompose the access process.**

A typical memory access under virtual memory requires:
- $T_{TLB}$ = Time to look up the TLB ($\varepsilon$, often ignored).
- $T_{mem}$ = Time for one main-memory access ($T$).

When a TLB **hit** occurs, only one main-memory access (for the actual data) is needed, in addition to the TLB lookup. Total time = $T_{TLB} + T$.

When a TLB **miss** occurs, we must:
1. Access main memory to read the PTE from the page table.
2. Access main memory again to read the actual data.
3. Total time = $T_{TLB} + 2T$.

**Step 2: Compute the weighted average (Expected Value).**

Let $h$ be the probability of a TLB hit. Then $(1-h)$ is the probability of a miss. The expected access time is:

$$EAT = P(\text{hit}) \cdot T_{hit} + P(\text{miss}) \cdot T_{miss}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values.**

$$EAT = h \cdot (T_{TLB} + T) + (1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2T)$$

**Step 4: Expand and simplify algebraically.**

$$EAT = h \cdot T_{TLB} + h \cdot T + T_{TLB} + 2T - h \cdot T_{TLB} - 2h \cdot T$$

The $h \cdot T_{TLB}$ terms cancel:

$$EAT = T_{TLB} + h \cdot T + 2T - 2h \cdot T$$

Group the $T$ terms:

$$EAT = T_{TLB} + (2T - 2hT) + hT$$

$$EAT = T_{TLB} + 2T(1 - h) + hT$$

**Step 5: Final compact form (most commonly used in KTU).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\boxed{EAT = T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot T}$$
> 
> If $T_{TLB} = \varepsilon$ is ignored, the formula reduces to $EAT = (2 - h) \cdot T$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Standard Format)

**Question**: A system uses paging with a TLB. The TLB access time is **20 ns**, and main memory access time is **100 ns**. The TLB hit ratio is **90%**. The page table is stored in main memory. Calculate the Effective Access Time (EAT).

**Solution**:

**Step 1: Identify the given values.**
- $T_{TLB} = 20$ ns
- $T = 100$ ns
- $h = 0.90$ (so $1 - h = 0.10$)

**Step 2: Substitute into the EAT formula.**

$$EAT = h \cdot (T_{TLB} + T) + (1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2T)$$

**Step 3: Compute the hit-path time.**

$$T_{hit} = 20 + 100 = 120 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4: Compute the miss-path time.**

$$T_{miss} = 20 + 2(100) = 220 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 5: Compute the weighted average.**

$$EAT = (0.90)(120) + (0.10)(220)$$

$$EAT = 108 + 22$$

**Step 6: Final answer.**

$$\boxed{EAT = 130 \text{ ns}}$$

**Interpretation**: Without a TLB, every access would take $2T = 200$ ns. The TLB reduces this to 130 ns — a **35% speedup**.

---

### 3.3 Reverse Problem: Find the Hit Ratio

**Question**: In a system with $T = 100$ ns and $T_{TLB} = 20$ ns, the EAT is observed to be **140 ns**. What is the TLB hit ratio?

**Solution**:

**Step 1: Write the EAT equation.**

$$EAT = T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot T$$

**Step 2: Substitute the knowns.**

$$140 = 20 + (2 - h) \cdot 100$$

**Step 3: Isolate the parenthetical term.**

$$140 - 20 = (2 - h) \cdot 100$$

$$120 = 100(2 - h)$$

**Step 4: Divide by 100.**

$$2 - h = 1.2$$

**Step 5: Solve for $h$.**

$$h = 2 - 1.2 = 0.8$$

**Step 6: Final answer.**

$$\boxed{h = 80\%}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Hardware-Managed TLB Miss Handling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU issues virtual address VA] --> B{MMU checks TLB}
    B -->|Hit| C[Extract PFN from TLB]
    C --> D[Form Physical Address PA]
    D --> E[Access Main Memory once]
    E --> F[Return data to CPU]
    B -->|Miss| G[Hardware walks Page Table in main memory]
    G --> H[Locate correct PTE]
    H --> I[Load PTE into TLB evicting old entry]
    I --> J[Restart the memory access]
    J --> B
    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Software-Managed TLB Miss Handling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU issues virtual address VA] --> B{MMU checks TLB}
    B -->|Hit| C[Form PA and access data]
    C --> D[Return to user process]
    B -->|Miss| E[Hardware raises TLB miss exception]
    E --> F[Save process state trap to kernel]
    F --> G[OS TLB miss handler runs]
    G --> H[Walk page table in software]
    H --> I{Page in memory?}
    I -->|No| J[Handle page fault load from disk]
    J --> H
    I -->|Yes| K[Execute privileged write TLB instruction]
    K --> L[Return from exception restore state]
    L --> M[Re-execute faulting instruction]
    M --> B
    style E fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the TLB Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU["CPU Core"]
        VA[Virtual Address Generator]
    end
    subgraph MMU["Memory Management Unit MMU"]
        TLB[TLB Cache fully associative]
        PTW[Page Table Walker hardware]
        PAF[Physical Address Formatter]
    end
    subgraph RAM["Main Memory"]
        PT[Page Table multi-level]
        PD[Process Data actual pages]
    end
    VA -->|VPN lookup| TLB
    TLB -->|Hit PFN| PAF
    TLB -->|Miss signal| PTW
    PTW -->|Walk request| PT
    PT -->|PTE returned| PTW
    PTW -->|Insert PTE| TLB
    PAF -->|Physical address| PD
    PD -->|Data returned| CPU
    style TLB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style PTW fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*What is a TLB? What is a TLB miss?*

**Model Answer**:
The **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** is a small, fast, fully-associative hardware cache that stores a subset of Page Table Entries (PTEs) to accelerate virtual-to-physical address translation. **(1 mark)**

A **TLB miss** occurs when the MMU searches the TLB for a Virtual Page Number (VPN) and does not find a matching entry. The system must then consult the page table in main memory to retrieve the correct mapping. **(2 marks)**

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series-2]**
*Mention any two differences between hardware-managed and software-managed TLB.*

**Model Answer**:

| Aspect | Hardware-Managed TLB | Software-Managed TLB |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Miss Handler** | Hardware automatically walks page table | OS kernel handles the miss via a trap |
| **Flexibility** | Low; fixed replacement policy in hardware | High; OS can choose any policy |
| **Speed of Miss** | Faster (no mode switch) | Slower (involves kernel trap) |
| **Examples** | x86, MIPS | SPARC, Alpha |

*(Any two well-explained points = 3 marks)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks)

#### Question A — Full 14-Mark Question

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the two different approaches to handling a TLB miss in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a paging system with a TLB access time of **15 ns** and a main memory access time of **80 ns**. The TLB hit ratio is **85%**. If the page table is stored in main memory, calculate the **Effective Access Time (EAT)**. Now, find the new EAT if the hit ratio is improved to **98%**, and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

There are two primary strategies for handling a TLB miss:

**1. Hardware-Managed TLB (CISC approach):**
- On a miss, the hardware **automatically** consults the page table in main memory. **(1 mark)**
- It walks the multi-level page table, retrieves the PTE, and **loads it into the TLB**, possibly evicting a victim entry. **(1 mark)**
- The original memory access is then **restarted** transparently. **(1 mark)**
- The OS is **never invoked** during the miss. **(1 mark)**
- **Advantages**: Very fast; no software overhead. **Disadvantages**: Inflexible; replacement policy is hardwired. **(1 mark)**
- *Example architectures*: Intel x86, MIPS. **(1 mark)**
- *Diagram explanation*: Flow proceeds MMU → TLB → Miss → Page Table Walker → TLB Update → Retry. **(1 mark)**

**2. Software-Managed TLB (RISC approach):**
- On a miss, the hardware **generates a TLB exception/trap** to the OS. **(1 mark)**
- The control transfers to the OS's **TLB miss handler** running in kernel mode. **(1 mark)**
- The handler **walks the page table in software**, finds the PTE, and executes a **privileged instruction** to insert it into the TLB. **(1 mark)**
- The handler **returns from exception**, and the faulting instruction is **re-executed**. **(1 mark)**
- **Advantages**: Flexibility in replacement, page sizes, and policy. **Disadvantages**: Expensive trap overhead. **(1 mark)**
- *Example architectures*: SPARC, Alpha. **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given**: $T_{TLB} = 15$ ns, $T = 80$ ns.

**Case 1: Hit ratio = 85%**

$$EAT_1 = h \cdot (T_{TLB} + T) + (1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2T)$$

$$EAT_1 = 0.85 \cdot (15 + 80) + 0.15 \cdot (15 + 160)$$

$$EAT_1 = 0.85 \cdot 95 + 0.15 \cdot 175$$

$$EAT_1 = 80.75 + 26.25$$

$$EAT_1 = 107 \text{ ns} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**Case 2: Hit ratio = 98%**

$$EAT_2 = 0.98 \cdot 95 + 0.02 \cdot 175$$

$$EAT_2 = 93.1 + 3.5$$

$$EAT_2 = 96.6 \text{ ns} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**Comment**: Improving the hit ratio from 85% to 98% reduces EAT from **107 ns → 96.6 ns**, a savings of **10.4 ns (≈ 9.7%)**. This shows that the TLB hit ratio has a near-linear effect on performance, especially for high hit ratios where every 1% improvement yields measurable gains. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Question

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the structure of a TLB entry. What is meant by a TLB reach? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system uses two-level paging with a TLB. Memory access time is **100 ns**, TLB access time is **20 ns**, and TLB hit ratio is **90%**. Compute the EAT. Also, determine the minimum hit ratio required to keep EAT below **150 ns**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

A TLB entry contains the following fields:

| Field | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **Tag (VPN)** | The Virtual Page Number that identifies the page |
| **PFN** | The corresponding Physical Frame Number |
| **Valid Bit** | Indicates if the entry is currently valid |
| **Protection Bits** | Read/Write/Execute permissions |
| **Dirty Bit** | Whether the page has been modified |
| **ASID** | Address-Space ID for process isolation |

*(4 marks for the table/diagram)*

> [!NOTE]
> **TLB Reach** is the amount of virtual memory that can be accessed without causing a TLB miss. It is calculated as:
> 
> $$TLB\,Reach = TLB\,Entries \times Page\,Size$$
> 
> For example, a 64-entry TLB with 4 KB pages gives a reach of **256 KB**. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1: Identify parameters.**
- $T = 100$ ns, $T_{TLB} = 20$ ns, $h = 0.90$.
- Two-level paging → miss penalty is $3T$ (outer PT + inner PT + data).

**Step 2: Compute EAT.**

$$EAT = h \cdot (T_{TLB} + T) + (1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 3T)$$

$$EAT = 0.9 \cdot (20 + 100) + 0.1 \cdot (20 + 300)$$

$$EAT = 0.9 \cdot 120 + 0.1 \cdot 320$$

$$EAT = 108 + 32$$

$$EAT = 140 \text{ ns} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**Step 3: Find minimum $h$ for EAT < 150 ns.**

$$150 = h \cdot (120) + (1-h) \cdot (320)$$

$$150 = 120h + 320 - 320h$$

$$150 - 320 = (120 - 320)h$$

$$-170 = -200h$$

$$h = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85 \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**Step 4: Conclusion**: The minimum hit ratio required is **85%**.

**Verification**: At $h = 0.85$, $EAT = 0.85(120) + 0.15(320) = 102 + 48 = 150$ ns ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read This Carefully!**
> 
> 1. **Formula Confusion**: Students often write $EAT = h \cdot T + (1-h) \cdot 2T$ and **forget to add the TLB access time $T_{TLB}$**. Always include it explicitly.
> 2. **Two-Level Paging Miss Penalty**: For two-level paging, the miss cost is $3T$, **not** $2T$. Many students incorrectly use $2T$.
> 3. **Hit Ratio as Decimal vs Percentage**: In the formula, $h$ must be a decimal (0.90), not a percentage (90). Mixing these up gives an answer off by 100x.
> 4. **Unit Mismatch**: TLB time and memory time must be in the **same units** (both in ns). Convert if necessary.
> 5. **Answer Without Units**: KTU deducts marks for final numerical answers without units. Always write "ns" or "μs".

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — TLB Miss Handling**

- ⭐ TLB = hardware cache for page table entries; searched in parallel.
- ⭐ TLB miss = VPN not in TLB; must consult main-memory page table.
- ⭐ Two handling strategies: **Hardware-managed** (CISC, x86, MIPS) and **Software-managed** (RISC, SPARC, Alpha).
- ⭐ **Master Formula**: $EAT = h \cdot (T_{TLB} + T) + (1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2T)$ — for single-level PT.
- ⭐ For **two-level PT**: replace $2T$ with $3T$ in the miss term.
- ⭐ **TLB Reach** = (Number of TLB entries) × (Page size). Determines working set coverage.
- ⭐ A TLB miss in a software-managed scheme causes a **kernel trap**, which is far more expensive than a hardware miss.
- ⭐ Miss types: **compulsory** (first access), **capacity** (TLB too small), **conflict** (set-associative collision).
- ⭐ Performance gain is roughly **proportional to hit ratio**; the last few percent of hit ratio yield diminishing returns.
- ⭐ Real-world optimization: **Huge pages** (2 MB, 1 GB) reduce TLB pressure significantly.
- ⭐ The TLB does **not** store entire page tables; it stores a *subset* (recently used PTEs).
- ⭐ The TLB must be **flushed on context switch** (or use ASID tags) to prevent process A from accessing process B's memory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TLB Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TLB Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** is a small, high-speed **hardware associative cache (Content Addressable Memory — CAM)** built directly into the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** of a processor. It stores a finite number of **recently used virtual-to-physical address translations**, allowing the CPU to bypass the slower main-memory-resident page table for the majority of memory accesses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** TLB is positioned as a hardware assist to the **paging mechanism** in virtual memory. Every TLB operation is *transparent* to the application programmer but *critical* to system performance.

A TLB entry is a tagged record containing:

$$
\boxed{
\text{TLB\_Entry} \;=\; \{\, \text{Tag (VPN)},\; \text{Frame (PFN)},\; \text{Control Bits} \,\}
}
$$

where **VPN** = Virtual Page Number, **PFN** = Physical (Page) Frame Number.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Receptionist's Memory"

Imagine a hotel receptionist. A guest (CPU) repeatedly asks for Room 207 (virtual address). Instead of running to the master registry (page table) every time, the receptionist keeps a **small sticky note** that says "Room 207 → Tower B, Floor 3" (TLB entry). The next time the same guest asks, the answer is **instant (TLB hit)**. Only when a new, never-seen-before room number is requested does the receptionist consult the bulky registry (TLB miss → page table walk).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** TLB does *not* store page data — it stores **mappings (translations)**. Data is still fetched from main memory (or cache) using the physical address obtained from the TLB.

### 1.3 Standard Hardware Metrics

- **TLB Size:** Typically **32 – 4096 entries** in modern CPUs.
- **Lookup Latency:** **1 – 4 ns** (parallel associative search).
- **Hit Ratio ($h$):** **> 95 %** in well-tuned workloads.
- **Page Size Granularity:** **4 KB – 64 KB** per entry.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 TLB Entry Structure — Field-by-Field

A modern TLB entry is composed of the following logical fields:

| Field | Typical Width (bits) | Purpose |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Tag (VPN)** | 20 – 52 | The Virtual Page Number used for parallel CAM comparison. |
| **Frame (PFN)** | 20 – 40 | The Physical Frame Number to be concatenated with the page offset. |
| **Valid (V)** | 1 | Indicates whether the entry currently holds a valid translation. |
| **Dirty (D)** | 1 | Set by hardware on the first write; informs the OS during page replacement. |
| **Reference (R)** | 1 | Set on any access; aids the page-replacement algorithm (e.g., LRU approximation). |
| **Protection Bits** | 2 – 3 | Read / Write / Execute permissions enforced by the MMU. |
| **ASID / PID** | 6 – 16 | Address Space Identifier; prevents flush on every context switch. |
| **Global (G)** | 1 | Marks entries that are shared across all address spaces (e.g., kernel). |
| **Cacheable (C)** | 1 | Whether the page may reside in L1/L2 data caches. |

> [!NOTE]
> **ASID** is the reason modern TLBs do *not* have to be flushed entirely on every context switch — only entries with a *non-matching* ASID are evicted, dramatically reducing TLB thrashing.

### 2.2 TLB Operational Steps (Lookup Procedure)

1. The **MMU** receives a **Virtual Address** from the CPU.
2. The address is split into **VPN (upper bits)** and **Page Offset (lower bits)**.
3. The **VPN is broadcast in parallel** to the tag comparators of every TLB entry.
4. **If a match is found** → **TLB Hit** → The associated **PFN is latched** and combined with the offset to form the Physical Address.
5. **If no match is found** → **TLB Miss** → A **page-table walk** in main memory is initiated by the hardware.
6. On a miss, the **MMU (or OS)** updates the TLB by replacing an existing entry, governed by a replacement policy (LRU, pseudo-LRU, or random).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Effective Access Time (EAT) for an $n$-level page table with TLB:**

$$
\boxed{
EAT \;=\; t_{TLB} \;+\; t_M \,\bigl(\, n + 1 \;-\; n \cdot h \,\bigr)
}
$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Description | Typical Value |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| TLB Lookup Time | $t_{TLB}$ | Associative search latency | **1 – 4 ns** |
| Main Memory Access Time | $t_M$ | DRAM access latency | **60 – 200 ns** |
| TLB Hit Ratio | $h$ | Fraction of TLB accesses that hit | **0.80 – 0.99** |
| Effective Access Time | EAT | Average per-memory-reference time | **ns** |
| Page-Table Levels | $n$ | Number of page-table indirections | **1, 2, 3, or 4** |
| Speedup Factor | $S$ | $\text{EAT}_{\text{no-TLB}} \div \text{EAT}_{\text{TLB}}$ | **> 1** |

**Specialised reductions** (memorize these):

- Single-level page table ($n = 1$):  $EAT = t_{TLB} + t_M (2 - h)$
- Two-level page table ($n = 2$):  $EAT = t_{TLB} + t_M (3 - 2h)$
- TLB access negligible ($t_{TLB} \approx 0$):  $EAT \approx t_M (n+1 - n h)$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Isolation & Security:** TLB tags combined with ASID enforce per-process address spaces at hardware speed — foundational to OS security.
- **Performance Engineering:** Cache-TLB interplay directly affects **Amdahl's Law** limits in HPC, database engines, and JVM runtimes.
- **Embedded & Real-Time Systems:** Deterministic TLB behaviour is a hard requirement for **WCET (Worst-Case Execution Time)** analysis in automotive (AUTOSAR) and avionics (DO-178C) software.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Access Time for a Single-Level Page Table

Let:
- $h$ = probability of a **TLB hit** (hit ratio).
- $(1 - h)$ = probability of a **TLB miss** (miss ratio).
- $t_{TLB}$ = time to search the TLB.
- $t_M$ = time to access main memory once.

**Step 1 — Cost on a TLB Hit.**

On a hit, only the TLB and the actual data are touched.

$$
T_{hit} \;=\; t_{TLB} \;+\; t_M
$$

**Step 2 — Cost on a TLB Miss.**

The TLB is probed (and found empty), the **page table in main memory** is consulted, then the actual data is fetched.

$$
T_{miss} \;=\; t_{TLB} \;+\; t_M \;+\; t_M \;=\; t_{TLB} \;+\; 2 t_M
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the weighted average (EAT).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT \;=\; & h \cdot T_{hit} \;+\; (1 - h) \cdot T_{miss} \\[4pt]
\;=\; & h\,(t_{TLB} + t_M) \;+\; (1 - h)\,(t_{TLB} + 2 t_M) \\[4pt]
\;=\; & h\,t_{TLB} + h\,t_M + (1 - h)\,t_{TLB} + 2(1 - h)\,t_M \\[4pt]
\;=\; & t_{TLB}\,\bigl[h + (1 - h)\bigr] \;+\; t_M\,\bigl[h + 2(1 - h)\bigr] \\[4pt]
\;=\; & t_{TLB} \cdot 1 \;+\; t_M \cdot (2 - h)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{
EAT_{\text{single-level}} \;=\; t_{TLB} \;+\; t_M\,(2 - h)
}
$$

> Textual summary: The TLB lookup overhead $t_{TLB}$ is incurred **every** time, while the page-table access overhead $t_M$ is incurred only on misses, scaled by the miss ratio $(1-h)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Generalisation to $n$-Level Page Table

For a hierarchical page table with $n$ levels, a TLB miss requires walking $n$ page-table nodes *plus* one final data access.

**Step 1 — Cost on a Hit** (same as before):

$$
T_{hit} \;=\; t_{TLB} \;+\; t_M
$$

**Step 2 — Cost on a Miss** (one TLB + $n$ page-table nodes + data):

$$
T_{miss} \;=\; t_{TLB} \;+\; (n + 1)\,t_M
$$

**Step 3 — Weighted Average:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT \;=\; & h\,(t_{TLB} + t_M) \;+\; (1 - h)\,\bigl[t_{TLB} + (n + 1)\,t_M\bigr] \\[4pt]
\;=\; & t_{TLB} \;+\; h\,t_M \;+\; (n + 1)(1 - h)\,t_M \\[4pt]
\;=\; & t_{TLB} \;+\; t_M\,\bigl[\,h + (n + 1) - (n + 1)h\,\bigr] \\[4pt]
\;=\; & t_{TLB} \;+\; t_M\,\bigl[\,(n + 1) \;-\; n\,h\,\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final generalised result:**

$$
\boxed{
EAT \;=\; t_{TLB} \;+\; t_M\,(n + 1 - n\,h)
}
$$

**Verification against special cases** (used as cross-check in KTU valuations):

| Page-Table Depth ($n$) | EAT Expression | Plug-in Check |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $n = 0$ (no PT) | $t_{TLB} + t_M (1 - 0) = t_{TLB} + t_M$ | ✓ Matches raw TLB + memory |
| $n = 1$ | $t_{TLB} + t_M (2 - h)$ | ✓ Matches Derivation 1 |
| $n = 2$ | $t_{TLB} + t_M (3 - 2h)$ | ✓ Used in two-level paging problems |
| $n = 4$ | $t_{TLB} + t_M (5 - 4h)$ | ✓ Matches x86-64 with 48-bit virtual address |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem.** A system has $t_{TLB} = 20$ ns, $t_M = 100$ ns, and TLB hit ratio $h = 0.90$. It uses a single-level page table.
**Required:** (i) EAT with TLB. (ii) Speedup versus a no-TLB system.

**Step 1 — EAT with TLB.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT_{TLB} \;=\; & t_{TLB} + t_M (2 - h) \\[4pt]
\;=\; & 20 + 100 (2 - 0.90) \\[4pt]
\;=\; & 20 + 100 (1.10) \\[4pt]
\;=\; & 20 + 110 \;=\; 130 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — EAT without TLB (two memory accesses always required).**

$$
EAT_{\text{no-TLB}} \;=\; 2 \times t_M \;=\; 2 \times 100 \;=\; 200 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Speedup factor.**

$$
S \;=\; \frac{EAT_{\text{no-TLB}}}{EAT_{TLB}} \;=\; \frac{200}{130} \;\approx\; 1.538
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage improvement.**

$$
\text{Improvement} \;=\; \frac{200 - 130}{200} \times 100\% \;=\; 35\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** Even with a 90 % hit rate, the TLB delivers a **35 %** reduction in average memory access latency.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — TLB Organisations

| Organisation | Search Mechanism | Conflict Misses | Hardware Cost | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fully Associative** | Parallel CAM on *all* entries | **None** | Very High (N comparators) | L1 ITLB / DTLB in modern CPUs |
| **Set Associative** | Parallel CAM on one *set* of $k$ entries | Low (intra-set) | Moderate ($k$ comparators per set) | L2 unified TLB |
| **Direct Mapped** | Single-entry index lookup | High (inter-set) | Very Low (1 comparator) | Embedded / older MIPS cores |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — TLB-Based Address Translation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cpu["CPU Generates Virtual Address"]
    split["Split into VPN and Offset"]
    tlbchk{"TLB Lookup VPN Match"}
    hitfn["Read PFN from TLB Entry"]
    misspt["Page Table Walk in Main Memory"]
    loadpfn["Load PFN from Page Table Entry"]
    update["Update TLB Replace LRU Entry"]
    combine["Combine PFN and Offset Form Physical Address"]
    accmem["Access Physical Memory Read or Write Data"]
    retdata["Return Data to CPU"]

    cpu --> split
    split --> tlbchk
    tlbchk -- Hit --> hitfn
    tlbchk -- Miss --> misspt
    misspt --> loadpfn
    loadpfn --> update
    update --> combine
    hitfn --> combine
    combine --> accmem
    accmem --> retdata
```

**Reading the diagram:** The left branch handles a **TLB hit** (fast path: 1 TLB + 1 memory). The right branch handles a **TLB miss** (slow path: 1 TLB + 1 page-table access + 1 memory + 1 TLB update). Both paths converge at the physical memory access.

---

### 4.2 Figure 2 — TLB Entry Block Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    f1["Tag VPN 20 to 52 bits"]
    f2["Frame PFN 20 to 40 bits"]
    f3["Valid V 1 bit"]
    f4["Dirty D 1 bit"]
    f5["Reference R 1 bit"]
    f6["Protection RWX 3 bits"]
    f7["ASID Process ID 8 to 16 bits"]
    f8["Global G 1 bit"]

    f1 --> f2 --> f3 --> f4 --> f5 --> f6 --> f7 --> f8
```

**Reading the diagram:** A TLB entry is a fixed-width hardware register made of three logical zones:
- **Identification zone:** Tag (VPN) + ASID
- **Translation zone:** Frame (PFN)
- **Control zone:** V, D, R, Protection, Global

---

### 4.3 Figure 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the MMU + TLB Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPUSide["CPU Core"]
        gen["Virtual Address Generator"]
    end

    subgraph MMU["Memory Management Unit MMU"]
        split["VPN Offset Splitter"]
        tlb["TLB Associative Cache N Entries"]
        walker["Hardware Page Table Walker"]
        comp["Comparator and Hit Miss Logic"]
        perm["Protection Check Unit"]
    end

    subgraph RAM["Main Memory"]
        pt["Multi Level Page Table"]
        frames["Physical Frames Data"]
    end

    gen --> split
    split --> comp
    comp -- Parallel Match --> tlb
    comp -- No Match --> walker
    walker --> pt
    walker -- PFN Returned --> tlb
    tlb --> perm
    perm --> frames
    frames -- Data to CPU --> gen
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the high-level MMU architecture. The **Comparator block** simultaneously probes the TLB; on a miss, control is transferred to the **Hardware Page Table Walker**, which fetches the missing entry from RAM and refills the TLB. The **Protection Check** enforces read/write/execute rights *before* any physical-memory access is permitted.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB). Why is it considered an associative memory?**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** is a small, high-speed hardware cache inside the MMU that stores **recently used virtual-to-physical page translations**.
It is called an **associative memory** because, unlike conventional RAM that uses an *address* to fetch data, a TLB uses the **content (the VPN/tag) itself** to locate the corresponding PFN in a **single clock cycle** through **parallel comparison across all entries**. This *Content-Addressable* behaviour is what gives the TLB its O(1) lookup speed.

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between a TLB hit and a TLB miss. How does the TLB hit ratio influence the Effective Access Time (EAT)?**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | TLB Hit | TLB Miss |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | The requested VPN is found in the TLB. | The requested VPN is **not** in the TLB. |
| Memory Accesses | 1 (only data) | 2 or more (page table + data) |
| Speed | Fast (≈ 1 cycle) | Slow (multiple cycles / page walk) |

**Influence on EAT:** A higher hit ratio $h$ reduces EAT because the slower page-table walk is invoked less frequently. From $EAT = t_{TLB} + t_M(2 - h)$, as $h \to 1$, $EAT \to t_{TLB} + t_M$ (best case). As $h \to 0$, $EAT \to t_{TLB} + 2 t_M$ (worst case).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the structure of a typical TLB entry. Discuss the significance of each control field in virtual memory management. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A TLB entry consists of the following fields (refer to Figure 2 in SECTION 4):

1. **Tag (VPN)** — Stores the Virtual Page Number. During a lookup, the incoming VPN is **broadcast in parallel** to all tags for comparison.
2. **Frame (PFN)** — Contains the corresponding Physical Frame Number, which is concatenated with the page offset to form the final physical address.
3. **Valid Bit (V)** — Indicates whether the entry currently holds a meaningful translation. **Prevents** use of stale or uninitialised entries after a TLB flush.
4. **Dirty Bit (D)** — Set by hardware on the first write to the page. The OS inspects this bit during page replacement to decide whether the page must be written back to disk.
5. **Reference Bit (R)** — Set on any access. Helps the page-replacement algorithm (e.g., the **clock algorithm** uses it to approximate LRU).
6. **Protection Bits (R/W/X)** — Define access rights. The **Protection Check Unit** aborts any instruction that violates them, generating a **segmentation fault**.
7. **ASID (Address Space Identifier)** — Tags each entry with a process identifier, eliminating the need to flush the **entire** TLB on every context switch.
8. **Global Bit (G)** — Marks entries (typically kernel pages) that are exempt from ASID matching and therefore remain valid across all processes.

**Neat Diagram (textual representation):**

$$
\vert \;\text{VPN Tag}\; \vert \;\text{PFN Frame}\; \vert \;\text{V}\; \vert \;\text{D}\; \vert \;\text{R}\; \vert \;\text{Prot}\; \vert \;\text{ASID}\; \vert \;\text{G}\; \vert
$$

---

**(b)** Consider a paging system with TLB access time $= 20$ ns, main memory access time $= 100$ ns, and TLB hit ratio $= 90\%$. Compute: (i) the Effective Access Time, and (ii) the speedup over a system without TLB. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $t_{TLB} = 20$ ns, $t_M = 100$ ns, $h = 0.90$, $n = 1$ (single-level page table).

**(i) Effective Access Time (EAT):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT \;=\; & t_{TLB} + t_M (2 - h) \\[4pt]
\;=\; & 20 + 100 (2 - 0.90) \\[4pt]
\;=\; & 20 + 100 (1.10) \\[4pt]
\;=\; & 20 + 110 \;=\; \mathbf{130 \; ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) EAT without TLB:**

$$
EAT_{\text{no-TLB}} \;=\; 2 \times t_M \;=\; 2 \times 100 \;=\; 200 \text{ ns}
$$

**Speedup Factor:**

$$
S \;=\; \frac{200}{130} \;\approx\; \mathbf{1.54}
$$

**Percentage improvement:** $\frac{200 - 130}{200} \times 100\% = 35\%$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently forget to add the constant $t_{TLB}$ term — EAT must always include the TLB lookup time *unconditionally*. Watch out for partial marks loss in step (i) for omitting $t_{TLB}$ entirely. Also, in part (ii), the *without-TLB* cost is $2 t_M$ (page table + data), **not** $t_M$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the three principal organisations of a TLB — **fully associative**, **set associative**, and **direct mapped** — with diagrams. Compare them on the basis of hardware cost, search speed, and conflict misses. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Fully Associative TLB**
A given VPN can be placed in **any** of the $N$ entries. The incoming VPN is **simultaneously compared** with the tag of every entry using parallel hardware comparators.

- **Advantage:** Zero conflict misses; maximum flexibility.
- **Disadvantage:** Hardware cost grows **linearly with N** (N comparators needed). Power-hungry.

**2. Set Associative TLB**
Entries are grouped into $S$ sets, each holding $k$ ways. The VPN is split into a **set index** and a **tag**. The set is selected by index, and the $k$ entries within are searched in parallel.

- **Advantage:** Balances cost and hit rate ($k$ comparators per set).
- **Disadvantage:** Suffering **intra-set** conflict misses (e.g., 2-way conflicts).

**3. Direct Mapped TLB**
Each VPN maps to **exactly one** entry using an index function (typically low-order bits of the VPN).

- **Advantage:** Hardware cost is **minimal** (one comparator).
- **Disadvantage:** High **conflict** miss rate; thrashing under certain access patterns.

**Comparison Matrix:**

| Criterion | Fully Associative | Set Associative | Direct Mapped |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hardware Cost | Very High ($N$ comparators) | Moderate ($k$ per set) | Very Low (1 comparator) |
| Search Latency | 1 cycle (parallel) | 1 cycle (parallel within set) | 1 cycle (single lookup) |
| Conflict Misses | None | Intra-set only | High |
| Typical Deployment | L1 ITLB / DTLB | L2 unified TLB | Embedded / older cores |

---

**(b)** A two-level paging system uses a TLB with $t_{TLB} = 10$ ns, $t_M = 80$ ns, and $h = 95\%$. Calculate the EAT. Compare it with the EAT of a system that has *no* TLB. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $t_{TLB} = 10$ ns, $t_M = 80$ ns, $h = 0.95$, $n = 2$ (two-level page table).

**Step 1 — Cost on a TLB Hit.**

$$
T_{hit} \;=\; t_{TLB} + t_M \;=\; 10 + 80 \;=\; 90 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Cost on a TLB Miss** (TLB + Level-1 PT + Level-2 PT + Data).

$$
T_{miss} \;=\; t_{TLB} + 3\,t_M \;=\; 10 + 3 \times 80 \;=\; 10 + 240 \;=\; 250 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute EAT.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT \;=\; & h \cdot T_{hit} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{miss} \\[4pt]
\;=\; & 0.95 \times 90 + 0.05 \times 250 \\[4pt]
\;=\; & 85.5 + 12.5 \;=\; \mathbf{98 \; ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using the generalised formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT \;=\; & t_{TLB} + t_M (n + 1 - n h) \\[4pt]
\;=\; & 10 + 80\,(2 + 1 - 2 \times 0.95) \\[4pt]
\;=\; & 10 + 80\,(3 - 1.9) \\[4pt]
\;=\; & 10 + 80 \times 1.1 \;=\; 10 + 88 \;=\; \mathbf{98 \; ns} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — EAT without TLB (always walk both levels + access data).**

$$
EAT_{\text{no-TLB}} \;=\; 3 \times t_M \;=\; 3 \times 80 \;=\; 240 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 5 — Speedup Factor.**

$$
S \;=\; \frac{240}{98} \;\approx\; 2.45
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** For a 2-level page table, a TLB miss costs **3** memory accesses, not 2. Students who write $EAT = 2 t_M$ for the no-TLB baseline will lose 1–2 marks immediately. Always *count the indirection levels* in the diagram.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TLB** = *Hardware* associative cache inside the MMU, **not** a software data structure.
- A TLB entry stores a **mapping** (VPN → PFN), **not** the page data itself.
- **ASID** field prevents the entire TLB from being flushed on every context switch — a high-yield KTU point.
- **EAT (single-level):** $EAT = t_{TLB} + t_M(2 - h)$.
- **EAT (generalised $n$-level):** $EAT = t_{TLB} + t_M(n + 1 - n h)$.
- **Hit ratio $h$** is *not* the same as the page hit ratio. A TLB hit can occur even when a page fault happens later.
- **Fully Associative TLB** → no conflict misses, expensive; **Direct Mapped** → cheap, many conflicts; **Set Associative** → compromise.
- The TLB lookup is **unconditional** — every reference pays $t_{TLB}$.
- A TLB **miss** does **not** automatically mean a **page fault** — the page may still be in memory; the OS just has to walk the page table to find its frame.
- The **Replacement Policy** (LRU, Random) is a *hardware-implemented* policy, distinct from the *OS-level* page-replacement algorithm (e.g., Clock, FIFO).
- Always quote **units (ns)** in EAT calculations to earn full marks in KTU valuations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reducing the Page Table Size; Going Beyond Physical Memory - Swap Space
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reducing the Page Table Size; Going Beyond Physical Memory - Swap Space # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Page Table Size Problem In a **linear (flat) page table**, the OS must maintain one entry for every virtual page in the entire virtual address space. For a modern system with a 32-bit address space and **4 KB** pages, this implies: $$\text{Number of Pages} = \frac{2^{32}}{2^{12}} = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ entries}$$ If each **Page Table Entry (PTE)** consumes **4 bytes** (typical for x86), the page table itself is **4 MB** — which is bearable. But for a **64-bit** address space, even assuming only **48 bits** of virtual addressing, the number of pages is $2^{36}$, requiring a page table of $2^{36} \times 4\text{B} = \mathbf{256\text{ GB}}$. This is **physically impossible** to keep in memory contiguously for every process. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A naive linear page table scales with the **virtual address space size**, not the program's actual memory usage. This wastes physical memory and is the primary motivation for advanced page table structures. ## 1.2 Beyond Physical Memory — The Concept of Swap Space Physical memory (RAM) is **finite and expensive**. The OS uses a portion of the **secondary storage (HDD/SSD)** called **Swap Space** to emulate additional memory. When RAM is full, **inactive pages** are *evicted* to swap to make room for active pages. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (Arpaci-Dusseau, KTU Reference):** *Swap space* is a designated region of the disk where the OS stores pages that have been *paged out* from physical memory. The presence of a valid bit (`Present/Absent` bit) in the PTE tracks whether the page is currently in RAM or on disk. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy **Analogy 1 — The Page Table as a Library Index:** Imagine a vast library (your virtual address space) with **one million** possible book slots. A *linear page table* is like a single index card box listing every slot — most of which are empty because the library only holds 50,000 actual books. You waste 95% of the index box. A *multi-level page table* is like a hierarchical catalog: you only open drawers (sub-tables) for sections that actually contain books. **Analogy 2 — Swap Space as a Storage Locker:** Think of your desk (RAM) as having limited working space. When it fills up, you shove overflow folders into a **basement locker (swap space)**. The locker is much larger but slower to access. You keep a sticky note on each desk item saying, *"If absent, look in locker cabinet #X, drawer #Y."* That sticky note is the **PTE with Present bit = 0**. ## 1.4 The Four Big Techniques (KTU High-Yield) | # | Technique | Core Idea | |---|-----------|-----------| | 1 | **Simple/Linear Page Table** | One big array indexed by VPN | | 2 | **Segmentation + Paging** | One page table per logical segment | | 3 | **Multi-Level Page Table** | Page the page table itself (tree) | | 4 | **Inverted Page Table** | One entry per physical frame, not per virtual page | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Swap Space Policies** that KTU emphasizes: > - **Page-Out (Eviction):** Move page from RAM → Disk > - **Page-In (Swap-In):** Move page from Disk → RAM on demand > - **Present Bit (P) in PTE:** 1 = in RAM, 0 = in swap/disk # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Reduce Page Table Size? — The 'Why' 1. **Memory Overhead:** Per-process page tables consume physical RAM that could be used by user programs. 2. **Scalability:** Linear tables cannot scale to 64-bit address spaces. 3. **Sparse Address Spaces:** Most processes use only a *fraction* of their virtual address range; linear tables reserve space for the unused region. 4. **TLB Reach:** Smaller, more cache-friendly page tables improve TLB miss handling performance. ## 2.2 Technique 1: Linear (Flat) Page Table - One entry per virtual page. - Indexed directly: `PTE_VA = PageTableBase + VPN × sizeof(PTE)`. - **Pros:** Simple, single memory access for translation (after TLB miss). - **Cons:** Wastes memory; infeasible for 64-bit. ## 2.3 Technique 2: Segmentation + Paging Combine base/bound registers (segments) with paging. Each **segment** (code, stack, heap) has its own page table. - **Code segment:** small, fixed. - **Heap segment:** grows up — only allocated pages matter. - **Stack segment:** grows down — only allocated pages matter. > Pros: Eliminates huge unused regions between segments. Cons: Still requires external fragmentation in segment base allocation; complexity in OS. ## 2.4 Technique 3: Multi-Level Page Table (THE KTU FAVOURITE) The page table itself is **paged**.
