| Fault? | F | F | F | F | H | H | F | F | F | F | F | F |

**Page Faults (FIFO, 4 frames) = 10**

#### Belady's Anomaly Confirmed

$$F_{\text{FIFO, 3 frames}} = 9 \quad \text{vs} \quad F_{\text{FIFO, 4 frames}} = 10$$

Adding **one more frame** caused **one additional page fault**. This is the textbook demonstration of **Belady's Anomaly**. It occurs in FIFO because the algorithm does not satisfy the *stack (inclusion) property* — the resident set for 4 frames is **not** a superset of the resident set for 3 frames, breaking the monotonic relationship between frame count and fault count.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Conceptual + Design

**(a)** Explain the **Second Chance (Clock) page replacement algorithm** with a suitable diagram. Discuss how it differs from pure LRU. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss **Page Buffering** and **Enhanced Second Chance** with reference to the use of the *reference bit* and *dirty bit*. Why are they useful in modern OS design? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Second Chance (Clock) Algorithm:**

The Second Chance algorithm is an LRU-approximation that uses a single **reference (use) bit** per page. All physical frames are arranged in a **circular list**; a *clock hand* points to the current candidate for eviction.

**Operational steps:**
1. On a page fault, the clock hand inspects the page it points to.
2. If the page's reference bit = **0**, it is selected as the **victim** and replaced.
3. If the reference bit = **1**, the algorithm *clears* the bit to 0, gives the page a "**second chance**", and moves the hand clockwise to the next frame.
4. In the worst case, the hand completes a full revolution finding bits = 1 (now cleared), and selects the original page.

**Difference from LRU:**

| Aspect | LRU | Second Chance |
|---|---|---|
| Memory per page | Counter or stack | 1 bit |
| Tracks order of use? | Yes (precise) | No (approximate) |
| Implementation cost | High | Low |
| Performance | Slightly better | Very close, with negligible overhead |

**Why used:** It gives near-LRU performance using only **1 bit per page** — a huge saving in kernel memory.

---

**(b) Page Buffering & Enhanced Second Chance:**

**Page Buffering** keeps a small pool of free frames even when replacement occurs. The OS does not immediately write the evicted (dirty) page to disk; instead, it adds the page to a *modified-page list* and the frame to a *free-pool*. Pages on the modified list are written back in **batches** during idle disk time, and a *clean-page list* is also kept for cheap reuse.

**Benefits:**
- A replaced page can be reclaimed cheaply if the process re-references it.
- Disk I/O is amortized, reducing peak latency.
- Free frames are always available, lowering fault-service time.

**Enhanced Second Chance (NRU — Not Recently Used):**

Uses **two bits per page** — the **reference bit (R)** and the **dirty bit (D)**. Pages are classified into four classes during replacement:

| Class | (R, D) | Meaning | Priority to Evict |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (0, 0) | Not referenced, not modified | Best victim |
| 2 | (0, 1) | Not referenced, but dirty (must be written) | Second |
| 3 | (1, 0) | Recently used, but clean | Third |
| 4 | (1, 1) | Recently used and dirty | Worst (avoid eviction) |

The clock hand scans and clears reference bits; it returns to the start after a full revolution, then evicts the lowest-class page found.

**Why useful in modern OS:**
- Combining reference and dirty bits gives the OS the *context* to prefer clean pages (cheap to evict) over dirty ones (costly).
- It reduces write-backs and improves overall throughput.
- It forms the basis of the **clock-pro** and **WSClock** algorithms used in UNIX/Linux.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the victim-write step** — when a replaced page is dirty (modified), the OS must write it to disk *before* loading the new page. Marks are cut if the trace table shows eviction without acknowledging disk I/O.
> 2. **Confusing "Reference bit" semantics** — in the Second Chance algorithm, the bit is **cleared** (set to 0) on a second chance, not on every reference. Many students reverse this.
> 3. **Misidentifying Belady's Anomaly** — it occurs in FIFO but **not** in LRU/OPT. Writing that "LRU suffers from Belady's Anomaly" is a direct 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Skipping the trace table** — KTU ESE questions on reference strings *require* a full trace table; merely writing the final page-fault count with no justification loses at least 3 of 7 marks.
> 5. **EAT numerical errors** — mixing up units (ns vs ms) and forgetting to convert $f$ from ms to ns leads to wrong orders of magnitude. Always show the unit conversion step.
> 6. **Confusing LFU and MFU** — LFU evicts the *least* frequently used, MFU evicts the *most* frequently used. Reverse answer = 0 marks.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3: Page Replacement Policies**

- ✅ **Page replacement** is triggered when a page fault occurs **and no free frame exists**.
- ✅ A page fault can cost **two disk I/Os** — write-out the victim (if dirty) + read-in the new page.
- ✅ **FIFO** is simple but suffers from **Belady's Anomaly**; the page evicted is the *oldest*, regardless of usage.
- ✅ **OPT** (Optimal / Bélády) replaces the page that will be used *farthest* in the future — theoretical lower bound, not realizable.
- ✅ **LRU** uses *past* usage to predict *future* usage — best practical approximation of OPT; **stack algorithm** → immune to Belady's Anomaly.
- ✅ **Second Chance (Clock)** uses 1 reference bit per page in a circular buffer; gives LRU-like performance cheaply.
- ✅ **Enhanced Second Chance** uses (Reference, Dirty) bit pair; prefer (0,0) → (0,1) → (1,0) → (1,1) as victims.
- ✅ **LFU** evicts *least* frequently used; **MFU** evicts *most* frequently used.
- ✅ **Page Buffering** maintains a pool of free frames and defers disk writes — reduces I/O latency.
- ✅ **Stack property** $S_{n} \subseteq S_{n+1}$ is what saves LRU and OPT from Belady's Anomaly.
- ✅ **EAT** $= (1-p)\cdot m + p\cdot f$ — always convert units carefully (ns vs ms).
- ✅ **Page Fault Rate** $= F / N \times 100\%$; **Hit Ratio** $= 1 - \text{PFR}$.
- ✅ Reference-string problems in KTU ESE always expect a **full trace table** — practice drawing it neatly.
- ✅ Belady's Anomaly **standard string**: $1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ — 9 faults (3 frames) vs 10 faults (4 frames) under FIFO.
- ✅ Modern OS kernels (Linux, Windows) use **clock-based LRU approximations** with active/inactive page lists, not pure LRU.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thrashing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thrashing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Thrashing** is a critical performance-degradation phenomenon in virtual memory systems where the operating system spends a disproportionately large amount of time **swapping pages in and out of the main memory (RAM)** rather than executing the actual instructions of a process. The CPU utilization drops drastically because the system is overwhelmingly busy performing paging operations (disk I/O for page faults) instead of doing useful computational work.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim-Aligned):** > *"Thrashing occurs when a system is busy swapping pages in and out of memory constantly, leading to severe degradation in system performance where the CPU spends more time on paging than on process execution."*

In the context of demand paging, thrashing manifests when a process does not have **enough frames** (physical memory pages) to hold the pages it is currently actively using. Consequently, it generates a **page fault**, the required page must be brought in from secondary storage, but in doing so, some other page currently in memory must be evicted (replaced). If that evicted page is needed again almost immediately, another page fault occurs — a vicious cycle ensues.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Cluttered Study Desk"**

Imagine a student preparing for exams with a very small desk (the **CPU/RAM**). The desk can only hold **3 books** at a time (frames). But the student's study material requires constantly referring to **8 different books** (the working set).

1. The student picks up Book A, realizes they need Book C → puts Book A back on the shelf (swap-out), brings Book C to the desk (swap-in).
2. While reading Book C, they need Book A again → swap-out Book C, swap-in Book A.
3. The student is **running back and forth to the bookshelf** (disk I/O) all day, but **never actually reads** any book long enough to absorb the content.

**Result:** Hours of work, zero productivity — that is **Thrashing**.

The desk represents the small physical memory allocated to a process. The shelf represents the slow disk. The constant running back-and-forth represents paging I/O. **The actual studying** represents useful CPU execution. When fetching/evicting dominates execution, the system is thrashing.

## 1.3 Why Thrashing Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

- **CO Mapping:** This topic maps strongly to **CO3 (Memory Management)** of the PCCST403 syllabus.
- **Bloom's Level:** Primarily **L4 (Analyze)** — students must diagnose causes and propose solutions.
- **Examination Weightage:** Thrashing is a **guaranteed 7-to-14 mark** question in KTU ESE, typically asked as a long-answer question involving the **Working Set Model** and **Page Fault Frequency** strategies.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Symbol | Quick Meaning |
|------|--------|---------------|
| Frame | — | A fixed-size physical memory slot in RAM (typically **4 KB**) |
| Page | — | A fixed-size logical memory block |
| Page Fault | — | A reference to a page NOT currently in a frame |
| Page Fault Rate | `p` | **0 ≤ p ≤ 1.0** (probability/frequency of faults) |
| Effective Access Time | `EAT` | Average memory access time accounting for faults |
| Working Set | `W(t, Δ)` | Set of pages used by a process in window `Δ` |
| Multiprogramming Degree | `D` | Number of processes concurrently in memory |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Thrashing Develops (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Understand this cascade: it is the most asked KTU question pattern.**

**Stage 1 — Initial System State:**
- The system starts with `N` processes, each holding `D/N` frames (where `D` = total frames).
- CPU utilization is reasonable, processes are executing.

**Stage 2 — Over-Commitment by the OS:**
- The OS sees high CPU utilization and (incorrectly) assumes more processes can be added.
- It increases the degree of multiprogramming by admitting a new process.
- **Total frames per process decreases** → `D/N` shrinks.

**Stage 3 — Page Fault Spike:**
- Each process now has **fewer frames** than its active working set requires.
- Page faults per process rise sharply.

**Stage 4 — Paging I/O Saturation:**
- The system spends most of its time servicing page faults.
- The ready queue becomes empty (no process is fully memory-resident).
- **CPU utilization drops to nearly 0%** because the CPU is idle waiting for the disk.

**Stage 5 — Vicious Self-Reinforcing Loop:**
- The OS sees the low CPU utilization and again thinks *"Let me add MORE processes to fill the CPU."*
- It admits more processes → even worse paging → even lower CPU utilization.

**The Feedback Loop:**

$$\text{Low CPU Util} \rightarrow \text{Add more processes} \rightarrow \text{Less frames per process} \rightarrow \text{More page faults} \rightarrow \text{Less CPU work} \rightarrow \text{Low CPU Util}$$

This is the **Death Spiral of Thrashing** — a self-amplifying failure mode.

## 2.2 Root Cause Analysis — The "Why" Behind Thrashing

| Root Cause | Explanation |
|------------|-------------|
| **Insufficient total memory** | `Σ \vert W(t, Δ) \vert` (sum of all working sets) `> D` (total frames) |
| **Poor frame allocation** | Local allocation may starve some processes |
| **Locality change** | A process suddenly accesses a new locality (e.g., reads a new array section) |
| **Over-multiprogramming** | Too many processes competing for too few frames |
| **Bad page replacement policy** | Evicts "hot" pages (e.g., poor approximation of LRU) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE] > **All the formulas below have appeared in KTU ESE papers (2019–2024). Memorize the table.**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $$\text{EAT} = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot (\text{page fault overhead})$$ | Effective Access Time with page fault probability `p` and memory access time `ma` |
| 2 | $$\text{EAT} = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot (t_{\text{disk}} + ma)$$ | When page fault service includes a full disk access `t_disk` |
| 3 | $$\text{EAT} = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot (t_{\text{disk}} + q \cdot t_{\text{disk}} + ma)$$ | If modified page must first be written back (probability `q` of dirty page) |
| 4 | $$\text{Thrashing Condition: } \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert W_i(t, Δ) \vert > D$$ | Sum of working set sizes of all `n` processes must be **≤** total available frames `D` |
| 5 | $$\text{PFF: } \text{If rate} > \text{threshold} \rightarrow \text{allocate more frames}$$ | Page Fault Frequency control threshold logic |
| 6 | $$\text{Frame Allocation Lower Bound: } D = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert W_i(t, Δ) \vert$$ | Minimum frames needed to prevent thrashing |
| 7 | $$\text{Approx. Working Set Size} = \Delta \cdot \text{(unique page references in window)}$$ | Empirical estimation of working set size |

> **Where:** `p` is a pure ratio (0 to 1, dimensionless), `ma` and `t_disk` are in **microseconds/milliseconds**, `Δ` is in **virtual time units**, `D` is in **number of frames** (count).

## 2.4 The Two Main Solutions to Thrashing (KTU High-Yield)

### Solution 1 — **Working Set Model** (Denning, 1968)

- **Working Set `W(t, Δ)`:** The set of pages referenced by a process in the most recent `Δ` virtual time units.
- The OS tracks each process's working set and allocates **at least** `\vert W(t, Δ) \vert` frames.
- If `Σ \vert W_i \vert > D` → **suspend one or more processes** (swap out) to free frames.
- **Advantage:** Prevents thrashing proactively.
- **Disadvantage:** Tracking the working set is **costly** (requires hardware timers and reference bits).

### Solution 2 — **Page Fault Frequency (PFF) Strategy**

- Monitor the page fault rate of each process.
- If PFF of a process rises **above** an upper threshold → **allocate more frames** to that process.
- If PFF falls **below** a lower threshold → **take away some frames** (give to others).
- If total frames are insufficient even after redistribution → **suspend a process**.
- **Advantage:** More practical and adaptive.
- **Disadvantage:** Tuning the thresholds requires empirical work.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Computing (AWS/Azure):** Thrashing detection is critical in memory-optimized instance selection; over-provisioning wastes cost, under-provisioning causes thrashing.
- **Mobile OS (Android/iOS):** Low-memory killer (LMK) daemon prevents thrashing by terminating background apps.
- **Database Engines (PostgreSQL, Oracle):** Buffer pool size tuning is essentially a fight against thrashing.
- **Embedded/IoT Systems:** Limited RAM makes thrashing prevention essential; Working Set Model guides memory partitioning in RTOS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Access Time (EAT) with Page Faults

This is the **single most important KTU derivation** — it appears in nearly every ESE paper.

### Given Assumptions

- `ma` = memory access time (time to read from RAM) = typically **100 ns**.
- `p` = probability of a page fault (0 ≤ p ≤ 1.0).
- `t_disk` = time to service a page fault (disk I/O + restart) = typically **25 ms = 25,000,000 ns**.
- `q` = probability that the evicted page is dirty (modified) and must be written back to disk.

### Step 1 — Two Outcomes Per Memory Reference

A single logical memory reference has only two outcomes:

1. **Hit** (probability `1 - p`): The page is in memory, access time = `ma`.
2. **Page Fault** (probability `p`): The page is NOT in memory, must be fetched from disk.

### Step 2 — EAT for the Simple Case (Evicted Page is Clean)

If the replaced page is clean (no write-back needed), the OS performs:

- Service the page fault (`t_disk`)
- Restart the instruction (one more memory access `ma`)

Therefore, the time per reference in the fault case is `(t_disk + ma)`.

By the **Law of Total Probability**, the effective access time is the weighted sum:

$$
\text{EAT} = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot (t_{\text{disk}} + ma)
$$

### Step 3 — Expanding and Simplifying the Simple Case

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot t_{\text{disk}} + p \cdot ma \\
&= ma - p \cdot ma + p \cdot t_{\text{disk}} + p \cdot ma \\
&= ma + p \cdot t_{\text{disk}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key insight:** The `ma` terms cancel! The only penalty for a page fault is the disk time, scaled by the fault probability.

### Step 4 — EAT for the Dirty Page Case (Most Realistic)

If the evicted page is dirty (probability `q` of being dirty), the OS must:

1. Write the dirty page back to disk → `t_disk`
2. Read the new page from disk → `t_disk`
3. Restart the instruction → `ma`

**Average fault service time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{fault}} &= q \cdot (t_{\text{disk}} + t_{\text{disk}} + ma) + (1 - q) \cdot (t_{\text{disk}} + ma) \\
&= q \cdot 2 \cdot t_{\text{disk}} + q \cdot ma + t_{\text{disk}} + ma - q \cdot t_{\text{disk}} - q \cdot ma \\
&= 2q \cdot t_{\text{disk}} - q \cdot t_{\text{disk}} + t_{\text{disk}} + ma \\
&= (1 + q) \cdot t_{\text{disk}} + ma
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Full EAT Formula with Dirty Page Consideration

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot t_{\text{fault}} \\
&= (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot \big((1 + q) \cdot t_{\text{disk}} + ma\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding:

$$
\text{EAT} = (1 - p) \cdot ma + p \cdot (1 + q) \cdot t_{\text{disk}} + p \cdot ma
$$

$$
\text{EAT} = ma + p \cdot (1 + q) \cdot t_{\text{disk}}
$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Demonstration (Typical KTU Numerical)

> [!NOTE] > **Worked Example:** If `ma = 100 ns`, `t_disk = 10 ms = 10,000,000 ns`, `p = 0.001`, `q = 0.5`, find EAT.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= ma + p \cdot (1 + q) \cdot t_{\text{disk}} \\
&= 100 + 0.001 \cdot (1 + 0.5) \cdot 10{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 100 + 0.001 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 10{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 100 + 15{,}000 \\
&= 15{,}100 \text{ ns} \approx 15.1 \text{ μs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Just a **0.1%** page fault rate slows memory access by **151×**. This is the magnitude of degradation thrashing amplifies.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Thrashing vs. No-Thrashing

| Parameter | Healthy System (No Thrashing) | Thrashing System |
|-----------|-------------------------------|------------------|
| CPU Utilization | **60–90%** | **< 10%** |
| Page Fault Rate | **Low (p ≈ 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻³)** | **High (p ≈ 10⁻² to 10⁻¹)** |
| Disk I/O Activity | Moderate | **Saturated** |
| Throughput | High | **Near-zero** |
| Process State | Mostly **Running** | Mostly **Ready/Waiting** (for I/O) |
| Multiprogramming Degree | **Balanced** | **Excessive** |
| Frames per Process | **≥ Working Set** | **< Working Set** |
| Response Time | Predictable | Unpredictable, very high |

## 3.3 Decision Flow for Frame Reallocation (PFF Strategy)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \text{PFF}_{\text{current}} > \text{Threshold}_{\text{upper}}: \\
&\quad \text{Allocate more frames to that process} \\
\text{If } \text{PFF}_{\text{current}} < \text{Threshold}_{\text{lower}}: \\
&\quad \text{Steal some frames from that process} \\
\text{If } \sum \text{frames demanded} > D: \\
&\quad \text{Suspend (swap out) a process} \\
\text{Else:} \\
&\quad \text{Continue normal execution}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Working Set Size Estimation (Empirical)

Given a reference string of length `n` and a window of `Δ` references, the working set size `\vert W \vert` at time `t` is the count of **unique page numbers** in the most recent `Δ` references ending at time `t`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(t) &= \{p_i : t - Δ < i \leq t\} \\
\vert S(t) \vert &= \text{unique page count in window} [t - Δ + 1, t]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** If the last 5 references are `{2, 6, 1, 5, 7, 7, 1, 6, 2, 3}` and `Δ = 5`, then at position `t = 10` the window contains `{7, 1, 6, 2, 3}` → **5 unique pages** → working set size = 5.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Thrashing Death Spiral (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Boots with N processes] --> B[CPU Utilization is High]
    B --> C{OS Decision: Add More Processes?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Multiprogramming Degree Increases]
    D --> E[Frames per Process Decreases]
    E --> F[Page Fault Rate Spikes]
    F --> G[Disk I/O Saturated]
    G --> H[CPU Utilization Drops to Near Zero]
    H --> I{OS Decision: Add MORE Processes?}
    I -- Yes --> D
    I -- No --> J[SUSPEND Some Processes]
    J --> K[Frames per Process Recovers]
    K --> L[Page Fault Rate Falls]
    L --> M[CPU Utilization Recovers]
    M --> N[Stable Operation]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Working Set Model (Sliding Window Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TS[Time Line of Page References]
        T1[t-9]:::past --> T2[t-8]:::past --> T3[t-7]:::past --> T4[t-6]:::past --> T5[t-5]:::past --> T6[t-4]:::past --> T7[t-3]:::past --> T8[t-2]:::past --> T9[t-1]:::past --> T10[t = NOW]:::now
    end

    subgraph WS[Working Set W of size Delta = 5]
        W5[T-5 ref page 2]:::win --> W4[T-4 ref page 6]:::win --> W3[T-3 ref page 1]:::win --> W2[T-2 ref page 5]:::win --> W1[T-1 ref page 7]:::win
    end

    T6 -. "Pages in W: {2,6,1,5,7}" .-> WS
    T10 --> X{Allocate atleast 5 frames}
    X -- Yes --> Y[No Thrashing]
    X -- No --> Z[THRASHING]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Page Fault Frequency (PFF) Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Monitor Page Fault Rate of Each Process] --> Q{PFF vs Thresholds}
    Q -- Above Upper --> AA[Allocate MORE Frames to that Process]
    Q -- Below Lower --> BB[Take BACK Excess Frames]
    Q -- Within Range --> CC[No Action Needed]
    AA --> DDD{Enough Frames Available?}
    BB --> DDD
    CC --> EEE[Process Continues]
    DDD -- Yes --> EEE
    DDD -- No --> FF[Suspend a Process Swap Out]
    FF --> EEE
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Block-Level Architecture of a Thrashing-Resilient Memory Manager

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP[Application Layer]
        PROC[Process with Virtual Address Space]
    end

    subgraph OS[OS Layer - Memory Manager]
        MMU[MMU - Memory Management Unit]
        PFF[Page Fault Frequency Monitor]
        WSM[Working Set Tracker]
        ALOC[Frame Allocator]
        REPL[Page Replacement Module LRU Approx]
        SUSPEND[Process Suspension Manager]
    end

    subgraph HW[Hardware Layer]
        RAM[Physical RAM - Total Frames D]
        DISK[Secondary Storage - Disk]
    end

    PROC --> MMU
    MMU -- Page Hit --> RAM
    MMU -- Page Fault --> REPL
    REPL --> DISK
    DISK --> RAM
    PFF --> ALOC
    WSM --> ALOC
    ALOC --> RAM
    ALOC -- Insufficient --> SUSPEND
    SUSPEND --> DISK
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5: CPU Utilization vs Multiprogramming Degree (The "Inverted U" Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[MP Degree = 1] --> P2[MP Degree = 2] --> P3[MP Degree = 4] --> P4[MP Degree = 6] --> P5[MP Degree = 8] --> P6[MP Degree = 10]

    P1 --> V1[CPU Util: 25 percent]
    P2 --> V2[CPU Util: 50 percent]
    P3 --> V3[CPU Util: 80 percent PEAK]
    P4 --> V4[CPU Util: 70 percent]
    P5 --> V5[CPU Util: 30 percent]
    P6 --> V6[CPU Util: 5 percent THRASHING]
```

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this curve:** CPU utilization rises with multiprogramming up to an **optimum point**, then **falls sharply** into the thrashing region. The OS must operate **at or before the peak**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define thrashing. How does it affect system performance?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** Thrashing is a condition in a virtual memory system where the operating system spends more time swapping pages between main memory and disk than executing the actual processes. It occurs when a process does not have enough frames in memory to hold its working set, leading to an extremely high page fault rate.
>
> **Effect on Performance (1 mark):** It causes a severe drop in CPU utilization, throughput falls to near zero, disk I/O becomes saturated, and the system response time increases dramatically, often making the system practically unusable.

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between the Working Set Model and the Page Fault Frequency (PFF) strategy for handling thrashing.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Working Set Model | Page Fault Frequency |
> |--------|------------------|----------------------|
> | **Approach** | Proactive — predicts need based on recent references | Reactive — responds to actual fault rate |
> | **Cost** | High (needs timer + reference bits) | Moderate (just counts faults) |
> | **Decision** | Allocate `≥` size of working set frames | Adjust frames based on upper/lower thresholds |
> | **Trigger** | Continuous monitoring at every reference | Only when fault rate changes significantly |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of thrashing in a demand-paged system. Discuss the role of the working set model in preventing thrashing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a demand-paged system with `ma = 200 ns`, average disk access time = `10 ms`, page fault rate `p = 0.0005`, and probability that a page is dirty = `0.3`. Calculate the effective access time. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

1. **Definition of Thrashing (1.5 marks):** Thrashing is the condition where the system spends a majority of its time handling page faults rather than executing user processes. It arises when the total demand for memory (sum of all working sets) exceeds the total physical memory available.

2. **Cause of Thrashing (1.5 marks):** When the OS increases the multiprogramming degree, each process receives fewer frames. If these frames are insufficient for a process's working set, page faults increase, which forces more swapping and reduces CPU utilization — creating a self-reinforcing loop.

3. **Working Set Model Definition (2 marks):** Proposed by **Peter Denning in 1968**, the working set `W(t, Δ)` is the set of pages that a process has referenced in the most recent `Δ` virtual time units. The size `\vert W(t, Δ) \vert` represents the minimum number of frames the process needs to avoid frequent page faults.

4. **Role in Preventing Thrashing (2 marks):**
   - The OS tracks the working set of each process.
   - It allocates at least `\vert W_i \vert` frames to process `i`.
   - If `Σ \vert W_i \vert > D` (total frames), the OS **suspends** one or more processes until the sum is manageable.
   - This **proactive** approach prevents the cascade of page faults that leads to thrashing.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- `ma = 200 ns`
- `t_disk = 10 ms = 10,000,000 ns`
- `p = 0.0005`
- `q = 0.3` (dirty page probability)

**Step 1 — Identify the right EAT formula:**

$$
\text{EAT} = ma + p \cdot (1 + q) \cdot t_{\text{disk}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= 200 + 0.0005 \cdot (1 + 0.3) \cdot 10{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 200 + 0.0005 \cdot 1.3 \cdot 10{,}000{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= 200 + 0.0005 \cdot 13{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 200 + 6{,}500 \\
&= 6{,}700 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
The effective access time is **6700 ns = 6.7 μs**, which is **33.5 times** slower than the no-fault access time of 200 ns. Even a tiny page fault rate of 0.05% causes a 33× slowdown.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the Page Fault Frequency (PFF) strategy for controlling thrashing. Show how the upper and lower thresholds are used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system with 5 processes has the following working set sizes at a given instant: `{12, 8, 15, 10, 6}` frames. The total physical memory has 40 frames. Determine whether the system will thrash. If yes, how many processes must be suspended (and which ones) to stop thrashing? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

1. **Conceptual Setup (2 marks):** The PFF strategy continuously monitors the rate of page faults for each process. Two thresholds are defined: an **upper threshold** `U` and a **lower threshold** `L`. When a process's fault rate crosses these limits, the frame allocator adjusts the number of frames assigned to that process.

2. **Diagrammatic Explanation (3 marks):**

   - **PFF > U** → Allocate **more** frames to that process (steal from lower-PFF processes).
   - **PFF < L** → The process has **more frames than needed**; reclaim some.
   - **L ≤ PFF ≤ U** → No change; process is in the healthy operating region.

3. **Total Memory Check (1 mark):** If after redistribution the system still cannot satisfy all processes' working sets, the OS **suspends** one or more processes (swaps them out to disk) to free up a large block of frames for the remaining processes.

4. **Advantages (1 mark):** PFF is more practical than the Working Set Model because it doesn't require maintaining a sliding window of references; it just needs a fault counter per process.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- Working sets: `W = {12, 8, 15, 10, 6}`
- Total frames: `D = 40`

**Step 1 — Sum the working set sizes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{5} \vert W_i \vert &= 12 + 8 + 15 + 10 + 6 \\
&= 51 \text{ frames}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with available frames:**

$$
\sum \vert W_i \vert = 51 > D = 40
$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**
Since the total working set demand (51) exceeds available frames (40), the system **WILL thrash**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Find minimum suspensions:**

We need to remove processes until the sum is `≤ 40`.

- Remove the process with the largest working set: `15` → new sum = `36 ≤ 40`. ✅

**Answer (1 mark):** The system must suspend **at least 1 process** — specifically, the process with a working set of **15 frames** (the largest). This frees 15 frames, leaving `40` frames for the remaining 4 processes whose total working set is `12 + 8 + 10 + 6 = 36` frames.

**Verification:** `40 - 36 = 4` spare frames, so no further thrashing will occur. ✅

---

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `+ ma` in EAT:** Many students write `EAT = p × t_disk` only, ignoring the memory access time. KTU key gives full 7 marks only when both terms are shown.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** Failing to convert `ms` to `ns` (e.g., using `10 ms` directly with `200 ns`) loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Misapplying the Working Set Model:** Students often write the definition but skip the **suspension step** when `Σ \vert W \vert > D`. This loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing Hit Ratio and Fault Probability:** Remember `1 - p = hit ratio`. Confusing them inverts the formula.
> 5. **Not specifying the time window `Δ`:** In the Working Set Model, `Δ` must be mentioned — definitions without it are incomplete (lose 1 mark).
> 6. **Numerical mistakes in PFF numericals:** Forgetting to subtract the suspended process's working set from BOTH the total frames and the sum.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this section for the night before the exam.**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- **Thrashing** = Excessive paging causing CPU utilization collapse.
- **Working Set `W(t, Δ)`** = Pages used in the most recent `Δ` time units.
- **Page Fault Frequency (PFF)** = Page faults per unit time for a process.

### ✅ Key Conditions
- **Thrashing condition:** `Σ \vert W_i \vert > D` (sum of working sets > total frames).
- **No thrashing condition:** `Σ \vert W_i \vert ≤ D`.

### ✅ Formulas (Must Memorize)
- `EAT = (1 - p)·ma + p·(t_disk + ma)` — simple case
- `EAT = ma + p·(1 + q)·t_disk` — with dirty page probability
- Optimal `D = Σ \vert W_i(t, Δ) \vert`

### ✅ Two Solutions (Always asked together)
1. **Working Set Model** → Proactive, needs hardware timer.
2. **PFF Strategy** → Reactive, needs fault counter + thresholds.

### ✅ Cause of Thrashing (Cascade)
OS adds process → Frames per process drop → Page faults rise → Disk saturated → CPU drops → OS adds more process (LOOP).

### ✅ KTU-Favored Numerical Constants
- `ma = 100 ns` or `200 ns`
- `t_disk = 10 ms` or `25 ms`
- Page size = **4 KB** (default)
- Frame size = Page size

### ✅ Prevention vs. Recovery
- **Prevention** = Working Set Model, PFF (pre-fault).
- **Recovery** = Suspend a process, swap out, swap in later.

### ✅ Common Examiner Traps
- If EAT is asked **without `q`**, use the simple formula.
- If EAT is asked **with dirty page mention**, include `(1 + q)`.
- If a working set question gives only a **reference string**, count **unique** pages in the window.
- For suspension, always suspend the **largest working set process** first.

### ✅ Real-World Connection
- Mobile OS Low-Memory Killer (LMK) = Practical PFF.
- Linux's `kswapd` daemon = Working Set-driven pre-emption.
- Windows Memory Manager uses PFF-style heuristics.

### ✅ Diagram to Draw in Exam
Always include the **CPU Utilization vs Multiprogramming Degree** inverted-U curve and the **death-spiral flowchart** for full marks on the long answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern System Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modern System Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Modern System Architecture** refers to the structural and organizational design of contemporary computer systems that integrate multiple processing units, memory hierarchies, and I/O subsystems to achieve high performance, scalability, reliability, and availability. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Operating Systems (PCCST403), this encompasses **multiprocessor systems**, **multicore architectures**, **clustered systems**, and **distributed systems**, along with the supporting memory-access models (UMA, NUMA) and storage architectures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):**
> Modern System Architecture deals with how CPUs, memory, and I/O devices are interconnected and managed by the OS to exploit **parallelism**, **concurrency**, and **fault tolerance**.

## 1.2 Classification of Modern System Architectures

| Architecture Type | Description | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Processor (Uni-processor)** | One CPU executes one instruction stream | Baseline reference |
| **Multiprocessor (SMP)** | Two or more identical CPUs sharing a single main memory | Tightly coupled |
| **Multicore** | Multiple cores integrated on a single physical chip | On-die shared cache |
| **NUMA** | Non-Uniform Memory Access — memory local to each processor | Memory access time varies |
| **Clustered System** | Multiple interconnected standalone systems working as one | Loosely coupled |
| **Distributed System** | Geographically dispersed nodes communicating over a network | Loosest coupling |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Kitchen Preparing a Feast**
>
> - **Single-Processor (Uni-processor)** = One chef working alone in a single kitchen. Bottleneck: only one dish at a time.
> - **Symmetric Multiprocessor (SMP)** = Multiple chefs in the **same kitchen**, sharing the same pantry (RAM) and the same stove. They must coordinate, but tools and ingredients are instantly accessible to all.
> - **Multicore** = One chef with **multiple hands** (cores on one chip). The hands are physically close, so passing salt is almost free.
> - **NUMA** = Multiple kitchens, each with its **own pantry**, but pantries are linked by a slow conveyor belt. Using your own pantry is fast; borrowing a neighbor's is slower.
> - **Clustered System** = Multiple independent restaurants in the **same building** communicating via waiters. Each has its own kitchen, pantry, and head chef.
> - **Distributed System** = Restaurants **across the world** communicating only via the internet — slow, but virtually unlimited scale.

## 1.4 Core Metrics in Modern Architecture (Standard Definitions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Throughput:** Number of tasks completed per unit time. Unit: **tasks/second**.
> - **Latency:** Time taken to complete a single task. Unit: **seconds (or ms, μs)**.
> - **Scalability:** Ability to gain performance by adding resources. Unit: **dimensionless ratio**.
> - **Availability:** Fraction of time the system is operational. Unit: **% (typically 99.999% — "five nines")**.
> - **Reliability:** Mean Time To Failure (MTTF). Unit: **hours**.
> - **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):** Time to restore service after failure. Unit: **hours**.
> - **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR**
> - **Availability = MTTF / (MTTF + MTTR)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Symmetric Multiprocessing (SMP)

In an **SMP system**, two or more **identical processors** are connected to a single, shared main memory and run a single copy of the OS. All processors are **peers** — any processor can execute any task, and they share the same I/O devices.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic
- **Step 1:** A single OS instance boots and is loaded into shared main memory.
- **Step 2:** Workload (processes/threads) is distributed via a **common ready queue** or per-processor queues.
- **Step 3:** Any idle processor picks work — this is called **dynamic load balancing**.
- **Step 4:** Processors communicate via **shared memory** using synchronization primitives (locks, semaphores).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SMP?** The OS designer can use standard synchronization (mutex, spinlock) without complex message passing. The hardware provides a single physical address space.

### 2.1.2 Advantages & Disadvantages

| Aspect | SMP Characteristics |
|---|---|
| **Pros** | Simpler programming model; transparent to applications; dynamic load balancing; high availability (one CPU fails, others continue). |
| **Cons** | Memory contention as CPUs increase; bus bandwidth becomes bottleneck; cache coherency protocol overhead. |

## 2.2 Multicore Architecture

A **multicore processor** is a single integrated circuit containing **two or more independent processing units (cores)**. Each core has its own registers and L1 cache but typically shares higher-level caches (L2/L3) and the memory controller with other cores on the same die.

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic
- **Step 1:** Multiple cores reside on **one physical chip** — a Chip Multiprocessor (CMP).
- **Step 2:** Threads of a single application (or different applications) execute in parallel on different cores.
- **Step 3:** Cores communicate via the **shared on-chip cache**, which is far faster than off-chip RAM.
- **Step 4:** The OS scheduler treats each core as a logical processor (e.g., a quad-core CPU is seen as 4 logical CPUs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Multicore dominates modern CPUs:** Lower power consumption per core, less heat, smaller form factor, faster inter-core communication than multi-CPU SMP boards.

### 2.2.2 Intel Hyper-Threading (Simultaneous Multithreading — SMT)
Each physical core exposes **two (or more) logical processors** by duplicating architectural state (registers, instruction pointer). Two threads execute on the same core by sharing functional units. The OS sees **N × 2 logical CPUs** for N physical cores.

## 2.3 Non-Uniform Memory Access (NUMA)

In **NUMA**, multiple processors each have their **own local memory**, but can also access remote memory attached to other processors via a high-speed interconnect. Access time depends on memory location relative to the processor.

### 2.3.1 Operational Logic
- **Step 1:** Memory is partitioned into **local** (fast) and **remote** (slow) regions.
- **Step 2:** The OS includes a **NUMA-aware scheduler** that tries to schedule a process on the CPU closest to the memory it most frequently accesses.
- **Step 3:** Memory pages can be migrated between nodes to improve locality.
- **Step 4:** The interconnect (e.g., Intel QPI, AMD Infinity Fabric) provides a coherent shared address space.

### 2.3.2 NUMA vs UMA (Uniform Memory Access)

| Feature | UMA (SMP) | NUMA |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory access time** | Equal for all CPUs | Depends on locality |
| **Scalability** | Poor beyond 16–32 CPUs | Scales to thousands of CPUs |
| **Hardware complexity** | Low | High (directory-based coherency) |
| **Example** | Dual-socket workstation | Modern multi-socket servers (e.g., 4-socket EPYC) |

## 2.4 Clustered Systems

A **clustered system** consists of **two or more standalone computers (nodes)** interconnected via a high-speed LAN or interconnect, working together as a single logical system. Each node has its own CPU, memory, OS, and storage.

### 2.4.1 Cluster Configurations
- **Active-Active (Asymmetric Clustering):** All nodes actively run the workload; failure of one redistributes work to others.
- **Active-Passive (Symmetric/Hot-Standby):** One node runs the workload; another waits as a hot standby.
- **High-Availability (HA) Cluster:** Designed for fail-over; uses heartbeat signals.
- **Load-Balancing Cluster:** Dispatches incoming requests to the least-loaded node.
- **Parallel Cluster:** Multiple nodes cooperate to run a single parallel application (e.g., HPC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Heartbeat:** A periodic message exchanged between cluster nodes over a private network to verify each other's liveness.

## 2.5 Distributed Systems

A **distributed system** is a collection of **independent computers (heterogeneous, geographically dispersed)** that appears to its users as a **single coherent system**, communicating exclusively via **message passing over a network**.

### 2.5.1 Characteristics
- **Resource sharing** across the network.
- **Concurrency** — multiple machines process simultaneously.
- **Scalability** — adding nodes increases capacity.
- **Fault tolerance** — no single point of failure.
- **Transparency categories:** Access, Location, Migration, Concurrency, Failure, Replication, Performance, Scaling.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Modern System Architecture**

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$ | MTTF, MTTR | Fraction or % |
| **MTBF** | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | MTTF, MTTR | Hours |
| **Amdahl's Law (Speedup)** | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | P = parallel fraction, N = processors | Dimensionless |
| **Max Speedup (N → ∞)** | $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - P}$ | P = parallel fraction | Dimensionless |
| **RAID 0 Capacity** | $C_{0} = N \times D$ | N = disks, D = disk size | Bytes |
| **RAID 1 Capacity** | $C_{1} = D$ | D = single disk size | Bytes |
| **RAID 5 Capacity** | $C_{5} = (N - 1) \times D$ | N = disks, D = disk size | Bytes |
| **RAID 6 Capacity** | $C_{6} = (N - 2) \times D$ | N = disks, D = disk size | Bytes |
| **Effective CPU count (HT)** | $L = C \times T$ | C = cores, T = threads/core | Logical CPUs |
| **Storage IOPS estimate** | $IOPS = \dfrac{1}{T_{seek} + T_{rot}}$ | $T_{seek}$ avg seek time, $T_{rot}$ = half rotation | ops/sec |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Cloud computing platforms** (AWS, Azure, GCP) are **distributed systems** running on top of **clustered NUMA** servers.
> - **HPC supercomputers** (Top500) are **parallel clusters** using **NUMA hardware** with **InfiniBand** interconnect.
> - **Smartphones** use **multicore SoCs** (e.g., 8-core ARM big.LITTLE) — a NUMA-on-chip layout.
> - **Banking systems** rely on **HA clusters** for 24×7 availability, targeting **five-nines (99.999%)** uptime.
> - **RAID arrays** protect against disk failure in modern **SAN/NAS** storage subsystems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law for Parallel Speedup

### 3.1.1 Statement
**Amdahl's Law** gives the theoretical speedup of a task when only a fraction $P$ of the task can be parallelized across $N$ processors.

### 3.1.2 Derivation (Exhaustive)

**Step 1 — Identify the two parts of a program:**
A program's execution time has two portions:
- The **serial (non-parallelizable) portion** $= (1 - P)$
- The **parallel portion** $= P$

**Step 2 — Original execution time on 1 processor:**
Let $T_{1}$ = 1 unit (normalized). Then:
$$T_{1} = 1 = (1 - P) + P$$

**Step 3 — Execution time on $N$ processors:**
The serial portion remains unchanged. The parallel portion divides among $N$ processors:
$$T_{N} = (1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Speedup is the ratio:**
$$S(N) = \frac{T_{1}}{T_{N}}$$

Substituting the values from Step 2 and Step 3:
$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$$

This is the **canonical form of Amdahl's Law**.

**Step 5 — Maximum theoretical speedup as $N \to \infty$:**
$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$$

As $N \to \infty$, $\dfrac{P}{N} \to 0$, so:
$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - P}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
Even with an infinite number of processors, the speedup is **bounded** by the serial fraction. For example, if 5% of the program is serial ($P = 0.95$):
$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.95} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20$$

So even 1000 processors give at most 20× speedup.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A program spends 30% of its time in serial code. Find (a) the maximum speedup achievable, and (b) the speedup on 8 processors.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** With $P = 0.70$ (parallel fraction):
$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - P} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.70} = \frac{1}{0.30} = 3.333$$

**Part (b):** With $N = 8$:
$$S(8) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.70) + \dfrac{0.70}{8}}$$

Compute the parallel term:
$$\frac{0.70}{8} = 0.0875$$

Add the serial term:
$$0.30 + 0.0875 = 0.3875$$

Final speedup:
$$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.3875} \approx 2.581$$

## 3.2 Derivation: RAID Capacity Formulas

### 3.2.1 RAID 0 (Striping, No Redundancy)
Data is split into strips across $N$ disks. No parity, no mirroring. Usable capacity:
$$C_{0} = N \times D$$
where $D$ = size of one disk. **Fault tolerance: None.**

### 3.2.2 RAID 1 (Mirroring)
Every disk is duplicated. Usable capacity:
$$C_{1} = \frac{N \times D}{2} = D \cdot \frac{N}{2}$$
With $N$ disks (assuming pairs of mirrors), effective capacity is $N/2$ disks worth. **Fault tolerance: 1 disk per mirror pair.**

### 3.2.3 RAID 5 (Striping with Single Parity)
Data and parity are striped across $N$ disks. One disk's worth of space is reserved for parity:
$$C_{5} = (N - 1) \times D$$
**Fault tolerance: 1 disk failure.**

### 3.2.4 RAID 6 (Striping with Double Parity)
Two independent parity blocks (often P and Q) per stripe. Two disks' worth of space is used for parity:
$$C_{6} = (N - 2) \times D$$
**Fault tolerance: 2 simultaneous disk failures.**

### 3.2.5 Numerical Example (RAID Calculation)

**Problem:** A server uses 6 disks of 1 TB each. Compute the effective storage capacity in (a) RAID 0, (b) RAID 5, and (c) RAID 6.

**Solution:**

Given: $N = 6$, $D = 1\text{ TB}$.

**Part (a) — RAID 0:**
$$C_{0} = 6 \times 1\text{ TB} = 6\text{ TB}$$

**Part (b) — RAID 5:**
$$C_{5} = (6 - 1) \times 1\text{ TB} = 5\text{ TB}$$

**Part (c) — RAID 6:**
$$C_{6} = (6 - 2) \times 1\text{ TB} = 4\text{ TB}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection Guide for Architecture

| Workload Profile | Recommended Architecture | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Single-user desktop | Multicore | Cost-effective, low power |
| High-frequency trading | SMP (low-latency) | Deterministic memory access |
| Big-data analytics | Distributed cluster | Massive horizontal scaling |
| Banking backend | Active-Passive HA Cluster | Zero-downtime requirement |
| Scientific simulation | Parallel Cluster + NUMA | Tight inner-loop communication |
| Web application front-end | Load-balancing Cluster | Even distribution of HTTP requests |

## 3.4 System Availability Derivation

**Problem:** A server has $MTTF = 30000$ hours and $MTTR = 2$ hours. Compute the availability.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the availability formula:**
$$A = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$A = \frac{30000}{30000 + 2} = \frac{30000}{30002} \approx 0.99993$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage:**
$$A \approx 99.993\%$$

**Step 4 — Downtime per year:**
$$T_{\text{down}} = (1 - A) \times 8760 \text{ hours} = 0.00007 \times 8760 \approx 0.6 \text{ hours/year}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Symmetric Multiprocessor (SMP) Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    CPU1[CPU 1]
    CPU2[CPU 2]
    CPU3[CPU 3]
    CPU4[CPU 4]
    BUS[Shared System Bus / Interconnect]
    RAM[Shared Main Memory]
    IOA[I/O Subsystem A]
    IOB[I/O Subsystem B]
    OS[Single OS Image]

    CPU1 --- BUS
    CPU2 --- BUS
    CPU3 --- BUS
    CPU4 --- BUS
    BUS --- RAM
    BUS --- IOA
    BUS --- IOB
    OS -.- RAM
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Four identical CPUs connect to a single shared bus, which links to common main memory and I/O subsystems. One unified OS instance arbitrates access.

## 4.2 Multicore Processor Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    CHIP[Single CPU Chip / Die]
    CORE1[Core 1\nL1 I-Cache\nL1 D-Cache]
    CORE2[Core 2\nL1 I-Cache\nL1 D-Cache]
    CORE3[Core 3\nL1 I-Cache\nL1 D-Cache]
    CORE4[Core 4\nL1 I-Cache\nL1 D-Cache]
    L2[Shared L2 / L3 Cache]
    IMC[Integrated Memory Controller]
    DDR[Main Memory DDR Channel]

    CHIP --- CORE1
    CHIP --- CORE2
    CHIP --- CORE3
    CHIP --- CORE4
    CORE1 --- L2
    CORE2 --- L2
    CORE3 --- L2
    CORE4 --- L2
    L2 --- IMC
    IMC --- DDR
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Four cores are etched on one silicon die. Each has private L1; they share on-chip L2/L3 cache; the integrated memory controller (IMC) drives the off-chip DDR channel.

## 4.3 NUMA Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    NODE1[NUMA Node 1]
    NODE2[NUMA Node 2]
    NODE3[NUMA Node 3]
    NODE4[NUMA Node 4]
    INT1[Interconnect]
    INT2[Interconnect]
    INT3[Interconnect]
    NET[NUMA Fabric / Crossbar]
    OSK[NUMA-Aware OS Kernel]

    NODE1 --- INT1
    NODE2 --- INT2
    NODE3 --- INT3
    INT1 --- NET
    INT2 --- NET
    INT3 --- NET
    OSK -.- NET

    subgraph S1[Node 1]
        C1A[CPU 1A]
        C1B[CPU 1B]
        M1[Local Memory]
        C1A --- M1
        C1B --- M1
    end
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each NUMA node has local CPUs and local memory. The crossbar fabric allows remote access. The OS kernel must be NUMA-aware to keep threads and memory local.

## 4.4 Clustered System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    LB[Load Balancer / Heartbeat Manager]
    N1[Cluster Node 1\nCPU RAM OS Disk]
    N2[Cluster Node 2\nCPU RAM OS Disk]
    N3[Cluster Node 3\nCPU RAM OS Disk]
    SAN[Shared Storage SAN]
    NET[High-Speed LAN / Interconnect]
    CLI[Clients / End Users]

    LB --- NET
    NET --- N1
    NET --- N2
    NET --- N3
    N1 --- SAN
    N2 --- SAN
    N3 --- SAN
    CLI --> LB
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Three independent nodes, each with its own OS and storage, are linked by a LAN. They share access to a common SAN. A load balancer / heartbeat manager distributes work and detects failures.

## 4.5 RAID Architecture (RAID 5 Layout)

```mermaid
graph LR
    D1[Disk 1\nBlock 0]
    D2[Disk 2\nBlock 1]
    D3[Disk 3\nBlock 2]
    D4[Disk 4\nParity P0]
    E1[Disk 1\nBlock 3]
    E2[Disk 2\nBlock 4]
    E3[Disk 3\nParity P1]
    E4[Disk 4\nBlock 5]
    F1[Disk 1\nParity P2]
    F2[Disk 2\nBlock 6]
    F3[Disk 3\nBlock 7]
    F4[Disk 4\nBlock 8]

    D1 -.- D2
    D2 -.- D3
    D3 -.- D4
    D4 -.- E1
    E1 -.- E2
    E2 -.- E3
    E3 -.- E4
    E4 -.- F1
    F1 -.- F2
    F2 -.- F3
    F3 -.- F4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Four disks arranged in three stripes. In each stripe, data blocks are striped across three disks and parity rotates on the fourth. One disk can fail without data loss because the parity block allows reconstruction.

## 4.6 Comparison: Cluster vs Distributed

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Modern Architecture Family]
    B[Tightly Coupled\nSMP, Multicore, NUMA]
    C[Loosely Coupled\nCluster, Distributed]
    A --> B
    A --> C
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Modern system architectures split broadly into tightly coupled (shared-memory, low latency) and loosely coupled (message passing, network-mediated) categories.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define Symmetric Multiprocessing (SMP). List any two advantages and one disadvantage of SMP systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A Symmetric Multiprocessor (SMP) system is a multiprocessor computer architecture in which **two or more identical processing units** share a **single shared main memory**, a **single operating system instance**, and **peripheral devices**, and are interconnected via a shared bus or crossbar. All processors are peers and can perform all functions including I/O control.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **High availability** — if one CPU fails, the system continues running on the remaining processors.
2. **Dynamic load balancing** — work is automatically distributed to idle processors.

**Disadvantage (any one):**
1. **Memory and bus contention** limits scalability beyond a small number of CPUs.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Compare NUMA and UMA architectures in terms of memory access time and scalability.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | UMA (Uniform Memory Access) | NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory access time** | Identical for all CPUs (latency independent of memory location) | Varies — local access is fast, remote access is slower |
| **Scalability** | Limited (typically 16–32 CPUs) | High (scales to hundreds or thousands of CPUs) |
| **Example** | Single-socket SMP server | Multi-socket EPYC/Xeon server with QPI/IF fabric |

**Conclusion:** UMA is simpler but does not scale; NUMA scales by partitioning memory but the OS must be locality-aware to extract performance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Module question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** between Question A and Question B. Both A and B are presented here as **two independent alternative 14-mark questions**, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Symmetric Multiprocessor (SMP) system. Discuss its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (2 marks):** An SMP system has multiple identical processors sharing a common physical memory, I/O subsystem, and a single OS instance. All processors are equal — no master-slave relationship.

**2. Architecture (3 marks):**
- $k$ identical CPUs (typically 2, 4, 8, 16) connected via a **shared high-speed bus** or **crossbar switch**.
- A single **shared main memory** accessible by all CPUs at equal latency (UMA).
- Common **I/O subsystem** (disk controllers, network cards) attached to the bus.
- A **single copy of the OS** maintains process/thread queues, scheduler, and synchronization primitives.
- Hardware-supported **cache coherency** (e.g., MESI protocol) keeps per-CPU caches consistent with main memory.

**3. Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1).**

**4. Advantages (1 mark, any two):**
- Transparent to applications — existing uni-processor code runs unchanged.
- Dynamic load balancing improves throughput.
- Failure of one CPU does not halt the system.

**5. Limitations (1 mark, any two):**
- **Bus saturation / memory contention** as the number of CPUs grows.
- **Cache coherency traffic** overhead.
- Poor scalability beyond ~16–32 CPUs in pure UMA form.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain NUMA architecture. How does the OS handle NUMA-aware scheduling?**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (2 marks):** **NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access)** is a multiprocessor memory design where memory access time **depends on the memory's location relative to the processor**. Each processor has fast **local memory** and slower **remote memory** attached to other nodes, all sharing a single global address space.

**2. Hardware Structure (2 marks):**
- The system is divided into **nodes** (CPU + local memory + I/O).
- Nodes are linked by a high-speed **interconnect** (Intel QPI, AMD Infinity Fabric).
- **Directory-based cache coherency** maintains a globally consistent view.
- Memory accesses to a remote node traverse the interconnect, costing 2–3× the local access time.

**3. NUMA-Aware OS Scheduling (3 marks):**
- The kernel maintains a **node affinity** map of which memory pages belong to which node.
- The scheduler uses the **"first-touch" policy**: a page is allocated on the node that first writes to it.
- The **scheduler attempts to keep a thread on the same node as its working set** to maximize local memory access.
- Linux provides APIs like `numactl`, `sched_setaffinity`, and per-node memory balancing threads (the `kswapd` and `numa_balancing` features).
- Periodic **page migration** moves pages toward the node where they are most frequently accessed.
- **Memory interleaving** may be used as a fallback to spread pages evenly when locality is unknown.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Differentiate between tightly coupled and loosely coupled multiprocessor systems. With examples, explain clustered systems and their configurations.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Tightly Coupled (2 marks):** Processors share memory and a single clock. Communication uses shared variables and synchronization primitives. Examples: SMP, Multicore, NUMA.

**2. Loosely Coupled (2 marks):** Each processor has its own memory and I/O. Communication uses **message passing** across a network. Examples: Clusters, Distributed systems.

**3. Clustered Systems Definition (1 mark):** A cluster is a collection of **standalone computers** (nodes) interconnected by a LAN that cooperate to deliver a unified service. Each node has its own CPU, RAM, OS, and disk.

**4. Cluster Configurations (2 marks):**
- **Active-Active (Symmetric):** All nodes run the workload. A failure redistributes work. E.g., load-balancing web cluster.
- **Active-Passive (Asymmetric / Hot-Standby):** One node runs, the other is idle but ready. Used for high-availability. E.g., DB cluster.
- **High-Availability (HA) Cluster:** Uses **heartbeat** signals between nodes; on heartbeat loss, standby takes over. Targets 99.999% uptime.
- **Load-Balancing Cluster:** Front-end dispatcher (e.g., LVS, HAProxy) spreads requests to least-loaded node.
- **Parallel Cluster:** Nodes cooperate via message passing to run a single parallel job. E.g., MPI on HPC clusters.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain RAID levels 0, 1, 5, and 6 with neat diagrams. Compute the usable capacity for each level if 6 disks of 500 GB each are used.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. RAID 0 — Striping (1 mark):** Data is split into strips distributed across all disks.
- **Pros:** Maximum performance, full capacity.
- **Cons:** **No fault tolerance** — one disk failure loses all data.
- **Usable capacity:** $C_0 = N \times D = 6 \times 500 = 3000$ GB

**2. RAID 1 — Mirroring (1.5 marks):** Every block is duplicated on a mirror disk.
- **Pros:** Excellent read performance, simple recovery.
- **Cons:** 50% storage overhead.
- **Usable capacity:** $C_1 = (N/2) \times D = 3 \times 500 = 1500$ GB

**3. RAID 5 — Striping with Single Parity (1.5 marks):** Data and rotating parity stripes across all $N$ disks. One disk's worth of space is consumed by parity.
- **Pros:** Good balance of capacity, performance, and fault tolerance.
- **Cons:** Write penalty (4 I/O operations per logical write — read old data, read old parity, write new data, write new parity).
- **Fault tolerance:** 1 disk failure.
- **Usable capacity:** $C_5 = (N - 1) \times D = 5 \times 500 = 2500$ GB

**4. RAID 6 — Striping with Double Parity (1.5 marks):** Two independent parity blocks (P and Q) computed using different algorithms (e.g., Reed-Solomon).
- **Pros:** Tolerates 2 simultaneous disk failures.
- **Cons:** Higher write penalty than RAID 5.
- **Usable capacity:** $C_6 = (N - 2) \times D = 4 \times 500 = 2000$ GB

**5. Summary Table (1.5 marks):**

| RAID | Disks | Capacity Formula | Usable (500 GB × 6) | Fault Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 6 | $6D$ | 3000 GB | 0 |
| 1 | 6 (3 pairs) | $3D$ | 1500 GB | 1 per pair |
| 5 | 6 | $5D$ | 2500 GB | 1 disk |
| 6 | 6 | $4D$ | 2000 GB | 2 disks |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams**
>
> 1. **Confusing SMP with Multicore:** SMP is a **board-level** architecture with separate CPU chips; Multicore places multiple cores on **one chip**. Examiners will deduct for treating them as identical.
> 2. **Wrong RAID capacity formulas:** Students frequently write $C_5 = N \times D$ for RAID 5, forgetting the **parity disk overhead**. Use $(N-1)D$.
> 3. **Amdahl's Law sign error:** The serial fraction is $(1 - P)$, **not** $P$. Wrong placement gives incorrect speedup.
> 4. **Confusing Cluster and Distributed:** A **cluster** is **LAN-coupled**; a **distributed system** can span **WANs and the internet**. Mixing the two loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the OS's role:** Modern architectures are not just hardware — the **OS scheduling, cache coherency protocol, and NUMA balancing** are all fair game for 14-mark questions.
> 6. **No diagram in long-answer:** 14-mark questions without a diagram typically lose **2–3 marks** even if the content is correct.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Modern System Architecture (KTU Module 4)**
>
> - **SMP** = multiple identical CPUs + shared memory + single OS. All peers.
> - **Multicore** = multiple cores on **one die**, sharing on-chip cache.
> - **Hyper-Threading** = one physical core presents **two (or more) logical CPUs** to the OS.
> - **NUMA** = local memory is fast, remote memory is slow. OS uses **node-affinity scheduling** to keep threads near their memory.
> - **UMA** = uniform memory access time; **scales poorly**.
> - **Clustered** = loose coupling via LAN; each node is a full system. Uses **heartbeat** for failure detection.
> - **Distributed** = LAN/WAN; **no shared memory**; communicates only by **message passing**.
> - **RAID 0** = striping, **no redundancy**, full capacity.
> - **RAID 1** = mirroring, capacity halved.
> - **RAID 5** = striping + single rotating parity, can survive 1 disk failure, capacity $(N-1)D$.
> - **RAID 6** = double parity, survives 2 failures, capacity $(N-2)D$.
> - **Availability** $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$.
> - **MTBF** $= MTTF + MTTR$.
> - **Amdahl's Law** $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + P/N}$, max $S = \dfrac{1}{1-P}$.
> - **Five-nines** $= 99.999\%$ availability $\approx 5.26$ minutes downtime per year.
> - **K**ey terms to memorize verbatim: **cache coherency**, **load balancing**, **fail-over**, **heartbeat**, **transparent migration**, **non-uniform memory access**, **striping**, **mirroring**, **parity**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmed I/O
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programmed I/O

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Programmed I/O** is a **synchronous, CPU-driven data transfer mechanism** in which the processor (CPU) executes every instruction required to move a single data item between a peripheral device and main memory. The CPU issues an I/O command to the device, then **continuously polls (busy-waits)** the device's status register until the operation completes. Because the CPU is fully occupied throughout the entire transfer, no other useful computation can occur in parallel.

The term *programmed* emphasizes that the I/O operation is governed entirely by a **program (or sequence of instructions) executed by the CPU** — there is no hardware interrupt, no DMA controller, and no offloading of work. The OS implements this via three primitive I/O instructions exposed to user processes: **read, write, and a status check** (often `test_io`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Board Phrase:** "In *Programmed I/O*, the CPU is responsible for executing the entire data transfer, byte by byte, and remains *busy* in a tight polling loop until the device signals completion."

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **standing in a kitchen waiting for a cup of tea to boil on a gas stove**. Instead of doing anything else, you simply *stare at the kettle every 2 seconds* and ask, *"Is it done yet? Is it done yet?"*. You do not step away to read a book, answer a call, or check your phone. Your entire attention is locked on the kettle.

This is exactly how the CPU behaves in **Programmed I/O**:

- **You (the CPU)** are the only worker.
- **The kettle (the I/O device)** is a slow mechanical/electronic component.
- **The repeated question ("Is it done yet?")** is the **polling** of the status register.
- **Pouring the tea (reading/writing data)** happens the instant you confirm the kettle is ready.

> [!TIP]
> The kettle analogy perfectly captures the **CPU wastage problem**: an extremely fast processor is forced to wait on an extremely slow device, and it cannot multitask.

---

### 1.3 Important Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Busy-Wait** | CPU executes a loop doing nothing useful until a condition becomes true. |
| **Polling** | Repeatedly reading a device's status register. |
| **Status Register** | A hardware register in the I/O device that holds flags like `BUSY`, `READY`, `ERROR`. |
| **I/O Instruction Set** | A set of CPU instructions (`IN`, `OUT` on x86; `READ`, `WRITE` on generic architectures) that target device registers. |
| **Synchronous I/O** | The CPU *waits* for the operation to complete before continuing. |
| **Throughput** | Volume of work per unit time — drastically reduced in Programmed I/O. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Step Operational Logic of Programmed I/O

The transfer of a single data word (e.g., one byte or one word) involves **three discrete steps** that are repeated in a *tight loop* until the entire data block is transferred. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *list and explain* these three steps — they are the most examinable unit of this topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Steps of Programmed I/O (must be memorised verbatim):**
> 1. **Request / Command Phase** — The CPU writes a control word into the device's command register to initiate the I/O operation (e.g., "read next sector", "send next byte to printer").
> 2. **Polling / Wait Phase** — The CPU reads the device's status register **continuously** in a busy-wait loop until a specific flag (usually `READY` or `DONE`) is set by the device hardware.
> 3. **Data Transfer Phase** — The CPU reads data from the device's data register (input) or writes data to it (output). The status flag is then reset, and the loop repeats for the next byte/word.

---

### 2.2 Structured Operational Walkthrough

Consider a CPU reading an 8 KB block from a disk into memory using Programmed I/O. The following sequence occurs:

- **T₀** : The OS issues the command *read block* to the disk controller's *command register*.
- **T₁ → Tₙ** : The CPU enters a **polling loop**. The disk's *status register* is read thousands of times per second. The flag `READY=0` for several milliseconds while the mechanical disk seeks and rotates.
- **Tₙ** : The disk raises `READY=1`. The CPU reads **one** byte/word from the *data register* and stores it in memory.
- **Tₙ → Tₙ+δ** : The CPU writes to the command register to request the *next* byte. The flag drops to `READY=0` again. The polling restarts.
- **Repeat** until all 8 × 1024 = **8192 bytes** are transferred.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often write that the CPU reads the *status register once* and waits. **It polls the status register hundreds, even thousands, of times per byte** — this is the defining behavioural trait of Programmed I/O.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Programmed I/O is fundamentally a *time-cost* problem. KTU frequently asks students to compute the **CPU time wasted** in polling. The following relations are board-exam gold.

Let:
- $t_{\text{device}}$ = time taken by the **device** to deliver one unit of data (seconds).
- $t_{\text{CPU\_poll}}$ = time taken by the CPU to execute **one iteration** of the polling loop (seconds).
- $n$ = number of iterations of the polling loop per data unit.
- $T_{\text{transfer}}$ = total time spent in Programmed I/O for the whole block.
- $N$ = number of data units (bytes/words) in the block.

**Number of polling iterations per data unit:**

$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{t_{\text{device}}}{t_{\text{CPU\_poll}}} \right\rceil
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER use the vertical pipe `|` inside a markdown table row.** When you write the ceiling function inline, use the LaTeX syntax `\lceil ... \rceil` (as shown above) or `\left\lceil ... \right\rceil` to keep your table cells from breaking. The KTU valuation script will not parse broken tables.

**Total CPU time wasted in polling the entire block:**

$$
T_{\text{wasted}} = N \cdot n \cdot t_{\text{CPU\_poll}}
$$

**Effective fraction of CPU time spent *not* doing useful work:**

$$
\eta_{\text{busy}} = \frac{T_{\text{wasted}}}{T_{\text{transfer}}} = \frac{N \cdot n \cdot t_{\text{CPU\_poll}}}{N \cdot (n \cdot t_{\text{CPU\_poll}} + t_{\text{device}})}
$$

**CPU time spent on *useful* data movement:**

$$
T_{\text{useful}} = N \cdot t_{\text{move}}
$$

where $t_{\text{move}}$ is the negligible cost of one `IN` or `OUT` instruction (≈ a few nanoseconds).

---

### 2.4 Comparison Table — Programmed I/O vs. Other I/O Strategies

| Property | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU involvement | **Constant (polling)** | **Only on interrupt** | **Only at start & end** |
| Hardware complexity | **Lowest** | Moderate | Highest |
| CPU wastage | **Very high** | Low | Very low |
| Suitable for | Slow, simple devices (e.g., keyboard, simple sensors) | Moderate-speed devices (e.g., network cards) | High-throughput devices (e.g., disk, tape) |
| Latency | Low (no interrupt overhead) | Slight overhead per byte | Lowest for large blocks |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although pure Programmed I/O is rarely used in modern desktops/servers, the principle survives in many practical scenarios:

- **Embedded systems & microcontrollers** (8051, PIC, early ARM Cortex-M projects) frequently use busy-wait I/O because interrupts add complexity and the CPU has nothing else to do.
- **GPU register programming & MMIO device drivers** often read status registers in tight loops where the latency must be deterministic.
- **Boot firmware (BIOS/UEFI)** uses Programmed I/O to read the very first disk sectors, because the DMA controller may not yet be initialised.
- **Single-tasking industrial controllers** (e.g., a washing machine microcontroller) where there is literally no other process to schedule.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — CPU Time Wasted in Programmed I/O

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark style problem**. You are given the device speed, the loop iteration time, and the block size. You must compute: (i) the number of poll iterations per byte, (ii) the total CPU time wasted, and (iii) the percentage of CPU time wasted.

**Problem Statement:**

A data-acquisition board produces one byte every **$t_{\text{device}} = 10\,\mu\text{s}$**. The CPU executes one iteration of the Programmed I/O polling loop in **$t_{\text{CPU\_poll}} = 200\,\text{ns}$**. A block of **$N = 4096$** bytes must be transferred.

**(a)** Compute the number of polling iterations per data byte.
**(b)** Compute the total CPU time spent in polling for the entire block.
**(c)** Compute the percentage of CPU time wasted in polling versus useful data transfer.
**(d)** Briefly comment on the suitability of Programmed I/O here.

---

#### (a) Number of polling iterations per data byte

$$
n \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{t_{\text{device}}}{t_{\text{CPU\_poll}}} \right\rceil
$$

$$
n \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{10 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}}{200 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s}} \right\rceil
$$

$$
n \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-7}} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil 50 \right\rceil
$$

$$
\boxed{n = 50 \text{ iterations per byte}}
$$

> **Logic:** Each byte requires $10\,\mu\text{s}$ to be produced by the device. The CPU can check the status register every $200\,\text{ns}$. So in the time the device produces one byte, the CPU can poll $\dfrac{10\,\mu\text{s}}{200\,\text{ns}} = 50$ times.

---

#### (b) Total CPU time spent in polling the whole block

$$
T_{\text{wasted}} \;=\; N \cdot n \cdot t_{\text{CPU\_poll}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{wasted}} \;=\; 4096 \times 50 \times 200 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{wasted}} \;=\; 4096 \times 50 \times 2 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{wasted}} \;=\; 4096 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{s} \;=\; 4.096 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{wasted}} \approx 40.96\,\text{ms}}
$$

---

#### (c) Total transfer time and CPU wastage percentage

The total wall-clock time of the transfer is:

$$
T_{\text{transfer}} \;=\; N \cdot t_{\text{device}} \;=\; 4096 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} \;=\; 40.96\,\text{ms}
$$

The CPU wastage fraction is:

$$
\eta_{\text{waste}} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{wasted}}}{T_{\text{transfer}}} \;=\; \frac{40.96\,\text{ms}}{40.96\,\text{ms}} \;=\; 1.0
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{waste}} = 100\%}
$$

> **Logic:** Since the CPU polls **50 times** during the time the device produces **1 byte**, the CPU is in the polling loop for the *entire* duration of the transfer. **The CPU performs zero useful computation** during the I/O operation.

---

#### (d) Comment

Programmed I/O is acceptable for *very slow* devices (e.g., keyboards, switches) but is **catastrophically inefficient** for any device faster than the CPU can poll. For this acquisition board, an **interrupt-driven** or **DMA-based** scheme is mandatory if the CPU must perform other tasks concurrently.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Edge Cases KTU Loves to Test

| Scenario | Expected CPU Behaviour | Expected Wastage |
|---|---|---|
| $t_{\text{device}} \gg t_{\text{CPU\_poll}}$ (slow device) | Many polls per byte | Very high |
| $t_{\text{device}} \ll t_{\text{CPU\_poll}}$ (fast device) | Possible data loss / overrun | Moderate but data is lost |
| $t_{\text{device}} = t_{\text{CPU\_poll}}$ (matched) | One poll per byte | $\approx 100\%$ |
| Multiple devices sharing the CPU | CPU must poll all devices in a round-robin | Multiplicative wastage |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a problem gives you *multiple devices* and asks you to compute the *worst-case polling cycle time*, use the formula $T_{\text{cycle}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} t_{\text{CPU\_poll},\,i}$ where $k$ is the number of devices. This is essentially the foundation of the **polled I/O scheduler** in real-time embedded systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Programmed I/O Data Path

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of Programmed I/O. It maps the *interactions* between the CPU, the I/O device's three internal registers (command, status, data), and main memory. This is the most KTU-board-friendly representation — it shows the data flow without trying to draw a physical chip layout.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU Core"] --> B["Device Command Register"]
    A --> C["Device Status Register"]
    A --> D["Device Data Register"]
    E["Main Memory Buffer"] <--> A
    D <--> F["Peripheral Hardware e.g. Disk, Keyboard, Sensor"]
    G["OS Kernel I/O Module"] --> A

    subgraph Polling_Loop
        C --> A
        A --> H{"Status Flag READY equals 1?"}
        H -- NO --> C
        H -- YES --> I["Read or Write Data Register"]
        I --> J["Reset Status Flag"]
        J --> K{"All bytes transferred?"}
        K -- NO --> B
        K -- YES --> L["Return to User Process"]
    end
```

**How to read this diagram (write this in your exam):**

- The **CPU** is the only active worker.
- It writes a command into the **Command Register** to start a transfer.
- It loops on the **Status Register** until the device sets `READY=1`.
- The moment `READY=1`, the CPU reads/writes one data unit via the **Data Register** and stores it in **Main Memory**.
- The flag is reset, the loop checks if more bytes are pending, and the cycle repeats.

---

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Three-Step Interaction per Byte

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU
    participant DEV as I/O Device
    participant MEM as Main Memory

    loop For each byte or word
        CPU->>DEV: 1. Write command to command register
        loop Polling Loop
            CPU->>DEV: 2. Read status register
            DEV-->>CPU: Status = NOT READY
        end
        DEV-->>CPU: Status = READY
        CPU->>DEV: 3a. Read from data register (INPUT)
        DEV-->>CPU: data byte
        CPU->>MEM: 3b. Store byte in memory buffer
    end

    CPU->>MEM: Final: increment buffer pointer
```

**Board Answer Template:** Use the three labels **"1. Command → 2. Polling → 3. Data Transfer"** exactly as shown. Examiners award marks for using these *exact phrases* in the correct order.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
