### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

Define **Programmed I/O**. Why is it called *synchronous* I/O?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Programmed I/O is a data-transfer technique in which the **CPU directly controls** the entire I/O operation by executing instructions that read or write the device's registers. The CPU issues a command, then enters a **busy-wait loop**, repeatedly reading the device's status register until the device signals that it is ready to transfer data. Only then does the CPU move one data unit between the device and memory.

It is called **synchronous** because the CPU's program execution is **synchronised with the device's data production rate** — the CPU cannot proceed past the polling loop until the device becomes ready, and the device cannot deliver data without the CPU actively retrieving it. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for busy-wait explanation, 1 for synchronous nature.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

List the **three steps** involved in transferring a single byte using Programmed I/O.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Command Phase:** The CPU writes a control word into the device's **command register** to initiate the I/O operation (e.g., "read next byte").
2. **Polling Phase:** The CPU reads the device's **status register** in a tight loop until the `READY` flag is set by the device hardware.
3. **Data Transfer Phase:** The CPU reads the data byte from the **data register** (input operation) or writes a byte to it (output operation), and the flag is reset for the next iteration. *(3 marks — 1 mark per step.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part B question has an **internal choice**. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** — the student answers *either one full set*. Each is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the operation of **Programmed I/O**. Clearly mark the role of the CPU, the device's command, status, and data registers, and explain the three phases of operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Programmed I/O is a CPU-centric I/O technique where the processor executes every instruction required to move data between an I/O device and main memory. The block diagram has three principal actors — the **CPU**, the **I/O device (with three registers)**, and **main memory** — connected via the system bus.

**Phase 1 — Command Phase:**
The CPU fetches and executes an *I/O command instruction* (e.g., `OUT` to the command port). This writes a control word (such as "read next sector") into the device's **command register**. The device interprets the command and starts its internal operation — e.g., a disk begins seeking, a printer starts moving the print head.

**Phase 2 — Polling Phase:**
The CPU reads the device's **status register** in a tight `read-test-branch` loop. Typical status flags include `BUSY`, `READY`, and `ERROR`. The loop terminates when `READY=1`, indicating the device has data ready (input) or is ready to accept data (output). This is the famous **busy-wait** loop.

**Phase 3 — Data Transfer Phase:**
The CPU executes a single `IN` or `OUT` instruction to read from / write to the device's **data register**, and stores the byte in a memory buffer. The status flag is cleared, and the loop returns to Phase 1 for the next byte until the entire block is transferred.

**Conclusion:** Programmed I/O is simple and requires no special hardware, but it wastes enormous CPU cycles because the processor is locked in the polling loop and cannot execute any other process.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for diagram, 2 for command phase, 2 for polling phase, 1 for data transfer phase.)*

---

**(b)** A character printer can print **$5$ characters per second**. The CPU executes the Programmed I/O polling loop in **$t_{\text{CPU\_poll}} = 100\,\mu\text{s}$**. A line of **$80$ characters** is to be printed.

Compute:
- (i) the number of polling iterations per character,
- (ii) the total CPU time spent polling the line,
- (iii) the percentage of CPU time wasted in polling. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given data:

- Printer speed: $5$ characters/second $\Rightarrow$ $t_{\text{device}} = \dfrac{1}{5} = 0.2\,\text{s} = 200{,}000\,\mu\text{s}$ per character.
- $t_{\text{CPU\_poll}} = 100\,\mu\text{s}$ per iteration.
- $N = 80$ characters.

**(i) Number of polling iterations per character:**

$$
n \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{t_{\text{device}}}{t_{\text{CPU\_poll}}} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{200{,}000\,\mu\text{s}}{100\,\mu\text{s}} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil 2000 \right\rceil
$$

$$
\boxed{n = 2000 \text{ iterations per character}}
$$

**(ii) Total CPU time spent polling the entire line:**

$$
T_{\text{wasted}} \;=\; N \cdot n \cdot t_{\text{CPU\_poll}} \;=\; 80 \times 2000 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{wasted}} \;=\; 160{,}000 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} \;=\; 0.16\,\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{wasted}} = 0.16\,\text{s} = 160\,\text{ms}}
$$

**(iii) Percentage of CPU time wasted:**

Total transfer time for the line:

$$
T_{\text{transfer}} \;=\; N \cdot t_{\text{device}} \;=\; 80 \times 0.2\,\text{s} \;=\; 16\,\text{s}
$$

Wastage percentage:

$$
\eta_{\text{waste}} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{wasted}}}{T_{\text{transfer}}} \times 100\% \;=\; \frac{0.16}{16} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{waste}} = 1\%}
$$

**Conclusion:** For a *very slow* device like a 5-cps printer, the CPU wastage is only 1%, which is acceptable — Programmed I/O is suitable here. *(Marks: 2 for (i), 3 for (ii), 2 for (iii).)*

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Programmed I/O** and **Interrupt-Driven I/O** under the following heads: (i) CPU involvement, (ii) hardware complexity, (iii) CPU wastage, (iv) suitability for high-speed devices. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Head of Comparison | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) CPU involvement** | CPU is **fully occupied** in a busy-wait loop until the device is ready. CPU polls status register **continuously**. | CPU **issues a command and continues** with other work. The device raises an **interrupt** when ready; CPU handles the interrupt via an **ISR (Interrupt Service Routine)**. |
| **(ii) Hardware complexity** | **Minimal** — only command, status, and data registers needed in the device. | **Moderate** — requires an **interrupt controller** (e.g., 8259 PIC, APIC), interrupt request lines, and a vector table in the OS. |
| **(iii) CPU wastage** | **Very high** — CPU wastes hundreds to thousands of cycles polling per byte. | **Low** — CPU only spends time in the ISR for the few microseconds needed to transfer one byte. |
| **(iv) Suitability for high-speed devices** | **Poor** — for any device faster than the polling loop, **data loss / overrun** occurs because the CPU cannot poll fast enough. | **Good** — interrupts handle bursts and high speeds efficiently because the device waits for the CPU to service it. |

**Conclusion:** Interrupt-driven I/O is the clear winner for any non-trivial system, but Programmed I/O remains the simplest and is still used in simple microcontrollers and boot code. *(Marks: ~1.5–2 per head, 1 for the concluding statement.)*

---

**(b)** Explain with a **neat flowchart / sequence diagram** how the **CPU and an I/O device cooperate** during a Programmed I/O read of a 4 KB block. Identify clearly where CPU wastage occurs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The cooperation proceeds as follows:

1. The user process invokes a `read(fd, buffer, 4096)` system call. The OS kernel translates this to a Programmed I/O request.
2. The CPU writes a *read-block* command into the device's **command register**.
3. The device begins its internal operation (e.g., seeking the disk, rotating the platter).
4. The CPU enters a **polling loop**:
   - Read the **status register** into a CPU register.
   - Test the `READY` bit.
   - If not ready, **branch back to the read** (this is the busy-wait).
5. When `READY=1`, the CPU executes one `IN` instruction to read one byte/word from the **data register**, then writes it to `buffer[i]` in main memory and increments `i`.
6. The status register is reset by the device (or by the CPU writing a clear command).
7. Steps 4–6 repeat until all **4096 bytes** are transferred.
8. The CPU returns to the user process.

**Where CPU wastage occurs:**
- In step 4, the CPU executes *only* a read-test-branch instruction. **No useful work is performed.** For a typical disk, the device may take **5–10 ms** to deliver the first byte, during which the CPU may have executed **tens of thousands of wasted instructions**.

**Conclusion:** Programmed I/O is conceptually simple and easy to implement, but the polling loop is the single biggest source of CPU wastage. This motivates the use of **Interrupt-Driven I/O** and **DMA** in any performance-sensitive system. *(Marks: 3 for the step-by-step description, 2 for the diagram, 2 for identifying the wastage point.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - ❌ **Writing the polling loop as a one-time wait.** It is a *loop*. Use the word "repeatedly" or "continuously".
> - ❌ **Confusing command register with status register.** Command register = CPU writes; Status register = CPU reads. Data register = both directions.
> - ❌ **Omitting units** in numerical problems. $n$ is dimensionless, $T$ is in seconds, $t$ is in seconds. Always carry units through every line.
> - ❌ **Using the wrong formula** $T = N \times t_{\text{device}}$ when the question asks for CPU *polling* time. The CPU's polling time is $N \times n \times t_{\text{CPU\_poll}}$, **not** the device time.
> - ❌ **Writing "CPU is idle"** in Programmed I/O. The CPU is *busy* — it is just busy doing *nothing useful*. Use the phrase **"busy-wait"**, not "idle".
> - ❌ **Forgetting to draw the three registers** in the block diagram. Examiners explicitly check for the labels *Command Register, Status Register, Data Register*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Definition:** Programmed I/O is a **CPU-driven, synchronous** I/O method where the CPU **polls** the device's status register in a **busy-wait** loop.
- ✅ **Three Phases:** **Command → Polling → Data Transfer** (memorise this exact order).
- ✅ **Three Registers:** *Command Register (CPU→Device), Status Register (Device→CPU), Data Register (Bidirectional)*.
- ✅ **Key Formula 1:** Number of polls per byte $\,n = \left\lceil \dfrac{t_{\text{device}}}{t_{\text{CPU\_poll}}} \right\rceil$.
- ✅ **Key Formula 2:** Total CPU wastage $\,T_{\text{wasted}} = N \cdot n \cdot t_{\text{CPU\_poll}}$.
- ✅ **Key Formula 3:** Total transfer time $\,T_{\text{transfer}} = N \cdot t_{\text{device}}$.
- ✅ **Wastage Percentage:** $\,\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{wasted}}}{T_{\text{transfer}}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Biggest Drawback:** CPU is **locked** in the polling loop and cannot run any other process — destroys parallelism.
- ✅ **Best Use Case:** *Very slow* devices (keyboard, simple sensors) or systems with no other process to run.
- ✅ **Alternatives:** **Interrupt-Driven I/O** (for moderate-speed devices) and **DMA** (for high-speed block transfers).
- ✅ **Comparison Knobs:** CPU involvement, hardware complexity, wastage, suitability — be ready to fill a 4-row comparison table.
- ✅ **Exam Buzzwords to Use:** *busy-wait, polling, status register, synchronous, CPU-bound, I/O-bound, system bus, command register, data register, ISR.*
- ✅ **Common Pitfall:** Never say "CPU waits" or "CPU is idle" — always say "**CPU is busy in a polling loop**".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Interrupt (KTU Definition):** An *interrupt* is a hardware- or software-generated asynchronous signal/event that causes the **CPU** to suspend its current execution of a program, save its current state (context), and transfer control to a predefined routine known as an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** or **Interrupt Handler**. After servicing, the CPU resumes the interrupted task.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Operating Systems framework (PCCST403 – Module 4), interrupts are classified as the **fundamental mechanism** by which an Operating System achieves:
- **I/O device coordination** (without busy-waiting / polling),
- **Trap handling** (system calls, divide-by-zero, page faults),
- **Process preemption** (timer interrupts enabling round-robin scheduling),
- **Fault/Exception recovery** (segmentation faults, protection violations).

The interruption mechanism is the *core enabler* of the OS acting as a **Resource Manager** and **Control Program**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doorbell of the CPU" 🛎️

Imagine a **chef cooking a complex recipe** (the CPU executing a user process). The chef is *deeply focused* — stirring, measuring, plating. Suddenly:

- 🚪 The **doorbell rings** (an **interrupt signal** from the I/O controller).
- 👨‍🍳 The chef **pauses**, marks down the exact step where he stopped ("Add salt next" → saved in a **notepad = context/stack**).
- 🚶 Walks to the door, **opens it, and handles the delivery** (executes the **Interrupt Service Routine**).
- 🔙 Returns to the kitchen, **reads the notepad**, and **resumes from "Add salt next"** (restores **context**, continues execution).

This is precisely how an interrupt works! The chef *does not abandon the recipe*; he **suspends** it, handles the *higher-priority event*, and then **gracefully resumes**.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Insight:** Without interrupts, the CPU would have to *continuously poll* every device ("Are you ready yet? How about now?"). This wastes **CPU cycles**. Interrupts allow the CPU to *sleep* until notified — saving power and improving throughput.

---

## 1.3 Why Interrupts Are Mandatory in Modern OS Design

| Reason | Explanation |
|--------|-------------|
| **Asynchronous I/O** | Devices like keyboards, disks, and network cards operate at vastly different speeds. Interrupts decouple the CPU from device speed. |
| **Multitasking / Preemption** | Timer interrupts forcibly switch between processes, enabling **time-sharing**. |
| **Protection & Security** | Hardware traps (illegal memory access) generate interrupts that hand control to the OS kernel. |
| **System Calls** | Software interrupts (`INT 0x80` in Linux, `syscall` in modern x86-64) bridge user-mode to kernel-mode. |

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Interrupt Analysis (KTU High-Yield Bold Terms)

- **Interrupt Latency (T_lat):** Time elapsed between the interrupt signal assertion and the start of ISR execution.
- **Interrupt Response Time (T_resp):** Time from signal assertion to ISR completion.
- **Interrupt Recovery Time:** Time to restore context and resume the interrupted task.
- **IRQ (Interrupt Request Line):** A physical/dedicated signal line used by devices to demand CPU attention.

---

## 1.5 Interrupt Mechanism — A Bird's-Eye View

```
Device Ready  →  IRQ Signal  →  Interrupt Controller (PIC/APIC)
       ↓
  CPU Finishes Current Instruction
       ↓
  Save PC, PSW, Registers  →  Jump to ISR (via IVT/IDT)
       ↓
  Execute ISR
       ↓
  Restore State  →  Resume Interrupted Program
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Interrupts (KTU Module 4 Core)

Interrupts are broadly partitioned into **three structural families**:

### 🔹 A. Hardware Interrupts (Asynchronous)
Generated by **external hardware devices** to signal the CPU about events (e.g., keyboard keypress, disk I/O completion, network packet arrival).

| Sub-Type | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Maskable Interrupts (IRQ)** | Can be ignored/delayed by setting the **Interrupt Flag (IF = 0)** in the PSW. | I/O device interrupts |
| **Non-Maskable Interrupts (NMI)** | **Cannot** be ignored — used for critical hardware failures. | Power failure, memory parity error |

### 🔹 B. Software Interrupts / Traps (Synchronous)
Generated **deliberately** by the executing program or **automatically** by the CPU on detecting an error condition.

| Sub-Type | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Program-generated (syscall)** | Explicit instruction invokes OS service. | `INT 0x21`, `SYSCALL` |
| **Trap / Exception** | Raised by CPU on exceptional condition. | Divide-by-zero, page fault |
| **Fault** | Recoverable — re-executes the instruction after handling. | Page fault (demand paging) |
| **Abort** | Non-recoverable — terminates the process. | Severe hardware error |

### 🔹 C. Timer Interrupts
Special hardware interrupts fired at **fixed intervals** by a programmable interval timer (PIT) or **Local APIC Timer**. They are the *heartbeat* of preemptive multitasking.

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme Part A questions, examiners frequently ask: *"Differentiate between a Trap and an Interrupt."* Use this classification as your answer backbone.

---

## 2.2 The Interrupt Handling Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

The interrupt mechanism is governed by a **precise sequence** of actions. Understanding each step is critical for KTU's 14-mark analytical questions.

1. **Device Asserts IRQ** → The I/O device sets its dedicated interrupt request line **HIGH**.
2. **Interrupt Controller Aggregates** → The **PIC (Programmable Interrupt Controller — Intel 8259)** or **APIC (Advanced PIC)** prioritizes and forwards the signal to the CPU's **INTR pin**.
3. **CPU Completes Current Instruction** → CPU does **NOT** interrupt mid-instruction — it waits for the instruction boundary to maintain atomicity.
4. **Interrupt Acknowledgement (INTA)** → CPU sends an acknowledge signal to the controller, requesting the **interrupt vector number**.
5. **Context Saving (Push onto Kernel Stack)** → The CPU automatically pushes the **Program Counter (PC)**, **Program Status Word (PSW)**, and sometimes other registers onto the **kernel stack**.
6. **Vector Lookup** → Using the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** in legacy BIOS mode or the **Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT)** in protected/long mode, the CPU locates the ISR address.
7. **ISR Execution** → Control transfers to the kernel-mode ISR, which services the device (e.g., reads a keystroke, transfers a disk buffer).
8. **End of Interrupt (EOI)** → ISR sends an EOI signal to the interrupt controller, re-arming it for future interrupts.
9. **Context Restoration (IRET Instruction)** → The `IRET` (Interrupt Return) instruction pops PC, PSW, and CS from the stack — resuming the original task as if nothing happened.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet 📐

The following table is your **one-stop reference** for all interrupt-related equations, boundary conditions, and timing metrics.

| Parameter / Formula | Mathematical Expression | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---------------------|------------------------|-------|---------------------------|
| **Interrupt Latency** | $T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{save}}$ | µs / ns | Must be ≤ **deadline** for hard real-time systems |
| **Interrupt Response Time** | $T_{\text{response}} = T_{\text{latency}} + T_{\text{ISR}}$ | µs / ns | Total time from signal to ISR completion |
| **Total Interrupt Overhead** | $T_{\text{overhead}} = T_{\text{latency}} + T_{\text{ISR}} + T_{\text{restore}}$ | µs / ns | Full context-switch cost of an interrupt |
| **Throughput Penalty** | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{ISR}}}{T_{\text{response}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Higher % = more time servicing interrupts |
| **Maximum Sustainable Interrupt Rate** | $f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{response}}}$ | Hz / kHz | Inverse of response time |
| **Worst-Case Latency Bound (Real-Time)** | $T_{\text{WCET}} \leq T_{\text{deadline}}$ | µs | **Critical** for hard real-time OS (RTOS) |
| **Interrupt Nesting Depth** | $N_{\text{depth}} \leq \dfrac{\text{Stack Size}}{\text{Frame Size}}$ | Levels | Limited by kernel stack memory |
| **Mean Inter-Arrival Time (Poisson)** | $\mathbb{E}[T] = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | s | For stochastic interrupt models |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Numerical problems on interrupt latency are rare, but **definition-based and architecture-based 14-mark questions** are extremely common. Memorize the **IVT/IDT** distinction — it appears almost every year.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Role of Interrupts |
|--------|-------------------|
| **Embedded Systems (ARM Cortex-M)** | NVIC (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller) provides deterministic, low-latency response in microcontrollers. |
| **Real-Time OS (RTOS — VxWorks, FreeRTOS)** | Interrupt latency bounds are *contractual*; missed deadlines = system failure (e.g., airbag controllers). |
| **Database Engines** | Disk I/O completion interrupts wake sleeping threads handling disk reads. |
| **Networking** | NIC interrupts trigger packet reception paths in kernel TCP/IP stacks. |
| **Power Management** | Wake-on-LAN uses interrupts to wake a sleeping CPU from S3 (Suspend-to-RAM). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Interrupt Response Time Decomposition

We now derive the **canonical interrupt response time equation** used by both KTU and the industry. This forms the basis of every "calculate the worst-case latency" question.

### 🎯 Objective
Express the total time between an interrupt signal's assertion and the moment the original program resumes execution, as a sum of well-defined atomic phases.

---

### Step 1 — Identify the Atomic Phases

An interrupt's lifecycle consists of **five** distinct, sequential phases:

1. **Detection Phase (T_detect)** — Time for the CPU to recognize that an interrupt has been asserted.
2. **In-Flight Instruction Completion (T_instr)** — Time for the CPU to finish the instruction currently in the pipeline.
3. **Context Save Phase (T_save)** — Time to push PC, PSW onto the kernel stack.
4. **ISR Execution Phase (T_ISR)** — Time for the Interrupt Service Routine itself.
5. **Context Restore Phase (T_restore)** — Time to pop state and resume the original task.

> **Assumption:** The interrupt controller (PIC/APIC) introduces negligible propagation delay compared to CPU phases — hence it is subsumed into $T_{\text{detect}}$.

---

### Step 2 — Write the Total Time Expression

The total time from signal assertion to program resumption is the **sum** of all phases:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{instr}} + T_{\text{save}} + T_{\text{ISR}} + T_{\text{restore}}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Group the Phases Logically

We conventionally group these into **three** higher-level quantities:

- **Interrupt Latency (T_latency):** Time from interrupt assertion → ISR start.
- **ISR Execution Time (T_ISR):** Self-explanatory.
- **Recovery Time (T_recover):** Time from ISR completion → resumption of original program.

Mathematically:

$$
T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{instr}} + T_{\text{save}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{latency}} + T_{\text{ISR}} + T_{\text{recover}}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Introduce the Standard KTU Notation

Aligning with textbook convention (Stallings, Silberschatz):

$$
T_{\text{response}} = T_{\text{latency}} + T_{\text{ISR}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{overhead}} = T_{\text{response}} + T_{\text{recover}}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Conversion Logic:** *Response time* excludes the recovery phase because it represents "service completion," whereas *overhead* is the total CPU time diverted from the user process.

---

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A CPU has a clock cycle of **2 ns**. An interrupt arrives. The phases consume the following cycles:
> - $T_{\text{detect}}$ = **5 cycles**
> - $T_{\text{instr}}$ = **8 cycles** (worst-case single instruction)
> - $T_{\text{save}}$ = **6 cycles**
> - $T_{\text{ISR}}$ = **120 cycles**
> - $T_{\text{restore}}$ = **4 cycles**
>
> **Find:** Interrupt Latency, Response Time, Total Overhead, and the maximum sustainable interrupt rate.

---

#### Step 5a — Convert Cycles to Time

Each cycle = **2 ns**.

$$
T_{\text{detect}} = 5 \times 2 \text{ ns} = 10 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{\text{instr}} = 8 \times 2 \text{ ns} = 16 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{\text{save}} = 6 \times 2 \text{ ns} = 12 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{\text{ISR}} = 120 \times 2 \text{ ns} = 240 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{\text{restore}} = 4 \times 2 \text{ ns} = 8 \text{ ns}
$$

---

#### Step 5b — Compute Latency

$$
T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{instr}} + T_{\text{save}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{latency}} = 10 \text{ ns} + 16 \text{ ns} + 12 \text{ ns} = 38 \text{ ns}
$$

---

#### Step 5c — Compute Response Time

$$
T_{\text{response}} = T_{\text{latency}} + T_{\text{ISR}} = 38 \text{ ns} + 240 \text{ ns} = 278 \text{ ns}
$$

---

#### Step 5d — Compute Total Overhead

$$
T_{\text{overhead}} = T_{\text{response}} + T_{\text{restore}} = 278 \text{ ns} + 8 \text{ ns} = 286 \text{ ns}
$$

---

#### Step 5e — Maximum Sustainable Interrupt Rate

$$
f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{response}}} = \dfrac{1}{278 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}} \approx 3.597 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
f_{\max} \approx 3.597 \text{ MHz}
$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Interrupt Performance Trade-offs

| Technique | Effect on Latency | Effect on Throughput | Use Case |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|----------|
| **Interrupt Coalescing** | ⬆ Increases (worse) | ⬆ Improves | High-speed NICs (10 Gbps+) |
| **Polling Instead of Interrupts** | ⬇ Decreases (better for predictable latency) | ⬇ Wastes CPU cycles | Real-time control loops |
| **Interrupt Nesting** | ⬇ Allows urgent interrupts first | ⬇ Slightly lower (overhead of stack ops) | Hierarchical priority devices |
| **MSI/MSI-X (Message-Signaled Interrupts)** | ⬇ Lower latency via direct lines | ⬆ Better scalability | Modern PCIe devices |
| **Bottom Halves / Tasklets (Linux)** | ⬇ ISR offloads work | ⬆ Keeps ISRs short | Network stack, disk drivers |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Interrupt Handling Architecture Flow

The diagram below traces the **complete interrupt lifecycle** — from device assertion through ISR execution to program resumption.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Device Ready / IRQ Asserted"] --> B["Interrupt Controller PIC or APIC Receives Signal"]
    B --> C{"Interrupt Enabled IF equals 1?"}
    C -- No --> Z["Masked Interrupt Ignored / Held Pending"]
    C -- Yes --> D["CPU Finishes Current Instruction"]
    D --> E["CPU Sends INTA to Controller"]
    E --> F["Controller Returns Interrupt Vector Number"]
    F --> G["Lookup IDT or IVT for ISR Address"]
    G --> H["Push PC and PSW onto Kernel Stack"]
    H --> I["Transfer Control to ISR in Kernel Mode"]
    I --> J["ISR Services Device e.g. Read Buffer"]
    J --> K["ISR Sends EOI to Controller"]
    K --> L{"More Pending Interrupts?"}
    L -- Yes --> G
    L -- No --> M["IRET Pops PC and PSW from Stack"]
    M --> N["Resume Original User Process"]

    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style I fill:#FFADAD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style J fill:#BDB2FF,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style N fill:#90BE6D,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Z fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Interrupt Classification Hierarchy

This diagram visually classifies interrupts into their **hardware/software families** — essential for KTU's "classify and explain" question type.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Interrupts"] --> P["Hardware Interrupts Async"]
    R --> Q["Software Interrupts and Traps Sync"]
    R --> T["Timer Interrupts Special Class"]

    P --> P1["Maskable IRQ"]
    P --> P2["Non Maskable NMI"]

    Q --> Q1["Program Generated Syscall"]
    Q --> Q2["Trap Exception"]
    Q --> Q3["Fault Recoverable"]
    Q --> Q4["Abort Non Recoverable"]

    T --> T1["PIT Programmable Interval Timer"]
    T --> T2["APIC Timer Local"]
    T --> T3["HPET High Precision Event Timer"]

    style R fill:#FFE66D,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P fill:#A0E7E5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Q fill:#FFAEBC,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style T fill:#B4F8C8,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Interrupt Vector Table (IVT / IDT) Lookup Mechanism

The IDT is a **kernel-resident array** mapping vector numbers to ISR addresses. The diagram illustrates how a vector number maps to an ISR handler.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CPU Receives Vector N"] --> B["Base Address of IDT from IDTR Register"]
    B --> C["Offset = N times 8 bytes in 64 bit"]
    C --> D["Read ISR Address from IDT Entry"]
    D --> E["Read CS Code Segment Selector"]
    E --> F["Load ISR Address into IP and CS"]
    F --> G["Begin ISR Execution"]

    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFADAD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style G fill:#90BE6D,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Interrupt Subsystem

This **block diagram** shows the physical and logical components cooperating to deliver an interrupt from a peripheral to the CPU.

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  INTERRUPT SUBSYSTEM (Block View)              │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  [Keyboard] [Disk Controller] [NIC]  ─── Hardware Devices
        │           │            │
        └───────────┼────────────┘
                    │  (IRQ Lines 1, 14, 11 etc.)
                    ▼
       ┌──────────────────────────────┐
       │  PIC 8259A  or  IO-APIC      │ ← Prioritizes & Multiplexes
       └──────────────────────────────┘
                    │  (Single INTR pin)
                    ▼
       ┌──────────────────────────────┐
       │          CPU Core            │
       │  ┌────────────────────────┐  │
       │  │ IDTR  →  IDT  →  ISR   │  │
       │  │ Kernel Stack (context) │  │
       │  └────────────────────────┘  │
       └──────────────────────────────┘
                    │
                    ▼
       [Interrupt Service Routine Executes]
                    │
                    ▼
       [EOI → IRET → Resume User Process]
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Define an interrupt. List the different types of interrupts.**

**Model Answer:**

> An interrupt is an asynchronous signal/event — originating from hardware or software — that temporarily suspends the CPU's current execution, transfers control to a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, and later resumes the interrupted task.

**Types of Interrupts:**

1. **Hardware Interrupts** — Generated by external devices (keyboard, disk, NIC).
2. **Software Interrupts / Traps** — Generated by program instructions (system calls) or CPU exceptions (divide-by-zero).
3. **Timer Interrupts** — Generated by the system timer at fixed intervals for preemptive scheduling.

**Sub-classification of Hardware Interrupts:**
- **Maskable** (can be disabled via IF flag)
- **Non-Maskable (NMI)** (cannot be disabled)

*(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for typed classification.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between a Trap and an Interrupt.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Interrupt | Trap |
|-----------|-----------|------|
| **Source** | External hardware (asynchronous) | Internal program / CPU (synchronous) |
| **Trigger** | Device assertion of IRQ line | Execution of `INT`/`SYSCALL` or error condition |
| **Predictability** | Asynchronous — can occur any time | Synchronous — occurs at a predictable point |
| **Examples** | Keyboard keypress, disk I/O completion | Divide-by-zero, page fault, `write()` syscall |
| **Maskable?** | Often maskable (NMI is exception) | Usually not maskable |

*(3 marks — 1.5 for table content + 1.5 for explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the interrupt handling mechanism in a computer system. Discuss the role of the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR), Interrupt Vector Table (IVT), and Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT) in detail.**

#### Part (a) — Interrupt Handling Mechanism & Diagram (7 Marks)

The interrupt handling mechanism in a modern computer system follows a **well-defined sequence** coordinated by hardware (CPU + interrupt controller) and software (OS kernel):

1. **Interrupt Request Generation:** The I/O device asserts its dedicated **IRQ line** when it requires CPU service. For example, a network card raises IRQ 11 when a packet arrives.

2. **Interrupt Controller Aggregation:** The **PIC (Programmable Interrupt Controller — Intel 8259A)** or **IO-APIC** receives the request, determines its **priority** relative to other pending requests, and forwards a single **INTR** signal to the CPU.

3. **CPU Instruction Completion:** The CPU, upon detecting the INTR signal, **finishes the currently executing instruction** before acknowledging. This guarantees **instruction-level atomicity**.

4. **Interrupt Acknowledgement (INTA):** The CPU pulses the INTA line, prompting the interrupt controller to place the **interrupt vector number** on the data bus.

5. **Context Save:** The CPU automatically pushes the **Program Counter (PC)**, **Code Segment (CS)**, and **Flags/PSW** onto the **kernel stack**, ensuring the task can be resumed seamlessly.

6. **ISR Invocation via IVT/IDT:** The CPU uses the vector number as an index into the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** — in legacy BIOS — or the **Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT)** — in protected/long mode. Each entry holds the address of the corresponding ISR.

7. **ISR Execution:** Control transfers to the kernel-mode ISR, which performs device-specific actions (e.g., transferring data, signaling the waiting process).

8. **End of Interrupt (EOI):** The ISR writes to the controller's EOI register, re-arming it for subsequent interrupts.

9. **Context Restoration (IRET):** The `IRET` instruction pops the saved state, restoring PC, CS, and PSW. Execution resumes in the original process.

```
[Device] → [IRQ Line] → [PIC/APIC] → [INTR to CPU]
              → [INTA cycle] → [Vector Lookup] → [ISR Run]
              → [EOI] → [IRET] → [Resume User Task]
```

#### Part (b) — Role of ISR, IVT, and IDT (7 Marks)

**Interrupt Service Routine (ISR):**
- A **kernel-mode function** that services the interrupting device.
- Must be **short, non-blocking, and re-entrant** to handle nested interrupts.
- In modern kernels (Linux), ISRs are split into a **top half** (urgent, runs with interrupts disabled) and a **bottom half** (deferred work via tasklets or work queues).

**Interrupt Vector Table (IVT):**
- A **fixed 1 KB table** residing at physical address **0x00000** in legacy real-mode x86 systems.
- Contains **256 entries**, each 4 bytes (2-byte offset + 2-byte segment) → points to ISR handlers.
- Used by **DOS and BIOS-level** code; obsolete in protected mode.

**Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT):**
- The **protected-mode successor** to IVT, introduced with the **Intel 80286/80386**.
- Located at the **physical address stored in the IDTR register** (variable location, kernel-chosen).
- Entries are **8-byte gate descriptors** (in 32-bit mode) or **16-byte** (in 64-bit long mode) containing:
  - ISR offset (lower 16 / 32 bits)
  - Segment selector
  - Type and attributes (Interrupt Gate, Trap Gate, Task Gate)
  - ISR offset (upper bits)
- Supports **prioritized, hierarchical handling** via gate types and DPL (Descriptor Privilege Level) checks — critical for **system call security**.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Interrupt Handling):**  
> A common mistake is **confusing IVT with IDT**. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if students state IVT exists in modern Linux/Windows. **Rule of thumb:**  
> - **IVT → Legacy / DOS / Real mode (1980s era)**  
> - **IDT → Modern OS / Protected / Long mode (Linux, Windows, macOS)**  
> Also, students often **omit the INTA cycle** — remember, the CPU does **not** automatically know the vector; it must *ask* the controller via INTA.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q: Explain the various types of interrupts. Discuss the difference between maskable and non-maskable interrupts. How does the OS handle nested interrupts?**

#### Part (a) — Types of Interrupts (7 Marks)

The Operating System handles **three primary categories** of interrupts:

**1. Hardware Interrupts (External, Asynchronous):**
- Originate from I/O devices external to the CPU.
- Triggered by physical events (e.g., key press, disk read completion).
- Routed through IRQ lines to a PIC/APIC, then forwarded to the CPU.

**2. Software Interrupts / Traps (Internal, Synchronous):**
- Generated **synchronously** when the CPU executes a specific instruction (`INT n`, `SYSCALL`) or detects an exceptional condition during execution.
- Examples: division by zero, invalid opcode, page fault, breakpoint (`INT 3`).
- Used by user programs to invoke **system calls** — the protected entry point into the kernel.

**3. Timer Interrupts:**
- Special class fired by a **programmable interval timer (PIT)** or **APIC timer**.
- Crucial for **preemptive multitasking** — the scheduler gains control at every tick.
- Enables **time slicing**, **alarm signals**, and **context-switch deadlines**.

#### Part (b) — Maskable vs Non-Maskable, and Nested Interrupts (7 Marks)

**Maskable vs Non-Maskable Interrupts:**

| Feature | Maskable Interrupt (IRQ) | Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI) |
|---------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Can be disabled?** | Yes — clear the **IF flag** in PSW | **No** — cannot be masked by software |
| **Use Case** | Normal I/O devices (keyboard, mouse, NIC) | Critical hardware failures (power loss, memory parity) |
| **Priority** | Lower; can be reordered by the controller | Highest; bypasses all masking logic |
| **Vector Line** | IRQ lines (typically 0–15 in legacy PIC) | Dedicated NMI pin on CPU |
| **Example Scenario** | Printer ready signal | Power supply failure in a server |

**Handling Nested Interrupts:**

When an ISR is already executing and a higher-priority interrupt arrives, the OS must decide:

1. **Disable Interrupts (Simplest Approach):** The ISR sets `IF = 0` upon entry. No nesting possible — but **increases worst-case latency** for high-priority events.

2. **Priority-Based Nesting (Preferred):** Each IRQ has a **priority level** (e.g., in Linux, `IRQF_DISABLED` historically disabled IRQs). If a new interrupt has **higher priority**, the ISR:
   - Saves current register state (already on kernel stack).
   - Re-enables interrupts (`STI` / `IF = 1`).
   - Invokes the higher-priority ISR (deeper into the stack).
   - On return, continues the original ISR.

3. **Stack Management:** Kernel must allocate **sufficient stack space** for the **maximum nesting depth**, else a **kernel stack overflow** occurs (a critical real-time bug).

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Nested Interrupts):**  
> Students often forget to mention the **role of kernel stack depth** in supporting nesting. Also, many confuse **priority with vector number** — vector number is the *index* in IDT; priority is a *logical ranking* assigned by the OS/controller. Examiners award full 7 marks **only if both concepts are addressed**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

> 📋 **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- 🔹 An **interrupt** is an **asynchronous** event that suspends the CPU's current task and transfers control to an **ISR**.
- 🔹 **Hardware interrupts** come from external devices; **software interrupts/traps** come from internal CPU/program events.
- 🔹 **Maskable interrupts** can be disabled via the **IF flag**; **Non-Maskable Interrupts (NMI)** cannot be ignored.
- 🔹 **Trap** = synchronous exception (e.g., divide-by-zero); **Interrupt** = asynchronous external event (e.g., I/O).
- 🔹 The CPU **finishes the current instruction** before servicing an interrupt (atomicity guarantee).
- 🔹 **Interrupt Latency** = time from signal assertion → ISR start.
- 🔹 **Interrupt Response Time** = latency + ISR execution time.
- 🔹 **IVT (Interrupt Vector Table)** is used in **legacy BIOS/real-mode**; located at fixed address **0x00000**, size **1 KB (256 × 4 bytes)**.
- 🔹 **IDT (Interrupt Descriptor Table)** is used in **modern protected/long mode**; located via the **IDTR register**; entries are 8/16-byte **gate descriptors**.
- 🔹 **EOI (End of Interrupt)** signal must be sent to the PIC/APIC after ISR completion — else the controller will not fire further interrupts on that line.
- 🔹 **IRET** is the assembly instruction that restores context and resumes the original program.
- 🔹 **Timer interrupts** enable **preemptive multitasking** — the scheduler's heartbeat.
- 🔹 **Nested interrupts** require **priority-based re-enabling** of interrupts and adequate **kernel stack depth**.
- 🔹 The formula $T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{instr}} + T_{\text{save}}$ is the **canonical decomposition**.
- 🔹 The maximum sustainable interrupt rate is $f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{response}}}$.
- 🔹 Real-time systems require **bounded interrupt latency** — missed deadlines = system failure.
- 🔹 **MSI/MSI-X** are modern PCIe message-signaled interrupts that bypass legacy IRQ lines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Device Interaction Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Device Interaction Methods?

**Device Interaction Methods** in an Operating System (OS) refer to the systematic techniques and mechanisms through which the CPU communicates with, controls, and exchanges data with peripheral (I/O) devices such as keyboards, disks, printers, and network cards. These methods form the foundation of the **I/O Subsystem**, a critical component of the kernel responsible for managing data flow between the main memory and external devices.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PCCST403 - Operating Systems, Module 4), this topic primarily covers three principal techniques for data transfer:
1. **Programmed I/O (Polling)**
2. **Interrupt-Driven I/O**
3. **Direct Memory Access (DMA)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCST403 - Module 4):** Students are expected to understand the *trade-offs* between CPU involvement, throughput, and latency in each device interaction method. This is a high-weightage conceptual topic in Part A and Part B ESE questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a busy restaurant:
- **The CPU** is the head chef who can only cook one dish at a time.
- **The I/O Device** (e.g., a slow oven) is an external helper.
- **Programmed I/O** → The chef keeps opening the oven door to check if the dish is ready (*busy waiting*).
- **Interrupt-Driven I/O** → The chef sets a timer and continues cooking; the timer *alerts* (interrupts) the chef when done.
- **DMA** → The chef assigns an assistant (*DMA controller*) to monitor the oven, freeing the chef entirely to do other work until the assistant reports completion.

This analogy helps visualize **CPU utilization** and **overhead** in each approach.

## 1.3 Types of I/O Devices

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Stallings / Silberschatz Terminology):** An I/O device is classified by its **data transfer rate**, **access method**, **data representation**, and **direction of data flow**.

| Device Class | Examples | Typical Speed | Interaction Style |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Human Interface Devices (HID)** | Keyboard, Mouse | ~100–1000 bytes/sec | Character / Event-based |
| **Storage Devices** | HDD, SSD | ~100 MB/s – 5 GB/s | Block-based |
| **Communication Devices** | NIC, Modem | ~1 Mbps – 100 Gbps | Stream / Packet-based |
| **Special-Purpose** | GPU, Sensors | Variable | Custom protocol |

## 1.4 The Core Trade-off Equation

All three interaction methods balance two competing metrics:
- **CPU Utilization (U)** = Fraction of time CPU is doing useful work.
- **I/O Latency (L)** = Time delay between request and completion.

The OS designer's job is to **maximize U** while **minimizing L**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** *Programmed I/O* has high predictability but poor CPU utilization. *Interrupt-driven I/O* improves utilization but introduces interrupt overhead. *DMA* provides the best of both, but adds hardware complexity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Device Interaction Methods — Detailed Breakdown

### 🔹 Method 1: Programmed I/O (Polling / Busy-Wait)

In **Programmed I/O**, the CPU is in complete control. It repeatedly reads the device's status register until the device becomes ready, then transfers data one word/byte at a time.

**Operational Steps:**
1. CPU issues an I/O command to the device.
2. CPU enters a **busy-wait loop**, continuously polling the status register.
3. Once the device signals *ready*, the CPU reads/writes a data word.
4. Steps 2–3 repeat until the entire data block is transferred.
5. CPU resumes the executing process.

**Pros:** Simple to implement; no interrupt hardware required.
**Cons:** Wastes CPU cycles; CPU cannot perform other tasks during transfer.

---

### 🔹 Method 2: Interrupt-Driven I/O

Here, the CPU issues an I/O request and **continues executing other instructions**. When the device is ready, it raises a hardware **interrupt** that suspends the CPU, runs an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, and resumes the previous process.

**Operational Steps:**
1. CPU issues an I/O command and **returns to other work**.
2. Device controller processes the request asynchronously.
3. Upon completion, device raises an **interrupt signal (IRQ)**.
4. CPU saves context, jumps to the **ISR**, transfers data, and returns.

**Pros:** Higher CPU utilization; supports concurrency.
**Cons:** Each word/byte transferred still requires an interrupt (high overhead for bulk transfer); complex kernel design.

---

### 🔹 Method 3: Direct Memory Access (DMA)

**DMA** offloads the entire data transfer to a dedicated hardware controller — the **DMA Controller (DMAC)**. The CPU only sets up the transfer parameters and is interrupted *once* upon completion.

**Operational Steps:**
1. CPU programs the DMAC with source address, destination address, and byte count.
2. CPU returns to execute other processes.
3. DMAC transfers data directly between device and main memory, **bypassing the CPU**.
4. DMAC raises a single interrupt when the block transfer is complete.

**Pros:** Maximum CPU utilization; ideal for block devices (disks, tapes).
**Cons:** Requires DMA-capable hardware; potential memory contention (cycle stealing).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` notation for absolute values — never use the `|` pipe inside a markdown table cell.**

| Symbol | Definition | Formula / Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_{cpu}$ | Time CPU spends on useful computation | $T_{cpu} = N \times t_{exec}$ | seconds |
| $T_{io}$ | Time spent waiting for I/O device | $T_{io} = \frac{D}{R_d}$ | seconds |
| $U_{cpu}$ | CPU Utilization (fraction) | $U_{cpu} = \frac{T_{cpu}}{T_{cpu} + T_{io}}$ | unitless (0–1) |
| $T_{total}$ | Total time for one I/O operation | $T_{total} = T_{setup} + T_{transfer} + T_{interrupt}$ | seconds |
| $D$ | Total data to transfer | $D$ (user-defined) | bytes |
| $R_d$ | Device data transfer rate | $R_d$ (device-specific) | bytes/sec |
| $N$ | Number of data words | $N = \frac{D}{w}$ (where $w$ = word size) | words |
| $O_{int}$ | Interrupt overhead per event | $O_{int}$ (constant, hardware-dependent) | cycles |
| $E_{dma}$ | DMA efficiency | $E_{dma} = 1 - \frac{t_{steal}}{T_{total}}$ | unitless (0–1) |
| $T_{cycle\_steal}$ | Time CPU is paused per DMA cycle | $\frac{w}{R_{bus}}$ | seconds |

## 2.3 Performance Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These comparisons are critical in **real-time OS design** (RTOS), **embedded systems** (automotive ECUs, IoT devices), and **high-performance computing** (HPC cluster design).

| Parameter | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Involvement** | 100% (busy) | Partial (per word) | Minimal (one interrupt per block) |
| **CPU Utilization (U_cpu)** | Low | Moderate | **High** |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| **Number of Interrupts** | 0 | $N$ (per word) | 1 (per block) |
| **Best Suited For** | Simple, low-speed devices | Medium-speed, character devices | High-speed, block devices |
| **Latency** | Predictable | Moderate | Lowest (for bulk) |
| **Example Use** | Old microcontrollers | Keyboard, serial port | Disk, NIC, GPU |

## 2.4 The I/O Performance Trade-off

The general **throughput equation** for an I/O subsystem is:

$$ \text{Throughput} = \frac{D}{T_{total}} = \frac{D}{T_{setup} + T_{transfer} + T_{overhead}} $$

For **Programmed I/O**, $T_{overhead} = N \times t_{poll}$ (poll time per word).
For **Interrupt I/O**, $T_{overhead} = N \times O_{int}$.
For **DMA**, $T_{overhead} = O_{int} + N \times t_{steal}$.

This explains *why* DMA dominates in modern systems like SSDs, NVMe drives, and 100 Gbps network interfaces.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: CPU Utilization in Programmed I/O

**Scenario:** A process needs to read $D$ bytes from a device at rate $R_d$, with each poll cycle taking $t_{poll}$ seconds.

**Given:**
- $D$ = total bytes to transfer
- $R_d$ = device data rate (bytes/sec)
- $t_{poll}$ = time for one CPU poll check
- $w$ = data bus word size (bytes)

**Step 1 — Compute the number of words to transfer:**
The total number of words the CPU must handle is the total bytes divided by the word width:
$$ N = \frac{D}{w} $$
*Logic:* Each word requires one status check and one data transfer operation by the CPU.

**Step 2 — Compute the time to actually perform the transfer at the device rate:**
The device takes a finite amount of time to deliver all bytes:
$$ T_{device} = \frac{D}{R_d} $$
*Logic:* If the device produces $R_d$ bytes per second, then $D$ bytes requires $D$ divided by $R_d$ seconds.

**Step 3 — Compute the total CPU time consumed (busy-wait + actual transfer handling):**
The CPU spends time polling for every word plus the actual transfer handling:
$$ T_{cpu} = N \cdot t_{poll} + N \cdot t_{handle} = N \cdot (t_{poll} + t_{handle}) $$
*Logic:* The CPU cannot do anything else during this window — it is fully occupied.

**Step 4 — Compute the total elapsed time:**
The total wall-clock time is bounded by the slower of the CPU or the device:
$$ T_{total} = T_{cpu} + T_{device} \quad \text{(approximately)} $$
*Logic:* In practice, the CPU and device work in lockstep; the total time is their sum when the CPU leads.

**Step 5 — Final CPU Utilization formula:**
$$ U_{cpu} = \frac{T_{cpu}}{T_{total}} = \frac{N \cdot (t_{poll} + t_{handle})}{N \cdot (t_{poll} + t_{handle}) + \frac{D}{R_d}} $$
*Logic:* This ratio quantifies how "busy" the CPU is. As $R_d \to 0$ (slow device), $U_{cpu} \to 1$ — CPU is fully wasted. As $R_d \to \infty$ (fast device), $U_{cpu} \to 0$ — CPU is mostly idle waiting. Programmed I/O is therefore *terrible* for very slow devices, ironically wasting the most CPU time on them.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DMA Cycle Stealing vs. Burst Mode

A DMA controller can operate in two modes: **Burst Mode** (DMAC owns the bus for the entire transfer) and **Cycle Stealing** (DMAC borrows one bus cycle per word from the CPU).

**Step 1 — Burst Mode total time:**
In burst mode, the CPU is fully blocked for the entire block transfer:
$$ T_{burst} = \frac{D}{R_{bus}} $$
*Logic:* The bus transfers at rate $R_{bus}$ bytes/sec; total bytes $D$ yields this time.

**Step 2 — Cycle Stealing total time:**
The DMAC steals one cycle per word. Each cycle transfers $w$ bytes in time $\frac{w}{R_{bus}}$:
$$ T_{steal} = N \cdot \frac{w}{R_{bus}} = \frac{D}{R_{bus}} $$
*Logic:* The total bytes stolen equals the total bytes transferred, so the *total* stolen time equals the burst time — but it is fragmented.

**Step 3 — CPU Impact (interleaved case):**
The CPU loses $T_{steal}$ of compute time spread over the transfer, but can intersperse useful work:
$$ U_{cpu,steal} = 1 - \frac{T_{steal}}{T_{device} + T_{setup}} $$
*Logic:* The CPU "stops" for a cycle per word, but resumes between steals.

**Step 4 — Compare the two modes:**
$$ \text{Efficiency}_{\text{burst}} > \text{Efficiency}_{\text{steal}} \quad \text{(for single transfer)} $$
*Logic:* Burst mode gives more throughput per transfer, but **Cycle Stealing** allows CPU to remain partially responsive — important for real-time systems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Interrupt-Driven I/O Overhead

**Given:** A system transfers $D$ bytes via interrupt I/O with word size $w$ and per-interrupt overhead $O_{int}$.

**Step 1 — Number of interrupts generated:**
$$ N_{int} = \frac{D}{w} = N $$

**Step 2 — Total interrupt overhead:**
$$ T_{overhead} = N_{int} \cdot O_{int} = \frac{D}{w} \cdot O_{int} $$

**Step 3 — CPU utilization while waiting for I/O:**
The CPU executes the user process during the wait, with effective speed reduced by interrupt handling:
$$ U_{cpu} = 1 - \frac{T_{overhead}}{T_{total}} = 1 - \frac{\frac{D}{w} \cdot O_{int}}{\frac{D}{R_d} + T_{overhead} + T_{setup}} $$

**Step 4 — Limiting case analysis:**
- If $D$ is large: $U_{cpu} \to 1 - \frac{R_d \cdot O_{int}}{w}$ — utilization plateaus.
- If $O_{int}$ is small: $U_{cpu} \to 1$ — system is efficient.
- This is why modern NICs and disks prefer **DMA + single interrupt** over per-word interrupts.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Method | $T_{cpu}$ Expression | $U_{cpu}$ Trend (vs. $D$) | Interrupt Count |
|---|---|---|---|
| Programmed I/O | $N(t_{poll} + t_{handle})$ | $U_{cpu} \to 1$ as $D$ grows | 0 |
| Interrupt-Driven I/O | $\frac{D}{w} \cdot O_{int}$ | $U_{cpu} \to 1 - \frac{R_d O_{int}}{w}$ | $N$ |
| DMA (Burst) | $O_{int} + T_{setup}$ | $U_{cpu} \to 1$ | 1 |
| DMA (Cycle Steal) | $O_{int} + N \cdot t_{steal}$ | $U_{cpu} \to 1 - \frac{R_d \cdot t_{steal}}{w}$ | 1 |

> [!TIP]
> **Takeaway:** *DMA Burst* dominates in throughput; *DMA Cycle Steal* dominates in real-time responsiveness; *Interrupt I/O* is a middle ground; *Programmed I/O* is acceptable only for very short, latency-critical transfers (e.g., GPIO toggling in embedded MCUs).

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Programmed I/O

This diagram shows the control and data flow when the CPU directly polls and reads from an I/O device.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["USER PROCESS"] --> B["CPU ISSUES I/O COMMAND"]
    B --> C["WRITE TO DEVICE CONTROL REGISTER"]
    C --> D["POLL DEVICE STATUS REGISTER"]
    D --> E{"STATUS EQUAL READY"}
    E -- NO --> D
    E -- YES --> F["READ DATA FROM DATA REGISTER"]
    F --> G{"ALL WORDS TRANSFERRED"}
    G -- NO --> D
    G -- YES --> H["RESUME USER PROCESS"]
    H --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** Notice the **closed loop** between *D* (poll) and *E* (check status). This is the **busy-wait loop** that defines Programmed I/O.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Interrupt-Driven I/O

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["USER PROCESS"] --> B["CPU ISSUES I/O COMMAND"]
    B --> C["CPU RETURNS TO OTHER WORK"]
    C --> D["DEVICE PROCESSES REQUEST ASYNCHRONOUSLY"]
    D --> E{"DEVICE READY"}
    E -- NO --> D
    E -- YES --> F["DEVICE RAISES HARDWARE INTERRUPT"]
    F --> G["CPU SAVES CONTEXT AND JUMPS TO ISR"]
    G --> H["ISR READS DATA FROM DATA REGISTER"]
    H --> I{"ALL WORDS TRANSFERRED"}
    I -- NO --> G
    I -- YES --> J["ISR SENDS EOI AND RETURNS"]
    J --> K["CPU RESTORES USER PROCESS"]
    K --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** Observe that the CPU **branches away** to do other work after the initial command, and is only **interrupted** when the device is ready. This is the key efficiency gain.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DMA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["USER PROCESS"] --> B["CPU SETS UP DMA CONTROLLER"]
    B --> B1["SOURCE ADDRESS"]
    B --> B2["DESTINATION ADDRESS"]
    B --> B3["BYTE COUNT"]
    B --> C["CPU RETURNS TO OTHER WORK"]
    C --> D["DMAC TAKES CONTROL OF SYSTEM BUS"]
    D --> E["DMAC TRANSFERS DATA DEVICE TO MEMORY DIRECTLY"]
    E --> F{"ALL BYTES TRANSFERRED"}
    F -- NO --> E
    F -- YES --> G["DMAC RELEASES BUS AND RAISES INTERRUPT"]
    G --> H["CPU EXECUTES DMA COMPLETION HANDLER"]
    H --> I["RESUME USER PROCESS"]
    I --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** The CPU is involved in only **two** moments: (1) setup and (2) completion. The bulk of the transfer happens in the **DMAC → Memory** path with no CPU intervention.

## 4.4 Comparative State Machine of All Three Methods

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> CommandIssued: Issue I/O Request
    CommandIssued --> Polling: Method PIO
    CommandIssued --> Executing: Method Interrupt
    CommandIssued --> DMAActive: Method DMA
    Polling --> Transferring: Status Ready
    Executing --> InterruptPending: Wait for IRQ
    InterruptPending --> Transferring: ISR Triggered
    DMAActive --> DMATransfer: DMAC Owns Bus
    DMATransfer --> Transferring: Per Word Transfer
    Transferring --> Transferring: Loop until block done
    Transferring --> Done: Block Complete
    Done --> Idle: CPU Resumes
```

**Reading the diagram:** The state machine highlights that all three methods **converge** at the `Transferring` state, but differ in the **path** to reach it. PIO is synchronous and CPU-bound; Interrupt and DMA are asynchronous and CPU-free during transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Insight:** The trend in modern systems is *DMA + interrupts* for block devices (NVMe SSDs, GPUs) and *Interrupt-only* for character devices (USB HID). Pure PIO is now reserved for very low-level boot sequences and simple microcontrollers.

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Differentiate between Programmed I/O and Interrupt-driven I/O. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O |
|---|---|---|
| CPU Action | Busy-waits, polls status | Continues other work, waits for IRQ |
| Mechanism | CPU-driven | Device-driven (interrupt) |
| CPU Utilization | Low (wasted in polling) | Higher (overhead only on interrupt) |

- **Advantage of PIO:** Simple to implement; no interrupt hardware needed. *(1 mark)*
- **Disadvantage of PIO:** Wastes CPU cycles during slow I/O. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Advantage of Interrupt I/O:** Better CPU utilization; supports multitasking. *(1 mark)*
- **Disadvantage of Interrupt I/O:** Each data word needs an interrupt — high overhead for bulk transfer. *(0.5 mark)*

**Mark Distribution:** *Definition (1) + Comparison (1) + Advantage/Disadvantage (1) = 3 marks.*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** What is DMA? Why is it preferred over Programmed I/O for disk transfers?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a method where a dedicated hardware controller (DMAC) transfers data directly between an I/O device and main memory **without CPU intervention**, raising only a single interrupt upon completion. *(1.5 marks)*

**Why DMA is preferred for disks:** *(1.5 marks)*
1. Disks transfer **large blocks** (KBs to MBs). PIO would issue millions of instructions.
2. DMA offloads the entire block transfer, freeing the CPU for other processes — maximizing throughput.
3. Only **one interrupt** is generated per block, drastically reducing kernel overhead.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ Question A — **[KTU ESE Model Question — 14 Marks, with internal choice]**

**(a)** Explain the three methods of I/O data transfer in an operating system with neat diagrams. Compare their CPU utilization. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A system uses a DMA controller to transfer a 16 KB block from a disk to memory. The disk transfer rate is 4 MB/s, the bus bandwidth is 100 MB/s, and the CPU is interrupted once on completion. The DMA setup time is 5 μs and the interrupt service routine takes 1 μs. Calculate: (i) the time for the actual data transfer, (ii) the total time including overhead, and (iii) the CPU utilization if the CPU would have been busy for 80 μs doing other useful work during the transfer. *(7 marks)*

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The three methods of I/O data transfer are:
1. **Programmed I/O (Polling):** CPU continuously polls the device status register until the device is ready, then reads/writes a data word. The CPU is fully occupied during transfer. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Interrupt-Driven I/O:** CPU issues the I/O command, then continues executing other instructions. The device raises a hardware interrupt (IRQ) when ready; the CPU suspends, runs the ISR, and resumes. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Direct Memory Access (DMA):** CPU configures the DMAC (source, destination, count), then returns to other work. The DMAC handles the entire block transfer independently, raising a single interrupt on completion. *(2 marks)*

**Comparison of CPU Utilization:** *(2 marks)*

$$ U_{cpu,PIO} = \frac{N(t_{poll} + t_{handle})}{N(t_{poll} + t_{handle}) + \frac{D}{R_d}} \to 1 \text{ as } D \to \infty $$

$$ U_{cpu,INT} = 1 - \frac{N \cdot O_{int}}{\frac{D}{R_d} + N \cdot O_{int}} $$

$$ U_{cpu,DMA} \approx 1 \text{ (CPU only interrupted once)} $$

**Conclusion:** DMA provides the highest CPU utilization for bulk transfers; PIO is the lowest.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 16 \text{ KB} = 16 \times 1024 = 16384 \text{ bytes}$
- $R_d = 4 \text{ MB/s} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}$
- $R_{bus} = 100 \text{ MB/s} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}$
- $T_{setup} = 5 \text{ μs}$
- $T_{isr} = 1 \text{ μs}$
- Useful CPU work $T_{useful} = 80 \text{ μs}$

**(i) Time for actual data transfer:** *(2 marks)*

The data transfer time is bounded by the slower of the device rate and the bus rate. The device rate (4 MB/s) is the bottleneck:

$$ T_{transfer} = \frac{D}{R_d} = \frac{16384}{4 \times 10^6} = 4.096 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4096 \text{ μs} $$

**Note:** Bus transfer alone would take $\frac{16384}{100 \times 10^6} = 163.84 \text{ μs}$, so the **disk is the bottleneck**.

**(ii) Total time including overhead:** *(2 marks)*

$$ T_{total} = T_{setup} + T_{transfer} + T_{isr} $$

$$ T_{total} = 5 \text{ μs} + 4096 \text{ μs} + 1 \text{ μs} = 4102 \text{ μs} $$

**(iii) CPU utilization:** *(3 marks)*

The CPU is interrupted only once (when DMA completes), so its overhead time is $T_{isr} = 1 \text{ μs}$ of servicing. It spends $T_{useful} = 80 \text{ μs}$ on real work and the rest is idle:

$$ T_{idle} = T_{total} - T_{useful} - T_{isr} = 4102 - 80 - 1 = 4021 \text{ μs} $$

CPU Utilization is the fraction of time the CPU is doing useful work:

$$ U_{cpu} = \frac{T_{useful}}{T_{total}} = \frac{80}{4102} \approx 0.0195 = 1.95\% $$

**Comment:** The CPU is **severely underutilized** for this slow I/O — the disk transfer dominates. This is *expected* in I/O-bound workloads and motivates techniques like **multiprogramming** to overlap I/O of one process with CPU work of another.

---

### ❓ Question B — **Alternative 14-Mark Choice**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a DMA controller in burst mode and cycle-stealing mode. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An interrupt-driven I/O system transfers 1 KB of data in word units of 2 bytes. Each interrupt takes 5 μs to service, and the device delivers data at 50 KB/s. If the CPU is engaged in 200 μs of useful computation between interrupt services, calculate: (i) the number of interrupts generated, (ii) total interrupt overhead, and (iii) effective CPU utilization. *(7 marks)*

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A **DMA Controller (DMAC)** is a hardware component that performs data transfer between I/O devices and main memory autonomously, after being initialized by the CPU.

**Block Diagram (Burst Mode):** *(3.5 marks)*

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CPU"] -- INIT SETUP --> B["DMA CONTROLLER"]
    B -- TAKES BUS CONTROL --> C["SYSTEM BUS"]
    C --> D["I/O DEVICE"]
    C --> E["MAIN MEMORY"]
    B -- SINGLE INTERRUPT --> A
```

- **Burst Mode:** The DMAC takes exclusive control of the system bus for the entire block transfer. The CPU is **fully blocked** for the duration. Best for maximum throughput, but CPU latency is poor. *(1.5 marks)*

**Cycle-Stealing Mode:** *(2 marks)*

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CPU"] -- INIT SETUP --> B["DMA CONTROLLER"]
    B -- STEALS ONE CYCLE --> C["SYSTEM BUS"]
    C --> D["I/O DEVICE"]
    C --> E["MAIN MEMORY"]
    A -- CONTINUES WORK --> F["CPU EXECUTES INSTRUCTIONS"]
    B -- SINGLE INTERRUPT --> A
```

- **Cycle-Stealing Mode:** The DMAC transfers one word per bus cycle, then releases the bus back to the CPU. The CPU executes instructions between cycles. Best for real-time responsiveness, with a small throughput penalty.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $D = 1 \text{ KB} = 1024 \text{ bytes}$
- $w = 2 \text{ bytes}$ (word size)
- $O_{int} = 5 \text{ μs}$ (per interrupt)
- $R_d = 50 \text{ KB/s} = 50 \times 10^3 \text{ bytes/s}$
- $T_{useful} = 200 \text{ μs}$ (useful computation between IRQs)

**(i) Number of interrupts generated:** *(2 marks)*

$$ N = \frac{D}{w} = \frac{1024}{2} = 512 \text{ interrupts} $$

**(ii) Total interrupt overhead:** *(2 marks)*

$$ T_{overhead} = N \cdot O_{int} = 512 \times 5 \text{ μs} = 2560 \text{ μs} $$

**(iii) Effective CPU utilization:** *(3 marks)*

Total time elapsed is the device transfer time plus overhead:

$$ T_{device} = \frac{D}{R_d} = \frac{1024}{50 \times 10^3} = 0.02048 \text{ s} = 20480 \text{ μs} $$

$$ T_{total} = T_{device} + T_{overhead} = 20480 + 2560 = 23040 \text{ μs} $$

Effective CPU utilization (useful work ÷ total time):

$$ U_{cpu} = \frac{T_{useful}}{T_{total}} = \frac{200}{23040} \approx 0.00868 = 0.87\% $$

**Conclusion:** Interrupt overhead is significant for fine-grained word-level transfers; this is why modern systems use **block-level DMA with a single interrupt per block**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert KB → bytes and MB/s → bytes/s. *Always write the unit at every step.*
> 2. **Confusing Device Rate vs. Bus Rate:** The transfer time uses the **slower** (device) rate, not the faster (bus) rate. Many students incorrectly use $R_{bus}$ and lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting DMA Setup Time:** $T_{setup}$ is part of $T_{total}$ and must be added.
> 4. **CPU Utilization Formula:** Use $U_{cpu} = \frac{T_{useful}}{T_{total}}$, **not** $U_{cpu} = 1 - \frac{T_{overhead}}{T_{total}}$ unless explicitly defined that way. Both are equivalent, but the former is the standard KTU expected form.
> 5. **Diagram Labels:** Avoid vague labels like "I/O box." Use precise terms: *Device Control Register, Status Register, Data Register, DMA Controller, System Bus.*

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The three device interaction methods are **Programmed I/O, Interrupt-Driven I/O, and DMA**.
- ✅ **Programmed I/O** = CPU polling; high overhead, low complexity, no interrupts.
- ✅ **Interrupt-Driven I/O** = CPU does other work, device raises IRQ; better utilization, but $N$ interrupts per block.
- ✅ **DMA** = DMAC handles bulk transfer; one interrupt per block; highest CPU utilization.
- ✅ Key formula: $U_{cpu} = \frac{T_{cpu}}{T_{total}} = \frac{T_{useful}}{T_{setup} + T_{transfer} + T_{overhead}}$.
- ✅ Data transfer time: $T_{transfer} = \frac{D}{R_d}$ (use **device** rate, not bus).
- ✅ DMA has two modes: **Burst** (high throughput, CPU blocked) and **Cycle Stealing** (CPU remains responsive).
- ✅ For **bulk devices** (disk, NIC, GPU) → use **DMA**.
- ✅ For **character devices** (keyboard, mouse) → use **Interrupt I/O**.
- ✅ For **simple embedded MCUs** → use **Programmed I/O** (no extra hardware).
- ✅ In KTU numericals, always show: **Given → Formula → Substitution → Final Answer with units**.
- ✅ In diagrams, distinguish **Device Registers** (Control, Status, Data) clearly.
- ✅ Remember the **trade-off triangle:** *Hardware complexity ↑ → CPU utilization ↑ → Latency ↓*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering "Compare" questions, always use a **table** with at least 3 parameters (CPU involvement, interrupt count, suitability). Examiners award easy marks for structured presentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Device Driver
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Device Driver

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Device Driver** is a specialized, privileged software component of the operating system that acts as a translator and intermediary between the physical hardware device and the higher-level operating system kernel or user applications. It encapsulates all device-specific knowledge—including control registers, interrupt handling logic, data transfer protocols, and timing constraints—and exposes a uniform, abstract interface to the rest of the OS through a well-defined **Device Driver Interface (DDI)** or **Kernel Interface**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the device driver is formally classified as part of the **I/O Subsystem** and operates at the kernel level (Ring 0 in x86 architectures), enabling **device independence** for upper-layer software.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A device driver is the *only* software component permitted to directly execute I/O instructions, access memory-mapped device registers, and handle hardware-generated interrupts. All other OS components must interact with the hardware *exclusively* through the driver's registered entry points.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **tourist in Japan** who speaks only English. You want to order food at a local restaurant, read subway signs, and ask for directions. You cannot directly communicate with the locals (the **hardware**).

You hire a **personal translator** (the **device driver**) who:
- Speaks both English and Japanese fluently.
- Knows the cultural rules of every restaurant, train station, and shop.
- Translates your high-level request ("I want to go to Tokyo Tower") into the precise local protocol (the correct ticket, the right train line, the proper etiquette).
- Handles all the low-level "awkwardness" (currency, gestures, timing) so you don't have to.

**The Device Driver is the translator between the OS (the tourist) and the Hardware (the locals).**

Without the driver, the OS would need to know the intricate, proprietary details of *every* device ever manufactured. With the driver, the OS only needs to know one universal language: the **standard I/O interface**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Metric / Constant | Standard Value / Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Driver Execution Privilege** | **Kernel Mode (Ring 0)** | Required for direct hardware access |
| **Typical Driver Size** | **10 KB – 100 KB** | Varies from minimal to complex (e.g., GPU drivers) |
| **I/O Port Address Space (x86)** | **64 KB** (ports 0x0000 – 0xFFFF) | Used for port-mapped I/O |
| **Interrupt Request Lines (IRQs)** | **0 – 15** (legacy PIC) / **0 – 23+** (APIC) | Hardware signaling mechanism |
| **DMA Channels (Legacy)** | **8 channels** (0–7, with channel 4 as cascade) | Used for high-speed data transfer |
| **Driver Load Time** | **Boot time** or **hot-plug event** | Determines system responsiveness |
| **Context Switch Overhead** | **~1–10 μs** per driver call | Critical for real-time performance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Device Independence**
> The principle that programs written for the OS should be able to access *any* I/O device without requiring modification. The device driver is the architectural mechanism that achieves this property.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of the I/O Subsystem

The operating system I/O subsystem is organized into a **layered architecture**, with the device driver occupying a pivotal position. From top (user) to bottom (hardware), the layers are:

1. **User Application Layer** — Issues high-level requests (e.g., `read()`, `write()`).
2. **Library Layer (e.g., glibc)** — Translates system calls into kernel-level operations.
3. **I/O System Common Code (Kernel)** — Implements the generic, device-independent interface. Provides:
   - Buffering
   - Caching
   - Spooling
   - Naming (file descriptors, paths)
   - Protection
   - Allocation (which process gets the device)
   - Error reporting
4. **Device Driver Layer** ⭐ — Implements the *device-specific* code. This is our focus.
5. **Hardware Controller** — The physical electronic circuitry of the device.
6. **Physical Device** — The actual mechanical/optical/magnetic component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The boundary between Layer 3 (common code) and Layer 4 (driver) is the **Device Driver Interface (DDI)**. Above this line, the OS is *device-independent*; below it, everything is *device-specific*.

### 2.2 Functions of a Device Driver

A device driver must perform the following essential functions:

- **Device Initialization and Reset** — Configuring the controller registers upon boot or hot-plug.
- **Command Issuance** — Translating high-level I/O requests into device command sequences.
- **Interrupt Handling** — Registering and servicing Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs).
- **Buffer Management** — Implementing DMA, ring buffers, or scatter-gather lists.
- **Data Transfer Coordination** — Synchronizing between CPU, controller, and memory.
- **Error Detection and Recovery** — Handling timeouts, parity errors, and device failures.
- **Power Management** — Suspending/resuming devices based on system state.
- **Hot-Plug Detection** — Managing dynamic device insertion/removal (e.g., USB).

### 2.3 Classification of Device Drivers

Drivers are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Classification Axis | Categories | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **By Transfer Mode** | **Character / Block / Network** | Granularity and addressability of I/O |
| **By Execution Context** | **Synchronous / Asynchronous (Interrupt-driven)** | When driver code runs |
| **By Loading Time** | **Static (compiled into kernel) / Dynamic (loadable modules)** | When the driver enters memory |

#### 2.3.1 Character Drivers
- Transfer data as a **stream of bytes**, sequentially.
- Do **not** support random access via seeks.
- Examples: keyboards, mice, serial ports, terminals.
- Typically use `get()`, `put()` operations.

#### 2.3.2 Block Drivers
- Transfer data in **fixed-size blocks** (e.g., **512 bytes**, **4 KB**).
- Support **random access** (seek operations).
- Examples: hard disks, SSDs, optical drives, USB flash drives.
- Interface via the buffer cache for performance.

#### 2.3.3 Network Drivers
- Transfer data in **packets (frames)**, not bytes or blocks.
- Driven primarily by **packet arrival interrupts** (asynchronous).
- Examples: Ethernet cards, Wi-Fi adapters.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The network driver category is sometimes debated because, internally, the OS treats network devices as *pseudo-block* devices or uses a separate **socket-based** I/O path. Be prepared to justify the classification in a 7-mark question.

### 2.4 The Device Driver Interface (DDI)

The DDI is the **standardized contract** between the device-independent I/O code and the device-specific driver. A typical DDI includes:

- **Open / Close** — Initialize and release the device.
- **Read / Write** — Perform data transfer.
- **Ioctl (I/O Control)** — Device-specific commands (e.g., set baud rate).
- **Strategy / Start** — Initiate an I/O operation.
- **Interrupt Handler** — Registered callback for hardware interrupts.
- **Dma Setup** — Configure DMA channels for the device.
- **Poll / Check** — Status inquiry for busy-wait devices.

### 2.5 Data Transfer Mechanisms Employed by Drivers

The driver must choose among **three fundamental mechanisms** to move data between the device and memory:

1. **Programmed I/O (Polling / Busy-Wait)**
   - The CPU repeatedly checks a status register until the device is ready.
   - Pros: Simple, deterministic, no interrupt overhead.
   - Cons: Wastes CPU cycles; inefficient for slow devices.
   - **Best for:** High-speed devices where data is always available (e.g., memory-mapped network controllers in data-center scenarios).

2. **Interrupt-Driven I/O**
   - The device raises an interrupt when it is ready; the CPU executes an ISR.
   - Pros: CPU is free during device operation.
   - Cons: Interrupt overhead; complexity in ISR design.
   - **Best for:** Most modern devices (keyboards, disks, network cards).

3. **Direct Memory Access (DMA)**
   - A dedicated **DMA controller** transfers data between the device and main memory *without CPU intervention*.
   - Pros: Maximizes throughput; frees CPU for other work.
   - Cons: Requires DMA-capable hardware; more complex driver logic.
   - **Best for:** High-bandwidth devices (disks, network cards, sound cards).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Modern drivers often use a **hybrid approach**: **DMA for bulk data transfer + Interrupts for completion signaling**. The CPU issues the DMA command, then proceeds with other work; when DMA completes, the device raises an interrupt to wake the blocked process.

### 2.6 I/O Scheduling (Disk Drivers)

For block device drivers, multiple I/O requests are pending simultaneously. The driver implements (or inherits) a **scheduling algorithm** to minimize seek time and rotational latency:

| Algorithm | Strategy | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **FCFS** | First-Come, First-Served | Baseline; fair but slow |
| **SSTF** | Shortest Seek Time First | Reduces seek time; may starve |
| **SCAN (Elevator)** | Sweeps back-and-forth | Classic exam topic |
| **C-SCAN** | Circular SCAN | Uniform wait time |
| **LOOK / C-LOOK** | Like SCAN but only as far as last request | More efficient than SCAN |
