| **EDF** | Earliest Deadline First | Real-time systems |

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Service Time (TAT)** | $T_{service} = T_{seek} + T_{rotational\ latency} + T_{transfer}$ | ms or s | Disk I/O performance |
| **Rotational Latency** | $T_{rot} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times T_{rotation} = \dfrac{30}{RPM}$ | ms | Average half-rotation |
| **Transfer Time** | $T_{transfer} = \dfrac{B}{N \times S}$ | s | Bytes / (tracks × sectors) |
| **Disk Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{B_{total}}{T_{service}}$ | MB/s | Performance metric |
| **Interrupt Latency Bound** | $T_{latency} \leq T_{deadline} - T_{work}$ | μs | Real-time guarantee |
| **DMA Transfer Rate** | $R_{DMA} = \dfrac{B}{T_{DMA}} = f_{bus} \times W_{bus}$ | Bytes/s | Bus bandwidth limit |
| **Driver Overhead** | $O_{driver} = T_{ISR} + T_{deferred}$ | μs | Kernel performance cost |
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Reliability metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The device driver is the cornerstone of **embedded systems engineering** (automotive ECUs, IoT devices, medical equipment), **driver development for custom hardware** (FPGA accelerators), and **OS porting** (Android, RTOS for microcontrollers). Mastering the driver interface is essential for any systems-level engineer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Rotational Latency

The **rotational latency** is the average time the disk controller must wait for the desired sector to rotate under the read/write head.

**Given:**
- Disk rotational speed = $N$ revolutions per minute (RPM).
- One full rotation takes $T_{rotation} = \dfrac{60}{N}$ seconds.

**Step 1 — Express one rotation in standard units.**

$$
T_{rotation} = \dfrac{60}{N} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to milliseconds for engineering convenience.**

$$
T_{rotation} = \dfrac{60 \times 1000}{N} = \dfrac{60000}{N} \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Average rotational latency = half of a full rotation.**

On average, the desired sector is positioned halfway through the rotation when a request arrives. Therefore:

$$
T_{rot,\ avg} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times T_{rotation} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{60}{N} = \dfrac{30}{N} \text{ seconds}
$$

In milliseconds:

$$
T_{rot,\ avg} = \dfrac{30 \times 1000}{N} = \dfrac{30000}{N} \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.** For a 7200 RPM disk:

$$
T_{rot,\ avg} = \dfrac{30000}{7200} = 4.1667 \text{ ms}
$$

**Interpretation:** On average, the disk spends about **4.17 ms** in rotational latency per I/O request. This is a *fixed* cost the driver cannot eliminate — it is a physical property of the hardware.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Disk Access Time (FCFS Analytical Model)

**Given:**
- Seek time for a typical request: $T_{seek}$ (e.g., average **8 ms**).
- Rotational latency: $T_{rot}$ (e.g., **4.17 ms** at 7200 RPM).
- Transfer time for $B$ bytes: $T_{transfer}$.

**Step 1 — State the total service time formula.**

$$
T_{service} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the transfer time.** If the disk has $S$ sectors per track, each of $K$ bytes, and the request spans $n$ sectors:

$$
T_{transfer} = \dfrac{n}{S} \times T_{rotation}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the rotational period.** With $N$ RPM:

$$
T_{transfer} = \dfrac{n}{S} \times \dfrac{60}{N} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Combined expression.**

$$
T_{service} = T_{seek} + \dfrac{30}{N} + \dfrac{n}{S} \times \dfrac{60}{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.** Suppose $N = 7200$ RPM, $S = 1000$ sectors/track, $n = 1$ sector, $T_{seek} = 8$ ms:

$$
T_{rot} = \dfrac{30}{7200} = 0.00417 \text{ s} = 4.17 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{transfer} = \dfrac{1}{1000} \times \dfrac{60}{7200} = 8.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 0.00833 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{service} = 8 + 4.17 + 0.00833 = 12.18 \text{ ms}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** **Seek time + rotational latency dominate** (≈ 99.93% of total access time). The driver can optimize these by reordering requests (scheduling algorithms), but the *physical* lower bound is dictated by the hardware.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Data Transfer Mechanisms

| Analytical Criterion | Programmed I/O (Polling) | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Utilization** | Very Low (busy-wait) | High (CPU free during I/O) | Very High (CPU fully free) |
| **Throughput** | Low (single byte/instruction) | Medium (block per interrupt) | Very High (block per DMA) |
| **Driver Complexity** | **Low** | Medium | High |
| **Hardware Cost** | None | Interrupt controller required | DMA controller required |
| **Suitable Devices** | Embedded controllers, fast sensors | Keyboards, mice, low-speed I/O | Disks, network, sound, GPU |
| **Determinism** | **High** (worst-case bounded) | Medium (interrupt jitter) | Medium (DMA completion) |
| **Latency** | **Lowest** (no interrupt overhead) | Medium (interrupt dispatch) | Low (completion interrupt only) |
| **Power Consumption** | High (CPU always active) | Lower (CPU sleeps) | Lowest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked *"Which transfer mechanism is best for a high-speed disk?"*, the correct answer is **DMA**, not interrupt-driven I/O. Interrupts are still involved, but only for *completion notification* — the bulk transfer is offloaded to the DMA controller.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: SCAN Disk Scheduling Algorithm

**Request Queue** (cylinder numbers): 98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67
**Initial Head Position:** 53
**Direction:** Moving towards the higher cylinder numbers (towards 199).

**Step 1 — Sort the request queue in ascending order.**

$$
\{14,\ 37,\ 53,\ 65,\ 67,\ 98,\ 122,\ 124,\ 183\}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the requests on either side of the head position (53).**
- Right of 53: $65,\ 67,\ 98,\ 122,\ 124,\ 183$
- Left of 53: $37,\ 14$

**Step 3 — SCAN services requests in the direction of motion first (towards higher cylinders).**

Service sequence:

$$
53 \to 65 \to 67 \to 98 \to 122 \to 124 \to 183 \to 199 \to 37 \to 14
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate total head movement (seek distance).**

$$
(65 - 53) + (67 - 65) + (98 - 67) + (122 - 98) + (124 - 122) + (183 - 124) + (199 - 183) + (199 - 37) + (37 - 14)
$$

$$
= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 + 162 + 23 = 331 \text{ cylinders}
$$

**Step 5 — Average seek time** (if $T_{seek} = 0.1$ ms per cylinder):

$$
T_{avg\ seek} = 0.1 \times 331 = 33.1 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** SCAN reduces seek time by **~40%** compared to FCFS (which would have given 640 cylinders for this queue). This is why elevator algorithms are deployed in production disk drivers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Device Driver Position in OS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["USER APPLICATION"] --> B["SYSTEM CALL INTERFACE"]
    B --> C["DEVICE INDEPENDENT I/O CODE"]
    C --> D["DEVICE DRIVER INTERFACE DDI"]
    D --> E["DEVICE DRIVER LAYER"]
    E --> F["DEVICE CONTROLLER"]
    F --> G["PHYSICAL HARDWARE"]
    H["INTERRUPT"] --> E
    I["DMA CONTROLLER"] --> F
    E --> I
```

**Component Roles:**

- **A — User Application:** Issues `read()` / `write()`.
- **B — System Call Interface:** Validates parameters, transitions to kernel mode.
- **C — Device Independent I/O Code:** Handles buffering, caching, naming, protection.
- **D — DDI:** The contractual boundary.
- **E — Device Driver:** Our focus — device-specific code, ISR, DMA setup.
- **F — Device Controller:** Electronic interface (e.g., disk controller ASIC).
- **G — Physical Hardware:** The actual mechanical / magnetic / optical device.
- **H — Interrupt Line:** Hardware-to-driver notification.
- **I — DMA Controller:** Offloads data transfer from CPU.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Driver Internal State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> INIT : load_module
    INIT --> READY : init_complete
    READY --> BUSY : request_received
    BUSY --> READY : transfer_complete
    BUSY --> ERROR : timeout_or_fault
    ERROR --> READY : recovery_successful
    ERROR --> HALT : unrecoverable_fault
    READY --> IDLE : unload_module
    HALT --> [*]
```

**State Descriptions:**

- **IDLE** — Driver is loaded but not bound to a device.
- **INIT** — Driver is performing hardware probing and configuration.
- **READY** — Driver is initialized and waiting for I/O requests.
- **BUSY** — Driver is actively servicing an I/O operation.
- **ERROR** — Driver has detected a fault; attempting recovery.
- **HALT** — Driver has given up; the device is unusable.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: DMA + Interrupt Hybrid Transfer Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU
    participant DRV as Device Driver
    participant DMA as DMA Controller
    participant DEV as Device
    participant MEM as Main Memory
    CPU->>DRV: read(request, buffer, size)
    DRV->>DMA: setup_channel(src=dev, dst=mem, count)
    DMA->>DEV: begin_transfer
    loop Data Transfer
        DEV->>DMA: byte/block data
        DMA->>MEM: byte/block data
    end
    DEV->>DRV: interrupt (DONE)
    DRV->>CPU: wake_up_process
    CPU->>MEM: copy_to_user(buffer)
```

**Flow Analysis:**

1. The CPU invokes the driver's `read` entry point.
2. The driver programs the DMA controller with source, destination, and count.
3. The DMA controller orchestrates the block transfer *without CPU involvement*.
4. On completion, the device raises an interrupt.
5. The driver services the interrupt, wakes the blocked process, and the kernel copies data to user space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric and free of reserved keywords. All labels use clean uppercase text without markdown formatting, as required by the KTU compilation safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a device driver. List any three of its functions.**

**Model Answer:**

A **device driver** is a privileged software component of the operating system that encapsulates the device-specific control logic and provides a uniform, device-independent interface to higher-level OS code.

**Three functions of a device driver:**

1. **Device Initialization and Reset** — The driver configures the device's control registers and brings the hardware to a known ready state when the system boots or when the device is hot-plugged.

2. **Interrupt Handling** — The driver registers an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) which is invoked whenever the device requires CPU attention (e.g., data ready, transfer complete, error).

3. **Data Transfer Coordination** — The driver selects and implements an appropriate transfer mechanism — **programmed I/O**, **interrupt-driven I/O**, or **DMA** — to move data efficiently between the device and main memory.

*Alternate acceptable functions: Buffer management, error recovery, power management, I/O scheduling.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between character and block device drivers.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Character Device Driver | Block Device Driver |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Granularity** | Stream of bytes | Fixed-size blocks (typically 512 B – 4 KB) |
| **Access Pattern** | Sequential only | Random access (seek) supported |
| **Buffering** | Usually unbuffered or line-buffered | Uses a **buffer cache** |
| **Examples** | Keyboard, mouse, serial port, terminal | Hard disk, SSD, CD-ROM |
| **I/O Commands** | `get`, `put` | `read`, `write`, `seek` |
| **Addressable Unit** | Byte | Block / sector |
| **Performance Optimization** | Minimal | Heavy (scheduling, prefetch) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Supplementary Note:** Network drivers form a third category — they transfer data in **packets (frames)** and are driven by asynchronous packet-arrival interrupts.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 4 Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the layered architecture of the I/O subsystem. Where does the device driver fit, and what is the significance of the Device Driver Interface (DDI)?**

**Model Solution:**

The I/O subsystem is organized into **six logical layers**, each with a specific responsibility:

**Layer 1 — User Application Layer:**
The topmost layer, where user programs issue high-level I/O requests through standard library calls such as `fopen()`, `fread()`, or `scanf()`. These calls are *device-independent* — the application does not know or care whether the destination is a disk, a network socket, or a terminal.

**Layer 2 — System Call Interface:**
Translates library calls into kernel system calls (`read()`, `write()`, `open()`, `close()`). Performs parameter validation, copies data across the user-kernel boundary, and transitions the CPU from user mode (Ring 3) to kernel mode (Ring 0).

**Layer 3 — Device-Independent I/O Software (Common Code):**
This layer provides the *generic*, *reusable* functionality that all I/O devices need:
- **Buffering** — Temporary storage to decouple producer/consumer speeds.
- **Caching** — Holding recently accessed data in faster memory.
- **Spooling** — Queuing output for devices that accept only one job at a time (e.g., printers).
- **Naming** — Mapping file names to device identifiers (e.g., `/dev/sda1`).
- **Protection** — Enforcing access permissions.
- **Allocation** — Deciding which process gets exclusive access to a device.
- **Error Reporting** — Standardized error codes (`EIO`, `ENODEV`, etc.).

**Layer 4 — Device Driver Layer (⭐ The Focus of This Question):**
This layer contains the *device-specific* code. Each driver knows:
- The layout of the device's **control registers**.
- The **command sequences** the device understands.
- How to **handle interrupts** from this particular device.
- How to **configure DMA** for this device.
- The device's **quirks, bugs, and timing constraints**.

**Layer 5 — Device Controller:**
The electronic hardware that physically interfaces the CPU with the device. Examples: the SATA controller ASIC, the USB host controller, the Ethernet MAC.

**Layer 6 — Physical Device:**
The actual mechanical or optical component — the disk platters, the laser diode, the magnetic head.

**Significance of the Device Driver Interface (DDI):**

The DDI is the **standardized contract** between Layer 3 (common code) and Layer 4 (driver). Its significance is fourfold:

1. **Device Independence:** Above the DDI, the OS sees only a uniform interface. New devices can be added by writing a new driver that conforms to the DDI — *no changes to the rest of the OS are required*.

2. **Code Reusability:** Common code (buffering, scheduling, error handling) is written *once* and reused across all devices.

3. **Portability:** Drivers can be ported across OS versions (e.g., Linux 5.x to 6.x) with minimal changes as long as the DDI remains stable.

4. **Modularity:** Drivers can be developed, tested, debugged, and loaded/unloaded *independently* of the rest of the kernel.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe the three data transfer mechanisms used by device drivers: Programmed I/O, Interrupt-Driven I/O, and DMA. Compare them with respect to CPU utilization and throughput.**

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism 1: Programmed I/O (Polling / Busy-Wait):**
The CPU executes a tight loop, repeatedly reading the device's status register until a "ready" flag is set. Only then does the CPU read/write the data.

$$
\text{Poll:} \quad \text{while}\ (\text{STATUS\_REGISTER}.\text{ready}\ == 0)\ \{\ \text{wait};\ \}
$$

- **CPU Utilization:** Very poor — the CPU is fully occupied *doing nothing useful* for the entire I/O duration.
- **Throughput:** Low — the CPU is the bottleneck.
- **Determinism:** Highest — the response time is bounded by a single instruction.

**Mechanism 2: Interrupt-Driven I/O:**
The CPU issues an I/O command and proceeds with other work. When the device is ready, it raises a hardware interrupt. The CPU suspends the current process, executes the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, transfers the data, and returns to the previous work.

- **CPU Utilization:** High — the CPU is free during device operation.
- **Throughput:** Medium — bounded by the interrupt dispatch overhead (~1–10 μs per interrupt) and the data transfer rate per interrupt.
- **Determinism:** Medium — interrupt latency is bounded but not constant (depends on interrupt priority and the code currently running).

**Mechanism 3: Direct Memory Access (DMA):**
The CPU configures a separate **DMA controller** with the source address, destination address, and byte count. The DMA controller then orchestrates the entire data transfer *autonomously*. The CPU is free to execute other processes. When DMA completes, the device raises an interrupt to signal completion.

- **CPU Utilization:** Very high — the CPU is freed from the entire bulk transfer.
- **Throughput:** Very high — limited only by the bus bandwidth and the device speed.
- **Determinism:** Medium — DMA is non-preemptible for a single transfer, but the completion interrupt is fast.

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU Utilization | Very Low | High | Very High |
| Throughput | Low | Medium | Very High |
| Driver Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Hardware Support | None | Interrupt controller | DMA controller |
| Best Use Case | Embedded, real-time, fast sensors | Keyboards, mice, low-speed | Disks, network, sound, GPU |

**Engineering Conclusion:** Modern high-performance systems use a **hybrid approach** — **DMA for the bulk transfer** + **interrupt for completion notification**. The CPU is freed for the entire transfer duration; it is interrupted *only once*, at the end, to deliver the data to the waiting process.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks] With neat diagrams, explain the working of the SCAN and C-SCAN disk scheduling algorithms. Apply both algorithms to the following request queue and compute total head movement:**

- **Request Queue:** 95, 180, 34, 119, 11, 123, 62, 64
- **Initial Head Position:** 50
- **Direction (SCAN):** Moving towards higher cylinder numbers
- **Total Cylinders:** 0 to 199

**Model Solution:**

**SCAN Algorithm (Elevator Algorithm):**
The head moves in the current direction servicing all requests in its path, reaches the end of the disk, reverses direction, and services the remaining requests.

**Step 1 — Sort the queue.**

$$
\{11,\ 34,\ 50,\ 62,\ 64,\ 95,\ 119,\ 123,\ 180\}
$$

**Step 2 — Service requests in the direction of motion (towards higher cylinders).**

Service sequence: $50 \to 62 \to 64 \to 95 \to 119 \to 123 \to 180 \to 199 \to 34 \to 11$

**Step 3 — Compute total head movement.**

$$
(62 - 50) + (64 - 62) + (95 - 64) + (119 - 95) + (123 - 119) + (180 - 123) + (199 - 180) + (199 - 34) + (34 - 11)
$$

$$
= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 4 + 57 + 19 + 165 + 23 = 337 \text{ cylinders}
$$

**C-SCAN Algorithm (Circular SCAN):**
The head moves in the current direction servicing all requests, then jumps back to the beginning *without servicing any requests*, and resumes service. This provides a **uniform wait time** for all requests.

**Step 1 — Sort the queue.**

$$
\{11,\ 34,\ 50,\ 62,\ 64,\ 95,\ 119,\ 123,\ 180\}
$$

**Step 2 — Service requests moving towards higher cylinders, then wrap around.**

Service sequence: $50 \to 62 \to 64 \to 95 \to 119 \to 123 \to 180 \to 199 \to 0 \to 11 \to 34$

**Step 3 — Compute total head movement.**

$$
(62 - 50) + (64 - 62) + (95 - 64) + (119 - 95) + (123 - 119) + (180 - 123) + (199 - 180) + (199 - 0) + (11 - 0) + (34 - 11)
$$

$$
= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 4 + 57 + 19 + 199 + 11 + 23 = 382 \text{ cylinders}
$$

**Comparison:**

| Algorithm | Total Head Movement | Wait Time Variance |
|---|---|---|
| SCAN | 337 cylinders | High (middle requests served twice as often) |
| C-SCAN | 382 cylinders | **Low (uniform)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** C-SCAN trades *slightly higher* total movement for *much fairer* wait times — the preferred choice in **database servers and multimedia systems** where predictable latency is critical.

**Diagram (textual representation):**

```
Cylinders: 0 ............................. 50 (head) ............................ 199

SCAN path:      50 → 199 (rightward, servicing) → 11 (leftward, servicing)
C-SCAN path:    50 → 199 → 0 (jump) → 34 (rightward, servicing)
```

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Discuss Direct Memory Access (DMA) in detail. How does it work with interrupts to provide high-throughput I/O? Explain the role of the DMA controller registers.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
**Direct Memory Access (DMA)** is a technique in which data is transferred between an I/O device and main memory *without continuous CPU intervention*. A dedicated hardware component called the **DMA Controller** orchestrates the transfer.

**Why DMA?**
In a CPU-driven transfer, every byte must travel through the CPU's registers — this wastes CPU cycles and limits throughput. DMA frees the CPU by offloading the transfer to a specialized controller that can access the memory bus directly.

**The DMA Controller Registers:**

A typical DMA controller (e.g., Intel 8237) has the following registers per channel:

| Register | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Base Address Register (BAR)** | Starting memory address for the transfer |
| **Count Register (CR)** | Number of bytes to transfer |
| **Mode Register** | Transfer mode (read/write, auto-init, etc.) |
| **Mask Register** | Enable/disable a specific channel |
| **Command Register** | Global controller commands (start, stop) |
| **Status Register** | Channel status (request pending, transfer complete) |
| **Page Register** | High-order address bits (8-bit DMA limitation) |

**Working of DMA with Interrupts (Hybrid High-Throughput Mode):**

**Step 1 — Request Reception:**
The user's process invokes `read(fd, buffer, size)`. The kernel's common code layer receives the request, allocates a kernel buffer, and invokes the device driver's `strategy()` or `read()` entry point.

**Step 2 — DMA Setup (CPU programs the DMA controller):**
The driver writes the following to the DMA controller's registers:
- **Source/Destination Address** (e.g., the device's data port or memory buffer).
- **Count** (the number of bytes to transfer).
- **Direction** (device-to-memory or memory-to-device).
- **Channel Selection** (which DMA channel to use).
- **Mode bits** (single transfer, block transfer, cascade mode).

**Step 3 — Issue the I/O Command:**
The driver writes to the device's control register, instructing it to begin the data transfer. The device responds by asserting **DRQ (DMA Request)** to the DMA controller.

**Step 4 — DMA Acquisition of the Bus:**
The DMA controller asserts **HRQ (Hold Request)** to the CPU. The CPU completes its current instruction, releases the bus, and asserts **HLDA (Hold Acknowledge)**. The DMA controller now has full control of the memory bus.

**Step 5 — Autonomous Data Transfer:**
The DMA controller performs the entire transfer, byte by byte or block by block, reading from the device and writing to memory (or vice versa). The CPU is **completely free** to execute other processes or handle other interrupts.

**Step 6 — Interrupt on Completion:**
When the **Count Register** reaches zero, the DMA controller:
- Releases the bus (de-asserts HRQ).
- Asserts a **completion signal** to the device.
- The device raises a hardware interrupt to the CPU.

**Step 7 — Interrupt Service:**
The CPU invokes the driver's ISR, which:
- Reads the status register to confirm successful completion.
- Wakes the blocked user process (`wake_up_process()`).
- Returns from the interrupt.

**Step 8 — Data Delivery:**
The kernel copies the data from the kernel buffer to the user's buffer, and the `read()` call returns successfully.

**Block Diagram of DMA + Interrupt Interaction:**

```
+------+        +-------+        +-------+        +--------+
| CPU  | <----> | DMA   | <----> | Device| <----> | Memory |
+------+        +-------+        +-------+        +--------+
    |              ^                 |
    |              | HRQ/HLDA        | Interrupt
    |              | (bus grant)     | (completion)
    +--------------+-----------------+
```

**Key Advantages of DMA:**

1. **High Throughput** — Bulk transfer is offloaded to dedicated hardware.
2. **CPU Offload** — CPU can perform computation concurrently with I/O.
3. **Lower Power** — CPU can enter low-power states while DMA is active.
4. **Scalability** — Multiple DMA channels allow concurrent transfers (e.g., disk + network simultaneously).

**Disadvantages:**

1. **Hardware Cost** — Requires a DMA controller chip (or integrated equivalent).
2. **Cache Coherency** — DMA writes bypass the CPU cache, potentially causing stale data. Drivers must invalidate the cache after DMA.
3. **Complexity** — DMA setup and error handling add significant driver code.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "device driver" with "device controller."** The driver is *software* (kernel module). The controller is *hardware* (electronic chip). Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Stating "DMA = no CPU involvement."** Wrong! DMA still uses the bus, and the CPU is interrupted at *completion*. The correct phrasing is *"DMA offloads the bulk transfer, freeing the CPU."*
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the DDI** in the layered architecture answer. The DDI is the **boundary** and is worth **at least 1 mark** on its own.
> 4. **Mixing up SCAN and C-SCAN.** SCAN reverses direction at the end. C-SCAN *jumps* back to the beginning without servicing. A wrong diagram can cost **up to 4 marks**.
> 5. **Skipping the sort step** in disk scheduling problems. Examiners look for an *explicit* sorted queue as the first step.
> 6. **Using "port-mapped" and "memory-mapped" I/O interchangeably.** They are distinct mechanisms; using one does not imply the other.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — The Device Driver**

- **Definition:** A device driver is a privileged kernel-mode software component that encapsulates device-specific control logic and exposes a uniform interface to the OS.
- **Position in OS:** Layer 4 of the 6-layer I/O subsystem — *above* the hardware, *below* the device-independent common code.
- **Device Driver Interface (DDI):** The contract between device-independent code and the driver. **Enables device independence.**
- **Three Driver Types:**
  - **Character** — byte stream, sequential (keyboard, mouse).
  - **Block** — fixed-size blocks, random access (disk, SSD).
  - **Network** — packet-oriented, interrupt-driven (Ethernet, Wi-Fi).
- **Three Transfer Mechanisms:**
  - **Programmed I/O (Polling)** — CPU busy-waits. Low throughput, high determinism.
  - **Interrupt-Driven I/O** — Device signals CPU. Medium throughput.
  - **DMA** — Dedicated controller offloads transfer. Highest throughput. **Hybrid: DMA + completion interrupt is the modern standard.**
- **Key DMA Registers:** Base Address, Count, Mode, Mask, Page, Command, Status.
- **Disk Scheduling Algorithms:**
  - **FCFS** — fair but slow.
  - **SSTF** — shortest seek first, starvation possible.
  - **SCAN (Elevator)** — sweeps back-and-forth.
  - **C-SCAN** — uniform wait time, circular sweep.
  - **LOOK / C-LOOK** — practical variants (no need to go to disk end).
- **Average Rotational Latency:** $T_{rot} = \dfrac{30}{N}$ seconds, where $N$ = RPM.
- **Total Service Time:** $T_{service} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$.
- **Driver State Machine:** IDLE → INIT → READY ↔ BUSY → ERROR → HALT.
- **Critical Pitfalls:** Driver ≠ Controller. DMA still uses bus + interrupt. Always sort the disk request queue. Mention DDI explicitly.
- **Real-World Applications:** Linux kernel modules, Windows WDM/WDF drivers, Android HAL, RTOS device handlers, embedded firmware, USB hot-plug stacks, GPU drivers (CUDA / DirectX backends).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hard Disk: Geometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hard Disk: Geometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hard Disk Geometry** refers to the physical and logical organization of data on a magnetic hard disk drive (HDD). It describes how the surface of a rotating platter is divided into concentric circles (tracks), pie-shaped wedges (sectors), and stacked vertical columns (cylinders) to enable the disk controller to locate and retrieve data blocks with precision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> In the KTU 2024 *Operating Systems* syllabus, **disk geometry** is the foundational concept underlying *disk scheduling, formatting, partitioning, and block addressing*. The OS views the disk as an array of logically addressed blocks, while the disk firmware translates these into the physical **Cylinder-Head-Sector (CHS)** triplets or **Logical Block Addresses (LBA)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multi-Story Parking Lot"

Imagine a **multi-story circular parking garage**:
- Each **floor** = a **platter surface** (read/write side).
- A car parked on **Floor 3, Bay Ring 5, Slot 12** corresponds exactly to **Cylinder 3, Track (Head) 5, Sector 12**.
- The **elevator** moving between floors = the **head actuator** (seek motion).
- A **spinning turntable** carrying the cars = the **spindle** rotation.
- A **parking attendant scanning a barcode** = the **sector header** containing the sector ID.

Just as a parking system uses three coordinates (Floor, Ring, Slot) to uniquely locate a car, the disk geometry uses the **CHS triplet** to uniquely identify a sector on the platter.

### 1.3 Standard Disk Geometry Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> The following terms are **mandatory vocabulary** for KTU 2024 board examinations.

| Term | Standard Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Platter** | Rigid circular disk coated with magnetic material, typically **aluminum or glass** substrate. |
| **Surface** | One side of a platter; each surface has its own read/write **head**. |
| **Track** | A single concentric circle on a surface. Modern disks have **tens of thousands** of tracks. |
| **Sector** | The smallest individually addressable unit; standard size = **512 bytes** (legacy) or **4096 bytes (4 KiB)** in **Advanced Format (AF)** disks. |
| **Cylinder** | The set of all tracks that lie at the **same radial distance** from the spindle, across all surfaces. |
| **Spindle** | The central motor shaft on which platters rotate. |
| **CHS** | **Cylinder–Head–Sector** triplet — the original 3D physical addressing scheme. |
| **LBA** | **Logical Block Addressing** — modern linear addressing scheme. |
| **ZBR / ZCAV** | **Zoned Bit Recording** / **Zoned Constant Angular Velocity** — variable sectors per track. |
| **Inter-sector gap** | Small physical gap between sectors used for clock synchronization. |
| **Inter-track gap** | Small radial gap between adjacent tracks to reduce crosstalk. |

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Key Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard reference values used in KTU 2024 numerical problems:**
> - Sector size = **512 bytes** (classical) / **4096 bytes** (Advanced Format)
> - Disk rotation speeds: **5400 RPM, 7200 RPM, 10000 RPM, 15000 RPM**
> - 1 minute = **60 seconds**; rotational period $T = \frac{60}{\text{RPM}}$ seconds
> - Average rotational latency = $\frac{T}{2} = \frac{30}{\text{RPM}}$ seconds
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Disk Geometry

**Step 1 — The Platter Stack**
A hard disk contains **one or more platters** mounted on a common spindle. A typical 3.5-inch enterprise disk may contain **2 to 5 platters**, giving **4 to 10 recording surfaces**. The outermost surfaces are usually used for data, while inner surfaces may contain **servowedge** information used for head positioning.

**Step 2 — Track Formation**
As the platter spins, the read/write head, mounted on a movable **actuator arm**, paints a concentric circle of magnetization. Each complete revolution under the head lays down one **track**. Tracks are numbered from the **outer edge (Track 0)** inward.

**Step 3 — Sector Subdivision**
Each track is divided into fixed-size **sectors** during **low-level formatting**. A **sector header** (preamble, address mark, track/sector ID, CRC) precedes the user data area. The header allows the controller to verify it is reading the correct sector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 512 bytes originally?**
> The **512-byte sector** was chosen by IBM in the 1950s–1970s as a compromise between formatting overhead and transfer granularity. Modern drives use **4-KiB (4096-byte) sectors** internally, emulating 512 bytes (called **512e**) for OS compatibility.

**Step 4 — Cylinder Concept**
Because all surfaces share a common actuator, when the heads are positioned over **Track 42 on Surface 0**, they are simultaneously over **Track 42 on all surfaces**. The set of these co-radial tracks forms a **cylinder**. Cylinder addressing is faster because switching between cylinders does not require any head motion — only an electronic head-switch.

**Step 5 — From Physical to Logical: CHS vs LBA**
- **CHS addressing** is a 3D coordinate: `(cylinder, head, sector)`. Historically limited by BIOS to 24 bits: 10 bits cylinder + 8 bits head + 6 bits sector = 1024 × 256 × 63 ≈ **8.4 GB**.
- **LBA (Logical Block Addressing)** flattens the disk into a 1D sequence of blocks numbered **0, 1, 2, …, N−1**, with each block typically **512 bytes** (or 4 KiB). Modern disks are addressed in LBA by the OS, and the disk's firmware translates LBA → CHS internally.

**Step 6 — Zoned Bit Recording (ZBR / ZCAV)**
Because outer tracks are physically longer than inner tracks, **ZBR** groups tracks into **zones** and assigns **more sectors** to outer zones. This is why modern disks report **different sector counts per track** across zones.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — these formulas appear in 80% of KTU 2024 disk-geometry numericals.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{\text{disk}} = N_C \times N_H \times N_S \times B$ | Total disk capacity (CHS) | bytes |
| 2 | $C_{\text{disk}} = N_{\text{LBA}} \times B$ | Total capacity via LBA | bytes |
| 3 | $T_{\text{rot}} = \frac{60}{\text{RPM}}$ | Time for one full revolution | seconds |
| 4 | $T_{\text{rot}}^{(\text{avg})} = \frac{T_{\text{rot}}}{2} = \frac{30}{\text{RPM}}$ | Average rotational latency | seconds |
| 5 | $T_{\text{rot}}^{(\text{max})} = \frac{60}{\text{RPM}}$ | Maximum rotational latency (worst case) | seconds |
| 6 | $R_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{B \times N_S^{(\text{track})}}{T_{\text{rot}}}$ | Track transfer rate | bytes/sec |
| 7 | $T_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{D}{R_{\text{transfer}}}$ | Time to transfer $D$ bytes | seconds |
| 8 | $T_{\text{access}} = T_{\text{seek}} + T_{\text{rot}}^{(\text{avg})} + T_{\text{transfer}}$ | Total average access time | seconds |
| 9 | $T_{\text{seek}}^{(\text{avg})} = \frac{T_{\text{seek}}^{(\text{max})}}{3}$ | Average seek time (rule of thumb) | seconds |
| 10 | $T_{\text{seek}}^{(\text{full-strokes})} = T_{\text{seek}}^{(\text{max})}$ | Full-stroke seek = max seek | seconds |
| 11 | $\text{Bits per inch} = \frac{\text{bits}}{\pi \times d}$ | Areal density (linear) | bpi |
| 12 | $\text{Areal Density} = \text{BPI} \times \text{TPI}$ | Bits per square inch | bits/in² |

Where:
- $N_C$ = number of cylinders, $N_H$ = number of heads (= surfaces), $N_S$ = sectors per track
- $B$ = bytes per sector, $D$ = number of bytes to be transferred
- $d$ = track diameter at given radius

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RAID design**: Disk geometry directly impacts **stripe size** selection and **rebuild time** estimation.
- **Database tuning**: DBAs compute optimal **extent sizes** using cylinder/track capacity to minimize head switches.
- **SSD emulation**: Understanding CHS/LBA helps in designing **NVMe namespaces** and **flash translation layer (FTL)** mapping tables.
- **Disk forensics**: Sector-level addressing is critical in **data recovery** and **carving** deleted files.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Industrial Context**: Modern OS like Linux uses the **`hdparm`** utility to query geometry, and the kernel's block layer exposes geometry via `/sys/block/sda/queue/geometry` (logical) and `/sys/block/sda/queue/hw_sector_size` (physical).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Disk Capacity from CHS Geometry

**Given:**
- A disk with $N_C$ cylinders, $N_H$ recording surfaces (heads), $N_S$ sectors per track, and $B$ bytes per sector.

**Step 1 — Identify the addressing tuple.**
Each uniquely addressable unit is a sector, identified by the triplet $(c, h, s)$.

**Step 2 — Count sectors per track.**
For a fixed cylinder $c$ and head $h$, varying $s$ from $1$ to $N_S$ yields $N_S$ sectors on that track.

$$N_{\text{sectors/track}} = N_S$$

**Step 3 — Count sectors per surface.**
A surface has $N_C$ tracks (one per cylinder position) and $N_S$ sectors per track:

$$N_{\text{sectors/surface}} = N_C \times N_S$$

**Step 4 — Count total sectors on disk.**
There are $N_H$ surfaces, so total sectors is:

$$N_{\text{total sectors}} = N_H \times N_C \times N_S$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by bytes per sector.**
Total disk capacity:

$$C_{\text{disk}} = N_H \times N_C \times N_S \times B$$

This is the canonical KTU formula. ✅

### 3.2 Derivation: Average Access Time

**Step 1 — Decompose access time into three independent phases.**

The OS issues a read request for sector $X$ located at $(c_X, h_X, s_X)$. The head is currently at $(c_0, h_0, s_0)$.

$$T_{\text{access}} = T_{\text{seek}} + T_{\text{rot}} + T_{\text{transfer}}$$

**Step 2 — Quantify the seek time.**
Seek time = time for actuator to move from current track to target track. With the standard industry assumption:

$$T_{\text{seek}}^{(\text{avg})} = \frac{T_{\text{seek}}^{(\text{max})}}{3}$$

This assumes uniform random distribution of requests over the track range, giving a triangular probability distribution with mean = 1/3 of the maximum.

**Step 3 — Quantify the rotational latency.**
Once the head arrives at the correct track, the platter must rotate until the target sector passes under the head. With uniform random arrival phase, the average wait is half a revolution:

$$T_{\text{rot}}^{(\text{avg})} = \frac{1}{2} \times T_{\text{rot}} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{60}{\text{RPM}} = \frac{30}{\text{RPM}} \;\; \text{seconds}$$

**Step 4 — Quantify transfer time.**
To transfer $D$ bytes, the controller must read $D/B$ consecutive sectors. On a single track with $N_S$ sectors, the transfer rate is:

$$R_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{N_S \times B}{T_{\text{rot}}} = \frac{N_S \times B \times \text{RPM}}{60} \;\; \text{bytes/sec}$$

Hence:

$$T_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{D}{R_{\text{transfer}}} = \frac{60 \times D}{N_S \times B \times \text{RPM}}$$

**Step 5 — Combine.**

$$T_{\text{access}} = \frac{T_{\text{seek}}^{(\text{max})}}{3} + \frac{30}{\text{RPM}} + \frac{60 \times D}{N_S \times B \times \text{RPM}}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU 2024 Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These examples are framed exactly as KTU 2024 university-level numerical problems expect.

---

#### **Example 1 — Disk Capacity**

A disk has **1024 cylinders, 8 heads (surfaces), 63 sectors/track, and 512 bytes/sector**. Compute the total capacity.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Substitute into the CHS capacity formula.

$$C_{\text{disk}} = N_H \times N_C \times N_S \times B$$

Step 2 — Plug in values.

$$C_{\text{disk}} = 8 \times 1024 \times 63 \times 512$$

Step 3 — Compute stepwise.

$$= 8 \times 1024 \times 63 \times 512$$

$$= 8192 \times 63 \times 512$$

$$= 516096 \times 512$$

$$= 264,241,152 \;\; \text{bytes}$$

Step 4 — Convert to MB and GB.

$$= \frac{264{,}241{,}152}{2^{20}} = 252 \;\; \text{MB}$$

$$= \frac{252}{1024} \approx 0.246 \;\; \text{GB}$$

This is approximately **252 MB**, consistent with the historical **504 MB** BIOS CHS limit (×2 for two-sided platters) era. ✅

---

#### **Example 2 — Average Access Time**

A disk rotates at **7200 RPM**, has **average seek time = 4 ms**, and **150 sectors/track** of **512 bytes** each. Compute the average time to read **16 sectors** (8 KB) from a single track.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rotational latency (average).**

$$T_{\text{rot}} = \frac{60}{7200} = \frac{1}{120} \;\; \text{s} \approx 8.333 \;\; \text{ms}$$

$$T_{\text{rot}}^{(\text{avg})} = \frac{8.333}{2} \approx 4.167 \;\; \text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — Transfer time for 16 sectors.**

Bytes to transfer:

$$D = 16 \times 512 = 8192 \;\; \text{bytes}$$

Transfer rate:

$$R_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{150 \times 512 \times 7200}{60} = \frac{55{,}296{,}000}{60} = 921{,}600 \;\; \text{bytes/s}$$

Transfer time:

$$T_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{8192}{921{,}600} \approx 0.00889 \;\; \text{s} = 8.89 \;\; \text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — Total access time.**

$$T_{\text{access}} = 4 \;\; \text{ms} + 4.167 \;\; \text{ms} + 8.89 \;\; \text{ms} \approx 17.06 \;\; \text{ms}$$

✅ **Answer:** Approximately **17.06 ms**.

---

#### **Example 3 — LBA ↔ CHS Conversion**

A disk uses the classical CHS scheme. Compute the **LBA** of the sector at **(Cylinder 200, Head 4, Sector 25)**, assuming 16 heads, 63 sectors/track.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the standard CHS → LBA mapping.**

$$\text{LBA} = (C \times N_H + H) \times N_S + (S - 1)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$\text{LBA} = (200 \times 16 + 4) \times 63 + (25 - 1)$$

$$= (3200 + 4) \times 63 + 24$$

$$= 3204 \times 63 + 24$$

$$= 201{,}852 + 24 = 201{,}876$$

✅ **Answer:** LBA = **201,876**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: ZBR vs Constant Geometry

| Parameter | Constant Geometry (Classical) | Zoned Bit Recording (Modern) |
|---|---|---|
| Sectors/track | **Same** for all tracks | **Varies** by zone (outer = more) |
| Rotation speed | Constant angular velocity | Constant angular velocity |
| Areal density | **Low** (limited by inner) | **High** (optimized per zone) |
| Capacity utilization | **~30–40%** of outer track | **~80–90%** of outer track |
| Used in | Floppy disks, old MFM/RLL | All modern drives post-1995 |
| KTU relevance | Foundational concept | Exam-favorite for short notes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Disk Geometry — Platter, Track, Sector, Cylinder

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture** of how the components interact, since a literal 3D platter drawing cannot be rendered in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP["SPINDLE MOTOR (Common Axis)"]
        S1[Platter 1 - Top Surface]
        S2[Platter 1 - Bottom Surface]
        S3[Platter 2 - Top Surface]
        S4[Platter 2 - Bottom Surface]
    end

    subgraph HDA["HEAD DISK ASSEMBLY - ACTUATOR ARM"]
        H1[Head 1 reads S1]
        H2[Head 2 reads S2]
        H3[Head 3 reads S3]
        H4[Head 4 reads S4]
    end

    subgraph TRACK["CONCENTRIC TRACK LAYOUT - Top View of One Platter"]
        T0[Track 0 - outermost ring - 63+ sectors]
        T1[Track 1 - second ring - 63 sectors]
        T2[Track 2 - mid ring - 50 sectors - ZBR zone 2]
        T3[Track 3 - innermost - 30 sectors - ZBR zone 3]
    end

    subgraph SECTOR["SECTOR STRUCTURE - 512 bytes or 4 KB"]
        SEC1[Preamble - sync pattern]
        SEC2[Address Mark - sector ID]
        SEC3[Track and Sector Number]
        SEC4[CRC - error check]
        SEC5[Data Area - 512/4096 bytes]
        SEC6[ECC - Error Correction Code]
    end

    SP --> TRACK
    HDA --> SP
    S1 --> T0
    T0 --> SECTOR
    T1 --> SECTOR
    T2 --> SECTOR
    T3 --> SECTOR
```

### 4.2 CHS → LBA Translation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OS[Operating System Request] --> LBA_REQ[Logical Block Address LBA Number]
    LBA_REQ --> FW[Disk Firmware - Microcontroller]
    FW --> TRANS[CHS Translation Logic]
    TRANS --> C_OUT[Cylinder Number]
    TRANS --> H_OUT[Head Number]
    TRANS --> S_OUT[Sector Number]
    C_OUT --> ACT[Actuator - Seek to cylinder]
    H_OUT --> HSW[Head Switch - Electronic]
    S_OUT --> WAIT[Wait for Rotational Latency]
    ACT --> WAIT
    HSW --> WAIT
    WAIT --> READ[Read Sector Data]
    READ --> DMA[DMA Transfer to OS Buffer]
```

### 4.3 Disk Access-Time Decomposition Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ[Disk Request Issued] --> SK[SEEK TIME - actuator arm moves]
    SK --> ROT[ROTATIONAL LATENCY - platter rotates to sector]
    ROT --> XFER[TRANSFER TIME - data read off disk]
    XFER --> DONE[Sector delivered to OS]

    SK -. avg .-> SKV[Ts max divided by 3]
    ROT -. avg .-> ROTV[Half of one revolution]
    XFER -. formula .-> XFERV[D divided by transfer rate]
```

### 4.4 Cylinder Concept — Vertical Stacking of Co-Radial Tracks

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CYL42["CYLINDER NUMBER 42 - all surfaces aligned at same radius"]
        SF0[Surface 0 - Track 42]
        SF1[Surface 1 - Track 42]
        SF2[Surface 2 - Track 42]
        SF3[Surface 3 - Track 42]
    end

    SF0 --- SF1
    SF1 --- SF2
    SF2 --- SF3

    HEAD_SW[HEAD SWITCH - no arm motion, only electronic switch between heads - takes microseconds]

    SF0 -. active head .- HEAD_SW
    SF1 -. standby .- HEAD_SW
    SF2 -. standby .- HEAD_SW
    SF3 -. standby .- HEAD_SW
```

### 4.5 Sector Internal Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SECTOR_BLOCK[One Physical Sector] --> P[Preamble 15 bytes - bit synchronization]
    P --> AM[Address Mark - marks start of ID]
    AM --> ID[ID Field - cylinder, head, sector, CRC]
    ID --> GAP1[Gap 1 - separation]
    GAP1 --> DATA[Data Field 512 or 4096 bytes]
    DATA --> ECC[ECC Field - error correction]
    ECC --> GAP2[Gap 2 - buffer to next sector]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define disk geometry. With a neat diagram, explain the terms: track, sector, cylinder, and head.*

**Model Answer:**

**Disk geometry** is the logical and physical arrangement of data on the magnetic platters of a hard disk, specifying how tracks, sectors, and cylinders are organized to enable data storage and retrieval.

- **Track:** A concentric circle on a platter surface, formed as the head writes data during one full platter revolution.
- **Sector:** The smallest addressable unit on a track, classically **512 bytes**, containing a header, data, and ECC.
- **Cylinder:** The set of all co-radial tracks (same radius) on every surface. Switching within a cylinder requires only an **electronic head-switch** (microseconds), not a mechanical seek.
- **Head:** The electromagnetic transducer on the actuator arm that reads/writes one specific surface.

A request from the OS is translated into a **CHS triplet (Cylinder, Head, Sector)** or an **LBA number**, which the disk controller resolves to physical geometry. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between CHS addressing and LBA addressing.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | CHS Addressing | LBA Addressing |
|---|---|---|
| **Form** | 3D tuple $(C, H, S)$ | 1D linear index $n$ |
| **Fields** | Cylinder, Head, Sector | Single block number |
| **Capacity limit (BIOS)** | $\sim$**8.4 GB** (24-bit) | $\sim$**2 TiB** (32-bit), **8 ZiB** (64-bit) |
| **OS view** | Direct physical | Logical; firmware translates |
| **Used by** | BIOS, old bootloaders | Modern OS, SATA, NVMe |
| **Conversion** | $\text{LBA} = (C \cdot N_H + H) \cdot N_S + (S-1)$ | $C = \text{LBA} \div (N_H \cdot N_S)$; etc. |

CHS exposes geometry to the OS; LBA abstracts it. Modern drives expose only LBA and hide geometry behind firmware. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the physical and logical structure of a hard disk with a diagram. Define the terms track, sector, cylinder, and platter.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hard disk has the following specifications:
- 4 platters (8 surfaces)
- 2048 cylinders
- 128 sectors per track
- 512 bytes per sector
- Spindle speed = 5400 RPM
- Average seek time = 6 ms

Compute:
1. Total disk capacity in GB.
2. Average rotational latency in ms.
3. Total average access time to read 32 KB of contiguous data from a single track.

---

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

The **hard disk drive (HDD)** is a non-volatile storage device using rotating magnetic platters and a movable read/write head.

- **Platter**: A rigid circular disk (3.5" diameter typical), coated with a thin ferromagnetic layer. Modern disks use **2–5 platters** stacked on a common spindle.
- **Surfaces**: Each platter has **two surfaces** (top and bottom). A separate head reads each surface.
- **Tracks**: Concentric rings on a surface, numbered from **0 (outermost)** to $N_C - 1$ (innermost).
- **Sectors**: Each track is divided into arcs called sectors. **Classical size = 512 bytes.** Each sector has a **header** (ID + CRC) and a **data area**.
- **Cylinder**: All tracks at the **same radius** on all surfaces form a cylinder. Cylinder addressing minimizes seek operations.
- **Head/Actuator**: The arm carrying read/write heads pivots on a voice-coil actuator.

Logical addressing is achieved by **CHS (Cylinder-Head-Sector)** or **LBA (Logical Block Addressing)**. The OS issues an LBA; the disk's firmware converts it to CHS for physical access. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram description, 5 for term definitions)*

*(Refer to Section 4.1 for the diagram block.)*

---

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Part (b.1) — Total Disk Capacity:**

Using the canonical formula:

$$C_{\text{disk}} = N_H \times N_C \times N_S \times B$$

$$C_{\text{disk}} = 8 \times 2048 \times 128 \times 512 \;\; \text{bytes}$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$= 8 \times 2048 = 16{,}384$$

$$16{,}384 \times 128 = 2{,}097{,}152$$

$$2{,}097{,}152 \times 512 = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \;\; \text{bytes}$$

$$= \frac{1{,}073{,}741{,}824}{2^{30}} = 1 \;\; \text{GB}$$

✅ **Answer: 1 GB.** *(2 marks)*

---

**Part (b.2) — Average Rotational Latency:**

$$T_{\text{rot}} = \frac{60}{\text{RPM}} = \frac{60}{5400} = \frac{1}{90} \;\; \text{s} = 11.111 \;\; \text{ms}$$

$$T_{\text{rot}}^{(\text{avg})} = \frac{11.111}{2} \approx 5.556 \;\; \text{ms}$$

✅ **Answer: ≈ 5.56 ms.** *(2 marks)*

---

**Part (b.3) — Total Average Access Time for 32 KB:**

**Step 1 — Compute bytes to read.**

$$D = 32 \;\; \text{KB} = 32 \times 1024 = 32{,}768 \;\; \text{bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Compute number of sectors.**

$$N_{\text{secs}} = \frac{32768}{512} = 64 \;\; \text{sectors}$$

**Step 3 — Compute transfer rate.**

$$R_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{N_S \times B \times \text{RPM}}{60} = \frac{128 \times 512 \times 5400}{60}$$

$$= \frac{128 \times 512 \times 90}{1} = 128 \times 46080 = 5{,}898{,}240 \;\; \text{bytes/sec}$$

**Step 4 — Compute transfer time.**

$$T_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{32768}{5898240} \approx 0.005556 \;\; \text{s} = 5.556 \;\; \text{ms}$$

**Step 5 — Sum up.**

$$T_{\text{access}} = T_{\text{seek}} + T_{\text{rot}}^{(\text{avg})} + T_{\text{transfer}}$$

$$T_{\text{access}} = 6 + 5.556 + 5.556 = 17.112 \;\; \text{ms}$$

✅ **Answer: ≈ 17.11 ms.** *(3 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the components of disk access time. Derive the formula for average access time and average rotational latency.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a hard disk with **6 platters, 2400 cylinders, 200 sectors/track, 512 bytes/sector, rotating at 10000 RPM.** The average seek time is **5 ms**.
1. Compute the total disk capacity.
2. Compute the track transfer rate.
3. A file of **200 KB** is to be read sequentially from the disk. Assuming it is stored on contiguous tracks and no inter-track seeks are needed beyond one initial seek, compute the total time to read it.

---

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Disk access time** has **three components**, executed in sequence:

1. **Seek time ($T_{\text{seek}}$):** Time to move the actuator arm from the current cylinder to the target cylinder. Measured in **milliseconds**. Average seek = $T_{\text{seek}}^{(\text{max})} / 3$.

2. **Rotational latency ($T_{\text{rot}}$):** Time for the platter to rotate so that the target sector lies under the head. Maximum = one full revolution. Average = half revolution.

3. **Transfer time ($T_{\text{transfer}}$):** Time to actually read/write the bytes once the head is over the right sector. Depends on disk rotation speed and bytes per track.

**Derivation of Average Access Time:**

$$T_{\text{access}} = T_{\text{seek}} + T_{\text{rot}} + T_{\text{transfer}}$$

Assuming uniform random request distribution:

$$T_{\text{access}} = \frac{T_{\text{seek}}^{(\text{max})}}{3} + \frac{T_{\text{rot}}}{2} + T_{\text{transfer}}$$

**Derivation of Average Rotational Latency:**

Time for one revolution: $T_{\text{rot}} = \frac{60}{\text{RPM}}$ seconds.

Since the target sector position is uniformly distributed over $[0, T_{\text{rot}}]$, expected wait = mean of uniform distribution:

$$T_{\text{rot}}^{(\text{avg})} = \frac{0 + T_{\text{rot}}}{2} = \frac{T_{\text{rot}}}{2} = \frac{30}{\text{RPM}} \;\; \text{seconds}$$

✅ *(7 marks: 3 for diagram, 4 for derivation)*

*(Refer to Section 4.3 for the timing decomposition block.)*

---

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Part (b.1) — Total Disk Capacity:**

$$N_H = 6 \times 2 = 12 \;\; \text{heads}$$

$$C_{\text{disk}} = 12 \times 2400 \times 200 \times 512 \;\; \text{bytes}$$

Step-by-step:

$$= 12 \times 2400 = 28{,}800$$

$$28{,}800 \times 200 = 5{,}760{,}000$$

$$5{,}760{,}000 \times 512 = 2{,}949{,}120{,}000 \;\; \text{bytes}$$

$$= \frac{2{,}949{,}120{,}000}{2^{30}} \approx 2.747 \;\; \text{GB}$$

✅ **Answer: ≈ 2.75 GB.** *(2 marks)*

---

**Part (b.2) — Track Transfer Rate:**

$$R_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{N_S \times B \times \text{RPM}}{60} = \frac{200 \times 512 \times 10000}{60}$$

$$= \frac{1{,}024{,}000{,}000}{60} \approx 17{,}066{,}666.67 \;\; \text{bytes/sec}$$

$$\approx 16.28 \;\; \text{MB/s}$$

✅ **Answer: ≈ 16.28 MB/s.** *(2 marks)*

---

**Part (b.3) — Time to Read 200 KB Sequentially:**

**Step 1 — Bytes to read.**

$$D = 200 \times 1024 = 204{,}800 \;\; \text{bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Number of sectors to read.**

$$N_{\text{secs}} = \frac{204800}{512} = 400 \;\; \text{sectors}$$

**Step 3 — Number of full tracks spanned (data is contiguous).**

$$\text{Tracks} = \left\lceil \frac{400}{200} \right\rceil = 2 \;\; \text{tracks}$$

**Step 4 — Rotational latency (one initial wait).**

$$T_{\text{rot}}^{(\text{avg})} = \frac{30}{10000} = 0.003 \;\; \text{s} = 3 \;\; \text{ms}$$

**Step 5 — Transfer time for 2 full tracks worth of data (since data is contiguous and 400 sectors = 2 tracks exactly).**

$$T_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{204800}{17066666.67} \approx 0.012 \;\; \text{s} = 12 \;\; \text{ms}$$

(Equivalently, time to read 2 full tracks = $2 \times T_{\text{rot}} = 2 \times 6 \text{ ms} = 12$ ms.) ✅

**Step 6 — Total time.**

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{seek}} + T_{\text{rot}}^{(\text{avg})} + T_{\text{transfer}}$$

$$= 5 + 3 + 12 = 20 \;\; \text{ms}$$

✅ **Answer: 20 ms.** *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $N_H = 2 \times \text{platters}$**: Heads = surfaces, not platters. Each platter has 2 surfaces, each with its own head. Mistaking $N_H$ alone for platters loses **2 marks** in capacity problems.
>
> 2. **Using $T_{\text{rot}} / 2$ in milliseconds incorrectly**: Students often write $T_{\text{rot}} = 1/\text{RPM}$ in seconds, forgetting the **60** in the numerator. The correct form is $T_{\text{rot}} = 60 / \text{RPM}$ seconds.
>
> 3. **Mixing MB ($10^6$) and MiB ($2^{20}$)**: KTU examiners accept $2^{20}$ for MB in capacity conversions. Using $10^6$ may be marked down depending on the question wording.
>
> 4. **Assuming $N_S$ is constant in ZBR disks**: For ZBR disks, $N_S$ varies by zone. Use the **zone-specific** sector count, not the average.
>
> 5. **Skipping units**: KTU mandates units in every numerical answer. Always state **bytes, MB, ms, s** explicitly.
>
> 6. **Wrong CHS → LBA formula**: Use $\text{LBA} = (C \cdot N_H + H) \cdot N_S + (S - 1)$, **not** $(C \cdot N_H + H) \cdot N_S + S$. The $(-1)$ is because sector numbering is 1-based in CHS but 0-based in LBA.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Hard Disk: Geometry**

- ✅ **Disk geometry** = physical/logical organization of platters into tracks, sectors, cylinders.
- ✅ **Sector** = smallest addressable unit (512 B classical, 4 KiB modern).
- ✅ **Track** = one concentric circle on a surface.
- ✅ **Cylinder** = set of all co-radial tracks across all surfaces; head-switch is microseconds.
- ✅ **Platter × 2 = surfaces = heads** ($N_H$).
- ✅ **CHS** = 3D addressing; **LBA** = 1D linear addressing. Modern systems use LBA.
- ✅ **LBA to CHS**: $L = (C \cdot N_H + H) \cdot N_S + (S-1)$.
- ✅ **Capacity formula**: $C_{\text{disk}} = N_H \cdot N_C \cdot N_S \cdot B$ bytes.
- ✅ **Rotation period**: $T_{\text{rot}} = 60 / \text{RPM}$ seconds.
- ✅ **Average rotational latency** = $T_{\text{rot}} / 2 = 30 / \text{RPM}$ seconds.
- ✅ **Average seek time** = $T_{\text{seek}}^{(\text{max})} / 3$.
- ✅ **Total access time** = $T_{\text{seek}} + T_{\text{rot}}^{(\text{avg})} + T_{\text{transfer}}$.
- ✅ **Transfer rate** = $(N_S \cdot B \cdot \text{RPM}) / 60$ bytes/sec.
- ✅ **ZBR (Zoned Bit Recording)** groups tracks into zones, with more sectors on outer tracks — used in all modern HDDs.
- ✅ **Areal density** = BPI × TPI, measured in bits/in².
- ✅ **Cylinder access** is faster than head-to-cylinder access — exploited in **FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN** disk scheduling algorithms.
- ✅ **Inter-sector and inter-track gaps** are physical regions used for synchronization; they reduce usable capacity.
- ✅ **Servo information** is embedded in sectors to allow precise head positioning.
- ✅ **Advanced Format (AF)** uses 4-KiB physical sectors, with 512-byte emulation (512e) for OS compatibility.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disk Scheduling; Case Study: Linux I/O Schedulers - Elevator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Disk Scheduling; Case Study: Linux I/O Schedulers - Elevator # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Disk Scheduling? > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** > **Disk Scheduling** is a *resource management discipline* within the operating system kernel's I/O subsystem that determines the **service order** of competing block-device (disk) I/O requests awaiting processing in the disk queue. The objective is to **minimize mechanical latency** (seek time + rotational latency) and **maximize throughput** by re-ordering requests so that the disk arm's physical movement is optimized. The disk arm (actuator) is the dominant bottleneck of rotational storage. Every request to a different cylinder forces a *seek*, measured in milliseconds. The OS does NOT know in advance the cylinder layout, but it DOES know the pending request queue. By intelligently choosing *which request to service next*, the kernel can drastically reduce the **Total Head Movement (THM)**. ### Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Lift (Elevator) Analogy 🏥 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition:** Imagine a multi-storey hospital lift. Passengers (I/O requests) keep pressing different floor buttons (cylinder numbers). The lift operator (disk scheduler) can choose the order in which to visit floors. | Strategy in Lift Terms | Algorithm Equivalent | Real-World Effect | |---|---|---| | Serve the person who shouted first, in that order | **FCFS** | Lift jumps randomly between floors — exhausting! | | Always serve the nearest floor request | **SSTF** | Closest floor first; can starve far floors | | Move monotonically up, then down | **SCAN / Elevator** | Predictable; no starvatio | | Move up, then jump to ground floor and go up again | **C-SCAN** | Uniform wait time for all | | Move up only to the *highest pending floor*, then reverse | **LOOK** | No wasted travel to extremes | | C-SCAN + no travel to extremes | **C-LOOK** | Best uniform wait, minimal travel | The **SCAN algorithm** is literally called the **Elevator Algorithm** because its back-and-forth sweep is identical to how a building elevator services floors. ### 1.2 The Linux I/O Scheduler — A Case Study in Elevator Design Linux historically used a scheduler literally named the **"Linus Elevator"** (Linux 2.2 onwards), which was a direct implementation of the SCAN/LOOK algorithm with a merge-sort on request insertion. Modern Linux (≥ 2.6, current 6.x) replaces it with a pluggable multi-queue (blk-mq) framework offering **Deadline, BFQ, Kyber, and None** schedulers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Performance Metrics (highlighted for KTU):** > - **Seek Time (Ts)** — Time to position the read/write head over the correct track. Dominant cost. > - **Rotational Latency (Tr)** — Wait for the sector to rotate under the head. > - **Transfer Time (Tt)** — Time to actually read/write the data block. > - **Total Access Time = Ts + Tr + Tt** > - **Total Head Movement (THM)** — Sum of all cylinder-to-cylinder seeks (used to compare algorithms). --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Taxonomy of Disk Scheduling Algorithms ### A. First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) - **Logic:** Serve requests strictly in the order they arrive. - **Implementation:** Simple FIFO queue. - **Pros:** Zero overhead, perfectly *fair*, no starvation. - **Cons:** Ignores geometry → potentially catastrophic arm thrashing. - **Best Use Case:** Light load, fairness > throughput. ### B. Shortest Seek Time First (SSTF) - **Logic:** Always service the *nearest* request (closest cylinder). - **Implementation:** A min-heap or sorted list keyed by distance from current head. - **Pros:** Greedy → locally optimal, low THM. - **Cons:** **Starvation** of far-end requests; not optimal globally; high variance in response time. ### C. SCAN Algorithm (THE ELEVATOR) - **Logic:** The arm moves in one direction (say, increasing cylinder numbers), servicing every request along the way, **until the last cylinder**. Then it reverses and does the same in the opposite direction. - **Implementation:** Two-pass sorted list, plus current direction bit. - **Pros:** Bounded starvation; predictable wait; strong throughput. - **Cons:** Cylinders at extremes serviced only once per round trip. ### D. C-SCAN (Circular SCAN) - **Logic:** Like SCAN, but on reaching the end, the arm **jumps back to cylinder 0** without servicing any request on the return trip. Then it resumes service going forward. - **Pros:** Provides a **uniform wait time** for all requests (since each gets serviced at most once per *forward sweep*). - **Cons:** Wasteful jump (no service during return). ### E. LOOK and C-LOOK - **Optimization:** SCAN/C-SCAN need not travel to cylinder 0 or the maximum. They **"look ahead"** to the farthest pending request and reverse there. - **Result:** Identical service order to SCAN/C-SCAN but with strictly *less* head movement. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** > - **SCAN** = Elevator going to the **roof and basement**. > - **LOOK** = Elevator going to the **highest and lowest requested floors only**. ## 2.2 Linux I/O Scheduler Evolution (Case Study) | Scheduler | Era | Core Idea | Starvation Handling | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Linus Elevator** | Linux 2.2 | Single sorted queue + merge | None (greedy) | Historical teaching example | | **Deadline** | Linux 2.6 | 4 queues: read FIFO, write FIFO, read sorted, write sorted + per-request expiry | Per-request read/write **deadline** (500ms / 5s default) | DBs, general server | | **Anticipatory** | Linux 2.6 | Adds 8ms "anticipation" stall to catch dependent sync reads | Implicit (waits) | Workloads with locality | | **CFQ** | Linux 2.6–5 | Per-process time-sliced round-robin queues | Per-process fairness | Desktop, multi-user | | **Noop** | Linux 2.6+ | FIFO only — relies on device's internal queue | FIFO order | SSDs, NVMe, SAN | | **BFQ** | Linux 4.12+ | Budget-Fair-Queueing (proportional bandwidth, low latency) | Weighted fairness | Desktop, low-latency | | **Kyber / mq-deadline / None** | Linux 5+ (blk-mq) | Multi-queue, per-CPU, scales to thousands of devices | Deadline-based | Modern NVMe, datacenters | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is it called "Elevator" in Linux?** > The original Linux 2.2 *elevator* merged adjacent requests and sorted them by sector, then dispatched in a single monotonic sweep — **the exact behaviour of the SCAN disk scheduling algorithm**! The metaphor stuck. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Metric / Equation | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Total Access Time | $T_{access} = T_s + T_r + T_t$ | ms | Sum of three physical delays | | Average Seek Time (disk-specific) | $T_s \approx k \cdot d + b$ | ms | $d$=distance in cylinders, $k,b$=constants | | Rotational Latency (avg) | $T_r = \dfrac{1}{2r}$ | ms | $r$ = rotational speed in rev/sec | | Transfer Time | $T_t = \dfrac{b}{N \cdot r}$ | ms | $b$ = bytes to transfer, $N$ = bytes per track | | Total Head Movement (THM) | $THM = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \vert h_{i+1} - h_i \vert$ | cylinders | $h_i$ = cylinder of $i$-th serviced request | | Throughput | $\Theta = \dfrac{N_{req}}{T_{total}}$ | req/sec | Higher is better | | Variance of response time | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum (R_i - \bar{R})^2$ | ms² | Lower = more predictable | | Deadline expiry (Linux) | $T_{expiry} = T_{dispatch} + T_{deadline}$ | jiffies | Triggers starvation prevention | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Reminder for KTU exams:** Always express head movement in **cylinders (or tracks)**, not in bytes. The radial position determines the seek cost; angular position is rotational latency. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Database Engines (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB):** Reorder disk access to exploit prefetch and locality. - **Video Streaming Servers:** C-SCAN ensures *uniform jitter* across millions of requests. - **Solid State Drives (SSDs):** No mechanical arm → **Noop** scheduler is optimal (no reordering benefit). - **Datacenter Storage (NVMe over Fabrics):** Modern **multi-queue blk-mq** uses **None** scheduler because the device has thousands of internal queues. - **Embedded/Real-Time OS (RTEMS, VxWorks):** Custom SCAN variants guarantee bounded response. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Reference Problem (Used Universally for All Algorithms) > **Initial head position:** $h_{init} = 53$ > **Request queue (in arrival order):** $\{98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67\}$ > **Disk cylinder range:** $0$ to $199$ > **Direction of arm movement (where stated):** Towards *increasing* cylinder numbers. ## 3.2 Algorithm 1 — FCFS (FIFO) ### Step-by-Step Service Order The arm services the queue in the literal arrival order: $$53 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 14 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67$$ ### Step-by-Step Movement Calculation | Move # | From | To | Distance (cylinders) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 53 | 98 | $\vert 98-53 \vert = 45$ | | 2 | 98 | 183 | $\vert 183-98 \vert = 85$ | | 3 | 183 | 37 | $\vert 37-183 \vert = 146$ | | 4 | 37 | 122 | $\vert 122-37 \vert = 85$ | | 5 | 122 | 14 | $\vert 14-122 \vert = 108$ | | 6 | 14 | 124 | $\vert 124-14 \vert = 110$ | | 7 | 124 | 65 | $\vert 65-124 \vert = 59$ | | 8 | 65 | 67 | $\vert 67-65 \vert = 2$ | $$ \begin{aligned} THM_{FCFS} &= 45 + 85 + 146 + 85 + 108 + 110 + 59 + 2 \\ &= 640 \text{ cylinders} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Algorithm 2 — SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) ### Greedy Selection Process (Start at 53) Step 1: Pending = {98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67}. Distance from 53: - |98−53|=45, |183−53|=130, |37−53|=16, |122−53|=69, |14−53|=39, |124−53|=71, |65−53|=12, |67−53|=14 - **Minimum = 12 → 65** ✅ Step 2: At 65. Pending = {98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 67}. Distances: 33, 118, 28, 57, 51, 59, **2**. - **Minimum = 2 → 67** ✅ Step 3: At 67. Pending = {98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124}. Distances: 31, 116, 30, 55, 53, 57. - **Minimum = 30 → 37** ✅ Step 4: At 37. Pending = {98, 183, 122, 14, 124}. Distances: 61, 146, 85, **23**, 87. - **Minimum = 23 → 14** ✅ Step 5: At 14. Pending = {98, 183, 122, 124}. Distances: 84, 169, 108, 110. - **Minimum = 84 → 98** ✅ Step 6: At 98. Pending = {183, 122, 124}. Distances: 85, **24**, 26. - **Minimum = 24 → 122** ✅ Step 7: At 122. Pending = {183, 124}. Distances: 61, **2**. - **Minimum = 2 → 124** ✅ Step 8: At 124. Pending = {183}. Distance: **59 → 183** ✅ ### Service Order & Total Movement $$53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 14 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183$$ $$ \begin{aligned} THM_{SSTF} &= 12 + 2 + 30 + 23 + 84 + 24 + 2 + 59 \\ &= 236 \text{ cylinders} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** SSTF achieves a **63% reduction** vs FCFS. But notice: request 183 (the *farthest*) was served *last* — illustrating the **starvation risk**. ## 3.4 Algorithm 3 — SCAN (Elevator Algorithm) ### Logic Applied Arm moves from 53 → **increasing direction** → reaches 199 → reverses → moves to 0. Sort all requests > 53 in ascending order; sort all requests < 53 in descending order; append (or prepend) the end-cylinders. ### Sorted Lists - Forward (greater than 53): $\{65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183\}$ - Reverse (less than 53): $\{37, 14\}$ - End-of-disk reached before reverse: cylinder **199** - Service on return: $\{37, 14\}$ ### Service Order $$53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 199 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 14$$ ### Step-by-Step Movement | Move | From | To | Distance | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 53 | 65 | 12 | | 2 | 65 | 67 | 2 | | 3 | 67 | 98 | 31 | | 4 | 98 | 122 | 24 | | 5 | 122 | 124 | 2 | | 6 | 124 | 183 | 59 | | 7 | 183 | 199 | 16 | | 8 | 199 | 37 | 162 | | 9 | 37 | 14 | 23 | $$ \begin{aligned} THM_{SCAN} &= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 16 + 162 + 23 \\ &= 331 \text{ cylinders} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.5 Algorithm 4 — C-SCAN (Circular SCAN) ### Logic Applied Arm moves only in the *increasing* direction. After reaching 199, it **jumps** to 0 (no service on the return), then resumes increasing. ### Service Order $$53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 199 \rightarrow [\text{jump}] \rightarrow 0 \rightarrow 14 \rightarrow 37$$ ### Movement | Move | From | To | Distance | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1–7 | (same as SCAN forward) | | 12+2+31+24+2+59+16 = **146** | | 8 | 199 | 0 | 199 | | 9 | 0 | 14 | 14 | | 10 | 14 | 37 | 23 | $$ \begin{aligned} THM_{C\text{-}SCAN} &= 146 + 199 + 14 + 23 = 382 \text{ cylinders} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Trade-off:** C-SCAN moves *more* total cylinders than SCAN, but its *worst-case wait* is *uniform* — a critical property for **real-time and multimedia systems**. ## 3.6 Algorithm 5 — LOOK (SCAN Optimisation) ### Logic Applied No travel to extremes (199, 0). Reverse happens at the **farthest pending request**. ### Service Order $$53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 14$$ ### Movement $$ \begin{aligned} THM_{LOOK} &= (65-53) + (67-65) + (98-67) + (122-98) + (124-122) + (183-124) + (183-37) + (37-14) \\ &= 12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 146 + 23 \\ &= 299 \text{ cylinders} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.7 Algorithm 6 — C-LOOK (C-SCAN Optimisation) ### Service Order $$53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow [\text{jump}] \rightarrow 14 \rightarrow 37$$ ### Movement $$ \begin{aligned} THM_{C\text{-}LOOK} &= 146 + (183-14) + (37-14) \\ &= 146 + 169 + 23 = 338 \text{ cylinders} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.8 Comparative Analytical Matrix | Algorithm | THM (cylinders) | Starvation? | Uniform Wait? | Complexity | Best Workload | |---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---| | **FCFS** | **640** | ❌ No | ❌ No | $O(1)$ | Light, fairness-critical | | **SSTF** | **236** | ⚠️ Yes | ❌ No | $O(n \log n)$ | Locality-heavy, single-user | | **SCAN** | **331** | ❌ No | ❌ No | $O(n \log n)$ | Mixed workload | | **C-SCAN** | **382** | ❌ No | ✅ **Yes** | $O(n \log n)$ | Real-time, multimedia | | **LOOK** | **299** | ❌ No | ❌ No | $O(n \log n)$ | General purpose (modern default) | | **C-LOOK** | **338** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | $O(n \log n)$ | Real-time, optimized | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Counter-intuitive fact:** FCFS has the *highest* THM, yet SSTF's THM is *lower* than SCAN's in this example. Always compare numerically — never generalize. ## 3.9 Linux Deadline Scheduler — Analytical Walk-through The **deadline scheduler** in Linux maintains: - **Two sorted queues:** read FIFO, write FIFO. - **Two deadline queues:** read deadline (default **500 ms**), write deadline (default **5 s**). ### Decision Rule $$ \text{Dispatch} = \begin{cases} \text{earliest expired request} & \text{if any deadline expired (starvation prevention)} \\ \text{next request from sorted queue in current direction} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ ### Why Two Deadlines? - **Writes are batched and async** → larger deadline is acceptable. - **Reads block applications** → tight deadline prevents UI lag. - This is *engineered* to match **Elevator-like SCAN throughput** while providing **deadline-based QoS**. --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Disk Scheduling Algorithm Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Disk I/O Requests Queue] --> B{Scheduling Algorithm} B -->|FIFO Order| C[FCFS] B -->|Greedy Nearest| D[SSTF] B -->|Linear Sweep| E[SCAN Elevator] B -->|Circular Sweep| F[C-SCAN] B -->|Look Ahead| G[LOOK] B -->|Circular Look| H[C-LOOK] C --> C1[Simple, Fair
High THM] D --> D1[Low THM
Starvation Risk] E --> E1[No Starvation
End-Cylinder Travel] F --> F1[Uniform Wait
Wasted Return] G --> G1[Best of Both
No End Travel] H --> H1[Uniform + Efficient
Best for Real-time] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825 style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style H fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 ``` ## 4.2 Linux I/O Scheduler Stack — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph USERSPACE["User Space"] A1[Application read/write syscall] end subgraph KERNEL["Linux Kernel Block Layer"] B1[VFS Layer] B2[Page Cache] B3[I/O Scheduler Plug-in] B4[Generic Block Layer] B5[Device Driver] end subgraph DEVICES["Storage Devices"] C1[Rotational HDD] C2[SATA SSD] C3[NVMe SSD] end A1 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B3 --> B4 B4 --> B5 B3 --> S1{Scheduler Selection} S1 --> S2[None Noop] S1 --> S3[Deadline] S1 --> S4[BFQ] S1 --> S5[Kyber mq-deadline] S2 --> C3 S3 --> C1 S4 --> C2 S5 --> C3 style USERSPACE fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a style KERNEL fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 style DEVICES fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style S3 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 ``` ## 4.3 SCAN Elevator — Disk Head Movement Timeline ```mermaid timeline title SCAN Elevator Algorithm: Head Path from Cylinder 53 53 : Start 65 : Service R1 67 : Service R2 98 : Service R3 122 : Service R4 124 : Service R5 183 : Service R6 199 : End of Disk 37 : Service R7 reverse 14 : Service R8 reverse 0 : End of Reverse Sweep ``` ## 4.4 Linux Deadline Scheduler — Request State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> NewRequest NewRequest --> SortedQueue: Insert by sector NewRequest --> ReadFIFO: If read syscall NewRequest --> WriteFIFO: If write syscall ReadFIFO --> Expired: deadline 500ms passed WriteFIFO --> Expired: deadline 5s passed SortedQueue --> Dispatched: Arm reaches cylinder Expired --> Dispatched: Force dispatch Dispatched --> [*] note right of Expired: Starvation Prevention Trigger ``` --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Differentiate between SCAN and C-SCAN disk scheduling algorithms. Mention one disadvantage of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | SCAN (Elevator) | C-SCAN | |---|---|---| | Service after reaching end | Reverses and services in opposite direction | Jumps to cylinder 0 and starts a new forward sweep | | Wait time distribution | Non-uniform; cylinders near ends wait longer | Uniform — every request waits at most one full sweep | | Wasted movement | None in steady state | One full disk pass (199 cylinders) is wasted on the return jump | - **SCAN Disadvantage:** Cylinders just visited have *just* been serviced, causing bias towards the *middle cylinders*. *(1 mark)* - **C-SCAN Disadvantage:** The long jump from end-to-end cylinder is purely mechanical, no I/O is done. *(1 mark)* ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **What is the "elevator algorithm" in the context of disk scheduling? Why was the original Linux block I/O scheduler named "Linus Elevator"?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. The **SCAN algorithm** is commonly called the **Elevator algorithm** because the disk arm moves monotonically in one direction, servicing requests along its path, then reverses — exactly mimicking how a building elevator services floor requests as it moves up and down. *(1.5 marks)* 2. Linux kernel 2.2 introduced a scheduler that **merged adjacent requests** and **sorted them by sector** before dispatch, then served them in monotonic sweeps. Because of this SCAN-like behaviour, its author Linus Torvalds named it the **"Linus Elevator"** — establishing the now-famous "elevator" terminology in OS literature. *(1.5 marks)* --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students frequently **omit the initial head position in the calculation** (loses 1 mark). > - Confusing **SCAN** with **C-SCAN** movement (C-SCAN has a *jump* from end to 0). > - Confusing **SCAN** with **LOOK** (SCAN goes to 0 and 199; LOOK stops at extremes of pending requests). > - Not specifying the *direction* of arm movement — always state it explicitly. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]` **Suppose a disk has 200 cylinders (numbered 0 to 199). The head is initially at cylinder 53. The queue of pending requests in FIFO order is: 98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67. The arm is moving in the direction of increasing cylinder numbers.** **(a)** Calculate the total head movement using: (i) SSTF scheduling algorithm. (3 marks) (ii) SCAN scheduling algorithm. (4 marks) **(b)** Compare FCFS, SSTF and SCAN on the basis of throughput, starvation and response-time variance. Justify which is preferred for a **database server** workload.
