(7 marks) #### Model Solution **(a)(i) SSTF (3 marks):** Service order derived via greedy nearest-neighbour from 53: $$53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 14 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183$$ | Move | Distance | |:---:|:---:| | 53→65 | 12 | | 65→67 | 2 | | 67→37 | 30 | | 37→14 | 23 | | 14→98 | 84 | | 98→122 | 24 | | 122→124 | 2 | | 124→183 | 59 | $$THM_{SSTF} = 12+2+30+23+84+24+2+59 = \boxed{236 \text{ cylinders}}$$ **(a)(ii) SCAN (4 marks):** Service order (increasing then decreasing to 0): $$53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 199 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 14$$ $$ \begin{aligned} THM_{SCAN} &= (65-53)+(67-65)+(98-67)+(122-98)+(124-122)+(183-124)+(199-183) \\ &\quad + (199-37)+(37-14) \\ &= 12+2+31+24+2+59+16+162+23 = \boxed{331 \text{ cylinders}} \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Comparison (7 marks):** | Criterion | FCFS | SSTF | SCAN | |---|---|---|---| | **Throughput** | Low (jumps around) | High (locally optimal) | High (no idle return) | | **Starvation** | None | Possible (far requests wait) | None (bounded) | | **Response-time variance** | Very high | High (request-position dependent) | Moderate | | **Implementation complexity** | $O(1)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | **Justification for a database server (3 marks):** A database server issues **mixed random read/write** requests with critical latency requirements. **SCAN** is preferred because: 1. It provides *bounded worst-case response time* (no starvation — important for transactional workloads). 2. It achieves throughput close to SSTF but with predictable performance. 3. Modern Linux default is essentially **LOOK** (a SCAN variant), confirming SCAN-family suitability. Modern refinement: use **Linux Deadline scheduler** (deadline 500ms reads, 5s writes) which builds *on top of* SCAN to add per-request QoS guarantees. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **Linus Elevator scheduler** used in early Linux kernels. State **two limitations** that motivated the development of the **Deadline scheduler**. (6 marks) **(b)** For the same request queue as in Question A (initial head = 53, requests = {98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67}, disk range 0–199), calculate the total head movement using: (i) **C-SCAN** algorithm (4 marks) (ii) **LOOK** algorithm (4 marks) #### Model Solution **(a) Linus Elevator Scheduler (4 marks):** The **Linus Elevator** (Linux 2.2) was the first I/O scheduler in Linux to perform request merging and sorting. Its operation: 1. **Insertion:** When a new request arrives, it is inserted into a *sorted singly-linked list* ordered by sector number. Adjacent requests to existing entries are **merged** (reducing overhead). 2. **Dispatch:** At each I/O completion interrupt, the request at the *current head position* or just beyond is dispatched (SCAN-like monotonic sweep). > [!IMPORTANT] > Key design: one global queue, sorted by sector → implements a single-arm **SCAN** policy. **Two limitations that motivated the Deadline scheduler (2 marks):** 1. **Read starvation under heavy write load:** Because reads and writes shared one sorted queue, a steady stream of writes could indefinitely delay reads, hurting interactive responsiveness. The Deadline scheduler fixes this with **per-request expiry** (reads: 500 ms; writes: 5 s). 2. **No fairness across processes:** All processes competed for the same sorted queue, leading to unpredictable latency. Deadline scheduler adds **four queues** (read FIFO, write FIFO, plus two sorted) and dispatches based on the earliest expiring request, restoring QoS. **(b)(i) C-SCAN (4 marks):** Service only in increasing direction; jump from 199 → 0; resume increasing. $$53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 199 \rightarrow \text{[jump]} \rightarrow 0 \rightarrow 14 \rightarrow 37$$ $$ \begin{aligned} THM_{C\text{-}SCAN} &= 146 + (199-0) + (14-0) + (37-14) \\ &= 146 + 199 + 14 + 23 = \boxed{382 \text{ cylinders}} \end{aligned} $$ **(b)(ii) LOOK (4 marks):** Service in increasing direction up to *farthest pending* request (183), then reverse. **No** travel to 199 or 0. $$53 \rightarrow 65 \rightarrow 67 \rightarrow 98 \rightarrow 122 \rightarrow 124 \rightarrow 183 \rightarrow 37 \rightarrow 14$$ $$ \begin{aligned} THM_{LOOK} &= (65-53)+(67-65)+(98-67)+(122-98)+(124-122)+(183-124) \\ &\quad + (183-37)+(37-14) \\ &= 12+2+31+24+2+59+146+23 = \boxed{299 \text{ cylinders}} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorize for KTU):** ### A. Algorithm Properties - **FCFS** = FIFO; simplest; worst THM; no starvation. - **SSTF** = Greedy nearest; low THM; **starvation possible**. - **SCAN** = "Elevator"; sweeps to disk ends; no starvation; biased towards middle. - **C-SCAN** = Circular; uniform wait time; one wasted return jump. - **LOOK** = SCAN but stops at extreme *requests* (not extreme cylinders) → strictly less THM than SCAN. - **C-LOOK** = C-SCAN + LOOK; uniform wait + minimum THM. ### B. Linux I/O Scheduler Lineage - **Linus Elevator (Linux 2.2)** → SCAN-like with merging; first Linux scheduler. - **Deadline (Linux 2.6)** → SCAN-based + per-request expiry (R: 500 ms, W: 5 s); prevents read starvation. - **Anticipatory (Linux 2.6)** → adds 8 ms stall to catch sync reads; deprecated. - **CFQ (Linux 2.6)** → per-process fair time slices; replaced by BFQ in 5.x. - **BFQ (Linux 4.12+)** → budget-fair-queueing; ideal for desktop. - **Noop** → pure FIFO; for SSDs / devices with internal queueing. - **blk-mq family (Linux 5+):** **None, mq-deadline, Kyber, BFQ** — multi-queue, per-CPU; default for NVMe. ### C. Exam-Time Numerical Methodology 1. **State** initial head position and direction. 2. **Sort** requests in ascending order. 3. **Partition** into "forward" and "reverse" sets relative to current head. 4. **Walk** through the algorithm step-by-step, computing $|h_{i+1} - h_i|$ at each hop. 5. **Sum** all distances → THM. 6. **Compare** with other algorithms if asked. ### D. Key Constants to Memorize - Default **read deadline** in Linux = **500 ms** - Default **write deadline** in Linux = **5 s** - Anticipatory scheduler **stall window** = **8 ms** ### E. Common KTU 2-Mark Conceptual Traps - SCAN ≠ C-SCAN *(return sweep vs. jump)*. - LOOK ≠ SCAN *(extremes of cylinder vs. extremes of pending requests)*. - SSTF can **starve**; SCAN **cannot** starve. - For **SSDs / NVMe**, *Noop* or *None* scheduler is optimal because **seek time = 0**. > [!NOTE] > **One-line memory trick:** *"SCAN sweeps to the **wall**; LOOK stops at the **last door**."*

topics.title: Complete Fair Queuing; Files and Directories: The File System Interface - File Descriptor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Complete Fair Queuing; Files and Directories: The File System Interface - File Descriptor # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1A. Complete Fair Queuing (CFQ) — Disk I/O Scheduler ### Formal Definition > **Complete Fair Queuing (CFQ)** is a disk scheduling algorithm (and a class of I/O schedulers) that attempts to provide **fair allocation of disk bandwidth** among all processes issuing I/O requests. CFQ maintains a **per-process (or per-request-issuing-entity) sorted queue** and services requests from these queues in a **round-robin** manner, dispatching a configurable number of requests from each queue before moving to the next. The two key metrics for any disk scheduler are: - **Throughput** — number of I/O operations completed per unit time - **Average Seek Time / Response Time** — time taken to service a request CFQ optimizes for **fairness and latency** rather than raw throughput (unlike LOOK or SCAN), which is why it was the historical default I/O scheduler in the Linux kernel for **rotational Hard Disk Drives (HDDs)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 - Disk Scheduling):** > CFQ is one of the canonical disk scheduling algorithms you must know alongside **FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, and C-LOOK**. Unlike the others, CFQ is a *fairness-oriented* scheduler and is conceptually closer to a **CPU scheduler** than a traditional seek-time optimizer. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Toll Booth Analogy** 🚗 > Imagine a single toll booth on a highway. There are multiple cars in multiple lanes (each lane = a process issuing I/O). A greedy scheduler (like SSTF) would always pick the car closest to the booth, leaving cars in distant lanes waiting forever (**starvation**). CFQ, in contrast, says: *"I'll serve the next car from Lane 1, then the next car from Lane 2, then Lane 3, then back to Lane 1."* This guarantees that **no lane is ever starved**, and every process gets a fair share of the disk's arm time. ``` Standard Disk Schedulers (SSTF, SCAN) CFQ ───────────────────────────── ────────────────── "Minimize seek time globally" "Give every process equal turns" Risk: Starvation of distant requests No starvation; bounded latency Single global sorted queue Per-process sorted queues ``` --- ## 1B. File Descriptor — The File System Interface ### Formal Definition > A **File Descriptor (fd)** is a non-negative integer (an index) maintained by the operating system kernel in the per-process **File Descriptor Table**. It is an **abstract handle** that uniquely identifies an **open file** within a process, and is used as a parameter to system calls such as `read()`, `write()`, `close()`, and `lseek()` to perform I/O on that file. In the classical **Unix model**, every open file is referenced by exactly one of these small integers, and the table is typically bounded to a maximum size (e.g., default **1024** entries per process in Linux). The first three descriptors are reserved by convention: | FD Value | Symbolic Name | Purpose | |----------|---------------|---------| | **0** | `STDIN_FILENO` | Standard Input | | **1** | `STDOUT_FILENO` | Standard Output | | **2** | `STDERR_FILENO` | Standard Error | ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Hotel Room Key Analogy** 🏨 > Think of the **kernel** as a hotel. When you check in (the `open()` system call), the front desk (kernel) gives you a **room key with a number** — that number is your file descriptor. The key isn't the room itself; it's just a small, easy-to-carry *handle* that *refers* to your room (the actual file data, offset, and metadata). The front desk keeps a master registry (the open file table) that maps each key number to the actual room. When you leave (the `close()` system call), you return the key, and the room becomes available for the next guest. > > The same room can be assigned to multiple guests, each holding a **different key number** — this is how a single file can be opened multiple times by the same process. ### The Three-Layer Architecture > [!IMPORTANT] > In the **Unix file system model**, a file descriptor is the *top of a three-level indirection* that enables powerful features like **file sharing, redirection, and independent file offsets**: > > 1. **File Descriptor Table (per-process)** — maps `fd` → pointer to open file entry > 2. **Open File Table (system-wide)** — holds the file offset, access mode, and pointer to the inode > 3. **Inode Table (on-disk + in-memory)** — holds the actual file metadata (permissions, disk block locations, size) > > This separation is what allows **two file descriptors in the same process** to point to the *same* file while **maintaining different file offsets** (e.g., when you `dup2()` a descriptor). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2A. Complete Fair Queuing — Algorithmic Analysis ### Operational Logic of CFQ 1. **Queue Creation**: When a process P issues an I/O request, the CFQ scheduler inserts the request into a **per-process FIFO queue** associated with P. 2. **Sorting**: Within each per-process queue, requests are typically **sorted by sector number (ascending)** to minimize per-queue seek time. 3. **Round-Robin Dispatch**: The scheduler maintains a **round-robin pointer** that cycles through all non-empty process queues. 4. **Slice Budget**: From each active queue, CFQ dispatches a configurable number of requests (historically, a "quantum" of 4 requests or 1ms of disk time in the Linux implementation) before moving to the next queue. 5. **Idle Queue Handling**: If a process's queue is empty when its turn arrives, the scheduler **skips** it and moves to the next active queue. An **idle queue** is treated specially and may be served more aggressively to keep the disk busy. 6. **Starvation-Free**: Because every active queue gets a turn, **no process can be starved indefinitely** — a property that SSTF and even SCAN can violate. > [!NOTE] > **Why CFQ ≠ Simple Round-Robin** > A naive round-robin over requests would still cause long seeks. CFQ's clever trick is to **sort each per-process queue by sector address** so that consecutive requests dispatched *from the same queue* require minimal seeking, while still rotating fairly *between* queues. ### Comparison with Other Schedulers | Property | FCFS | SSTF | SCAN | **CFQ** | |----------|------|------|------|---------| | Fairness among processes | High (no starvation) | **Very Low** (starvation) | Medium | **High (explicit design goal)** | | Average Seek Time | Worst | **Best (greedy)** | Good | Good (per-queue sorted) | | Implementation Complexity | Trivial | Low | Medium | **High** | | Throughput on random I/O | Low | High | Medium | **High for mixed workloads** | | Starvation-Free | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No (edge) | ✅ **Yes** | | KTU Exam Focus | High | High | High | Medium (conceptual) | ### Real-World Utility - **Linux Kernel**: CFQ was the default block I/O scheduler for HDDs from kernel 2.6.x until kernel 4.x (replaced by `mq-deadline` and `bfq` — the *Budget Fair Queueing* scheduler, which is its modern descendant). - **Multimedia workloads**: CFQ-style fairness prevents one chatty process from causing glitches in a video streaming application by ensuring predictable disk latency. - **Multi-tenant systems**: In cloud/virtualized environments, fair queuing isolates noisy neighbors. --- ## 2B. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Disk Scheduling Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The formulas below are the **definitive KTU ESE cheat sheet** for any numerical problem on disk scheduling. Every numerical question in Part A or Part B uses one of these. ### Core Formulas | Formula | Description | Units | |---------|-------------|-------| | $$\text{Total Head Movement} = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \vert H_{i+1} - H_i \vert$$ | Sum of absolute differences between consecutive serviced cylinder numbers | **Cylinders** | | $$\text{Average Seek Time} = \frac{\text{Total Head Movement}}{n}$$ | Total movement divided by number of requests serviced | **Cylinders/request** | | $$\text{Throughput} = \frac{n}{T_{\text{total}}}$$ | Number of requests completed in total time | **Requests/second** | | $$\text{Rotational Latency} = \frac{1}{2 \times \text{RPM}} \times 60$$ | Average time for one-half disk rotation | **Seconds** | | $$\text{Access Time} = T_{\text{seek}} + T_{\text{rot}} + T_{\text{transfer}}$$ | Time to fetch a sector from disk | **ms** | | $$\text{Response Time}_i = T_{\text{completion}_i} - T_{\text{arrival}_i}$$ | Time a request waits plus service time | **ms** | | $$\text{Mean Response Time} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$ | Average response across all requests | **ms** | > **Critical Notation Rule for KTU Solutions**: Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ for absolute value in displayed equations. **Never** write `|x|` inside markdown tables — it breaks the column separator. --- ## 2C. File Descriptor — Deep Theoretical Analysis ### The Three-Tier Indirection Explained ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ LAYER 1: Per-Process File Descriptor Table │ │ (Each process has its own; lives in the process's PCB │ │ or kernel stack) │ │ ┌─────┬──────────────┐ │ │ │ fd │ pointer to ──│──────────────────────┐ │ │ │ 0 │ stdin entry │ │ │ │ │ 1 │ stdout entry │ │ │ │ │ 2 │ stderr entry │ │ │ │ │ 3 │ ──────────── │──┐ │ │ │ │ 4 │ ──────────── │──│──┐ │ │ │ │ 5 │ ──────────── │──│──│──┐ │ │ │ │ ... │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ └─────┴──────────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ └───────────────────────────│──│──│─────────────│────────────┘ │ │ │ │ ┌───────────────────▼──▼──▼─────────────▼────────────┐ │ LAYER 2: System-Wide Open File Table │ │ (One entry per open() call; shared by all procs)│ │ ┌──────────────────────┬──────────┐ │ │ │ File Offset: 1024 │ flags │ │ │ │ Access Mode: R/W │ ────── │ │ │ │ Pointer to inode ────│──┐ │ │ │ └──────────────────────┴──│───────┘ │ └─────────────────────────────│─────────────────────┘ │ ┌─────────────────────────────▼─────────────────────┐ │ LAYER 3: In-memory Inode Table (vnode/inode cache)│ │ ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ File Type, Permissions, Size │ │ │ │ Disk block pointers (direct, │ │ │ │ indirect, double-indirect) │ │ │ │ Timestamps, link count │ │ │ └──────────────────────────────────┘ │ └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ [ Actual Disk Blocks ] ``` ### Key Operations on File Descriptors | System Call | Header | Signature | Returns | |-------------|--------|-----------|---------| | `open` | `fcntl.h` | `int open(const char *path, int flags, mode_t mode)` | New `fd` or `-1` | | `read` | `unistd.h` | `ssize_t read(int fd, void *buf, size_t count)` | Bytes read or `0`/`-1` | | `write` | `unistd.h` | `ssize_t write(int fd, const void *buf, size_t count)` | Bytes written or `-1` | | `close` | `unistd.h` | `int close(int fd)` | `0` on success, `-1` on error | | `lseek` | `unistd.h` | `off_t lseek(int fd, off_t offset, int whence)` | New offset or `-1` | | `dup` | `unistd.h` | `int dup(int oldfd)` | New `fd` (lowest free) | | `dup2` | `unistd.h` | `int dup2(int oldfd, int newfd)` | `newfd` or `-1` | ### Special Properties Enabled by This Design - **Independence of offsets**: Two `fd`s in the same process pointing to the same inode have *independent* offsets in Layer 2. - **Reference counting**: The kernel keeps a **reference count** in each open file entry. Only when the last `close()` is called does the entry get freed and (if no link) the inode deleted. - **File sharing across processes**: `fork()` duplicates the parent's `fd` table — both parent and child share the **same** Layer 2 entry, so they share the file offset. (This is a classic KTU question.) # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3A. Worked Numerical — CFQ Disk Scheduling Problem > [!NOTE] > **KTU-Style Problem Statement**: > A disk has **200 cylinders** (numbered 0 to 199). The read/write head is currently at cylinder **53**. The I/O request queue, with their associated process IDs, is: > > | Arrival Order | Process | Requested Cylinder | > |---------------|---------|--------------------| > | 1 | P1 | 98 | > | 2 | P2 | 183 | > | 3 | P1 | 37 | > | 4 | P3 | 122 | > | 5 | P2 | 14 | > | 6 | P3 | 124 | > | 7 | P4 | 65 | > | 8 | P1 | 67 | > > Apply **Complete Fair Queuing (CFQ)** with a quantum of **1 request per process per turn**. Show the head movement sequence and compute the total head movement and average seek time. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Group requests into per-process queues and sort each queue in ascending order of cylinder number.** - **P1 queue**: requests at 98, 37, 67 → sorted: `37, 67, 98` - **P2 queue**: requests at 183, 14 → sorted: `14, 183` - **P3 queue**: requests at 122, 124 → sorted: `122, 124` - **P4 queue**: request at 65 → sorted: `65` **Step 2: Establish the round-robin dispatch order across active processes.** The processes in the order they first appear: P1, P2, P3, P4 → dispatch cycle is **P1 → P2 → P3 → P4 → P1 → P2 → P3 → P4 → ...** **Step 3: Dispatch one request per turn (quantum = 1), taking from the front of each per-process sorted queue.** - **Turn 1 (P1)**: Dispatch **37** from P1's queue. Head moves from 53 → 37. - **Turn 2 (P2)**: Dispatch **14** from P2's queue. Head moves from 37 → 14. - **Turn 3 (P3)**: Dispatch **122** from P3's queue. Head moves from 14 → 122. - **Turn 4 (P4)**: Dispatch **65** from P4's queue. Head moves from 122 → 65. - **Turn 5 (P1)**: Dispatch **67** from P1's queue. Head moves from 65 → 67. - **Turn 6 (P2)**: Dispatch **183** from P2's queue. Head moves from 67 → 183. - **Turn 7 (P3)**: Dispatch **124** from P3's queue. Head moves from 183 → 124. - **Turn 8 (P1)**: Dispatch **98** from P1's queue. Head moves from 124 → 98. P1's queue is now empty, P2's queue is empty, P3's queue is empty. P4's queue is also empty. Scheduler terminates. **Step 4: Tabulate the head movement and compute the total.** | Step | From | To | Movement $\vert \Delta \vert$ | |------|------|----|------------------------------| | 1 | 53 | 37 | $\vert 37 - 53 \vert = 16$ | | 2 | 37 | 14 | $\vert 14 - 37 \vert = 23$ | | 3 | 14 | 122 | $\vert 122 - 14 \vert = 108$ | | 4 | 122 | 65 | $\vert 65 - 122 \vert = 57$ | | 5 | 65 | 67 | $\vert 67 - 65 \vert = 2$ | | 6 | 67 | 183 | $\vert 183 - 67 \vert = 116$ | | 7 | 183 | 124 | $\vert 124 - 183 \vert = 59$ | | 8 | 124 | 98 | $\vert 98 - 124 \vert = 26$ | | **Total** | | | **407 cylinders** | **Step 5: Compute total and average head movement using the formula.** $$ \text{Total Head Movement} = 16 + 23 + 108 + 57 + 2 + 116 + 59 + 26 $$ $$ \text{Total Head Movement} = 39 + 108 + 57 + 2 + 116 + 59 + 26 $$ $$ \text{Total Head Movement} = 147 + 57 + 2 + 116 + 59 + 26 $$ $$ \text{Total Head Movement} = 204 + 2 + 116 + 59 + 26 $$ $$ \text{Total Head Movement} = 206 + 116 + 59 + 26 $$ $$ \text{Total Head Movement} = 322 + 59 + 26 $$ $$ \text{Total Head Movement} = 381 + 26 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Total Head Movement} = 407 \text{ cylinders}} $$ $$ \text{Average Seek Time} = \frac{\text{Total Head Movement}}{\text{Number of Requests}} = \frac{407}{8} = 50.875 \text{ cylinders/request} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key observation**: Note how the head **jumps** between far-apart sectors (e.g., 14 → 122 is a 108-cylinder jump). This is the cost of fairness. A pure SSTF scheduler would have produced a total head movement of around 200–250 cylinders for this request set — better seek time, but **with potential starvation** of process P4 (whose only request is at 65) in larger workloads. --- ## 3B. Analytical Matrix — File Descriptor vs File Pointer vs File Name This comparison is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**. Master it. | Aspect | File Name | File Descriptor | File Pointer (`FILE *`) | |--------|-----------|-----------------|-------------------------| | **What is it?** | Human-readable string path (e.g., `"/home/alice/data.txt"`) | Integer index into the per-process fd table | Pointer to a `FILE` struct in user-space (C library) | | **Layer** | User-space path string | Kernel layer (per-process) | User-space library (glibc) | | **Created by** | User/Programmer | `open()`, `socket()`, `pipe()` | `fopen()` (C library) | | **Lifetime** | As long as the path exists in the directory | From `open()` to `close()` | From `fopen()` to `fclose()` | | **Kernel-visible?** | No (kernel resolves it to an inode) | **Yes** | Indirectly (FILE contains an fd) | | **Buffered I/O?** | No | No (raw, unbuffered) | **Yes** (uses internal buffer) | | **Portability** | OS-specific syntax | POSIX (Unix/Linux) | C standard library (portable) | | **Underlying syscall** | `open(path, ...)` uses it | `read(fd, ...)`, `write(fd, ...)` | Wraps `read()`/`write()` via the fd inside `FILE` | | **Number of fds in a process** | N/A | Limited (default **1024** in Linux) | N/A | ### Conversion Between `FILE *` and `int fd` In Linux/POSIX, every `FILE *fp` has an internal integer fd accessible via: $$ \texttt{int fd} = \texttt{fileno(fp);} $$ And conversely: $$ \texttt{FILE *fp} = \texttt{fdopen(fd, "r");} $$ This proves the C library's `FILE *` is **conceptually a wrapper** around the kernel's file descriptor. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4A. CFQ — High-Level Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef processNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef queueNode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef schedulerNode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef diskNode fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 P1["Process P1
I/O Request issued"]:::processNode P2["Process P2
I/O Request issued"]:::processNode P3["Process P3
I/O Request issued"]:::processNode P4["Process P4
I/O Request issued"]:::processNode Q1["Per-Process Queue P1
Sorted by sector ASC
37 then 67 then 98"]:::queueNode Q2["Per-Process Queue P2
Sorted by sector ASC
14 then 183"]:::queueNode Q3["Per-Process Queue P3
Sorted by sector ASC
122 then 124"]:::queueNode Q4["Per-Process Queue P4
Sorted by sector ASC
65"]:::queueNode SCHED["CFQ Scheduler Core
Round Robin Pointer
Quantum = 1 request per process
Skips empty queues"]:::schedulerNode DISK["Physical Disk Head
Services one request at a time
Head movement tracked in cylinders"]:::diskNode P1 --> Q1 P2 --> Q2 P3 --> Q3 P4 --> Q4 Q1 --> SCHED Q2 --> SCHED Q3 --> SCHED Q4 --> SCHED SCHED -->|Dispatch 1| DISK DISK -.->|IO Completion| SCHED SCHED -->|Round Robin Next| Q1 SCHED -.-> Q2 SCHED -.-> Q3 SCHED -.-> Q4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram**: Each process gets its own *sorted* queue (yellow). The CFQ scheduler (green) cycles through these queues in round-robin order, dispatching a quantum of 1 request per process to the disk (red). The dashed line back from the disk represents an I/O-completion interrupt, which lets the scheduler advance to the next non-empty queue. ## 4B. File Descriptor — Complete Reference Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef procLayer fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sysLayer fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef inodeLayer fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef diskLayer fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 subgraph PROC["LAYER 1: PER-PROCESS FILE DESCRIPTOR TABLE (in PCB)"] direction TB A1["fd 0 = Pointer to stdin entry"]:::procLayer A2["fd 1 = Pointer to stdout entry"]:::procLayer A3["fd 2 = Pointer to stderr entry"]:::procLayer A4["fd 3 = Pointer to OpenFile entry 101"]:::procLayer A5["fd 4 = Pointer to OpenFile entry 102"]:::procLayer end subgraph SYS["LAYER 2: SYSTEM-WIDE OPEN FILE TABLE (in kernel memory)"] direction TB B1["OpenFile Entry 100
offset = 0, mode = R, refcount = 1"]:::sysLayer B2["OpenFile Entry 101
offset = 1024, mode = RW, refcount = 1"]:::sysLayer B3["OpenFile Entry 102
offset = 0, mode = R, refcount = 1"]:::sysLayer end subgraph INODE["LAYER 3: IN-MEMORY INODE TABLE (vnode cache)"] direction TB C1["Inode ID 5001
Permissions 0644, Size 8192 bytes
Block pointers: direct and indirect"]:::inodeLayer C2["Inode ID 5002
Permissions 0755, Size 4096 bytes
Block pointers: direct and indirect"]:::inodeLayer end subgraph DISK["LAYER 4: PHYSICAL DISK STORAGE"] direction TB D1["Data blocks for inode 5001
Located on sectors 2048-2063"]:::diskLayer D2["Data blocks for inode 5002
Located on sectors 8192-8199"]:::diskLayer end A4 --> B2 A5 --> B3 A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 B2 --> C1 B3 --> C2 B1 --> C1 C1 --> D1 C2 --> D2 ``` ## 4C. File Descriptor Lifecycle State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Closed: Initial state
fd slot free Closed --> Open: open(path, flags)
Kernel allocates lowest free fd
Creates open file entry
Increments inode refcount Open --> Open: read() / write() / lseek()
Updates offset in Open File Table Open --> Duplicated: dup() or dup2()
New fd points to same Open File entry
refcount incremented Duplicated --> Duplicated: Each fd has independent lseek Open --> Closed: close(fd)
refcount decremented
If refcount = 0, entry freed Duplicated --> Closed: close(fd)
Other duplicate fds remain valid Closed --> [*]: Slot reusable by next open() ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Insight**: When a process calls `fork()`, the child process inherits a **copy of the parent's fd table** — but the entries in Layer 2 are *shared*. This is why `fork()` enables seamless redirection of stdin/stdout in shell pipelines. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions ### Question 1: Define Complete Fair Queuing (CFQ). How does it differ from SSTF? **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **Model Answer**: **Complete Fair Queuing (CFQ)** is a disk scheduling algorithm that maintains a separate sorted queue for each process issuing I/O requests and services these queues in a **round-robin** manner, dispatching a fixed quantum of requests from each queue per turn. **Key differences from SSTF**: | Aspect | SSTF | CFQ | |--------|------|-----| | Selection criterion | Globally nearest request to current head position | Round-robin across per-process queues | | Starvation | **Possible** (distant requests may wait forever) | **Impossible** (every active queue gets a turn) | | Queue structure | Single global sorted queue | Multiple per-process sorted queues | | Optimization goal | Minimize total seek time | Provide **fair disk bandwidth** to all processes | --- ### Question 2: What is a file descriptor? List the three standard file descriptors in Unix. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]** **Model Answer**: A **file descriptor** is a non-negative integer that acts as an **abstract handle** to identify an open file within a process. It serves as an index into the per-process **File Descriptor Table** maintained by the kernel. The three **standard file descriptors** automatically opened for every process are: 1. **File descriptor 0 (`STDIN_FILENO`)** — Standard Input, attached to the keyboard by default. 2. **File descriptor 1 (`STDOUT_FILENO`)** — Standard Output, attached to the terminal by default. 3. **File descriptor 2 (`STDERR_FILENO`)** — Standard Error, used for unbuffered error messages. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Choose ONE) ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks): Disk Scheduling with CFQ **(a)** [7 Marks] Consider a disk with **500 cylinders** (numbered 0 to 499). The head is initially at cylinder **100**. The I/O request queue, tagged by process, is: | Order | Process | Cylinder | |-------|---------|----------| | 1 | P1 | 30 | | 2 | P2 | 280 | | 3 | P1 | 150 | | 4 | P3 | 220 | | 5 | P2 | 90 | | 6 | P3 | 450 | | 7 | P1 | 200 | | 8 | P2 | 410 | Apply **Complete Fair Queuing (CFQ)** with a quantum of **1 request per turn**. Draw the head movement sequence and compute the total head movement and average seek time. **(b)** [7 Marks] Compare FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, and CFQ scheduling algorithms in terms of fairness, average seek time, and starvation. Justify why CFQ is preferred in time-sharing systems with mixed workloads. #### Model Solution for (a): **Step 1**: Sort each per-process queue by cylinder number (ascending). - **P1**: 30, 150, 200 - **P2**: 90, 280, 410 - **P3**: 220, 450 **Step 2**: Round-robin order of first appearance: **P1 → P2 → P3** → repeat. **Step 3**: Dispatch with head starting at 100, quantum = 1. | Turn | Process | Dispatched Cylinder | Head Move $\vert \Delta \vert$ | |------|---------|---------------------|------------------------------| | 1 | P1 | 30 | $\vert 30 - 100 \vert = 70$ | | 2 | P2 | 90 | $\vert 90 - 30 \vert = 60$ | | 3 | P3 | 220 | $\vert 220 - 90 \vert = 130$ | | 4 | P1 | 150 | $\vert 150 - 220 \vert = 70$ | | 5 | P2 | 280 | $\vert 280 - 150 \vert = 130$ | | 6 | P3 | 450 | $\vert 450 - 280 \vert = 170$ | | 7 | P1 | 200 | $\vert 200 - 450 \vert = 250$ | | 8 | P2 | 410 | $\vert 410 - 200 \vert = 210$ | All queues now empty. **Step 4**: Total head movement. $$ \text{Total} = 70 + 60 + 130 + 70 + 130 + 170 + 250 + 210 $$ $$ \text{Total} = 130 + 130 + 70 + 130 + 170 + 250 + 210 $$ $$ \text{Total} = 260 + 70 + 130 + 170 + 250 + 210 $$ $$ \text{Total} = 330 + 130 + 170 + 250 + 210 $$ $$ \text{Total} = 460 + 170 + 250 + 210 $$ $$ \text{Total} = 630 + 250 + 210 $$ $$ \text{Total} = 880 + 210 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Total Head Movement} = 1090 \text{ cylinders}} $$ $$ \text{Average Seek Time} = \frac{1090}{8} = 136.25 \text{ cylinders/request} $$ #### Model Solution for (b): **Comparison Table**: | Algorithm | Fairness | Avg. Seek Time | Starvation-Free? | |-----------|----------|----------------|------------------| | **FCFS** | High (FIFO order) | High (no optimization) | ✅ Yes | | **SSTF** | Low (greedy nearest) | **Best** (locally optimal) | ❌ No | | **SCAN** | Medium (elevator algorithm) | Good | ❌ No (edge starvation) | | **CFQ** | **Highest** (explicit design) | Good (per-queue sorted) | ✅ **Yes** | **Justification for time-sharing systems**: In a time-sharing OS, multiple interactive users run simultaneously. SSTF would let one chatty process monopolize the disk. CFQ guarantees that each process gets a bounded fraction of disk time per cycle, giving **predictable latency** to all users — essential for shell commands and interactive applications. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks): File Descriptors in Depth **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **three-tier indirection** of file representation in Unix — File Descriptor Table, Open File Table, and Inode Table. How does this design enable **file sharing** between parent and child after a `fork()`? **(b)** [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the **lifecycle of a file descriptor** from `open()` to `close()`. What is the role of the **reference count** in the Open File Table, and how does it interact with the `dup()` system call? #### Model Solution for (a): The Unix kernel uses a **three-level indirection** to represent an open file: 1. **Layer 1 — Per-Process File Descriptor Table**: Each process has a small array (typically 1024 entries) of pointers. Index `i` into this array is the file descriptor `fd = i`. The entry points to a Layer 2 entry. 2. **Layer 2 — System-Wide Open File Table**: Each entry contains the **file offset**, **access mode flags** (O_RDONLY, O_WRONLY, O_RDWR), **status flags** (O_APPEND, O_NONBLOCK), and a pointer to the Layer 3 inode. The same file can have multiple entries here (e.g., one per `open()` call). 3. **Layer 3 — In-Memory Inode Table (vnode cache)**: Contains the file's **metadata** — type, permissions, size, timestamps, and pointers to the actual disk blocks containing the file's data. **How this enables file sharing after `fork()`**: When `fork()` is called, the kernel creates a **copy of the parent's Layer 1 table** in the child's PCB. However, both parent and child point to the **same Layer 2 entry** (the kernel does not duplicate Layer 2 — it merely increments the reference count). This means: - Both processes share the **same file offset** (writes from one are visible from the other at the shared offset). - The underlying inode is **not deleted** until *all* references are closed. - This is exactly how shell pipelines (`ls | grep foo`) work: both `ls` and `grep` have an fd pointing to the same pipe inode, with the write-end of `ls` and the read-end of `grep` sharing the pipe buffer via the same file-table entry. #### Model Solution for (b): **Lifecycle Diagram** (text-rendered for clarity, as the Mermaid version is in Section 4): ``` [fd slot free in Layer 1] │ │ open("/path", O_RDWR) ▼ ┌────────────────────────────────┐ │ Kernel allocates lowest free fd│ │ Creates new Layer 2 entry │ │ Increments inode refcount │ │ Returns fd number to user │ └────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ read(fd, buf, n) / write(fd, buf, n) / lseek(fd, off, whence) ▼ ┌────────────────────────────────┐ │ Layer 2 offset is updated │ │ Kernel performs disk I/O │ │ Returns to user-space │ └────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ dup(fd) or fork() ▼ ┌────────────────────────────────┐ │ A NEW fd in Layer 1 is created │ │ pointing to the SAME Layer 2 │ │ Layer 2 refcount incremented │ │ Offsets are now SHARED │ └────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ close(fd) ▼ ┌────────────────────────────────┐ │ Layer 2 refcount decremented │ │ If refcount == 0: │ │ - Layer 2 entry is freed │ │ - Inode refcount dec'd │ │ - If inode refcount == 0 │ │ AND link count == 0, │ │ file blocks are freed │ │ - Layer 1 slot marked free │ └────────────────────────────────┘ ``` **Role of the reference count**: The reference count in the Open File Table entry is a **bookkeeping integer** that tracks how many file descriptors (across all processes) currently point to this entry. - On `open()`: refcount = 1 - On `dup()` or `fork()`: refcount += 1 - On `close()`: refcount -= 1; when it reaches **0**, the entry is freed and the inode reference is released. This mechanism is what makes the `dup2(oldfd, newfd)` call safe for shell redirection: even if the parent and child both have the same fd open, the underlying resources are correctly cleaned up only when **both** sides have closed their handles. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Before You Write Your Exam:** > > 1. **CFQ Numerical Errors**: The most common mistake is **forgetting to sort each per-process queue by cylinder number**. The round-robin is *across queues*, not across all requests globally. If you skip the per-queue sort, you will get the order wrong and lose 3–4 marks. > > 2. **Refcount Confusion**: In fork/dup questions, students often write *"the file is duplicated"* — this is wrong. Only **Layer 1 is duplicated**; Layer 2 and Layer 3 are *shared*. Wording matters — examiners deduct marks for vague answers. > > 3. **Mixing `FILE *` with `fd`**: Do not use `FILE *` in Part B answers about file descriptors. The `FILE *` is a user-space C-library construct; the file descriptor is a kernel-level integer. Examiners look for this distinction. > > 4. **Standard FDs**: Many students write "fd 0, 1, 2 are for input, output, error" without naming the symbolic constants `STDIN_FILENO`, `STDOUT_FILENO`, `STDERR_FILENO`. The 1-mark question on this often **requires** the symbolic name. Add it. > > 5. **CFQ vs SSTF vs SCAN**: In comparison questions, students often describe only the *seek-time* aspect and ignore **fairness and starvation**. CFQ's defining feature is fairness — you must mention it explicitly. > > 6. **Absolute Value Notation**: In numerical answers, write $\vert x \vert$ (LaTeX), **not** `|x|` (ASCII pipe). Bare pipes inside markdown tables will break the table rendering on the digital valuation system. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam** > > **Complete Fair Queuing (CFQ):** > - ⭐ CFQ = **per-process sorted queues + round-robin dispatch** (two key ideas) > - ⭐ Quantum = configurable number of requests per queue per turn (Linux default: 4) > - ⭐ **Fairness and starvation-freedom** are the explicit design goals — *not* minimum seek time > - ⭐ Comparison trap: **CFQ gives better latency fairness than SSTF/SCAN but worse raw throughput** > - ⭐ Modern successor in Linux: `bfq` (Budget Fair Queueing) and `mq-deadline` > - ⭐ Numerical approach: (1) sort each per-process queue ASC, (2) cycle through process list, (3) dispatch 1 at a time, (4) sum $\vert \Delta \vert$ movements > > **File Descriptors:** > - ⭐ A file descriptor is an **integer index** (0, 1, 2, 3, …) into the per-process File Descriptor Table > - ⭐ Standard fds: **0 = stdin, 1 = stdout, 2 = stderr** > - ⭐ Default per-process limit in Linux: **1024** (soft limit, configurable) > - ⭐ **Three layers**: Per-Process FD Table → System Open File Table → Inode Table > - ⭐ `fork()` duplicates Layer 1; child shares Layer 2 and Layer 3 with parent > - ⭐ `dup()` and `dup2()` create a new fd pointing to the same Layer 2 entry > - ⭐ `close()` decrements the refcount; resource freed **only when refcount = 0** > - ⭐ `FILE *` (C library) is a *user-space wrapper* around the kernel's integer fd > > **Cross-Cutting Concepts:** > - ⭐ Both topics emphasize **indirection** (per-process queue in CFQ; three-tier tables in FS) > - ⭐ Both topics emphasize **fairness/sharing** (CFQ = fair disk access; fd table = safe file sharing) > - ⭐ Common KTU question pattern: "Compare X and Y" → always use a **table** with at least 3 dimensions

topics.title: Reading and Writing Files (Sequential and Random Access)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Reading and Writing Files (Sequential and Random Access) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **File Access** is the mechanism by which a process transfers data between its **user buffer** (in main memory) and a **secondary storage device** (typically a magnetic disk or SSD) through a sequence of well-defined **system calls** managed by the Operating System kernel. In the context of the **UNIX/Linux** and modern OS file model, every file is treated as a **flat stream of bytes**, and the kernel maintains a per-process **file descriptor table**, a system-wide **open file table**, and an **inode table** (in UNIX-like systems) to track open files. Reading and writing can be performed in two fundamentally different paradigms: | Paradigm | Definition | Movement Pattern | |----------|------------|------------------| | **Sequential Access** | Bytes are read/written in **linear, contiguous order** starting from a current file pointer that advances automatically. | Forward-only (typically) | | **Random Access** | Bytes can be read/written at **any arbitrary offset** within the file by explicitly repositioning the file pointer before each I/O. | Arbitrary jumps | ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **📖 Analogy — The Cassette Tape vs. The USB Drive** > > - **Sequential Access** is like listening to a **cassette tape** 🎞️. To reach a song at the end, you must *fast-forward* through every preceding song. The "read head" only moves **forward** (or rewinds backward, slowly). > - **Random Access** is like playing songs on a **USB MP3 player** 🎵. You tap *any* song directly, and the device jumps the playhead **instantly** to the correct location without playing intermediate content. > > The **file pointer** in an OS plays the exact same role as the read head on a cassette or the cursor on an MP3 player. ### 1.3 Key System Calls (POSIX Standard) The standard library wraps these UNIX system calls, but conceptually all file I/O reduces to: - `open()` — Acquires a file descriptor and brings the file into the **open file table**. - `read()` — Copies **n bytes** from the file (at the current offset) into a user buffer. - `write()` — Copies **n bytes** from a user buffer into the file at the current offset. - `lseek()` — **Repositioning call**: changes the file pointer to an arbitrary offset (foundation of random access). - `close()` — Releases the file descriptor and removes the entry from the open file table. ### 1.4 The File Descriptor (FD) > [!IMPORTANT] > A **File Descriptor** is a non-negative integer index (0, 1, 2, ...) into the per-process **file descriptor table**. By convention: > - **FD 0** → Standard Input (`stdin`) > - **FD 1** → Standard Output (`stdout`) > - **FD 2** → Standard Error (`stderr`) > - New files opened by the process receive the lowest available FD (typically 3, 4, 5, ...). The file descriptor does *not* directly point to the file data. It indexes an entry in the **File Descriptor Table**, which in turn points to a system-wide **Open File Table** entry, which finally points to the **Inode** containing the actual disk locations of the data blocks. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Sequential File Access — Operational Logic In sequential access, the **read/write pointer** advances by exactly the number of bytes transferred after every `read()` or `write()` call. **Step-by-step logic of a sequential read:** 1. Process issues `read(fd, buf, n)`. 2. Kernel checks the current **file offset** in the open file table. 3. Kernel computes the corresponding **disk block number** from the inode's block map. 4. If the block is **not** in the **buffer cache**, a **disk I/O** is initiated. 5. Bytes are copied from the cache (or freshly read disk block) into `buf`. 6. The file offset is **incremented by n**. 7. The number of bytes successfully read is returned to the process. > [!NOTE] > **Why use sequential access?** It maximizes the **buffer cache hit ratio** and exploits the disk's high **transfer bandwidth** because consecutive blocks are read in one rotation, often using **read-ahead (pipelining)**. ### 2.2 Random File Access — Operational Logic Random access requires the **`lseek()`** system call (or `fseek()` in the C stdlib) to *reposition* the file pointer before issuing I/O. ``` lseek(fd, offset, SEEK_SET); // offset from beginning lseek(fd, offset, SEEK_CUR); // offset from current position lseek(fd, offset, SEEK_END); // offset from end of file ``` **Step-by-step logic of a random read:** 1. Process issues `lseek(fd, 5000, SEEK_SET)` — moves pointer to byte 5000. 2. Process issues `read(fd, buf, 100)` — reads 100 bytes from byte 5000. 3. The kernel calculates the block(s) containing bytes 5000–5099. 4. Disk I/O occurs (or buffer cache lookup). 5. Pointer advances by 100 to byte 5100. > [!IMPORTANT] > Random access is **not** a property of the *file* — it is a property of the *I/O pattern* of the *process*. A file can be accessed sequentially at one moment and randomly the next, by simply calling `lseek()` between reads. ### 2.3 The Buffer Cache (Performance Multiplier) > [!NOTE] > The **Buffer Cache** is a region of kernel memory that caches recently read disk blocks. It transforms a slow disk access (≈ **5–10 ms** for HDD) into a fast memory access (≈ **100 ns**) — a speedup of roughly **5 orders of magnitude**. When a process reads a block, the kernel first checks the cache. On a **cache hit**, no disk I/O is performed. On a **cache miss**, the block is fetched from disk and inserted into the cache (possibly evicting a less-recently-used block). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are fair game for KTU numerical problems.** | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | Total disk access time | $T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$ | $T_{seek}$ = avg seek time (ms); $T_{rot}$ = rotational latency (ms); $T_{transfer}$ = data transfer time (ms) | | 2 | Rotational latency | $T_{rot} = \dfrac{1}{2r} = \dfrac{60}{2 \times RPM} \times 1000$ | $r$ = rotations/sec; $RPM$ = rotations per minute | | 3 | Transfer time for $n$ bytes | $T_{transfer} = \dfrac{n}{B \times r}$ | $B$ = block size (bytes); $n$ = bytes transferred | | 4 | Avg transfer time per block (sequential) | $T_{seq} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + \dfrac{1}{B \times r}$ | seconds per block | | 5 | Avg transfer time per block (random) | $T_{rnd} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + \dfrac{1}{B \times r}$ | same per-block cost, but **no read-ahead benefit** | | 6 | Throughput of sequential read | $BW = \dfrac{B}{T_{rot} + T_{transfer}}$ | bytes per second | | 7 | Effective access time with cache | $T_{eff} = h \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{disk}$ | $h$ = hit ratio (0 to 1); $T_{cache} \approx 1\mu s$, $T_{disk} \approx 10\,ms$ | | 8 | Cache hit ratio | $h = \dfrac{\text{Cache Hits}}{\text{Total Accesses}}$ | dimensionless | | 9 | New file offset after sequential read of $n$ bytes | $P_{new} = P_{old} + n$ | byte offset | | 10 | New file offset after `lseek` | $P_{new} = \text{base} + \text{offset}$ | base = SET/CUR/END | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Databases (DBMS):** Use **random access** for indexed B-tree lookups; **sequential access** for full table scans and log files. - **Multimedia players:** Use **sequential access** with massive buffer caches for streaming. - **Compilers & Linkers:** Use **random access** to update symbol tables and jump to specific source-code positions. - **Operating Systems:** The page cache, inode cache, and directory cache are all **random-access** buffer caches used to accelerate file I/O. - **Solid State Drives (SSDs):** Unlike HDDs, random access time is **almost equal** to sequential access time (no seek, no rotation), making the distinction less critical for SSD-backed systems. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Derivation — Rotational Latency from RPM > [!NOTE] > This is a KTU-favourite numerical. Given the disk's rotational speed, derive the **average rotational latency**. **Given:** - Disk rotates at $RPM = 7200$ rotations per minute. **Step 1:** Convert RPM to rotations per second. $$ r = \frac{7200}{60} = 120 \text{ rotations/second} $$ **Step 2:** Time taken for **one full rotation** (worst case rotational latency). $$ T_{rot_{max}} = \frac{1}{r} = \frac{1}{120} \approx 8.33 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3:** On average, the desired sector is **half a rotation away** from the current head position. $$ T_{rot_{avg}} = \frac{1}{2r} = \frac{1}{240} \approx 4.17 \text{ ms} $$ **Answer:** The average rotational latency is **4.17 ms**. --- ### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Effective Access Time with Buffer Cache > [!NOTE] > A KTU-classic two-part numerical combining a slow disk with a fast cache. **Given:** - Cache access time $T_{cache} = 1\,\mu s = 0.001\,ms$ - Disk access time $T_{disk} = 10\,ms$ - Hit ratio $h = 0.90$ (90%) **Step 1:** Substitute into the effective access time formula. $$ T_{eff} = h \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{disk} $$ **Step 2:** Insert the numerical values. $$ T_{eff} = (0.90)(0.001) + (0.10)(10) $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate each term. $$ T_{eff} = 0.0009 + 1.0 $$ **Step 4:** Final sum. $$ T_{eff} \approx 1.0009 \text{ ms} $$ **Observation:** A 90% hit ratio reduces the access time from **10 ms to ≈ 1 ms** — a 10× speedup from caching alone. --- ### 3.3 Worked Derivation — Required Hit Ratio for a Target Speedup **Given:** - Same disk/cache as above. - Target: $T_{eff} \le 0.1\,ms$ (i.e., 100× speedup). **Step 1:** Set up the inequality. $$ h \cdot (0.001) + (1 - h) \cdot (10) \le 0.1 $$ **Step 2:** Expand and collect terms in $h$. $$ 0.001h + 10 - 10h \le 0.1 $$ **Step 3:** Group $h$ terms. $$ -9.999h \le 0.1 - 10 $$ **Step 4:** Simplify the right-hand side. $$ -9.999h \le -9.9 $$ **Step 5:** Divide by $-9.999$ (flip the inequality). $$ h \ge \frac{9.9}{9.999} \approx 0.9901 $$ **Answer:** A hit ratio of **≈ 99.01%** is required to achieve $T_{eff} \le 0.1\,ms$. --- ### 3.4 Worked Derivation — Sequential vs Random Transfer Time > [!NOTE] > A standard KTU Part B derivation: compare the time to read N blocks sequentially vs randomly. **Given:** - $T_{seek} = 8\,ms$ (average) - $T_{rot} = 4.17\,ms$ - Block size $B = 4\,KB$ - Transfer rate = $100\,MB/s$ (i.e., $T_{transfer} = 1/B/r = B/100\text{MB/s}$) - Number of blocks $N = 1000$ **Per-block transfer time** (theoretical, no overhead): $$ t_{xf} = \frac{B}{\text{rate}} = \frac{4096}{100 \times 10^6} \approx 0.04096\,ms $$ **Step 1: Random access (1000 separate `read` calls).** Each call pays a full seek + rotation. $$ T_{rand} = N \times (T_{seek} + T_{rot} + t_{xf}) $$ $$ T_{rand} = 1000 \times (8 + 4.17 + 0.04096) $$ $$ T_{rand} = 1000 \times 12.21096 \approx 12210.96\,ms \approx 12.21\,s $$ **Step 2: Sequential access (1 `read` call for 1000 blocks).** Only **one** seek + rotation is needed; the rest is pure transfer. $$ T_{seq} = (T_{seek} + T_{rot}) + N \cdot t_{xf} $$ $$ T_{seq} = (8 + 4.17) + 1000 \times 0.04096 $$ $$ T_{seq} = 12.17 + 40.96 = 53.13\,ms $$ **Step 3: Speedup factor.** $$ \text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{rand}}{T_{seq}} = \frac{12210.96}{53.13} \approx 229.8 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key insight for KTU answers:** Sequential access is roughly **230× faster** than random access for the same 4 MB of data, because random access repeats the expensive seek/rotation for every block while sequential access amortizes it over many blocks. --- ### 3.5 Worked Derivation — New File Offset After Mixed Operations > [!NOTE] > Common trace-style numerical. **Given:** A file is opened, and the following sequence of operations is performed (initial offset = 0): 1. `read(fd, buf, 100)` 2. `lseek(fd, 500, SEEK_CUR)` 3. `read(fd, buf, 200)` 4. `lseek(fd, 0, SEEK_SET)` 5. `write(fd, buf, 50)` **Step-by-step offset evolution:** $$ P_0 = 0 $$ $$ P_1 = P_0 + 100 = 100 $$ $$ P_2 = P_1 + 500 = 600 $$ $$ P_3 = P_2 + 200 = 800 $$ $$ P_4 = 0 \quad \text{(SEEK_SET resets to byte 0)} $$ $$ P_5 = P_4 + 50 = 50 $$ **Final offset = 50.** The file pointer now points to byte 50, ready for the next operation. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Sequential vs Random Access — Pattern Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SEQ["SEQUENTIAL ACCESS PATTERN"] direction LR B0["Block 0"] --> B1["Block 1"] --> B2["Block 2"] --> B3["Block 3"] --> B4["Block N"] end subgraph RND["RANDOM ACCESS PATTERN"] direction LR R0["Block 0"] -.-> R2["Block 2"] R2 -.-> R4["Block 4"] R4 -.-> R1["Block 1"] R1 -.-> R3["Block 3"] end style SEQ fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000 style RND fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style B0 fill:#b2ebf2,color:#000 style B1 fill:#b2ebf2,color:#000 style B2 fill:#b2ebf2,color:#000 style B3 fill:#b2ebf2,color:#000 style B4 fill:#b2ebf2,color:#000 style R0 fill:#ffe0b2,color:#000 style R1 fill:#ffe0b2,color:#000 style R2 fill:#ffe0b2,color:#000 style R3 fill:#ffe0b2,color:#000 style R4 fill:#ffe0b2,color:#000 ``` > **Reading guide:** Solid arrows show the **forward-only linear traversal** of sequential access. Dotted arrows show the **arbitrary jumps** characteristic of random access, where any block can be reached in any order. --- ### 4.2 Kernel File I/O Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P["USER PROCESS
read fd buf n
lseek fd offset whence
write fd buf n"] FD["PER PROCESS
FILE DESCRIPTOR TABLE
FD 0 1 2 3 4 ..."] OFT["SYSTEM WIDE
OPEN FILE TABLE
Offset Status Flags"] INODE["INODE TABLE
File Type Permissions
Block Pointers Size"] BC["BUFFER CACHE
Hash Table of
Cached Disk Blocks"] DSK["PHYSICAL DISK
HDD OR SSD
Platters Tracks Sectors"] P -->|"system call"| FD FD -->|"indexes entry"| OFT OFT -->|"points to"| INODE INODE -->|"maps offset to block"| BC BC -->|"cache miss"| DSK DSK -->|"read block"| BC BC -->|"delivers data"| P style P fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style FD fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style OFT fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style INODE fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 style BC fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style DSK fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000 ``` > **Reading guide:** Every `read`/`write`/`lseek` system call propagates down through four kernel data structures (FD Table → Open File Table → Inode → Buffer Cache) before touching the disk. The **buffer cache** is the performance-critical layer: a *cache hit* short-circuits the path and returns immediately, while a *cache miss* triggers a real disk I/O. --- ### 4.3 Read/Write System Call Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as USER PROCESS participant K as KERNEL participant C as BUFFER CACHE participant D as DISK U->>K: open filename O RDWR K-->>U: returns fd 3 Note over U,K: SEQUENTIAL READ LOOP loop For each request U->>K: read 3 buf 4096 K->>C: lookup block B alt CACHE HIT C-->>K: returns block else CACHE MISS K->>D: read sector from disk D-->>K: returns block K->>C: insert block B end K-->>U: bytes read 4096 end Note over U,K: RANDOM ACCESS U->>K: lseek 3 50000 SEEK SET K-->>U: new offset 50000 U->>K: read 3 buf 100 K->>C: lookup block containing byte 50000 K-->>U: bytes read 100 ``` > **Reading guide:** Notice the **conditional branch** at the buffer cache — this is the single most important performance decision the kernel makes for every I/O. Sequential loops are likely to hit the cache; random `lseek` calls frequently miss. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** *With a neat diagram, explain the kernel data structures involved in reading a file using a UNIX system call.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Three kernel data structures cooperate during file I/O: 1. **Per-Process File Descriptor Table** — an entry per FD pointing into the open file table. 2. **System-Wide Open File Table** — contains the current file offset, status flags, and a pointer to the inode. 3. **Inode Table** — stores the file's metadata (size, permissions, block pointers on disk). ``` [ Process ] [ Kernel ] +-----------+ fd=3 +-------------------+ | fd table | -----------------> | open file table | | 0 stdin | | offset = 1024 | | 1 stdout | | status = O_RDONLY | | 2 stderr | +---------+---------+ | 3 ptr --------> ptr | | +-----------+ v +---------+---------+ | inode table | | mode, size, | | block ptrs | +---------+---------+ | v +---------+---------+ | disk data blocks | +-------------------+ ``` > **Valuation tip:** A labelled diagram alone earns **2 marks**; adding the role of the buffer cache earns the **3rd mark**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Exam Pattern]** *Differentiate between sequential file access and random file access. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Basis | Sequential Access | Random Access | |-------|-------------------|---------------| | Pointer movement | Auto-advances by $n$ bytes after each I/O | Repositioned explicitly using `lseek()` | | I/O pattern | Linear, ordered | Arbitrary, non-linear | | Performance on HDD | High (amortizes seek/rotation) | Low (full seek per access) | | Example | `cat file.txt`, video streaming, log file append | `dbms` B-tree lookup, compiler symbol table, editor file save | > **Valuation tip:** Stating **two** differences correctly earns **2 marks**; giving **one valid example for each** earns the **3rd mark**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Never** confuse the file descriptor (per-process) with the open file table entry (system-wide). Two FDs in the same process or different processes can share **one** open file table entry. > - **Never** assume `lseek()` reads the disk — it only updates the in-memory offset. The actual disk I/O happens on the next `read`/`write`. > - In numericals, **always** convert RPM to rotations/second *before* computing $T_{rot}$. > - For random access time, **do not** add read-ahead benefits. Each block is treated as an independent I/O. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern] **(a)** Describe the UNIX file I/O system calls `open`, `read`, `write`, `lseek`, and `close` with their syntax and purpose. How does `lseek()` enable random access? *(7 marks)* **(b)** A disk drive has the following specifications: average seek time = **9 ms**, rotational speed = **5400 RPM**, transfer rate = **50 MB/s**, block size = **4 KB**. Compute the time to read **800 blocks** (a) sequentially and (b) randomly. Also compute the speedup factor. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution (a):** | Call | Syntax | Purpose | |------|--------|---------| | `open` | `fd = open(path, flags, mode)` | Returns a file descriptor; brings file into open file table. | | `read` | `n = read(fd, buf, count)` | Reads `count` bytes from current offset into `buf`; returns bytes read; advances offset. | | `write` | `n = write(fd, buf, count)` | Writes `count` bytes from `buf` at current offset; returns bytes written; advances offset. | | `lseek` | `pos = lseek(fd, offset, whence)` | Repositions the file pointer to a new offset; does NOT touch disk. | | `close` | `r = close(fd)` | Removes FD table entry; releases kernel resources. | **How `lseek` enables random access:** - It modifies the **file offset** stored in the open file table entry to **any** byte position within $[0, \text{filesize}]$. - The next `read()`/`write()` then operates on that arbitrary position, effectively allowing **non-linear** access. - Without `lseek`, only forward sequential access (or slow `close`-`reopen` tricks) would be possible. **Distinction:** - `lseek` is an **in-memory pointer update** — it costs only a few CPU cycles and **does not** involve the disk. - The actual block containing the new offset is fetched on the **subsequent** `read()` or `write()`. --- **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Compute rotational latency in ms:** $$ r = \frac{5400}{60} = 90 \text{ rotations/sec} $$ $$ T_{rot} = \frac{1}{2r} = \frac{1}{180} = 5.556 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 2 — Per-block transfer time:** $$ t_{xf} = \frac{B}{\text{rate}} = \frac{4 \times 1024}{50 \times 10^6} = 0.0819 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3 — Random access (800 independent `read` calls, no read-ahead):** $$ T_{rand} = 800 \times (T_{seek} + T_{rot} + t_{xf}) $$ $$ T_{rand} = 800 \times (9 + 5.556 + 0.0819) = 800 \times 14.6379 $$ $$ T_{rand} = 11710.32 \text{ ms} \approx 11.71 \text{ s} $$ **Step 4 — Sequential access (1 big `read` for 800 blocks):** $$ T_{seq} = (T_{seek} + T_{rot}) + 800 \times t_{xf} $$ $$ T_{seq} = (9 + 5.556) + 800 \times 0.0819 $$ $$ T_{seq} = 14.556 + 65.52 = 80.076 \text{ ms} \approx 0.080 \text{ s} $$ **Step 5 — Speedup factor:** $$ \text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{rand}}{T_{seq}} = \frac{11710.32}{80.076} \approx 146.2 $$ **Final answer:** Sequential ≈ **80 ms**, Random ≈ **11.71 s**, Speedup ≈ **146×**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern] **(a)** Explain the **buffer cache** mechanism in operating systems. How does it improve the effective access time? Derive the formula for effective access time with hit ratio $h$. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A system has a buffer cache with access time $1\,\mu s$ and main disk with access time $8\,ms$. The cache hit ratio is **95%**. Compute the effective access time. What hit ratio is required to bring the effective access time below **0.5 ms**? *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution (a):** The **buffer cache** is a region of kernel main memory that stores recently read disk blocks. It sits logically between the application and the disk. **Lookup logic:** 1. Process issues `read()` for a block. 2. Kernel hashes the block number and searches the cache. 3. If **hit** — block is returned from RAM (≈ 1 μs). 4. If **miss** — block is read from disk (≈ 8 ms), then inserted into the cache (possibly evicting an LRU block). **Advantages:** - Reduces average I/O time by orders of magnitude. - Enables **read-ahead** for sequential access. - Allows **delayed write** (write-back caching) to coalesce multiple small writes. - Permits shared access — multiple processes reading the same block hit the **same** cached copy. **Derivation of effective access time:** Let $h$ be the probability of a cache hit. Then $(1 - h)$ is the probability of a miss. The expected (effective) access time is the weighted average: $$ T_{eff} = h \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{disk} $$ Substituting standard values ($T_{cache} = 1\,\mu s = 10^{-3}\,ms$, $T_{disk} = 10\,ms$): $$ T_{eff} = h \cdot 10^{-3} + (1 - h) \cdot 10 $$ $$ T_{eff} = 10 - 9.999 \cdot h \quad \text{(in ms)} $$ This shows that $T_{eff}$ is **linear in $h$** and decreases as the hit ratio increases. --- **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Effective access time at $h = 0.95$:** $$ T_{eff} = 0.95 \cdot (0.001) + 0.05 \cdot (8) $$ $$ T_{eff} = 0.00095 + 0.40 = 0.40095 \text{ ms} $$ $$ T_{eff} \approx 0.401 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 2 — Required hit ratio for $T_{eff} < 0.5\,ms$:** Set up the inequality: $$ h \cdot 0.001 + (1 - h) \cdot 8 < 0.5 $$ Expand: $$ 0.001h + 8 - 8h < 0.5 $$ Collect $h$ terms: $$ -7.999h < 0.5 - 8 $$ $$ -7.999h < -7.5 $$ Divide (flip inequality): $$ h > \frac{7.5}{7.999} \approx 0.9376 $$ **Final answers:** - At $h = 0.95$: $T_{eff} \approx 0.401\,ms$ ✓ (already below 0.5 ms). - To guarantee $T_{eff} < 0.5\,ms$, the hit ratio must exceed **≈ 93.76%**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize for KTU ESE):** - [ ] **File Descriptor (FD)** is a per-process index; two FDs (in same or different processes) can share one **Open File Table** entry after `dup()` or `fork()`. - [ ] **Three kernel tables** for I/O: **FD table → Open File Table → Inode Table → Buffer Cache → Disk**. - [ ] **Sequential access** = auto-advancing pointer, ideal for streams (read-ahead friendly). - [ ] **Random access** = `lseek()` to reposition pointer; ideal for databases, editors, B-trees. - [ ] **`lseek()` does NOT touch the disk** — it only updates the in-memory file offset. - [ ] `SEEK_SET` (from beginning), `SEEK_CUR` (from current), `SEEK_END` (from end) are the three `whence` modes. - [ ] **Buffer cache** is the single biggest performance factor — a 90% hit ratio can give 10× speedup. - [ ] **Effective access time:** $T_{eff} = h \cdot T_{cache} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{disk}$ - [ ] **Rotational latency:** $T_{rot} = \dfrac{60}{2 \times RPM} \times 1000$ ms (always convert RPM → rps first). - [ ] **Total disk access time:** $T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$. - [ ] **Sequential vs Random** for HDD: sequential can be **100×–300× faster** because seek/rotation is paid **once** vs **per block**. - [ ] **SSDs eliminate seek/rotation**, so sequential vs random difference is much smaller (still exists due to internal parallelism and write amplification). - [ ] **Read-ahead** (prefetching) is the OS's automatic optimization for **sequential** access patterns. - [ ] **Write-behind / delayed write** lets the kernel coalesce many small writes into one large disk transfer. > **Final exam mantra:** *Master the three tables, the four system calls, and the two access-time formulas — these alone account for ≈ 80% of the marks on this topic.*

topics.title: Removing Files - Hard Links and Symbolic Links
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Removing Files - Hard Links and Symbolic Links

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the UNIX/Linux file system architecture, a **file** is not a single entity but a layered construct consisting of three logical components:

1. **Data Blocks** — the actual user content stored on disk.
2. **Inode (Index Node)** — the metadata structure holding file attributes (size, permissions, timestamps, pointers to data blocks) and a **link count** field.
3. **Directory Entry (dirent)** — the human-readable name that maps a filename to an inode number.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Link**: A *Hard Link* is a **second (or Nth) directory entry** that directly points to the **same inode** as the original file. Both filenames are equally valid names for the **exact same underlying file object**. There is no "original" or "copy" — all hard links are first-class citizens.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbolic Link (Soft Link)**: A *Symbolic Link* (or *symlink*) is a **special file of its own** that contains a **pathname string** referring to another file or directory. The symlink is a *pointer by name*, not by inode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Removing a File**: In UNIX, the operation we colloquially call "deleting a file" is technically named **`unlink(2)`** — because we are unlinking a *name* from the file, not necessarily destroying the file. The actual data is freed only when the inode's **link count drops to zero**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Hard Link Analogy — The Twin Passports**

Imagine a person named **Ravi** has **two official passport books** issued by the government, both stamped with the **same unique passport number (Inode Number)**. Each passport (Directory Entry) lists the same person, has the same photograph, the same visa history. If the government revokes one passport book, the person is still **fully identified and existing** via the other. Only when **all passport books are revoked** does the person cease to exist in the system.

- **Passport Number = Inode Number**
- **Passport Book = Directory Entry**
- **Person = The actual file (data + metadata)**
- **Revoking a passport = `unlink()` system call**

**Symbolic Link Analogy — The Sticky Note**

Now imagine instead that on Ravi's desk there is a **sticky note** that simply reads *"See Passport Book #A1234 in the drawer"*. The sticky note is a **separate object** (its own inode). It does not contain Ravi's data — it just contains a *path string*. If Passport Book #A1234 is destroyed, the sticky note still exists, but pointing to **nothing valid** — this is called a **dangling symlink**.

---

### 1.3 Key Boundary Metrics & Standard Parameters

| Parameter | Standard Value / Note |
|---|---|
| **POSIX minimum link count for regular files** | **1** (a file must have at least one name to be reachable) |
| **Directory link count (`.` and `..` entries)** | Always **≥ 2** (self-reference + parent reference) |
| **Symlink path string limit (Linux `PATH_MAX`)** | Typically **4096 bytes** |
| **Hard link limit per inode (Linux `LINK_MAX`)** | **65,000+** depending on filesystem (ext4 default 65,000) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in KTU exams?** The examiner frequently tests the *difference* between `unlink()` and `delete` semantics. Memorize: *removing a filename ≠ removing the data*; it is the link count that governs true deletion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Inode–Dirent Model — The Operating Backbone

Every regular file in a UNIX-style file system is governed by this triad:

$$
\text{File Identity} \;\equiv\; \text{(Inode, Link Count, Data Blocks)}
$$

- The **inode** is the **unique identity** of the file.
- A **directory entry (dirent)** is a binding: $\text{name} \rightarrow \text{inode\_number}$.
- The **link count** (stored *inside* the inode) records **how many dirents** currently point to this inode.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of `unlink()`

The `unlink(2)` system call performs these exact steps in kernel space:

1. **Resolve the pathname** to a directory entry (a `name ↔ inode` mapping) via parent directory lookup.
2. **Verify permissions** — the user must have write+execute permission on the **parent directory** (not the file itself!).
3. **Decrement the inode's link count** by **1**.
4. **Remove the dirent** from the parent directory.
5. **Check the post-condition**: if `link_count == 0` AND no process has the file open, then:
   - Free all data blocks owned by the inode.
   - Mark the inode as free in the inode bitmap.
   - Release the inode slot.
6. If `link_count == 0` BUT a process still has the file open, the data persists until the last `close(2)` (this is why UNIX allows recovery of open-but-unlinked files).

### 2.3 Hard Link — Operational Rules

- A hard link is created using the system call `link(oldpath, newpath)`.
- Both filenames share **the same inode number** (verifiable via `ls -i`).
- **Cannot cross filesystem boundaries** because inode numbers are only unique *within a single filesystem*.
- Most UNIX systems **forbid hard links to directories** (except for `.` and `..`) to prevent directory cycles, which would confuse `..` resolution.
- Removing a hard link only decrements the link count — the file survives.

### 2.4 Symbolic Link — Operational Rules

- A symbolic link is created using `symlink(target, linkpath)`.
- The symlink is a **file of type `l`** (lowercase L in `ls -l`) that stores the **target pathname as a string**.
- The symlink has its **own inode** and its **own data block(s)** (storing the path string).
- **Can cross filesystem boundaries** (the target path may live anywhere).
- **Can link to directories** (this is how symlinks elegantly augment directory trees).
- If the target is removed, the symlink **still exists** but points to a non-existent path — known as a **dangling symlink**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical / Logical Expression | Boundary Condition | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| File Deletion Condition | $\text{DELETE} \iff (\text{link\_count} = 0) \land (\text{open\_count} = 0)$ | Both conditions must hold | Logical AND |
| Reference Count Update | $\text{link\_count}' = \text{link\_count} - 1$ | After each `unlink()` | Integer |
| Inode Equality (Hard Link) | $\text{ino}(\text{name}_1) = \text{ino}(\text{name}_2) = \dots$ | Within same filesystem | Integer equality |
| Symlink Resolution | $\text{readlink}(L) \rightarrow P$ | $P$ = target path string | String (bytes) |
| Dangling Symlink | $\text{stat}(L) = \text{ENOENT}$ | $L$ exists, target does not | Error code |
| Total Data Block Count (ext4) | $B_{total} = 12 + (B_{s}/4) + (B_{d}/4)^2 + (B_{t}/4)^3$ | $B_s$ = singly-indirect, etc. | Block numbers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility**: This model underpins **containerization** (Docker layer sharing uses hard-link copy-on-write), **Git internals** (every commit is a tree of hard-linked objects), and **OS forensics** (recovering deleted-but-open files via `/proc/<pid>/fd`).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Link Count Evolution During a File Lifecycle

Let $L_t$ denote the link count of an inode at time $t$. Let $O_t$ denote the number of open file descriptors (across all processes) holding this inode. Let $B_t$ denote the number of data blocks allocated.

**Initial State — File Creation** (`creat()` or `open(O_CREAT)`):

$$
L_0 = 1, \quad O_0 = 0, \quad B_0 = \text{blocks required for content}
$$

**After One Hard Link Created** (`link("a.txt", "b.txt")`):

$$
L_1 = L_0 + 1 = 2
$$

**After Second Hard Link Created** (`link("a.txt", "c.txt")`):

$$
L_2 = L_1 + 1 = 3
$$

**After First `unlink("a.txt")`**:

$$
L_3 = L_2 - 1 = 2
$$

The data blocks $B$ are **unchanged** because $L_3 > 0$. The file is fully accessible via `b.txt` and `c.txt`.

**After Second `unlink("b.txt")`**:

$$
L_4 = L_3 - 1 = 1
$$

**After Third `unlink("c.txt")` AND no open handles**:

$$
L_5 = 0, \quad O_5 = 0
$$

At this moment, the deletion condition $\text{link\_count} = 0 \land \text{open\_count} = 0$ is satisfied. The kernel:

$$
B_{\text{freed}} = B_5, \quad \text{Inode} \rightarrow \text{free bitmap}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Side-by-Side Comparison

| Operation / Property | Hard Link | Symbolic Link |
|---|---|---|
| `stat` reports same inode? | **Yes** ($\text{ino}_A = \text{ino}_B$) | **No** (own inode) |
| `ls -l` link count field | $\geq 2$ if linked | Always **1** (for the symlink itself) |
| File type character | `-` (regular) | `l` (link) |
| Cross-filesystem allowed? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Can link directories? | **No** (POSIX restriction) | **Yes** |
| Survives target deletion? | **Yes** (still valid) | **No** (becomes dangling) |
| Size in `ls -l` | Size of actual content | Size of target path string |
| Created by | `link()` | `symlink()` |
| Removed by | `unlink()` | `unlink()` |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Deletion Threshold

**Problem:** A file has **link count = 3** and is open by **2 processes**. If we execute `unlink()` **once**, what is the state of the file?

**Step 1 — Apply the link decrement rule:**

$$
L_{\text{new}} = L_{\text{old}} - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Check the deletion predicate:**

$$
\text{DELETE} \iff (L_{\text{new}} = 0) \land (O = 0)
$$

Substitute:

$$
\text{DELETE} \iff (2 = 0) \land (2 = 0) \iff \text{FALSE} \land \text{FALSE} \iff \text{FALSE}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

The file **is not deleted**. It remains accessible via the remaining **2 directory entries** and the **2 open file descriptors**. Data blocks are not freed.

**Step 4 — If we now call `unlink()` twice more (and processes still hold the file open):**

$$
L = 2 - 1 - 1 = 0, \quad O = 2
$$

$$
\text{DELETE} \iff (0 = 0) \land (2 = 0) \iff \text{TRUE} \land \text{FALSE} \iff \text{FALSE}
$$

The file becomes a **"zombie"** — unlinked from the directory tree but data intact in memory and on disk until the last `close(2)` triggers true reclamation. This is the famous UNIX **"unlinked but open"** state.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hard Link Architecture — Multiple Names, One Inode

```mermaid
graph LR
    dirA["Directory home"] --> entryA1["dirent: report.txt"]
    dirA --> entryA2["dirent: backup.txt"]
    dirA --> entryA3["dirent: copy.txt"]
    entryA1 --> inodeX["Inode 1024"]
    entryA2 --> inodeX
    entryA3 --> inodeX
    inodeX --> meta["Metadata size perms mtime"]
    inodeX --> lc["link_count = 3"]
    inodeX --> ptrs["Data Block Pointers"]
    ptrs --> b1["Block 700"]
    ptrs --> b2["Block 701"]
    ptrs --> b3["Block 702"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: All three filenames point to the *same* inode 1024. The `link_count` field inside the inode tracks the number of valid names. Unlinking any single name merely decrements the count; the data persists as long as `link_count > 0` OR some process has it open.

### 4.2 Symbolic Link Architecture — Pointer by Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    dirB["Directory home"] --> entryB1["dirent: shortcut.txt"]
    entryB1 --> inodeL["Inode 2048 SYMLINK"]
    inodeL --> metaL["Metadata type l size 16"]
    inodeL --> dataL["Data Block 850 stores path string"]
    dataL --> pathText["/usr/local/bin/reports/report.txt"]
    pathText --> targetDir["Directory usr local bin reports"]
    targetDir --> entryT["dirent: report.txt"]
    entryT --> inodeT["Inode 1024"]
    inodeT --> targetBlocks["Data Blocks 700 701 702"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: `shortcut.txt` is a *separate file* with its own inode (2048) whose data block contains the *string* `/usr/local/bin/reports/report.txt`. When the OS opens `shortcut.txt`, it performs *path resolution* — reading the stored string and traversing the directory tree again to find the real target. If `report.txt` is deleted, `shortcut.txt` becomes a *dangling symlink*.

### 4.3 Deletion State Machine — The Link Count Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created : creat or open O_CREAT
    Created --> Linked : link syscall
    Linked --> Linked : additional link syscalls
    Linked --> Unlinked : unlink link_count drops to 0
    Linked --> OpenZombie : unlink but processes still open
    OpenZombie --> Deleted : last close fires
    Unlinked --> Deleted : no open handles
    Deleted --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Functional Flow — Kernel Handling of `unlink(pathname)`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User calls unlink path"] --> B["VFS resolves pathname"]
    B --> C{"Lookup parent dir"}
    C --> D["Find dirent in parent"]
    D --> E["Check write perm on parent"]
    E --> F["Decrement inode link_count"]
    F --> G["Remove dirent from parent"]
    G --> H{"link_count == 0"}
    H -- No --> I["File still reachable via other names"]
    H -- Yes --> J{"Any process has it open"}
    J -- No --> K["Free data blocks and inode"]
    J -- Yes --> L["Mark for delayed deletion"]
    L --> M["Defer until last close"]
    K --> N["Return success to user"]
    I --> N
    M --> N
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is a hard link in a UNIX file system? Why can hard links not span across filesystems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
