A hard link is a second (or additional) directory entry that points to the **same inode** as the original file, making both filenames equally valid references to the same underlying data. Hard links cannot span filesystems because **inode numbers are only unique within a single filesystem** — if a hard link crossed filesystem boundaries, the kernel would be unable to unambiguously resolve the target inode number, and the link count mechanism would become inconsistent across separate storage volumes. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a symbolic link and a hard link. Can a symbolic link be created across filesystems? Justify.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **symbolic link** is a special file that stores a *pathname string* to another file and has its own inode, whereas a **hard link** is a directory entry that points to the *same inode* as the original file. **Yes**, symbolic links can span across filesystems because the symlink only stores a text path (e.g., `/mnt/usb/data.txt`), and the kernel resolves this path via the VFS layer transparently — no inode-number sharing is required. Hard links, in contrast, require inode number equality which is filesystem-local. (3 marks)

---

### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the UNIX file deletion mechanism in detail, with reference to inode link counts and the `unlink()` system call. How does the "unlinked but open" scenario work, and why is it significant? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The UNIX file system models a file as a triad: **data blocks (content)**, **inode (metadata)**, and **directory entries (names)**. The `unlink(pathname)` system call does not "delete a file" in the naive sense — it removes a *name* (a dirent) from its parent directory and decrements the inode's link count by 1.

The kernel follows this sequence:

1. Resolves the pathname to obtain the target dirent and its parent.
2. Verifies write+execute permission on the **parent directory**.
3. Decrements `inode.i_links_count` by 1.
4. Removes the dirent from the parent's data structure.
5. Checks the deletion predicate: $\text{DELETE} \iff (\text{link\_count} = 0) \land (\text{open\_count} = 0)$.

If the link count reaches zero **and** no process has the file open, the kernel frees the data blocks and releases the inode to the free pool.

**The "unlinked but open" scenario** occurs when `link_count` falls to zero but processes still hold the file open. In this case, the file is detached from the directory tree — no `ls` or `find` can locate it — yet the data persists in memory and on disk. The file becomes a "zombie" until the **last `close(2)`** fires, at which point the kernel performs the deferred deletion.

**Significance** (3 sub-points worth 1 mark each):

- Enables **atomic file replacement** patterns (write to temp file, `rename` over original — never leaves a half-written file visible).
- Allows processes to safely read/write files even after another process unlinks them (e.g., log rotation in UNIX: open log → unlink old name → keep writing to the inode).
- Useful in **forensics and recovery**: `/proc/<pid>/fd/<n>` exposes the file descriptor, allowing tools to recover "deleted" files that are still open.

---

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the structural difference between a hard link and a symbolic link. What happens to the file when (i) the last hard link is removed and (ii) the target of a symbolic link is deleted? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Structural Difference:**

A **hard link** is a directory entry that points *directly* to the same inode as the original file. There is only **one inode** for all hard links, and the inode's `link_count` field is incremented for each additional name. Removal of one hard link merely decrements the count; the file data and inode persist.

A **symbolic link** is an independent file (its own inode) whose data block contains a *textual path* to the target file. Removing the symlink with `unlink` deletes the symlink itself but leaves the target untouched. The kernel performs **path resolution** at every access.

(Diagrams: see Section 4.1 and 4.2 above — 2 marks for correctness of diagrams)

**(i) When the last hard link is removed:**

The inode's `link_count` becomes 0. If no process has the file open, the data blocks are returned to the filesystem's free block pool, and the inode is marked free. **The file ceases to exist.** If processes still hold it open, the file enters the "unlinked-but-open" state and persists until the last close.

**(ii) When the target of a symbolic link is deleted:**

The symbolic link **still exists** as a file (its inode and data block are intact), but its stored path now resolves to a non-existent file. This is called a **dangling symlink**. Operations like `cat mylink` will fail with `ENOENT` (No such file or directory), but `ls -l mylink` will still display the symlink with the arrow `->` pointing to the (now missing) target.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students frequently **confuse `unlink` with file destruction**. Always state the link-count predicate explicitly.
> - Do **not** claim hard links can be created on directories in UNIX/Linux — this is a **POSIX violation** and costs a mark.
> - When explaining dangling symlinks, **do not say the symlink is "broken"** — the symlink file itself is *valid*; only its *target* is missing.
> - Diagrams must show the **inode sharing** for hard links and **path string storage** for symlinks. Vague boxes labeled "file" lose marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of link count in UNIX inodes. How does the file system determine when a file's data blocks can be safely reclaimed? Derive the deletion predicate formally. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **link count** is an integer field stored *inside* the inode (in V6 UNIX, it was 16-bit; in modern systems, it is typically a 32-bit or 64-bit counter). It records the **number of directory entries (hard links)** that currently point to this inode. Every `link()` system call increments it; every `unlink()` decrements it.

The file system reclaims a file's data blocks **only when two independent conditions are simultaneously satisfied**:

$$
\text{DELETE} \iff (\text{link\_count} = 0) \land (\text{open\_count} = 0)
$$

**Derivation:**

Let $L_t$ = link count at time $t$, $O_t$ = number of open file descriptors pointing to the inode at time $t$, $B_t$ = data blocks owned at time $t$.

When `unlink()` is invoked:

$$
L_{t+1} = L_t - 1
$$

When `open()` succeeds:

$$
O_{t+1} = O_t + 1
$$

When `close()` succeeds:

$$
O_{t+1} = O_t - 1
$$

The kernel performs the check after each decrement:

$$
\text{If } L_{t+1} = 0 \text{ AND } O_{t+1} = 0, \text{ then FREE}(B_{t+1}) \text{ and release inode}
$$

**Why both conditions?** The link count guards against premature deletion while other names still reference the file. The open count guards against deleting a file that a running process is actively using (which would cause I/O to read freed memory or zeroed blocks). Together, they guarantee that **no possible path of access can ever reach freed storage** — a cornerstone of UNIX safety.

---

**(b)** Compare hard links and symbolic links across at least six parameters. Explain with an example why removing a hard link does not destroy the file, but removing the only path stored in a symbolic link renders the symlink "dangling". **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (6 parameters — 3 marks):**

| Parameter | Hard Link | Symbolic Link |
|---|---|---|
| Inode | Shared with original | Independent |
| Cross-filesystem | Not allowed | Allowed |
| Link to directories | Not allowed | Allowed |
| Survives target deletion | Yes (is the target) | No (becomes dangling) |
| Size in `ls -l` | Size of actual content | Size of path string |
| Command to create | `ln source link` | `ln -s source link` |

**Example demonstrating link removal behavior:**

Consider: `echo "Hello" > original.txt`

This creates a file with inode, say, **512**, and `link_count = 1`.

**Step 1 — Create hard link:**
`ln original.txt hardlink.txt`

Now both names point to inode 512. `ls -li` shows: `512 -rw-r--r-- 2 user user 6 ... original.txt` and `512 -rw-r--r-- 2 user user 6 ... hardlink.txt`. The number **2** in the third column is the link count.

**Step 2 — Remove the original:**
`rm original.txt`

Result: `link_count = 1`. The file is **still fully accessible** via `hardlink.txt`. The data is **not destroyed** because the inode still has a valid directory entry referencing it.

**Step 3 — Symbolic link creation:**
`ln -s original.txt softlink.txt`

This creates a *new* inode (say, 900) whose data block contains the string `original.txt`. Note that `original.txt` was already deleted in Step 2, so `softlink.txt` is **born as a dangling symlink**.

**Step 4 — Demonstration of dangling state:**
`cat softlink.txt` → `cat: softlink.txt: No such file or directory` (the *target* doesn't exist)
`ls -l softlink.txt` → `lrwxrwxrwx 1 user user 12 ... softlink.txt -> original.txt` (the symlink *itself* exists)

**Why hard link removal doesn't destroy the file:** Because hard links *are* the file's names — removing one name only decrements the count. The file persists as long as any name remains.

**Why symlink target deletion creates a dangling symlink:** Because the symlink is a *separate object* holding only a *path string*. When the target is deleted, the path becomes invalid, but the symlink file is not informed — it continues to exist, pointing to a non-existent location.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A file in UNIX = **data blocks + inode + dirent(s)** — three distinct concepts.
- ✅ `unlink()` removes a **name**, not a file. The file dies only when `link_count = 0` AND `open_count = 0`.
- ✅ **Hard link** = same inode, cannot cross filesystems, cannot link to directories (POSIX).
- ✅ **Symbolic link** = own inode storing a path string, can cross filesystems, can link to directories, becomes **dangling** if target is deleted.
- ✅ The deletion predicate is $\text{DELETE} \iff (L = 0) \land (O = 0)$ — **state this in every KTU answer**.
- ✅ "Unlinked but open" files are a real UNIX phenomenon used for **atomic file updates** and **log rotation**.
- ✅ `ls -li` shows inode numbers; identical inodes confirm a hard link.
- ✅ The `link_count` field in the inode is the **authoritative counter** for file persistence.
- ✅ Dangling symlinks are *not* errors in the symlink itself — they are **valid symlink files** pointing to non-existent targets.
- ✅ Real-world uses: Docker layer caching (hard links), `/usr/bin` shadowing via symlinks, Git object storage (content-addressed, hard-linked).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Creating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Creating" in Operating Systems?

In the context of **PCCST403 – Operating Systems (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the topic **"Creating"** refers to the mechanism by which an **Operating System (OS)** brings a new **Process** into existence. This is formally known as **Process Creation** — one of the most fundamental and critical responsibilities of the OS Process Manager.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
> **Process Creation** is the act by which the operating system (via system calls such as `fork()`, `vfork()`, `CreateProcess()`, or `clone()`) generates a new process — known as the **Child Process** — from an existing **Parent Process**. The new child inherits (or shares) certain attributes of the parent such as the program context, open file descriptors, environment variables, and resource limits.

Every program you run, every application you open, and every command you type in a shell ultimately triggers process creation. Without process creation, the system can run **only one program at a time** — making modern multitasking impossible.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a **restaurant kitchen** as your computer:

- The **Operating System** is the **Head Chef** (the manager).
- A **Process** is a **recipe being cooked** at a workstation.
- **Creating a new process** is like the Head Chef saying to a junior chef: *"Here, take this half-prepared recipe (a copy of the parent recipe), follow your own instructions for the remaining steps, and report back when done."*

In Linux/Unix, this is exactly what the `fork()` system call does — it **duplicates the current recipe** (process), and the new copy (child) can either **continue cooking the same dish** (parent's code) or **start cooking a completely new dish** by using `exec()`.

---

## 1.3 Why is Process Creation Important?

Process creation is the **foundation of multitasking, multiprocessing, and modular system design**. The reasons it is essential:

1. **Multi-tasking** — Multiple applications (browser, music, editor) must run simultaneously.
2. **Resource Utilization** — Better CPU utilization by switching between processes.
3. **Process Hierarchy** — Enables parent-child relationships (e.g., shell spawning commands).
4. **Modularity & Isolation** — Each program runs in its own protected memory space.
5. **Parallelism** — Enables modern multi-core processors to execute in true parallel.

---

## 1.4 Key Events that Trigger Process Creation

| # | Trigger Event | Real-world Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **System Initialization** | Boot-time processes like `init`, `systemd` |
| 2 | **User Request** | Double-clicking an application icon |
| 3 | **System Call** | A program calling `fork()` / `CreateProcess()` |
| 4 | **Batch Job Submission** | A cron-scheduled script firing |
| 5 | **Service Request** | A client request to a server (e.g., Apache fork) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Under the **2024 Scheme**, this topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Illustrate the concepts of processes, threads, and process scheduling."* Students must master both **Unix-style fork/exec** and **Windows-style CreateProcess** paradigms, as questions from either are routinely asked in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

## 1.5 Types of Process Creation

Process creation is broadly classified into two architectural models:

1. **Unix/Linux Model** — Uses **`fork()` + `exec()`** two-step approach.
2. **Windows Model** — Uses a single unified **`CreateProcess()`** system call.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Tip for Exams:**
> - **Unix** = **Two steps** → `fork()` (clone), then `exec()` (replace).
> - **Windows** = **One step** → `CreateProcess()` (clone + replace together).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Step-by-Step Logic of Process Creation

When the OS creates a new process, a strict sequence of operations is performed. Understanding **why** each step happens is critical for KTU board examinations.

### 🔹 Step 1: Assigning a Unique Process Identifier (PID)
- Every process must have a unique integer identifier called the **PID (Process ID)**.
- In Linux, PIDs are typically limited to **32768** (default) or **4194304** (`/proc/sys/kernel/pid_max`).
- The OS maintains a **PID table** in kernel memory to map PID → Process Control Block (PCB).

> **Why?** Without a unique ID, the OS cannot distinguish between processes for scheduling, signaling, or termination.

### 🔹 Step 2: Allocating the Process Control Block (PCB)
The **PCB** is a kernel data structure containing:
- **Process State** (New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated)
- **Program Counter (PC)**
- **CPU Registers**
- **Memory Management Info** (Page tables, base/limit registers)
- **Scheduling Information** (Priority, pointers to scheduling queues)
- **List of Open Files**
- **Parent PID, Child PIDs**
- **Resource Utilization** (CPU time, memory limits)

### 🔹 Step 3: Allocating Memory Space
The OS allocates a dedicated **virtual address space** for the new process, which is divided into:

| Memory Segment | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Text Segment** | Executable code (read-only) |
| **Data Segment** | Initialized global/static variables |
| **BSS Segment** | Uninitialized global variables |
| **Heap** | Dynamic memory (`malloc`, `new`) — grows upward |
| **Stack** | Function calls, local variables — grows downward |

### 🔹 Step 4: Initializing the PCB
The OS initializes all fields of the PCB:
- State → set to **NEW** or **READY**.
- Program Counter → set to the entry point of the program.
- Registers → cleared to zero.
- Priority → inherited from parent or set by policy.

### 🔹 Step 5: Linking the Process to Scheduling Queues
The newly created PCB is inserted into the **Ready Queue** (e.g., a run-queue implemented as a linked list or red-black tree in Linux's CFS scheduler).

### 🔹 Step 6: Other Initialization
- Set up signal handlers (inherited or default).
- Initialize I/O descriptors (stdin, stdout, stderr).
- Set up accounting fields (start time, CPU usage).
- Inherit or set environment variables.

---

## 2.2 Unix/Linux Process Creation: `fork()` + `exec()`

The **Unix philosophy** splits process creation into **two distinct system calls**:

### 🔸 `fork()` — Duplication Phase
```c
#include <unistd.h>
pid_t fork(void);
```
- Creates an **exact duplicate** of the calling process.
- Returns:
  - **To the parent**: PID of the newly created child (a positive integer).
  - **To the child**: `0`.
  - **On failure**: `-1`.
- The child gets a **copy** of the parent's memory (using **Copy-on-Write / COW** optimization).
- Both parent and child resume execution from the **line after** the `fork()` call.

> [!NOTE]
> **Copy-on-Write (COW) Optimization:**
> Instead of physically duplicating memory (expensive), `fork()` marks all pages as read-only in both parent and child. A **page fault** triggers a real copy only when one of them tries to write to a page. This makes `fork()` extremely fast.

### 🔸 `exec()` — Replacement Phase
```c
#include <unistd.h>
int execvp(const char *file, char *const argv[]);
```
- **Replaces** the current process image with a **new program**.
- The PID remains the same; only the memory image changes.
- Variants: `execl()`, `execv()`, `execle()`, `execve()`, `execlp()`, `execvp()`.

### 🔸 `wait()` / `waitpid()` — Synchronization Phase
```c
#include <sys/wait.h>
pid_t wait(int *status);
pid_t waitpid(pid_t pid, int *status, int options);
```
- Parent **blocks** until a child terminates.
- Collects the **exit status** of the child (preventing zombies).

### 🔸 Complete Fork-Exec Pattern
```c
pid_t pid = fork();

if (pid < 0) {
    // Fork failed
    perror("fork failed");
    exit(1);
}
else if (pid == 0) {
    // CHILD process
    printf("I am the child, PID = %d\n", getpid());
    execlp("/bin/ls", "ls", "-l", NULL);
    // If exec returns, it failed
    perror("exec failed");
    exit(1);
}
else {
    // PARENT process
    printf("I am the parent, child PID = %d\n", pid);
    wait(NULL);  // Wait for child to complete
    printf("Child finished.\n");
}
```

---

## 2.3 Windows Process Creation: `CreateProcess()`

Windows uses a **single, unified system call**:

```c
#include <windows.h>
BOOL CreateProcess(
    LPCSTR lpApplicationName,        // Name of executable
    LPSTR  lpCommandLine,            // Command line
    LPSECURITY_ATTRIBUTES lpProcessAttributes,
    LPSECURITY_ATTRIBUTES lpThreadAttributes,
    BOOL bInheritHandles,
    DWORD dwCreationFlags,
    LPVOID lpEnvironment,
    LPCSTR lpCurrentDirectory,
    LPSTARTUPINFO lpStartupInfo,
    LPPROCESS_INFORMATION lpProcessInformation
);
```

| Feature | Unix `fork()`+`exec()` | Windows `CreateProcess()` |
|---|---|---|
| Number of syscalls | 2 (`fork` then `exec`) | 1 (`CreateProcess`) |
| Program loading | Separate `exec()` step | Built into the call |
| Address space | Initially identical, replaced on exec | Created fresh in the call |
| Handles | Inherited file descriptors | Explicit handle inheritance |
| Return value | Child PID, 0, or -1 | `BOOL` success/fail + `PROCESS_INFORMATION` |

---

## 2.4 Process States & Transitions (Crucial for KTU)

After creation, a process moves through several **states**:

```
        ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │                                                  │
        ▼                                                  │
   ┌──────┐   Admitted   ┌───────┐  Scheduler dispatch  ┌────────┐
   │ NEW  │ ───────────► │ READY │ ───────────────────► │ RUNNING│
   └──────┘              └───────┘                       └────────┘
                            ▲     I/O or event wait          │  │
                            │                                │  │
                            │      I/O or event completion   │  │
                            └────────── Waiting ◄────────────┘  │
                                       (Blocked)               │
                                                                  │
                                       Exit / terminated         │
                                              ▼                   ▼
                                        ┌────────────┐      ┌──────────┐
                                        │ TERMINATED │      │  EXIT    │
                                        └────────────┘      └──────────┘
```

### State Definitions

| State | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **NEW** | Process is being created |
| **READY** | Waiting to be assigned to the CPU |
| **RUNNING** | Instructions are being executed |
| **WAITING / BLOCKED** | Waiting for some event (I/O completion, signal) |
| **TERMINATED** | Process has finished execution |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although OS is conceptual, certain **performance metrics** are tested numerically:

| Metric | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $T_{AT} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$ | Seconds / ms |
| **Waiting Time (WT)** | $T_{WT} = T_{AT} - T_{burst}$ | Seconds / ms |
| **Response Time (RT)** | $T_{RT} = T_{first\_CPU} - T_{arrival}$ | Seconds / ms |
| **CPU Utilization** | $U_{CPU} = \dfrac{T_{busy}}{T_{busy} + T_{idle}}$ | Percentage |
| **Throughput** | $X = \dfrac{N_{processes}}{T_{total}}$ | Processes/sec |
| **Context Switch Overhead** | $T_{CS\_total} = N_{switches} \times T_{avg\_per\_switch}$ | Seconds |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Rule:** In KTU answer sheets, when writing modulus or set notation inline, prefer `\vert` or `mid` instead of a vertical bar `\|` to avoid markdown rendering issues in digital answer copies.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Process Creation

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Web Servers** | Apache/Nginx use `fork()` or `clone()` to handle each client connection in a separate child process |
| **Compilers** | `gcc` invokes `cc1` as a child process for compilation |
| **Shells** | Bash forks a child for every command, then execs the binary |
| **Containers** | Docker uses Linux namespaces + cgroups (clone variants) to create isolated "processes" |
| **Operating System Boot** | `init`/`systemd` (PID 1) is the ancestor of all user processes |

---

## 2.7 Theoretical Foundations — Why Fork Works

The fork system call is grounded in the **Process Control Block (PCB) duplication theory**:

> A process is fundamentally defined by its execution context (registers, memory image, OS resources). To create a new process, the OS creates a **new PCB** and either:
> 1. **Duplicates the parent's context** (Unix fork), or
> 2. **Constructs a fresh context** for a specified executable (Windows CreateProcess).

The **copy-on-write** optimization arises from the observation that in most cases, the child immediately replaces its memory image via `exec()`, making a full copy wasteful.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Turnaround, Waiting, and Burst Times

For KTU numerical problems, the following derivations are repeatedly asked.

### 🔹 Derivation 1 — Waiting Time from Turnaround Time

**Starting Principle:**
A process spends time in three categories during its lifetime:
1. **Waiting in ready queue** (waiting to be scheduled).
2. **Executing on CPU** (burst time).
3. **Waiting in blocked queue** (waiting for I/O).

The **Turnaround Time** is the **total time from arrival to completion**, which includes all three:

$$T_{AT} = T_{waiting\_ready} + T_{burst} + T_{waiting\_I/O}$$

The **Waiting Time** is the **sum of time spent waiting only in the ready queue** (I/O wait is excluded by convention):

$$T_{WT} = T_{waiting\_ready}$$

Subtracting the second equation from the first (assuming negligible I/O wait or by KTU convention):

$$\boxed{T_{WT} = T_{AT} - T_{burst}}$$

### 🔹 Derivation 2 — Response Time from Scheduling Events

**Starting Principle:**
Response Time is measured as the duration between **arrival** and the **first time** the process gets the CPU:

$$T_{RT} = T_{first\_CPU\_allocation} - T_{arrival}$$

For **non-preemptive** scheduling:
$$T_{first\_CPU} = T_{arrival} + T_{waiting\_ready}$$
$$\therefore T_{RT} = T_{WT} \text{ (at first dispatch)}$$

For **preemptive** scheduling, the process may be interrupted, but Response Time still uses the **first** CPU allocation.

### 🔹 Derivation 3 — CPU Utilization Under Context Switching

**Given:**
- $T_{busy}$ = Total CPU-busy time
- $T_{idle}$ = Total CPU-idle time
- $N_{switches}$ = Number of context switches
- $T_{cs}$ = Average context switch time

**Real (effective) busy time:**

$$T_{busy\_effective} = T_{busy} - (N_{switches} \times T_{cs})$$

**Total elapsed time:**

$$T_{total} = T_{busy} + T_{idle}$$

**CPU Utilization:**

$$U_{CPU} = \frac{T_{busy\_effective}}{T_{total}} = \frac{T_{busy} - N_{switches} \cdot T_{cs}}{T_{busy} + T_{idle}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Every context switch incurs overhead. In KTU problems, if the OS performs **N context switches** of duration **T_cs each**, this total overhead is **subtracted** from effective CPU time.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Question)

### 📘 Problem Statement
Three processes arrive at time 0 with the following burst times under **FCFS (First-Come-First-Served)** scheduling:

| Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 ms | **8 ms** |
| P2 | 0 ms | **4 ms** |
| P3 | 0 ms | **2 ms** |

Assume each context switch takes **1 ms**. Compute average Waiting Time, Turnaround Time, and CPU Utilization.

### 🔹 Step 1: Draw the Gantt Chart

Since arrival times are equal (0), order by process name (P1 → P2 → P3):

```
| P1 (8ms) | CS | P2 (4ms) | CS | P3 (2ms) |
0          8   9        13  14         16  17
```

Total context switches between processes = **2** (between P1→P2 and P2→P3). No switch after P3 (no successor).

### 🔹 Step 2: Calculate Completion Times

- $T_{completion}(P1) = 8$ ms
- $T_{completion}(P2) = 8 + 1_{cs} + 4 = 13$ ms
- $T_{completion}(P3) = 13 + 1_{cs} + 2 = 16$ ms

### 🔹 Step 3: Calculate Turnaround Times

Using $T_{AT} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{AT}(P1) &= 8 - 0 = 8 \text{ ms} \\
T_{AT}(P2) &= 13 - 0 = 13 \text{ ms} \\
T_{AT}(P3) &= 16 - 0 = 16 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Average Turnaround Time:**

$$\overline{T_{AT}} = \frac{8 + 13 + 16}{3} = \frac{37}{3} \approx 12.33 \text{ ms}$$

### 🔹 Step 4: Calculate Waiting Times

Using $T_{WT} = T_{AT} - T_{burst}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{WT}(P1) &= 8 - 8 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
T_{WT}(P2) &= 13 - 4 = 9 \text{ ms} \\
T_{WT}(P3) &= 16 - 2 = 14 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Average Waiting Time:**

$$\overline{T_{WT}} = \frac{0 + 9 + 14}{3} = \frac{23}{3} \approx 7.67 \text{ ms}$$

### 🔹 Step 5: Calculate CPU Utilization

Total burst (busy) time = $8 + 4 + 2 = 14$ ms
Total context switch overhead = $2 \times 1 = 2$ ms
Effective busy time = $14$ ms (burst itself, not reduced — only idle is computed separately here)

$$U_{CPU} = \frac{T_{busy}}{T_{busy} + T_{idle}} = \frac{14}{16} \times 100\% = 87.5\%$$

(The 2 ms spent in context switching is overhead that *could* be counted as idle from CPU perspective; depending on interpretation, $U_{CPU} = 14 / 16 = 87.5\%$.)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Attribute | `fork()` (Unix) | `CreateProcess()` (Windows) |
|---|---|---|
| **System calls** | 2 (`fork` + `exec`) | 1 (unified) |
| **Memory at creation** | Identical copy of parent (COW) | Fresh image loaded from executable |
| **Performance** | Fast (COW) | Generally slower (loads executable) |
| **PID returned** | `>0` in parent, `0` in child | `BOOL` + `PROCESS_INFORMATION` |
| **File descriptors** | Inherited | Inherited if flag set |
| **Signal/Handle setup** | Signals inherited | Handles explicitly inheritable |
| **Used by** | Linux, macOS, BSD | Windows NT family |
| **Failure return** | `-1` | `FALSE` + `GetLastError()` |

---

## 3.4 Process Tree — Analytical Trace

For the following sequence:

```c
fork();   // Line A
fork();   // Line B
fork();   // Line C
```

Let the original process be **P0**. Number of processes created: $2^n = 2^3 = 8$.

**Trace logic:**
- After Line A: 2 processes (P0, P1)
- After Line B: Each of 2 forks once more → 4 processes
- After Line C: Each of 4 forks once more → 8 processes

Total = **8 processes**, including the original.

| Process | Line A (returned) | Line B (returned) | Line C (returned) |
|---|---|---|---|
| P0 | child PID | child PID | child PID |
| P1 | 0 | child PID | child PID |
| P2 | — | 0 | child PID |
| P3 | — | — | 0 |
| P4 | 0 | child PID | child PID |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| P7 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often answer "8 child processes." The correct answer is **8 total processes** (including the original).

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : fork / CreateProcess
    NEW --> READY : Admitted by OS
    READY --> RUNNING : Scheduler Dispatch
    RUNNING --> READY : Preempted / Time slice expired
    RUNNING --> WAITING : I/O request or event wait
    WAITING --> READY : I/O completion / event signaled
    RUNNING --> TERMINATED : exit / abort
    TERMINATED --> [*] : PCB deallocated
```

**Interpretation:**
- A process enters at **NEW** when `fork()` or `CreateProcess()` is invoked.
- After PCB setup, the OS transitions it to **READY** (admitted).
- The scheduler picks it → **RUNNING**.
- If preempted → back to **READY**.
- If it requests I/O → **WAITING**.
- On I/O completion → back to **READY**.
- On `exit()` → **TERMINATED**, and the PCB is freed.

---

## 4.2 Unix fork() Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parent Process executing] --> B[pid = fork]
    B --> C{fork successful?}
    C -- No --> D[Return -1, perror, exit]
    C -- Yes --> E{pid value check}
    E -- pid > 0 --> F[Parent Branch]
    E -- pid == 0 --> G[Child Branch]
    F --> F1[Continue parent execution]
    F1 --> F2[Optionally call wait]
    F2 --> F3[Parent continues or exits]
    G --> G1[Child executes code]
    G1 --> G2{Need new program?}
    G2 -- Yes --> H[exec family call]
    H --> H1[Memory image replaced]
    H1 --> H2[New program executes]
    G2 -- No --> G3[Continue as parent copy]
    G3 --> G4[Child calls exit]
    H2 --> H4[Child calls exit]
    F3 --> Z[Zombie until parent wait]
    G4 --> Z
    H4 --> Z
    Z --> Z2[Parent collects status, child reaped]
```

---

## 4.3 Process Memory Layout Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Low_Address["Low Memory Address"]
        TXT["TEXT Segment<br/>Executable Code (Read-Only)"]
        ROD["Read-Only Data<br/>String Literals"]
        DATA["DATA Segment<br/>Initialized Globals"]
        BSS["BSS Segment<br/>Uninitialized Globals"]
    end
    subgraph HEAP_REGION["HEAP — grows upward ↑"]
        HEAP["Dynamic Memory<br/>malloc, calloc, new"]
    end
    subgraph FREE_AREA["Free Virtual Memory"]
        FREE["(unused space)"]
    end
    subgraph STACK_REGION["STACK — grows downward ↓"]
        STACK["Stack Frames<br/>Local vars, return addresses"]
        ENV["Environment & Command-Line Args"]
    end
    High_Address["High Memory Address"] --> ENV
    STACK_REGION --> FREE_AREA
    FREE_AREA --> HEAP_REGION
    HEAP_REGION --> BSS
    BSS --> DATA
    DATA --> ROD
    ROD --> TXT
    TXT --> Low_Address
```

**Key Insight:** After `fork()`, both parent and child share the **same virtual layout** initially (thanks to Copy-on-Write). After `exec()`, the entire user-space segments are replaced with the new program's image.

---

## 4.4 Parent-Child Hierarchy (Typical Shell Behavior)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INIT["init / systemd (PID 1)"]
    SHELL["bash (PID 1000)"]
    P1["ls (PID 1001)"]
    P2["grep (PID 1002)"]
    P3["sort (PID 1003)"]

    INIT --> SHELL
    SHELL -- "ls -l | grep .c | sort" --> P1
    P1 -- "exec() pipe" --> P2
    P2 -- "exec() pipe" --> P3
```

**Interpretation:**
- `init` (PID 1) is the **ancestor of all processes**.
- The shell forks for every command in the pipeline.
- Each command performs `exec()` to replace itself with the target binary.
- Pipes connect the standard I/O between them.

---

## 4.5 fork() Tree Visualization (3 fork calls)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["P0 (original)"]
    P1["P1"]
    P2["P2"]
    P3["P3"]
    P4["P4"]
    P5["P5"]
    P6["P6"]
    P7["P7"]

    P0 -- "fork() #1" --> P1
    P0 -- "fork() #2 in P1" --> P2
    P1 -- "fork() #3 in P2" --> P3
    P0 -- "fork() #2 in P0" --> P4
    P4 -- "fork() #3 in P4" --> P5
    P1 -- "fork() #3 in P1" --> P6
    P0 -- "fork() #3 in P0" --> P7
```

> **Note:** This tree reflects the **dynamic call stack** of three sequential `fork()` calls. After 3 forks, there are exactly $2^3 = 8$ processes — P0, P1, P2, ..., P7 — all executing concurrently.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a Process Control Block (PCB)? List any four contents of a PCB.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Process Control Block (PCB)** is a kernel data structure maintained by the operating system for every active process. It serves as the **identity card and context holder** of the process — storing all information needed to manage and resume its execution.
>
> Four essential contents of a PCB are:
> 1. **Process State** — current state (New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated).
> 2. **Program Counter (PC)** — address of the next instruction to execute.
> 3. **CPU Registers** — accumulator, stack pointer, general-purpose registers.
> 4. **Memory Management Information** — base/limit registers, page tables, segment tables.
>
> *(Other valid contents: scheduling info, list of open files, parent PID, child PIDs, signal masks, accounting info.)* **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between `fork()` and `exec()` system calls in Unix.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `fork()` | `exec()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Creates a new child process as a duplicate of the parent | Replaces the current process image with a new program |
| **Process ID** | Returns child PID to parent, 0 to child | PID remains unchanged |
| **Memory** | Child gets a copy of parent's memory (COW) | Old memory image is destroyed; new program loaded |
| **Number of processes** | Increases by 1 | Number remains the same |
| **Typical usage** | To create a new process | To run a different program in that process |

> *(Any valid 3 valid differences = 3 marks; tabular form preferred.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question B (Module Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

---

#### **OPTION A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Explain the different **states of a process** with a neat state transition diagram. Mention the events causing each transition. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable C program, explain the working of the **`fork()` and `wait()`** system calls in Unix/Linux. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Option A**

#### **Part (a) — Process States (7 Marks)**

The OS tracks each process through a well-defined **lifecycle of states**:

| State | Description |
|---|---|
| **NEW** | Process is being created (PCB is being constructed) |
| **READY** | Process is waiting in the ready queue to be assigned to the CPU |
| **RUNNING** | Instructions are currently being executed on the CPU |
| **WAITING (BLOCKED)** | Process is waiting for some event (I/O completion, signal) |
| **TERMINATED** | Process has finished execution; PCB awaiting cleanup |

**State Transition Table (with triggering events):**

| # | From → To | Event / Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | NEW → READY | OS admits the process after PCB and memory allocation |
| 2 | READY → RUNNING | Scheduler dispatches the process (CPU assigned) |
| 3 | RUNNING → READY | Time quantum expires (preemptive) or process voluntarily yields |
| 4 | RUNNING → WAITING | Process requests I/O or waits on a signal/event |
| 5 | WAITING → READY | I/O completes or awaited event occurs |
| 6 | RUNNING → TERMINATED | Process calls `exit()` or is aborted by OS |

```
┌──────┐ admit ┌───────┐ dispatch ┌─────────┐
│ NEW  │──────►│ READY │─────────►│ RUNNING │
└──────┘       └───────┘◄────────└─────────┘
                  ▲  preempt │ │  exit
                  │          │ │      ▼
                  │       wait│ │  ┌──────────┐
                  │   ◄────────┘ │  │TERMINATED│
                  └──────────────┘  └──────────┘
                   I/O or event completion
```

**(7 marks breakdown:** 2 for list of states, 3 for diagram, 2 for transition events.)

---

#### **Part (b) — `fork()` and `wait()` Program (7 Marks)**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/wait.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main() {
    pid_t pid;

    pid = fork();   // Create a new process

    if (pid < 0) {
        // Error occurred
        fprintf(stderr, "Fork failed\n");
        return 1;
    }
    else if (pid == 0) {
        // CHILD process branch
        printf("Child: My PID = %d, Parent PID = %d\n",
               getpid(), getppid());
        printf("Child: Doing some work...\n");
        sleep(2);  // Simulate work
        printf("Child: Exiting now.\n");
        exit(42);  // Exit with status 42
    }
    else {
        // PARENT process branch
        printf("Parent: My PID = %d, Child PID = %d\n",
               getpid(), pid);
        printf("Parent: Waiting for child to finish...\n");

        int status;
        wait(&status);  // Block until child terminates

        if (WIFEXITED(status)) {
            printf("Parent: Child exited normally with code %d\n",
                   WEXITSTATUS(status));
        }
        printf("Parent: Continuing execution.\n");
    }

    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of working:**

1. The parent calls `fork()` to create a child.
2. `fork()` returns the child's PID in the parent, and `0` in the child.
3. Both processes execute **independently and concurrently** from the line after `fork()`.
4. The child performs its task, then calls `exit(42)`.
5. The parent calls `wait(&status)`, which **blocks** the parent until any child terminates.
6. Once the child exits, `wait()` returns, and the parent extracts the exit status using `WEXITSTATUS()`.
7. The parent then continues and exits normally.

**Output (approximate):**
```
Parent: My PID = 5000, Child PID = 5001
Parent: Waiting for child to finish...
Child: My PID = 5001, Parent PID = 5000
Child: Doing some work...
Child: Exiting now.
Parent: Child exited normally with code 42
Parent: Continuing execution.
```

**(7 marks breakdown:** 2 for code, 2 for explanation of `fork()`, 2 for explanation of `wait()`, 1 for output/result.)

---

#### **OPTION B — 14 Marks** *(Internal Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the **Unix process creation** mechanism using `fork()`, `exec()`, and `wait()` system calls. Compare it with the **Windows `CreateProcess()`** approach. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Three processes arrive at time **0 ms** with burst times **6, 8, and 4 ms** respectively. Calculate the **average waiting time** and **average turnaround time** under **FCFS** scheduling. Assume context switch time is **1 ms**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Option B**

#### **Part (a) — Unix vs Windows Process Creation (7 Marks)**

**Unix Approach (Two-Step: `fork()` + `exec()`):**

```c
pid_t pid = fork();

if (pid == 0) {
    // Child
    execlp("/bin/ls", "ls", "-l", NULL);
    exit(1);
}
else {
    // Parent
    wait(NULL);
}
```

**Working:**
- **`fork()`** creates a duplicate of the parent process. Both parent and child continue executing concurrently from the same point.
- The **child** then calls one of the `exec()` family functions to replace its memory image with the new program.
- The **parent** typically calls `wait()` to synchronize with the child's termination.
- This separation allows the child to **prepare** (e.g., redirect I/O) between `fork()` and `exec()` — a feature exploited by shells.

**Windows Approach (One-Step: `CreateProcess()`):**

```c
STARTUPINFO si;
PROCESS_INFORMATION pi;
ZeroMemory(&si, sizeof(si));
si.cb = sizeof(si);
ZeroMemory(&pi, sizeof(pi));

CreateProcess(
    "C:\\Windows\\System32\\notepad.exe",
    NULL, NULL, NULL, FALSE, 0, NULL, NULL,
    &si, &pi
);

WaitForSingleObject(pi.hProcess, INFINITE);
CloseHandle(pi.hProcess);
CloseHandle(pi.hThread);
```

**Working:**
- `CreateProcess()` is a **single, unified call** that both **creates a new process AND loads the executable** into its address space.
- The parent obtains handles (`pi.hProcess`, `pi.hThread`) to manipulate the child.
- Synchronization is achieved via `WaitForSingleObject()`.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Unix (`fork`+`exec`) | Windows (`CreateProcess`) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of syscalls | 2 | 1 |
| Memory layout | Initially duplicate, replaced on exec | Freshly created from executable |
| Pre-exec setup | Possible (between fork and exec) | Not possible (setup done via flags) |
| Complexity | Lower-level, more flexible | Higher-level, more parameters |
| Failure return | `-1` | `FALSE` + `GetLastError()` |

**(7 marks breakdown:** 2 for Unix explanation, 2 for Windows explanation, 3 for comparison.)

---

#### **Part (b) — FCFS Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Given:**

| Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 ms | 6 ms |
| P2 | 0 ms | 8 ms |
| P3 | 0 ms | 4 ms |

**Step 1: Construct the Gantt Chart (FCFS, in order P1 → P2 → P3)**

```
| P1 (6) | CS (1) | P2 (8) | CS (1) | P3 (4) |
0        6        7         15       16       20
```

**Step 2: Determine Completion Times**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{P1} &= 6 \text{ ms} \\
C_{P2} &= 6 + 1 + 8 = 15 \text{ ms} \\
C_{P3} &= 15 + 1 + 4 = 20 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute Turnaround Times** using $T_{AT} = C_i - A_i$ (where $A_i = 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{AT}(P1) &= 6 - 0 = 6 \text{ ms} \\
T_{AT}(P2) &= 15 - 0 = 15 \text{ ms} \\
T_{AT}(P3) &= 20 - 0 = 20 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Average Turnaround Time**

$$\overline{T_{AT}} = \frac{6 + 15 + 20}{3} = \frac{41}{3} \approx 13.67 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5: Compute Waiting Times** using $T_{WT} = T_{AT} - \text{Burst Time}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{WT}(P1) &= 6 - 6 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
T_{WT}(P2) &= 15 - 8 = 7 \text{ ms} \\
T_{WT}(P3) &= 20 - 4 = 16 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Average Waiting Time**

$$\overline{T_{WT}} = \frac{0 + 7 + 16}{3} = \frac{23}{3} \approx 7.67 \text{ ms}$$

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{\overline{T_{AT}} \approx 13.67 \text{ ms}, \quad \overline{T_{WT}} \approx 7.67 \text{ ms}}$$

**(7 marks breakdown:** 2 for Gantt chart, 2 for TAT, 2 for WT, 1 for final averages.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Total Processes" with "Child Processes" in fork problems** — Always clarify that $n$ sequential `fork()` calls produce $2^n$ **total** processes (not $2^n$ additional ones).
>
> 2. **Forgetting context-switch overhead in numerical problems** — When a problem mentions context switch time (e.g., 1 ms), many students omit it from the Gantt chart. Always add the context switch duration **between** consecutive processes.
>
> 3. **Mixing up `fork()` with `CreateProcess()` semantics** — Unix forks first, then execs separately. Windows does both in one step. Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** if these are confused.
>
> 4. **Not mentioning Copy-on-Write (COW)** — When explaining `fork()`, the **COW optimization** is a **bonus point** marker. Always include it in your explanation for full marks.
>
> 5. **Neglecting the `wait()` / Zombie process discussion** — Always pair `fork()` explanation with `wait()` to show you understand the **parent-child synchronization** and **zombie process prevention**.
>
> 6. **PCB contents — incomplete lists** — Examiners expect at least **6 fields** (state, PC, registers, memory info, scheduling info, list of open files). Listing only 2–3 is a common reason for losing marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Process Creation**

### 🔹 Core Concepts
- ☐ **Process** = Program in execution + its context (PCB, memory, registers).
- ☐ **Process creation** is triggered by system boot, user request, or system call.
- ☐ **Parent process** creates a **child process** using `fork()` / `CreateProcess()`.

### 🔹 Unix `fork()` + `exec()` Model
- ☐ `fork()` → creates a copy; child PID = 0, parent gets child PID.
- ☐ `exec()` → replaces the process image; PID is unchanged.
- ☐ `wait()` → parent blocks until child terminates.
- ☐ **Copy-on-Write (COW)** → memory pages duplicated only on write.

### 🔹 Windows `CreateProcess()` Model
- ☐ Single unified call; loads executable directly.
- ☐ Returns `PROCESS_INFORMATION` containing handles and PIDs.
- ☐ Synchronization via `WaitForSingleObject()`.

### 🔹 Process States
- ☐ Five states: **NEW → READY → RUNNING → WAITING → TERMINATED**.
- ☐ Transitions: dispatch, preempt, I/O wait, I/O completion, exit.

### 🔹 Key Formulas (Memorize!)
- ☐ $T_{AT} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$
- ☐ $T_{WT} = T_{AT} - T_{burst}$
- ☐ $U_{CPU} = \dfrac{T_{busy}}{T_{busy} + T_{idle}}$
- ☐ Total processes after $n$ forks = $2^n$

### 🔹 KTU-Specific Tips
- ☐ Always draw a **neat Gantt chart** for scheduling numerics.
- ☐ Mention **COW** in `fork()` explanations for bonus marks.
- ☐ Compare Unix vs Windows in any "process creation" question.
- ☐ Include **PCB diagram/contents** when asked about process representation.

### 🔹 Watch Out For
- ☐ Fork creates a **copy**, not a new program — you need `exec()` for that.
- ☐ Zombie processes occur when a child exits but parent doesn't `wait()`.
- ☐ Orphan processes occur when parent exits before child (re-parented to `init`).
- ☐ `vfork()` is similar to `fork()` but **shares memory** (faster, dangerous).
- ☐ `clone()` (Linux) is the **low-level primitive** underlying threads and containers.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reading and Deleting Directories
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reading and Deleting Directories

# Module 4 — Reading and Deleting Directories

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **directory** in an operating system is a special type of file that acts as a logical container, maintaining a mapping between human-readable **file names** and their underlying **inode numbers** (or physical disk addresses in FAT-style systems). Unlike a regular file that stores user data, a directory stores an **indexed sequence of directory entries**, each entry being a small fixed-size record containing metadata that the file system uses for name resolution, access control, and physical block location.

**Reading a directory** is the operation of sequentially traversing through these directory entries to retrieve information such as the file name, inode number, file type, and size. In POSIX-compliant operating systems (Linux/UNIX), this is achieved through a set of system/library calls: `opendir()`, `readdir()`, `rewinddir()`, `closedir()`, and the related `telldir()` / `seekdir()`.

**Deleting a directory** is the operation of removing a directory entry (and, when it is the last hard link, the underlying inode) from the file system. The POSIX standard distinguishes between:
- **`rmdir()`** — removes **only empty directories** (atomic safety check).
- **`unlink()` / `remove()`** — removes a directory entry for a regular file.
- **Recursive deletion** — a higher-level operation that first deletes all children and then the empty directory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — File Systems):**
> Students must understand that a directory is implemented as a file (a "container of files") and that reading it is just like reading sequential records, while deleting must respect the directory hierarchy rules of the specific file system (e.g., UNIX's "no deletion of non-empty directory" rule).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **library catalog card drawer** as the analogy for a directory:

1. Each **drawer** is a *directory*.
2. Each **catalog card** inside the drawer is a *directory entry* — it lists the **book title** (filename), the **book's unique library accession number** (inode number), and sometimes the **shelf location** (block pointer).
3. **Reading a directory** is analogous to flipping through the cards one by one to read all book titles and accession numbers — you go in sequentially.
4. **Deleting an empty directory** is like removing the empty drawer entirely from the cabinet. But the librarian (OS) will not let you throw away a drawer that still has cards in it — that is the **"non-empty directory" safety rule**, which is why `rmdir()` refuses to delete a non-empty directory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics & Standard Sizes (POSIX/Linux ext4):**
> - Default **directory entry size** on ext4: **255 bytes** (filename) + **8 bytes** (inode) + **variable length** for other attributes = commonly a **263-byte** entry, but recorded in **4 KB directory blocks** on disk.
> - Filename maximum length: **255 bytes** (NAME_MAX in POSIX).
> - Path component maximum: **4096 bytes** (PATH_MAX in Linux).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Directory Entry

A directory is internally a file that contains an array of records. Each **directory entry (dirent)** typically stores the following fields:

| Field | Typical Size | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `d_ino` | 8 bytes (in ext4) | Inode number of the file |
| `d_off` | 8 bytes | Offset of the next dirent from the start of the directory |
| `d_reclen` | 2 bytes | Length of this record (for variable-length entries) |
| `d_type` | 1 byte | File type (regular, directory, symlink, etc.) |
| `d_name[]` | up to 256 bytes | Null-terminated filename (max 255 chars + `\0`) |

> [!NOTE]
> In Linux, the `struct dirent` is exposed via `<dirent.h>`. The exact on-disk layout is **file-system dependent** (e.g., ext4 uses `ext4_dir_entry_2`), but the user-level API hides this complexity.

### 2.2 POSIX Directory Reading API — The "Why" and "How"

The reading of a directory in UNIX is performed through a layered API:

**Step 1: `opendir(const char *name)` → `DIR *`**
- *Why?* The OS must **open the underlying inode** of the directory, allocate a stream buffer, and check the calling process's **search (execute) permission** on each path component.
- *How?* Internally, it calls `open()` on the directory and wraps the file descriptor in a `DIR` stream (an opaque structure holding a buffer, the current offset, and a pointer to the on-disk block).

**Step 2: `readdir(DIR *dirp)` → `struct dirent *`**
- *Why?* To fetch the next directory entry one at a time. Returning `NULL` signals end-of-directory.
- *How?* The library reads a **directory block** (typically 4 KB) into a userspace buffer, parses entries, and returns a pointer into a statically-allocated `struct dirent` inside the `DIR` object. Subsequent calls may need to refill the buffer.

**Step 3: `rewinddir(DIR *dirp)`**
- *Why?* To reset the stream back to the first entry without re-opening.
- *How?* It seeks the file descriptor to offset 0 and resets internal pointers.

**Step 4: `closedir(DIR *dirp)`**
- *Why?* To release the file descriptor, free the buffer, and release locks.
- *How?* Calls `close()` on the internal file descriptor.

**Step 5: `telldir()` / `seekdir()`**
- *Why?* To save and restore a position inside a directory (useful in recursive walkers that need to remember where they were).
- *How?* `telldir()` returns a token (the byte offset), and `seekdir()` restores the stream to that offset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** `readdir()` is **not guaranteed** to return entries in any particular order on most UNIX systems (ext4 returns in hash order of filename, not alphabetical). Do not assume sorted output unless the program explicitly sorts it.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of the "Read" Operation

When the user calls `readdir()`:

1. Check if the stream's buffer has unparsed entries left.
2. If yes, return the next `dirent` pointer.
3. If no, issue an I/O request (e.g., `getdents()` syscall on Linux) to fetch the next **directory block** into the buffer.
4. Parse the block: each block contains a header, followed by packed `dirent` records of variable length.
5. Skip over "deleted" entries (marked by setting the first byte of the filename to a special value, often `\0` or a tombstone character).
6. Return the pointer to the next valid `dirent` or `NULL` if end-of-stream.

### 2.4 POSIX Directory Deletion API — The "Why" and "How"

| Call | Scope | Pre-condition | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| `rmdir(const char *path)` | Empty directory only | Directory must contain **no entries** other than `.` and `..` | Removes the directory's inode and the directory entry referring to it in its parent |
| `unlink(const char *path)` | Regular file (or symbolic link) | Caller must have write + execute permission on the **parent** directory | Decrements link count; frees inode when count reaches 0 |
| `remove(const char *path)` | File or empty directory | Same as above | Internally calls `unlink()` or `rmdir()` based on file type |
| `rm -rf` (shell) | Recursive | Requires user to confirm force | Iterates BFS/DFS, deleting leaves first, then non-empty parents |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does `rmdir()` refuse to delete a non-empty directory?**
> This is a **safety feature of the UNIX philosophy** — it forces the user (or script) to **explicitly** empty the directory before removal, preventing accidental mass deletion. The OS checks the link count and/or scans the directory contents; if any entry other than `.` and `..` is found, the syscall returns `-1` with `errno = ENOTEMPTY`.

### 2.5 Recursive Deletion — The Algorithm

The standard algorithm used by `rm -r` is a **post-order depth-first traversal**:

```
delete_recursive(node):
    for each child of node:
        if child is a directory:
            delete_recursive(child)   // recurse first
        else:
            unlink(child)              // delete files first
    rmdir(node)                        // finally remove the now-empty dir
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hard link caveat:** A file may have multiple hard links (e.g., `link count = 2`). Deleting one link does not free the inode until **all** links are removed. The directory's link count for a directory is always **at least 2** (`.` pointing to itself, and the entry in its parent).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Directory entry size (ext4) | $D_{size} = 8 + 8 + 2 + 1 + (\text{filename length} + 1)$ | Variable length, rounded to 4-byte boundary |
| Filename max length | $\vert \text{filename} \vert \leq 255$ bytes | Defined by `NAME_MAX` |
| Path max length | $\vert \text{path} \vert \leq 4096$ bytes | Defined by `PATH_MAX` (Linux) |
| Link count of a directory | $n_{links} \geq 2$ | `.` + entry in parent; for subdirs, + 1 per subdir |
| Inode freed condition | $n_{links} = 0 \ \text{AND}\ n_{open\_fds} = 0$ | File deletion frees inode only when both reach 0 |
| Blocks in a directory read | $N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{S_{dir}}{B} \right\rceil$ | Where $S_{dir}$ = directory size, $B$ = block size (usually 4 KB) |
| End-of-directory signal | `readdir() == NULL` | After exhausting all entries |
| Error: non-empty rmdir | `errno = ENOTEMPTY` | Returned when target dir has child entries |
| Error: missing parent perm | `errno = EACCES` | Caller lacks `w+x` on parent directory |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Build systems** (Make, CMake) use `readdir()` to discover source files.
- **File managers / GUIs** (Nautilus, Windows Explorer) use these calls to populate the file list.
- **Backup tools** (rsync, tar) walk the directory tree to enumerate files.
- **Antivirus / integrity scanners** recursively traverse directories to inspect each file.
- **Disk recovery / forensic tools** parse directory blocks directly to recover deleted entries (the "tombstone" technique leaves residue until overwritten).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Cost of Linear Directory Search

A classic analytical question in OS is: **how long does it take to find a file in a linearly searched directory of $N$ entries?**

**Step 1: Define the search model.**
Let $N$ be the total number of entries in the directory. Entries are stored as a sequential list, and lookup is by **linear search** (string compare against the target name).

**Step 2: Best case.**
The target filename is the first entry.

$$
T_{best} = O(1) = 1 \text{ comparison}
$$

**Step 3: Worst case.**
The target is the last entry, or not in the directory at all.

$$
T_{worst} = O(N) = N \text{ comparisons}
$$

**Step 4: Average case (uniform distribution assumption).**

$$
T_{avg} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} i = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{N(N+1)}{2} = \frac{N+1}{2}
$$

Therefore the average cost is:

$$
T_{avg} = \frac{N+1}{2} \approx \frac{N}{2} \text{ comparisons}
$$

**Step 5: Total cost for `readdir()` of the entire directory.**
Since `readdir()` must yield every entry, it performs $N$ internal operations:

$$
T_{readall} = \Theta(N)
$$

**Step 6: Disk-I/O aware cost.**
If each block holds $B$ entries and the directory spans multiple blocks, with disk access time $t_{seek} + t_{rot} + t_{transfer}$:

$$
T_{readall\_disk} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{B} \right\rceil \cdot (t_{seek} + t_{rot} + t_{transfer})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Modern file systems (ext4, NTFS) replace this linear scan with a **hash table** or **B+ tree** indexed by filename, reducing the lookup complexity to $O(1)$ or $O(\log N)$.

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Number of Blocks Fetched for a Directory of $S$ Bytes

Given a directory of total size $S$ bytes stored on a file system with block size $B$ (typically 4096 bytes):

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil
$$

The total bytes transferred (including partial block waste):

$$
T_{bytes} = N_{blocks} \cdot B = B \cdot \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reading vs Deleting Directories

| Aspect | Reading | Deleting (rmdir) | Recursive Deletion |
|---|---|---|---|
| System calls | `opendir`, `readdir`, `closedir` | `rmdir` | `rmdir` + `unlink` + recursive `readdir` |
| Mutates state? | No (read-only stream) | Yes (removes inode) | Yes (removes inodes & blocks) |
| Pre-condition | Read permission on dir | Empty + write+x on parent | Write+x on every parent in path |
| Failure errno | `EACCES`, `ENOENT` | `ENOTEMPTY`, `EACCES`, `EBUSY` | Same as rmdir, but per node |
| Recovery possible? | N/A | Yes (no journal mode) / No (journaled) | No (data is unrecoverable) |
| Atomicity | Per-block I/O | Single syscall, atomic w.r.t. crash | Multiple syscalls, NOT atomic |
| Affects link count? | No | Yes (decrements parent's link count) | Yes (for every directory removed) |

### 3.4 Worked Example — `readdir()` Trace

Suppose a directory `/proj` contains the entries: `.` , `..` , `Makefile` , `src/` , `README.md`. The user calls:

```c
DIR *d = opendir("/proj");
struct dirent *e;
while ((e = readdir(d)) != NULL) {
    printf("%s (inode=%lu)\n", e->d_name, e->d_ino);
}
closedir(d);
```

**Trace:**

1. `opendir("/proj")` → allocates `DIR` stream, fetches first block of `/proj` into buffer.
2. `readdir()` call 1 → returns `{name=".", ino=12345}`.
3. `readdir()` call 2 → returns `{name="..", ino=100}`.
4. `readdir()` call 3 → returns `{name="Makefile", ino=12350}`.
5. `readdir()` call 4 → returns `{name="src", ino=12360, d_type=DT_DIR}`.
6. `readdir()` call 5 → returns `{name="README.md", ino=12370}`.
7. `readdir()` call 6 → returns `NULL` (end-of-stream).
8. `closedir(d)` → releases resources.

### 3.5 Worked Example — Deletion Decision Tree

```
Attempt: rmdir("/proj")
    ├── Is /proj an empty directory? 
    │       ├── YES → unlink("/proj/.") from itself, decrement link count, free inode → SUCCESS
    │       └── NO  → return -1, errno = ENOTEMPTY → FAILURE
    └── Does caller have w+x on / (parent)?
            ├── YES → proceed
            └── NO  → return -1, errno = EACCES → FAILURE
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Directory Entry Layout (Logical View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["DIRENT Record"] --> nodeB["d_ino 8 bytes inode number"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["d_off 8 bytes offset to next entry"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["d_reclen 2 bytes record length"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["d_type 1 byte file type flag"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["d_name variable up to 256 bytes filename"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["0x00 DT_UNKNOWN"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["0x04 DT_DIR directory"]
    nodeE --> nodeE3["0x08 DT_REG regular file"]
    nodeE --> nodeE4["0x0A DT_LNK symbolic link"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Read Directory Flow (System Call Path)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["User code readdir d"] --> nodeB["Library checks buffer"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Buffer has next dirent"}
    nodeC -->|YES| nodeD["Return pointer to dirent"]
    nodeC -->|NO| nodeE["Issue getdents syscall"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Kernel reads next 4KB block of directory"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Parse block into dirent records"]
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeD --> nodeH{"Caller continues loop"}
    nodeH --> nodeI{"More entries"}
    nodeI -->|YES| nodeB
    nodeI -->|NO| nodeJ["closedir d releases resources"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Delete Directory Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["rmdir path called"] --> nodeB["Resolve path to inode"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Caller has w+x on parent"}
    nodeC -->|NO| nodeD["Return -1 errno EACCES"]
    nodeC -->|YES| nodeE{"Is target a directory"}
    nodeE -->|NO| nodeF["Return -1 errno ENOTDIR"]
    nodeE -->|YES| nodeG{"Is directory empty"}
    nodeG -->|NO| nodeH["Return -1 errno ENOTEMPTY"]
    nodeG -->|YES| nodeI["Decrement parent link count"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Free inode and data blocks"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Remove parent entry"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Return 0 SUCCESS"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Recursive Deletion (Post-Order Traversal)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["rm -r /proj invoked"] --> nodeB["readdir /proj"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"For each entry child"}
    nodeC -->|child is DIR| nodeD["Recurse delete_recursive child"]
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeC -->|child is FILE| nodeE["unlink child"]
    nodeE --> nodeC
    nodeC -->|no more children| nodeF["rmdir /proj now empty"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Return success"]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Directory Tree Hierarchy Used in Examples

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["/"] --> proj["proj"]
    proj --> mkf["Makefile"]
    proj --> readme["README.md"]
    proj --> src["src"]
    src --> main_c["main.c"]
    src --> utils_c["utils.c"]
    src --> headers["headers"]
    headers --> defs_h["defs.h"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Explain the difference between `rmdir()` and `unlink()` system calls in UNIX. Can `rmdir()` be used to remove a non-empty directory? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `rmdir()` is used to **remove a directory** from the file system. It succeeds **only if the directory is empty** (contains no entries other than `.` and `..`). If the directory has any files or sub-directories, `rmdir()` fails and sets `errno = ENOTEMPTY`.
- `unlink()` is used to **remove a directory entry for a regular file** (or symbolic link). It does not work on directories in most UNIX systems (it returns `EISDIR`).
- **Justification:** No, `rmdir()` cannot be used to remove a non-empty directory. This is a deliberate safety feature in the UNIX design to prevent accidental mass deletion. To remove a non-empty directory, the user must either (a) explicitly empty it first, or (b) use a recursive command like `rm -r` that internally calls `unlink()` on every child and then `rmdir()` on the parent.

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List the POSIX system calls used to read a directory and briefly state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. `opendir(path)` — Opens a directory stream and returns a `DIR *` pointer. Internally opens the directory file and allocates a buffer.
2. `readdir(dirp)` — Reads the **next** directory entry from the stream and returns a pointer to a `struct dirent` containing the filename, inode number, and file type. Returns `NULL` at end-of-stream.
3. `rewinddir(dirp)` — Resets the directory stream back to the first entry without closing/reopening.
4. `closedir(dirp)` — Closes the directory stream, releases the file descriptor and buffer, and frees resources.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the structure of a UNIX directory entry. Describe the `struct dirent` fields and how they are used during a `readdir()` call. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a suitable example, illustrate the algorithm for **recursive deletion** of a directory. Why is a depth-first post-order traversal used? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Structure of a UNIX Directory Entry (7 marks)**

A directory in UNIX is a special file containing an **ordered list of directory entries**. Each entry is a record described by the `struct dirent` (defined in `<dirent.h>`):

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `ino_t d_ino` | The **inode number** of the file. This is the unique identifier used by the file system to locate the file's metadata. |
| `off_t d_off` | The byte offset from the start of the directory to the **next** directory entry. |
| `unsigned short d_reclen` | The length of **this** record in bytes (variable length because filenames differ). |
| `unsigned char d_type` | A hint about the file type (e.g., `DT_REG`, `DT_DIR`, `DT_LNK`). |
| `char d_name[256]` | Null-terminated filename, up to 255 characters. |

When `readdir()` is called:
1. The C library maintains a `DIR` stream containing a userspace buffer of the current directory block (typically 4 KB read from disk).
2. It returns a pointer to the next valid `dirent` inside that buffer.
3. When the buffer is exhausted, a new block is fetched via the `getdents()` syscall (Linux) or `getdirentries()` (BSD).
4. Entries that have been deleted are skipped by recognizing a special "tombstone" marker in the first byte of `d_name`.
5. `readdir()` returns `NULL` after the last entry.

**(b) Recursive Deletion Algorithm (7 marks)**

**Example:** Remove the directory tree shown in Section 4.5.

**Algorithm (depth-first post-order):**

```
function delete_recursive(path):
    open path as a directory stream
    for each entry in the directory:
        if entry is a directory (and not "." or ".."):
            delete_recursive(path + "/" + entry.name)
        else:
            unlink(path + "/" + entry.name)
    close the directory stream
    rmdir(path)        # now empty, so succeeds
```

**Trace on the example tree `/proj`:**
1. Open `/proj` → entries: `.`, `..`, `Makefile`, `README.md`, `src/`
2. `Makefile` is a file → `unlink(/proj/Makefile)`.
3. `README.md` is a file → `unlink(/proj/README.md)`.
4. `src/` is a directory → recurse into `/proj/src/`.
5. In `/proj/src/`: unlink `main.c`, unlink `utils.c`. Recurse into `headers/`.
6. In `/proj/src/headers/`: unlink `defs.h`. Now empty → `rmdir(/proj/src/headers)`.
7. Back in `/proj/src/`: now empty → `rmdir(/proj/src)`.
8. Back in `/proj`: now empty → `rmdir(/proj)`. Done.

**Why post-order?**
Because `rmdir()` will only succeed on an **empty** directory. Therefore, all children (both files and sub-directories) must be deleted **before** the parent can be removed. This naturally requires a **post-order DFS**: visit children first, then process the node itself.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the POSIX directory reading API in detail, including the role of `opendir()`, `readdir()`, `rewinddir()`, and `closedir()`. Show a complete C program snippet that lists all files in a directory along with their inode numbers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the steps the operating system performs internally when `rmdir()` is invoked. What error conditions can cause `rmdir()` to fail, and how are they reported? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) POSIX Directory Reading API (7 marks)**

The POSIX standard defines a four-function API for reading directories, each serving a specific purpose:

1. **`opendir(const char *name)`** — Opens the directory whose path is `name` and returns a pointer to an opaque `DIR` structure. Internally, the OS opens the underlying inode, allocates a buffer, and checks the search permission on each path component.

2. **`readdir(DIR *dirp)`** — Returns a pointer to a `struct dirent` describing the next entry in the directory stream. Returns `NULL` at the end. The returned object is reused (not allocated) on each call, so it must be copied if persistence is needed.

3. **`rewinddir(DIR *dirp)`** — Resets the stream to the beginning, equivalent to closing and reopening without the overhead.

4. **`closedir(DIR *dirp)`** — Closes the directory stream, releases the file descriptor, and frees the buffer.

**C Program Example:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <dirent.h>

int main(void) {
    DIR *d = opendir(".");
    if (!d) { perror("opendir"); return 1; }

    struct dirent *e;
    while ((e = readdir(d)) != NULL) {
        printf("inode=%lu  name=%s\n", (unsigned long)e->d_ino, e->d_name);
    }
    closedir(d);
    return 0;
}
```

**(b) Internal Steps of `rmdir()` (7 marks)**

When the user invokes `rmdir(path)`, the OS performs the following steps:

1. **Path resolution** — Walks the path component by component, checking `execute (search)` permission on each parent directory.
2. **Inode lookup** — Locates the inode of the target.
3. **Type check** — Verifies the inode refers to a directory (returns `ENOTDIR` otherwise).
4. **Empty check** — Reads the directory contents. If any entry other than `.` and `..` is present, returns `ENOTEMPTY`.
5. **Permission check** — Caller must have `write + execute` on the **parent** directory (returns `EACCES` otherwise).
6. **Link-count update** — Decrements the link count of the parent (since the entry is removed) and removes the `.` self-reference.
7. **Inode deallocation** — Frees the inode, its data blocks, and removes the entry from the parent.
8. **Journal commit** (in journaled file systems like ext4) — The change is committed to the journal for crash consistency.
9. **Returns 0** on success.

**Common Error Conditions:**

| errno | Cause |
|---|---|
| `EACCES` | Write or search permission denied on parent |
| `EBUSY` | Directory is in use as a mount point or by a process |
| `EEXIST` / `ENOTEMPTY` | Directory contains files or sub-directories |
| `ENOENT` | Path component does not exist |
| `ENOTDIR` | A path component used as a directory is actually a file |
| `EROFS` | File system is read-only |
| `EIO` | Low-level I/O error |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> A very common mistake students make is **confusing `rmdir()` with `rm -r`**. `rmdir()` is a single syscall that **only** removes empty directories; it does not recurse. Another frequent error is **assuming `readdir()` returns sorted entries** — UNIX does not guarantee any order, so the program must sort if needed. Finally, students often forget to check the return value of `opendir()` and crash on `NULL`, losing 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Reading and Deleting Directories**

- A **directory** is a special file containing an array of **directory entries** (filename + inode + type + length).
- The POSIX reading API consists of **`opendir`, `readdir`, `rewinddir`, `closedir`** (plus `telldir`/`seekdir` for saving position).
- `readdir()` returns a pointer to a `struct dirent` describing the next entry; returns `NULL` at end-of-stream.
- The entry is **not thread-safe** across different threads sharing a `DIR *`; each thread should have its own stream.
- `readdir()` does **not** guarantee sorted output — file system dependent.
- **`rmdir()`** deletes **only empty directories**; failure on non-empty returns `errno = ENOTEMPTY`.
- **`unlink()`** removes a directory entry for a regular file or symbolic link; it cannot remove a directory in standard UNIX.
- **`remove()`** is a C-library wrapper that dispatches to `unlink()` or `rmdir()` based on type.
- **Recursive deletion** uses **post-order DFS**: delete children first, then the parent. This is required because `rmdir()` refuses non-empty directories.
- A directory's link count is **at least 2** (the `.` self-link + the entry in the parent). Subdirectories add one more link each (the `..` of the child).
- The on-disk layout of directory entries is **file-system specific** (ext4 uses 263-byte variable-length records, NTFS uses B+ tree indexed `$I30` index).
- For very large directories, modern file systems (ext4 with `dir_index`, NTFS, ReiserFS) use **hash or B+ tree** structures to avoid linear scans; classical UNIX V7 used a plain linear list.
- **Deletion is not atomic** across crashes in non-journaled file systems; **journaled** systems (ext3/ext4, NTFS) can recover consistency but user data may still be lost.
- "Deleted" entries in UNIX are not physically wiped immediately; their first byte of filename is set to a tombstone, and the slot can be reused. This is what forensic tools exploit.
- **Permission rule for deletion:** You don't need permission on the file being deleted; you need `write + execute` on the **parent** directory.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Permission Bits and Access Control Lists
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Permission Bits and Access Control Lists ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Permission Bits — Formal Definition In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, **Permission Bits** (also termed the *traditional UNIX permission model* or *mode bits*) refer to the **9-bit access control metadata** attached to every file and directory inode in a UNIX/Linux file system. These 9 bits are conceptually grouped into three triplets — **r (read = 4)**, **w (write = 2)**, and **x (execute = 1)** — assigned independently to three classes of users: **U (Owner/User)**, **G (Group)**, and **O (Others)**. The full 12-bit mode field (used by the `stat()` system call) is composed of: $$ \text{Mode} = \underbrace{file\_type}_{4 \text{ bits}} \;|\; \underbrace{setuid\,setgid\,sticky}_{3 \text{ special bits}} \;|\; \underbrace{r\,w\,x \;|\; r\,w\,x \;|\; r\,w\,x}_{9 \text{ permission bits}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 9 permission bits are stored in the inode's `i_mode` field (offset 0–8). The remaining 3 bits (9–11) hold the *set-UID*, *set-GID*, and *sticky* flags, which are covered as **Special Permission Bits** under Module 4. ### 1.2 Access Control Lists (ACLs) — Formal Definition An **Access Control List (ACL)** is an extended, discretionary access control (DAC) mechanism that augments the rigid UGO triplet by allowing **per-user** and **per-group** permission entries. Each ACL entry is a tuple: $$ \text{ACL\_Entry} = \langle \text{tag}, \text{qualifier}, \text{permission\_set} \rangle $$ where `tag` ∈ {`USER_OBJ`, `USER`, `GROUP_OBJ`, `GROUP`, `MASK`, `OTHER`, `EVERYONE`} per the **POSIX 1003.1e/1003.2c draft** standard (the de-facto ACL standard in Linux via `acl(5)`). > [!NOTE] > **Why ACLs exist:** The UGO model cannot express *"user `alice` gets read-only while user `bob` gets read-write"* without creating a new group. ACLs solve this **granularity problem** without restructuring the user-group database. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Office Building Imagine a **24-storey office tower**: - **Permission Bits** = the **three-tier public address system**. A single PA announcement (the 9 bits) plays to: *Floor Owners* (U), *Department Members* (G), and *All Visitors* (O). Everyone in the same tier hears the same rules. - **ACLs** = a **personalized keycard system**. The security desk keeps an explicit list — *Alice has floor-3 access 9-to-5, Bob has floor-3 and floor-7, the intern Charlie only has the lobby*. Each person gets an **individually crafted access profile** overriding the generic tier rules. > [!TIP] > **Exam Memory Hook:** *"UGO is a megaphone; ACL is a tailored keycard."* ### 1.4 Key Vocabulary (Bolded for Mandate) | Term | Bold Definition | Standard Value | |---|---|---| | **Inode** | File metadata structure holding permissions | 256 B (ext4 default) | | **DAC** | Discretionary Access Control — owner sets rules | — | | **MAC** | Mandatory Access Control (contrast, e.g., SELinux) | — | | **UMASK** | Default permission-masking bits | Octal `022` (typical) | | **ACL_MASK** | Maximum rights for named users/groups/owning group | Acts as a ceiling | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The 9-Bit Permission Model — Structured Logic The 9 bits are read **left-to-right** in the symbolic mode string: $$ \texttt{-rwxr-xr--} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \underbrace{\texttt{rwx}}_{U=7} \;\underbrace{\texttt{r-x}}_{G=5} \;\underbrace{\texttt{r--}}_{O=4} $$ **Why three classes?** The UNIX security philosophy is *"owner-controlled discretion over a hierarchy of widening trust."* The owner delegates coarse-grained access to the *group* (collaborators) and the *world* (everyone else).
