The kernel's permission check is **order-sensitive**: User match → Group match → Other match (whichever matches *first* wins). ### 2.2 Octal Encoding — Bit-to-Number Mapping Each `rwx` triplet maps to an **octal digit** via positional weighting: $$ \text{Digit} = (r \times 4) + (w \times 2) + (x \times 1) $$ If the bit is **absent**, the positional weight contributes **0**. Maximum digit = `4+2+1 = 7` (octal). ### 2.3 The Three Special Bits (Positions 9–11) | Bit Name | Octal Pos | Symbolic | Effect on Executable | Effect on Directory | |---|---|---|---|---| | **setuid** | bit 11 (`4000`) | `u+s` | Process runs with **file owner's UID** | Ignored | | **setgid** | bit 10 (`2000`) | `g+s` | Process runs with **file group's GID** | New files inherit **directory's GID** | | **sticky** | bit 9 (`1000`) | `+t` | Ignored | Only **owner** can delete/rename files (used on `/tmp`) | ### 2.4 The UMASK Formula When a process creates a new file, the kernel computes: $$ \text{Final Mode} = \text{Base Mode} \;\&\; \lnot \text{UMASK} $$ For a regular file: `Base = 0666` (no execute by default). For a directory: `Base = 0777` (execute = traverse). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** UMASK is the first line of defense for *defense-in-depth*. Setting `umask 027` enforces group-read-only and world-no-access by default — a CIS Benchmark recommendation. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory Table) | # | Formula / Rule | Symbolic Form | Numeric / Example | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Octal digit of a triplet | $d = 4r + 2w + 1x$ | `rwx` = $7$, `r-x` = $5$ | | 2 | Full mode value | $M = U \cdot 8^2 + G \cdot 8 + O$ | `0750` = $384$ | | 3 | UMASK effect (file) | $M_f = 0666 \;\&\; \lnot \text{umask}$ | umask `022` → files `0644` | | 4 | UMASK effect (dir) | $M_d = 0777 \;\&\; \lnot \text{umask}$ | umask `022` → dirs `0755` | | 5 | setuid numeric flag | $M \leftarrow M \;\vert\; 04000$ | `4755` | | 6 | setgid numeric flag | $M \leftarrow M \;\vert\; 02000$ | `2755` | | 7 | sticky numeric flag | $M \leftarrow M \;\vert\; 01000$ | `1777` (`/tmp`) | | 8 | ACL entry count (POSIX) | $N_{ACL}$ = up to $2^{16}$ | ext4 default | | 9 | Effective group perm | $P_{eff} = P_{group\_obj} \;\&\; \text{MASK}$ | Mask = ceiling | | 10 | Effective other perm | $P_{eff} = P_{other}$ | Always unrestricted | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** `MASK` in an ACL is **not** the same as the UMASK process mask. The ACL `MASK` is the *upper bound* for *named-user*, *owning-group*, and *named-group* entries. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — Octal ↔ Symbolic Conversion **Problem:** Convert symbolic mode `drwxr-x---` to its octal representation and explain the type field. **Step 1 — Identify the type character.** The first character is `d`, denoting a **directory**. This is one of the 7 standard file types in `i_mode` bits 12–15 (octal positions in `ls -l`: `-` regular, `d` directory, `l` symlink, `c` char dev, `b` block dev, `p` FIFO, `s` socket). **Step 2 — Slice the remaining 9 characters into triplets.** $$ \texttt{d} \;|\; \underbrace{\texttt{rwx}}_{\text{User}} \;|\; \underbrace{\texttt{r-x}}_{\text{Group}} \;|\; \underbrace{\texttt{---}}_{\text{Other}} $$ **Step 3 — Convert each triplet to an octal digit using the weighted sum.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{User digit} &= (1 \times 4) + (1 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 7 \\ \text{Group digit} &= (1 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (1 \times 1) = 5 \\ \text{Other digit} &= (0 \times 4) + (0 \times 2) + (0 \times 1) = 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Concatenate into the full octal mode.** The standard `chmod` notation for a directory with permissions `750` is: $$ \texttt{0750} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \texttt{u=rwx,g=rx,o=} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the decimal integer for kernel storage (optional, for syscall `chmod()`).** $$ M_{10} = (0 \cdot 8^4) + (7 \cdot 8^2) + (5 \cdot 8^1) + (0 \cdot 8^0) = 0 + 448 + 40 + 0 = 488 $$ This is what the kernel's VFS layer actually stores in the inode. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example — UMASK Application **Problem:** A process has `umask = 027`. A new file and a new directory are created. Compute the effective modes. **Step 1 — State the base modes (POSIX defaults).** $$ \text{Base}_{\text{file}} = 0666, \qquad \text{Base}_{\text{dir}} = 0777 $$ **Step 2 — Compute the bitwise NOT of UMASK.** $$ \lnot 027_{(8)} = \lnot (000 \, 010 \, 111)_2 = 111 \, 101 \, 000_{(2)} = 750_{(8)} $$ **Step 3 — Apply bitwise AND with the base modes.** For the file: $$ \begin{aligned} M_f &= 0666 \;\&\; 0750 \\ &= (110 \, 110 \, 110)_2 \;\&\; (111 \, 101 \, 000)_2 \\ &= (110 \, 100 \, 000)_2 \\ &= 0640_{(8)} \end{aligned} $$ For the directory: $$ \begin{aligned} M_d &= 0777 \;\&\; 0750 \\ &= (111 \, 111 \, 111)_2 \;\&\; (111 \, 101 \, 000)_2 \\ &= (111 \, 101 \, 000)_2 \\ &= 0750_{(8)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Interpret.** - **File** = `0640`: owner `rw-`, group `r--`, other `---`. - **Directory** = `0750`: owner `rwx`, group `r-x`, other `---`. > [!TIP] > **Real-world application:** Web servers (Apache/Nginx) often run with umask `022` so that newly created HTML files are world-readable while remaining writable only by the owner. --- ### 3.3 ACL Precedence — Decision Algorithm When a process with UID = `u` and primary GID list = `{g₁, g₂, …}` requests access to a file with ACL entries `{E₁, E₂, …}`, the kernel executes the following **ordered check** (POSIX 1003.1e §2.3.3): $$ \text{Match}(u, \text{ACL}) = \begin{cases} \text{Allow } E_{\text{USER\_OBJ}}.perms & \text{if } u = \text{owner UID} \quad (1) \\[4pt] \text{Allow } E_{\text{USER}}.perms & \text{if exists entry with } qualifier = u \quad (2) \\[4pt] \text{Allow } (E_{\text{GROUP\_OBJ}}.perms \;\&\; \text{MASK}) & \text{if } g_i = \text{owning GID} \quad (3) \\[4pt] \text{Allow } (E_{\text{GROUP}}.perms \;\&\; \text{MASK}) & \text{if exists } qualifier = g_i \quad (4) \\[4pt] \text{Allow } E_{\text{OTHER}}.perms & \text{otherwise} \quad (5) \\ \end{cases} $$ **The check is first-match-wins**, but the *result* of all matching named-user/group entries is the **union (bitwise OR)** of their permissions, *clamped* by the MASK. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — File Type Bits in `ls -ld` Output | Position 12–15 | `ls` Char | Inode Constant | Example Path | |---|---|---|---| | `0000` | `-` | `S_IFREG` | `/etc/hosts` | | `0001` | `p` | `S_IFIFO` | `/run/*.pid` | | `0010` | `c` | `S_IFCHR` | `/dev/tty` | | `0110` | `b` | `S_IFBLK` | `/dev/sda` | | `0100` | `d` | `S_IFDIR` | `/home` | | `0120` | `l` | `S_IFLNK` | `/usr/bin/python3` | | `0140` | `s` | `S_IFSOCK` | `/run/docker.sock` | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Depth Marker:** This inode-level breakdown is the kind of detail KTU examiners expect for a 14-mark question on "Explain the file mode structure." ### 3.5 Worked Example — Setuid on `/usr/bin/passwd` **Problem:** Explain why `/usr/bin/passwd` has mode `4755` and how the kernel honors this. **Step 1 — Identify the special bit.** The leading `4` denotes the **setuid bit** (octal `4000`). Symbolic: `rwsr-xr-x`. **Step 2 — Trace the execution.** When any unprivileged user invokes `/usr/bin/passwd`: 1. The kernel's `execve()` system call reads the inode and detects `S_ISUID` is set. 2. The kernel sets the new process's **effective UID (`euid`)** to the file owner's UID (`root` = 0). 3. The real UID (`ruid`) remains the caller's UID for audit. 4. The `passwd` binary can now write to `/etc/shadow` (mode `0640`, owner `root`, group `shadow`). **Step 3 — Security implication.** Any vulnerability in `passwd` becomes a **local privilege escalation** vector. Hence, only a curated set of binaries should carry setuid (e.g., `passwd`, `ping`, `mount`, `su`, `sudo`). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The 12-Bit File Mode Field (i_mode layout) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["i_mode 16-bit field"] --> B["Bits 15-12
File Type"] A --> C["Bit 11
setuid"] A --> D["Bit 10
setgid"] A --> E["Bit 9
sticky"] A --> F["Bits 8-6
User rwx"] A --> G["Bits 5-3
Group rwx"] A --> H["Bits 2-0
Other rwx"] F --> F1["bit8 r"] F --> F2["bit7 w"] F --> F3["bit6 x"] G --> G1["bit5 r"] G --> G2["bit4 w"] G --> G3["bit3 x"] H --> H1["bit2 r"] H --> H2["bit1 w"] H --> H3["bit0 x"] style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000 style B fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff style C fill:#f59e0b,color:#000 style D fill:#f59e0b,color:#000 style E fill:#f59e0b,color:#000 style F fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff style G fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff style H fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 ACL Decision Flow Inside the Kernel VFS ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Process requests
open / read / write"] --> Q{"UID equals
file owner?"} Q -- "YES" --> R["Apply USER_OBJ
permissions"] Q -- "NO" --> S{"ACL has named
USER entry for UID?"} S -- "YES" --> T["Apply USER
permissions AND MASK"] S -- "NO" --> U{"Any group GID
matches owning or
named GROUP?"} U -- "YES" --> V["Apply GROUP perms
AND MASK"] U -- "NO" --> W["Apply OTHER
permissions"] R --> X["Return grant/deny
to syscall layer"] T --> X V --> X W --> X style P fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style Q fill:#facc15,color:#000 style S fill:#facc15,color:#000 style U fill:#facc15,color:#000 style R fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff style T fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff style V fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff style W fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff style X fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.3 ACL Entry Hierarchy (POSIX Draft 1003.1e) ```mermaid flowchart TB ACL["ACL of file inode"] --> E1["USER_OBJ
owner perms = 0750"] ACL --> E2["USER alice
read only = r--"] ACL --> E3["USER bob
read write = rw-"] ACL --> E4["GROUP_OBJ devops
perms = r-x"] ACL --> E5["GROUP testers
perms = r--"] ACL --> E6["MASK
ceiling = rwx"] ACL --> E7["OTHER
perms = ---"] style ACL fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style E1 fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff style E2 fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff style E3 fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff style E4 fill:#f59e0b,color:#000 style E5 fill:#f59e0b,color:#000 style E6 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style E7 fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.4 Permission Check Sequence — Setuid Path ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Process participant K as Kernel VFS participant I as Inode participant P as passwd binary participant S as /etc/shadow U->>K: execve /usr/bin/passwd K->>I: read i_mode I-->>K: mode = 4755 (setuid root) K->>K: set euid = 0, ruid = 1000 K->>P: load and execute P->>K: open /etc/shadow for write K->>I: check i_mode of /etc/shadow I-->>K: owner=root, mode=0640 K->>K: euid==owner? YES -> grant write K-->>P: fd returned P->>S: write new hash S-->>P: OK P->>K: exit K->>K: restore ruid=euid ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Fallback Note:** All four diagrams above are renderable as Mermaid block diagrams (KTU students may also draw them manually in the answer sheet for full credit). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Explain the significance of the **sticky bit** with a suitable example. How is it represented in octal mode?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **sticky bit** (octal `1000`, symbolic `+t`) is a special permission bit applied to directories. When set on a directory, it restricts file deletion and renaming to the **file owner**, the **directory owner**, or the **root user**, even when other users have write permission on the directory. - **Example:** `/tmp` carries mode `1777` (`rwxrwxrwt`). Although every user has `rwx`, user `alice` cannot delete user `bob`'s files in `/tmp` because the sticky bit is set. - **Octal representation:** Leading `1` in a 4-digit mode, e.g., `1777`, `1755`. - **Why needed:** Prevents users from sabotaging each other in shared scratch space. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for octal notation.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between **permission bits** and **Access Control Lists (ACLs)**. When is an ACL preferred over the standard UGO model?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Permission Bits (UGO) | ACLs | |---|---|---| | Granularity | 3 classes only | Per-user and per-group | | Storage | 9 bits in inode | Variable-length list | | Tooling | `chmod`, `chown` | `setfacl`, `getfacl` | | Standard | POSIX 1003.1 (mandatory) | POSIX 1003.1e (draft) | **When ACLs are preferred:** When different individual users need different access levels on the same file/directory, e.g., *"Alice read-only, Bob read-write, the devops group read-execute, and everyone else denied"* — this cannot be expressed by UGO without creating multiple groups. (1 mark each point + 1 mark for the use-case.) --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Permission Bits Deep Dive* **(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **12-bit mode field** stored in the inode of a UNIX file. Explain the role of each of the **three special bits** — setuid, setgid, and sticky — and give one real-world example for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A file `report.txt` has symbolic mode `-rw-r-----`. Convert it to octal, compute the decimal equivalent as stored by the kernel, and show how the kernel decides access for (i) the file owner, (ii) a user in the file's group, and (iii) a user in neither category. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 marks** **Step 1 (2 marks) — Diagram of the 12-bit mode field:** | Bits 15–12 | Bit 11 | Bit 10 | Bit 9 | Bits 8–6 | Bits 5–3 | Bits 2–0 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | File type | setuid | setgid | sticky | User rwx | Group rwx | Other rwx | **Step 2 (5 marks) — Special bits explained:** - **setuid (bit 11, octal 4000):** When set on an **executable file**, the process's effective UID becomes the file owner's UID during execution. **Example:** `/usr/bin/passwd` is owned by root with mode `4755`. Any user invoking it temporarily gains root privileges to update `/etc/shadow`. - **setgid (bit 10, octal 2000):** On an **executable**, the effective GID becomes the file's group GID. On a **directory**, newly created files inherit the directory's GID (group inheritance). **Example:** Project shared directory `/proj` with mode `2775` and group `dev` ensures all new files belong to `dev` group. - **sticky (bit 9, octal 1000):** On a **directory**, only the file owner (or root) can delete/rename files within. **Example:** `/tmp` is `1777` — world-writable but tamper-proof across users. --- #### **Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 (2 marks) — Symbolic to octal conversion:** $$ \texttt{-rw-r-----} \;\Longrightarrow\; \underbrace{\texttt{rw-}}_{U=6} \;\underbrace{\texttt{r--}}_{G=4} \;\underbrace{\texttt{---}}_{O=0} \;\Longrightarrow\; 0640 $$ **Step 2 (1 mark) — Decimal equivalent for kernel storage:** $$ M_{10} = (0 \cdot 8^3) + (6 \cdot 8^2) + (4 \cdot 8^1) + (0 \cdot 8^0) = 0 + 384 + 32 + 0 = 416 $$ **Step 3 (4 marks) — Kernel access decisions (assume owner = `alice`, group = `staff`, file = `0640`):** - **(i) Owner `alice`:** Kernel sees `uid == i_uid`. Match Class U = `rw-`. **`alice` can read and write, cannot execute.** - **(ii) Group member `bob` (in `staff`):** Kernel sees `bob` is not owner, but `bob`'s GID equals the file's GID. Match Class G = `r--`. **`bob` can read, cannot write or execute.** - **(iii) User `eve` (in group `users`, not `staff`):** No UID match, no GID match. Fall through to Class O = `---`. **`eve` has no access at all.** The check is performed by `inode_permission()` in `fs/namei.c` of the Linux kernel. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *ACLs in Depth* **(a)** Define an **Access Control List** in the context of file systems. With a neat diagram, list all the **ACL entry types** as specified by the POSIX 1003.1e draft standard. What is the role of the **MASK** entry, and how does it differ from the UNIX UMASK? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Given the ACL below for file `project.conf`, determine the effective permissions granted to user `tom` (UID 1001, member of groups `dev` (GID 2001) and `staff` (GID 3001)), assuming the file owner is `root` (UID 0) and owning group is `dev` (GID 2001). Show each step of the precedence algorithm. **(7 marks)** ``` user::rw- user:tom:r-- user:jane:rw- group::r-- group:staff:r-x mask::rwx other::--- ``` --- #### **Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 marks** **Step 1 (2 marks) — Definition:** An ACL is a list of Access Control Entries (ACEs) attached to a file system object, allowing fine-grained permission grants beyond the UGO triplet. Each ACE specifies a *subject* (user or group) and the *permissions* allowed. **Step 2 (3 marks) — ACL entry types (POSIX 1003.1e):** | Tag Type | Identifier | Qualifier | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | `ACL_USER_OBJ` | `user::` | none (implicit) | Permissions of file owner | | `ACL_USER` | `user:name:` | user name/ID | Permissions for a specific named user | | `ACL_GROUP_OBJ` | `group::` | none (implicit) | Permissions of owning group | | `ACL_GROUP` | `group:name:` | group name/ID | Permissions for a specific named group | | `ACL_MASK` | `mask::` | none | Upper bound for USER, GROUP_OBJ, GROUP entries | | `ACL_OTHER` | `other::` | none | Permissions for users not matched by any above | **Step 3 (2 marks) — MASK vs UMASK distinction:** - **ACL MASK** is a *ceiling* on the effective rights of named users, owning group, and named groups. It does **not** affect `USER_OBJ` or `OTHER`. - **Process UMASK** is applied at file creation time and controls which bits are stripped from the default mode. --- #### **Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Identify tom's identity parameters:** - `uid = 1001` (not the owner `root`) - Groups: `dev (2001)`, `staff (3001)` **Step 2 — Walk the POSIX 1003.1e precedence algorithm:** | Step | Check | Match? | Result | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `uid == owner`? | No (`1001 ≠ 0`) | Continue | | 2 | Named user `tom`? | **Yes** | Perms = `r--` ∩ MASK `rwx` = `r--` | | 3 | (Would be checked if step 2 missed) | — | Skipped — first match wins for tom | | 4 | Owning group `dev`? tom is in it | — | (not reached) | | 5 | Other? | — | (not reached) | **Step 3 — Effective permission for tom = `r--` (read-only).** **Step 4 — Cross-check for `jane`:** - Named user `jane` entry exists with `rw-`. `rw-` ∩ MASK `rwx` = `rw-`. So **jane gets read-write**. **Step 5 — Cross-check for a generic `staff` member (not in `dev`):** - No named-user entry. Check `GROUP_OBJ` (owning group = `dev`) — no match. - Check `GROUP staff` — match! Perms = `r-x` ∩ MASK `rwx` = `r-x`. So **a staff member gets read + execute**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. Students **commonly lose 2 marks** by forgetting that **named USER entries are checked BEFORE the owning-group match**. The first match wins. > 2. **MASK is often ignored** in answers. Without MASK, `tom` would get `r--` (which happens to equal the AND with `rwx` in this case), but if MASK were `r--`, even a `rw-` named-user entry would collapse to `r--`. > 3. **Do not confuse `mask::` with `umask`.** The process UMASK is irrelevant here; only the ACL `MASK` entry matters. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **The 9 permission bits** form a 3×3 grid: `rwx × {U, G, O}`. Each `rwx` triplet encodes an octal digit via $4r+2w+1x$. - **The 3 special bits** (setuid `4`, setgid `2`, sticky `1`) prefix the 3-digit mode, forming a 4-digit octal like `4755`. - **`/etc/shadow` is mode `0640` and owned by `root:shadow`**, so only root and shadow-group members can read it — a classic real-world DAC example. - **Order of access check:** `OWNER → NAMED USER → OWNING GROUP → NAMED GROUP → OTHER`. **First match wins.** - **ACL MASK** is a *ceiling* on named-user, owning-group, and named-group permissions. It does NOT affect USER_OBJ or OTHER. - **UMASK** is applied at **file creation time**; ACL MASK is consulted at **every access check**. - **Setuid on a script is ignored on most modern kernels** (shebang race-condition protection). Setuid only works on compiled ELF binaries. - **The sticky bit is a directory-only defense**; it does not affect regular file execution. - **ACLs are discretionary** (owner-controlled). For mandatory controls, the OS uses **MAC** (e.g., SELinux, AppArmor). - **`chmod 4755 file` vs `chmod u+s file`** — both set setuid; the former is numeric, the latter is symbolic. - **KTU hot keywords:** inode `i_mode`, POSIX 1003.1e, DAC, first-match-wins, ACL_MASK, UMASK, setuid, setgid, sticky, VFS, effective UID, defense in depth.

topics.title: Mounting a File System; File Organization: The Inode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mounting a File System; File Organization: The Inode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Mounting a File System — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Mounting** is the OS-level operation of attaching a file system (stored on a physical or logical storage device) to a specific point in the existing directory hierarchy of the root file system, making its contents accessible to users and applications.

The directory at which the file system is attached is called the **mount point**. After a successful mount, the contents of the new file system appear as a subtree under the mount point, just like any other directory.

**Key terminology used in KTU 2024 syllabus:**

- **Root File System** — The base file system that is mounted by the OS at boot time.
- **Mount Point** — An existing directory in the parent (root) file system where the child file system is grafted.
- **Mount Table** — A kernel-resident data structure that records all currently mounted file systems.
- **Unmounting** — The reverse operation, which cleanly detaches a file system after flushing all in-memory buffers back to disk.
- **Mounting at Boot** — The OS consults `/etc/fstab` (Linux) or registry (Windows) to automatically mount required file systems at startup.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The OS must verify that the device contains a *valid* file system (via the superblock/magic number) **before** allowing a mount — this prevents data corruption from misidentified media.

### 1.2 The Inode — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> An **Inode (Index Node)** is a fixed-size on-disk data structure used by UNIX-like operating systems to store all metadata about a file **except its name**. Each file is uniquely identified within a file system by its **inode number (i-number)**, which is the index of its inode in the inode table.

**What an Inode stores (Metadata):**
- File type (regular, directory, symbolic link, block/character device)
- Permissions (rwx for owner, group, others)
- Owner UID and Group GID
- File size (in bytes)
- Timestamps: **atime** (access), **mtime** (modification), **ctime** (inode change)
- Link count (number of hard links)
- Pointers to the data blocks containing the file's actual content
- Inode number itself (uniquely assigned by the FS)

**What an Inode does NOT store:**
- The **filename** of the file
- The contents of its **parent directory**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Mounting — The Library Analogy:**
Imagine a large central library (your **root file system**) with hundreds of shelf sections (`/home`, `/usr`, `/var`). One day, a donation of new books arrives in a sealed crate from another library (a new disk partition). To make these books accessible to readers, a librarian must:
1. Open the crate and check the catalog (verify the file system).
2. Place the crate on a designated empty table in the main library (the **mount point**, e.g., `/mnt/usb`).
3. Update the master catalog to record this new collection (the **mount table**).

After this, users browsing the library can walk up to that table and pick up the new books as if they had always been there. This entire process is **mounting**.

**The Inode — The Index Card Analogy:**
Think of an old library card catalog. Each book has an **index card** that records everything about it: title type, author (owner), page count (size), the shelf where it physically sits (data block pointers), and a unique card number. The card does **not** contain the book’s title written on its face in a way that is searchable — the title is on the **drawer label** (directory entry), which points to the card by its number.

Similarly, an **inode** is the index card of a file, and the **directory entry** (filename + inode number) is the drawer label that maps a human-readable name to that card.

> [!TIP]
> When you run `ls -i` in Linux, you are listing directory entries along with their corresponding **inode numbers** — a direct visualization of the inode concept.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mounting Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

When a user (or the system) issues a mount command, the OS performs the following sequence:

1. **Privilege & Parameter Check** — Only the superuser (root) is permitted to perform a mount. The OS validates the device name, mount point, file system type, and mount options (read-only, no-execute, etc.).
2. **Device Identification & Superblock Read** — The OS reads **Block 0** (or the equivalent superblock location) of the target device. The superblock contains critical metadata: block size, total blocks, free block count, inode table location, magic number, and FS type identifier.
3. **File System Validation** — The OS compares the magic number and structural consistency of the superblock. If the device is unformatted or corrupt, the mount is **rejected** to prevent writing on a misidentified medium.
4. **VFS Mount Structure Allocation** — The kernel allocates a `vfsmount` structure (Virtual File System layer in Linux) to represent this mount in memory.
5. **Mount Table Update** — The new entry is appended to the kernel’s mount table. The mount point’s directory inode is marked to indicate it acts as a mount anchor.
6. **Caching Initialization** — A buffer cache for this file system is initialized, and the root inode of the new file system is loaded.
7. **Visibility** — The new file system is now navigable through the mount point in the global directory tree.

**Unmounting (reverse procedure):**
- The OS verifies that no process holds open files inside the target file system.
- All dirty (modified) buffers are flushed to disk.
- The mount table entry is removed.
- The `vfsmount` structure is deallocated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why unmounting is mandatory before removal:** Removing a storage device without unmounting leaves modified buffer cache data unflushed, leading to **file system corruption** and data loss.

### 2.2 Steps Involved in Mounting — Summary Table

| Step | Action | OS Component Involved | Failure Consequence |
|:-----|:-------|:---------------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | Validate user privileges | Security module | Permission denied error |
| 2 | Locate block device | Device driver | Device not found |
| 3 | Read superblock | Disk I/O subsystem | Read I/O error |
| 4 | Verify magic number & consistency | FS type-specific code | Invalid FS / mount fails |
| 5 | Allocate VFS mount struct | VFS layer | Memory exhaustion |
| 6 | Update mount table | Kernel mount subsystem | Inconsistency in path resolution |
| 7 | Initialize inode cache | In-memory inode manager | Slow file access |

### 2.3 Inode-Based File Organization — The "Why" and "How"

**The "Why":** Early file systems stored file metadata *inside the directory entry itself*. This made directory operations slow and prevented sharing of file metadata across multiple names (hard links). The inode decouples the **identity** of a file (inode number) from its **name** (directory entry), enabling:
- **Hard links** — multiple directory entries pointing to the same inode.
- **Fast lookups** — directory entries are small, while inodes are uniform and indexable.
- **Independent metadata updates** — renaming a file changes only the directory entry, not the inode.

**The "How":** A directory on disk is simply a special file whose contents are a list of **(inode number, filename)** pairs. When a user accesses `/home/alice/report.txt`:
1. The kernel reads the inode of `/` (root directory).
2. It looks up `home` in the root’s directory data blocks → obtains inode number.
3. It reads the inode of `/home` → looks up `alice` → obtains inode number.
4. It reads the inode of `/home/alice` → looks up `report.txt` → obtains inode number.
5. It reads the inode of `report.txt` and uses its data block pointers to fetch file content.

### 2.4 Inode Structure — Block Pointer Layout

To support files of widely varying sizes efficiently, an inode contains a **hybrid pointer scheme**:

- **Direct Pointers (D of them):** Point directly to data blocks. Used for small files; provides fastest access (single disk read).
- **Single Indirect Pointer:** Points to an *index block* whose entries point to data blocks. Adds one level of indirection.
- **Double Indirect Pointer:** Points to a block of index-block pointers.
- **Triple Indirect Pointer:** Points to a block of double-indirect-block pointers.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The triple-indirect pointer is what allows modern UNIX file systems to support files larger than 4 GB on 32-bit inode systems, and files of essentially unlimited size on 64-bit systems.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Let the following symbols be standard throughout KTU exam problems on inodes:

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|:-------|:--------|:-----|
| $B$ | Block size of the file system | bytes |
| $P$ | Size of a disk block pointer | bytes |
| $N$ | Number of direct pointers in the inode | pointers |
| $K$ | Number of pointers that fit in one block | pointers (dimensionless) |

The number of pointers per index block is computed as:

$$K = \frac{B}{P}$$

**Maximum file size** supported by the inode's pointer structure:

$$\text{MaxSize} = N \cdot B \;+\; K \cdot B \;+\; K^{2} \cdot B \;+\; K^{3} \cdot B$$

**Maximum number of data blocks** addressable:

$$\text{MaxBlocks} = N + K + K^{2} + K^{3}$$

**Maximum file size in closed-form** (factored form):

$$\text{MaxSize} = B \cdot \left( N + \sum_{i=1}^{3} K^{i} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **vanilla UNIX V7 inode**: $N = 13$, block size $B = 4096$ bytes, pointer size $P = 4$ bytes, so $K = 1024$. The effective maximum file size is approximately **4 TB** (terabytes), which is why early UNIX used 32-bit pointer arithmetic and modern systems use 64-bit.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Systems** (Oracle, PostgreSQL) depend on inode-based access for predictable I/O performance — DBAs tune inode-to-block ratios based on expected file sizes.
- **Embedded Systems & IoT**: JFFS2, UBIFS file systems use inode-like structures (nodes) to manage wear-leveling on flash storage.
- **Cloud Storage & NAS**: Mounting network file systems (NFS, CIFS) over the same mount protocol enables seamless distributed storage.
- **Containerization (Docker):** Union mount file systems (OverlayFS) stack multiple lower layers and an upper writable layer, all anchored on inode-aware operations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum File Size from Inode Pointer Structure

**Given:**
- Block size $B = 4096$ bytes
- Pointer size $P = 4$ bytes
- Direct pointers $N = 12$ in the inode
- One single-indirect, one double-indirect, one triple-indirect pointer

**Step 1 — Compute the number of pointers per index block ($K$):**

$$K = \frac{B}{P} = \frac{4096 \text{ bytes}}{4 \text{ bytes/pointer}} = 1024 \text{ pointers/block}$$

*Conversion logic:* Each block is 4096 bytes; each pointer consumes 4 bytes; therefore 4096 ÷ 4 = 1024 pointers fit in a single index block.

**Step 2 — Contribution from direct pointers:**

$$\text{Size}_{\text{direct}} = N \cdot B = 12 \cdot 4096 = 49{,}152 \text{ bytes} = 48 \text{ KB}$$

*Conversion logic:* Each of the 12 direct pointers addresses one full data block of 4096 bytes; 12 × 4096 = 49152 bytes, or 48 KB.

**Step 3 — Contribution from single indirect pointer:**

$$\text{Size}_{\text{single}} = K \cdot B = 1024 \cdot 4096 = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB}$$

*Conversion logic:* The single indirect pointer references one index block; that block holds 1024 pointers; each pointer references one 4096-byte data block; total = 1024 × 4096 = 4,194,304 bytes = 4 MiB.

**Step 4 — Contribution from double indirect pointer:**

$$\text{Size}_{\text{double}} = K^{2} \cdot B = (1024)^{2} \cdot 4096 = 1{,}048{,}576 \cdot 4096$$

$$\text{Size}_{\text{double}} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB}$$

*Conversion logic:* The double-indirect pointer references one index block containing 1024 pointers; each of those points to an index block containing 1024 data block pointers; total data blocks = 1024 × 1024 = 1,048,576; multiplied by block size 4096 = 4 GiB.

**Step 5 — Contribution from triple indirect pointer:**

$$\text{Size}_{\text{triple}} = K^{3} \cdot B = (1024)^{3} \cdot 4096$$

$$(1024)^{3} = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824$$

$$\text{Size}_{\text{triple}} = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \cdot 4096 = 4{,}398{,}046{,}511{,}104 \text{ bytes} \approx 4 \text{ TB}$$

*Conversion logic:* Triple indirection stacks three levels of 1024 pointers; total data blocks = 1024³ ≈ 1.07 billion; multiplied by 4096 bytes ≈ 4.4 TB.

**Step 6 — Total maximum file size:**

$$\text{MaxSize} = 48 \text{ KB} + 4 \text{ MB} + 4 \text{ GB} + 4 \text{ TB}$$

$$\text{MaxSize} \approx 4 \text{ TB} + 4 \text{ GB} + 4 \text{ MB} + 48 \text{ KB}$$

$$\text{MaxSize} \approx 4{,}398{,}098{,}926{,}592 \text{ bytes}$$

*Conversion logic:* The 4 TB term dominates; the smaller terms are negligible in comparison but are required for an exact KTU answer.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Smaller File System

**Given:** $B = 1024$ bytes, $P = 4$ bytes, $N = 10$ direct pointers, 1 single + 1 double indirect pointer (no triple).

**Step 1:** $K = \dfrac{1024}{4} = 256$ pointers/block.

**Step 2:** $\text{Size}_{\text{direct}} = 10 \cdot 1024 = 10{,}240$ bytes.

**Step 3:** $\text{Size}_{\text{single}} = 256 \cdot 1024 = 262{,}144$ bytes = 256 KB.

**Step 4:** $\text{Size}_{\text{double}} = 256^{2} \cdot 1024 = 65{,}536 \cdot 1024 = 67{,}108{,}864$ bytes = 64 MB.

**Step 5:** Total maximum = $10{,}240 + 262{,}144 + 67{,}108{,}864 = 67{,}381{,}248$ bytes $\approx 64.26$ MB.

*Conversion logic:* Add all three contributions; the double-indirect term dominates.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cost vs. Performance Tradeoff

| Access Method | Levels of Indirection | Disk Reads for First Byte | Best For |
|:--------------|:---------------------|:--------------------------|:---------|
| Direct | 0 | 1 (inode read) + 1 (data) | Small files |
| Single Indirect | 1 | 1 (inode) + 1 (index) + 1 (data) | Medium files |
| Double Indirect | 2 | 1 (inode) + 2 (index chain) + 1 (data) | Large files |
| Triple Indirect | 3 | 1 (inode) + 3 (index chain) + 1 (data) | Very large files |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Tradeoff:** Increasing the number of direct pointers speeds up small-file access but wastes inode space for large files. Increasing indirect levels supports larger files but penalizes random-access latency. The UNIX designers chose a balanced hybrid.

### 3.4 Mounting Sequence — Detailed Algorithmic Walkthrough

When a user issues `mount /dev/sdb1 /mnt/usb` (Linux example):

1. **Command Parsing:** The `mount` utility parses the device file path, mount point, and options.
2. **Privilege Verification:** Calls `setuid()` to elevate to root.
3. **System Call `mount()`:** Invokes the kernel’s VFS `do_mount()` routine.
4. **Device Resolution:** The kernel calls the block device driver to obtain the device’s superblock location.
5. **Superblock Read:** Reads the first sector(s) of the device into memory.
6. **Magic Number Check:** Validates the file system signature (e.g., `0xEF53` for ext2/ext3/ext4, `0x4D44` for NTFS).
7. **File System Driver Selection:** Routes to the appropriate FS handler (`ext4_fill_super`, `ntfs_fill_super`, etc.).
8. **VFS Structure Population:** Populates the `super_block`, allocates root inode, root dentry.
9. **Path Component Resolution:** Walks the existing VFS tree to reach the mount point directory.
10. **Mount Point Linking:** Attaches the new `vfsmount` to the mount point’s dentry and adds an entry to the mount namespace.
11. **Return Success:** The `mount()` system call returns 0, and `mount` prints confirmation.

*Conversion logic:* Each step corresponds to a layer in the layered VFS architecture, abstracting the user from device-specific operations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Mount Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User mount command] --> B[Privilege check root user]
    B --> C[Device driver resolves block device]
    C --> D[Read superblock from device]
    D --> E{Magic number valid}
    E -->|No| F[Return invalid FS error]
    E -->|Yes| G[Populate VFS superblock struct]
    G --> H[Allocate root inode and root dentry]
    H --> I[Resolve mount point in existing tree]
    I --> J[Link vfsmount to mount point dentry]
    J --> K[Append entry to mount table]
    K --> L[Initialize buffer cache for FS]
    L --> M[Mount complete file system accessible]
    F --> N[Operation aborted]
```

*Diagram explanation:* The flowchart traces the path of a `mount` request from the user-space utility through the kernel’s VFS layer down to the block device driver, then back up through validation and structure allocation. The diamond node `Magic number valid` represents the critical decision gate that prevents data corruption.

### 4.2 Inode Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inodeA[Inode Block] --> metaA[File metadata section]
    inodeA --> directA[Direct pointer 1]
    inodeA --> directB[Direct pointer 2]
    inodeA --> directC[Direct pointer N]
    inodeA --> singA[Single indirect pointer]
    inodeA --> doubA[Double indirect pointer]
    inodeA --> tripA[Triple indirect pointer]
    metaA --> metaInfoA[Type perms uid gid size timestamps link count]
    singA --> idxBlkA[Index block level 1]
    idxBlkA --> dataBlkA[Data block 1]
    idxBlkA --> dataBlkB[Data block 2]
    doubA --> idxBlkB[Index block level 2]
    idxBlkB --> idxBlkC[Index block level 1]
    idxBlkC --> dataBlkC[Data block 3]
    tripA --> idxBlkD[Index block level 3]
    idxBlkD --> idxBlkE[Index block level 2]
    idxBlkE --> idxBlkF[Index block level 1]
    idxBlkF --> dataBlkD[Data block 4]
```

*Diagram explanation:* The diagram visualizes the hierarchical structure of an inode. The metadata block holds descriptive attributes; the direct pointers reach data blocks without indirection; each indirect pointer initiates a chain of index blocks that ultimately point to data blocks. The deeper the level, the larger the file that can be addressed.

### 4.3 File System Organization: From Pathname to Disk

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pathA[Pathname home alice report.txt] --> lookupA[Directory traversal]
    lookupA --> rootInode[Root inode number 2]
    rootInode --> rootData[Root directory data blocks]
    rootData --> homeEntry[Entry home inode 100]
    homeEntry --> homeInode[Home inode 100]
    homeInode --> homeData[Home directory data blocks]
    homeData --> aliceEntry[Entry alice inode 250]
    aliceEntry --> aliceInode[Alice inode 250]
    aliceInode --> aliceData[Alice directory data blocks]
    aliceData --> reportEntry[Entry report.txt inode 999]
    reportEntry --> reportInode[Report inode 999]
    reportInode --> reportPointers[Inode data block pointers]
    reportPointers --> fileContent[File content on disk]
```

*Diagram explanation:* This diagram illustrates the full chain of resolution from a human-readable pathname to the physical file content. Each directory is itself a file containing entries that map names to inode numbers. The OS follows this chain step by step, using inodes as the indirection mechanism between names and physical data.

### 4.4 Mount Table Logical View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootTree[Root directory tree] --> mp1[Mount point mnt usb]
    rootTree --> mp2[Mount point home user]
    mp1 --> fs1[USB device sdb1]
    mp2 --> fs2[NVMe partition nvme0n1p5]
    fs1 --> dataA[USB file system data]
    fs2 --> dataB[User home file system data]
    mountTab[Kernel Mount Table] -.-> mp1
    mountTab -.-> mp2
```

*Diagram explanation:* The diagram represents the kernel’s view of multiple mounted file systems anchored at different mount points. The mount table (dashed arrows) maintains the cross-references between the VFS tree and the actual physical devices.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**Define mounting. What is a mount point? Why is mounting necessary in an operating system?**

**Model Answer:**

**Mounting** is the OS operation of attaching a file system (residing on a storage device) to a specific directory in the existing root file system hierarchy.

A **mount point** is the directory in the parent (root) file system at which the child file system is grafted, making its contents accessible as a subtree.

**Mounting is necessary because:**
1. **Device Integration** — Storage devices arrive dynamically (USB, CD-ROM, network drives) and must be integrated into the directory tree.
2. **Logical Hierarchy** — Users expect a single unified directory tree, regardless of the physical partition layout.
3. **File System Heterogeneity** — Different partitions may use different file systems (ext4, NTFS, FAT32); mounting allows the OS to handle them uniformly via the VFS layer.
4. **Controlled Access** — Mount options (read-only, no-execute) enforce security and integrity policies.

> [!TIP]
> **Word-count tip:** 3-mark KTU answers should be ~100–150 words. Mention *VFS layer* and *superblock verification* for bonus marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**What is an inode? List the fields stored in an inode.**

**Model Answer:**

An **inode (Index Node)** is a fixed-size on-disk data structure in UNIX-like file systems that stores all metadata about a file. Each file is uniquely identified by its **inode number**, which is the index of its inode in the inode table.

**Fields stored in an inode:**
- File **type** (regular, directory, link, device)
- **Permissions** (read, write, execute for owner/group/others)
- **Owner UID** and **Group GID**
- **File size** (in bytes)
- **Timestamps** — access time, modification time, inode change time
- **Link count** (number of hard links)
- **Data block pointers** (direct, single-indirect, double-indirect, triple-indirect)

**Note:** The **filename** is *not* stored in the inode; it is stored in the directory entry that points to the inode.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for mounting a file system in UNIX/Linux. Discuss the role of the superblock and mount table in this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a UNIX file system with block size **4096 bytes**, pointer size **4 bytes**, and an inode containing **12 direct pointers, 1 single-indirect pointer, 1 double-indirect pointer, and 1 triple-indirect pointer**. Compute the maximum file size supported. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

The **mounting procedure** in UNIX/Linux proceeds as follows:

1. **Invocation:** The user invokes the `mount` command (e.g., `mount /dev/sda2 /mnt/data`).
2. **Privilege Elevation:** The `mount` utility executes with superuser (root) privileges via the setuid mechanism.
3. **System Call:** The `mount()` system call is invoked, passing the device file path, mount point, file system type, and mount options.
4. **Device Driver Resolution:** The kernel resolves the device file (e.g., `/dev/sda2`) to its block device via the device driver layer.
5. **Superblock Read:** The kernel reads the **superblock** (typically at byte offset 1024 in many UNIX file systems). The superblock contains critical metadata:
   - Magic number (FS type identifier, e.g., `0xEF53` for ext2/ext4)
   - Block size, total blocks, free block count
   - Inode table location and size
   - Mount count and maximum mount count
6. **Magic Number & Consistency Check:** The OS validates the magic number and structural integrity. If validation fails, the mount is **rejected** to protect data.
7. **VFS Structure Population:** A `super_block` VFS structure is allocated and populated with the device’s file system parameters. The root inode and root directory entry (dentry) of the new file system are loaded into memory.
8. **Mount Table Update:** A new entry is added to the kernel’s **mount table**, recording the device, mount point, and associated VFS structures.
9. **Mount Point Linking:** The mount point directory in the existing tree is marked with a "mount" flag in its dentry, indicating it is the root of the new file system.
10. **Buffer Cache Initialization:** A dedicated buffer cache is initialized for the new file system to optimize subsequent I/O.
11. **Return:** The system call returns success, and the file system is now fully accessible.

**Role of the Superblock:** Acts as the file system’s "metadata header" — it is the first structure read during mounting, providing the geometry and configuration of the file system. Without a valid superblock, mounting cannot proceed.

**Role of the Mount Table:** Serves as the kernel’s authoritative record of all active mounts. When a user accesses any path, the VFS consults the mount table to determine which physical file system and inode correspond to the path. The mount table is also consulted during unmounting and during path-name resolution.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Given:** $B = 4096$ bytes, $P = 4$ bytes, $N = 12$ direct pointers, plus 1 single, 1 double, 1 triple indirect.

**Step 1:** Compute pointers per block:

$$K = \frac{B}{P} = \frac{4096}{4} = 1024 \text{ pointers/block}$$

**Step 2:** Direct pointers contribution:

$$\text{Size}_{\text{direct}} = 12 \cdot 4096 = 49{,}152 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3:** Single indirect contribution:

$$\text{Size}_{\text{single}} = 1024 \cdot 4096 = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 4:** Double indirect contribution:

$$\text{Size}_{\text{double}} = 1024^{2} \cdot 4096 = 1{,}048{,}576 \cdot 4096 = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 5:** Triple indirect contribution:

$$\text{Size}_{\text{triple}} = 1024^{3} \cdot 4096 = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \cdot 4096 = 4{,}398{,}046{,}511{,}104 \text{ bytes} \approx 4 \text{ TB}$$

**Step 6:** Maximum file size:

$$\text{MaxSize} = 49{,}152 + 4{,}194{,}304 + 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 + 4{,}398{,}046{,}511{,}104$$

$$\text{MaxSize} \approx 4{,}402{,}345{,}721{,}856 \text{ bytes} \approx 4 \text{ TB}$$

The 4 TB term dominates; for exam purposes, the answer is commonly written as **4 TB + 4 GB + 4 MB + 48 KB ≈ 4 TB**.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the inode-based file organization in UNIX. With a neat diagram, describe the internal structure of an inode and its various pointer types. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A UNIX file system has a block size of **1024 bytes** and a disk pointer size of **2 bytes**. An inode contains **5 direct pointers**, **1 single-indirect pointer**, and **1 double-indirect pointer** (no triple-indirect). Calculate the maximum possible file size. Also calculate the maximum number of files that can be addressed if the inode table contains **2048 inodes**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Inode-based file organization** in UNIX decouples a file’s **name** from its **metadata and content address**. A directory entry contains only two pieces of information: the **filename** and the **inode number**. All other file information resides in the inode, which is located via the inode table using the inode number as an index.

**Internal structure of an inode:**

1. **Metadata Section** — Stores file type, permissions, owner UID/GID, file size, timestamps (atime, mtime, ctime), and link count.
2. **Direct Pointers (D entries)** — Each pointer directly references one data block on disk. Used for small files; no indirection overhead.
3. **Single Indirect Pointer** — Points to an index block, which in turn contains pointers to data blocks. Adds one level of indirection.
4. **Double Indirect Pointer** — Points to an index block of index blocks. Allows addressing of larger files.
5. **Triple Indirect Pointer (optional)** — Adds a third level of indirection, enabling files of essentially unlimited size.

**Schematic (textual diagram):**

```
+-----------------------------------+
|   File Metadata (type, perm, etc.)|
+-----------------------------------+
|   Direct Pointer 0  ----->  Data  |
|   Direct Pointer 1  ----->  Data  |
|   ...                              |
|   Direct Pointer D-1 ---->  Data  |
+-----------------------------------+
|   Single Indirect  --->  Index    |
|                         Block     |
|                         (entries) |
|                         | | | ... |
|                         v v v     |
|                        Data Data  |
+-----------------------------------+
|   Double Indirect  --->  Index    |
|                         Block     |
|                         (entries) |
|                         |         |
|                         v         |
|                       Index Block |
|                       (entries)   |
|                       | | |       |
|                       v v v       |
|                     Data Data     |
+-----------------------------------+
```

**Why inode-based design?**
- Enables **hard links** (multiple names for one inode).
- Speeds up **directory lookups** (small directory entries).
- Allows **independent metadata updates** without rewriting directory structure.
- Permits **fast sequential access** for small files via direct pointers.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Given:** $B = 1024$ bytes, $P = 2$ bytes, $N = 5$ direct pointers, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect.

**Step 1:** Compute pointers per block:

$$K = \frac{B}{P} = \frac{1024}{2} = 512 \text{ pointers/block}$$

**Step 2:** Direct contribution:

$$\text{Size}_{\text{direct}} = 5 \cdot 1024 = 5{,}120 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3:** Single indirect contribution:

$$\text{Size}_{\text{single}} = 512 \cdot 1024 = 524{,}288 \text{ bytes} = 512 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 4:** Double indirect contribution:

$$\text{Size}_{\text{double}} = 512^{2} \cdot 1024 = 262{,}144 \cdot 1024 = 268{,}435{,}456 \text{ bytes} = 256 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 5:** Maximum file size:

$$\text{MaxSize} = 5{,}120 + 524{,}288 + 268{,}435{,}456 = 268{,}964{,}864 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\text{MaxSize} \approx 256.51 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 6:** Maximum number of files:

Each inode corresponds to exactly **one file**. If the inode table contains 2048 inodes:

$$\text{MaxFiles} = 2048 \text{ files}$$

(Note: This is a hard upper bound set by the file system designer; actual maximum may be lower if some inodes are reserved for system files, e.g., 10% reserved in ext2/ext3/ext4.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing file name with inode storage** — Filenames are stored in *directory entries*, not in inodes. Many students incorrectly include filename in the inode field list. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Forgetting the contribution of direct pointers** — In max-file-size problems, students often compute only the indirect contributions. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Unit confusion** — Always state units explicitly (KB, MB, GB, TB) and show byte-level calculations. **Penalty: 0.5 mark** for missing units.
> 4. **Inode number vs. file descriptor confusion** — An inode number is the on-disk identifier; a file descriptor is a per-process in-memory handle. Mixing them up costs marks.
> 5. **Skipping the mount table explanation** — A common question in KTU is the role of the mount table. Always mention it in mount-related answers, even if not explicitly asked.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Mounting & Inode):**

- **Mounting** is the act of attaching a file system at a **mount point** in the root directory tree.
- The OS **must validate the superblock** (magic number check) before allowing a mount — this prevents corruption.
- The **mount table** is a kernel-resident data structure recording all active mounts; it is consulted during every pathname resolution.
- **Unmounting** requires flushing all dirty buffers; pulling a device without unmounting risks data loss.
- An **inode** is a fixed-size on-disk structure storing all file **metadata except the name**.
- The **inode number (i-number)** uniquely identifies a file within a file system.
- A **directory entry** contains only the filename and the inode number; it does not store file metadata.
- Inode metadata includes: **type, permissions, UID, GID, size, atime, mtime, ctime, link count, block pointers**.
- Inode pointer scheme: **Direct (D), Single-indirect, Double-indirect, Triple-indirect**.
- Maximum number of pointers per index block: $K = B / P$.
- Maximum file size formula: $\text{MaxSize} = B \cdot \left(N + K + K^{2} + K^{3}\right)$.
- For UNIX V7 parameters ($B = 4096$, $P = 4$, $N = 12$), maximum file size is approximately **4 TB**.
- **Hard links** are made possible by the inode — multiple directory entries can point to the same inode.
- Mounting is **privileged** — only the superuser can mount/unmount.
- The **VFS layer** abstracts file system details, allowing heterogeneous file systems to coexist in one tree.
- **Filename is NOT in the inode** — this is a frequently tested point in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Multi-Level Index; Case Study: VFS Objects and Their Data Structures - The Inode Object
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** The Multi-Level Index; Case Study: VFS Objects and Their Data Structures - The Inode Object ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Multi-Level Index (Indexed Allocation Scheme) **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** The **Multi-Level Index** is a hybrid file-allocation strategy that combines the best features of **contiguous allocation** (efficient sequential access) and **linked allocation** (no external fragmentation). The disk address of the $i^{th}$ block of a file is maintained as a *pointer* inside a dedicated on-disk data structure called the **I-node (Index Node)**. Each I-node contains a small array of direct block pointers followed by one or more levels of **indirect pointers** (single, double, and triple indirect blocks), forming a *tree of disk-block addresses* rooted at the I-node. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the Linux **ext2/ext3/ext4** family, every file is described by exactly **one I-node**, and that I-node stores **15 disk-block pointers** — **12 direct, 1 singly-indirect, 1 doubly-indirect, and 1 triply-indirect**. This is the canonical "multi-level index" model you must know for the Board exam. **Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Card Catalogue" Picture:** Imagine a huge library with millions of books. Instead of a librarian walking you to the shelf (contiguous) or following a trail of breadcrumbs from book to book (linked), you are handed a single **catalogue card (the I-node)**. That card has: 1. **Twelve hand-written shelf numbers** — you can walk directly to those books (the *direct pointers*). 2. **One reference to another small card** listing *more* shelf numbers (the *single-indirect* block). 3. **One reference to a card listing cards that list shelf numbers** (the *double-indirect* block). 4. **One reference three levels deep** (the *triple-indirect* block). This tree structure keeps the *catalogue itself* tiny (15 numbers) while still being able to address a *gigantic* collection of books spread across the entire library — **without any external fragmentation** and with **near-constant-time access** to small files. --- ### 1.2 The VFS Inode Object (Case Study: Linux Kernel) **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** The **Virtual File System (VFS)** is an abstraction layer inside the Linux kernel that presents a *uniform object-oriented interface* to user-space programs while hiding the on-disk differences of underlying concrete file systems (ext4, NTFS, FAT, NFS, procfs, etc.). Every VFS object is a `struct` in C whose members are **function pointers** (operations tables) supplied by the underlying file system. The most important of these objects is the **inode object** (`struct inode`), which represents a *single file* in memory and caches the on-disk I-node together with kernel-private metadata (reference counts, locks, dirty bits, backing-page trees, etc.). > [!NOTE] > **Why a "Case Study" in your syllabus?** The VFS is the *conceptual heart* of how Linux supports dozens of file systems inside a single kernel. The `struct inode` is the most heavily reused VFS object — every `open()`, `read()`, `write()`, `stat()`, `unlink()` ultimately manipulates an `struct inode`. Examiners love testing this because it connects *theoretical OS concepts* (files, directories, permissions) with *real kernel code*. **Conceptual Analogy — The "Aadhaar/ID Card for Every File" Picture:** Think of every file in Linux as a citizen. Each citizen carries an **Aadhaar card (= the in-memory `struct inode`)** that the government (= the kernel) uses to: - Look up the citizen's name, address, date-of-birth, photo (= file size, owner UID/GID, timestamps, permission bits). - Find the **permanent home address on paper** (= the on-disk I-node blocks). - Call the right *department* (= the concrete file-system driver — ext4, FAT, NFS) to perform actions. - Keep a **counter** for how many people are currently looking at this card (= the reference count `i_count`) and a **"dirty/clean" stamp** (= `i_state`) to know when the in-memory copy must be flushed back to disk. The Aadhaar is *cached* in RAM (the in-memory `struct inode`); the actual paper record lives in the district office's cupboard (the on-disk I-node). They are **synchronised** by the kernel when needed. --- ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Linux ext2/ext3/ext4) | Parameter | Standard Value | |---|---| | Block size (configurable at `mkfs` time) | **1 KiB, 2 KiB, or 4 KiB** | | Pointer size (32-bit file system) | **4 bytes** | | Pointers per block (32-bit) | **256 (1 KiB) / 512 (2 KiB) / 1024 (4 KiB)** | | Direct pointers in I-node | **12** | | Indirect pointer levels | **3** (single, double, triple) | | Total pointers in I-node | **15** | | I-node size (ext4 default) | **256 bytes** | | Inode number type (ext4) | **32-bit** (theoretically 128-bit) | | Inode reference count field | `i_count` (atomic_t) | | Inode state flags | `i_state` (I_DIRTY, I_LOCK, I_FREEING, …) | | Inode dirty bit | `I_DIRTY` flag in `i_state` | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of the ext2/ext3/ext4 I-node Pointer Block The 15 block-pointer entries of the on-disk I-node are split as follows: | Pointer Slot | Index in `i_block[15]` | Type | Purpose | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | 0–11 | `i_block[0]` … `i_block[11]` | **Direct** | Point directly to the first 12 data blocks of the file. | | 12 | `i_block[12]` | **Single Indirect** | Points to an *index block* whose entries are data-block pointers. | | 13 | `i_block[13]` | **Double Indirect** | Points to an *index block* whose entries point to *more* index blocks. | | 14 | `i_block[14]` | **Triple Indirect** | Points to an index block whose entries point to index blocks whose entries point to index blocks whose entries are data-block pointers. | **The "Why" Behind This Layout (KTU-board favourite question):** - **Direct pointers** make the *common case* (small files, the first 48 KB at 4 KB blocks) blazingly fast — *one I-node read, one disk seek, done*. Most files in a typical Linux system are smaller than this, so they never touch an indirect block. - **Single indirect** extends addressing to medium files cheaply. - **Double and triple indirect** provide the *huge* address space required for multi-gigabyte files while keeping the I-node itself a fixed 256-byte structure. **Operational Walkthrough — How a block is read:** 1. User-space issues `read(fd, buf, size)`. 2. Kernel VFS layer receives the call, looks up the `struct file` → its `f_path.dentry` → the `struct inode`. 3. The kernel computes the desired *logical* block number `n` (0-indexed from start of file). 4. The kernel classifies `n`: - If $0 \le n \le 11$ → read `i_block[n]`, fetch the data block. - If $12 \le n \le 12 + P - 1$ → fetch the single-indirect block, read pointer at offset `n - 12`, fetch the data block. - If $12 + P \le n \le 12 + P + P^2 - 1$ → fetch double-indirect block, then the appropriate *level-1* index block, then the data block. - If $n \ge 12 + P + P^2$ → descend three levels of indirection. where $P$ = number of pointers per block = $B / 4$ for a block of size $B$ bytes and 4-byte pointers. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are derived from first principles and are valid for the standard Linux ext2/ext3/ext4 family with 4-byte block pointers. **No vertical bar `|` is used in any cell — we use `\mid` for absolute value / divisibility to keep markdown tables safe.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Typical value (B = 4 KiB, P = 1024) | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | Pointers per index block | $P \;=\; B \div 4$ | **1024** | | 2 | Data blocks via direct pointers | $D_{1} \;=\; 12$ | **12** | | 3 | Data blocks via single indirect | $D_{2} \;=\; P$ | **1024** | | 4 | Data blocks via double indirect | $D_{3} \;=\; P^{2}$ | **1 048 576** | | 5 | Data blocks via triple indirect | $D_{4} \;=\; P^{3}$ | **1 073 741 824** | | 6 | Total data blocks addressable | $D_{tot} \;=\; 12 + P + P^{2} + P^{3}$ | $\approx 1.07 \times 10^{9}$ | | 7 | Maximum file size (bytes) | $S_{max} \;=\; B \cdot D_{tot}$ | $\approx 4\ \text{TiB}$ | | 8 | Number of disk reads for byte at logical block $n$ (best case) | $1$ (for $n \le 11$) | 1 | | 9 | Number of disk reads for byte at logical block $n$ (single indirect) | $2$ | 2 | | 10 | Number of disk reads for byte at logical block $n$ (double indirect) | $3$ | 3 | | 11 | Number of disk reads for byte at logical block $n$ (triple indirect) | $4$ | 4 | | 12 | Logical block number $n$ to indirect-level classifier (general) | $n \in [12 + \sum_{k=1}^{r-1} P^{k},\ \ 12 + \sum_{k=1}^{r} P^{k} - 1] \Rightarrow \text{level } r$ | — | | 13 | Offset inside an indirect block at level $r$ | $\bigl(n - 12 - \sum_{k=1}^{r-1} P^{k}\bigr) \div P^{r-1} \pmod{P}$ | — | | 14 | `struct inode` size (ext4) | 256 bytes | **256** | | 15 | VFS inode-cache memory | unlimited (RAM-bound) | — | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility * **Databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB):** Engineers tune block size to maximise direct-pointer hits, eliminating index-block reads for hot data. * **Embedded systems & SSDs:** A *small* block size (1 KiB) is preferred to reduce wasted space in tiny files; a *large* block size (4 KiB) is preferred for streaming video to maximise throughput. * **Cloud & NAS storage:** The triple-indirect block is what allows ext4 to address single files up to **16 TiB (theoretically)**, sufficient for archival videos, scientific datasets, and VM disk images. * **Forensics / Recovery:** Tools like `debugfs`, `e2fsck`, and `extundelete` walk the indirect-block tree in exactly the order described above to rebuild deleted files. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Maximum File Size for a Given Block Size We derive the maximum logical file size $S_{max}$ supported by an I-node with 12 direct pointers and three levels of indirection, for an arbitrary block size $B$ (bytes) and 4-byte disk-block pointers. **Step 1 — Establish the number of pointers per index block.** Each index block is itself a data block of size $B$ bytes, and each entry is a 4-byte pointer. Therefore the number of pointers that one index block can hold is $$ P \;=\; \frac{B}{4} $$ **Step 2 — Count data blocks reachable at each indirection level.** - *Level 0 (direct pointers):* The I-node directly holds 12 pointers, each naming one data block. $$ D_{1} \;=\; 12 $$ - *Level 1 (single indirect):* One pointer in the I-node names an index block. That index block contains $P$ pointers, each naming a data block. $$ D_{2} \;=\; P \;\cdot\; 1 \;=\; P $$ - *Level 2 (double indirect):* One pointer names an index-of-index block, which contains $P$ pointers to single-indirect blocks, each of which contains $P$ data-block pointers. $$ D_{3} \;=\; P \;\cdot\; P \;\cdot\; 1 \;=\; P^{2} $$ - *Level 3 (triple indirect):* Three nested levels of index blocks precede the data block. $$ D_{4} \;=\; P \;\cdot\; P \;\cdot\; P \;\cdot\; 1 \;=\; P^{3} $$ **Step 3 — Sum the data blocks over all four levels.** $$ D_{tot} \;=\; 12 + P + P^{2} + P^{3} $$ **Step 4 — Convert block count to byte count (file size).** Each data block contributes $B$ bytes to the file, so $$ S_{max} \;=\; B \;\cdot\; D_{tot} \;=\; B \cdot \bigl(12 + P + P^{2} + P^{3}\bigr) $$ Substituting $P = B/4$ explicitly, $$ S_{max} \;=\; B \cdot \biggl(12 + \frac{B}{4} + \frac{B^{2}}{16} + \frac{B^{3}}{64}\biggr) $$ **Step 5 — Specialise to $B = 4\ \text{KiB} = 4096\ \text{bytes}$.** Compute $P$ first: $$ P \;=\; \frac{4096}{4} \;=\; 1024 $$ Now compute each term: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{1} &= 12 \\[2pt] D_{2} &= 1024 \\[2pt] D_{3} &= 1024^{2} \;=\; 1\,048\,576 \\[2pt] D_{4} &= 1024^{3} \;=\; 1\,073\,741\,824 \end{aligned} $$ Summing: $$ D_{tot} \;=\; 12 + 1024 + 1\,048\,576 + 1\,073\,741\,824 \;=\; 1\,074\,791\,436 $$ Multiplying by $B = 4096$: $$ S_{max} \;=\; 1\,074\,791\,436 \;\times\; 4096 $$ Compute the product (break into convenient parts): $$ \begin{aligned} S_{max} &= 1\,074\,791\,436 \times 4096 \\ &= 1\,074\,791\,436 \times (4 \times 1024) \\ &= 4 \times 1\,074\,791\,436 \times 1024 \\ &= 4\,299\,165\,744 \times 1024 \end{aligned} $$ Convert to TiB by dividing by $2^{40} = 1\,099\,511\,627\,776$: $$ S_{max} \;=\; \frac{4\,299\,165\,744 \times 1024}{1\,099\,511\,627\,776} \;\approx\; 4.00\ \text{TiB} $$ **Result:** With 4 KiB blocks, the maximum theoretical file size is approximately **4 TiB** (the exact value is $S_{max} = 4\,402\,343\,853\,056\ \text{bytes} \approx 4.004\ \text{TiB}$). **Step 6 — Specialise to $B = 1\ \text{KiB} = 1024\ \text{bytes}$.** $$ P = \frac{1024}{4} = 256 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} D_{1} &= 12 \\ D_{2} &= 256 \\ D_{3} &= 256^{2} \;=\; 65\,536 \\ D_{4} &= 256^{3} \;=\; 16\,777\,216 \end{aligned} $$ $$ D_{tot} = 12 + 256 + 65\,536 + 16\,777\,216 = 16\,843\,020 $$ $$ S_{max} = 16\,843\,020 \times 1024 = 17\,247\,252\,480\ \text{bytes} \approx 16.06\ \text{GiB} $$ **Step 7 — Specialise to $B = 2\ \text{KiB} = 2048\ \text{bytes}$.** $$ P = \frac{2048}{4} = 512 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} D_{1} &= 12 \\ D_{2} &= 512 \\ D_{3} &= 512^{2} \;=\; 262\,144 \\ D_{4} &= 512^{3} \;=\; 134\,217\,728 \end{aligned} $$ $$ D_{tot} = 12 + 512 + 262\,144 + 134\,217\,728 = 134\,480\,396 $$ $$ S_{max} = 134\,480\,396 \times 2048 = 275\,415\,851\,008\ \text{bytes} \approx 256.4\ \text{GiB} $$ --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Disk Reads to Access Logical Block $n$ The following matrix gives the **number of disk reads** required to fetch a single data block, and the **formula for the index-block address offsets** at each indirection level. Use this when a KTU question asks: *"To read block number 5000 of a file, how many disk accesses are needed?"* Let $P$ = pointers per block, $D_1 = 12$, $D_2 = P$, $D_3 = P^2$, $D_4 = P^3$. Cumulative offsets: $$ \begin{aligned} C_0 &= 0 \\ C_1 &= 12 \\ C_2 &= 12 + P \\ C_3 &= 12 + P + P^{2} \\ C_4 &= 12 + P + P^{2} + P^{3} \end{aligned} $$ | Logical block $n$ falls in | Indirection level $r$ | # of disk reads | Reason | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---| | $0 \le n \le C_1 - 1$ | Direct | **1** | Pointer is in the I-node itself (already in memory). | | $C_1 \le n \le C_2 - 1$ | Single | **2** | I-node → 1 index block → data block. | | $C_2 \le n \le C_3 - 1$ | Double | **3** | I-node → index → index → data. | | $C_3 \le n \le C_4 - 1$ | Triple | **4** | I-node → index → index → index → data. | **Worked Example 1 — Block number 100, B = 4 KiB (P = 1024).** - $C_1 = 12$, $C_2 = 12 + 1024 = 1036$, $C_3 = 12 + 1024 + 1024^2 = 1\,049\,612$. - $n = 100$ falls in $[12,\ 1036 - 1]$, i.e., single-indirect region. - **Answer:** **2 disk reads**. **Worked Example 2 — Block number 50 000, B = 4 KiB (P = 1024).** - $n = 50\,000$ falls in $[1036,\ 1\,049\,612 - 1]$, i.e., double-indirect region. - **Answer:** **3 disk reads**. **Worked Example 3 — Block number 2 000 000, B = 4 KiB (P = 1024).** - $n = 2\,000\,000$ falls in $[1\,049\,612,\ 1\,074\,791\,435]$, i.e., triple-indirect region. - **Answer:** **4 disk reads**. --- ### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Locating the Disk Block for Logical Block $n$ Given a logical block number $n$, we need to compute: 1. The indirection level $r$. 2. The offsets at every level of the indirect tree. **General formula for level $r$ (with $r \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$):** The *residual* block index inside level $r$ is $$ m \;=\; n \;-\; C_{r-1} $$ where $C_{r-1} = 12 + \sum_{k=1}^{r-2} P^{k}$ (the number of blocks addressed by all lower levels combined). For $r = 1$, $C_0 = 0$. The block is found at offset $$ o_r \;=\; m \div P^{r-1} \pmod{P} $$ and the *intra-data-block* offset is zero (we are locating whole blocks). For $r=1$ (single indirect) the formula collapses to $o_1 = m$. **Worked Example 4 — Compute the indirect tree walk for $n = 5000$ at $B = 4$ KiB.** - $C_1 = 12,\ C_2 = 1036$. Since $1036 \le 5000 < C_3 = 1\,049\,612$, $r = 2$ (double indirect). - $m = 5000 - 1036 = 3964$. - Offset in the *level-1* index-of-index block: $$ o_2 \;=\; \bigl\lfloor m / P \bigr\rfloor \;=\; \bigl\lfloor 3964 / 1024 \bigr\rfloor \;=\; 3 $$ - Offset inside the *level-2* single-indirect block: $$ o_1 \;=\; m \bmod P \;=\; 3964 \bmod 1024 \;=\; 3964 - 3 \times 1024 \;=\; 3964 - 3072 \;=\; 892 $$ - **Walk:** Read the I-node (cached) → follow `i_block[13]` to the *double-indirect index block* → read entry `o_2 = 3` to get a *single-indirect index block* → read entry `o_1 = 892` to get the actual data block. **Disk reads:** 1 (I-node, cached) + 1 (double-indirect index) + 1 (single-indirect index) + 1 (data block) = **3** (the I-node is usually already in the inode cache, so effective = 3 fresh reads). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Multi-Level Index (ext2/ext3/ext4 I-node) ```mermaid flowchart TB inode["I-node (256 bytes)"] p0["i_block[0] Direct"] p1["i_block[1] Direct"] p2["i_block[2] Direct"] p3["i_block[3] Direct"] p4["i_block[4] Direct"] p5["i_block[5] Direct"] p6["i_block[6] Direct"] p7["i_block[7] Direct"] p8["i_block[8] Direct"] p9["i_block[9] Direct"] p10["i_block[10] Direct"] p11["i_block[11] Direct"] p12["i_block[12] Single Indirect Pointer"] p13["i_block[13] Double Indirect Pointer"] p14["i_block[14] Triple Indirect Pointer"] idx1["Single-Indirect Index Block
P = 1024 pointers (4 KiB)"] idx2a["Double-Indirect L1 Index Block
P pointers"] idx2b["Single-Indirect Index Block
P pointers"] idx3a["Triple-Indirect L1 Index Block"] idx3b["Triple-Indirect L2 Index Block"] idx3c["Single-Indirect Index Block"] d0["Data Block 0"] d1["Data Block 1"] d11["Data Block 11"] d12["Data Block 12 (via single)"] d1023["Data Block 1035 (via single)"] d1036["Data Block 1036 (via double)"] d5000["Data Block 5000 (via double)"] dLast["Data Block ~1.07e9 (via triple)"] inode --> p0 --> d0 inode --> p1 --> d1 inode --> p11 --> d11 inode --> p12 --> idx1 idx1 --> d12 idx1 --> d1023 inode --> p13 --> idx2a idx2a --> idx2b idx2b --> d1036 idx2b --> d5000 inode --> p14 --> idx3a idx3a --> idx3b idx3b --> idx3c idx3c --> dLast ``` > **Reading guide:** Solid arrows from any pointer cell to a *Data Block* indicate a *single disk seek*. Following an *Index Block* requires an *additional* disk read before reaching the data block. --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — VFS `struct inode` and Its Embedding in the Linux Kernel ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph USpace["User Space"] app["Application Program
(open / read / write / stat)"] end subgraph VFS["VFS Layer (kernel generic)"] sfile["struct file
f_pos, f_mode, f_op"] sdent["struct dentry
d_name, d_inode"] sinode["struct inode
i_mode, i_size, i_uid,
i_nlink, i_count, i_state,
i_sb, i_op, i_fop, i_ino"] super["struct super_block
s_magic, s_op, s_root,
s_inodes, s_files"] end subgraph ConcreteFS["Concrete File-System Driver"] ext4ops["ext4_inode_operations"] ext4fops["ext4_file_operations"] fopsTable["inode->i_op->lookup, create, unlink
file->f_op->read, write, mmap"] end subgraph Disk["On-Disk Storage"] dinode["On-disk inode (256 B)
i_block[15] pointers
i_mode, i_size, i_atime, …"] dataBlocks["Data Blocks
(file contents)"] end app -- "syscall" --> sfile sfile -- "f_path.dentry" --> sdent sdent -- "d_inode" --> sinode sinode -- "i_sb" --> super super -- "s_op" --> ext4ops sinode -- "i_op" --> ext4ops sfile -- "f_op" --> ext4fops ext4ops --> fopsTable sinode -- "flush via write_inode" --> dinode dinode -- "i_block[12..14]" --> dataBlocks ``` > **Key idea:** The in-memory `struct inode` is a *cache* of the on-disk inode, *augmented* with kernel-private fields (`i_count`, `i_state`, the address-space page tree, etc.). When the kernel needs to read a file, it dereferences `inode->i_op->lookup` or `file->f_op->read` to call the *concrete* ext4/FAT/NFS routine — the rest of the kernel does not know or care which file system is underneath. --- ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How a `read()` Travels Through VFS ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Step 1: User open / read syscall"] --> B["Step 2: sys_open / sys_read
allocates struct file"] B --> C["Step 3: VFS resolves
pathname → dentry → inode"] C --> D["Step 4: inode cached?
YES → reuse
NO → read from disk"] D --> E["Step 5: call file->f_op->read
(concrete ext4_file_operations.read)"] E --> F["Step 6: walk i_block[15] tree
to locate target data block"] F --> G["Step 7: copy data to user buffer
via copy_to_user"] G --> H["Step 8: update i_atime, i_count
mark dirty if modified"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]** *With a neat diagram, explain the multi-level index scheme of file allocation used in the Unix/Linux file system.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The multi-level index scheme is a form of *indexed allocation* where every file is described by an **I-node (index node)** containing a fixed array of disk-block pointers. - The I-node holds **12 direct pointers** `i_block[0] … i_block[11]` that address the first 12 data blocks of the file. - Pointer `i_block[12]` is a **single-indirect pointer** — it points to an index block that itself holds up to $P = B/4$ pointers to data blocks. - Pointer `i_block[13]` is a **double-indirect pointer** — it points to an index block whose entries point to *more* index blocks, which in turn point to data blocks. This addresses $P^2$ data blocks. - Pointer `i_block[14]` is a **triple-indirect pointer**, addressing $P^3$ data blocks. The advantages are (i) **no external fragmentation** (data blocks can be scattered), (ii) **fast access for small files** (most files use only the direct pointers), and (iii) **support for very large files** (up to $\approx 4$ TiB with 4 KiB blocks). The disadvantage is the extra disk reads needed to traverse multiple index-block levels for large files. *Diagram: see Section 4.1 above (Mermaid block diagram).* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]** *List any six important fields of the VFS `struct inode` and state their purpose.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Field | Purpose | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | `i_ino` | The inode number (unique within a file system); the on-disk identity of the file. | | 2 | `i_mode` | File type and permission bits (regular file, directory, rwx for owner/group/other). | | 3 | `i_uid` / `i_gid` | Owner user-ID and group-ID, used for access control. | | 4 | `i_size` | Logical size of the file in bytes. | | 5 | `i_nlink` | Hard-link count — when this drops to 0 and `i_count` is 0, the inode is freed. | | 6 | `i_count` | In-memory reference count; how many kernel entities currently hold a pointer to this inode. | | 7 | `i_state` | State flags such as `I_DIRTY`, `I_LOCK`, `I_FREEING`, `I_CLEAR`. | | 8 | `i_sb` | Pointer to the super-block of the file system this inode belongs to. | | 9 | `i_op` | Pointer to `struct inode_operations` (lookup, create, unlink, mkdir, …). | | 10 | `i_fop` | Pointer to `struct file_operations` for the *default* file ops. | (Any six well-explained fields fetch full 3 marks.) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Part B questions in the University ESE carry **14 marks** and are split into two sub-parts — usually **7 + 7** marks each. The student is offered an **internal choice** between two completely different questions. We model both choices below. --- #### **Question A — Multi-Level Index & Maximum File Size** [14 marks] **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, ESE May 2024]** **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **multi-level index** allocation scheme used in the Unix/Linux file system. Explain how the **i-node** is used to locate a data block when the *logical block number* of the desired byte is large. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** Consider an ext2 file system with a **block size of 4 KB** and **4-byte disk-block pointers**.     (i) Compute the **maximum file size** that can be supported by a single I-node.     (ii) How many **disk reads** are required to read logical block number **5000** of a file? Justify your answer.     (iii) How many **disk reads** are required to read logical block number **2 000 000** of a file? Justify your answer. **\[7 marks\]** --- **Model Solution to Q-A (a) [7 marks]:** *See the discussion in Section 2.1 and the diagram in Section 4.1.* - The I-node is a 256-byte on-disk structure containing 15 block pointers: **12 direct, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, 1 triple-indirect**. **[1 mark]** - The first 12 data blocks are read using the 12 direct pointers — only the I-node is needed (already in the inode cache). **[1 mark]** - For logical block number $n \ge 12$ the kernel classifies $n$: * $12 \le n \le 12 + P - 1$: read `i_block[12]`, then offset $(n - 12)$ in the single-indirect index block. **[1 mark]** * $12 + P \le n \le 12 + P + P^{2} - 1$: read `i_block[13]`, then a two-level walk — the offset inside the outer index is $\lfloor (n - 12 - P) / P \rfloor$ and the offset inside the inner index is $(n - 12 - P) \bmod P$. **[1 mark]** * For $n \ge 12 + P + P^{2}$: read `i_block[14]` and walk three levels of indirection similarly. **[1 mark]** - For example, to read logical block $n$ in the *double-indirect* region, the algorithm is: 1. Read I-node (cached) → fetch `i_block[13]` → block $A$. 2. Read block $A$, take entry $\lfloor (n - 12 - P) / P \rfloor$ → block $B$. 3.
