Read block $B$, take entry $(n - 12 - P) \bmod P$ → that is the desired data block. **[1 mark]** - This is why access to small files (block $n < 12$) is **one disk access** (just the data block — I-node is cached), while access to very large files may require **up to 4 disk accesses**. **[1 mark]** - Neat diagram awarding: full marks if the I-node + 12 direct + 3 indirect pointers + at least one expanded indirect level are clearly shown. **[1 mark]** --- **Model Solution to Q-A (b) [7 marks]:** *Given:* $B = 4096$ bytes, pointer size = 4 bytes $\Rightarrow P = B/4 = 1024$. **(i) Maximum file size — 3 marks** From the formula derived in Section 3.1: $$ S_{max} = B \cdot \bigl(12 + P + P^{2} + P^{3}\bigr) $$ Substituting $B = 4096,\ P = 1024$: $$ \begin{aligned} 12 + 1024 + 1024^{2} + 1024^{3} &= 12 + 1024 + 1\,048\,576 + 1\,073\,741\,824 \\ &= 1\,074\,791\,436\ \text{blocks} \end{aligned} $$ $$ S_{max} = 1\,074\,791\,436 \times 4096 \approx 4.40 \times 10^{12}\ \text{bytes} \approx \mathbf{4\ TiB} $$ **(ii) Disk reads for $n = 5000$ — 2 marks** Compute cumulative thresholds: - $C_1 = 12$ - $C_2 = 12 + 1024 = 1036$ - $C_3 = 12 + 1024 + 1024^{2} = 1\,049\,612$ - $C_4 = 12 + 1024 + 1024^{2} + 1024^{3} = 1\,074\,791\,436$ Since $C_2 = 1036 \le 5000 < C_3 = 1\,049\,612$, block 5000 lies in the **double-indirect** region. Therefore the kernel must perform **3 disk reads** to fetch the data block: 1. Read the double-indirect index block (named by `i_block[13]`). 2. Read the appropriate single-indirect index block (pointed to by entry $\lfloor (5000 - 1036)/1024 \rfloor = 3$ in the double-indirect block). 3. Read the data block itself (pointed to by entry $(5000 - 1036) \bmod 1024 = 892$ in that single-indirect block). **Answer: 3 disk reads.** (The I-node itself is already cached in memory, so we do not count it.) **(iii) Disk reads for $n = 2\,000\,000$ — 2 marks** Since $2\,000\,000 \ge C_3 = 1\,049\,612$ and $2\,000\,000 < C_4 = 1\,074\,791\,436$, block $2\,000\,000$ lies in the **triple-indirect** region. The kernel must perform **4 disk reads** to fetch the data block: 1. Read the triple-indirect level-1 index block (named by `i_block[14]`). 2. Read the appropriate triple-indirect level-2 index block. 3. Read the appropriate single-indirect index block. 4. Read the data block itself. **Answer: 4 disk reads.** --- #### **Question B — VFS Inode Object Case Study** [14 marks] **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, ESE Dec 2023]** **(a)** What is the **Virtual File System (VFS)** in Linux? Explain the role of the **`struct inode`** object in VFS. List **any seven important fields** of `struct inode` and state their purpose. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** Differentiate between the **in-memory `struct inode`** and the **on-disk inode**. How does the kernel keep them synchronised? What happens when `i_count` and `i_nlink` both reach **zero**? **\[7 marks\]** --- **Model Solution to Q-B (a) [7 marks]:** **Definition of VFS — 2 marks** The **Virtual File System (VFS)** is an *abstraction layer* inside the Linux kernel that exposes a **single, uniform, object-oriented API** to user-space programs (`open`, `read`, `write`, `stat`, …) while delegating the actual on-disk operations to one of several concrete file-system drivers (ext4, FAT, NFS, procfs, sysfs, …). VFS is implemented as a set of C `struct`s whose members are **function pointers** (operations tables) supplied by the underlying file system. This is what allows Linux to *transparently* mount and use many file systems at the same time. **Role of `struct inode` — 2 marks** The **`struct inode`** is the most important VFS object. It represents **a single file** as the kernel sees it in memory, and is the *in-RAM cache* of the on-disk inode. Every VFS operation on a file — `open`, `read`, `write`, `chmod`, `unlink`, `stat` — ultimately dereferences the `struct inode` (directly or via `struct file` → `struct dentry` → `struct inode`). The inode caches the file's *metadata* (type, permissions, owner, size, timestamps, link count) and links to *operations tables* (`i_op`, `i_fop`) supplied by the concrete file system, so that the VFS layer can call the correct driver routine. **Seven important fields of `struct inode` — 3 marks** | # | Field | Purpose | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | `i_ino` | Inode number — unique within a file system, the on-disk identity of the file. | | 2 | `i_mode` | File type (regular, dir, symlink, …) and permission bits (rwx for owner/group/other). | | 3 | `i_uid` / `i_gid` | Owner user-ID and group-ID, used by the permission-check logic. | | 4 | `i_size` | Logical file size in bytes. | | 5 | `i_nlink` | Hard-link count. The file is logically deleted when this drops to 0. | | 6 | `i_count` | In-memory reference count. Incremented on every `iget`/`d_instantiate`, decremented on every `iput`. | | 7 | `i_state` | Bitmask of state flags: `I_DIRTY`, `I_LOCK`, `I_FREEING`, `I_CLEAR`, `I_NEW`, etc. | | 8 | `i_sb` | Pointer to the `struct super_block` of the file system this inode belongs to. | | 9 | `i_op` | Pointer to `struct inode_operations` (lookup, create, unlink, mkdir, rename, …). | | 10 | `i_atime` / `i_mtime` / `i_ctime` | Last access / modification / metadata-change timestamps. | | 11 | `i_blocks` | Number of 512-byte sectors actually allocated to the file. | | 12 | `i_mapping` | Address-space object holding the page cache for the file's data. | (Any seven well-explained fields → 3 marks.) --- **Model Solution to Q-B (b) [7 marks]:** **Difference between in-memory `struct inode` and on-disk inode — 3 marks** | Aspect | In-Memory `struct inode` | On-Disk Inode | |:---|:---|:---| | Location | RAM (in the VFS inode cache / slab allocator). | A 256-byte record in the file system's *inode table* on disk. | | Lifetime | From `iget()` until `iput()` drops the refcount to 0 and the cache reclaims it. | As long as the file exists on disk. | | Fields | All on-disk fields **plus** kernel-private fields: `i_count`, `i_state`, `i_lock`, `i_mapping`, `i_io_list`, `i_wb_list`, dirty-page tracking, etc. | Only the persistent fields: `i_mode`, `i_uid`, `i_size`, `i_atime`, `i_mtime`, `i_ctime`, `i_nlink`, `i_block[15]`, etc. | | Purpose | Cache for fast access; supplies function-pointer tables for the VFS. | Source of truth that survives a reboot / power-cycle. | | Locking | Protected by `inode->i_lock` (per-inode spinlock) and the `inode_lock` global rw-semaphore. | No locking — physical storage. | | Size | Variable (large; many pointers and lists). | Fixed (256 B in ext4). | | Reference count | Maintained explicitly (`i_count`). | None — `i_nlink` (hard-link count) is the closest analogue. | **Synchronisation — 2 marks** The kernel keeps the two synchronised by: 1. **Reading:** When a pathname is resolved and the corresponding `struct inode` is *not* in the cache, the kernel calls the file-system's `read_inode` (or `read_inode` superblock op) which reads the on-disk record into the `struct inode` fields. 2. **Writing:** Whenever a `struct inode` is marked dirty (`I_DIRTY` set in `i_state`) — typically by `setattr`, `write`, `truncate`, `chmod`, etc. — the kernel writes it back via the super-block operation `write_inode`. This is triggered (a) synchronously by `sync/fsync`/`fdatasync`, (b) lazily by `sync_supers` every 5 seconds, and (c) by `umount`. **What happens when `i_count` and `i_nlink` both reach 0 — 2 marks** * `i_nlink = 0` ⇒ the file is **logically unlinked**: the directory entry is removed, no new name can reach this file, and the file system may *deallocate* its data blocks the next time the inode is flushed. (In some file systems, e.g., ext4 with `orphan_list`/journal, the actual block deallocation is deferred until the last reference is closed.) * `i_count = 0` ⇒ the in-memory `struct inode` is no longer referenced by any kernel data structure (`struct file`, `struct dentry`, …). The kernel calls the superblock's `drop_inode` (or `delete_inode`) operation, which invokes the file-system's `clear_inode`/`delete_inode` routine to release the inode's resources (page cache pages, etc.) and put the `struct inode` back into the slab free list. * When **both** are zero, the file is **completely gone** — the on-disk inode is free to be reused, the data blocks are released, and the in-memory cache slot is returned to the slab allocator. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students typically lose marks on this topic:** > > 1. **Forgetting the block-size dependence in maximum-file-size questions.** Writing "max file size = 16 GB" without stating the *assumed block size* (1 KiB) loses 1 mark immediately. Always quote $B$ first, then compute $P$, then sum. > 2. **Confusing `i_count` with `i_nlink`.** `i_count` is an *in-memory* reference count (kernel pointers); `i_nlink` is the on-disk hard-link count. Examiners *will* deduct marks for mixing them up. > 3. **Counting the I-node read in "disk reads".** When asked "how many disk reads to fetch block $n$?", the I-node is **already in the inode cache** — do not count it. Only count *fresh* index-block + data-block reads. > 4. **Writing the inode-pointer layout as "0..11 direct, 12 single, 13 double, 14 triple" is correct, but reversing the numbering or saying "13 direct + 2 indirect" is a common slip — read the question carefully.** > 5. **VFS questions:** Forgetting that `i_op` (inode operations) and `f_op` (file operations) are *function-pointer tables* (operations vtables) is a half-mark deduction. VFS is essentially *object-oriented C*. > 6. **Not drawing a diagram in the multi-level-index answer.** Even a 30-second sketch of the 15-pointer I-node earns an easy 1 mark and makes the rest of the answer easier to follow. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Multi-level index = indexed allocation with 12 direct + 1 single + 1 double + 1 triple pointer in a single I-node.** ✅ - **Block pointer size = 4 bytes; pointers per block $P = B/4$.** ✅ - **Maximum data blocks = $12 + P + P^{2} + P^{3}$.** ✅ - **Maximum file size $S_{max} = B \cdot (12 + P + P^{2} + P^{3})$.** ✅ - $B = 1$ KiB → $S_{max} \approx 16$ GiB. - $B = 2$ KiB → $S_{max} \approx 256$ GiB. - $B = 4$ KiB → $S_{max} \approx 4$ TiB. - **Logical-block-to-level classifier:** - $0 \le n \le 11$ → direct → 1 disk read. - $12 \le n \le 12 + P - 1$ → single → 2 disk reads. - $12 + P \le n \le 12 + P + P^{2} - 1$ → double → 3 disk reads. - $12 + P + P^{2} \le n$ → triple → 4 disk reads. - **Indirect-tree offset formula:** $m = n - C_{r-1}$; offset at level $r$ is $\lfloor m / P^{r-1} \rfloor \bmod P$. ✅ - **VFS = uniform API over heterogeneous file systems using `struct`s with function pointers.** ✅ - **`struct inode` = in-memory cache of on-disk inode + kernel-private fields.** ✅ - **Critical `struct inode` fields:** `i_ino`, `i_mode`, `i_uid`/`i_gid`, `i_size`, `i_nlink`, `i_count`, `i_state`, `i_sb`, `i_op`, `i_fop`, `i_mapping`, timestamps. ✅ - **`i_count` ≠ `i_nlink`.** `i_count` = in-RAM refs; `i_nlink` = hard links on disk. ✅ - **Sync mechanism:** `read_inode` (cache miss) and `write_inode` (dirty flush via `fsync`/`sync`/5-second `sync_supers` tick/`umount`). ✅ - **When both counts = 0:** file is *completely* gone; data blocks freed; `struct inode` returned to slab allocator. ✅ - **VFS objects besides inode:** `struct file` (open file description), `struct dentry` (directory entry), `struct super_block` (mounted file system). ✅ - **No external fragmentation, small files are fast (direct pointers), huge files are possible (triple indirect) — this is *the* reason Linux ext4 uses this layout.** ✅ - **Exam tip:** Always state $B$, compute $P$, then show the level classifier for any "how many disk reads?" question. ✅

topics.title: Inode Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST403 - OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Inode Operations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition An **Inode (Index Node)** is a fundamental on-disk data structure used by Unix-like file systems (e.g., **ext2**, **ext3**, **ext4**, **UFS**) to store all metadata about a file **except its name and the actual data contents**. Every file and directory in the system is represented by exactly one inode, identified by a unique **inode number (i-number)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** An *inode* is a control structure that contains the key information needed by the OS for any particular file. It acts as the bridge between the *human-readable filename* in a directory entry and the *physical disk blocks* where file data actually resides. ### 1.2 Anatomy of an Inode (Classic Unix V7 / ext-style) | Metadata Field | Purpose | |---|---| | Mode | File type + 9 permission bits (rwx for owner/group/others) | | Owner ID (UID) | User identifier of the file owner | | Group ID (GID) | Group identifier of the file owner | | File Size | Logical size of the file in bytes | | Timestamps | atime, mtime, ctime (access, modification, inode-change) | | Link Count | Number of hard links pointing to this inode | | Block Pointers | 10 direct + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect pointers | | Indirect Block Count | Number of blocks used for indirect addressing | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Library Catalog Analogy** 📚 > Imagine a massive library. A *book* is a file, and a *library catalog card* is the inode. > - The **card** lists the book's title, author, shelf location, and unique catalog number — but it does **not** contain the book itself. > - The **bookshelf position** (block pointers) tells the librarian where the actual pages (data blocks) are stored. > - You can have **multiple catalog cards** (directory entries / hard links) pointing to the same book without creating a duplicate. > - Destroy the card and the book becomes "unfindable" on the shelves, even if the pages technically still exist. This is exactly how an inode works: the **filename** lives in the directory, but the **inode** is what the kernel actually manipulates. The kernel never "sees" a filename during read/write — it only sees an **inode number**. ## 1.4 Why Inodes Exist — The Design "Why" - **Decoupling names from data:** A single file can have multiple names (hard links) without duplicating data. - **Efficient metadata access:** Inodes are stored in a fixed, predictable location (the inode table), allowing the kernel to find file attributes via a simple `inode_number → index` calculation. - **Locality:** Since metadata is separate from data, accessing the inode doesn't necessarily require reading the file's data blocks. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & Operations Breakdown ## 2.1 The Core Inode Operations The kernel exposes file manipulation through **system calls**, but internally these always end up calling inode-level routines from the Virtual File System (VFS) layer. ### 2.1.1 File Creation — `create()` 1. Allocate a **free inode** from the filesystem's inode bitmap. 2. Initialize the inode (mode bits, owner, timestamps, link count = 1, block pointers = 0). 3. Allocate a **directory entry** in the parent directory, binding the human-readable name to the new i-number. 4. Write the updated directory block back to disk. 5. Return the new file descriptor to the user process. ### 2.1.2 File Deletion — `unlink()` 1. Locate the directory entry in the parent directory. 2. **Decrement the link count** of the target inode. 3. If `link_count == 0` → free all data blocks (using the block-pointer map) → free the inode (mark bit in inode bitmap as 0). 4. Remove the directory entry from its parent directory. 5. If the file is currently open by any process, the **actual data blocks are NOT freed** until the last file descriptor is closed (this is the famous "unlink-while-open" Unix semantics). > [!WARNING] > **Open File Reference:** Each open file also has an *in-memory* inode copy in the kernel's open-file table. Disk inode ↔ in-memory inode synchronization is handled via the `i_count` (in-memory reference count) and `i_nlink` (on-disk link count). ### 2.1.3 Writing to a File — `write()` 1. Locate the inode via the file descriptor's open-file entry. 2. Compute the **target byte offset** within the file. 3. Compute which **logical block number** holds that offset: $\text{block\_num} = \lfloor \text{offset} / B \rfloor$, where $B$ is the block size. 4. Compute the **byte offset within the block**: $\text{block\_offset} = \text{offset} \bmod B$. 5. If the block is not yet allocated → allocate a new block from the data bitmap → update the inode's block pointer. 6. Copy bytes from the user buffer to the kernel buffer (and eventually to disk via the page cache / buffer cache). ### 2.1.4 Reading from a File — `read()` 1. Locate the inode via the open file table. 2. Compute the logical block number using the same formula as write. 3. Determine *which* inode block pointer holds this block: - If `block_num < 10` → **direct pointer**. - If `10 ≤ block_num < 10 + N` → **single-indirect pointer**. - If `10+N ≤ block_num < 10+N+N²` → **double-indirect pointer**. - Else → **triple-indirect pointer**. 4. Read the block into the buffer cache, then copy the requested bytes to the user buffer. ### 2.1.5 Directory Operations - **Creating a directory (`mkdir`)**: Same as `create()` but the inode is flagged as a directory, and two special entries `.` and `..` are inserted. - **Removing a directory (`rmdir`)**: Only succeeds if the directory is empty (link count of `.` equals 2) and no process has it open. ### 2.1.6 Symbolic Links — `symlink()` - Creates a **new file of type `LNK`** whose data blocks contain the **pathname** of the target file. - Does **not** increment the target's link count. - Can span across filesystems. - Can dangle (point to a deleted file). ### 2.1.7 Hard Links — `link()` - Creates a **new directory entry** that points to an **existing inode**. - Increments the target's `i_nlink`. - **Cannot** cross filesystem boundaries. - Cannot link to a directory (except by the superuser in some systems) to prevent cycles. ## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Block Address Calculation Reference** (Block size = $B$ bytes, Block pointer size = $P$ bytes, so $N = B/P$ pointers fit in one indirect block) | Parameter | Formula / Value | Example ($B=4096$, $P=4$, $N=1024$) | |---|---|---| | Direct blocks addressed | 10 | 10 | | Single-indirect blocks addressed | $N$ | 1024 | | Double-indirect blocks addressed | $N^2$ | 1,048,576 | | Triple-indirect blocks addressed | $N^3$ | 1,073,741,824 | | Data via direct pointers | $10 \times B$ | **40 KB** | | Data via single-indirect | $N \times B$ | **4 MB** | | Data via double-indirect | $N^2 \times B$ | **4 GB** | | Data via triple-indirect | $N^3 \times B$ | **4 TB** | | **Max file size** | $(10 + N + N^2 + N^3) \times B$ | ≈ **4 TB** | | Logical block number | $\lfloor \text{byte\_offset} / B \rfloor$ | offset 8193 → block 2 | | Byte offset inside block | $\text{byte\_offset} \bmod B$ | offset 8193 → 1 | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Database systems** (PostgreSQL, Oracle) bypass the OS page cache entirely (`O_DIRECT`) to control block placement at the inode level. - **Filesystem forensics tools** (e.g., `debugfs`, `The Sleuth Kit`) read inodes directly to recover deleted files before the data blocks are overwritten. - **Container storage drivers** (OverlayFS, AUFS) stack multiple filesystems and rely on inode-level copy-on-write semantics. - **Log-structured file systems** (LFS, Flash-Friendly File System F2FS) treat inodes as immutable records written once and chained. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum File Size Given a 4-Level Block Pointer Structure **Given:** - Block size $B = 4096$ bytes $= 2^{12}$ bytes. - Block pointer size $P = 4$ bytes $= 2^{2}$ bytes. - Number of pointers per indirect block: $N = B / P = 2^{12} / 2^{2} = 2^{10} = 1024$. **Find:** Maximum addressable file size in bytes. ### Step 1 — Direct Region The inode holds **10 direct block pointers**. Each pointer references one block of $B$ bytes. $$ \text{Data}_{\text{direct}} = 10 \times B = 10 \times 2^{12} = 2^{12} \times 10 $$ ### Step 2 — Single-Indirect Region The single-indirect pointer points to an index block containing $N = 1024$ block pointers, each addressing $B$ bytes. $$ \text{Data}_{\text{single}} = N \times B = 2^{10} \times 2^{12} = 2^{22} $$ ### Step 3 — Double-Indirect Region The double-indirect pointer points to a block whose $N$ entries each point to a single-indirect block. Total addressable data blocks: $N \times N = N^2 = 2^{20}$. $$ \text{Data}_{\text{double}} = N^2 \times B = 2^{20} \times 2^{12} = 2^{32} $$ ### Step 4 — Triple-Indirect Region Similarly, total addressable data blocks: $N^3 = 2^{30}$. $$ \text{Data}_{\text{triple}} = N^3 \times B = 2^{30} \times 2^{12} = 2^{42} $$ ### Step 5 — Combine All Four Regions $$ \text{Max Size} = (10 + N + N^2 + N^3) \times B $$ $$ \text{Max Size} = (10 \times 2^{12}) + 2^{22} + 2^{32} + 2^{42} $$ $$ \text{Max Size} = 40\,960 + 4\,194\,304 + 4\,294\,967\,296 + 4\,398\,046\,511\,104 $$ $$ \text{Max Size} \approx 4.398 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes} \approx 4 \text{ TB} $$ > [!NOTE] > The dominant term is the triple-indirect contribution $N^3 \times B = 2^{42}$. The direct, single, and double contributions are negligible in comparison. ## 3.2 Derivation: Locating the Correct Block Pointer Level for a Given Byte Offset **Problem:** A file uses 4 KB blocks. The byte offset within the file is $O = 12{,}500$ bytes. Determine (a) the logical block number, (b) the byte offset inside the block, and (c) which inode pointer level holds the block. ### Part (a) — Logical Block Number $$ \text{block\_num} = \left\lfloor \frac{O}{B} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{12500}{4096} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 3.0517... \rfloor = 3 $$ ### Part (b) — Byte Offset Within the Block $$ \text{block\_offset} = O \bmod B = 12500 \bmod 4096 = 12500 - (3 \times 4096) = 12500 - 12288 = 212 $$ ### Part (c) — Pointer Level Compare `block_num = 3` against the boundary constants: - Direct region: 0 to 9 → covers 10 blocks. - Single-indirect region: 10 to 10 + 1024 − 1 = 1033. Since $3 < 10$, this block is held by **direct pointer #3** of the inode. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Inode Operations | Operation | System Call | Inode State Change | Directory State Change | Block Allocation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Create file | `open(O_CREAT)` | New inode allocated, link=1, size=0 | New entry added to parent dir | None initially | | Write data | `write()` | Size grows, block pointers populated | None | Data blocks allocated on demand | | Read data | `read()` | atime updated | None | None (read from cache or disk) | | Unlink file | `unlink()` | link_count decremented; freed if 0 | Entry removed from parent dir | Data blocks freed if link=0 | | Rename file | `rename()` | Unchanged (same inode) | Entry moved between directories | None | | Hard link | `link()` | link_count incremented | New entry added elsewhere | None | | Symbolic link | `symlink()` | New inode of type LNK | New entry added | Small data block holds pathname | | mkdir | `mkdir()` | New inode of type DIR, link=2 (for `.`) | New entry; `.` and `..` entries inside | One block for `.` and `..` | ## 3.4 Derivation: Inode Number to Disk Location In a partition with a fixed inode table, the disk byte offset of the i-th inode is: $$ \text{InodeDiskAddress} = \text{InodeTableStart} + (i - 1) \times \text{InodeSize} $$ **Example:** Inode table starts at byte 65,536. Inode size is 256 bytes. Find the address of inode #50. $$ \text{Address} = 65536 + (50 - 1) \times 256 = 65536 + 49 \times 256 = 65536 + 12544 = 78080 $$ # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] **What is an inode? List any four fields stored in an inode.** **Model Answer:** An **inode (index node)** is an on-disk data structure used by Unix-like file systems to store all metadata about a file, except its name. Each file is uniquely identified by an inode number, and the directory entry merely maps a filename to this number. Four important inode fields: 1. **File type and mode** (regular file, directory, permissions). 2. **File size** in bytes. 3. **Timestamps** (atime, mtime, ctime). 4. **Block pointers** (10 direct, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, 1 triple-indirect). *(Acceptable alternatives: UID, GID, link count, indirect block count.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **Differentiate between hard links and symbolic links with respect to inode operations.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Hard Link | Symbolic (Soft) Link | |---|---|---| | Target | Points to the **same inode number** | Points to a **filename/pathname** | | Inode effect | Increments target's `i_nlink` | Creates a **new inode** of type `LNK` | | Cross-filesystem | **Not allowed** | Allowed | | Dangling reference | Cannot exist (inode is alive) | **Can dangle** if target is deleted | | Directory link | Restricted (to prevent cycles) | Allowed | | `ls -l` shows | Same link count increase | `-> target` arrow notation | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts, typically 7 marks each. Both internal choices below follow this exact pattern. --- ### Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Pattern] **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a Unix inode. Describe the role of direct, single-indirect, double-indirect, and triple-indirect block pointers in addressing file data. **Model Solution:** The Unix inode is a 256-byte (typical) on-disk structure that holds all file metadata. Its layout is: 1. **Mode field (2 bytes):** Encodes file type and 12 permission bits (rwx for owner/group/others + setuid, setgid, sticky). 2. **UID (2 bytes) and GID (2 bytes):** Owner and group identifiers. 3. **File size (4 or 8 bytes):** Logical size in bytes. 4. **Timestamps (12 bytes):** atime, mtime, ctime. 5. **Link count (2 bytes):** Number of hard links. 6. **Block count (4 bytes):** Number of 512-byte sectors used. 7. **Block pointer array (52 bytes):** 13 pointers of 4 bytes each. **Block Pointer Roles:** - **10 Direct Pointers:** Each directly references a data block. They allow fast access to the first $10B$ bytes of a file. Example: with 4 KB blocks, this gives **40 KB**. - **1 Single-Indirect Pointer:** Points to an index block containing $N$ pointers to data blocks. Supports $N \times B$ additional bytes. With $N=1024$ and $B=4096$, this is **4 MB**. - **1 Double-Indirect Pointer:** Points to an index block whose $N$ entries each point to a single-indirect block. Supports $N^2 \times B$ bytes, i.e., **4 GB** with standard parameters. - **1 Triple-Indirect Pointer:** Two extra levels of indirection. Supports $N^3 \times B$ bytes, i.e., **4 TB** with standard parameters. **Total maximum file size:** $$ (10 + N + N^2 + N^3) \times B $$ Refer to the **Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram** for the visual layout. **(b) [7 Marks]** Given a filesystem with a block size of 4 KB and 4-byte block pointers, calculate: 1. The maximum file size. 2. The number of disk accesses required to read byte offset 5,000,000 in a file. 3. The logical block number and intra-block offset for byte 5,000,000. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Constants:** - $B = 4096$ bytes. - $P = 4$ bytes. - $N = B / P = 4096 / 4 = 1024$ pointers per indirect block. **Step 2 — Maximum file size:** $$ \text{Max} = (10 + 1024 + 1024^2 + 1024^3) \times 4096 $$ $$ \text{Max} = (10 + 1024 + 1{,}048{,}576 + 1{,}073{,}741{,}824) \times 4096 $$ $$ \text{Max} \approx 1{,}074{,}791{,}434 \times 4096 $$ $$ \text{Max} \approx 4.402 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes} \approx 4 \text{ TB} $$ **Step 3 — Logical block number and intra-block offset:** $$ \text{block\_num} = \left\lfloor \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{4096} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 1220.70... \rfloor = 1220 $$ $$ \text{block\_offset} = 5{,}000{,}000 \bmod 4096 = 5{,}000{,}000 - (1220 \times 4096) = 5{,}000{,}000 - 4{,}997{,}120 = 2880 $$ **Step 4 — Determine pointer level:** - Direct region: 0–9 (10 blocks) - Single-indirect: 10–1033 (1024 blocks) - Double-indirect: 1034–1,049,609 Since $1220 \le 1033$, this block is reached via the **single-indirect pointer**. **Step 5 — Disk accesses to read byte 5,000,000:** 1. Read the **inode** to get the single-indirect pointer → **1 access**. 2. Read the **single-indirect index block** to find the address of data block #1220 → **1 access**. 3. Read the **data block** containing the byte → **1 access**. **Total = 3 disk accesses.** --- ### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Pattern] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the inode operations: file creation, file deletion, and creation of a symbolic link. How does the kernel ensure data integrity if a file is deleted while still open? **Model Solution:** **1. File Creation (`create()`):** - The kernel scans the inode bitmap to find a free inode. - It initializes all fields: mode, owner, timestamps, link count = 1, size = 0, all block pointers = 0. - It allocates a directory entry in the parent directory, mapping the new filename to the allocated i-number. - The parent directory's block is flushed to disk. **2. File Deletion (`unlink()`):** - The kernel locates the directory entry for the given name in the parent directory. - It decrements the target inode's link count (`i_nlink--`). - If `i_nlink == 0`: - Truncate the file by freeing all data blocks referenced by the block pointer array. - Mark the inode as free in the inode bitmap. - Remove the directory entry from the parent directory. - The parent directory's block is flushed. **3. Symbolic Link Creation (`symlink()`):** - Allocate a new inode of type `LNK`. - Store the target's pathname in the new file's data blocks. - Create a new directory entry in the parent directory mapping the link name to the new i-number. - Crucially, the **target inode's link count is NOT incremented**. **Data Integrity when a file is unlinked while open:** The kernel maintains **two separate counts**: - `i_nlink` (on-disk link count): tracks directory entries. - `i_count` (in-memory reference count): tracks open file descriptors and VFS references. When `unlink()` is called, only `i_nlink` is decremented. The data blocks are freed **only when both** `i_nlink == 0` **and** `i_count == 0`. Until all processes close the file, the in-memory inode (and the buffer cache pages) keep the data alive. The data is written back to the same disk blocks that are now "unlinked" but not yet freed. **(b) [7 Marks]** A filesystem uses 1 KB blocks and 4-byte disk block addresses. The inode has 8 direct pointers, 1 single-indirect, 1 double-indirect, and 1 triple-indirect pointer. Calculate: 1. Maximum file size. 2. Maximum number of file blocks addressable. 3. Number of disk accesses to access byte offset 6,000,000. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Constants:** - $B = 1024$ bytes. - $P = 4$ bytes. - $N = B / P = 1024 / 4 = 256$ pointers per indirect block. **Step 2 — Maximum number of addressable blocks:** $$ \text{Max blocks} = 8 + 256 + 256^2 + 256^3 $$ $$ = 8 + 256 + 65{,}536 + 16{,}777{,}216 $$ $$ = 16{,}843{,}016 \text{ blocks} $$ **Step 3 — Maximum file size:** $$ \text{Max bytes} = 16{,}843{,}016 \times 1024 \approx 1.7247 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes} \approx 16 \text{ GB} $$ **Step 4 — Disk accesses for byte offset 6,000,000:** Logical block number: $$ \text{block\_num} = \left\lfloor \frac{6{,}000{,}000}{1024} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 5859.375 \rfloor = 5859 $$ **Pointer level determination:** - Direct: 0–7 - Single-indirect: 8 to $8 + 256 - 1 = 263$ - Double-indirect: 264 to $264 + 65{,}536 - 1 = 65{,}799$ Since $264 \le 5859 \le 65{,}799$, this block is in the **double-indirect** region. **Disk accesses:** 1. Read the **inode** → 1 access. 2. Read the **double-indirect index block** (top level) → 1 access. 3. Read the appropriate **single-indirect index block** (second level) → 1 access. 4. Read the **data block** → 1 access. **Total = 4 disk accesses.** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Confusing `i_nlink` with `i_count`** — These are two completely different counters. Examiners frequently test this distinction. `i_nlink` is on-disk (directory entries), `i_count` is in-memory (open file descriptors). > 2. **Forgetting to update the parent directory block** — A common error in `create()`, `unlink()`, `link()`, `rename()`. The directory block itself must be written to disk. > 3. **Wrong formula for $N$** — Some students write $N = B + P$ or $N = B \times P$ instead of $N = B / P$. Always $N$ is "how many pointers fit in one block". > 4. **Miscounting pointer levels** — There are exactly **13 pointers total** in a classic inode (10 direct + 3 indirect). The "block pointer region" is 52 bytes (13 × 4). > 5. **Hard link vs. soft link mistakes** — A symbolic link creates a *new* inode; a hard link does not. The link count semantics are the most-asked concept in Part A. > 6. **Skipping the access-count step in numerical problems** — When the question asks "calculate the disk accesses", students often just find the block number and stop. Always show the access count explicitly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - **Inode** = on-disk metadata structure; **not** the filename and **not** the data. - Every file = **exactly one inode**, identified by a unique **inode number (i-number)**. - Inode fields: **mode, UID, GID, size, atime/mtime/ctime, link count, block pointers**. - **Block pointer region** = 10 direct + 1 single-indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect = **13 pointers total**. - $N = B / P$ = pointers per indirect block. - Maximum file size = $(10 + N + N^2 + N^3) \times B$. - For $B = 4096, P = 4$ (i.e., $N = 1024$): Max size ≈ **4 TB**. - Disk access count = **1 (inode) + indirection levels + 1 (data)**. - Direct → 2 accesses total. - Single-indirect → 3 accesses total. - Double-indirect → 4 accesses total. - Triple-indirect → 5 accesses total. - **Hard link** = new directory entry to existing inode (no new inode); increments `i_nlink`; cannot cross filesystems. - **Symbolic link** = new inode of type LNK whose data is the target's pathname; can dangle; can cross filesystems. - **File deletion** with `i_nlink == 0` and `i_count > 0` → data is preserved until last FD closes. - **Directory operations** (`mkdir`, `rmdir`) require special handling: `.` and `..` entries, plus the empty-directory rule for `rmdir`. - The kernel never operates on filenames for I/O — it only operates on **in-core inode copies** referenced by file descriptors. - Inode address on disk = $\text{InodeTableStart} + (i - 1) \times \text{InodeSize}$. - **KTU keyword triggers**: "explain inode structure", "block pointer addressing", "hard vs soft link", "max file size calculation", "disk access count" — these are the highest-frequency exam question patterns.
